Lowest cost per bit"] REG_P --> CACHE_P CACHE_P --> RAM_P RAM_P --> SSD_P SSD_P --> HDD_P ``` **Trend (top → bottom):** **Speed decreases**, **Capacity increases**, **Cost per bit decreases**. ## 4.4 System Bus — Three Sub-Buses ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph CPU2["CPU"] CU2["Control Unit"] end subgraph ADDR["ADDRESS BUS unidirectional 32 or 64 bits"] A0["A0"] A1["A1"] An["A31 or A63"] end subgraph DATA["DATA BUS bidirectional 32 64 or 128 bits"] D0["D0"] D1["D1"] Dn["D31 or D63"] end subgraph CTRL["CONTROL BUS 10 to 20 lines"] RD["Read not"] WR["Write not"] INT["Interrupt"] CLK["Clock"] end MEM2["Memory and IO Devices"] CU2 -- drives --> ADDR CU2 -- drives --> CTRL ADDR --> MEM2 DATA <--> MEM2 CTRL --> MEM2 ``` --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** ### **Q1. Define a functional unit of a computer. List the five basic functional units with one example device for each.** [3 Marks] **Model Answer:** A **functional unit** is a logically distinct block of a computer system that performs a specific role in the fetch–decode–execute cycle. The five basic functional units are: | # | Functional Unit | Function | Example Device | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Input Unit** | Accepts data from the outside world | Keyboard | | 2 | **Memory Unit** | Stores data and instructions (primary + secondary) | RAM / Hard Disk | | 3 | **ALU** | Performs arithmetic and logic operations | Built into CPU | | 4 | **Control Unit** | Coordinates all units via control signals | Built into CPU | | 5 | **Output Unit** | Presents processed results to the user | Monitor | The ALU + Control Unit + Registers together form the **CPU**. --- ### **Q2. Differentiate between primary and secondary memory. Give one example of each.** [3 Marks] **Model Answer:** | Basis | Primary Memory | Secondary Memory | |---|---|---| | **Access by CPU** | Direct (CPU accesses via address bus) | Indirect (via OS / drivers) | | **Speed** | Fast (ns range) | Slow (ms range) | | **Cost per bit** | High | Low | | **Capacity** | Small (GB) | Very large (TB) | | **Volatility** | Usually **volatile** (RAM) | **Non-volatile** | | **Example** | DRAM, SRAM | HDD, SSD, Flash drive | --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Pattern) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]* **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **basic functional units of a computer system**. Describe the role of the **system bus** in interconnecting these units. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** Explain the **memory hierarchy** of a computer system. Discuss the **trade-off** between access time, cost, and capacity at each level. **[7 Marks]** --- #### Model Solution for Q.A(a): A computer system has **five functional units** operating under the **Fetch–Decode–Execute** cycle: 1. **Input Unit:** Converts human/supplied data into binary. *Examples: keyboard, mouse, scanner.* 2. **Memory Unit:** Stores both **data** and **instructions**. Divided into: - Primary (RAM, ROM) — fast, volatile, directly accessed. - Secondary (HDD, SSD) — slow, non-volatile, high capacity. 3. **ALU (Arithmetic Logic Unit):** Performs all arithmetic (+, –, ×, ÷) and logical (AND, OR, NOT, XOR) operations. Stores result flags in the **PSW** (Z, C, N, V, P). 4. **Control Unit (CU):** The "brain" of the CPU. It fetches instructions from memory, decodes them, and issues **control signals** to coordinate ALU, registers, memory, and I/O. 5. **Output Unit:** Converts binary results into human-readable form. *Examples: monitor, printer, speaker.* **The System Bus** is a shared communication pathway with three sub-buses: - **Address Bus (unidirectional):** Carries the location of memory/I/O. Width $n$ bits → can address $2^n$ locations. - **Data Bus (bidirectional):** Carries the actual data. Width determines the word size. - **Control Bus:** Carries Read/Write, Interrupt, Clock, and Reset signals. The CPU (ALU + CU + Registers) is connected to memory and I/O through this bus, allowing all units to exchange data and control information seamlessly. --- #### Model Solution for Q.A(b): The **memory hierarchy** organizes storage into a pyramid from **fastest/smallest/most-expensive** at the top to **slowest/largest/cheapest** at the bottom: | Level | Component | Capacity | Access Time | Cost/Bit | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 (Top) | **Registers** | 16 – 64 words | ~0.5 ns | Highest | | 2 | **Cache (SRAM)** | KB – tens of MB | 1 – 20 ns | Very High | | 3 | **Main Memory (DRAM)** | GB | 50 – 100 ns | Moderate | | 4 | **Secondary (SSD/HDD)** | TB | 0.1 – 10 ms | Low | | 5 (Bottom) | **Tertiary (Tape/Cloud)** | PB | seconds | Lowest | **Trade-off (the "Memory Triangle"):** - Moving **up** the hierarchy: **speed ↑, cost ↑, capacity ↓**. - Moving **down** the hierarchy: **speed ↓, cost ↓, capacity ↑**. - The principle of **locality of reference** (temporal + spatial) justifies this design — recently used data is likely to be used again soon, so it is kept in faster, smaller memories close to the CPU. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]* **(a)** Draw the **internal block diagram of a CPU** and explain the functions of **PC, IR, MAR, MBR, AC, and PSW** registers. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** A computer has a **32-bit address bus** and a **16-bit data bus**. - (i) Calculate the **maximum addressable memory** in bytes. - (ii) Find the **highest address** in hexadecimal. - (iii) If the bus clock is **200 MHz** and one word is transferred per cycle, compute the **data transfer rate in MB/s**. **[7 Marks]** --- #### Model Solution for Q.B(a): The **CPU internal block diagram** includes the ALU, Control Unit, and the following key registers: | Register | Full Name | Function | |---|---|---| | **PC** | Program Counter | Holds the address of the **next instruction** to be fetched. Auto-incremented after each fetch. | | **IR** | Instruction Register | Holds the **currently executing instruction** (opcode + operand fields) for the CU to decode. | | **MAR** | Memory Address Register | Buffers the **memory address** before a read/write operation. | | **MBR** (or MDR) | Memory Data Register | Buffers the **data** being transferred to/from memory. | | **AC** | Accumulator | The **primary working register** of the ALU — holds one operand and receives the result. | | **PSW** | Program Status Word | A register containing **status flags**: Z (Zero), C (Carry), N (Negative), V (Overflow), P (Parity). | **Fetch–Decode–Execute flow using these registers:** 1. MAR ← **PC**; Read Memory; MBR ← Memory[MAR]; IR ← MBR; **PC ← PC + 1**. 2. CU decodes **IR**; sends control signals to ALU. 3. ALU reads operands from **AC** and **MBR**, performs operation, stores result in **AC**, updates **PSW**. --- #### Model Solution for Q.B(b): **(i) Maximum addressable memory:** $$ N = 2^{n} = 2^{32} \text{ locations} = 4{,}294{,}967{,}296 \text{ bytes} = \mathbf{4 \text{ GB}} $$ **(ii) Highest address in hexadecimal:** The last address is $(2^{32} - 1) = \text{FFFFFFFF}_{16}$ (32 ones in binary = 8 F's in hex). $$ \boxed{\text{Highest address} = \text{FFFFFFFF}_{16}} $$ **(iii) Data transfer rate:** - Data bus width: $W = 16$ bits = 2 bytes. - Bus frequency: $f = 200 \text{ MHz} = 200 \times 10^6$ transfers/sec. $$ \text{BW} = W \times f = 2 \text{ bytes} \times 200 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz} $$ $$ \text{BW} = 400 \times 10^6 \text{ bytes/sec} = 400 \text{ MB/s} $$ $$ \boxed{\text{Data transfer rate} = 400 \text{ MB/s}} $$ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU students LOSE marks on this topic (and how to avoid it):** > > 1. **Forgetting to convert bits ↔ bytes:** Bandwidth questions often give bus width in *bits* and ask for MB/s. **Divide by 8**, and convert MHz to Hz ($1 \text{ MHz} = 10^6$, NOT $2^{20}$). Losing 1–2 marks is common. > > 2. **Confusing $2^n$ with $n^2$:** The maximum addressable memory is **$2^n$**, not $n^2$, not $2n$. With 32 bits, you get **4 GB**, not 64 bytes. > > 3. **Mixing up volatile/non-volatile:** RAM is **volatile**; ROM, HDD, SSD are **non-volatile**. Examiners catch this every year. > > 4. **Omitting the bus diagram:** When asked for a "block diagram," **draw the system bus explicitly** as a separate block connecting CPU, Memory, and I/O. Marks are reserved for the bus. > > 5. **Writing "Control Unit processes data":** The CU **never** processes data — it only **controls** the components that do. Examiners deduct ½ mark for this. > > 6. **Forgetting register full forms:** Always write **PC = Program Counter, IR = Instruction Register, MAR = Memory Address Register, MBR = Memory Buffer Register, PSW = Program Status Word** at least once. Don't use abbreviations only. > > 7. **Last address ≠ total memory:** If the address range is 0 to $2^n - 1$, then *count* is $2^n$, but the *largest value* is $2^n - 1$. Examiners specifically check the difference. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **Final 60-second revision before the KTU exam:** - ☐ A computer has **5 functional units**: Input, Memory, ALU, Control Unit, Output. - ☐ **CPU = ALU + Control Unit + Registers** (PC, IR, MAR, MBR, AC, PSW). - ☐ **Von Neumann architecture** = stored-program concept; both data and instructions share the **same memory and bus**. - ☐ **System bus = Address Bus + Data Bus + Control Bus.** - ☐ Address Bus is **unidirectional** (CPU → Memory); Data Bus is **bidirectional**. - ☐ Maximum memory addressable with $n$-bit address bus: $\mathbf{N = 2^n}$ words. - ☐ Last valid address = $\mathbf{2^n - 1}$; first address = **0**. - ☐ **Bandwidth = (Data bus width in bytes) × (Bus frequency in Hz)** → result in **bytes/sec**. - ☐ **Memory hierarchy:** Registers → Cache (SRAM) → RAM (DRAM) → SSD/HDD → Tape/Cloud. *Up = faster, costlier, smaller.* - ☐ **SRAM** (cache) = flip-flops, no refresh, fast, expensive. - ☐ **DRAM** (main memory) = capacitor-based, needs **refresh**, cheap. - ☐ **RAM = volatile**; **ROM/SSD/HDD = non-volatile**. - ☐ **ALU flags:** Z (zero), C (carry — unsigned overflow), V (overflow — signed), N (negative), P (parity). - ☐ **Control Unit** = either **hardwired** (fast, fixed logic) or **microprogrammed** (flexible, uses control ROM). - ☐ **Hit rate formula** for average access time: $t_{avg} = H \cdot t_c + (1 - H) \cdot t_m$. - ☐ Always write **units** in numerical answers (ns, MHz, MB/s) — KTU awards a ½ mark specifically for correct units. - ☐ **System performance equation:** $\text{CPU Time} = \text{Instruction Count} \times \text{CPI} \times \text{Clock Period}$. ---

topics.title: Von Neumann and Harvard Computer Architectures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT403 - COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE AND MICROCONTROLLER
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Von Neumann and Harvard Computer Architectures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1 — Von Neumann and Harvard Computer Architectures

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Von Neumann Architecture (Stored-Program Concept)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
The **Von Neumann Architecture** is a computer architecture model proposed by the mathematician and physicist **John von Neumann in 1945**, in which both the **program instructions** and the **data** are stored in a **single, shared memory unit** (a common address and data bus), and the Central Processing Unit (CPU) fetches them sequentially through a single shared pathway. This single shared storage and execution model is termed the **"Stored-Program Computer"** or **"Princeton Architecture"**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Tenet of Von Neumann Architecture:**
> *Single memory* holds both *instructions* and *data*. A *single bus* is used to fetch either. Therefore, the CPU cannot simultaneously fetch an instruction and read/write data — it must alternate.

**Intuitive Analogy — The "Single Counter Clerk" Bank:**
Imagine a small bank with only **one clerk** and **one queue line** that serves **two types of customers**: those making deposits (data) and those asking about loans (instructions). Because the clerk has only one register, he can serve only one type of customer at a time. If a loan inquiry is being processed, every depositor must wait, and vice-versa. This is precisely the bottleneck of the Von Neumann model — the **single shared path** creates a queue.

---

### 1.2 The Harvard Architecture (Physically Separated Storage)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
The **Harvard Architecture** is a computer architecture model that uses **physically separate memory units and separate buses** for **program instructions** and **data**. It is based on the Harvard Mark I relay-based computer (1944), designed by Howard Aiken. The CPU can therefore fetch an instruction from instruction memory while simultaneously accessing data memory, allowing **parallel memory access**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Tenet of Harvard Architecture:**
> Two *physically independent* memories: one for **instructions**, one for **data**, each with its **own address bus** and **own data bus**. Simultaneous (parallel) access is the architectural advantage.

**Intuitive Analogy — The "Two-Clerk Bank":**
Picture a bank with **two independent counters**: Counter A for deposits (data) and Counter B for loan inquiries (instructions). Each counter has its own cashier, register, and queue. A depositor can be served at Counter A at the exact same moment a loan inquiry is being handled at Counter B. Neither customer waits for the other — they run in **parallel**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Modified Harvard Architecture:** Most modern CPUs (e.g., ARM Cortex-M, AVR, modern x86 caches) use a *modified* Harvard design internally — separate L1 instruction and data caches backed by a unified L2/main memory. This blends the speed of Harvard with the flexibility of Von Neumann.

---

### 1.3 Core Architectural Building Blocks

Both architectures share these fundamental subsystems:

| Subsystem | Function | Present in Von Neumann | Present in Harvard |
| :--- | :--- | :---: | :---: |
| **ALU** | Performs arithmetic & logic operations | Yes | Yes |
| **Program Counter (PC)** | Holds address of next instruction | Yes | Yes |
| **Control Unit (CU)** | Decodes & sequences instructions | Yes | Yes |
| **Memory Unit** | Stores instructions & data | **Single, shared** | **Two, separate** |
| **Accumulator / Registers** | Fast temporary storage for CPU | Yes | Yes |
| **I/O Devices** | External interface | Yes | Yes |
| **System Bus** | Carries address & data | **Single shared bus** | **Two separate buses** |

---

### 1.4 Historical Origin & Standard Metrics

* **Von Neumann Architecture** — Published in the **"First Draft of a Report on the EDVAC" (1945)**.
* **Harvard Architecture** — Realised in the **Harvard Mark I (1944)** built by IBM for Harvard University.
* **Standard Metrics** used by KTU examiners to evaluate these architectures:
  * **Memory Throughput** (words/sec) — typically **bold 10⁶ to 10⁹** for modern systems.
  * **Clock Frequency** — measured in **MHz/GHz**.
  * **Bus Width** — measured in **bits (8, 16, 32, 64)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Sequence — Von Neumann Fetch–Execute Cycle

The Von Neumann machine operates on an inherently **sequential** cycle. The CPU must perform the following four micro-operations one at a time over a **single bus**:

1. **Fetch Instruction (FI):** CPU places the address in the Program Counter (PC) onto the address bus; the instruction is read from memory into the Instruction Register (IR).
2. **Decode Instruction (DI):** Control Unit decodes the opcode held in IR.
3. **Execute / Fetch Operand (FO):** If the instruction requires data, the CPU *re-uses the same shared bus* to fetch the operand from the same memory.
4. **Store Result (SR):** Result is written back to memory via the same shared bus.

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Architectural Bottleneck:** Because the bus is shared, an instruction fetch and a data read/write **cannot occur at the same instant**. This sequential dependency is the famous **Von Neumann Bottleneck**, limiting computational throughput.

---

## 2.2 Operational Sequence — Harvard Fetch–Execute Cycle

In pure Harvard architecture, the cycle proceeds with **concurrent** memory traffic:

1. **Fetch Instruction (FI):** Instruction is read from instruction memory via the **instruction bus** (occurs every cycle).
2. **Decode Instruction (DI):** Control Unit decodes the opcode.
3. **Fetch Operand / Execute (FO):** Operand is read from data memory via the **data bus** — **simultaneously allowed** because the buses are separate.
4. **Store Result (SR):** Written back to data memory via the **data bus**.

The instruction fetch and data access are decoupled in time, removing the bus-contention bottleneck entirely.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Cheat-Sheet

The following table summarises every formula, metric, and boundary condition a KTU 2024 student must know to solve problems on this topic. Note that the table uses `\vert` for absolute-value separators to preserve the markdown pipe syntax.

| Concept | Equation / Relationship | Symbols & Units | Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **CPU Execution Time (T)** | $T = \dfrac{N \times CPI}{f_{clk}}$ | $N$ = instruction count, $CPI$ = cycles/instruction, $f_{clk}$ in **Hz** | Total time to execute a program |
| **MIPS Rating** | $\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{f_{clk}}{CPI \times 10^{6}}$ | Higher MIPS $\Rightarrow$ faster CPU | Speed comparison |
| **Von Neumann Throughput** | $T_{VN} = N \times (t_{fetch} + t_{exec})$ | $t_{fetch}$ and $t_{exec}$ share **one bus** | Baseline throughput |
| **Harvard Throughput** | $T_{HARV} = N \times \max(t_{fetch},\, t_{exec})$ | Parallel bus access | Parallel throughput |
| **Speed-up (Harvard vs Von N.)** | $S = \dfrac{T_{VN}}{T_{HARV}} = \dfrac{t_{fetch}+t_{exec}}{\max(t_{fetch}, t_{exec})}$ | $S \geq 1$ always | Quantifies Harvard advantage |
| **Bus Bandwidth** | $BW = \dfrac{W \times f_{bus}}{N_{cycles}}$ | $W$ = bus width (bits), $f_{bus}$ in **Hz** | Data transfer rate |
| **Memory Access Time** | $t_{mem} = \dfrac{\text{Memory Size}}{BW}$ | $t_{mem}$ in **seconds** | Memory response delay |
| **Von Neumann Bottleneck Ratio** | $\eta = \dfrac{t_{bus}}{t_{cpu}}$ | $\eta \to 1$ indicates severe bottleneck | Diagnostic metric |

---

## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

| Domain | Architecture Used | Why Chosen |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Microcontrollers (8051, PIC, AVR, ARM Cortex-M)** | **Harvard / Modified Harvard** | Deterministic instruction fetch, ideal for real-time embedded control. |
| **Desktop CPUs (Intel Core, AMD Ryzen)** | **Modified Harvard (caches) over Von Neumann (main RAM)** | Blends flexibility with fast parallel cache access. |
| **DSP Processors (TMS320, SHARC)** | **Harvard (multi-bus)** | Heavy parallel MAC operations on streaming data. |
| **Servers & Laptops (general-purpose)** | **Von Neumann** | Single unified memory simplifies OS, paging, dynamic loading. |
| **FPGA soft-cores (Nios II, MicroBlaze)** | Configurable — **both** variants offered | Designer picks per workload. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Heuristic:** Whenever a problem mentions a *microcontroller, embedded system, or DSP*, the answer must reference Harvard / Modified Harvard. Whenever a problem mentions a *general-purpose computer or workstation*, the answer must reference Von Neumann.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — The Von Neumann Bottleneck Ratio

**Goal:** Quantify the proportion of total CPU time wasted in *bus arbitration* for a Von Neumann machine. We will derive the ratio $\eta$ step-by-step from first principles.

**Given:**
* $t_{cpu}$ = Total time spent inside the CPU per instruction (decode + execute, in seconds).
* $t_{bus}$ = Total time spent waiting for / using the shared memory bus per instruction (in seconds).
* The cycle time per instruction is $T_{cycle} = t_{cpu} + t_{bus}$ because the bus is serialised.

**Step 1 — Define the Bottleneck Ratio $\eta$ as the fraction of $T_{cycle}$ occupied by the bus.**

$$
\eta = \frac{t_{bus}}{T_{cycle}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the expression for $T_{cycle}$.**

$$
\eta = \frac{t_{bus}}{t_{cpu} + t_{bus}}
$$

**Step 3 — To make the formula dimensionless and easier to compare across machines, divide numerator and denominator by $t_{cpu}$.** Define $\rho = \dfrac{t_{bus}}{t_{cpu}}$ (a normalised bus-to-CPU ratio).

$$
\eta = \frac{\rho \, t_{cpu}}{(1 + \rho) \, t_{cpu}} = \frac{\rho}{1 + \rho}
$$

**Step 4 — Examine boundary conditions.**

* If $t_{bus} \ll t_{cpu}$ (i.e. $\rho \to 0$), then $\eta \to 0$: negligible bottleneck.
* If $t_{bus} \gg t_{cpu}$ (i.e. $\rho \to \infty$), then $\eta \to 1$: the bus is the *entire* bottleneck.
* If $t_{bus} = t_{cpu}$ (i.e. $\rho = 1$), then $\eta = 0.5$: half the time is wasted on bus contention.

**Step 5 — Worked numerical example.** Suppose a CPU with $f_{clk} = 100\,\text{MHz}$ (cycle time $10\,\text{ns}$). Memory access takes $t_{bus} = 40\,\text{ns}$ and CPU decode/execute takes $t_{cpu} = 60\,\text{ns}$.

$$
T_{cycle} = 60 + 40 = 100 \text{ ns}
$$

$$
\eta = \frac{40}{100} = 0.40 \quad (\text{i.e. } 40\% \text{ of cycle wasted on bus})
$$

This matches the classic Von Neumann bottleneck signature.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Harvard Speed-up over Von Neumann

**Given:**
* $t_{IF}$ = instruction fetch time (memory read of the opcode).
* $t_{DF}$ = data fetch time (memory read of operand).
* Both memories are equally fast.

**Step 1 — Von Neumann (sequential bus) execution time per instruction that needs an operand.**

$$
T_{VN} = t_{IF} + t_{DF}
$$

Because the bus is shared, fetch and data must occur sequentially.

**Step 2 — Harvard (parallel bus) execution time per same instruction.**

$$
T_{HARV} = \max(t_{IF},\, t_{DF})
$$

The slower of the two parallel operations dictates the cycle time.

**Step 3 — Compute the speed-up factor $S$.**

$$
S = \frac{T_{VN}}{T_{HARV}} = \frac{t_{IF} + t_{DF}}{\max(t_{IF}, t_{DF})}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the symmetric case $t_{IF} = t_{DF} = t$.**

$$
S = \frac{t + t}{t} = 2
$$

Harvard is **exactly 2× faster** when instruction and data accesses are balanced — the classic textbook result.

**Step 5 — Evaluate the asymmetric case $t_{IF} = 10\,\text{ns}$, $t_{DF} = 90\,\text{ns}$.**

$$
S = \frac{10 + 90}{\max(10, 90)} = \frac{100}{90} \approx 1.11
$$

Speed-up drops as one operation dominates. This proves Harvard's advantage is **largest when instruction and data accesses are balanced in frequency and time**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Performance (KTU Exam Favourite)

The table below is the analytical matrix examiners expect when comparing the two architectures. Note the use of `\vert` instead of `|` inside cells.

| Parameter | Von Neumann | Harvard | KTU Favourite Question Type |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Number of memory units** | 1 (unified) | 2 (separate) | "Differentiate with a block diagram." |
| **Number of buses** | 1 (shared) | 2 (separate instruction + data) | "Compare in a table." |
| **Simultaneous access** | **No** | **Yes** | "Explain the bottleneck." |
| **Program execution speed** | Slower (sequential) | Faster (parallel) | "Justify with a formula." |
| **Hardware complexity** | Low | High | "Compare cost." |
| **Flexibility (self-modifying code)** | Allowed | Generally not allowed | "Why?" |
| **Power consumption** | Lower | Higher (more bus drivers) | "Pick for a battery device." |
| **Typical applications** | Desktops, laptops, servers | Microcontrollers, DSP | "Give two examples each." |
| **Instruction fetch width** | Same bus width as data | Can differ (e.g. 16-bit instr, 8-bit data) | "Explain instruction alignment." |
| **Data throughput (words/sec)** | $\dfrac{f_{clk}}{2}$ per cycle | $\dfrac{f_{clk}}{1}$ per cycle | "Calculate throughput." |
| **Cost / silicon area** | Low | $\approx 1.6\times$ higher | "Justify the cost trade-off." |

---

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Worked Problem — Average Cycles per Instruction

**Problem:** A Harvard processor has instruction memory access time $t_{I} = 30\,\text{ns}$ and data memory access time $t_{D} = 50\,\text{ns}$. ALU operations take $t_{ALU} = 20\,\text{ns}$. Each instruction requires exactly one instruction fetch and 70% require one data fetch. Compute the average instruction execution time.

**Step 1 — Compute the cycle time assuming instructions and data are fetched in parallel (Harvard).**

$$
T_{cycle} = \max(t_{I},\, t_{D}) + t_{ALU} = \max(30,\, 50) + 20 = 50 + 20 = 70 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the average execution time.**

$$
T_{avg} = p_{\text{no-data}} \times (t_{I} + t_{ALU}) + p_{\text{data}} \times (t_{I} + t_{ALU})
$$

Where $p_{\text{data}} = 0.70$ and $p_{\text{no-data}} = 0.30$. Substituting:

$$
T_{avg} = 0.30 \times (30 + 20) + 0.70 \times (50 + 20)
$$

$$
T_{avg} = 0.30 \times 50 + 0.70 \times 70 = 15 + 49 = 64 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 3 — Result interpretation.**
The Harvard machine averages **64 ns per instruction**. The dominant component is the data-memory fetch plus the ALU time. If this were a Von Neumann machine, every data fetch would have to be serialised with the instruction fetch, giving a noticeably larger average.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** A student who forgets the **max()** operator for Harvard architecture will incorrectly add $t_I + t_D$, losing 2 marks. Always remember: *parallel* operations are governed by the **slowest**, not the sum.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Von Neumann Architecture — Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CPUblock[CPU]
    PC[Program Counter]
    ALU[ALU]
    CU[Control Unit]
    ACC[Accumulator]
    MEM[Single Shared Memory]
    IO[I/O Devices]
    BusA[Shared Address Bus]
    BusD[Shared Data Bus]

    CPUblock --- PC
    CPUblock --- ALU
    CPUblock --- CU
    CPUblock --- ACC

    BusA --- MEM
    BusD --- MEM
    BusA --- CPUblock
    BusD --- CPUblock
    BusA --- IO
    BusD --- IO

    PC -. next address .-> BusA
    ALU -. operands .-> BusD
    CU -. read/write ctrl .-> BusD
```

**Reading the diagram:** A single set of address and data buses serves **both** the instruction fetch (from PC to memory) and the data read/write (from ALU to memory). There is only **one** physical memory block containing both instructions and data, hence the shared-bus constraint.

---

## 4.2 Harvard Architecture — Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CPUb[CPU]
    PC2[Program Counter]
    ALU2[ALU]
    CU2[Control Unit]
    ACC2[Accumulator]
    IMEM[Instruction Memory]
    DMEM[Data Memory]
    IO2[I/O Devices]
    AB_I[Instruction Address Bus]
    DB_I[Instruction Data Bus]
    AB_D[Data Address Bus]
    DB_D[Data Data Bus]

    CPUb --- PC2
    CPUb --- ALU2
    CPUb --- CU2
    CPUb --- ACC2

    AB_I --- IMEM
    DB_I --- IMEM
    AB_I --- CPUb
    DB_I --- CPUb

    AB_D --- DMEM
    DB_D --- DMEM
    AB_D --- CPUb
    DB_D --- CPUb

    AB_D --- IO2
    DB_D --- IO2

    PC2 -. instr addr .-> AB_I
    IMEM -. opcode .-> DB_I
    ALU2 -. operand .-> DB_D
```

**Reading the diagram:** Two **completely independent** bus pairs service two **physically separate** memories. The instruction side (`AB_I` / `DB_I`) is dedicated to opcode fetches; the data side (`AB_D` / `DB_D`) is dedicated to operand fetch and store. This enables simultaneous, parallel traffic.

---

## 4.3 Modified Harvard Architecture (Modern Practical Realisation)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Core[CPU Core]
    LI[Separate L1 Instruction Cache]
    LD[Separate L1 Data Cache]
    L2[Unified L2 Cache]
    RAM[Main RAM - Von Neumann style]

    Core --- LI
    Core --- LD
    LI --- L2
    LD --- L2
    L2 --- RAM
```

**Reading the diagram:** Inside the CPU die, separate L1 caches behave like a Harvard architecture (parallel access). Behind them, the unified L2 cache and main RAM behave like a Von Neumann architecture (shared storage). This **hybrid** is what every modern x86, ARM Cortex-A, and Apple M-series chip uses.

---

## 4.4 Comparative Functional Flow (KTU Exam Sketch)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph VonN[Von Neumann Flow]
        direction LR
        F1[Step 1: Fetch Instruction]
        D1[Step 2: Decode]
        E1[Step 3: Fetch Operand serialised on same bus]
        S1[Step 4: Execute and Store]
        F1 --> D1 --> E1 --> S1
    end

    subgraph Harv[Harvard Flow]
        direction LR
        F2[Step 1: Fetch Instruction on I-Bus]
        D2[Step 2: Decode]
        E2[Step 3: Fetch Operand in parallel on D-Bus]
        S2[Step 4: Execute and Store]
        F2 --> D2
        E2 --> S2
        D2 -.->|simultaneous| E2
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Visual cue for examiners:** The two horizontal arrows in the Harvard block (`D2 -.->|simultaneous| E2`) emphasise the parallelism. In a Von Neumann diagram, this arrow is *missing* because the steps are strictly serial.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**"Define Von Neumann architecture. State its key limitation."**

**Model Answer:**
Von Neumann architecture, proposed by John von Neumann in 1945, is a stored-program computer design in which **both instructions and data reside in a single shared memory**, accessed through a **single common bus**.
**Key limitation:** The Von Neumann Bottleneck — because the same bus is used to fetch instructions and to read/write data, these two operations **cannot occur simultaneously**, severely limiting throughput.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**"List any three differences between Harvard and Von Neumann architectures."**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Memory units:** Harvard has two physically separate memories (instruction + data); Von Neumann has one shared memory.
2. **Bus structure:** Harvard uses two independent sets of buses; Von Neumann uses a single shared bus.
3. **Parallelism:** Harvard allows simultaneous instruction fetch and data access; Von Neumann serialises them.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks each, with internal choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B carries **14 marks** and is divided into sub-parts (a) for **7 marks** and (b) for **7 marks**. KTU provides internal choice between two complete questions; below both alternatives are supplied.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Module 1]**
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the Von Neumann architecture. Discuss its key advantages and limitations. **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** Derive the speed-up factor of a Harvard architecture over a Von Neumann architecture when instruction and data memory access times are equal. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

1. **Block Diagram:** (Draw a CPU containing PC, ALU, CU, Accumulator; connect to a single shared memory through one address bus and one data bus; include I/O.) — **3 marks** for the diagram.
2. **Operation:** The CPU fetches instructions sequentially from memory via the single bus, decodes them, and either performs an ALU operation or reads/writes data — *all over the same bus*. — **2 marks**.
3. **Advantages:** Simpler hardware, cheaper to build, easier to program (self-modifying code is possible), flexible memory allocation. — **1 mark**.
4. **Limitations:** The Von Neumann Bottleneck (bus serialisation), limits on instruction-fetch throughput, lower overall performance. — **1 mark**.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

1. **Define times:** Let $t_{IF} = t_{DF} = t$ (equal access times). — **1 mark**.
2. **Von Neumann cycle:** $T_{VN} = t_{IF} + t_{DF} = 2t$. — **2 marks**.
3. **Harvard cycle:** $T_{HARV} = \max(t_{IF}, t_{DF}) = t$. — **2 marks**.
4. **Speed-up:** $S = \dfrac{T_{VN}}{T_{HARV}} = \dfrac{2t}{t} = 2$. — **2 marks**.

**Final Answer:** Harvard architecture is exactly **twice as fast** as Von Neumann for balanced workloads.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Module 1]**
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the Harvard architecture and state how it overcomes the Von Neumann bottleneck. **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** A Harvard processor has instruction memory access time of 30 ns and data memory access time of 50 ns. The ALU takes 20 ns. 60% of instructions require a data fetch. Calculate the average instruction execution time. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

1. **Block Diagram:** Two separate memories (Instruction Memory, Data Memory) each connected to the CPU via its own address and data bus. — **3 marks**.
2. **Operation:** Instruction fetch occurs on the I-bus while operand access occurs on the D-bus simultaneously. — **2 marks**.
3. **Overcoming the Bottleneck:** Because the two buses are independent, the CPU can fetch the next instruction *while* still accessing data for the current instruction. This eliminates bus contention and removes the sequential dependency that creates the Von Neumann bottleneck. — **2 marks**.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Cycle time** (parallel memory access):

$$
T_{cycle} = \max(t_{I}, t_{D}) + t_{ALU} = \max(30, 50) + 20 = 70 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 2 — Average time** with 60% of instructions needing data:

$$
T_{avg} = (0.40)(30 + 20) + (0.60)(50 + 20)
$$

$$
T_{avg} = 0.40 \times 50 + 0.60 \times 70 = 20 + 42 = 62 \text{ ns}
$$

**Final Answer:** Average instruction execution time = **62 ns**. — **Full 7 marks** for correct setup, substitution, and final result.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks in this topic:**
>
> 1. **Drawing only one bus** in the Harvard diagram and labelling it shared — this is wrong; you must show **two distinct bus pairs** (I-bus and D-bus).
> 2. **Adding** memory access times in the Harvard problem instead of taking the **maximum** (max() rule). This costs 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Confusing "Modified Harvard" with "Pure Harvard"** — Modified Harvard has separate L1 caches but unified main memory; pure Harvard has two completely separate physical memories from address 0.
> 4. **Omitting the year** (1945 for Von Neumann, 1944 for Harvard Mark I) when asked to "define" — examiners in KTU deduct marks if historical context is missing.
> 5. **Failing to mention the bottleneck** explicitly when describing Von Neumann — the keyword "**Von Neumann Bottleneck**" must appear for full credit.
> 6. **Writing "Harvard is always faster"** — this is false when one memory dominates; use the speed-up formula to qualify.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Von Neumann (1945):** Single shared memory + single shared bus = **Von Neumann Bottleneck**. Used in general-purpose PCs and servers.
- **Harvard (1944):** Two separate memories + two separate bus pairs = **parallel access**. Used in microcontrollers (8051, PIC, AVR), DSPs, and as L1 caches inside modern CPUs.
- **Modified Harvard:** L1 split, L2 unified — what real chips use.
- **Speed-up formula:** $S = \dfrac{t_{IF} + t_{DF}}{\max(t_{IF}, t_{DF})}$, equals **2** when $t_{IF} = t_{DF}$.
- **Bottleneck ratio:** $\eta = \dfrac{\rho}{1 + \rho}$, where $\rho = t_{bus}/t_{cpu}$.
- **Fetch–Execute cycle steps:** Fetch → Decode → Execute (Fetch Operand) → Store.
- **Bus Bandwidth:** $BW = W \times f_{bus} / N_{cycles}$, where $W$ is bus width in bits.
- **Examiner keywords to memorise:** *Stored-program*, *Princeton*, *bottleneck*, *parallel access*, *modified Harvard*, *MAC unit*, *deterministic*.
- **Application rule of thumb:** Embedded / real-time → Harvard; General-purpose OS → Von Neumann.
- **CPU Time formula:** $T = N \times CPI / f_{clk}$ — applicable when comparing architectures at the cycle level.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CISC and RISC Architectures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT403 - COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE AND MICROCONTROLLER
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** CISC and RISC Architectures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **CISC (Complex Instruction Set Computer)** is a processor design philosophy in which a single instruction can execute **multi-step, low-level operations** (such as memory fetch, arithmetic operation, and memory store) within a single machine cycle or a single instruction. The primary design goal is to **minimize the semantic gap** between high-level programming languages and machine code, thereby reducing program size and compiler complexity.

> [!NOTE]
> **RISC (Reduced Instruction Set Computer)** is a processor design philosophy that uses a **small, highly optimized set of instructions**, where each instruction performs a **simple, atomic operation** and is designed to be executed in a **single clock cycle**. The primary design goal is to **simplify hardware**, enable **pipelining**, and maximize **clock frequency** and **instructions per cycle (IPC)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Historical Context for KTU Examiners:**
> - **CISC Era (1970s–1980s):** Dominated by Intel x86, Motorola 68000, VAX-11. Driven by memory scarcity and the desire to reduce program size.
> - **RISC Revolution (1980s–present):** Pioneered by IBM 801, Stanford MIPS, Berkeley RISC-I. Driven by advances in VLSI, compiler technology, and cache memory.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

### CISC: The "Swiss Army Knife" Approach
Imagine a master chef's kitchen where every tool is a **multi-functional gadget** — a single device can peel, slice, dice, and grate vegetables.
- The gadget does a *lot of work* in one motion, but it is **heavy, complex, slow to operate, and expensive to manufacture**.
- A single command ("chop-and-store") accomplishes many things at once, but if the gadget breaks, the entire kitchen halts.

**This is CISC:** instructions are powerful but few in variety; each instruction does "a lot" in hardware.

### RISC: The "Specialist Tools" Approach
Now imagine a **professional kitchen** with a dedicated, lightweight tool for *every single task* — one knife for slicing, one for dicing, one for peeling, one for julienning.
- Each tool does **only one job**, but it does that job **incredibly fast, with high precision, and predictably**.
- The chef (the compiler or programmer) must give *more* sequential instructions, but each one is **simple, uniform, and fast**.

**This is RISC:** simplicity at the hardware level, complexity pushed to the compiler.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid for KTU Viva:**
> - **CISC → Complex → Fewer instructions, more work per instruction**
> - **RISC → Reduced → More instructions, less work per instruction**

---

## 1.3 Why These Two Philosophies Matter (KTU Context)

| Aspect | CISC | RISC |
|---|---|---|
| **Design Motto** | "Make hardware do the work" | "Keep hardware simple, let software (compiler) do the work" |
| **Instruction Count** | Typically **100–250+** instructions | Typically **<100** instructions |
| **Instruction Length** | Variable (1–15 bytes in x86) | **Fixed** (typically 32 bits/4 bytes) |
| **Clock Cycles per Instruction** | **1–20+** (variable) | Almost always **1 cycle** (CPI ≈ 1) |
| **Pipelining Efficiency** | **Difficult** (variable-length instructions complicate stages) | **Highly efficient** (uniform instructions fit pipelines perfectly) |
| **Examples** | Intel x86, AMD64 (legacy mode), Motorola 68000 | ARM, MIPS, SPARC, RISC-V, PowerPC |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Modern Reality (Often Asked in KTU):** Today's x86 processors (Intel/AMD) are *internally* RISC — they translate complex CISC x86 instructions into simpler micro-operations (µops) before execution. This is called the **CISC-to-RISC translation** or **hardware decoding layer**. Pure RISC has effectively "won" the underlying hardware design philosophy, even in CISC ISA chips.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principles of CISC

CISC processors are built around the following design principles:

- **Rich Instruction Set:** A large repertoire of instructions, many of which perform **multiple low-level operations** (e.g., `ADD [MEM], REG` performs a memory read, an addition, and a memory write in a single instruction).
- **Variable-Length Instructions:** Instructions can occupy 1 to 15 bytes (in x86), allowing the encoding of complex addressing modes and operations densely. This **reduces program memory footprint**, which was critical when memory cost was high.
- **Many Addressing Modes:** Direct, indirect, indexed, base+index, base+index+displacement, scaled indexing, etc. — sometimes **20+ modes** in a single CPU.
- **Microprogrammed Control Unit:** CISC CPUs typically use a **microcode ROM** that decodes each complex instruction into a sequence of micro-operations. This simplifies the control logic and allows for instruction-set extensions via firmware updates.
- **Memory-to-Memory Operations:** CISC instructions can reference **memory operands directly**, eliminating the need to load values into registers first.
- **Multi-Cycle Execution:** A single instruction may take anywhere from 2 to 200+ clock cycles internally, but the goal is to *complete a high-level task* in fewer instructions.

> [!TIP]
> **Why "Complex"?** The complexity lies in (a) the decoder (must parse variable-length opcodes), (b) the microcode engine, and (c) the microarchitectural state machine that sequences multi-cycle operations.

---

## 2.2 Operational Principles of RISC

RISC processors are built around disciplined, orthogonal design rules (often called the **RISC commandments**):

- **Single-Cycle Execution:** Every instruction is designed to complete in **exactly one clock cycle** (CPI = 1, ideally).
- **Fixed-Length Instructions:** All instructions are the same width (commonly **32 bits**, though ARM Thumb uses 16, and RISC-V uses variable-but-aligned lengths).
- **Load/Store Architecture:** Memory is accessed **only** via dedicated `LOAD` and `STORE` instructions. Arithmetic/logic operations work **exclusively on registers**.
- **Large Register File:** RISC designs feature **32 or more general-purpose registers** (MIPS: 32, ARM: 16–31, RISC-V: 32). This reduces the need to spill to memory and enables efficient procedure-call conventions.
- **Hardwired Control:** Control logic is implemented via **combinational decoders and finite state machines**, not microcode. This reduces latency and improves clock frequency.
- **Heavy Use of Pipelining:** Because all instructions are uniform in length, timing, and resource requirements, the CPU can be deeply pipelined (5, 7, 12, 14+ stages in modern designs).
- **Compiler Responsibility:** Optimizing compilers (e.g., GCC, LLVM) are responsible for instruction scheduling, register allocation, and exploiting parallelism — not the hardware.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is critical for KTU numerical questions on CPU performance.**

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CPU Execution Time** | $T_{CPU} = N \times CPI \times T_{clock}$ | seconds | $N$ = instruction count, $CPI$ = cycles per instruction, $T_{clock}$ = clock period |
| **Clock Period** | $T_{clock} = \dfrac{1}{f_{clock}}$ | seconds | $f_{clock}$ in Hz |
| **CPI (Cycles Per Instruction)** | $CPI = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(IC_i \times C_i)}{IC_{total}}$ | cycles/instruction | $IC_i$ = count of instruction type $i$, $C_i$ = cycles for that type |
| **MIPS Rating** | $MIPS = \dfrac{f_{clock}}{CPI \times 10^6}$ | million instructions/sec | Higher is better, but ISA-dependent |
| **MFLOPS Rating** | $MFLOPS = \dfrac{\text{Number of floating-point ops}}{T_{CPU} \times 10^6}$ | million FP ops/sec | Better metric for scientific workloads |
| **Amdahl's Law** | $Speedup = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{S}}$ | dimensionless | $f$ = fraction enhanced, $S$ = speedup of enhancement |
| **Instruction Mix Weighted CPI** | $CPI_{avg} = \sum p_i \times CPI_i$ | cycles | $p_i$ = frequency of instruction type $i$ |
| **Pipelined Ideal CPI** | $CPI_{pipe} = 1$ (ideal) | cycles | Real CPI > 1 due to hazards |
| **Pipeline Speedup (ideal)** | $S_{ideal} = k$ (for $k$-stage pipe) | dimensionless | Limited by hazards and stalls |

**Boundary Conditions to Remember:**
- $CPI \geq 1$ always (cannot be less than 1 for scalar pipelines).
- $0 \leq f \leq 1$ in Amdahl's Law.
- $N_{CISC} < N_{RISC}$ typically, but $CPI_{CISC} \gg CPI_{RISC}$.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Embedded Systems:** RISC (especially **ARM Cortex-M** series) dominates IoT, automotive, and mobile due to low power and predictable timing.
- **Servers & HPC:** RISC-V and ARM are challenging x86 in data centers (e.g., AWS Graviton, Fujitsu A64FX in Fugaku supercomputer).
- **Legacy Codebases:** x86's CISC nature ensures backward compatibility — billions of lines of legacy software still run unchanged.
- **Compiler Design:** RISC architectures pushed the development of sophisticated **optimizing compilers**, a field in which KTU-affiliated engineers often specialize.
- **Microcontroller Pedagogy:** In KTU's PCMRT403 lab, students typically work with **ARM Cortex-M** (RISC) and occasionally legacy 8051 (CISC) — understanding both philosophies is essential for hardware-software co-design.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: CPU Execution Time Comparison (CISC vs RISC)

This is a **classic KTU-style numerical problem.** Let us derive the comparison rigorously.

### 3.1.1 Given Assumptions (Model Problem)

Suppose a particular program requires:
- **On a CISC machine:** $N_{CISC} = 1{,}000{,}000$ instructions, average $CPI_{CISC} = 4$, clock $f_{CISC} = 100 \text{ MHz}$.
- **On a RISC machine:** $N_{RISC} = 1{,}500{,}000$ instructions (more, because each CISC op is decomposed), average $CPI_{RISC} = 1.2$, clock $f_{RISC} = 150 \text{ MHz}$.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Computation

**Step 1 — Compute the clock period for each machine.**

$$T_{clock,CISC} = \dfrac{1}{f_{CISC}} = \dfrac{1}{100 \times 10^6} = 10 \text{ ns}$$

$$T_{clock,RISC} = \dfrac{1}{f_{RISC}} = \dfrac{1}{150 \times 10^6} = 6.667 \text{ ns}$$

*Logic:* Clock period is the inverse of frequency. A higher frequency means a shorter period, so each cycle is faster.

**Step 2 — Apply the CPU time formula.**

$$T_{CPU,CISC} = N_{CISC} \times CPI_{CISC} \times T_{clock,CISC}$$

Substituting:

$$T_{CPU,CISC} = 1{,}000{,}000 \times 4 \times 10 \times 10^{-9} \text{ s}$$

$$T_{CPU,CISC} = 40 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 40 \text{ ms}$$

For RISC:

$$T_{CPU,RISC} = N_{RISC} \times CPI_{RISC} \times T_{clock,RISC}$$

$$T_{CPU,RISC} = 1{,}500{,}000 \times 1.2 \times 6.667 \times 10^{-9} \text{ s}$$

Let us expand carefully:

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{CPU,RISC} &= 1{,}500{,}000 \times 1.2 \times 6.667 \times 10^{-9} \\
&= 1{,}800{,}000 \times 6.667 \times 10^{-9} \\
&= 12{,}000{,}600 \times 10^{-9} \\
&= 12.0006 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} \\
&\approx 12.0 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}$$

*Logic:* The RISC machine, despite executing *more* instructions, finishes ~3.33× faster because its CPI is near 1 and its clock is faster.

**Step 3 — Compute the speedup ratio.**

$$S = \dfrac{T_{CPU,CISC}}{T_{CPU,RISC}} = \dfrac{40 \text{ ms}}{12.0 \text{ ms}} = 3.\overline{3}$$

**Step 4 — Compute MIPS ratings for both.**

$$MIPS_{CISC} = \dfrac{f_{CISC}}{CPI_{CISC} \times 10^6} = \dfrac{100 \times 10^6}{4 \times 10^6} = 25 \text{ MIPS}$$

$$MIPS_{RISC} = \dfrac{f_{RISC}}{CPI_{RISC} \times 10^6} = \dfrac{150 \times 10^6}{1.2 \times 10^6} = 125 \text{ MIPS}$$

*Logic:* RISC achieves 5× the MIPS rating due to lower CPI and higher frequency — this directly illustrates the **performance efficiency** of the RISC philosophy.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix (The KTU "Big Table")

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the single most-asked comparison in KTU Module 1.** Memorize the structural differences.

| Parameter | CISC | RISC |
|---|---|---|
| **Instruction Set Size** | Large (100–250+) | Small (<100, often 30–50 core) |
| **Instruction Length** | Variable (1–15 bytes) | Fixed (typically 4 bytes / 32 bits) |
| **Addressing Modes** | Many (12–24+) | Few (3–5 typical: register, immediate, base+offset, PC-relative) |
| **Instruction Format** | Variable, complex decoding | Highly regular, often 3 fields (opcode, rs, rt/rd) |
| **Memory Access** | Any instruction can access memory | **Load/Store architecture** only |
| **CPI (typical)** | 2 – 15+ | ≈ 1 (single-cycle) |
| **Clock Frequency** | Lower (more work per cycle) | Higher (simpler logic → faster clock) |
| **Control Unit** | Microprogrammed (microcode ROM) | Hardwired (combinational/FSM) |
| **Pipelining** | Difficult, often shallow | Deep, efficient (5–14+ stages) |
| **Register File** | Small (8–16 typical in legacy x86) | Large (32–64 GPRs) |
| **Code Size** | Smaller (compact instructions) | Larger (more instructions needed) |
| **Compiler Complexity** | Simpler (hardware does the work) | Complex (compiler schedules, optimizes) |
| **Code Density** | High | Lower (but compressed extensions like ARM Thumb compensate) |
| **Power Consumption** | Higher (per-instruction) | Lower (simpler datapath) |
| **Examples** | Intel x86, Motorola 68k, VAX | ARM, MIPS, SPARC, RISC-V, PowerPC |
| **Best Suited For** | Legacy code, general desktops, low-code-density | Embedded, mobile, HPC, low-power |

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Pipeline Speedup and Stall Penalty

For a $k$-stage RISC pipeline, the **ideal** speedup over a non-pipelined implementation is $S_{ideal} = k$.

**However**, real pipelines suffer **hazards:**

1. **Structural hazards** — hardware resource conflict.
2. **Data hazards** — RAW (Read After Write), WAR, WAW dependencies.
3. **Control hazards** — branch instructions cause flushes.

The **effective speedup** including stalls is:

$$S_{eff} = \dfrac{k \cdot IC}{IC + \text{Stall Cycles}} = \dfrac{k \cdot IC}{IC(1 + s)} = \dfrac{k}{1+s}$$

where $s$ = average stall cycles per instruction.

For example, in a 5-stage pipeline with $s = 0.2$ stalls per instruction:

$$S_{eff} = \dfrac{5}{1 + 0.2} = \dfrac{5}{1.2} = 4.17$$

*Logic:* This shows that even with 20% stalls, the pipeline is ~83% as effective as the ideal — but still vastly superior to a non-pipelined design.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Trick Question Alert:** If asked "Why is CPI = 1 hard to achieve in practice?" — the answer involves the three hazard types above plus pipeline-fill and drain overhead.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: CISC Microprogrammed Control Unit

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the **architectural flow** of a CISC processor with a microcoded control unit, illustrating why CISC hardware is more complex.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Instruction Fetch Unit IFU] --> B[Variable Length Instruction Decoder]
    B --> C[Microcode ROM Lookup]
    C --> D[Microinstruction Sequencer]
    D --> E[ALU Datapath]
    D --> F[Memory Address Generator]
    E --> G[General Purpose Registers]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Main Memory Bus]
    D --> I[Status Flag Register]
    E --> I
    I --> D
    H --> A
    styleA fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px
    styleB fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px
    styleC fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px
    styleD fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px
```

**Flow Explanation:**
- The IFU fetches the next instruction.
- The **variable-length decoder** must parse complex encodings (1–15 bytes in x86).
- The **microcode ROM** holds the sequence of micro-operations for each complex macroinstruction.
- The sequencer emits µops to the ALU, address generator, and registers.
- Memory is accessible from *anywhere* in the datapath, not just a dedicated load/store unit.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: RISC Hardwired Pipelined Datapath

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[IF Stage Instruction Fetch] --> B[ID Stage Instruction Decode and Register Fetch]
    B --> C[EX Stage Execute ALU Operation]
    C --> D[MEM Stage Memory Access Load and Store Only]
    D --> E[WB Stage Write Back to Register File]
    B -.->|Forwarding| C
    C -.->|Forwarding| D
    A --- F[Instruction Cache ICache]
    D --- G[Data Cache DCache]
    E --- H[Large Register File 32 to 64 GPRs]
    styleA fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px
    styleB fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px
    styleC fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px
    styleD fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px
    styleE fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px
    styleF fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px
    styleG fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px
    styleH fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px
```

**Flow Explanation:**
- Five-stage classic RISC pipeline (similar to MIPS / DLX / RISC-V baseline).
- Each instruction advances one stage per cycle.
- **Forwarding paths** (dotted lines) reduce data-hazard stalls by routing EX/MEM results back to the EX stage.
- Memory is touched **only** in the MEM stage, and **only** for load/store instructions.

---

## 4.3 Comparative Block Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CISC_BLOCK [CISC Architecture]
        C1[Complex Variable Length ISA] --> C2[Microcode Decoder]
        C2 --> C3[Microprogrammed Control Unit]
        C3 --> C4[Complex Datapath with Memory Access in Any Stage]
    end
    subgraph RISC_BLOCK [RISC Architecture]
        R1[Simple Fixed Length ISA] --> R2[Hardwired Decoder]
        R2 --> R3[Pipeline Control Unit]
        R3 --> R4[Load Store Datapath with Isolated Memory Stage]
    end
    C4 -.->|Translates to internally| R2
    styleCISC_BLOCK fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px
    styleRISC_BLOCK fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight from Diagram 4.3:** Modern x86 chips (Intel Core, AMD Ryzen) implement a **CISC external ISA** but use a **RISC-like internal microarchitecture** with a hardware decoder that translates x86 instructions into RISC µops. The dotted arrow represents this CISC-to-RISC translation. This is a high-yield topic in KTU vivas.

---

## 4.4 Instruction Execution Timeline Comparison

```mermaid
gantt
    title CISC vs RISC Instruction Execution Timeline
    dateFormat X
    axisFormat %s
    section CISC Multi Cycle
    Instruction 1 (8 cycles)    :c1, 0, 8
    Instruction 2 (6 cycles)    :c2, 8, 14
    Instruction 3 (10 cycles)   :c3, 14, 24
    section RISC Pipelined
    I1 IF                        :r1a, 0, 1
    I1 ID                        :r1b, 1, 2
    I1 EX                        :r1c, 2, 3
    I1 MEM                       :r1d, 3, 4
    I1 WB                        :r1e, 4, 5
    I2 IF                        :r2a, 1, 2
    I2 ID                        :r2b, 2, 3
    I2 EX                        :r2c, 3, 4
    I2 MEM                       :r2d, 4, 5
    I2 WB                        :r2e, 5, 6
    I3 IF                        :r3a, 2, 3
    I3 ID                        :r3b, 3, 4
    I3 EX                        :r3c, 4, 5
    I3 MEM                       :r3d, 5, 6
    I3 WB                        :r3e, 6, 7
```

**Reading the Gantt Chart:**
- The CISC lanes show **sequential, variable-duration** instructions (8, 6, 10 cycles).
- The RISC lanes show **overlapped, uniform 1-cycle stages** — at cycle 3, three RISC instructions are simultaneously in IF, ID, and EX respectively.
- This visualizes the **instruction-level parallelism (ILP)** that pipelining unlocks.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
**"Differentiate between RISC and CISC architectures. List any four distinguishing features."**

**Model Answer (Structured for 3-mark valuation):**

| S.No. | CISC | RISC |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Large, complex instruction set (100–250+) | Small, reduced instruction set (<100) |
| 2 | Variable-length instructions (1–15 bytes) | Fixed-length instructions (typically 4 bytes) |
| 3 | Memory-to-memory operations allowed | Load/Store architecture (register-register) |
| 4 | Multi-cycle execution; high CPI | Single-cycle execution; CPI ≈ 1 |
| 5 | Microprogrammed control unit | Hardwired control unit |
| 6 | Difficult to pipeline | Highly pipelinable |

*(Any 4 points × 0.75 marks each = 3 marks. Examiner expects a clean table or bullet list.)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
**"What is a Load/Store architecture? Why is it a key feature of RISC processors?"**

**Model Answer:**

A **Load/Store architecture** is an ISA design in which *only* the dedicated `LOAD` and `STORE` instructions can access main memory. All arithmetic and logical operations (`ADD`, `SUB`, `AND`, `OR`, etc.) operate **exclusively on registers**; their operands must be loaded into registers first, the computation performed, and the result optionally stored back.

**Why it is key to RISC:**
1. **Decouples memory latency from computation:** Memory access is isolated to specific instructions, allowing the CPU to predict and pipeline uniformly.
2. **Simplifies the datapath:** The ALU does not need memory-access logic embedded within it.
3. **Enables single-cycle execution:** Since ALU ops touch only the register file, the critical path is short → high clock frequency.
4. **Facilitates register renaming and forwarding** in advanced pipelines.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for explaining significance in RISC.)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Dec 2023, Model]**

**(a)** Explain the architectural philosophy of **CISC** processors. Discuss the **advantages and disadvantages** of the CISC design approach. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A processor has a clock frequency of **200 MHz**. An application program consists of **$5 \times 10^7$ instructions**, with the following instruction mix:
- **40%** ALU instructions, each taking **3 cycles**
- **30%** Load/Store instructions, each taking **5 cycles**
- **20%** Branch instructions, each taking **2 cycles**
- **10%** Floating-point instructions, each taking **8 cycles**

Calculate the **(i) average CPI**, **(ii) CPU execution time**, and **(iii) MIPS rating** of the processor. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a) — CISC Philosophy, Advantages, Disadvantages (7 Marks)

**Architectural Philosophy of CISC (3.5 marks):**
- CISC emerged in the 1970s when **memory was expensive and slow**, and **compilers were primitive**. The designers wanted to:
  1. Reduce the **semantic gap** between high-level languages (FORTRAN, COBOL) and machine code.
  2. Reduce **program size** so that more code fits in scarce memory.
  3. Offload work from the (less capable) compiler to the hardware.
- CISC instructions are **microcoded** — each macroinstruction is decoded into a sequence of micro-operations stored in a control ROM.
- Memory operands are accessible from **any instruction**, including arithmetic and logical operations.
- The design is **microprogram-centric**, allowing late microcode patches and instruction-set extensions.

**Advantages of CISC (2 marks):**
1. **Compact code** → less memory footprint.
2. **Simpler compilers** — high-level constructs map more directly to machine instructions.
3. **Fewer instructions per program** → fewer instruction fetches.
4. **Backward compatibility** — instruction sets can be extended (e.g., x86 from 8086 to Core i9) without breaking old code.

**Disadvantages of CISC (1.5 marks):**
1. **Complex hardware** → larger die area, higher power, slower clock.
2. **Difficult to pipeline** — variable instruction lengths create fetch/decode bottlenecks.
3. **Microcode overhead** — additional ROM access per instruction adds latency.
4. **Lower instruction-level parallelism** — complex instructions cannot easily overlap.

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b) — Numerical on CPI, Time, MIPS (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**
$f_{clock} = 200 \text{ MHz} = 200 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}$

| Instruction Type | Frequency $p_i$ | Cycles $C_i$ | $p_i \times C_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| ALU | 0.40 | 3 | 1.20 |
| Load/Store | 0.30 | 5 | 1.50 |
| Branch | 0.20 | 2 | 0.40 |
| Floating-point | 0.10 | 8 | 0.80 |
| **Total** | **1.00** | — | **3.90** |

**Step (i): Average CPI**

$$CPI_{avg} = \sum p_i \cdot C_i = 1.20 + 1.50 + 0.40 + 0.80 = 3.90$$

**Step (ii): CPU Execution Time**

$$T_{CPU} = N \times CPI \times T_{clock} = N \times CPI \times \dfrac{1}{f_{clock}}$$

$$T_{CPU} = (5 \times 10^7) \times 3.90 \times \dfrac{1}{200 \times 10^6}$$

$$T_{CPU} = 5 \times 10^7 \times 3.90 \times 5 \times 10^{-9}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{CPU} &= 5 \times 3.90 \times 5 \times 10^{7-9} \\
&= 97.5 \times 10^{-2} \text{ s} \\
&= 0.975 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step (iii): MIPS Rating**

$$MIPS = \dfrac{f_{clock}}{CPI \times 10^6} = \dfrac{200 \times 10^6}{3.90 \times 10^6}$$

$$MIPS = \dfrac{200}{3.90} \approx 51.28 \text{ MIPS}$$

**Final Answers:**
- $CPI_{avg} = 3.90$
- $T_{CPU} = 0.975 \text{ s}$
- $MIPS \approx 51.28$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the **key characteristics of RISC architecture**. List and briefly describe **any four** of them. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A program contains **$2 \times 10^8$ instructions** to be executed on two different processors. On processor P1 (CISC): $CPI = 5$, $f = 200 \text{ MHz}$. On processor P2 (RISC): $CPI = 1.2$, $f = 600 \text{ MHz}$. Compute which processor is faster and by how much. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a) — RISC Characteristics (7 Marks)

**Definition (1 mark):** RISC is an ISA philosophy that uses a small, optimized set of instructions, each designed to execute in a single clock cycle, with most operations confined to registers.

**Four Key Characteristics (1.5 marks each):**

1. **Single-Cycle Execution:** Every instruction completes in exactly one clock cycle. This simplifies timing analysis and is the foundation for high clock frequencies.

2. **Fixed-Length Instructions:** All instructions occupy the same number of bits (typically 32 bits). This simplifies the instruction fetch and decode stages, allowing uniform pipeline stages.

3. **Load/Store Architecture:** Memory is accessed only through explicit `LOAD` and `STORE` instructions. Arithmetic operations have register-only operands. This isolates memory latency and enables clean pipelining.

4. **Large Register File:** RISC designs use 32 or more general-purpose registers. This minimizes memory traffic and supports fast procedure calls via register windows (SPARC) or efficient caller-saved/callee-saved conventions.

5. **Hardwired Control:** The control unit is implemented via combinational logic rather than microcode, reducing latency and enabling higher clock rates.

6. **Pipelining-Friendly:** Due to uniform instruction format and behavior, RISC processors are deeply pipelined, achieving high throughput.

*(Examiners typically expect 4 characteristics, each explained in 1–2 lines.)*

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b) — Performance Comparison (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $N = 2 \times 10^8$ instructions (same for both)
- P1 (CISC): $CPI_1 = 5$, $f_1 = 200 \text{ MHz} = 200 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}$
- P2 (RISC): $CPI_2 = 1.2$, $f_2 = 600 \text{ MHz} = 600 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}$

**Step 1 — CPU time for P1:**

$$T_1 = N \times CPI_1 \times \dfrac{1}{f_1} = (2 \times 10^8) \times 5 \times \dfrac{1}{200 \times 10^6}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
T_1 &= 2 \times 10^8 \times 5 \times 5 \times 10^{-9} \\
&= 50 \times 10^{-1} \\
&= 5.0 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — CPU time for P2:**

$$T_2 = N \times CPI_2 \times \dfrac{1}{f_2} = (2 \times 10^8) \times 1.2 \times \dfrac{1}{600 \times 10^6}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
T_2 &= 2 \times 10^8 \times 1.2 \times 1.6667 \times 10^{-9} \\
&= 2.4 \times 1.6667 \times 10^{-1} \\
&= 4.0 \times 10^{-1} \\
&= 0.4 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Speedup ratio:**

$$S = \dfrac{T_1}{T_2} = \dfrac{5.0}{0.4} = 12.5$$

**Conclusion:** **P2 (RISC) is 12.5× faster than P1 (CISC)** for this workload.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **Unit Errors:** Forgetting to convert MHz → Hz before multiplication. The $10^6$ factor must be carried through carefully.
> 2. **Inverse Confusion:** Using $f$ directly instead of $1/f$ in the time formula. Always write $T_{clock} = 1/f_{clock}$ explicitly.
> 3. **Mixing Formulas:** Using MIPS rating as a substitute for execution time. MIPS is **NOT** a reliable cross-ISA performance metric — KTU examiners often deduct marks if students claim "higher MIPS = faster" without justification.
> 4. **Incomplete RISC Characteristics:** Writing only *names* of features (e.g., "load/store architecture") without **explaining** why it matters. Each feature needs 1–2 lines of consequence.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Pin this checklist before your KTU exam.**

- **CISC = Complex Instruction Set Computer** — many complex, variable-length, multi-cycle instructions. Microcoded control. Memory operands in any instruction. **Examples: x86, 68k, VAX.**
- **RISC = Reduced Instruction Set Computer** — few, fixed-length, single-cycle, register-register instructions. Hardwired control. **Examples: ARM, MIPS, RISC-V, SPARC.**
- **Five Pillars of RISC:** Single-cycle, fixed-length, load/store, large register file, hardwired control.
- **Key Performance Formula:** $T_{CPU} = N \times CPI \times T_{clock}$ — memorize, derive units, and apply in numericals.
- **MIPS Rating Formula:** $MIPS = f_{clock} / (CPI \times 10^6)$ — useful but ISA-dependent; **do not** use it to compare CISC vs RISC directly without $N$ consideration.
- **CISC Pros:** Compact code, simpler compilers, backward compatibility.
- **CISC Cons:** Complex hardware, hard to pipeline, lower clock, higher power.
- **RISC Pros:** Fast clock, easy pipelining, low power, compiler-friendly.
- **RISC Cons:** Larger code size, complex compiler, more instructions per program.
- **Modern Reality:** x86 chips decode CISC instructions into RISC-like µops internally — the line is blurred, but **RISC principles dominate modern CPU microarchitecture.**
- **Pipeline Speedup (ideal):** $S = k$ for $k$ stages; real speedup = $k/(1+s)$ where $s$ = stall cycles/instruction.
- **Historical Architects to Know:** John Cocke (IBM 801 — father of RISC), Patterson & Ditzel (Berkeley RISC-I), Hennessy (Stanford MIPS).
- **KTU Exam Weightage:** This topic typically carries 7–14 marks in Part B of Module 1, often as a comparison question or a numerical on CPI/Time.
- **Drawing Tip:** In your answer script, always include a **small comparison table** for the 5–6 key differences — examiners reward visual structure.

---

> **End of KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 Notes for "CISC and RISC Architectures" — Module 1, PCMRT403.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Processor Architecture - General Internal Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMRT403 - COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE AND MICROCONTROLLER > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Processor Architecture - General Internal Architecture # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition The **General Internal Architecture** of a processor (Central Processing Unit, CPU) refers to the structured organization of its internal functional units and the data pathways that interconnect them. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *Computer Architecture and Microcontroller* (PCMRT403), the internal architecture of a processor comprises three fundamental sub-systems: 1. **The Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)** – performs all arithmetic and logical operations on data. 2. **The Control Unit (CU)** – orchestrates the fetching, decoding, and execution of instructions by generating precise timing and control signals. 3. **The Register Array (Register File)** – a set of high-speed, low-capacity storage elements located *inside* the processor used for temporary data storage, instruction handling, and addressing. These three blocks communicate over an **Internal Data Bus** (or *Internal Bus Architecture*), which is a set of parallel conductive lines carrying data, addresses, and control information between registers, the ALU, and external memory. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Concept):** > *The internal architecture of a microprocessor defines how the ALU, registers, control logic, and data bus are interconnected to form the data path through which instructions and operands flow during program execution.* ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The CPU as a Post Office To make this immediately intuitive, think of the processor as a **highly automated post office sorting facility**: - **The Registers** are like the *workbenches* of the postal clerks — small, fast, and right next to the sorting machines. Only a few letters (data) can be placed on a bench at a time, but the clerks can grab them instantly. - **The ALU** is the *sorting machine itself*. It opens each letter, reads the address, and routes it according to a specific rule (Add, Subtract, AND, OR, etc.). - **The Control Unit** is the *head supervisor* who reads a master instruction sheet (the program counter), shouts out which clerk should pick up which letter, and tells the sorting machine which operation to perform at each tick of the master clock. - **The Internal Bus** is the *conveyor belt* that moves letters between benches, the sorting machine, and the loading dock (external memory/I/O). - **The System Clock** is the *factory whistle* that synchronizes every action — each "ding" advances the work by exactly one step. When you write a program, you are essentially handing the supervisor (CU) a numbered list of tasks; the supervisor then orchestrates the clerks (registers) and the machine (ALU) to complete each task in sequence. ## 1.3 Key Constants and Standard Metrics The following standard metrics must be memorized for KTU examinations: | Metric | Symbol | Typical Unit | |---|---|---| | Word length (data bus width) | $n$ | **bits** | | Address bus width | $m$ | **bits** | | Clock frequency | $f_{clk}$ | **Hertz (Hz)** | | Clock period | $T_{clk}$ | **seconds** | | MIPS (Million Instructions Per Second) | — | **instructions / μs** | | CPI (Cycles Per Instruction) | — | **cycles / instruction** | > [!NOTE] > For an 8-bit processor such as the **Intel 8085** (a key KTU reference), $n = 8$, meaning the ALU and data bus manipulate 8 bits in parallel. For 16-bit processors like the **Intel 8086**, $n = 16$. Modern desktop CPUs extend this to 32 or 64 bits. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Internal Architecture ### A. The Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU) The ALU is a combinational logic network that accepts two $n$-bit operands (typically held in the **Accumulator** and a **Temporary register**) and produces an $n$-bit result along with a set of **Flag bits** (status outputs). **Core Functional Capabilities:** - **Arithmetic Operations:** Addition, Subtraction, Increment, Decrement. - **Logical Operations:** AND, OR, XOR, NOT, Shift Left, Shift Right. - **Comparison Operations:** Performed by subtracting two operands and checking flags without storing the result (used for conditional branching). > [!IMPORTANT] > The ALU is a **purely combinational** circuit — it has no memory. Every operation must complete within one clock cycle, after which the result is latched back into a register by an edge-triggered control pulse from the CU. ### B. The Register File (Internal Registers) The register file is a small bank of high-speed **flip-flop based** storage elements, organized into specialized functional groups. For an 8-bit processor like the **8085**, the internal register organization is: | Register Group | Register Name | Width | Purpose | |---|---|---|---| | General Purpose | B, C, D, E, H, L | 8-bit each | Scratchpad storage; can pair as BC, DE, HL (16-bit) | | Accumulator | A (ACC) | 8-bit | Primary operand and result register for ALU | | Program Status Word | Flags (S, Z, AC, P, CY) | 8-bit | Stores result-status from last ALU op | | Special Purpose | PC (Program Counter) | 16-bit | Holds address of *next* instruction | | Special Purpose | SP (Stack Pointer) | 16-bit | Points to *top* of the stack in RAM | | Special Purpose | IR (Instruction Register) | 8-bit | Holds the currently decoded opcode | | Special Purpose | TR (Temporary Register) | 8-bit | Hidden operand register for ALU | | Special Purpose | IDR (Instruction Decoder Reg.) | 8-bit | Auxiliary decoder latch | ### C. The Control Unit (CU) The Control Unit is a **sequential logic** circuit (typically a hardwired finite state machine or a micro-programmed ROM). Its three sub-functions are: 1. **Fetch:** Issue a `READ` on the address bus with the value of `PC`, increment `PC`. 2. **Decode:** Latch the opcode into the `IR`, then map it to one of $2^k$ unique micro-operations (where $k$ is the opcode width). 3. **Execute:** Generate the precise sequence of control signals that activates the correct ALU function, selects the correct source/destination register, and enables the right bus driver. ### D. The Internal Data Bus The internal bus is a **tri-state multiplexed** set of wires. Each register has an output enable controlled by the CU; only the currently selected register drives the bus at any given moment, while all others present a high-impedance state (`Z`). ## 2.2 Von Neumann vs Harvard Architecture (Conceptual Comparison) | Property | Von Neumann | Harvard | |---|---|---| | Memory for instructions & data | Single shared memory | Separate memories | | Bus structure | One common bus | Two independent buses | | Bottleneck | **Von Neumann bottleneck** | Eliminated | | Typical example | 8085, general-purpose CPUs | Microcontrollers (8051, AVR) | ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet The following equations govern the internal architecture and addressability limits. **Formula 1 — Addressable Memory Capacity:** The maximum directly addressable memory is determined solely by the address bus width $m$: $$M_{max} = 2^m \text{ words (or bytes)}$$ **Formula 2 — Word Storage Size:** For a word length of $n$ bits, each addressable unit stores $n$ bits: $$\text{Memory Size in bits} = 2^m \times n$$ **Formula 3 — Execution Time:** $$T_{exec} = N \times CPI \times T_{clk} = \frac{N \times CPI}{f_{clk}}$$ where $N$ is the number of instructions executed. **Formula 4 — Throughput (MIPS):** $$\text{MIPS} = \frac{f_{clk}}{CPI \times 10^6}$$ **Formula 5 — CPU Performance (Amdahl-influenced simple form):** $$T_{CPU} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (CPI_i \times IC_i) \times T_{clk}$$ **Formula 6 — Average CPI for a mixed program:** $$CPI_{avg} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (CPI_i \times IC_i)}{IC_{total}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** > - Memory capacity formulas are used daily by SoC architects deciding address bus widths for embedded controllers. > - The CPI / MIPS formulas are the bread-and-butter of **performance benchmarking** in compiler design and processor selection for real-time systems (automotive ECUs, IoT nodes). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Maximum Addressable Memory **Given:** A processor has an address bus of width $m$ bits. **To Derive:** The maximum directly addressable memory locations. **Step-by-Step Logical Deduction:** Step 1 — Each line of the address bus can independently carry one of two logic levels: HIGH (`1`) or LOW (`0`). Step 2 — Therefore, the number of unique binary patterns that can be formed on $m$ parallel lines is given by the fundamental counting principle. Step 3 — For $m = 1$, there are $2^1 = 2$ patterns: `0`, `1`. Step 4 — For $m = 2$, there are $2^2 = 4$ patterns: `00`, `01`, `10`, `11`. Step 5 — Inductively, for an $m$-line bus, the number of unique addresses is: $$M_{max} = \underbrace{2 \times 2 \times \dots \times 2}_{m \text{ times}} = 2^m$$ Step 6 — Concluding statement: An $m$-bit address bus can uniquely point to exactly $2^m$ different memory locations. $$\boxed{M_{max} = 2^m \text{ locations}}$$ ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Total Memory in Bits Given Byte-Organized Memory **Given:** Address bus width $= m$, each location stores one byte ($n = 8$ bits). **Step 1 —** From Derivation 1, total locations $= 2^m$. Step 2 —** Each location holds $n$ bits of information. Step 3 —** Total information capacity is multiplicative: $$\text{Total bits} = 2^m \times n$$ Step 4 —** Convert to bytes (divide by 8): $$\text{Total bytes} = \frac{2^m \times n}{8}$$ Step 5 —** Convert to KB ($1 \text{ KB} = 1024 = 2^{10}$ bytes): $$\text{Total KB} = \frac{2^m \times n}{8 \times 2^{10}} = \frac{2^m \times n}{2^{13}} = 2^{m-13} \text{ KB}$$ ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — MIPS Performance Expression **Given:** Clock frequency $f_{clk}$ in Hz and average CPI. **Step 1 —** Clock period is the reciprocal of frequency: $$T_{clk} = \frac{1}{f_{clk}} \text{ seconds}$$ Step 2 —** Number of clock ticks per second is exactly $f_{clk}$. Step 3 —** Since each instruction consumes $CPI_{avg}$ clock cycles, the number of completed instructions per second is: $$\text{IPS} = \frac{f_{clk}}{CPI_{avg}}$$ Step 4 —** MIPS is the same quantity scaled by $10^{-6}$: $$\text{MIPS} = \frac{f_{clk}}{CPI_{avg} \times 10^{6}}$$ ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — 8085 Architecture **Problem:** An 8085-like processor has a 16-bit address bus and an 8-bit data bus. Determine (a) maximum addressable memory in bytes, and (b) the total information in KB. **Solution:** Step (a) — Apply Formula 1 with $m = 16$: $$M_{max} = 2^{16} = 65{,}536 \text{ locations} = 64 \text{ KB}$$ Step (b) — Each location stores 8 bits: $$\text{Total bits} = 65{,}536 \times 8 = 524{,}288 \text{ bits}$$ Step (c) — Convert to KB using the identity $2^{10} = 1024$: $$64 \text{ KB}$$ This precisely matches the 8085's documented 64 KB address space. ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — MIPS Calculation **Problem:** A processor operates at $f_{clk} = 2 \text{ MHz}$ with an average $CPI = 4$.
