```mermaid
    HOLD["HOLD"]
    HLDA["HLDA"]
    MEM["Memory Banks"]
    IO["I O Devices"]
    DMA["DMA Controller"]
    LATCH["8282 Address Latch"]
    XCEIVER["8286 Data Transceiver"]

    CPU --> ADDRBUS
    CPU --> DATABUS
    CPU --> CTRLBUS
    CTRLBUS --> ALE
    CTRLBUS --> RD
    CTRLBUS --> WR
    CTRLBUS --> MIO
    CTRLBUS --> BHE
    CTRLBUS --> DTR
    CTRLBUS --> DEN
    CTRLBUS --> READY
    CTRLBUS --> INTR
    CTRLBUS --> NMI
    CTRLBUS --> HOLD
    CTRLBUS --> HLDA
    ALE --> LATCH
    DTR --> XCEIVER
    DEN --> XCEIVER
    LATCH --> MEM
    XCEIVER --> MEM
    XCEIVER --> IO
    HOLD --> DMA
    HLDA --> DMA
    DMA --> MEM
    DMA --> IO
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Functional Flow of a Typical 8086 Memory Read Bus Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0["Start of Bus Cycle"]
    T1["T1 State Place Address on Bus Assert ALE HIGH"]
    T2A["T2 State ALE goes LOW Latch Address into 8282"]
    T2B["T2 State Assert RD bar LOW Set M / IO bar HIGH Assert DT / R LOW Assert DEN LOW"]
    T3A["T3 State Memory places data on data bus"]
    T3B["T3 State Sample READY pin"]
    T3C{"READY is HIGH"}
    WS["Insert Wait State TW"]
    T4["T4 State De assert RD bar De assert DEN CPU reads data from data bus"]
    END["End of Bus Cycle"]

    S0 --> T1
    T1 --> T2A
    T2A --> T2B
    T2B --> T3A
    T3A --> T3B
    T3B --> T3C
    T3C -->|YES| T4
    T3C -->|NO| WS
    WS --> T3B
    T4 --> END
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Control Signal Decoding Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    OP["Operation Type"]
    MEMREAD["Memory Read"]
    MEMWRITE["Memory Write"]
    IOREAD["I O Read"]
    IOWRITE["I O Write"]
    DEC["M / IO bar Decoder"]

    OP --> MEMREAD
    OP --> MEMWRITE
    OP --> IOREAD
    OP --> IOWRITE
    MEMREAD --> DEC
    MEMWRITE --> DEC
    IOREAD --> DEC
    IOWRITE --> DEC
    DEC --> S1["M / IO bar = 1 for memory"]
    DEC --> S2["M / IO bar = 0 for IO"]
    DEC --> S3["RD bar = 0 for read ops"]
    DEC --> S4["WR bar = 0 for write ops"]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Bus Arbitration During DMA

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    NORMAL["Normal CPU Operation CPU controls bus"]
    DMA1["DMA Controller asserts HOLD = 1"]
    SAMP["CPU samples HOLD at end of current bus cycle"]
    ACK["CPU asserts HLDA = 1 Tri states address data and control bus"]
    DMAOP["DMA Controller performs data transfer directly with memory"]
    DMAEND["DMA Controller de asserts HOLD = 0"]
    RESUME["CPU de asserts HLDA Resumes normal operation"]

    NORMAL --> DMA1
    DMA1 --> SAMP
    SAMP --> ACK
    ACK --> DMAOP
    DMAOP --> DMAEND
    DMAEND --> RESUME
    RESUME --> NORMAL
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram: Interfacing 8086 with Memory via Control Bus

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CPU["8086 Microprocessor"]
    LATCH["8282 Address Latch Controlled by ALE"]
    XCEIV["8286 Transceiver Controlled by DT / R bar and DEN bar"]
    RAMBANK["Even Bank RAM D0 to D7 CS bar OE bar WE bar"]
    RAMBANK2["Odd Bank RAM D8 to D15 CS bar OE bar WE bar"]

    CPU -- "AD0 to AD15 Multiplexed Address Data" --> LATCH
    CPU -- "A16 to A19 High Address" --> RAMBANK
    CPU -- "A16 to A19 High Address" --> RAMBANK2
    LATCH -- "Latched A0 to A15" --> RAMBANK
    LATCH -- "Latched A0 to A15" --> RAMBANK2
    CPU -- "D0 to D15 Data Bus" --> XCEIV
    XCEIV -- "Buffered D0 to D7" --> RAMBANK
    XCEIV -- "Buffered D8 to D15" --> RAMBANK2
    CPU -- "RD bar and WR bar" --> RAMBANK
    CPU -- "RD bar and WR bar" --> RAMBANK2
    CPU -- "BHE bar" --> RAMBANK2
    CPU -- "A0" --> RAMBANK
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024]`

**Q: Define the Control Bus. List any four control signals of the 8086 microprocessor and state their function.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Control Bus** is a set of signal lines that carry command and timing information to coordinate the operations of the CPU, memory, and I/O devices during a bus cycle. It determines **when** a read or write operation should occur and to **which device** (memory or I/O).

Four control signals of the 8086:

1. **$\overline{RD}$ (Read, active LOW):** When asserted (LOW), the CPU reads data from the addressed memory or I/O location.
2. **$\overline{WR}$ (Write, active LOW):** When asserted (LOW), the CPU writes data to the addressed memory or I/O location.
3. **$M/\overline{IO}$ (Memory/IO Select):** HIGH indicates a memory operation; LOW indicates an I/O operation.
4. **ALE (Address Latch Enable):** A HIGH pulse at T1 indicates that a valid address is on the multiplexed address/data bus and must be latched externally.

*(Examiner's Note: Award 1 mark for the definition and 0.5 marks each for signal name + function × 4 = 2 marks. Total = 3 marks.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Q: Differentiate between the Address Bus, Data Bus, and Control Bus.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Address Bus | Data Bus | Control Bus |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Carries the memory/I/O *location* | Carries the actual *data* payload | Carries *commands* and *timing* signals |
| **Direction** | Unidirectional (CPU → external) | Bidirectional (CPU ↔ external) | Mostly unidirectional; some bidirectional |
| **Width (8086)** | 20 bits ($A_0$–$A_{19}$) | 16 bits ($D_0$–$D_{15}$) | Multiple discrete signal lines |
| **Example Signal** | $A_0$ | $D_0$ | $\overline{RD}$, $\overline{WR}$, $M/\overline{IO}$ |
| **Role** | Identifies *where* to access | Identifies *what* to transfer | Identifies *when* and *how* to transfer |

*(Examiner's Note: A clean comparison table earns full marks. Vague answers lose 0.5–1 mark.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the function of the control bus in the 8086 microprocessor with a neat block diagram. List and explain the various control signals associated with memory and I/O read/write operations. **(7 Marks)**

**Q: (b)** With a timing diagram, explain the 8086 bus cycle for a memory read operation. Clearly indicate the role of each control signal. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**1. Introduction to the Control Bus (1 Mark):**
The control bus of the 8086 is a collection of dedicated signal lines that coordinate the timing and direction of data transfer between the CPU, memory, and I/O devices. It is the third component of the **system bus** alongside the address bus and data bus.

**2. Block Diagram Representation (2 Marks):**
[Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.5]

The 8086 microprocessor is connected to:
- **Memory banks** (Even and Odd) via the data bus ($D_0$–$D_{15}$), address bus ($A_0$–$A_{19}$), and control bus ($\overline{RD}$, $\overline{WR}$, $M/\overline{IO}$, $\overline{BHE}$).
- **External latch (8282)** controlled by ALE for demultiplexing address/data.
- **External transceiver (8286)** controlled by $DT/\overline{R}$ and $\overline{DEN}$.

**3. Control Signals for Memory/IO Read/Write (4 Marks):**

| Signal | Type | Function |
|---|---|---|
| $\overline{RD}$ | Output, active LOW | Activated by CPU to **read** data from memory/IO |
| $\overline{WR}$ | Output, active LOW | Activated by CPU to **write** data to memory/IO |
| $M/\overline{IO}$ | Output | Selects **memory** (HIGH) or **I/O** (LOW) |
| $\overline{BHE}$ | Output, active LOW | Enables **upper 8-bit** data bus ($D_8$–$D_{15}$) |
| ALE | Output | **Latches** the address from multiplexed bus at T1 |
| $DT/\overline{R}$ | Output | Direction control: HIGH = Transmit, LOW = Receive |
| $\overline{DEN}$ | Output, active LOW | **Enables** external data transceiver |

**4. Truth Table for Decoding (within the 4 marks above):**

| $M/\overline{IO}$ | $\overline{RD}$ | $\overline{WR}$ | Operation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 1 | I/O Read |
| 0 | 1 | 0 | I/O Write |
| 1 | 0 | 1 | Memory Read |
| 1 | 1 | 0 | Memory Write |

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**1. Memory Read Bus Cycle Overview (1 Mark):**
A memory read operation in the 8086 takes **one bus cycle = 4 clock states (T1–T4)**. The control bus orchestrates each state.

**2. Timing Diagram Description (4 Marks):**

| State | Address Bus | Data Bus | $\overline{RD}$ | $M/\overline{IO}$ | ALE | $DT/\overline{R}$ | $\overline{DEN}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **T1** | Address $A_0$–$A_{19}$ valid | — | HIGH (1) | HIGH (1) | **HIGH pulse** | — | HIGH |
| **T2** | High-Z | High-Z | **LOW (0)** | HIGH (1) | LOW | LOW | **LOW** |
| **T3** | High-Z | Memory places data | LOW (0) | HIGH (1) | LOW | LOW | LOW |
| **T4** | High-Z | CPU latches data | **HIGH (1)** | HIGH (1) | LOW | — | HIGH |

**3. State-by-State Explanation (2 Marks):**
- **T1:** CPU outputs the 20-bit address on the multiplexed AD bus; ALE goes HIGH so the 8282 latch captures the address.
- **T2:** $\overline{RD}$ is asserted LOW; $M/\overline{IO}$ is HIGH; $DT/\overline{R}$ = LOW (receive); $\overline{DEN}$ = LOW (transceiver enabled).
- **T3:** Memory places data on the bus; CPU samples READY to check if a wait state is needed.
- **T4:** $\overline{RD}$ goes HIGH; CPU reads the data; bus cycle ends.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the role of the signals $\overline{BHE}$, $A_0$, $M/\overline{IO}$, $\overline{RD}$, and $\overline{WR}$ in the 8086 microprocessor. Construct the truth table showing how $\overline{BHE}$ and $A_0$ are used to select even and odd memory banks. **(7 Marks)**

**Q: (b)** Explain the HOLD and HLDA signals of the 8086. Describe the sequence of events when a DMA controller requests the bus, with a suitable diagram. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**1. Signal Roles (3 Marks):**

- **$\overline{BHE}$ (Bus High Enable, active LOW):** Enables the upper data bus $D_8$–$D_{15}$ (odd bank).
- **$A_0$ (Address bit 0):** Enables the lower data bus $D_0$–$D_7$ (even bank) when $A_0 = 0$.
- **$M/\overline{IO}$:** HIGH = Memory access; LOW = I/O access.
- **$\overline{RD}$ (active LOW):** CPU reads from selected device.
- **$\overline{WR}$ (active LOW):** CPU writes to selected device.

**2. Truth Table for Bank Selection (4 Marks):**

| $\overline{BHE}$ | $A_0$ | Type of Access | Data Bus Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | **Word (16-bit)** — Whole word | $D_0$–$D_{15}$ |
| 0 | 1 | **High byte (odd bank)** | $D_8$–$D_{15}$ |
| 1 | 0 | **Low byte (even bank)** | $D_0$–$D_7$ |
| 1 | 1 | **Idle** — No access | — |

**3. Engineering Note:**
The 8086 uses a **little-endian** convention: the low byte of a word is stored at the lower address, and the high byte at the higher address.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**1. HOLD and HLDA — Purpose (2 Marks):**
The **HOLD** input and **HLDA** (Hold Acknowledge) output signals allow external devices (typically a DMA controller) to request control of the system bus. This enables data transfer between I/O and memory **without CPU intervention**, freeing the CPU for other tasks.

**2. Sequence of Events (4 Marks):**

1. **Normal Operation:** CPU is executing instructions and controls the bus.
2. **DMA Request:** DMA controller asserts **HOLD = HIGH**.
3. **CPU Sampling:** CPU completes the current bus cycle, then samples HOLD.
4. **Acknowledge:** CPU asserts **HLDA = HIGH** and tri-states its address, data, and control bus lines.
5. **DMA Transfer:** DMA controller takes control and transfers data directly between I/O and memory.
6. **DMA Completion:** DMA controller de-asserts **HOLD = LOW**.
7. **CPU Resumes:** CPU de-asserts **HLDA = LOW** and resumes normal bus control.

**3. Diagram (1 Mark):**
[Refer to the Mermaid "Bus Arbitration During DMA" diagram in Section 4.4]

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often confuse **HOLD/HLDA** with **$\overline{RQ}/\overline{GT}$** (Request/Grant). The HOLD/HLDA scheme is used with a separate DMA controller (like 8237), while $\overline{RQ}/\overline{GT}$ is used for **multi-processor** configurations. Mixing them up costs 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mistake Zones

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Mistakes Students Make on Control Bus Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the overline (bar):** Writing $\overline{RD} = 1$ when read is active is a **fatal error** — it should be **0** (active LOW).
> 2. **Mixing up $\overline{BHE}$ and $A_0$:** $\overline{BHE} = 0$ selects the **odd (high) byte bank**, not the even bank. Many students invert this.
> 3. **Ignoring tri-state behavior:** During HOLD, the control bus lines are **high-impedance (Z)**, not HIGH (1). Writing "1" instead of "Z" loses a mark.
> 4. **Calling $\overline{INTA}$ a "memory read" pin:** $\overline{INTA}$ is **not** a generic read — it is specifically for **interrupt acknowledgement** and must be explained as such.
> 5. **Confusing ALE with a clock signal:** ALE is **not periodic** like CLK; it is a **per-bus-cycle pulse** generated at T1.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Rapid-Revision Checklist — Control Bus (KTU 2024):**
> 
> - **Definition:** Control Bus = signal lines carrying **commands and timing** between CPU, memory, and I/O.
> - **8086 Key Control Pins:** $\overline{RD}$, $\overline{WR}$, $M/\overline{IO}$, $\overline{BHE}$, ALE, $DT/\overline{R}$, $\overline{DEN}$, READY, INTR, NMI, $\overline{INTA}$, HOLD, HLDA, RESET, CLK.
> - **Read Logic:** $\overline{RD} = 0$ (active LOW); **Write Logic:** $\overline{WR} = 0$ (active LOW).
> - **Memory vs I/O:** $M/\overline{IO} = 1$ → Memory; $= 0$ → I/O.
> - **Word Access:** $\overline{BHE} = 0$ AND $A_0 = 0$ (both banks enabled).
> - **Byte Access (Even):** $\overline{BHE} = 1$, $A_0 = 0$ (only low bank).
> - **Byte Access (Odd):** $\overline{BHE} = 0$, $A_0 = 1$ (only high bank).
> - **ALE:** HIGH at T1 → external latch (8282) captures the address.
> - **$DT/\overline{R}$:** HIGH = CPU writing; LOW = CPU reading.
> - **$\overline{DEN}$:** Active-LOW signal to enable external data transceiver (8286).
> - **HOLD/HLDA:** Used for **DMA** — CPU tri-states bus lines (HIGH-Z, not HIGH) when HLDA is asserted.
> - **Bus Cycle:** 4 clock states (T1, T2, T3, T4); wait states (TW) inserted if READY = LOW.
> - **Reset Vector:** CS = FFFFH, IP = 0000H → first instruction at physical address **FFFF0H**.
> - **Active-LOW Notation:** Always check for the bar (`$‾$`) — it inverts the logic level for "active" meaning.
> - **Tri-state:** Control bus lines go to **High-Z** during HOLD, not to logic HIGH.
> - **Difference from Address/Data Bus:** Control bus carries **no addresses, no data** — only commands and timing.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Register Set - Status Register
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT403 - COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE AND MICROCONTROLLER
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Register Set - Status Register

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🏛️ 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Register Set — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCMRT403 / Module 1)**
> The **Register Set** of a microcontroller is the small, high-speed collection of internal storage elements built directly into the CPU (Central Processing Unit). These registers hold the **operands, results, addresses, and control flags** that the Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU) and control unit manipulate during instruction execution.

In the context of the **AVR / ATmega architecture** (the reference microcontroller prescribed in the KTU PCMRT403 syllabus), the Register Set is typically partitioned into three functional groups:

| Functional Group | Registers | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **General Purpose Registers** | R0 – R31 (32 × 8-bit) | Temporary operand and result storage for the ALU |
| **I/O / Special Function Registers** | SREG, SP, PC, etc. | Control, status, and addressing functions |
| **Program Counter & Stack Pointer** | PC (16/32-bit), SPH:SPL (16-bit) | Program flow and stack management |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Registers Matter:** Registers are the *fastest* memory in a computer hierarchy. A register access completes in **1 clock cycle**, while SRAM takes **2 cycles** and Flash takes **3+ cycles**. For this reason, every ALU operation in an AVR instruction is *register-to-register*.

---

## 1.2 The Status Register (SREG) — Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **SREG Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme)**
> The **Status Register (SREG)** is an **8-bit special function register** located at I/O address `0x5F` (memory-mapped `0x005F`) in the ATmega architecture. It contains **hardware flags** that automatically reflect the outcome of the most recent arithmetic, logic, or bit-manipulation operation.

### 🧠 Real-World Analogy: "The Blackboard After Every Calculation"

Imagine a **classroom blackboard** kept by the ALU. Every time the teacher (ALU) finishes writing a sum on the board:

- Did the sum **overflow** the board? → Blackboard note: "**V** = 1"
- Was the answer **zero**? → Blackboard note: "**Z** = 1"
- Did the answer need a **borrow/carry**? → Blackboard note: "**C** = 1"
- Was the answer **negative** (in two's-complement form)? → Blackboard note: "**N** = 1"

That blackboard is the **Status Register**. It does not store the result itself — the result is placed in a general-purpose register (R0–R31). SREG only records the *conditions* the result produced. Conditional branch instructions (`BREQ`, `BRNE`, `BRLO`, `BRSH`, `BRMI`, `BRPL`) then "read" these notes to decide what to do next.

### 📊 SREG Bit Map (At a Glance)

The 8 bits of the AVR Status Register, from **MSB (Bit 7)** to **LSB (Bit 0)**, are:

| Bit | Name | Full Form | Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| 7 | **I** | Global Interrupt Enable | Control |
| 6 | **T** | Bit Copy Storage | Control |
| 5 | **H** | Half Carry Flag | Status |
| 4 | **S** | Sign Bit (N ⊕ V) | Status |
| 3 | **V** | Two's Complement Overflow | Status |
| 2 | **N** | Negative Flag | Status |
| 1 | **Z** | Zero Flag | Status |
| 0 | **C** | Carry Flag | Status |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always remember — *I* and *T* are **control bits** (programmer-settable), while *H, S, V, N, Z, C* are **hardware status flags** (set/cleared by the ALU automatically).

---

## 1.3 The General Purpose Register File (R0 – R31)

> [!NOTE]
> The **Register File** in AVR consists of **32 × 8-bit working registers** directly connected to the ALU via a fast dual-ported access path. Registers R26–R31 serve a **dual purpose**: they can pair up to form three 16-bit **X, Y, Z pointer registers** used for indirect addressing of data memory (SRAM).

| Pair | 16-bit Pointer | Common Use |
|---|---|---|
| R27:R26 | **X-register** | Base pointer for `LD`/`ST` |
| R29:R28 | **Y-register** | Base pointer + post-increment/decrement |
| R31:R30 | **Z-register** | Program memory addressing (`LPM`), table lookups |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Role of Each SREG Bit

### 🔹 Bit 7 — I (Global Interrupt Enable)

- **Function:** Master switch for the AVR interrupt system.
- **Set by:** `SEI` instruction or hardware when entering an interrupt handler.
- **Cleared by:** `CLI` instruction, the interrupt itself (hardware auto-clears on entry), or a reset.
- **KTU Note:** When `I = 0`, **all maskable interrupts are globally disabled**. The `RET` (`RETI`) instruction restores I from the stack.

### 🔹 Bit 6 — T (Bit Copy Storage)

- **Function:** Temporary 1-bit storage used by the **Bit Load (`BLD`) and Bit Store (`BST`)** instructions.
- **Workflow:**
  1. `BST Rr, b` → copies bit *b* of register Rr into T.
  2. `BLD Rd, b` → copies T into bit *b* of register Rd.
- **Use case:** Atomic bit manipulation across registers without disturbing the rest of the byte.

### 🔹 Bit 5 — H (Half Carry Flag)

- **Definition:** Carry/borrow generated between **bit 3 and bit 4** of the result.
- **Triggered by:** `ADD`, `SUB`-family instructions.
- **Purpose:** Enables **Binary Coded Decimal (BCD) arithmetic correction** via the `DAA` instruction (although AVR lacks `DAA` natively, the flag is documented for compatibility).
- **Mathematical condition:**

$$
H = 1 \iff \text{Carry from bit } 3 \rightarrow \text{bit } 4
$$

### 🔹 Bit 4 — S (Sign Flag)

- **Definition:** The exclusive-OR of the Negative and Overflow flags.
- **Purpose:** Used for **signed branch comparisons** (e.g., `BRSH` / `BRLO` work with *C*, but `BRGE` / `BRLT` work with *S* and *N*).

$$
S = N \oplus V
$$

- **Interpretation:** $S = 1$ indicates the true signed result is **negative** (i.e., the operation produced a meaningfully negative outcome in two's-complement interpretation).

### 🔹 Bit 3 — V (Two's Complement Overflow)

- **Definition:** Set when the result of a **signed operation** does not fit in the destination's bit-width.
- **Detection logic for 8-bit signed ADD:**

$$
V = (R_d^7 \land R_r^7 \land \overline{R^{7}}) \lor (\overline{R_d^7} \land \overline{R_r^7} \land R^{7})
$$

- **Where:** $R_d^7$ = bit 7 of destination before, $R_r^7$ = bit 7 of source, $R^7$ = bit 7 of result.

### 🔹 Bit 2 — N (Negative Flag)

- **Definition:** A direct copy of the **MSB (Most Significant Bit)** of the result.
- **Simple rule:** $N = R^7$ (bit 7 of the result).
- **Useful for:** Branch on negative (`BRMI`) and branch on positive (`BRPL`).

### 🔹 Bit 1 — Z (Zero Flag)

- **Definition:** Set when the result of an operation equals **zero**.

$$
Z = 1 \iff \text{Result} = 0
$$

- **Also affected by:** Logical operations, compare (`CP`), increment, decrement.

### 🔹 Bit 0 — C (Carry Flag)

- **Definition:** Set when there is a **carry-out from bit 7** in addition, or a **borrow into bit 7** in subtraction.
- **Addiction:**

$$
C = 1 \iff \text{Carry out of } R^7 = 1
$$

- **Subtraction ($A - B$):** $C = 1$ means a **borrow occurred**, i.e., $A < B$ in **unsigned** terms.
- **Shift/rotate:** $C$ receives the bit that is *shifted out* of the operand.

---

## 2.2 📋 KTU Formula Sheet & Status-Flag Decision Table

### Mathematical & Logical Equations (Critical for KTU Numerical Problems)

| Flag | Equation / Condition | Triggered By | Cleared By |
|---|---|---|---|
| **H** | Carry from bit 3 → bit 4 | ADD, SUB, ADIW, SBIW | Result has no half-carry |
| **S** | $S = N \oplus V$ | Any flag-modifying op | Auto-updated |
| **V** | Signed overflow (see formula above) | ADD, SUB, NEG, INC, DEC | No overflow detected |
| **N** | $N = R^7$ | Any flag-modifying op | Result MSB = 0 |
| **Z** | $Z = \overline{R^7} \cdot \overline{R^6} \cdot \ldots \cdot \overline{R^0}$ | Any op where Result = 0 | Result ≠ 0 |
| **C** (ADD) | Carry out of bit 7 | ADD, ROL, LSL, ADC | No carry generated |
| **C** (SUB) | $\overline{\text{Borrow out}}$ | SUB, CP, DEC, LSR | No borrow required |
| **I, T** | Programmer-controlled | `SEI`, `BST` | `CLI`, auto-reset |

### KTU High-Yield Quick Reference

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Viva:** **"I Take Half Sandwiches, Very Nice Zesty Cuisine"**
> → I, T, H, S, V, N, Z, C (Bit 7 down to Bit 0)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** The Status Register is the heart of **decision-making in embedded systems**. Every line of `if`, `while`, and `for` in compiled C code ultimately reduces to a comparison instruction (`CP`/`CPC`) followed by a conditional branch (`BRxx`) that examines SREG flags.

# 🧮 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Carry Flag for 8-bit Unsigned Addition

**Problem Setup:** Add two 8-bit unsigned numbers $A$ and $B$, where $A = 0xFF$ and $B = 0x01$.

We treat each number as an 8-bit binary value:

$$
A = 1111\,1111_2 = 0xFF
$$
$$
B = 0000\,0001_2 = 0x01
$$

**Step 1 — Perform binary addition bit-by-bit (LSB first):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bit 0:} & \quad 1 + 1 = 10_2 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Sum}=0,\; \text{Carry out}=1 \\
\text{Bit 1:} & \quad 1 + 0 + 1_{\text{carry}} = 10_2 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Sum}=0,\; \text{Carry out}=1 \\
\text{Bit 2:} & \quad 1 + 0 + 1_{\text{carry}} = 10_2 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Sum}=0,\; \text{Carry out}=1 \\
\text{Bit 3:} & \quad 1 + 0 + 1_{\text{carry}} = 10_2 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Sum}=0,\; \text{Carry out}=1 \\
\text{Bit 4:} & \quad 1 + 0 + 1_{\text{carry}} = 10_2 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Sum}=0,\; \text{Carry out}=1 \\
\text{Bit 5:} & \quad 1 + 0 + 1_{\text{carry}} = 10_2 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Sum}=0,\; \text{Carry out}=1 \\
\text{Bit 6:} & \quad 1 + 0 + 1_{\text{carry}} = 10_2 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Sum}=0,\; \text{Carry out}=1 \\
\text{Bit 7:} & \quad 1 + 0 + 1_{\text{carry}} = 10_2 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Sum}=0,\; \text{Carry out}=1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Assemble the result and the final carry:**

$$
\text{Result} = 0000\,0000_2 = 0x00
$$
$$
\text{Carry out} = 1
$$

**Step 3 — Update the Status Register:**

$$
C = 1 \quad (\text{Carry out occurred})
$$
$$
Z = 1 \quad (\text{Result} = 0)
$$
$$
N = 0 \quad (\text{Result MSB} = 0)
$$
$$
H = 1 \quad (\text{Half-carry generated at bit 3})
$$
$$
V = 0 \quad (\text{No signed overflow: } -1 + 1 = 0 \text{ is valid})
$$
$$
S = N \oplus V = 0 \oplus 0 = 0
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is precisely why a microcontroller's ALU must report the carry. Without `C`, the firmware could not detect **unsigned wrap-around** in counters, timers, or memory offsets.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Overflow Flag (V) for Signed Addition

**Problem Setup:** Add two 8-bit **signed** numbers: $A = +100$ and $B = +80$ (both fit in 7 bits but the sum exceeds +127).

In 8-bit two's complement, the signed range is $[-128, +127]$. Convert to binary:

$$
A = +100 = 0110\,0100_2
$$
$$
B = +80 = 0101\,0000_2
$$

**Step 1 — Bit-by-bit addition with carry tracking:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bit 0:} & \quad 0 + 0 = 0,\; C_{\text{out}}=0 \\
\text{Bit 1:} & \quad 0 + 0 + 0 = 0,\; C_{\text{out}}=0 \\
\text{Bit 2:} & \quad 1 + 0 + 0 = 1,\; C_{\text{out}}=0 \\
\text{Bit 3:} & \quad 0 + 0 + 0 = 0,\; C_{\text{out}}=0 \\
\text{Bit 4:} & \quad 0 + 1 + 0 = 1,\; C_{\text{out}}=0 \\
\text{Bit 5:} & \quad 1 + 0 + 0 = 1,\; C_{\text{out}}=0 \\
\text{Bit 6:} & \quad 1 + 0 + 0 = 1,\; C_{\text{out}}=0 \\
\text{Bit 7:} & \quad 0 + 1 + 0 = 1,\; C_{\text{out}}=0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Read the result:**

$$
R = 1011\,0100_2
$$

**Step 3 — Interpret the result as signed two's complement:**

$$
1011\,0100_2 = -76 \quad (\text{signed})
$$

But the true mathematical sum is $+180$, which is **outside** the representable range $[-128, +127]$. The operation has **overflowed**.

**Step 4 — Apply the overflow-detection formula:**

$$
V = (R_d^7 \land R_r^7 \land \overline{R^7}) \lor (\overline{R_d^7} \land \overline{R_r^7} \land R^7)
$$

Substituting:
- $R_d^7 = 0$ (MSB of A)
- $R_r^7 = 0$ (MSB of B)
- $R^7 = 1$ (MSB of result)

$$
\begin{aligned}
V & = (0 \land 0 \land 0) \lor (1 \land 1 \land 1) \\
  & = 0 \lor 1 \\
  & = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Update the SREG:**

$$
V = 1, \quad N = R^7 = 1, \quad C = 0, \quad Z = 0, \quad S = N \oplus V = 1 \oplus 1 = 0
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why V matters for signed code:** A `BRMI` test (checks N) would *incorrectly* conclude the result is negative. A `BRVS` test (checks V) correctly flags the signed overflow, allowing the firmware to take corrective action (e.g., saturate, log an error, or extend to 16 bits).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Carry Flag for Subtraction (Borrow Detection)

**Problem Setup:** Compute $A - B$ where $A = 0x20$ and $B = 0x35$ (unsigned). Expected: $A < B$, so the subtraction must borrow.

The AVR computes $A - B$ internally as $A + (\overline{B}) + 1$ (two's complement addition). Let us track the carry-out:

$$
A = 0010\,0000_2
$$
$$
B = 0011\,0101_2
$$
$$
\overline{B} = 1100\,1010_2
$$
$$
\overline{B} + 1 = 1100\,1011_2 \quad (\text{this is } -B)
$$

**Performing the 8-bit addition $A + (-B)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \phantom{+} 0010\,0000 \\
+ & \phantom{0} 1100\,1011 \\
\hline
& \phantom{0} 1110\,1011 \quad \text{(Result)} \\
& \text{Carry out} = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Interpret the result:**

$$
R = 1110\,1011_2 = 0xEB = -21 \text{ (signed)} = 235 \text{ (unsigned)}
$$

**Step 3 — Determine the Carry flag using AVR's definition:**

For subtraction, the AVR sets $C = 1$ when **no carry out** is generated (i.e., a borrow was needed). Since the carry out is 0:

$$
C = \overline{C_{\text{out}}} = 1
$$

**Final SREG after subtraction:**

$$
C = 1 \quad (\text{Borrow occurred}), \quad N = 1, \quad Z = 0, \quad V = 0
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students confuse the borrow convention. In AVR, **C = 1 means "borrow happened"** (i.e., unsigned A < unsigned B). The result itself is correctly stored, but $C$ tells you the *unsigned comparison* outcome.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Summary of Flag Behaviour for Common Operations

| Operation | H | S | V | N | Z | C |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `ADD Rd, Rr` | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| `ADC Rd, Rr` | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| `SUB Rd, Rr` | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| `SBC Rd, Rr` | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| `AND Rd, Rr` | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ |
| `OR Rd, Rr` | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ |
| `EOR Rd, Rr` | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ |
| `COM Rd` (1's comp) | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ (always 1) |
| `NEG Rd` (2's comp) | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| `INC Rd` | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ |
| `DEC Rd` | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ |
| `LSL Rd` (left shift) | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| `LSR Rd` (right shift) | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ |
| `ASR Rd` (arith. right shift) | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| `CPI Rd, K` (compare) | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |

✅ = flag is updated; ❌ = flag is **not** affected (or defined as 0).

# 📝 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 🅰️ PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model-Q]**
> **Q1.** List the eight bits of the AVR Status Register (SREG) in order from MSB to LSB. Classify each as either a *control bit* or a *status flag*.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Bit Position | Name | Classification |
|---|---|---|
| Bit 7 | **I** — Global Interrupt Enable | Control |
| Bit 6 | **T** — Bit Copy Storage | Control |
| Bit 5 | **H** — Half Carry Flag | Status |
| Bit 4 | **S** — Sign Bit | Status |
| Bit 3 | **V** — Two's Complement Overflow | Status |
| Bit 2 | **N** — Negative Flag | Status |
| Bit 1 | **Z** — Zero Flag | Status |
| Bit 0 | **C** — Carry Flag | Status |

The bits **I** and **T** are programmer-settable control bits, while **H, S, V, N, Z, C** are hardware status flags automatically updated by the ALU.

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model-Q]**
> **Q2.** What is the function of the **H (Half Carry) flag** in the AVR Status Register? On which operations is it set? Briefly explain its utility.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **H flag** indicates a carry generated between the lower and upper nibbles (i.e., from bit 3 to bit 4) of an 8-bit arithmetic operation. It is updated by `ADD`, `ADC`, `SUB`, `SBC`, `SUBI`, `SBCI`, `ADIW`, and `SBIW` instructions. Its primary utility is in **Binary Coded Decimal (BCD) arithmetic**, where the lower and upper 4-bit digits must be corrected separately using the half-carry signal. Although the AVR core itself does not have a native `DAA` instruction, the H flag is preserved for compatibility and for software-emulated BCD operations.

---

## 🅱️ PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> ### ✅ CHOICE A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified Past Year]**
> **Q.A(a)** Explain the architecture of the AVR Status Register (SREG) with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of each flag bit in detail. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **AVR Status Register (SREG)** is an 8-bit special function register at I/O address `0x5F`. Each bit reflects either a control state or a hardware-updated status flag.

**Diagram:**

| Bit 7 | Bit 6 | Bit 5 | Bit 4 | Bit 3 | Bit 2 | Bit 1 | Bit 0 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **I** | **T** | **H** | **S** | **V** | **N** | **Z** | **C** |

**Role of each bit:**

1. **I (Bit 7):** Master interrupt enable. Set by `SEI`, cleared by `CLI` or hardware on interrupt entry.
2. **T (Bit 6):** Bit-copy latching for `BST` and `BLD` instructions.
3. **H (Bit 5):** Half-carry between bit 3 and bit 4 (used in BCD arithmetic).
4. **S (Bit 4):** Sign flag, defined as $S = N \oplus V$. Indicates signed-result negativity.
5. **V (Bit 3):** Two's-complement overflow. Set when a signed operation yields a result outside $[-128, +127]$.
6. **N (Bit 2):** Negative flag. Mirrors bit 7 of the result.
7. **Z (Bit 1):** Zero flag. Set when the result equals zero.
8. **C (Bit 0):** Carry flag. In addition, indicates carry-out; in subtraction, indicates borrow (i.e., $C=1$ when unsigned minuend < subtrahend).

> **Q.A(b)** Perform the following 8-bit unsigned additions in the AVR and determine the final values of the **H, S, V, N, Z, C** flags of SREG: **(7 marks)**
> - **(i)** $0xA5 + 0x5B$
> - **(ii)** $0x7F + 0x01$

**Model Solution:**

### Part (i): $0xA5 + 0x5B$

Step 1 — Convert to binary and add:

$$
A = 1010\,0101_2, \quad B = 0101\,1011_2
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \phantom{+} 1010\,0101 \\
+ & \phantom{0} 0101\,1011 \\
\hline
& \phantom{0} 1\,0000\,0000 \quad \text{(9-bit result)} \\
& R = 0000\,0000_2 = 0x00,\; C_{\text{out}}=1
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 2 — Evaluate each flag:

- $C = 1$ (carry out of bit 7)
- $Z = 1$ (result = 0)
- $N = 0$ (bit 7 of result = 0)
- $H = 0$ (bit 3 → bit 4: $0+1+0 = 1$, no half-carry)
- Signed overflow: $R_d^7 = 1, R_r^7 = 0, R^7 = 0$; with mixed sign bits, no overflow.
- $V = 0$
- $S = N \oplus V = 0 \oplus 0 = 0$

**Final SREG: `I T 0 0 0 0 1 1` → bits `[H S V N Z C] = 000011`**

### Part (ii): $0x7F + 0x01$

Step 1 — Binary addition:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \phantom{+} 0111\,1111 \\
+ & \phantom{0} 0000\,0001 \\
\hline
& \phantom{0} 1000\,0000 \\
& R = 1000\,0000_2 = 0x80,\; C_{\text{out}}=0
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 2 — Flags:

- $C = 0$ (no carry out)
- $Z = 0$ (result ≠ 0)
- $N = 1$ (bit 7 of result = 1)
- $H = 1$ (carry from bit 3 → bit 4)
- Signed overflow: both operands positive ($+127$ and $+1$), result MSB = 1 means the signed value is $-128$. The mathematical sum $+128$ exceeds the signed range.
- $V = 1$
- $S = N \oplus V = 1 \oplus 1 = 0$

**Final SREG: `H S V N Z C = 1 0 1 1 0 0`**

---

> ### ✅ CHOICE B (14 Marks) — Alternative Selection

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified Past Year]**
> **Q.B(a)** Differentiate between the **General Purpose Registers (GPRs)** and the **Special Function Registers (SFRs)** in the AVR architecture. Why is the R26–R31 register group given a dual purpose? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Aspect | General Purpose Registers (R0–R31) | Special Function Registers (SFRs) |
|---|---|---|
| **Quantity** | 32 | Varies (SREG, SP, ports, timers, etc.) |
| **Primary Use** | ALU operands and results | I/O, control, status |
| **Address Space** | $0x00$–$0x1F$ (Register File) | $0x20$–$0x5F$ (I/O) |
| **Width** | 8-bit | Mostly 8-bit (PC/SP are 16-bit) |
| **Access Speed** | 1 cycle | 1 cycle for in/out; 2 for memory-mapped |

**Dual purpose of R26–R31:** These six registers can pair up to form the three 16-bit indirect-addressing pointers **X (R27:R26), Y (R29:R28), and Z (R31:R30)**. The pairing is implemented in hardware and supports indexed addressing modes such as `LD Rd, X+`, `ST Rd, -Y`, and `LPM Rd, Z+` (program memory reads for table lookups). The 16-bit pointer is essential for accessing the entire 64 KB SRAM and 32 KB program memory of the AVR.

> **Q.B(b)** A piece of AVR assembly executes `LDI R16, 0xF0` followed by `SUBI R16, 0x80`. Compute the resulting value of R16 and the full SREG state after execution. Justify the values of **C, H, N, V, S, Z**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Step 1 — Decode operands:
- `R16` ← `0xF0` = `1111\,0000_2` (this is $-16$ in signed 8-bit, or $240$ unsigned)
- `SUBI R16, 0x80` = `R16 - 0x80` where $0x80 = 1000\,0000_2$ ($-128$ signed, $128$ unsigned)

Step 2 — Perform the subtraction $0xF0 - 0x80$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \phantom{+} 1111\,0000 \\
- & \phantom{0} 1000\,0000 \\
\hline
& \phantom{0} 0111\,0000
\end{aligned}
$$

Internally, the AVR computes $0xF0 + (\overline{0x80} + 1) = 0xF0 + 0x80$ (as $-0x80$ in two's complement). Wait — careful: $-0x80$ in two's complement is $0x80$ itself (since `+128` is unrepresentable, $-128$ is `0x80`).

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \phantom{+} 1111\,0000 \\
+ & \phantom{0} 1000\,0000 \\
\hline
& 1\,0111\,0000 \quad \text{(9-bit intermediate)} \\
& R = 0111\,0000_2 = 0x70, \quad C_{\text{out}} = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 3 — Interpret the flags:

- **C** (subtraction): $C = 1$ if **no carry out**. Since $C_{\text{out}} = 1$, the carry-**complement** gives $C = 0$. So **C = 0** (no borrow; unsigned $0xF0 = 240 \ge 0x80 = 128$).
- **Z**: Result $0x70 \neq 0$, so **Z = 0**.
- **N**: $R^7 = 0$, so **N = 0**.
- **H** (bit 3 → bit 4 borrow): compute as $\overline{(\text{no borrow from bit 3})}$. Subtracting bit 3: $0 - 0 = 0$, no borrow. Subtracting bit 4: $0 - 0 = 0$, no borrow. **H = 0**.
- **V** (signed overflow): $R_d^7 = 1$ (operand $-16$), $R_r^7 = 1$ (subtrahend $-128$ in signed view), $R^7 = 0$ (result $+112$). Both operands had MSB = 1; using the formula:
  $$V = (1 \land 1 \land \overline{0}) \lor (\overline{1} \land \overline{1} \land 0) = (1) \lor (0) = 1$$
  Wait — let us re-check the standard AVR V rule for SUB: $V = \overline{R_d^7} \land R_r^7 \land R^7$ OR $R_d^7 \land \overline{R_r^7} \land \overline{R^7}$.
  Substituting: $\overline{1} \land 1 \land 0 \lor 1 \land \overline{1} \land \overline{0} = 0 \lor 0 = 0$. So **V = 0** (no signed overflow; $-16 - (-128) = +112$ is valid).
- **S** = $N \oplus V = 0 \oplus 0 = 0$.

**Final Results:**
- `R16 = 0x70 = 0111 0000₂`
- `SREG = 0b I T 0 0 0 0 0 0` (bits H S V N Z C = `000000`)

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks (KTU Board Examiner — Module 1)**
>
> 1. **Confusing the Carry flag meaning in ADD vs SUB.** In ADD, $C=1$ means *carry generated*. In SUB, $C=1$ means *borrow occurred* (i.e., unsigned minuend < subtrahend). Forgetting to take the complement for subtraction is the **#1 most common mistake**.
>
> 2. **Sign flag confusion.** $S$ is **not the same** as $N$. The Sign bit is computed as $N \oplus V$. Students who write $S = N$ lose 1 mark.
>
> 3. **Half-carry scope.** H is **only** the carry from bit 3 to bit 4 (between nibbles). It is **not** the carry from bit 7 (that is the C flag).
>
> 4. **Forgetting that I and T are not arithmetic flags.** They are **control bits**; arithmetic instructions never modify them.
>
> 5. **Not showing the bit-7 in V-detection derivations.** Always show the equation $V = (R_d^7 \land R_r^7 \land \overline{R^7}) \lor (\overline{R_d^7} \land \overline{R_r^7} \land R^7)$ explicitly to earn full marks.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Scheme**

- ✅ The **Register Set** of AVR consists of **32 × 8-bit GPRs (R0–R31)** plus **Special Function Registers (SREG, PC, SP, I/O ports)**.
- ✅ The **SREG** is an **8-bit register** at I/O address **`0x5F`**, holding 6 status flags + 2 control bits.
- ✅ **I (Bit 7)** — Global Interrupt Enable; **T (Bit 6)** — Bit Copy Storage. Both are *control* bits.
- ✅ **C (Bit 0)** — Carry (addition) / Borrow (subtraction). Always check its meaning *per instruction*.
- ✅ **Z (Bit 1)** — Set iff result = 0. Drives `BREQ` / `BRNE` branches.
- ✅ **N (Bit 2)** — Copy of result bit 7. Drives `BRMI` / `BRPL`.
- ✅ **V (Bit 3)** — Signed two's-complement overflow. Drives `BRVS` / `BRVC`.
- ✅ **S (Bit 4)** — Defined as $S = N \oplus V$. Drives signed-compare branches `BRSH`/`BRLO` and `BRGE`/`BRLT`.
- ✅ **H (Bit 5)** — Half-carry (bit 3 → bit 4). Used in BCD arithmetic.
- ✅ Registers **R26:R27, R28:R29, R30:R31** form the **X, Y, Z** 16-bit pointer registers.
- ✅ For any arithmetic problem in KTU, **always tabulate all 6 status flags** (H, S, V, N, Z, C) — partial credit is lost if even one is missing.
- ✅ The SREG is **read-modify-write** sensitive; read it into a GPR (`IN R16, SREG`) before testing or saving.
- ✅ After reset, SREG = `0x00` — all interrupts disabled, all flags cleared.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Accumulator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT403 - COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE AND MICROCONTROLLER
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Accumulator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Accumulator (ACC / AC):** A special-purpose register inside the **Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)** of a Central Processing Unit (CPU) that is used to store the intermediate results of arithmetic and logic operations. In accumulator-based architectures, it acts as the **implicit default operand** and the **implicit destination** for almost all arithmetic, logical, and data-movement instructions.

The accumulator is the single most important data register in classic accumulator-based CPUs (e.g., the historical **Intel 8085**, **Motorola 6800**, and **MOS 6502**). Its bit-width is equal to the **word length** of the processor — typically **8 bits** in 8-bit microprocessors and **16 bits** in modern controllers.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of the accumulator as the **whiteboard of a math teacher**.

- The teacher writes a number on the whiteboard (load into ACC).
- A student writes a new number on a small piece of paper and hands it to the teacher (the operand is given to the ALU).
- The teacher performs the addition mentally and **erases the old number, writing the new total back on the whiteboard** (the result is always stored back in the ACC).
- The whiteboard is also the only place the class (the next instruction) looks for the answer.

In the same way, the CPU does not have multiple general-purpose registers to choose from in early architectures — **everything funnels through the accumulator**. It is the only place the ALU "writes to" by default, and one of the operands of every ALU operation is always the accumulator.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics to Remember:**
> - **Size:** Equal to the processor's word length (e.g., **8 bits** for 8085, **16 bits** for 8086, **32 bits** for ARM Cortex-M).
> - **Location:** Inside the **ALU / Register File**, not in main memory.
> - **Speed:** **Zero wait states** — it is the fastest storage available to the CPU.
> - **Visibility:** Implicit, not specified in the machine instruction (hence called an *implied* or *implicit* addressing mode).

### 1.3 Role in the Instruction Set

The accumulator participates in addressing as well as computation. In the **8085 microprocessor**, the accumulator is one of the few registers allowed in certain special addressing modes:

- **Register A** is the only 8-bit register that can be used in **I/O addressing** (`IN`/`OUT` instructions).
- It is the only register that can perform **rotates** through the carry flag (`RAL`, `RAR`).
- It is the only register used by the **`DAA` (Decimal Adjust Accumulator)** instruction.
- It is mandatory in **logical instructions** such as `ANA`, `ORA`, `XRA`, `CMP`, and `CMA`.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — How the Accumulator Works

A typical accumulator-based ALU operation follows a fixed micro-sequence:

1. **Fetch** the instruction from memory (e.g., `ADD B`).
2. **Decode** → the CPU control unit recognizes this as an ALU operation whose *destination* is the accumulator.
3. **Read operand 1** → The accumulator value is fed into one of the ALU inputs.
4. **Read operand 2** → The specified register/memory value is fed into the other ALU input.
5. **Execute** → The ALU performs the arithmetic/logic function.
6. **Store result** → The ALU output is written **back into the accumulator**, overwriting its previous content.
7. **Set flags** → The status register (flags) is updated based on the result (Zero, Carry, Sign, Parity, Auxiliary Carry).

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Accumulator Architecture

- **Reduced Instruction Length:** Since the accumulator is *implicit*, the machine code does not need to specify a destination register. This keeps the opcode compact — a critical design constraint when memory and bus widths were extremely narrow in early microprocessors.
- **Simpler Hardware:** Only **one main ALU input latch** and **one output register** are required, reducing the transistor count and therefore the cost.
- **Sequential Computation Friendly:** Multi-step calculations (e.g., `(A+B)*C + D`) are natural: each step leaves its result in the ACC, ready for the next.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Sheet

| Concept / Symbol | Description | Equation / Definition | Unit / Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| ACC | Accumulator Register | Holds intermediate ALU result | bits (size = word length) |
| $N$ | Word length of the processor | Number of bits in ACC | bits |
| $V_{\text{ACC}}$ | Numeric value stored in ACC | $V_{\text{ACC}} \in [0, 2^N - 1]$ (unsigned) | Decimal equivalent |
| Overflow Flag | Set on signed overflow | $V = C_{\text{in}} \oplus C_{\text{out}}$ of MSB | Boolean |
| Zero Flag | Set when result is zero | $Z = 1 \iff \text{ACC} = 0$ | Boolean |
| Carry Flag | Carry out of MSB | $C_{y} = 1 \iff V_{\text{ACC}} > 2^N - 1$ | Boolean |
| Auxiliary Carry | Carry from bit 3 → bit 4 | Used by `DAA` for BCD correction | Boolean |
| Instruction Length | Bytes per ALU op | **1 byte** (implied destination) | Bytes |
| Effective Address (EA) | I/O via accumulator | $\text{EA} = \text{(port address byte)} \text{ as } 8\text{-bit}$ | Hex |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners frequently test the *flags generated by an ALU operation on the accumulator*. Memorize how `ADD`, `SUB`, `ANA`, `ORA`, `XRA`, `CMP`, and `CMA` affect the **S, Z, P, CY, AC** flags differently.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Embedded control:** 8051, PIC, and AVR microcontrollers all retain a dedicated **ACC/A register** for backward compatibility and compact code density.
- **DSP and MAC operations:** Modern Digital Signal Processors (DSPs) include a hardware **MAC (Multiply-Accumulate)** unit that implicitly accumulates products — a direct descendant of the accumulator concept.
- **Compiler code generation:** Compiler back-ends target accumulator-like registers first when emitting code, even on **RISC** machines, because the source-level expression evaluation is naturally accumulator-style.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Accumulator Behaviour Across 8085 Instruction Classes

The table below maps each 8085 instruction class to the source, destination, and flag effects on the accumulator.

| Instruction Class | Example | Source 1 | Source 2 | Destination | Flags Affected |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Arithmetic | `ADD B` | ACC | Register B | ACC | S, Z, P, CY, AC |
| Arithmetic | `ADI 05H` | ACC | Immediate 8-bit | ACC | S, Z, P, CY, AC |
| Logic | `ANA B` | ACC | Register B | ACC | S, Z, P, CY=0, AC=1 |
| Logic | `XRA A` | ACC | ACC | ACC (= 0) | Z=1, CY=0, AC=0 |
| Compare | `CMP B` | ACC | Register B | **No write** | S, Z, P, CY, AC |
| Complement | `CMA` | ACC | — | ACC (bitwise NOT) | **None** |
| Rotate | `RLC` | ACC | — | ACC (rotated) | CY only |
| Decimal Adjust | `DAA` | ACC | — | ACC (BCD-corrected) | S, Z, P, CY, AC |
| I/O | `IN 01H` | Port | — | ACC | **None** |
| I/O | `OUT 01H` | ACC | — | Port | **None** |
| Stack | `PUSH PSW` | ACC, Flags | — | Stack | — |
| Stack | `POP PSW` | Stack | — | ACC, Flags | — |

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Worked Example — Multi-Byte Addition

**Problem:** Add two 16-bit numbers stored in memory locations `2050H` (low byte) and `2051H` (high byte) with numbers at `2052H` and `2053H`. Store the 16-bit result at `2054H` (low) and `2055H` (high).

**Analytical Walkthrough:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{LXI H, 2050H} \quad \rightarrow \text{HL points to first number} \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{MOV A, M} \quad \rightarrow \text{ACC} \leftarrow M[2050H] \quad \text{(low byte of Num1)} \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{INX H} \quad \rightarrow \text{HL} \leftarrow 2051H \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{ADD M} \quad \rightarrow \text{ACC} \leftarrow \text{ACC} + M[2051H] \quad \text{(WAIT — wrong target)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> The accumulator always *receives the result*. So the cleanest sequence is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{LXI H, 2050H} \quad \rightarrow \text{HL} = 2050H \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{MOV A, M} \quad \rightarrow \text{ACC} = M[2050H] \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{INX H} \quad \rightarrow \text{HL} = 2051H \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{ADD M} \quad \rightarrow \text{ACC} = \text{ACC} + M[2051H] \quad \text{(LO byte of result)} \\
\text{Step 5:} \quad & \text{STA 2054H} \quad \rightarrow M[2054H] \leftarrow \text{ACC} \\
\text{Step 6:} \quad & \text{MVI A, 00H} \quad \rightarrow \text{ACC} = 0 \\
\text{Step 7:} \quad & \text{ACI 00H} \quad \rightarrow \text{ACC} = 0 + \text{CY} \quad \text{(propagates carry)} \\
\text{Step 8:} \quad & \text{STA 2055H} \quad \rightarrow M[2055H] \leftarrow \text{ACC} \quad \text{(HI byte of result)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Key Insight (the "Why"):** Because the ACC is the *only* place the ALU can write, every intermediate result MUST live there temporarily. The `ACI 00H` trick is the canonical way to *propagate* the carry flag of the previous addition into the ACC.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Numerical Flag Derivation

Suppose `ACC = 0x7F` and we execute `ADD B` where `B = 0x01`.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Binary addition:} \quad & 0\,111\,1111 \;+\; 0\,000\,0001 \;=\; 1\,000\,0000 \\
\text{ACC result:} \quad & \text{ACC} = 0x00 \\
\text{Carry Flag:} \quad & C_{y} = 1 \quad \text{(carry out of bit 7)} \\
\text{Zero Flag:} \quad & Z = 1 \quad \text{(ACC became zero)} \\
\text{Sign Flag:} \quad & S = 0 \quad \text{(MSB of result is 0)} \\
\text{Parity Flag:} \quad & P = 1 \quad \text{(even number of 1s = 0)} \\
\text{Aux Carry:} \quad & AC = 1 \quad \text{(carry from bit 3 to bit 4)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the standard "exam-style" sub-question in KTU: *"After execution of `ADD B`, ACC contains `00H` and CY = 1. Justify all flag values."* Always walk through the binary addition explicitly.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture — Accumulator Inside the CPU Datapath

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Program Counter] --> A2[Instruction Register]
    A2 --> A3[Control Unit]
    A3 --> A4[ALU Control Signals]
    A4 --> A5[Arithmetic Logic Unit]
    node1[Memory Data Bus] --> node2[Operand B Latch]
    A6[Accumulator ACC] --> A5
    node2 --> A5
    A5 --> A6
    A5 --> A7[Status Flags Register]
    A6 --> node1
    A7 --> A3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The dotted line of intent is — the **Accumulator (ACC)** supplies **one ALU operand**, the **Operand B Latch** supplies the other, and the **ALU writes the result back to the ACC** (the closed feedback loop on the right of the diagram). The status register feeds the control unit to enable **conditional jumps** (e.g., `JZ`, `JC`).

### 4.2 Instruction Execution Flow — What Happens to the Accumulator

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA[Fetch Opcode from Memory] --> stepB[Decode in Control Unit]
    stepB --> stepC{Is ALU Operation?}
    stepC -- Yes --> stepD[Latch Operand 1 from ACC]
    stepC -- No --> stepE[Execute Non-ALU Path]
    stepD --> stepF[Fetch Operand 2 from Register or Memory]
    stepF --> stepG[ALU Performs Operation ADD SUB ANA etc]
    stepG --> stepH[Write Result Back into ACC]
    stepH --> stepI[Update Status Flags S Z P CY AC]
    stepI --> stepJ[End of Instruction Cycle]
    stepE --> stepJ
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Diagram — Accumulator in the 8085 Register Model

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CPU[8085 Microprocessor CPU]
        node3[Accumulator A 8 bits]
        node4[Flag Register F 8 bits]
        node5[General Purpose B C D E H L]
        node6[Special PC SP IX IY]
    end
    node3 --- node4
    node3 <--> node5
    node3 <--> node6
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 / 3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, 3 Marks]**
Define the term **Accumulator**. Why is it called an *implicit* operand in 8085 instructions?

**Model Answer:**
The accumulator is an **8-bit register** in the 8085 ALU that stores intermediate results of arithmetic, logical, and data-transfer operations. It is called an *implicit* (or *implied*) operand because the machine code of ALU instructions does not contain a bit-field specifying the accumulator as either the source or the destination — its involvement is *assumed by the opcode itself*. For example, `ADD B` is a 1-byte instruction; the CPU automatically takes one operand from `A` and stores the result back in `A`.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, 3 Marks]**
List **any four** 8085 instructions that operate *only* on the accumulator. Mention the flag(s) affected by each.

**Model Answer:**

| Instruction | Function | Flag(s) Affected |
|---|---|---|
| `CMA` | Complement ACC | **None** |
| `STC` | Set Carry | **CY = 1** (only) |
| `DAA` | Decimal adjust ACC | S, Z, P, CY, AC |
| `RLC` | Rotate ACC left | **CY only** |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]`

**(a)** Explain the role of the accumulator in the 8085 microprocessor with a neat block diagram. Discuss how it is involved in the `ADD`, `ANA`, `CMP`, and `DAA` instructions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write an 8085 assembly language program to **add two 8-bit numbers stored at memory locations `2000H` and `2001H`** and store the result at `2002H`. Show the flag values if the numbers are `5AH` and `A5H`. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(a):**

1. **Role of the Accumulator:** The accumulator (register `A`) is the primary 8-bit working register of the 8085. It is the only register that can directly receive data from the **data bus** and the **input port**. It is one of the two operands for every arithmetic/logic instruction and the *default destination* for all of them.

2. **Block diagram** (refer to SECTION 4.1 of these notes).

3. **Involvement in key instructions:**

| Instruction | Operation | Result Destination | Flags Set |
|---|---|---|---|
| `ADD r / ADD M` | `ACC ← ACC + operand` | ACC | S, Z, P, CY, AC |
| `ANA r / ANA M` | `ACC ← ACC AND operand` | ACC | S, Z, P, AC=1, CY=0 |
| `CMP r / CMP M` | `ACC − operand` (no write) | ACC unchanged | S, Z, P, CY, AC |
| `DAA` | BCD correction of ACC | ACC | S, Z, P, CY, AC |

> In `CMP`, although the destination *is* logically the accumulator for the subtraction, the result is **discarded** and only the flags are retained. The accumulator's content is preserved.

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(b):**

The required 8085 program:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Line 1:} \quad & \text{LDA 2000H} \quad \rightarrow \text{ACC} \leftarrow M[2000H] = 5AH \\
\text{Line 2:} \quad & \text{MOV B, A} \quad \rightarrow B = 5AH \\
\text{Line 3:} \quad & \text{LDA 2001H} \quad \rightarrow \text{ACC} \leftarrow M[2001H] = A5H \\
\text{Line 4:} \quad & \text{ADD B} \quad \rightarrow \text{ACC} \leftarrow 5AH + A5H \\
\text{Line 5:} \quad & \text{STA 2002H} \quad \rightarrow M[2002H] \leftarrow \text{ACC} \\
\text{Line 6:} \quad & \text{HLT}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical evaluation of flags:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Binary addition:} \quad & 0101\,1010 \;+\; 1010\,0101 \;=\; 1\,1111\,1111 \\
\text{Result in ACC:} \quad & \text{ACC} = 0xFF \\
\text{Carry Flag:} \quad & C_{y} = 0 \quad \text{(no carry out of bit 7)} \\
\text{Zero Flag:} \quad & Z = 0 \quad \text{(ACC} \neq 0\text{)} \\
\text{Sign Flag:} \quad & S = 1 \quad \text{(MSB of result is 1)} \\
\text{Parity Flag:} \quad & P = 1 \quad \text{(8 ones = even parity)} \\
\text{Aux Carry:} \quad & AC = 0 \quad \text{(no carry from bit 3 to bit 4)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Final result stored: `M[2002H] = FFH`.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted]`

**(a)** Differentiate between **implicit**, **immediate**, and **register addressing** modes with one example each. State which mode the accumulator uses in the `CMP M` instruction. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An 8085 system has a sensor value `3FH` in the accumulator and a threshold `0AH` in register `B`. Write the program to **check whether the sensor reading exceeds the threshold**, and if so, output `01H` to output port `08H`; otherwise output `00H`. List the final flag status. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(a):**

| Mode | Operand Location | Example | Instruction Length |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Implicit** | Inherent in opcode (e.g., ACC) | `CMA` (complement ACC) | **1 byte** |
| **Immediate** | Given literally in instruction | `MVI A, 05H` | **2 bytes** |
| **Register** | Inside a CPU register | `MOV A, B` | **1 byte** |

The instruction `CMP M` uses **Implicit addressing for the ACC** (since `A` is assumed) combined with **Direct memory addressing for the operand** `M[HL]`. The accumulator itself is not encoded in the opcode — it is implied.

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(b):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Line 1:} \quad & \text{MVI A, 3FH} \quad \rightarrow \text{ACC} = 3FH \\
\text{Line 2:} \quad & \text{MVI B, 0AH} \quad \rightarrow B = 0AH \\
\text{Line 3:} \quad & \text{CMP B} \quad \rightarrow \text{computes ACC} - B = 3FH - 0AH = 35H \\
\text{Line 4:} \quad & \text{JC LESS} \quad \rightarrow \text{if CY=1 (borrow), jump to LESS label} \\
\text{Line 5:} \quad & \text{MVI A, 01H} \quad \rightarrow \text{ACC} = 01H \quad \text{(sensor} \geq \text{threshold)} \\
\text{Line 6:} \quad & \text{OUT 08H} \quad \rightarrow \text{send 01H to port 08H} \\
\text{Line 7:} \quad & \text{JMP DONE} \\
\text{LESS:} \quad & \text{MVI A, 00H} \quad \rightarrow \text{ACC} = 00H \quad \text{(sensor} < \text{threshold)} \\
\text{Line 9:} \quad & \text{OUT 08H} \quad \rightarrow \text{send 00H to port 08H} \\
\text{DONE:} \quad & \text{HLT}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Flag status after `CMP B` for 3FH vs 0AH:**

- $S = 0$ (MSB of `35H` is 0)
- $Z = 0$ (result not zero, so sensor > threshold → no jump to LESS)
- $P = 0$ (odd number of 1s in `35H` = 4 ones → wait, 4 ones = even → $P = 1$)
- $C_{y} = 0$ (no borrow)
- $AC = 0$

> Output sent to port `08H` = **01H** (since 3FH > 0AH).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Writing `ADD M` when you mean `MOV A, M + ADD B` in multi-byte arithmetic** — students forget that `ADD M` adds the *memory* operand but stores the result *only* in the accumulator. State this explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing the `CMP` flag behaviour with `SUB`** — `CMP` does *not* write to the accumulator; if the next instruction reads `A`, it will still have the *original* value. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this.
> 3. **Forgetting Auxiliary Carry in `ANA`** — `ANA` clears CY and sets AC=1 regardless of operands. This is a frequently-missed ½-mark question.
> 4. **Forgetting that `DAA` works only *after* an `ADD` instruction** — it is a post-add BCD corrector, not a standalone BCD adder.
> 5. **Mixing up the parity flag convention** — in 8085, **P = 1 means EVEN parity** (not odd). Many students invert this.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Accumulator (A)** is the **8-bit working register** of the 8085, located in the **ALU**.
- It is an **implicit** operand in nearly all ALU instructions — the destination bit is *not encoded* in the opcode.
- **Result destination:** Always the accumulator for `ADD`, `SUB`, `ANA`, `ORA`, `XRA`, `ADI`, `SUI`, `ANI`, `ORI`, `XRI`, `INR`, `DCR`, `CMA`, `DAA`, rotates, and I/O.
- **No write** to accumulator for `CMP` / `CPI` (only flags change).
- **Flag-affecting instructions** must be memorised: `CMA` → none; `STC` → CY; `CMC` → CY; `CMP` → S, Z, P, CY, AC; `DAA` → S, Z, P, CY, AC.
- **Parity convention:** P = 1 ⇔ even number of 1s in the result.
- **Word length of ACC = word length of CPU = 8 bits in 8085**.
- The accumulator is the **only register** that can be used with `IN`, `OUT`, and `DAA`.
- The accumulator + flag register together form the **PSW (Processor Status Word)** and are pushed/popped together by `PUSH PSW` / `POP PSW`.
- In **multi-byte arithmetic**, the carry flag of the low-byte operation must be **propagated** to the high-byte addition using `ACI 00H` (or equivalent) — because the ACC is the only place the high-byte partial sum can be accumulated.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Program Counter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT403 - COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE AND MICROCONTROLLER
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Program Counter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

The **Program Counter (PC)** is a special-purpose CPU register that contains the **memory address of the next instruction** to be fetched from memory for execution. In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *Computer Architecture and Microcontroller*, the Program Counter is defined as the primary **sequencing register** of the Central Processing Unit, responsible for maintaining the instruction execution flow by automatically incrementing (or being explicitly modified by control-transfer instructions such as `JMP`, `CALL`, `BRANCH`, or `RET`) after every fetch operation.

In a typical KTU-targeted microcontroller architecture (e.g., **8051**, **ARM Cortex-M**, or **PIC**), the Program Counter is one of the most critical hardware resources. Its width (number of bits) directly determines the maximum addressable program memory space of the processor:

$$
\text{Maximum Addressable Memory} = 2^{n} \text{ bytes}
$$

where $n$ is the number of bits in the Program Counter.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCMRT403, Module 1):** The Program Counter is introduced as part of the *CPU Internal Architecture* and *Instruction Fetch-Decode-Execute Cycle*. Students must be able to explain the role of PC, its auto-increment behaviour, and its modification during branch/jump/call instructions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are reading a **recipe book** while cooking. You have a **finger (bookmark)** pointing at the current step you are executing. After you finish a step, your finger naturally slides down to the next step. This finger is exactly like the **Program Counter**.

- **Normal sequential execution** → Your finger moves down to the next step automatically (PC increments).
- **Conditional instruction** ("If the sauce is too salty, skip to garnishing") → Your finger jumps to a different page (PC is loaded with a new branch target address).
- **Sub-routine call** ("Prepare the white sauce from page 42, then come back") → Your finger notes the current page (saved on the stack), jumps to page 42 (PC loaded with subroutine address), and returns to the noted page after finishing (PC restored from stack).

This analogy clarifies the three fundamental operations of the PC: **auto-increment, branch-load, and stack-restore**.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Hardware Parameters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key PC Specifications Across Common Microcontrollers (KTU Reference):**
>
> | Microcontroller | PC Width | Addressable Program Memory |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Intel 8051** | **16-bit** | 64 KB ($2^{16}$) |
> | **PIC16F** | 13-bit (PCLATH + PCL) | 8 KB |
> | **ARM Cortex-M0** | **32-bit** | 4 GB ($2^{32}$) |
> | **AVR (ATmega328)** | 16/17-bit | Up to 128 KB |

The Program Counter is therefore **hardware-specific**, and its bit-width is a fixed architectural parameter of the CPU.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of the Program Counter

The PC operates under three distinct modes during program execution. Understanding these is critical for KTU examination questions on instruction sequencing.

#### Mode 1: Auto-Increment (Sequential Flow)

Under normal conditions, after the CPU fetches the current instruction, the PC is automatically incremented by the size (in bytes) of the fetched instruction. This ensures the CPU is always pointing at the next instruction in memory.

$$
\text{PC}_{\text{new}} = \text{PC}_{\text{old}} + \text{Instruction Size (in bytes)}
$$

For variable-length instruction sets (e.g., 8051: 1-byte, 2-byte, or 3-byte instructions), the increment value depends on the opcode just fetched.

#### Mode 2: Conditional/Unconditional Branch (Direct Address Load)

When a branch, jump, or call instruction is executed, the PC is loaded with a completely new address supplied by the instruction:

$$
\text{PC}_{\text{new}} = \text{Branch Target Address}
$$

Example (8051): `SJMP rel` → PC = PC + 2 + `rel` (relative offset).

#### Mode 3: Stack Restore (Return from Subroutine)

For return-type instructions (`RET`, `RETI`), the PC is restored from the stack:

$$
\text{PC}_{\text{new}} = \text{Memory}[\text{SP}]; \quad \text{SP} = \text{SP} + 2
$$

This is how the CPU "remembers" where to continue after a subroutine completes.

### 2.2 Why is the Program Counter Essential?

- **Instruction Sequencing**: Without the PC, the CPU would have no way to know which instruction to fetch next.
- **Control Flow Implementation**: High-level constructs (`if-else`, `while`, `for`, function calls) are all ultimately implemented by manipulating the PC.
- **Interrupt Handling**: When an interrupt occurs, the current PC is pushed onto the stack, and the PC is loaded with the Interrupt Service Routine (ISR) vector address.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Formula Reference for PC Calculations (KTU 2024 Scheme):**

| Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Max Addressable Memory | $M = 2^{n}$ bytes | $n$ = PC bit-width |
| Auto-increment | $\text{PC} \leftarrow \text{PC} + k$ | $k$ = instruction length in bytes |
| Relative Branch | $\text{PC} \leftarrow \text{PC} + k + \text{offset}$ | offset is a signed 2's complement number |
| Absolute Jump | $\text{PC} \leftarrow \text{addr}_{16}$ | 8051: `LJMP`, `AJMP` |
| Stack Return | $\text{PC} \leftarrow \text{Memory}[\text{SP}]$ | Pop operation; SP incremented by 2 (16-bit PC) |
| Memory Mapping | $\text{Physical Address} = \text{PC value}$ | Direct PC-to-memory mapping |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Program Counter is not just a textbook concept; it underpins:

- **Debugger Design**: Tools like GDB, Keil µVision, and MPLAB X use the PC to set breakpoints and single-step through firmware.
- **Crash Diagnostics**: A "stuck PC" or "invalid PC" value is the first thing engineers check when firmware enters a HardFault (ARM) or hangs (8051).
- **Bootloader Programming**: Bootloaders manipulate the PC indirectly by writing to the reset vector location in flash memory.
- **RTOS Context Switching**: The OS saves/restores the PC of each task to enable multitasking.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Address Calculation for Relative Branch (8051 Example)

Consider an 8051 instruction `SJMP rel` located at address `1000H`. The instruction `SJMP` is a **2-byte instruction**: 1 byte for opcode + 1 byte for signed relative offset `rel`.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Step 1:** When `SJMP rel` is fetched, the PC points to address `1000H`. After fetching the 2-byte instruction, the PC auto-increments to point to the next instruction:

$$
\text{PC}_{\text{after fetch}} = 1000\text{H} + 2 = 1002\text{H}
$$

**Step 2:** The 8051 hardware computes the branch target by adding the signed offset to the **updated** PC value (this is a KTU-favourite nuance):

$$
\text{PC}_{\text{target}} = \text{PC}_{\text{after fetch}} + \text{rel}
$$

**Step 3:** Substituting numerical values. Suppose `rel = 50H` (positive jump forward by 80 decimal):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PC}_{\text{target}} &= 1002\text{H} + 0050\text{H} \\
&= 1052\text{H}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** For a backward jump (negative offset), suppose `rel = FEH`. Recall that in 2's complement 8-bit signed representation, `FEH = -2` decimal:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PC}_{\text{target}} &= 1002\text{H} + \text{FEH} \\
&= 1002\text{H} - 0002\text{H} \\
&= 1000\text{H}
\end{aligned}
$$

This shows that the branch effectively jumps back to the same instruction's address — an infinite loop scenario used in embedded firmware for system "watchdog" stalls.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: PC Behaviour Across Instruction Categories

The following matrix is a high-yield KTU analysis showing how the PC changes for each major instruction category:

| Instruction Category | Example (8051) | PC Change Mechanism | New PC Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Data Transfer** | `MOV A, #55H` | Auto-increment | $\text{PC} + 2$ |
| **Arithmetic** | `ADD A, R0` | Auto-increment | $\text{PC} + 1$ or $\text{PC} + 2$ |
| **Logical** | `ANL A, #0FH` | Auto-increment | $\text{PC} + 2$ |
| **Unconditional Jump** | `LJMP 2000H` | Direct load | $2000\text{H}$ |
| **Conditional Jump** | `JC rel` | Conditional load | $\text{PC} + 2 + \text{rel}$ if carry=1, else $\text{PC} + 2$ |
| **Subroutine Call** | `LCALL 3000H` | Push PC+3, then load | $3000\text{H}$ (return addr on stack) |
| **Return** | `RET` | Pop from stack | $\text{Memory}[\text{SP}]$ (restored) |
| **Interrupt** | Hardware INT0 | Hardware vector push | $0003\text{H}$ (8051 default vector) |

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** An 8051 executes `AJMP addr11` at memory location `0400H`, where `addr11 = 500H`. Compute the final PC value after the jump.

**Given:**
- `AJMP` is a 2-byte instruction (1 byte opcode + 11-bit address).
- Starting PC: $0400\text{H}$.
- 11-bit target address: $500\text{H}$.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: PC after fetch} &= 0400\text{H} + 2 = 0402\text{H} \\
\text{Step 2: AJMP loads PC} &= \text{Target} = 0500\text{H} \\
\text{Final PC} &= 0500\text{H}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> `AJMP` is a **2-byte absolute jump within a 2 KB page**. KTU students often confuse it with `LJMP` (3-byte, 16-bit absolute). Read the opcode carefully!

### 3.4 PC Behaviour in an Interrupt Cycle

During an external interrupt on the 8051:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PC}_{\text{current}} &\rightarrow \text{Pushed to Stack (low byte first, then high byte)} \\
\text{PC}_{\text{new}} &\leftarrow 0003\text{H} \quad \text{(for INT0)} \\
\text{Stack Pointer SP} &\leftarrow \text{SP} - 2
\end{aligned}
$$

This explains why ISR code starts at fixed vector addresses (0003H, 000BH, 0013H, etc.) in the 8051 architecture.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Program Counter in the Fetch-Decode-Execute Cycle

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the role of the Program Counter within the CPU's instruction execution pipeline.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Power On Reset] --> B[PC Loaded with 0000H Reset Vector]
    B --> C[Fetch Instruction at PC address]
    C --> D[Increment PC by instruction size]
    D --> E[Decode Instruction Opcode]
    E --> F{Is it a Branch Jump Call or RET}
    F -- No --> G[Execute Instruction]
    F -- Yes --> H[Modify PC via Branch or Stack]
    G --> I[Repeat from Fetch]
    H --> I
    I --> C
    style A fill:#ffcc00,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#ff6666,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#66ccff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#99ff99,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#cc99ff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#ffaa66,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#99ccff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style H fill:#ff99cc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 PC Interaction with Memory and Stack

This architecture-level diagram maps how the Program Counter interfaces with program memory, the stack pointer, and the instruction register.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CPU["CPU Core Block"]
        PCReg["Program Counter PC 16-bit"]
        IR["Instruction Register IR"]
        SP["Stack Pointer SP"]
        ALU["ALU and Decoder"]
    end
    subgraph MEM["External Program Memory"]
        ROM["ROM or Flash 0000H to FFFFH"]
    end
    subgraph STK["Stack Memory in Internal RAM"]
        StackArea["Stack Area 08H to 7FH default"]
    end
    PCReg -- Address Bus --> ROM
    ROM -- Instruction Data --> IR
    IR --> ALU
    ALU -- Control Signals --> PCReg
    PCReg -- Push on Call Interrupt --> StackArea
    StackArea -- Pop on RET RETI --> PCReg
    style CPU fill:#e6f2ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px
    style MEM fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806600,stroke-width:2px
    style STK fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#800000,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: PC Update Decision Flow

This diagram models the *decision logic* the CPU uses to determine how to update the PC after each fetch — a common KTU 14-mark analytical question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start Fetch Cycle] --> S2[Opcode loaded from PC address]
    S2 --> S3[Opcode sent to Instruction Decoder]
    S3 --> D1{Opcode Class}
    D1 -- Data Movement Arithmetic Logic --> U1[PC auto increments by 1 2 or 3]
    D1 -- Unconditional Jump --> U2[PC loaded with absolute target]
    D1 -- Conditional Jump --> D2{Condition Flag true}
    D2 -- Yes --> U3[PC loaded with branch target]
    D2 -- No --> U4[PC auto increments]
    D1 -- Subroutine Call --> U5[Push PC+3 to Stack then load new PC]
    D1 -- Return --> U6[Pop saved PC from Stack]
    U1 --> E1[End of Cycle]
    U2 --> E1
    U3 --> E1
    U4 --> E1
    U5 --> E1
    U6 --> E1
    style S1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px
    style D1 fill:#ffd966,stroke:#806600,stroke-width:2px
    style D2 fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#800000,stroke-width:2px
    style E1 fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d,stroke-width:2px
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
**Define the Program Counter. What is its role in the instruction execution cycle of a CPU?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Program Counter (PC)** is a special-purpose CPU register that holds the **memory address of the next instruction** to be fetched from program memory. Its primary roles are:

1. **(1 Mark)** During the fetch phase of the instruction cycle, the CPU places the contents of the PC on the address bus to read the next instruction from memory.
2. **(1 Mark)** After fetching the instruction, the PC is automatically incremented by the instruction length (in bytes), so that it points to the subsequent instruction in sequential execution.
3. **(1 Mark)** During branch, jump, call, return, or interrupt operations, the PC is loaded with a new address supplied by the instruction or by hardware, thereby implementing control flow.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Paper]**
**A 16-bit microcontroller has a Program Counter of width 16 bits. Calculate the maximum addressable program memory. What happens when the PC overflows beyond FFFFH?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**(1 Mark) Maximum Memory Calculation:**

$$
M = 2^{16} = 65{,}536 \text{ bytes} = 64 \text{ KB}
$$

**(1 Mark) PC Overflow Behaviour:**
When the PC value exceeds `FFFFH`, it rolls over to `0000H` (reset vector address). In most microcontrollers, this triggers a **system reset** or undefined behaviour, since `0000H` is the designated boot address.

**(1 Mark) Engineering Note:**
For systems requiring larger programs, techniques such as **bank switching** (8051), **memory paging**, or migration to a 32-bit PC architecture (e.g., ARM Cortex-M) are employed.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice Format

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Sequential + Branch PC Analysis*

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1]**

**a)** Explain the role of the Program Counter in the **fetch-decode-execute cycle** with a neat block diagram. How does the PC behave for a 3-byte instruction versus a 1-byte instruction in the 8051? **(7 Marks)**

**b)** An 8051 microcontroller executes the following code segment starting at memory location `0100H`:

| Address | Instruction (Hex) | Mnemonic |
|---|---|---|
| 0100H | `80 05` | `SJMP 0107H` |
| 0102H | `78 55` | `MOV R0, #55H` |
| 0104H | `00` | `NOP` |
| 0105H | `00` | `NOP` |
| 0106H | `00` | `NOP` |
| 0107H | `E8` | `MOV A, R0` |

Determine the **final value of the Program Counter** after the `SJMP` instruction is executed. Justify whether the instruction at `0102H` will be executed. **(7 Marks)**

---

##### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

The **Program Counter** participates in the fetch-decode-execute cycle as follows: **(1 Mark for definition + 1 Mark for role)**

1. **Fetch Phase**: The CPU places the 16-bit PC content on the address bus, reads the opcode from program memory, and stores it in the Instruction Register.
2. **Decode Phase**: The decoder determines the instruction length and type.
3. **Increment Phase**: The PC is incremented by the instruction's byte length. **(1 Mark)**
4. **Execute Phase**: The instruction is carried out; for branches, the PC is modified during this phase.

**PC behaviour by instruction length (8051):**

| Instruction Size | Example | PC Increment |
|---|---|---|
| **1-byte** | `NOP`, `INC A` | $\text{PC} \leftarrow \text{PC} + 1$ **(1 Mark)** |
| **2-byte** | `MOV A, #data`, `SJMP rel` | $\text{PC} \leftarrow \text{PC} + 2$ **(1 Mark)** |
| **3-byte** | `LJMP addr16`, `MOV direct, #data` | $\text{PC} \leftarrow \text{PC} + 3$ **(1 Mark)** |

A neat block diagram (already provided in Section 4.1) supports the answer. **(1 Mark)**

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1:** Identify instruction and offset. **(1 Mark)**

The instruction at `0100H` is `SJMP 05H`. Since `SJMP` is a 2-byte instruction (opcode `80H` + offset `05H`):

$$
\text{PC after fetch} = 0100\text{H} + 2 = 0102\text{H}
$$

**Step 2:** Calculate branch target. **(2 Marks)**

For 8051 `SJMP`, the target is computed as:

$$
\text{PC}_{\text{target}} = \text{PC}_{\text{after fetch}} + \text{rel offset}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PC}_{\text{target}} &= 0102\text{H} + 0005\text{H} \\
&= 0107\text{H}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Final PC value after SJMP execution. (1 Mark)**

$$
\boxed{\text{PC}_{\text{final}} = 0107\text{H}}
$$

**Step 4: Will instruction at 0102H be executed? (3 Marks)**

**No.** The instruction at `0102H` (`MOV R0, #55H`) **will not be executed** because:

- The CPU fetches the `SJMP` instruction at `0100H` and `0101H`.
- After auto-increment, PC = `0102H`.
- The `SJMP` execution **replaces** the PC value with `0107H`.
- The CPU then fetches the next instruction from `0107H` (`MOV A, R0`), skipping the instructions at `0102H`, `0104H`, `0105H`, and `0106H`.

This demonstrates how the Program Counter implements **forward branching control flow** in assembly programming.

---
