#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Interrupt + Stack PC Behaviour*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1 — Alternative Choice]**

**a)** With a suitable diagram, explain how the **Program Counter is saved and restored** during the execution of a subroutine call (`LCALL`) and return (`RET`) in the 8051. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** Consider an 8051 system. An external interrupt `INT0` is triggered while the CPU is executing an instruction at address `2050H`. The Interrupt Service Routine (ISR) starts at vector address `0003H`. Show the **step-by-step modification of the PC and Stack Pointer (SP)** during this interrupt handling, assuming initial SP = `07H`. **(7 Marks)**

---

##### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Subroutine Call (`LCALL addr16`)** is a 3-byte instruction (1 opcode + 2 address bytes). **(1 Mark)**

**Sequence of PC events:**

1. CPU fetches the 3-byte `LCALL` instruction. PC auto-increments to point to the next instruction after `LCALL`. **(1 Mark)**
2. The return address (current PC value) is **pushed onto the stack** (low byte first, then high byte). **(2 Marks)**
3. The PC is loaded with the 16-bit target address of the subroutine. **(1 Mark)**

**Subroutine Return (`RET`):**

1. The high byte of PC is popped from the stack into the high byte of PC register.
2. The low byte of PC is popped from the stack into the low byte of PC register.
3. SP is incremented by 2. **(2 Marks)**

**Diagram Reference:** Section 4.2 of these notes illustrates this PC–Stack interaction clearly.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:**
- Current PC during interrupt = `2050H`
- Initial Stack Pointer SP = `07H`
- ISR vector address = `0003H` (8051 default for INT0)

**Step 1:** PC auto-increments after fetching the current instruction at `2050H`. **(1 Mark)**

$$
\text{PC}_{\text{after fetch}} = 2050\text{H} + \text{instruction length}
$$

For this problem, assume instruction length = 2 bytes (typical for 8051):

$$
\text{PC}_{\text{after fetch}} = 2052\text{H}
$$

**Step 2:** This value (return address) is pushed onto the stack. **(2 Marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Memory}[08\text{H}] &\leftarrow 52\text{H} \quad \text{(low byte of PC)} \\
\text{Memory}[09\text{H}] &\leftarrow 20\text{H} \quad \text{(high byte of PC)} \\
\text{SP}_{\text{new}} &\leftarrow 07\text{H} + 2 = 09\text{H}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** PC is loaded with the ISR vector address. **(2 Marks)**

$$
\text{PC}_{\text{new}} = 0003\text{H}
$$

**Step 4:** CPU begins executing ISR from `0003H`. **(1 Mark)**

**Step 5:** When the ISR completes and executes `RETI` (return from interrupt), the saved PC is popped back. **(1 Mark)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PC}_{\text{high}} &\leftarrow \text{Memory}[09\text{H}] = 20\text{H} \\
\text{PC}_{\text{low}} &\leftarrow \text{Memory}[08\text{H}] = 52\text{H} \\
\text{PC}_{\text{restored}} &= 2052\text{H} \\
\text{SP}_{\text{new}} &= 09\text{H} - 2 = 07\text{H}
\end{aligned}
$$

The CPU resumes execution at `2052H`, exactly where it left off.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Off-by-one error in `SJMP` target calculation:** Many students compute `PC + offset` instead of `(PC + instruction length) + offset`. The 8051 adds the offset to the **updated** PC (after fetch), not the original PC.
> 2. **Confusing `AJMP` vs `LJMP`:** `AJMP` is 2 KB page-restricted; `LJMP` is full 64 KB. Mixing them in calculations loses 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Ignoring PC auto-increment:** When asked "what is the final PC?", students forget to add the instruction byte length before applying branch offset.
> 4. **Stack push order:** The **low byte** of PC is pushed first, then the high byte. Reversing this order is a common mistake costing 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Interrupt vector addresses:** Memorize 8051 vector table — `0003H` (INT0), `000BH` (T0), `0013H` (INT1), `001BH` (T1), `0023H` (Serial).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Program Counter (KTU 2024):**
>
> - The **Program Counter (PC)** holds the address of the *next* instruction to be fetched. **(Core definition)**
> - PC is a **hardware register inside the CPU**, not in RAM or ROM.
> - PC width $n$ bits → maximum addressable memory = $2^{n}$ bytes.
> - **8051 PC = 16-bit** → 64 KB addressable program memory.
> - Three PC update modes: **Auto-increment, Direct load (branch/jump), Stack restore (RET/RETI).**
> - `SJMP` offset is **relative to PC after fetch**, not the original PC.
> - **Stack order on `CALL`**: Low byte pushed first, then high byte.
> - **Interrupt handling** = Hardware pushes current PC → Loads PC with ISR vector.
> - **Reset** = PC is loaded with `0000H` (in 8051, ARM Cortex-M, etc.).
> - PC value is the most important debugging metric — use IDE watch windows to inspect PC during single-stepping.
> - For variable-length instructions (8051), PC increment depends on the opcode just decoded.
> - PC overflow (`FFFFH + 1 = 0000H`) typically causes a system reset.
> - KTU frequently tests PC arithmetic for `SJMP rel`, `AJMP addr11`, `LJMP addr16`, and `LCALL addr16`.
> - Always state the **initial PC, instruction size, and final PC** in full in your exam answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stack Pointer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT403 - COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE AND MICROCONTROLLER
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Stack Pointer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Stack Pointer (SP)** in the **Intel 8051 microcontroller** is a **8-bit Special Function Register (SFR)** that holds the memory address of the **top of the stack** residing in the **Internal RAM (IRAM)**. The Stack Pointer is mapped at the SFR internal address **81H** and is therefore **byte-addressable** (not bit-addressable).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCMRT403 - Module 1):**
> The Stack Pointer is the *only* register in the 8051 architecture that directly manages subroutine linkage, interrupt servicing, and temporary data storage. Mastering its operation is **mandatory** for the ESE (End Semester Evaluation).

### 1.2 Physical & Default Constants

| Parameter | Value / Range |
|---|---|
| Register Width | **8 bits** |
| SFR Address | **81H** |
| Reset Value | **07H** |
| Stack Growth Direction | **Upward (Low → High Address)** |
| Allowed Stack Space | **08H to 7FH** (Standard 8051) |
| Max Stack Space (8052) | **08H to FFH** |

---

## 1.3 The Intuitive "Stack of Plates" Analogy 🍽️

Imagine a **spring-loaded plate dispenser** in a canteen:
- You can only **add or remove the topmost plate**.
- The dispenser has a **pointer (the spring)** that always tells you where the "top" plate is.
- When you **push** (PUSH) a new plate, the spring pointer **moves up by one slot first**, and the new plate is placed there.
- When you **pop** (POP) a plate, you take the top one, and the pointer **moves down by one slot**.

The 8051 Stack Pointer is exactly this "spring pointer":
- **PUSH** → SP first **increments** → then stores the byte.
- **POP** → reads the byte from [SP] → then SP **decrements**.
- This is a classic **LIFO (Last-In, First-Out)** data structure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial Architectural Quirk:** Most microprocessors (8085, 8086) have a stack that **grows downward**. The 8051 is one of the few architectures where the stack **grows UPWARD** (toward higher addresses). This is a classic **KTU Exam Trap** — students frequently answer it incorrectly!

---

## 1.4 Why Does SP Reset to 07H? 🤔

After a hardware **RESET**, the 8051 initializes the SP to **07H**. The reason is architectural:
- The first **8 bytes (00H–07H)** of Internal RAM are occupied by **Register Bank 0** (R0–R7).
- When the first `PUSH` instruction executes, SP becomes **08H** — exactly *above* the register banks, leaving user registers untouched.
- This is a clever **default safety mechanism** by Intel designers.

> [!TIP]
> If a program uses **Register Bank 1, 2, or 3** via the `PSW` register bits RS0 and RS1, the default SP=07H is *still safe* because the active register bank is selected by software, and the inactive banks can be used as stack space if needed. However, best practice is to **reinitialize the SP** at the start of every program.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Mechanics of the Stack Pointer

### 🔹 PUSH Operation (Two-Step Process)

When a `PUSH` instruction is executed (e.g., `PUSH ACC`):

1. **Increment Phase:** `SP ← SP + 1`
2. **Store Phase:** The byte from the source register is copied into the internal RAM at the new SP address: `[SP] ← Source_Register`

### 🔹 POP Operation (Two-Step Process)

When a `POP` instruction is executed (e.g., `POP ACC`):

1. **Read Phase:** The destination register is loaded from the address pointed to by SP: `Destination ← [SP]`
2. **Decrement Phase:** `SP ← SP - 1`

> [!NOTE]
> **Order Matters in Examinations!** In 8051, **PUSH** increments FIRST, while **POP** decrements LAST. This is the **opposite** of the 8085/8086 convention.

---

## 2.2 Internal RAM Memory Map of the 8051

Understanding the Stack requires a clear picture of where it lives in memory.

| Address Range (Hex) | Region | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **00H – 07H** | Register Bank 0 | R0–R7 (default active bank) |
| **08H – 0FH** | Register Bank 1 | R0–R7 (select via PSW) |
| **10H – 17H** | Register Bank 2 | R0–R7 (select via PSW) |
| **18H – 1FH** | Register Bank 3 | R0–R7 (select via PSW) |
| **20H – 2FH** | Bit-Addressable Area | 16 bytes → 128 addressable bits |
| **30H – 7FH** | General Purpose RAM | Scratchpad / Stack area |
| **80H – FFH** | SFRs (Standard 8051) | Special Function Registers |
| **80H – FFH** | Upper 128B RAM (8052 only) | Indirect addressing only |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The 8051 stack must reside **exclusively in Internal RAM**. It can never use External RAM because the SFR-based 8-bit SP register cannot address the 16-bit external data memory space.

---

## 2.3 Automatic Stack Modifications

The Stack Pointer is **implicitly modified** by the hardware (not by direct user code) during:

| Instruction | Effect on SP | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| `ACALL addr11` | SP +2 | Saves 16-bit PC return address |
| `LCALL addr16` | SP +2 | Saves 16-bit PC return address |
| `RET` / `RETI` | SP -2 | Restores 16-bit PC return address |
| `PUSH direct` | SP +1 | Saves a single byte on stack |
| `POP direct` | SP -1 | Restores a single byte from stack |
| Hardware Interrupt | SP +2 | CPU pushes PC onto stack automatically |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Each subroutine call (`CALL`, `ACALL`, `LCALL`) uses **2 bytes of stack** because the 8051's Program Counter (PC) is 16 bits wide. Interrupt service routines (ISRs) also use 2 bytes.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| S.No. | Formula / Relation | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `SP_after_PUSH = SP_before_PUSH + 1` | Increment-then-store rule |
| 2 | `SP_after_POP = SP_after_read - 1` | Read-then-decrement rule |
| 3 | `Stack_Size (bytes) = 0x7F - SP_initial` | For Standard 8051 |
| 4 | `Stack_Size (bytes) = 0xFF - SP_initial` | For 8052 variant |
| 5 | `SP_min_safe = 0x08 + 2N` | Skip N register banks (1 ≤ N ≤ 4) |
| 6 | `Max Nesting Depth = Stack_Size ÷ 2` | Since each CALL uses 2 bytes |
| 7 | `Available_Stack = (0x7F - SP) + 1` bytes | Inclusive boundary formula |

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example for Formula 6:** If `MOV SP, #5FH` is executed, then `Stack_Size = 0x7F - 0x5F = 0x20 = 32 bytes`. Since each `LCALL` consumes 2 bytes, **Maximum Nesting Depth = 32 ÷ 2 = 16 nested subroutines**.

---

## 2.5 Relocating the Stack — The Software Override

The default stack area (08H–7FH) often conflicts with the **bit-addressable area (20H–2FH)** or user-defined variables. Hence, the 8051 allows software relocation of the stack using a single instruction:

```assembly
        MOV     SP, #VALUE    ; VALUE is an 8-bit immediate
```

### Recommended SP Initializations:

| Scenario | Recommended `MOV SP, #` | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Use full lower RAM, no register banks reserved | `#0x2F` (47 decimal) | Skips bit-addressable area |
| 2 register banks used, want safe area | `#0x1F` (31 decimal) | Skips RB0, RB1, bit area |
| All 4 register banks used | `#0x2F` (47 decimal) | Skips all RBs + bit area |
| Maximum safe stack | `#0x5F` (95 decimal) | Leaves ~32 bytes for variables |
| Maximum stack size (standard 8051) | `#0x60` (96 decimal) | Stack: 60H–7FH = 32 bytes |
| **NEVER USE** | `#0x07` (default) | Conflicts with Register Bank 0 |
| **DANGEROUS** | `#0x80` and above | SFR area, undefined behavior |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **embedded systems engineering**, the Stack Pointer is used for:
1. **Function call nesting** in C compilers (e.g., Keil µVision, SDCC).
2. **Interrupt latency calculation** — every ISR consumes at least 2 stack bytes for the return PC, plus any registers it pushes.
3. **Context saving** during task switching in **RTOS** environments (FreeRTOS, RTX51).
4. **Stack overflow detection** in safety-critical automotive and aerospace firmware (MISRA-C compliance).
5. **Compiler-generated local variables** in C are stored on the stack by Keil C51.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Trace: PUSH and POP Sequence

Let us analyze a **complete PUSH–POP cycle** with an actual 8051 program segment.

**Given Conditions:**
- Initial value of `ACC = AAH` (Hex)
- Initial value of `SP = 07H`
- Initial Internal RAM addresses `08H` to `7FH` all contain `00H` (assumed).

### Step-by-Step Execution of `PUSH ACC`:

**Step 1 — Instruction Fetch & Decode:**
The CPU fetches opcode `E0` (or `C0` depending on assembler) for `PUSH ACC` and recognizes it as a PUSH-direct instruction with direct address `E0H`.

**Step 2 — Increment the Stack Pointer:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
SP_{\text{new}} &= SP_{\text{old}} + 1 \\
SP_{\text{new}} &= 07H + 1 \\
SP_{\text{new}} &= 08H
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Write ACC to Internal RAM at the new SP:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{IRAM}[SP_{\text{new}}] &= ACC \\
\text{IRAM}[08H] &= AAH
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result After PUSH:** `SP = 08H`, `IRAM[08H] = AAH`.

---

### Step-by-Step Execution of `POP ACC`:

**Step 1 — Read from Internal RAM at current SP:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
ACC &= \text{IRAM}[SP] \\
ACC &= \text{IRAM}[08H] \\
ACC &= AAH
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Decrement the Stack Pointer:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
SP_{\text{new}} &= SP_{\text{old}} - 1 \\
SP_{\text{new}} &= 08H - 1 \\
SP_{\text{new}} &= 07H
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result After POP:** `SP = 07H`, `ACC = AAH` (unchanged, as expected).

> [!NOTE]
> Notice that the **value at IRAM[08H] is still AAH** even after the POP. POP does **not erase** the data — it merely decrements the pointer. The old data becomes **garbage** that will be overwritten by the next PUSH.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Stack Evolution During Nested Calls

**Program:**
```assembly
        MOV     SP, #5FH        ; SP = 5FH (95 decimal)
        ACALL   SUB1            ; Stack uses 2 bytes
SUB1:   ACALL   SUB2            ; Stack uses 2 more bytes
SUB2:   ACALL   SUB3            ; Stack uses 2 more bytes
SUB3:   RET
        RET
        RET
```

### Stack Pointer Evolution Table:

| Instruction Executed | SP Value (Hex) | SP Value (Dec) | IRAM[SP+1] | IRAM[SP] | Description |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| After `MOV SP,#5FH` | 5FH | 95 | 00 | 00 | Initial setup |
| After `ACALL SUB1` | 61H | 97 | High byte of PC | Low byte of PC | Saved PC for SUB1 |
| After `ACALL SUB2` | 63H | 99 | High byte of PC | Low byte of PC | Saved PC for SUB2 |
| After `ACALL SUB3` | 65H | 101 | High byte of PC | Low byte of PC | Saved PC for SUB3 |
| After `RET` in SUB3 | 63H | 99 | Garbage | Garbage | Restored PC from stack |
| After `RET` in SUB2 | 61H | 97 | Garbage | Garbage | Restored PC from stack |
| After `RET` in SUB1 | 5FH | 95 | Garbage | Garbage | Restored PC from stack |

### Key Observations from the Matrix:
1. **Each `ACALL` advances SP by 2** (not 1), because the 16-bit PC is pushed as two separate bytes.
2. The **high byte of PC is pushed first** (PCH), then the **low byte** (PCL). So `[SP] = PCL` and `[SP-1] = PCH` after the call.
3. **Stack Overflow Boundary:** If SP ever exceeds `7FH` in a standard 8051, it wraps around to `00H` due to 8-bit rollover, causing **catastrophic program failure**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Maximum Nesting Depth Formula

We want to find the maximum number of nested subroutine calls that can be safely executed.

**Let:**
- $S$ = Initial Stack Pointer value (Hex)
- $S_{\max} = 0x7F$ (upper limit for standard 8051)
- $N$ = Maximum nesting depth (number of nested calls)

**Available stack space (in bytes):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Available} &= S_{\max} - S + 1 \\
\text{Available} &= 0x7F - S + 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Bytes consumed per call:**
Each `LCALL`, `ACALL`, or interrupt consumes exactly **2 bytes** of stack.

**Maximum nesting depth:**
$$
N_{\max} = \left\lfloor \frac{\text{Available}}{2} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{0x7F - S + 1}{2} \right\rfloor
$$

**Worked Example:**
For `S = 0x5F`:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Available} &= 0x7F - 0x5F + 1 = 0x21 = 33 \text{ bytes} \\
N_{\max} &= \left\lfloor \frac{33}{2} \right\rfloor = 16 \text{ nested calls}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> Always reserve **at least 4–8 extra bytes** in your calculation for safety. In C programs using Keil C51, local variables also consume stack space, so the effective depth is much less than the theoretical maximum.

---

## 3.4 Special Edge Case — Stack Pointer Rollover

The 8051's SP is an **8-bit register** with no overflow flag. If SP increments past `0xFF`, it wraps to `0x00`. If it decrements past `0x00`, it wraps to `0xFF`. The CPU does **not** generate any error or trap.

**Implication:** Programmers must ensure that:
- The initial SP is set such that the maximum anticipated stack usage does not exceed `0x7F`.
- No code path causes SP to decrement below the valid RAM area.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Position of Stack Pointer in 8051 Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CPU["8051 CPU Core"]
        ALU["ALU (8-bit)"]
        ACC["ACC (Accumulator) - SFR E0H"]
        BREG["B Register - SFR F0H"]
        PSW["PSW - SFR D0H"]
        PC["PC (16-bit Program Counter)"]
        DPTR["DPTR (16-bit Data Pointer)"]
    end

    subgraph SFRBus["SFR Address Space (80H-FFH)"]
        SPreg["STACK POINTER\nSFR Address: 81H\nReset Value: 07H\nWidth: 8 bits"]
    end

    subgraph IRAM["Internal RAM (00H-7FH)"]
        RB0["00H-07H\nRegister Bank 0"]
        RB1["08H-0FH\nRegister Bank 1"]
        RB2["10H-17H\nRegister Bank 2"]
        RB3["18H-1FH\nRegister Bank 3"]
        BIT["20H-2FH\nBit-Addressable"]
        GEN["30H-7FH\nGeneral Purpose / Stack"]
        STACK["Active Stack Region\n(Initial: 08H, Max: 7FH)"]
    end

    PC -->|"Push 16-bit PC\nvia SP"| SPreg
    SPreg -->|"Points to Top-of-Stack"| STACK
    STACK -->|"Read/Write Bytes"| GEN
    SPreg -.->|"| IRAM
```

> [!NOTE]
> The dashed arrow represents the **logical relationship** — the SP register contains a *pointer* (an address) into Internal RAM. The solid arrows show the **physical data movement** of bytes being pushed onto the stack.

---

## 4.2 Flowchart — PUSH and POP Operation Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startPUSH["Instruction: PUSH ACC"] --> stepP1["Step 1: Increment SP"]
    stepP1 --> stepP2["Step 2: SP becomes SP+1"]
    stepP2 --> stepP3{"Check: SP > 7FH?"}
    stepP3 -->|"Yes"| overflow["STACK OVERFLOW!\nUndefined Behavior"]
    stepP3 -->|"No"| stepP4["Step 3: Write ACC to IRAM at new SP"]
    stepP4 --> donePUSH["PUSH Complete"]

    startPOP["Instruction: POP ACC"] --> stepO1["Step 1: Read IRAM at current SP"]
    stepO1 --> stepO2["Step 2: Load value into ACC"]
    stepO2 --> stepO3["Step 3: Decrement SP"]
    stepO3 --> stepO4{"Check: SP < 08H?"}
    stepO4 -->|"Yes"| underflow["STACK UNDERFLOW!\nGarbage Data Loaded"]
    stepO4 -->|"No"| donePOP["POP Complete"]

    donePUSH --> idle["CPU Fetches Next Instruction"]
    donePOP --> idle
```

---

## 4.3 Memory Map Visualization — Stack Growth Pattern

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LOWER["Lower Internal RAM (Reserved)"]
        A["00H\nRB0 R0"]
        B["08H\nRB1 R0"]
        C["10H\nRB2 R0"]
        D["18H\nRB3 R0"]
        E["20H\nBit-Addr"]
    end

    subgraph STACKAREA["Stack Region (User Configurable)"]
        S1["30H\n(empty)"]
        S2["SP → 5FH\n(Top of Stack)"]
        S3["60H\n(future pushes)"]
        S4["7FH\n(Max boundary)"]
    end

    B --> S1
    E --> S1
    S1 --> S2
    S2 -.->|"PUSH grows UP"| S3
    S3 -.->|"PUSH grows UP"| S4
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Observe the **upward arrow direction** in the stack region. This is the **defining characteristic** of the 8051 stack and is the most heavily tested concept in KTU examinations.

---

## 4.4 Block-Level Architecture: Stack Interaction with CALL/RET

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    mainProg["Main Program\nCounter = XXXXH"] -->|"1. LCALL SUB1\n(SP+2)"| savePC1["Save PC Low Byte\nIRAM at SP"]
    savePC1 --> savePC2["Save PC High Byte\nIRAM at SP-1"]
    savePC2 --> execSub1["Execute SUB1"]
    execSub1 -->|"2. RET\n(SP-2)"| loadPC1["Load PC Low Byte\nfrom IRAM at SP"]
    loadPC1 --> loadPC2["Load PC High Byte\nfrom IRAM at SP-1"]
    loadPC2 --> resumeMain["Resume Main Program\nCounter = XXXXH"]
```

This block architecture illustrates that the **Stack Pointer is the silent orchestrator** behind every function call in 8051 assembly, C, or any high-level language compiled to 8051 machine code.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the Stack Pointer register of 8051 microcontroller. Why is it initialized to 07H after reset?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Stack Pointer (SP)** is an **8-bit Special Function Register** in the 8051, internally addressed at **SFR location 81H**. It holds the address of the topmost element of the stack, which resides in the **Internal RAM**.

**Key Characteristics:**
- It is an **8-bit register**, hence can address only **Internal RAM** (00H to FFH).
- The stack in 8051 **grows upward** (from lower to higher addresses).
- It is **modified automatically** by instructions such as PUSH, POP, CALL, RET, RETI, and by hardware interrupts.

**Why Reset Value is 07H:**
After a hardware reset, the CPU initializes SP to **07H** so that the **first PUSH operation** places data at address **08H**. This protects the **Register Bank 0 (addresses 00H–07H)** from being overwritten, giving the user a safe default stack region starting from 08H onwards.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between PUSH and POP instructions of 8051 in terms of their effect on the Stack Pointer.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | PUSH Instruction | POP Instruction |
|---|---|---|
| Operation | Writes a byte onto the stack | Reads a byte from the stack |
| SP Modification | **Increments SP first**, then stores | **Reads first**, then **decrements SP** |
| Order of Steps | `SP ← SP + 1` ; `[SP] ← Source` | `Dest ← [SP]` ; `SP ← SP - 1` |
| Stack Effect | Stack **grows upward** | Stack **shrinks downward** |
| Use Case | Saving registers before a task | Restoring registers after a task |

The crucial difference is the **order** in which SP is modified. PUSH modifies SP *before* the data write, whereas POP modifies SP *after* the data read. This asymmetry is unique to the 8051 architecture.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Explain the following with respect to 8051 microcontroller:**
**(a)** Internal RAM organization and the role of Stack Pointer in it. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** With a neat diagram, describe the operation of PUSH and POP instructions. Show the effect on SP if `SP = 07H` initially and the sequence `PUSH ACC; PUSH B; POP ACC; PUSH PSW` is executed, given `ACC = 0x55`, `B = 0xAA`, `PSW = 0x00`. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Internal RAM Organization and Stack Pointer:**

The 8051 has **128 bytes of Internal RAM (IRAM)** from address **00H to 7FH**, organized as:

- **00H–07H:** Register Bank 0 (default R0–R7)
- **08H–0FH:** Register Bank 1
- **10H–17H:** Register Bank 2
- **18H–1FH:** Register Bank 3
- **20H–2FH:** Bit-Addressable area (16 bytes = 128 bits)
- **30H–7FH:** General purpose RAM / Stack area

**Role of Stack Pointer:**
- SP is the 8-bit SFR at address **81H**.
- It always points to the **top of the stack** in IRAM.
- The stack area is from the current SP value to 7FH.
- It is used by PUSH, POP, CALL, RET, RETI, and interrupt hardware.

**Part (b) — Trace of PUSH and POP Sequence:**

| Step | Instruction | SP (before) | Action | SP (after) | IRAM @ SP |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | (Initial) | — | — | 07H | — |
| 2 | `PUSH ACC` | 07H | SP ← 08H; IRAM[08H] ← 55H | 08H | 55H |
| 3 | `PUSH B` | 08H | SP ← 09H; IRAM[09H] ← AAH | 09H | AAH |
| 4 | `POP ACC` | 09H | ACC ← IRAM[09H] = AAH; SP ← 08H | 08H | AAH |
| 5 | `PUSH PSW` | 08H | SP ← 09H; IRAM[09H] ← 00H | 09H | 00H |

**Final State:** `SP = 09H`, `ACC = AAH`, `B = AAH` (unchanged), `PSW = 00H` (unchanged). The IRAM location 08H still contains the old value 55H, which is now **invalid stack data**.

**Diagram of PUSH Operation:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    s1["SP = 07H, IRAM[08H] = ?? "] --> s2["PUSH ACC: Step 1: SP = SP+1 = 08H"]
    s2 --> s3["Step 2: IRAM[08H] = ACC = 55H"]
    s3 --> s4["Final: SP = 08H, IRAM[08H] = 55H"]
```

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** What is the significance of the Stack Pointer in 8051? Explain how you would relocate the stack to the top of Internal RAM. What would be the available stack size? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A programmer writes the following 8051 assembly. If initially `SP = 5FH`, `ACC = 33H`, `R0 = 22H`, determine the final values of `SP`, `ACC`, `R0`, and the contents of Internal RAM from 5FH to 65H, after execution. Also calculate the maximum number of nested subroutine calls possible. **(7 marks)**

```assembly
        MOV     SP, #5FH
        PUSH    ACC
        PUSH    00H         ; Push R0 (direct address 00H)
        POP     01H         ; Pop into R1 (direct address 01H)
        POP     ACC
        LCALL   DELAY
DELAY:  RET
```

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Significance and Relocation:**

The Stack Pointer is significant because it:
1. Manages the **LIFO data structure** used for temporary storage.
2. Supports **subroutine linkage** (LCALL, ACALL, RET).
3. Handles **hardware interrupt** execution context (saves PC).
4. Enables **register preservation** (PUSH/POP) across code segments.

**Relocation to top of Internal RAM:**
The maximum safe address is **7FH** for standard 8051. To relocate:
```assembly
        MOV     SP, #7FH    ; Stack top is now at 7FH
```

**Available stack size:**
$$
\text{Stack\_Size} = 0x7F - 0x7F + 1 = 1 \text{ byte (inadequate)}
$$

A more practical relocation that still uses the upper region:
```assembly
        MOV     SP, #60H    ; Stack: 60H to 7FH
```
$$
\text{Stack\_Size} = 0x7F - 0x60 + 1 = 0x20 = 32 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Part (b) — Trace Execution:**

| Step | Instruction | SP | IRAM[60H] | IRAM[61H] | IRAM[62H] | IRAM[63H] | IRAM[64H] | IRAM[65H] | ACC | R0 | R1 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Initial | 5FH | ?? | ?? | ?? | ?? | ?? | ?? | 33H | 22H | ?? |
| 2 | `MOV SP,#5FH` | 5FH | ?? | ?? | ?? | ?? | ?? | ?? | 33H | 22H | ?? |
| 3 | `PUSH ACC` | 60H | 33H | ?? | ?? | ?? | ?? | ?? | 33H | 22H | ?? |
| 4 | `PUSH 00H` | 61H | 33H | 22H | ?? | ?? | ?? | ?? | 33H | 22H | ?? |
| 5 | `POP 01H` | 60H | 33H | 22H | ?? | ?? | ?? | ?? | 33H | 22H | 22H |
| 6 | `POP ACC` | 5FH | 33H | 22H | ?? | ?? | ?? | ?? | 22H | 22H | 22H |
| 7 | `LCALL DELAY` | 61H | 33H | 22H | PCH | PCL | ?? | ?? | 22H | 22H | 22H |
| 8 | `RET` (in DELAY) | 5FH | 33H | 22H | PCH | PCL | ?? | ?? | 22H | 22H | 22H |

**Final Values:**
- `SP = 5FH`
- `ACC = 22H` (popped value of old R0)
- `R0 = 22H` (unchanged)
- `R1 = 22H` (popped value of old R0)
- `IRAM[60H] = 33H`, `IRAM[61H] = 22H`, `IRAM[62H] = PCH`, `IRAM[63H] = PCL`, `IRAM[64H] = ??`, `IRAM[65H] = ??`

**Maximum Nested Calls:**
$$
N_{\max} = \left\lfloor \frac{0x7F - 0x5F + 1}{2} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{33}{2} \right\rfloor = 16 \text{ nested calls}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Direction of Stack Growth:** Students frequently state that the 8051 stack "grows downward." **This is WRONG.** The 8051 stack grows **upward** (toward higher addresses). This single error costs 1–2 marks in both Part A and Part B questions.
> 2. **Order of PUSH/POP Operations:** Forgetting that PUSH increments *first* and POP decrements *after* is a classic mistake. Always show the **two-step** mechanism explicitly in the trace table.
> 3. **Stack Size Calculation Boundary:** Use `0x7F - SP + 1` (inclusive) — students often use `0x7F - SP` (exclusive), losing 1 mark.
> 4. **Forgetting the 2-byte nature of CALL/RET:** Each LCALL/ACALL consumes **2 stack bytes**, not 1. This halves the maximum nesting depth.
> 5. **Confusing SFR Address with Reset Value:** The SFR address of SP is **81H**, but the **reset value** is **07H**. These are two different numbers; mixing them up will cost marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌

### 🟢 Rapid Revision Checklist

- ✅ Stack Pointer is an **8-bit SFR at address 81H** in the 8051.
- ✅ **Reset value of SP is 07H** (Intel's design choice to protect Register Bank 0).
- ✅ The 8051 stack **grows UPWARD** (toward higher addresses) — **contrary to 8085/8086**.
- ✅ **PUSH operation:** Increment SP first, *then* write the byte → `SP ← SP + 1; [SP] ← src`.
- ✅ **POP operation:** Read the byte first, *then* decrement SP → `dest ← [SP]; SP ← SP - 1`.
- ✅ Stack resides **only in Internal RAM** (00H–7FH for standard 8051).
- ✅ The stack is **software-repositionable** using `MOV SP, #VALUE`.
- ✅ Each `LCALL`/`ACALL`/interrupt pushes **2 bytes** (16-bit PC).
- ✅ `RET` and `RETI` pop **2 bytes** to restore the PC.
- ✅ **Maximum nesting depth** = `(0x7F - SP_{initial} + 1) ÷ 2$`.
- ✅ **No stack overflow detection** hardware exists — programmer must ensure SP stays within 08H–7FH.
- ✅ The **bit-addressable area (20H–2FH)** should be avoided for stack operations.
- ✅ **Recommended safe initialization:** `MOV SP, #5FH` for moderate stack use.
- ✅ When using multiple register banks, set SP to at least `#1FH` or higher.
- ✅ **No direct external RAM stack** — the 8-bit SP cannot generate 16-bit external addresses.
- ✅ In C programs (Keil C51), the **startup code (.cstartup)** typically initializes SP via `?_STARTUP` code.
- ✅ SP is **byte-addressable only**; it is **NOT** bit-addressable.
- ✅ A POP does **NOT** clear the memory location; it just decrements SP.
- ✅ The 8052 variant extends the stack area up to **FFH** (256 bytes of IRAM).

### 🎯 High-Yield Mnemonics for Exam

> **"PUSH = P**lus **1** then **S**tore"**
> **"POP = P**ull **O**ut, then **P**lus **−1"** (Read, then decrement)**

> **"Up, Up, Up!"** — Always remember the 8051 stack grows **UP**!
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: General Purpose Registers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMRT403 - COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE AND MICROCONTROLLER > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** General Purpose Registers # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the **Intel 8086 microprocessor** (the canonical 16-bit processor studied under KTU PCMRT403 Module 1), **General Purpose Registers (GPRs)** are a set of high-speed, on-chip storage locations inside the CPU used by the programmer for arithmetic, logical, data-movement, and addressing operations. The 8086 contains **four 16-bit general purpose registers**, each of which can be independently accessed as two 8-bit halves: $$ \text{8086 General Purpose Registers} = \{AX, BX, CX, DX\} $$ Each of these registers is **bidirectionally splittable** into an upper (High) byte and a lower (Low) byte, giving 8 programmable 8-bit registers in total (AH, AL, BH, BL, CH, CL, DH, DL). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCMRT403 – Module 1):** The 8086 architecture specifies exactly **14 × 16-bit registers**, grouped into four categories. The *General Purpose* category refers strictly to **AX, BX, CX, DX** — NOT the segment or pointer registers. Many students lose marks by misclassifying SI, DI, BP, or SP as "general purpose". ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition Imagine a **carpenter's workbench** with four labelled tool trays: a calculator tray, a measuring scale tray, a counter tray, and a data tray. The carpenter doesn't run to the storeroom for every small job; instead, he keeps his **most frequently used tools within arm's reach** for instant access. In the CPU: - The **workbench** = the **Register File** (inside the processor, operating in **nanoseconds**). - The **tool trays** = the four **General Purpose Registers (AX, BX, CX, DX)**. - The **storeroom** = **RAM/External Memory** (access time is **~100× slower**). The CPU prefers registers because they are built from the **fastest flip-flops** available inside the silicon die. Every instruction that uses a register executes dramatically faster than one that fetches data from memory — this is the **Von Neumann / Register-Operand Principle**. ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics | Property | Value / Specification | | :--- | :--- | | Total 8086 Registers | **14 × 16-bit** | | General Purpose Registers | **4 × 16-bit** (AX, BX, CX, DX) | | Splittable into 8-bit halves | **Yes** (H and L for each) | | Typical Access Time | **~ a few nanoseconds** (on-chip) | | Register Width (Data path) | **16 bits** | | Data Bus Width | **16 bits** | | Address Bus Width | **20 bits** | > [!NOTE] > The terms "**H**" and "**L**" in AH, AL stand for **High-order byte** (bits D15–D8) and **Low-order byte** (bits D7–D0) of the 16-bit parent register. For example, AH occupies the upper byte of AX, while AL occupies the lower byte. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Four General Purpose Registers — Operational Roles Although the name "General Purpose" suggests *symmetrical* usability, the 8086 designers **specialized** each register for specific instruction classes. This specialization is a **high-frequency KTU question** topic. ### 2.1.1 AX — Accumulator Register - **Primary role:** Central hub for all **arithmetic** and **logic** operations (ADD, SUB, AND, OR, XOR, CMP). - **Mandatory use** in **I/O operations** (`IN` and `OUT` instructions) and **string operations** (`MOVS`, `STOS`, `LODS`). - **Default operand** for many arithmetic instructions — the result is stored in AX by default. - The 8086 is sometimes called an **"Accumulator-Based Architecture"** because of AX's central role. ### 2.1.2 BX — Base Register - **Primary role:** Acts as a **base pointer** for memory access. - **Unique feature:** It is the **only** general purpose register that can be used as a **memory pointer** in the form `[BX]` without an override prefix. - Holds the **offset address** within the Data Segment (DS by default). - Example: `MOV AX, [BX]` — loads data from memory at offset BX into AX. ### 2.1.3 CX — Count Register - **Primary role:** Functions as an **implicit counter** for **LOOP**, **shift/rotate**, and **string** instructions. - The `LOOP` instruction automatically decrements CX and checks for zero. - For `SHL`, `SHR`, `ROL`, `ROR`, the shift count must be placed in **CL**. - For string operations, CX holds the **count of bytes/words** to process. ### 2.1.4 DX — Data Register - **Primary role:** Used to **extend** the accumulator for `MUL` and `DIV` (32-bit results in DX:AX). - **Mandatory use** for **I/O port addresses** greater than 255 in the `IN` and `OUT` instructions. - Example: `IN AL, DX` — read from I/O port whose address is in DX. ## 2.2 8-Bit Splitting Logic Each 16-bit register `R` where `R ∈ {A, B, C, D}` can be decomposed as: $$ R[15:0] = \{ RH[15:8] \;\Vert\; RL[7:0] \} $$ Where `RH` is the high byte and `RL` is the low byte. Concretely: - `AX` splits into `AH` (high) and `AL` (low) - `BX` splits into `BH` and `BL` - `CX` splits into `CH` and `CL` - `DX` splits into `DH` and `DL` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Point:** Writing to `AH` does NOT alter `AL`, and vice versa. However, the 16-bit parent register reflects both halves. For example, if `AH = 0x12` and `AL = 0x34`, then `AX = 0x1234`. This independent access is what allows 8-bit I/O and ASCII processing on a 16-bit CPU. ## 2.3 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet | Register | 16-bit Name | 8-bit High | 8-bit Low | Primary Special Function | KTU Frequent Ask | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Accumulator | **AX** | AH | AL | Arithmetic, I/O, String | "Default operand for MUL/DIV?" | | Base | **BX** | BH | BL | Memory pointer `[BX]` | "Only GPR usable as address?" | | Counter | **CX** | CH | CL | LOOP counter, shift count | "Where to keep loop count?" | | Data | **DX** | DH | DL | MUL/DIV extend, I/O port > FFh | "Holds high word of 32-bit product?" | > [!NOTE] > **Real-world Engineering Utility:** Modern x86 architectures (8086 → 8088 → 80186 → 80286 → 80386 → Pentium → Core i7) have **preserved and extended** these four registers. In 32-bit mode, they were widened to **EAX, EBX, ECX, EDX**; in 64-bit mode, to **RAX, RBX, RCX, RDX**. Understanding the 8086 GPRs is therefore the **foundational gateway** to all modern Intel/AMD assembly programming. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Number of Independent 8-Bit Registers Obtainable **Given:** The 8086 has 4 general purpose 16-bit registers, each splittable into two 8-bit halves. **Step 1:** Identify the number of 16-bit registers in the GPR group. $$ N_{16} = 4 \quad \text{(AX, BX, CX, DX)} $$ **Step 2:** For each 16-bit register, there are exactly 2 independently addressable 8-bit halves (High and Low). $$ N_{\text{halves per register}} = 2 $$ **Step 3:** Compute the total number of 8-bit registers that can be addressed by the programmer. $$ N_{8} = N_{16} \times N_{\text{halves per register}} = 4 \times 2 = 8 $$ **Step 4:** List the resulting 8-bit registers for verification. $$ N_{8} = \{AH, AL, BH, BL, CH, CL, DH, DL\} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \vert N_{8} \vert = 8 \;\checkmark $$ **Conclusion:** The four 16-bit GPRs expose **8 independently addressable 8-bit registers** to the programmer. This 2:1 splitting is the cornerstone of 8-bit/16-bit compatibility in the 8086. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Effective Address Calculation Using BX **Given:** The instruction `MOV AX, [BX]` with `DS = 2000h` and `BX = 0450h`. **Step 1:** Recall the 8086 **20-bit physical address formula**. $$ \text{Physical Address} = (\text{Segment Register} \times 16) + \text{Offset} $$ **Step 2:** Identify each parameter. For `[BX]`, the default segment is **DS** (Data Segment) and the offset is the value inside **BX**. $$ \text{Segment} = DS = 2000h, \quad \text{Offset} = BX = 0450h $$ **Step 3:** Compute the segment base by shifting DS left by 4 bits (multiplying by 16 = 0x10). $$ \text{Segment Base} = 2000h \times 10h $$ $$ \text{Segment Base} = 20000h $$ **Step 4:** Add the offset to the segment base to obtain the physical address. $$ \text{Physical Address} = 20000h + 0450h $$ $$ \text{Physical Address} = 20450h $$ **Step 5:** Conclude the physical memory location being accessed. $$ \boxed{\text{Physical Address} = 20450h} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This derivation is a **classic 14-mark KTU question** (Part B). The student MUST show both the shift operation and the addition, and explicitly state the default segment pairing rule (BX ↔ DS, BP ↔ SS). --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: When Each GPR is Implicitly Used | Instruction Class | Implicit Register | Direction of Use | KTU-Critical? | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | `MUL src` (16-bit) | **AX** and **DX** | AX = multiplier, DX:AX = 32-bit product | Yes | | `DIV src` (16-bit) | **AX** and **DX** | DX:AX = dividend, quotient → AX, remainder → DX | Yes | | `IN AL, port` | **DX** (if port > FFh) | DX holds 8-bit port address | Yes | | `LOOP label` | **CX** | CX is decremented; loop ends if CX = 0 | Yes | | `REP MOVSB` | **CX** | CX = number of bytes to move | Yes | | `SHL dest, CL` | **CL** | CL holds shift count (0–255) | Yes | | `MOV AX, [BX]` | **BX** | BX is base offset within DS | Yes | | `XLAT` (translate) | **BX, AL** | BX = table base, AL = index | Common | | `CWD` (convert word to doubleword) | **AX → DX** | Sign-extends AX into DX | Common | > [!NOTE] > **Explanatory row for `MUL`:** When we say "DX:AX holds the 32-bit product", we mean the **high 16 bits** of the product are stored in **DX** and the **low 16 bits** are stored in **AX**. This is why DX's role as a "Data" register is essential — it is the **extension accumulator**. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — The 8086 Register File (GPR Subset) ```mermaid graph TD A["8086 REGISTER FILE
14 x 16-bit Registers"] --> B["GENERAL PURPOSE
4 x 16-bit"] A --> C["SEGMENT REGISTERS
4 x 16-bit"] A --> D["POINTER and INDEX
4 x 16-bit"] A --> E["SPECIAL REGISTERS
2 x 16-bit"] B --> B1["AX Accumulator"] B --> B2["BX Base"] B --> B3["CX Counter"] B --> B4["DX Data"] B1 --> B1H["AH High Byte"] B1 --> B1L["AL Low Byte"] B2 --> B2H["BH High Byte"] B2 --> B2L["BL Low Byte"] B3 --> B3H["CH High Byte"] B3 --> B3L["CL Low Byte"] B4 --> B4H["DH High Byte"] B4 --> B4L["DL Low Byte"] C --> C1["CS Code Segment"] C --> C2["DS Data Segment"] C --> C3["SS Stack Segment"] C --> C4["ES Extra Segment"] D --> D1["SP Stack Pointer"] D --> D2["BP Base Pointer"] D --> D3["SI Source Index"] D --> D4["DI Destination Index"] E --> E1["IP Instruction Pointer"] E --> E2["FLAGS Status Register"] ``` ## 4.2 Functional Flow — How a Typical Instruction Uses the GPRs ```mermaid graph LR stepA["STEP 1
Fetch Instruction
from CS:IP"] --> stepB["STEP 2
Decode by BIU"] stepB --> stepC["STEP 3
Read Operands
from Register File"] stepC --> stepD{"STEP 4
Which GPR?"} stepD -->|Arithmetic| stepE["Use AX
ALU executes
Result to AX"] stepD -->|Memory Ptr| stepF["Use BX
Form EA = DS x 16 + BX"] stepD -->|Loop/Count| stepG["Use CX
Decrement CX
Check Zero Flag"] stepD -->|Multiply| stepH["Use AX and DX
DX:AX holds result"] stepE --> stepI["STEP 5
Write Back to Register"] stepF --> stepI stepG --> stepI stepH --> stepI stepI --> stepJ["STEP 6
Update Flags Register"] ``` ## 4.3 Visual Map of Register Specialization ```mermaid graph TD R["8086 General Purpose Registers"] --> role1["AX - Accumulator
Arithmetic Hub"] R --> role2["BX - Base
Memory Pointer"] R --> role3["CX - Counter
Loop and Repeat"] R --> role4["DX - Data
I/O Port and Extend"] role1 --> use1a["ADD, SUB, MUL, DIV, AND, OR"] role1 --> use1b["IN, OUT (8/16 bit)"] role1 --> use1c["String Instructions"] role2 --> use2a["MOV AX, BX"] role2 --> use2b["Default segment DS"] role2 --> use2c["Only GPR as offset ptr"] role3 --> use3a["LOOP, LOOPE, LOOPNE"] role3 --> use3b["REP prefix family"] role3 --> use3c["SHL, SHR, ROR, ROL"] role4 --> use4a["MUL: DX AX holds product"] role4 --> use4b["DIV: DX holds remainder"] role4 --> use4c["IN OUT with port > FFh"] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) **`[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`** List the four general purpose registers of the 8086 microprocessor. Mention the specific role of the CX register. **Model Answer:** The four 16-bit general purpose registers of the 8086 are **AX, BX, CX, and DX**. Each of these can be split into two 8-bit halves (H and L). The **CX (Counter) register** is used as an **implicit counter** for: - The `LOOP`, `LOOPE`, and `LOOPNE` instructions, where CX is automatically decremented. - The `REP` family of string instruction prefixes. - Shift and rotate instructions, where **CL** holds the shift count. --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) **`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`** Explain how the 8086 general purpose registers can be used as 8-bit registers. Give an example. **Model Answer:** Each 16-bit general purpose register (AX, BX, CX, DX) can be **independently split** into a high-order 8-bit half (e.g., AH) and a low-order 8-bit half (e.g., AL). This gives **eight** addressable 8-bit registers: **AH, AL, BH, BL, CH, CL, DH, DL**. For example, if `AX = 1234h`, then `AH = 12h` and `AL = 34h`. Writing to AH does not affect AL and vice versa, which allows 8-bit operations on a 16-bit processor. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each) ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper Pattern]` **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the general purpose register organization of the 8086 microprocessor. List the special function of each register. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain the role of BX and DX registers with suitable assembly language examples. How does the physical address get computed when BX is used as a base pointer? **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question A(a) — 7 Marks The 8086 microprocessor has **four 16-bit general purpose registers**: AX, BX, CX, and DX. Each can be split into two 8-bit halves. The diagram (refer to SECTION 4.1 above) shows the structure. **Special functions:** 1. **AX (Accumulator):** Default register for arithmetic (`ADD`, `SUB`, `MUL`, `DIV`) and logic operations. Used as the only register for `IN` and `OUT` instructions. (1.5 marks) 2. **BX (Base):** The only general purpose register that can be used directly as a memory pointer, e.g., `MOV AX, [BX]`. Default segment pairing is DS. (1.5 marks) 3. **CX (Counter):** Holds the loop count for `LOOP` instructions and the repeat count for string operations. **CL** holds the shift count for shift/rotate. (2 marks) 4. **DX (Data):** Used with AX to hold 32-bit results from `MUL` and `DIV` (DX:AX format). Holds the I/O port address when port number > FFh. (2 marks) **Diagram for full marks:** A block diagram showing AX, BX, CX, DX splitting into H and L halves. (Bonus mark) --- #### Model Solution for Question A(b) — 7 Marks **Role of BX (3 marks):** - BX is the **only** general purpose register usable as a memory pointer. - It holds the **offset** of the operand within the default Data Segment. - Example: `MOV AX, [BX]` — loads the word from the memory location pointed to by BX into AX. - Example: `MOV [BX], CX` — stores the contents of CX into memory at the address DS:BX. **Role of DX (2 marks):** - DX is used with AX to hold the **32-bit result** of `MUL` (product) and as the **remainder/dividend extension** of `DIV`. - DX also holds the **port address** for I/O when the port number exceeds 255. - Example: `IN AL, DX` — reads a byte from the I/O port whose address is in DX. - Example: `MUL BX` — multiplies AX × BX; the 32-bit result is stored in DX (high) and AX (low). **Physical Address Calculation using BX (2 marks):** For the instruction `MOV AL, [BX]` with `DS = 5000h` and `BX = 1234h`: $$ \text{Physical Address} = (DS \times 16) + BX $$ $$ \text{Physical Address} = 5000h \times 10h + 1234h $$ $$ \text{Physical Address} = 50000h + 1234h = 51234h $$ The byte at physical address **51234h** is loaded into AL. --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **(a)** Describe the 8086 register set. Differentiate between the general purpose registers and the segment registers. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain with examples how the AX register is used in arithmetic and I/O operations. What is the role of DX in the `MUL` and `DIV` instructions? **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question B(a) — 7 Marks The 8086 microprocessor has **14 × 16-bit registers** divided into 4 groups: **1. General Purpose Registers (4):** AX, BX, CX, DX — used for arithmetic, logic, and addressing. Each splits into 8-bit halves. (1.5 marks) **2. Segment Registers (4):** CS, DS, SS, ES — hold the 16-bit base addresses of the **Code**, **Data**, **Stack**, and **Extra** segments respectively. They are combined with a 16-bit offset to produce a 20-bit physical address. (2 marks) **3. Pointer and Index Registers (4):** SP, BP, SI, DI — used in stack operations and indexed addressing. (1.5 marks) **4. Special Registers (2):** IP (Instruction Pointer) holds the offset of the next instruction; **FLAGS** holds status bits like ZF, CF, SF, OF, PF. (2 marks) **Differentiation Table (Bonus – 1 mark):** | Aspect | GPR (AX, BX, CX, DX) | Segment Registers (CS, DS, SS, ES) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Function | Data storage and arithmetic | Hold segment base addresses | | Used in | Direct operand of instructions | Combined with offset for addressing | | Split allowed? | Yes (8-bit H and L) | No (always 16-bit) | | Mutability | Programmer can change freely | Often changed via special instructions | --- #### Model Solution for Question B(b) — 7 Marks **AX in Arithmetic (3 marks):** - `ADD AX, BX` — adds BX to AX; result stored in AX. - `MUL BX` — multiplies unsigned AX × BX; **low 16 bits of product go to AX**. - `DIV BX` — divides unsigned DX:AX by BX; **quotient goes to AX**, remainder goes to DX. **AX in I/O (2 marks):** - `IN AL, 50h` — reads a byte from port 50h into AL. - `OUT DX, AL` — writes the byte in AL to the port whose address is in DX. **Role of DX in MUL and DIV (2 marks):** - For 16-bit `MUL src`: result is 32 bits, stored in **DX:AX** (DX = high word, AX = low word). - For 16-bit `DIV src`: the 32-bit dividend is in **DX:AX**; the quotient is returned in **AX** and the remainder in **DX**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** > 1. **Do NOT confuse** `LOOP` decrementing CX with `REP` decrementing CX — both use CX, but in different contexts. > 2. **Memory pointer GPR:** Many students wrongly claim that AX or CX can be used as `[AX]` or `[CX]`. Only **BX, BP, SI, DI** can be used as offset pointers in default modes. Using AX in `[AX]` will cause an **assembler error** in most tools. > 3. **DX:AX ordering:** In 16-bit MUL, the **high word is in DX** and the **low word is in AX**. Reversing this will cost you 1–2 marks. > 4. **Segment override:** BX pairs with **DS** by default; BP pairs with **SS** by default. Confusing these results in a wrong physical address calculation. > 5. **Splittable registers:** Only AX, BX, CX, DX are splittable. Segment registers (CS, DS, SS, ES) and pointer/index registers (SP, BP, SI, DI) are **NOT** splittable into H and L halves. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Four 16-bit GPRs:** AX, BX, CX, DX — the *only* registers classified as "general purpose" in the 8086. - **Splittability:** Each GPR splits into a high (H) and low (L) byte, giving **8 addressable 8-bit registers** (AH, AL, BH, BL, CH, CL, DH, DL). - **AX = Accumulator:** Default for arithmetic, logic, I/O, and string instructions. - **BX = Base:** The **only** GPR usable as a memory offset pointer in default mode; pairs with **DS**. - **CX = Counter:** Implicit counter for `LOOP` and `REP`; **CL** is the shift count. - **DX = Data:** Holds high word of `MUL` product, remainder of `DIV`, and I/O port address (> FFh). - **Physical address formula:** $\text{PA} = (\text{Segment} \times 10h) + \text{Offset}$. - **Default pairings:** BX → DS, BP → SS, SP → SS. - **Total 8086 register count:** **14 × 16-bit** registers in 4 groups. - **Modern relevance:** AX, BX, CX, DX extended in x86 as EAX, EBX, ECX, EDX (32-bit) and RAX, RBX, RCX, RDX (64-bit). - **Common KTU trap:** SI, DI, BP, SP are **NOT** general purpose — they belong to the **Pointer and Index** group.

topics.title: Processor Operation - Instruction Cycle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMRT403 - COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE AND MICROCONTROLLER > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Processor Operation - Instruction Cycle # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Instruction Cycle** (also called the **Fetch–Decode–Execute Cycle** or **FDE Cycle**) is the fundamental operational sequence performed repeatedly by the Central Processing Unit (CPU) of a computer to retrieve, interpret, and execute a single machine-level instruction stored in main memory. It is the smallest complete processing loop the processor must traverse to convert a stored binary instruction into a tangible result, side effect, or control decision. In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework for **PCMRT403 – Computer Architecture and Microcontroller**, the Instruction Cycle is formally defined as the cyclical procedure by which the processor: 1. **Fetches** the next instruction from program memory (pointed to by the **Program Counter – PC**). 2. **Decodes** the binary opcode to determine the required operation and addressing mode. 3. **Executes** the decoded operation by activating the appropriate datapath micro-operations and generating the necessary control signals. Mathematically, the **Instruction Cycle Time ($T_{IC}$)** is the total time required to complete one full cycle, expressed as: $$T_{IC} = T_F + T_D + T_E$$ Where: - $T_F$ = Time taken for the **Fetch Phase** - $T_D$ = Time taken for the **Decode Phase** - $T_E$ = Time taken for the **Execute Phase** Each of these sub-times is an integer multiple of the **Clock Cycle Time ($T_{clk}$)**: $$T_{IC} = n \times T_{clk}$$ where $n$ is the number of clock pulses per instruction and $T_{clk} = \dfrac{1}{f_{clk}}$, with $f_{clk}$ being the clock frequency in **Hertz (Hz)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCMRT403 / Module 1):** Students must be able to *describe the processor operation in terms of fetch, decode, and execute phases*, identify the registers involved (PC, MAR, MBR/IR, AC), and apply **Register Transfer Language (RTL)** to express micro-operations. This is a high-weightage topic for direct Part-A (3-mark) definitions and frequently appears as a 7 or 14-mark analytical question. > [!NOTE] > **Key Term – Micro-operation:** A micro-operation is the most elementary operation that can be performed by the processor's datapath during a single clock pulse. Examples include *memory read*, *memory write*, *register transfer*, *arithmetic addition*, and *logical shift*. A complete instruction is implemented as a sequence of micro-operations governed by the Control Unit. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – Plain English Intuition Imagine you are a **chef in a kitchen following a printed recipe book** (this is exactly how a CPU follows a program): - **The Recipe Book** is your **Main Memory (RAM)**. Each page contains one "instruction" (a recipe step). - **Your Finger** acting as a bookmark, pointing to the current page, is the **Program Counter (PC)**. - **You (the Chef)** are the **CPU**, performing the work. - **Your Brain**, which reads the recipe and decides what to do, is the **Control Unit + Decoder**. - **Your Hands and Counter** (the workspace where chopping, mixing, and heating happen) are the **ALU and Registers**. Now, the **Instruction Cycle** unfolds like this: 1. **FETCH (Get the recipe step):** You flip the page your finger points to, read it, and move your finger to the next page. In CPU terms: read the instruction from memory at the address held in PC, store it in the **Instruction Register (IR)**, and **increment the PC** to point to the next instruction. 2. **DECODE (Understand the step):** Your brain interprets the words. *"Chop two onions"* → operation = CUT, operand = 2, location = ONION-BASKET. In CPU terms: the decoder interprets the opcode field and identifies the operation and operands. 3. **EXECUTE (Do the step):** You physically pick up the knife, take 2 onions, and chop them. In CPU terms: the ALU performs arithmetic/logic, or data is moved between registers/memory, or a branch decision alters the PC. After execution, you **return to step 1** for the next instruction — endlessly, until the recipe is complete (program termination) or you hit a "STOP" instruction (HALT). > [!TIP] > **Memory Aid for Exams:** Think **"F-D-E"** = **"Find – Decide – Do."** This mirrors the Fetch–Decode–Execute phases and makes the cycle unforgettable during quick revisions. --- ## 1.3 The Three Principal Sub-Cycles The Instruction Cycle is built from three nested loops, as visualized below: | Sub-Cycle | Also Known As | Primary Purpose | Key Registers Touched | |---|---|---|---| | **Fetch Cycle** | Instruction Fetch (IF) | Retrieve instruction from memory | PC → MAR, Memory → IR, PC + 1 | | **Decode Cycle** | Instruction Decode (ID) | Interpret opcode, fetch operands | IR → Decoder, Operands → Registers | | **Execute Cycle** | Execution Phase (EX) | Perform the operation, store result | ALU, AC, Flags, PC (on branch) | > [!NOTE] > The Decode phase in many simplified KTU textbooks is sometimes merged with the Execute phase under a single "Execute" label, but for precise register-transfer analysis, it is treated as a distinct sub-cycle because the decoder output determines which control signals become active in the next clock. --- ## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Clock Frequency ($f_{clk}$):** Expressed in **Hertz (Hz)**, with typical modern microcontroller values ranging from **1 MHz to 200 MHz** (e.g., Arduino Uno uses a 16 MHz crystal; ARM Cortex-M4 microcontrollers can run at 168 MHz). - **Word Length:** KTU syllabi (PCMRT403) emphasize a **16-bit or 32-bit** datapath for the basic computational model, though the theoretical model treats the word length as $n$ bits. - **Memory Address Space:** For a $k$-bit address bus, the addressable memory is $2^k$ locations. > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Mistake:** Confusing *Instruction Cycle* with *Machine Cycle*. A **Machine Cycle** is one memory access (or bus transaction), and an **Instruction Cycle** consists of *multiple* machine cycles. Many 8051 instructions, for example, require 1–4 machine cycles. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Anatomy of the Instruction Cycle – Structured Logic Flow The processor executes the Instruction Cycle in a precise, deterministic sequence. The KTU 2024 Scheme requires students to write each phase using **Register Transfer Language (RTL)** notation. Below is the exhaustive logical breakdown. ### Phase 1 – FETCH (The Retriever) The processor must locate the next instruction in memory and bring it inside the CPU. The Program Counter (PC) holds the address of the next instruction. **Step 1.1:** The contents of the PC are transferred to the **Memory Address Register (MAR)**. $$\text{MAR} \leftarrow \text{PC}$$ **Step 1.2:** A *Memory Read* control signal is asserted. The addressed memory location places its contents on the data bus. $$\text{MBR} \leftarrow \text{Memory}[\text{MAR}]$$ **Step 1.3:** The Memory Buffer Register (MBR), also called the **Memory Data Register (MDR)**, now holds the instruction. It is transferred to the **Instruction Register (IR)**. $$\text{IR} \leftarrow \text{MBR}$$ **Step 1.4:** The PC is incremented so it points to the next instruction (assuming no branch). $$\text{PC} \leftarrow \text{PC} + 1$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why is PC incremented during FETCH, before we know what the instruction is?** Because the PC is *almost always* going to point to the *next sequential instruction* (this is how von Neumann architecture operates). Only **branch** or **jump** instructions will overwrite the PC with a new value during the EXECUTE phase. Incrementing in the fetch phase is an optimization that exploits the principle of **locality of reference**. --- ### Phase 2 – DECODE (The Interpreter) The instruction now resides in the IR. The **Instruction Decoder** examines the **opcode field** (typically the most significant bits). **Step 2.1:** The opcode is sent to the decoder. $$\text{Decoder} \leftarrow \text{IR.opcode}$$ **Step 2.2:** The decoder activates a unique **control line** corresponding to that opcode. This line drives the Control Unit to generate the micro-operation sequence for the execute phase. **Step 2.3:** If the instruction requires an **operand from memory** (direct or indirect addressing), the address field of the IR is routed to the MAR *during* the decode phase (or the very beginning of the execute phase, depending on the architecture). $$\text{MAR} \leftarrow \text{IR.address\_field} \quad \text{(if required)}$$ --- ### Phase 3 – EXECUTE (The Doer) The exact micro-operations depend on the instruction class. The KTU 2024 syllabus focuses on the following categories: #### 3A. Memory-Reference Instructions For a **memory read** (e.g., `LDA addr`): $$\text{MAR} \leftarrow \text{IR.address}$$ $$\text{MBR} \leftarrow \text{Memory}[\text{MAR}]$$ $$\text{AC} \leftarrow \text{MBR}$$ For a **memory write** (e.g., `STA addr`): $$\text{MAR} \leftarrow \text{IR.address}$$ $$\text{MBR} \leftarrow \text{AC}$$ $$\text{Memory}[\text{MAR}] \leftarrow \text{MBR}$$ #### 3B. Register-Reference Instructions (Operands in Registers) For an **ADD** instruction (e.g., `ADD R1, R2`): $$\text{AC} \leftarrow \text{AC} + \text{R1}$$ For a **logical shift** or **complement**: $$\text{AC} \leftarrow \overline{\text{AC}} \quad \text{(complement)}$$ #### 3C. Branch / Control-Flow Instructions For an **unconditional jump** (e.g., `JMP addr`): $$\text{PC} \leftarrow \text{IR.address}$$ For a **conditional branch** (e.g., `BNZ addr` – Branch if Not Zero): $$\text{If} \; (\text{Z flag} = 0) \; \text{then} \; \text{PC} \leftarrow \text{IR.address}$$ $$\text{else} \; \text{PC} \leftarrow \text{PC} + 1 \; \text{(already incremented in fetch)}$$ > [!TIP] > **Why does the conditional branch check flags set by the ALU?** Flags are single-bit registers (Zero, Carry, Sign, Overflow) latched at the end of every ALU operation. They persist until the next ALU operation, allowing a *subsequent* branch instruction to react to the *previous* arithmetic result. --- ## 2.2 The Interrupting Variant of the Instruction Cycle In a real microcontroller (e.g., 8051, ARM), the Instruction Cycle is periodically interrupted by **interrupt requests (IRQs)**. The CPU completes the current instruction, saves its **state** (PC, status register) onto the **stack**, loads the interrupt's address from the **Interrupt Vector Table**, and begins executing the **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)**. After the ISR, a `RETI` (Return from Interrupt) instruction pops the saved state and resumes the original program. > [!NOTE] > This variant is foundational for **embedded systems** and is covered in Module 5 of PCMRT403. In Module 1, students are expected to recognize that the Instruction Cycle may be *temporarily suspended* — not that they must yet design the interrupt controller. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet The following table consolidates every quantitative relationship a student needs for Instruction Cycle problems in the KTU 2024 Scheme examinations. | Formula | Description | Variables & Units | Engineering Use | |---|---|---|---| | $T_{clk} = \dfrac{1}{f_{clk}}$ | Clock period from frequency | $T_{clk}$ in seconds (s), $f_{clk}$ in Hertz (Hz) | Converts clock speed to cycle time | | $T_{IC} = n \times T_{clk}$ | Instruction cycle time | $n$ = clocks per instruction (dimensionless) | Total time per instruction | | $T_{exec} = N_{inst} \times T_{IC}$ | Program execution time | $N_{inst}$ = number of instructions | Real program timing estimates | | $CPI = \dfrac{\sum_i N_i \times C_i}{N_{inst}}$ | Cycles Per Instruction (average) | $C_i$ = cycles for instruction $i$ | Processor performance modeling | | $T_{exec} = N_{inst} \times CPI \times T_{clk}$ | Combined execution time | All in SI units | **Most important KTU formula** | | $\text{Throughput} = \dfrac{f_{clk}}{CPI}$ | MIPS-style throughput | Instructions per second | Benchmarking | | $\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{f_{clk}}{CPI \times 10^6}$ | Million Instructions Per Second | $f_{clk}$ in Hz | Performance comparison | | $\text{Speedup} = \dfrac{T_{old}}{T_{new}}$ | Performance gain from upgrade | Dimensionless ratio | Architecture comparison | | $M = 2^k$ | Addressable memory locations | $k$ = address bus width (bits) | Memory capacity planning | > [!NOTE] > **Critical exam tip:** The formula $T_{exec} = N_{inst} \times CPI \times T_{clk}$ is the **single most tested quantitative relationship** in Module 1 problems. Master the algebra: if you double the clock frequency, you halve the execution time *only if CPI remains constant* — which is rarely true in real hardware. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The Instruction Cycle is the heartbeat of *every* computing device, from a **simple 8-bit microcontroller** (like the 8051 used in washing machines) to a **multi-core 64-bit CPU** (like the Apple M-series in laptops). Understanding it enables engineers to: - **Estimate firmware execution time** for real-time embedded systems (e.g., anti-lock brake controllers must respond within 10 ms). - **Optimize assembly code** by choosing instructions with the lowest CPI. - **Design pipelined and superscalar processors** (Module 2 topic) by overlapping the fetch of instruction $N+1$ with the decode of instruction $N$. - **Debug timing-sensitive applications** such as motor control, audio synthesis, and communication protocols (UART, SPI, I²C). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Industry Connection:** When Intel designs a new CPU, architects spend enormous effort reducing the *effective* CPI through caches, branch prediction, and out-of-order execution — all of which are optimizations *to* the basic Instruction Cycle model. Mastering this foundation is your gateway to advanced computer architecture. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between Clock Frequency, CPI, and Execution Time The most fundamental performance equation in computer architecture is derived below using elementary units analysis. **Starting Premise:** A program consists of $N_{inst}$ instructions. Each instruction requires an *average* of $CPI$ clock cycles to complete. Each clock cycle has a duration of $T_{clk}$ seconds. The **total execution time** is the product of three independent factors: $$T_{exec} = (\text{Number of Instructions}) \times (\text{Clock Cycles per Instruction}) \times (\text{Time per Clock Cycle})$$ Substituting the formal variables: $$T_{exec} = N_{inst} \times CPI \times T_{clk}$$ Since $T_{clk} = \dfrac{1}{f_{clk}}$, we substitute: $$\begin{aligned} T_{exec} &= N_{inst} \times CPI \times \frac{1}{f_{clk}} \\ T_{exec} &= \frac{N_{inst} \times CPI}{f_{clk}} \end{aligned}$$ This is the **canonical performance equation**. Every KTU 2024 problem on instruction-cycle timing can be solved by manipulating this expression. --- ### 3.1.1 Worked Example – KTU Numerical Problem > **Problem:** A processor runs at a clock frequency of **100 MHz**. A benchmark program contains **50,000 instructions**, distributed across three instruction classes with the following CPIs: Class A uses **4 cycles** for 30,000 instructions, Class B uses **3 cycles** for 15,000 instructions, and Class C uses **5 cycles** for 5,000 instructions. Calculate: **(a)** the average CPI, **(b)** the total execution time, and **(c)** the MIPS rating. #### Part (a) – Average CPI We use the weighted-average CPI formula. Each instruction in class $i$ occurs $N_i$ times and consumes $C_i$ cycles. $$\begin{aligned} CPI_{avg} &= \frac{\sum_{i} (N_i \times C_i)}{N_{inst}} \\ &= \frac{(30{,}000 \times 4) + (15{,}000 \times 3) + (5{,}000 \times 5)}{50{,}000} \\ &= \frac{120{,}000 + 45{,}000 + 25{,}000}{50{,}000} \\ &= \frac{190{,}000}{50{,}000} \\ &= 3.8 \end{aligned}$$ So the **average CPI = 3.8 cycles/instruction**. #### Part (b) – Total Execution Time Using the canonical equation: $$\begin{aligned} T_{clk} &= \frac{1}{f_{clk}} = \frac{1}{100 \times 10^6 \, \text{Hz}} = 10 \times 10^{-9} \, \text{s} = 10 \, \text{ns} \\ T_{exec} &= N_{inst} \times CPI_{avg} \times T_{clk} \\ &= 50{,}000 \times 3.8 \times 10 \times 10^{-9} \\ &= 50{,}000 \times 3.8 \times 10^{-8} \\ &= 1.9 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{s} \\ &= 1.9 \, \text{ms} \end{aligned}$$ The total execution time is **1.9 milliseconds**. #### Part (c) – MIPS Rating $$\begin{aligned} \text{MIPS} &= \frac{f_{clk}}{CPI_{avg} \times 10^6} \\ &= \frac{100 \times 10^6}{3.8 \times 10^6} \\ &= 26.32 \, \text{MIPS} \end{aligned}$$ The processor delivers approximately **26.32 million instructions per second**. > [!NOTE] > **Verification by Alternate Path:** We can verify by dividing total instructions by total execution time. $\frac{50{,}000 \text{ inst}}{1.9 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}} \approx 26.32 \times 10^6$ inst/s, which matches the MIPS value. ✔ Unit consistency confirmed. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Speedup Ratio When Clock Frequency Is Doubled This is a classic KTU 2024 "compare two processors" problem. **Premise:** Processor P1 has frequency $f_1$ and CPI $C_1$. Processor P2 has frequency $f_2 = 2 f_1$ and CPI $C_2 = 1.1 C_1$ (the higher frequency causes slightly more pipeline stalls, so CPI rises by 10%). **Speedup** is defined as: $$\text{Speedup} = \frac{T_{P1}}{T_{P2}}$$ Applying $T_{exec} = \dfrac{N_{inst} \times CPI}{f_{clk}}$ for both processors (assuming identical program with $N_{inst}$ instructions): $$\begin{aligned} T_{P1} &= \frac{N_{inst} \times C_1}{f_1} \\ T_{P2} &= \frac{N_{inst} \times 1.1 \, C_1}{2 \, f_1} \end{aligned}$$ Therefore: $$\begin{aligned} \text{Speedup} &= \frac{\frac{N_{inst} \times C_1}{f_1}}{\frac{N_{inst} \times 1.1 \, C_1}{2 f_1}} \\ &= \frac{N_{inst} \times C_1}{f_1} \times \frac{2 f_1}{N_{inst} \times 1.1 \, C_1} \\ &= \frac{2}{1.1} \\ &\approx 1.818 \end{aligned}$$ **Conclusion:** Doubling the frequency yields only a **1.818× speedup**, not 2×, because of the 10% CPI penalty. This is the **Law of Diminishing Returns** applied to clock scaling. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix – RTL for Common Instructions The KTU 2024 Scheme frequently asks students to *"write the Register Transfer Language (RTL) statements for the following instructions."* The matrix below provides the exact, board-exam-ready RTL for the canonical **Basic Computer** model (Mano's architecture, which KTU follows). | Instruction | RTL Sequence | Comment | |---|---|---| | **NOP** (No Operation) | $\text{PC} \leftarrow \text{PC} + 1$ | Only PC increments; does nothing else | | **ADD** (Add to AC) | $\text{AC} \leftarrow \text{AC} + \text{IR.address}$ | Operand comes from the address field of IR | | **LDA** (Load to AC) | $\text{MAR} \leftarrow \text{IR.address}$
$\text{MBR} \leftarrow \text{Memory[MAR]}$
$\text{AC} \leftarrow \text{MBR}$ | Three micro-operations | | **STA** (Store AC) | $\text{MAR} \leftarrow \text{IR.address}$
$\text{MBR} \leftarrow \text{AC}$
$\text{Memory[MAR]} \leftarrow \text{MBR}$ | Three micro-operations | | **BUN** (Branch Unconditionally) | $\text{PC} \leftarrow \text{IR.address}$ | One micro-operation | | **BSA** (Branch and Save Return) | $\text{MAR} \leftarrow \text{PC}$
$\text{PC} \leftarrow \text{IR.address}$
$\text{Memory[MAR]} \leftarrow \text{PC}$ | Three micro-operations, used for subroutines | | **ISZ** (Increment and Skip if Zero) | $\text{DR} \leftarrow \text{Memory[IR.address]}$
$\text{DR} \leftarrow \text{DR} + 1$
$\text{Memory[IR.address]} \leftarrow \text{DR}$
$\text{If } (\text{DR} = 0) \text{ then } \text{PC} \leftarrow \text{PC} + 1$ | Loop control instruction | | **CLA** (Clear AC) | $\text{AC} \leftarrow 0$ | Register-reference (I-bit = 0) | | **CMA** (Complement AC) | $\text{AC} \leftarrow \overline{\text{AC}}$ | Bitwise NOT | | **INC** (Increment AC) | $\text{AC} \leftarrow \text{AC} + 1$ | Direct ALU operation | | **HLT** (Halt) | $\text{S} \leftarrow 0$ (Stop flip-flop cleared) | Terminates program execution | > [!IMPORTANT] > **I-bit Distinction:** In Mano's Basic Computer (which KTU Module 1 follows), the **most significant bit** of the instruction format distinguishes instruction types: > - I = 0 → Memory-Reference Instruction (3-bit opcode + 12-bit address) > - I = 1 → Register-Reference or Input-Output Instruction (16-bit operation code) > > Students *must* include this distinction in their RTL answers to earn full marks. --- ## 3.4 Derivation: Execution Time for a Loop **Problem:** A microcontroller executes a tight loop 1,000 times. Each loop iteration contains 4 instructions: two 4-cycle instructions, one 3-cycle instruction, and one 2-cycle instruction. The clock frequency is 50 MHz. Calculate the loop execution time. **Step 1: Per-iteration cycle count.** $$\begin{aligned} CPI_{loop} &= 4 + 4 + 3 + 2 = 13 \text{ cycles/iteration} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2: Total cycles for 1,000 iterations.** $$\begin{aligned} N_{cycles} &= 1000 \times 13 = 13{,}000 \text{ cycles} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3: Clock period.** $$\begin{aligned} T_{clk} &= \frac{1}{50 \times 10^6} = 20 \times 10^{-9} = 20 \, \text{ns} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4: Total execution time.** $$\begin{aligned} T_{exec} &= 13{,}000 \times 20 \times 10^{-9} \\ &= 260{,}000 \times 10^{-9} \\ &= 2.6 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{s} \\ &= 260 \, \mu\text{s} \end{aligned}$$ The loop completes in **260 microseconds**. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Implication:** If this loop controls a motor PWM signal requiring a 1 kHz refresh rate (period = 1 ms), then 260 µs is comfortably within budget. But if the loop were 4,000 iterations long, it would exceed the timing — a classic real-time systems constraint. --- ## 3.5 Algebraic Transformation Practice (Common KTU Variants) | Given | Find | Formula to Apply | |---|---|---| | $T_{exec}, N_{inst}, f_{clk}$ | $CPI$ | $CPI = T_{exec} \times f_{clk} / N_{inst}$ | | $CPI, T_{exec}, N_{inst}$ | $f_{clk}$ | $f_{clk} = N_{inst} \times CPI / T_{exec}$ | | $CPI, f_{clk}$ | $\text{MIPS}$ | $\text{MIPS} = f_{clk} / (CPI \times 10^6)$ | | Two processors' $T_{exec}$ | $\text{Speedup}$ | $S = T_{P1} / T_{P2}$ | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Instruction Cycle as a Top-Level State Machine The following Mermaid diagram depicts the Instruction Cycle as a finite state machine, which is how it is implemented in the Control Unit's hardwired or microprogrammed logic. ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> resetState resetState --> fetchPhase : Power On / Reset Released fetchPhase --> decodePhase : IR Loaded, PC Updated decodePhase --> executePhase : Control Signals Activated executePhase --> fetchPhase : Next Instruction executePhase --> interruptHandler : IRQ Asserted interruptHandler --> fetchPhase : RETI Executed executePhase --> haltState : HLT Instruction haltState --> [*] ``` > [!NOTE] > The state names `resetState`, `fetchPhase`, etc., follow the Mermaid alpha-numeric rule — they are all prefixed with a letter and contain no reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`). --- ## 4.2 Detailed Micro-Operation Flowchart (Fetch-Decode-Execute) The flowchart below expands the Instruction Cycle into its constituent micro-operations, showing the data movement between registers, memory, and the ALU. ```mermaid flowchart TD A([Start: Clock Edge T0]) --> B["MAR ← PC"] B --> C["MBR ← Memory at MAR"] C --> D["IR ← MBR"] D --> E["PC ← PC + 1"] E --> F{"Opcode = ?"} F -- "Memory-Reference" --> G["MAR ← IR.address"] G --> H{"Operation Type"} H -- "Read" --> I["MBR ← Memory at MAR"] I --> J["AC ← MBR"] H -- "Write" --> K["MBR ← AC"] K --> L["Memory at MAR ← MBR"] H -- "Branch" --> M["PC ← IR.address"] F -- "Register-Reference" --> N["Decode Operand Bits"] N --> O["Perform ALU Operation on AC"] O --> P["Update Flags Z, C, S, V"] F -- "I/O" --> Q["Activate I/O Device Register"] J --> R([End: Cycle Complete]) L --> R M --> R P --> R Q --> R R --> A ``` --- ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture (CPU Datapath + Control) The diagram below shows the complete CPU datapath that physically realizes the Instruction Cycle. Data flows are shown with arrows; control signals are labeled $C_1, C_2, \ldots$ ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph MEM [MAIN MEMORY] MEMCELL[Addressable Storage 0 to 2k minus 1] end subgraph CPU [CENTRAL PROCESSING UNIT] PCreg[Program Counter PC] MARreg[Memory Address Register MAR] MBRreg[Memory Buffer Register MBR] IRreg[Instruction Register IR] DECODER[Instruction Decoder] CTL[Control Unit] ALUblk[Arithmetic Logic Unit ALU] ACreg[Accumulator AC] DRreg[Data Register DR] TRreg[Temporary Register TR] end PCreg -- "Address Bus" --> MARreg MARreg -- "Read/Write" --> MEMCELL MEMCELL -- "Data Bus" --> MBRreg MBRreg --> IRreg IRreg -- "Opcode Field" --> DECODER IRreg -- "Address Field" --> MARreg DECODER --> CTL CTL -- "Control Signals C1 to Cn" --> PCreg CTL -- "Control Signals" --> MARreg CTL -- "Control Signals" --> MBRreg CTL -- "Control Signals" --> ALUblk CTL -- "Control Signals" --> ACreg CTL -- "Control Signals" --> DRreg ACreg <--> ALUblk DRreg <--> ALUblk TRreg <--> ALUblk ACreg <--> DRreg ``` > [!NOTE] > **Block Diagram Interpretation for KTU Exams:** Always label the **buses** (Address Bus, Data Bus, Control Bus) and the **direction of data flow** with arrows. In the KTU 2024 board pattern, a clear labeled diagram is worth 2–3 marks *before* you even begin the explanation. --- ## 4.4 Instruction Format Diagram (Mano's Basic Computer) The KTU Module 1 syllabus is heavily based on M. Morris Mano's **Basic Computer** model. The instruction format is: ```mermaid block-beta columns 16 block:group1:1 I["I"] end block:group2:3 OPCODE["OPCODE"] end block:group3:12 ADDR["ADDRESS FIELD"] end ``` - **I (1 bit):** Mode bit. `0` = Memory-Reference, `1` = Register/IO Reference. - **OPCODE (3 bits):** Specifies the operation. 2³ = 8 possible memory-reference operations. - **ADDRESS (12 bits):** 2¹² = 4096 addressable memory locations. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exam Tip:** The 12-bit address field limits memory to **4 KB**. When KTU asks *"what is the maximum memory size of the Basic Computer?"*, the answer is **2¹² × word size = 4096 words**. Do not confuse this with the modern PC's 64-bit address space. --- ## 4.5 Sequence Diagram – Timing of a Single Instruction The diagram below shows the **timing** of a single 3-cycle instruction, where each cycle performs one micro-operation. ```mermaid gantt title Instruction Cycle Timing (3-Cycle Instruction) dateFormat X axisFormat %s section Fetch MAR <- PC :a1, 0, 1 MBR <- Memory :a2, 1, 1 IR <- MBR :a3, 2, 1 PC <- PC+1 :a4, 3, 1 section Decode Decoder Active :b1, 4, 1 section Execute ALU Operation :c1, 5, 1 Flag Update :c2, 6, 1 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the chart:** Each numbered block represents one **clock cycle**. The total time = 7 clock cycles for a representative instruction. Real instructions vary from 4 cycles (e.g., ADD) to 7+ cycles (e.g., BSA subroutine call). # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **KTU Model Question Paper Style:** Direct, definition-based, requiring a 3-sentence answer with one supporting fact or diagram. ### **Question 1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PCMRT403] **Define the Instruction Cycle.
