topics.title: Instruction Execute
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT403 - COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE AND MICROCONTROLLER
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Instruction Execute

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Instruction Execute** is the third and most operationally intensive phase of the **Instruction Cycle** (Fetch–Decode–Execute). After the Control Unit has successfully fetched the instruction from main memory (Fetch phase) and interpreted its opcode/operands (Decode phase), the *Execute* phase performs the actual operation specified by the instruction. This involves activating the appropriate functional units of the **CPU data path** — such as the **Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)**, registers, buses, and memory interfaces — through a precisely timed sequence of **micro-operations**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Instruction Execution is the process by which the CPU carries out the operation decoded from the instruction word, using the data path and control signals generated by the Control Unit, producing a result, modifying the Program Counter, or changing the processor state.

The execution phase can be formally defined as a deterministic **finite-state transformation** of the processor state:

$$\text{State}_{\text{after}} = f(\text{State}_{\text{before}},\ \text{Opcode},\ \text{Operands})$$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of the **Instruction Cycle** as a chef preparing a dish in a professional kitchen:

| Phase | Kitchen Analogy |
|---|---|
| **Fetch** | Reading the recipe from a cookbook (memory) |
| **Decode** | Understanding what the recipe asks (interpreting ingredients/steps) |
| **Execute** | Actually **cooking** the dish — chopping, mixing, baking |

The *Execute* phase is where all the *real action* happens. The chef (CPU) now operates on the ingredients (data) using tools (ALU, registers, buses) to produce the final dish (result). Just like a chef may need different tools and timing depending on whether the recipe says "fry" or "boil", the Execute phase behaves differently based on whether the instruction says "ADD", "LOAD", or "JUMP".

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students:** The Execute phase is not a single clock cycle. Modern CPUs break execution into multiple micro-cycles (T-states) and micro-operations. A simple `ADD` may take 1 cycle, while a `MUL` or memory write may take several.

### 1.3 Categories of Instructions During Execution

The Execute phase behaves differently based on the instruction class. KTU 2024 Scheme groups them as:

1. **Data Transfer Instructions** — `MOV`, `LOAD`, `STORE`
2. **Arithmetic Instructions** — `ADD`, `SUB`, `MUL`, `DIV`
3. **Logical Instructions** — `AND`, `OR`, `XOR`, `NOT`, shifts, rotates
4. **Control Transfer Instructions** — `JMP`, `CALL`, `RET`, conditional branches
5. **I/O and System Instructions** — `IN`, `OUT`, `HLT`, `NOP`

Each category triggers a **distinct micro-operation sequence** within the data path.

### 1.4 Performance Metrics

- **CPI (Cycles Per Instruction):** Average clock cycles to execute one instruction. Typical RISC: **CPI ≈ 1**; CISC: **CPI ≈ 4–10**.
- **Execution Time:** Standard KTU formula:
$$\text{Execution Time} = \text{Instruction Count} \times \text{CPI} \times \text{Clock Cycle Time}$$
- **Throughput:** Number of instructions completed per unit time.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Micro-Operations of the Execute Phase

The Execute phase consists of a sequence of **micro-operations**, each completing in one clock cycle. These micro-operations move data between registers, perform ALU computations, and update the Program Counter.

#### 2.1.1 Micro-Operations for Data Transfer Instruction (`MOV R1, R2`)

| Step | Micro-Operation | Description |
|------|----------------|-------------|
| T1 | `R1 ← R2` | Internal register-to-register data transfer via internal bus |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Note: Register transfers occur over the **internal CPU bus** and do **not** require memory access, making them the fastest instructions.

#### 2.1.2 Micro-Operations for Memory Read (`LOAD R1, [Addr]`)

| Step | Micro-Operation | Description |
|------|----------------|-------------|
| T1 | `MAR ← Address` | Place effective address into Memory Address Register |
| T2 | `MBR ← M[MAR]` | Read memory contents into Memory Buffer Register |
| T3 | `R1 ← MBR` | Transfer data from MBR to destination register |

#### 2.1.3 Micro-Operations for Memory Write (`STORE R1, [Addr]`)

| Step | Micro-Operation | Description |
|------|----------------|-------------|
| T1 | `MAR ← Address` | Address loaded into MAR |
| T2 | `MBR ← R1` | Data from register moved to MBR |
| T3 | `M[MAR] ← MBR` | Memory write activated |

#### 2.1.4 Micro-Operations for Arithmetic (`ADD R1, R2`)

| Step | Micro-Operation | Description |
|------|----------------|-------------|
| T1 | `A ← R1` | Place operand1 in ALU input register A |
| T2 | `B ← R2` | Place operand2 in ALU input register B |
| T3 | `ALU: ADD` | ALU performs addition |
| T4 | `R1 ← ALU_Out` | Store result in destination register |
| T5 | `FLAGS ← Status` | Update condition code flags (Z, C, N, V) |

#### 2.1.5 Micro-Operations for Control Transfer (`JMP Address`)

| Step | Micro-Operation | Description |
|------|----------------|-------------|
| T1 | `PC ← Address` | Unconditional jump — direct modification of PC |

#### 2.1.6 Micro-Operations for Conditional Branch (`BNZ Label`)

| Step | Micro-Operation | Description |
|------|----------------|-------------|
| T1 | `IF Z = 0 THEN PC ← Address` | Test Zero flag; update PC only if branch taken |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pipeline Impact:** Conditional branches in pipelined processors cause **control hazards**. Modern CPUs mitigate this with **branch prediction**, but KTU-2024 expects awareness of basic branch delay handling.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Unit / Notes |
|---------|----------------------|--------------|
| **Execution Time** | $T_{exec} = N \times CPI \times T_c$ | seconds |
| **CPU Clock Frequency** | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{T_c}$ | Hz |
| **Throughput** | $TP = \dfrac{N}{T_{exec}} = \dfrac{f_c}{CPI}$ | instructions/sec |
| **CPI** | $CPI = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} CPI_i \times IC_i}{IC_{total}}$ | cycles/instruction |
| **MIPS** | $\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{f_c}{CPI \times 10^6}$ | millions/sec |
| **MFLOPS** | $\text{MFLOPS} = \dfrac{\text{FLOPs}}{T_{exec} \times 10^6}$ | millions of FP ops/sec |
| **Amdahl's Law** | $S_{overall} = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{k}}$ | speedup factor |
| **Micro-operation Rate** | $\mu_{rate} = \dfrac{1}{T_c}$ | micro-ops/sec |
| **Branch Penalty** | $P_{branch} = N_{flush} \times CPI$ | wasted cycles |
| **Effective CPI** | $CPI_{eff} = CPI_{base} + \sum (\text{hazard penalty})$ | cycles |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the CPI formula above, $N$ = Instruction Count, $CPI$ = Cycles Per Instruction, $T_c$ = Clock Period, $f_c$ = Clock Frequency, $f$ = parallel fraction, $k$ = speedup of enhanced portion.

### 2.3 Control Unit: Generating Execute Signals

The Control Unit (CU) generates **control signals** during Execute based on the opcode. KTU 2024 distinguishes two implementations:

#### A. Hardwired Control
- Combinational logic gates generate fixed control signals.
- **Fast** but **inflexible**.
- Signals are outputs of decoders based on:
$$\text{Control Signal} = f(\text{Opcode},\ \text{T-state},\ \text{Condition Flags})$$

#### B. Microprogrammed Control
- A **Control Memory (CM)** stores microinstructions.
- Each machine instruction is interpreted as a sequence of microinstructions.
- Uses a **Microprogram Counter (μPC)** and **Control Address Register (CAR)**.
- Supports **firmware updates** — flexible but slower.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Point:** CISC processors (e.g., Intel x86) typically use **microprogrammed control**, while RISC processors (e.g., ARM, MIPS) favor **hardwired control** for speed.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Instruction execution principles directly influence:

- **CPU Design:** Determines CPI, pipeline depth, power budget.
- **Compiler Optimization:** Instruction scheduling, branch prediction hints.
- **Embedded Systems:** Deterministic execution time for RTOS scheduling.
- **GPU Computing:** SIMD execution reuses one control unit across many ALUs.
- **FPGA Design:** Custom datapaths implement user-defined instruction sets.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Execution Time of a Program

We derive the **total execution time** for a program containing mixed instruction types — a common KTU numerical problem.

#### Given:

- $IC_1$ instructions of type 1 with $CPI_1$
- $IC_2$ instructions of type 2 with $CPI_2$
- ...
- $IC_n$ instructions of type n with $CPI_n$
- Clock frequency $f_c$ (Hz), so $T_c = \dfrac{1}{f_c}$

#### Derivation:

The total instruction count is:

$$N_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} IC_i$$

The weighted average CPI is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
CPI_{avg} &= \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (IC_i \times CPI_i)}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} IC_i} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (IC_i \times CPI_i)}{N_{total}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Total clock cycles required:

$$Cycles_{total} = N_{total} \times CPI_{avg} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (IC_i \times CPI_i)$$

Total execution time:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{exec} &= Cycles_{total} \times T_c \\[6pt]
&= \left[\sum_{i=1}^{n} (IC_i \times CPI_i)\right] \times \frac{1}{f_c} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (IC_i \times CPI_i)}{f_c}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Pattern)

**Problem:** A processor has a clock frequency of **500 MHz**. The instruction mix for a benchmark is:

| Instruction Type | IC (Instruction Count) | CPI |
|------------------|------------------------|-----|
| ALU               | 60,000                 | 1   |
| Load              | 25,000                 | 5   |
| Store             | 15,000                 | 4   |
| Branch            | 10,000                 | 3   |

**Find:** (a) Total execution time, (b) CPI average, (c) MIPS rating.

#### Solution:

**Step 1:** Calculate total instructions.

$$N_{total} = 60{,}000 + 25{,}000 + 15{,}000 + 10{,}000 = 110{,}000$$

**Step 2:** Calculate total cycles.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Cycles_{total} &= (60{,}000 \times 1) + (25{,}000 \times 5) \\
&\quad + (15{,}000 \times 4) + (10{,}000 \times 3) \\[4pt]
&= 60{,}000 + 125{,}000 + 60{,}000 + 30{,}000 \\[4pt]
&= 275{,}000 \text{ cycles}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Calculate clock period.

$$T_c = \frac{1}{f_c} = \frac{1}{500 \times 10^6} = 2 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{s} = 2\ \text{ns}$$

**Step 4:** Compute execution time.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{exec} &= Cycles_{total} \times T_c \\[4pt]
&= 275{,}000 \times 2 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{s} \\[4pt]
&= 550 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{s} = 0.55\ \text{ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Compute average CPI.

$$CPI_{avg} = \frac{Cycles_{total}}{N_{total}} = \frac{275{,}000}{110{,}000} = 2.5$$

**Step 6:** Compute MIPS rating.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MIPS} &= \frac{f_c}{CPI_{avg} \times 10^6} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{500 \times 10^6}{2.5 \times 10^6} \\[4pt]
&= 200\ \text{MIPS}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** Always show units and intermediate steps. Partial marks are awarded for correctly setting up the formula even if arithmetic is wrong.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Micro-Operations Across Instruction Classes

This matrix captures the canonical micro-operation sequence for each instruction class — frequently asked in KTU Part A (3-mark) questions.

| Instruction Class | Active Functional Units | Sequence of Micro-Ops | Updates PC? | Updates Flags? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Register Transfer** (`MOV`) | Internal bus | `Rd ← Rs` | No | No |
| **Memory Read** (`LOAD`) | MAR, MBR, Bus | `MAR←Addr → MBR←M[MAR] → Rd←MBR` | No | No |
| **Memory Write** (`STORE`) | MAR, MBR, Bus | `MAR←Addr → MBR←Rd → M[MAR]←MBR` | No | No |
| **Arithmetic/Logic** (`ADD/SUB/AND/OR`) | ALU, Flags Reg | `A←Rd → B←Rs → ALU→Result → Rd←Result` | No | **Yes** |
| **Shift/Rotate** (`SHL/ROR`) | Shifter | `A←Rd → Shift→Result → Rd←Result` | No | **Yes** (carry flag) |
| **Unconditional Jump** (`JMP`) | PC | `PC←Target` | **Yes** | No |
| **Conditional Branch** (`BNE/BEQ`) | PC, Flags | `IF flag condition THEN PC←Target` | **Conditional** | No |
| **Subroutine Call** (`CALL`) | PC, Stack | `Push(PC+1) → PC←Target` | **Yes** | No |
| **Return** (`RET`) | PC, Stack | `PC←Pop()` | **Yes** | No |
| **I/O Instruction** (`IN/OUT`) | I/O Bus, Accumulator | `Acc←Port_data or Port←Acc` | No | No |
| **Halt** (`HLT`) | Control Unit | `Halt processor clock` | No | No |

### 3.4 Derivation of Effective CPI with Hazards

If a pipelined processor encounters **N hazards** per program with an average penalty of $p$ cycles each:

$$
\begin{aligned}
CPI_{eff} &= CPI_{base} + (N_{hazards} \times p) \\[6pt]
&= CPI_{base} + \text{Hazard Penalty}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Example:** If base CPI = 1.5, and we have 0.2 branch hazards per instruction with a penalty of 4 cycles:

$$
\begin{aligned}
CPI_{eff} &= 1.5 + (0.2 \times 4) \\[4pt]
&= 1.5 + 0.8 \\[4pt]
&= 2.3
\end{aligned}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Instruction Execute Phase — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Decoded Opcode and Operands] --> B{Opcode Class Decoder}
    B -->|Data Transfer| C1[Register File Read]
    B -->|Arithmetic Logic| C2[ALU Operand Latch]
    B -->|Memory Access| C3[Address Bus Driver]
    B -->|Control Transfer| C4[Branch Target Logic]
    
    C1 --> D1[Internal Data Bus Transfer]
    C1 --> D2[Write Back to Destination Register]
    
    C2 --> E1[ALU Functional Core]
    E1 --> E2[Status Flag Register Update]
    E1 --> D2
    
    C3 --> F1[Memory Address Register MAR Load]
    F1 --> F2[Memory Read or Write via System Bus]
    F2 --> F3[Memory Data Register MBR Update]
    F3 --> D2
    
    C4 --> G1[Condition Flag Evaluation]
    G1 --> G2{Flag Condition Met?}
    G2 -->|Yes| G3[Program Counter PC Update to Target]
    G2 -->|No| G4[PC remains incremented]
    
    D2 --> H[Result Writeback Complete]
    E2 --> H
    F3 --> H
    G3 --> H
    G4 --> H
    
    H --> I[Proceed to Next Instruction Fetch]
    
    style A fill:#FFD700
    style B fill:#87CEEB
    style E1 fill:#90EE90

```


### 4.4 Memory Execute Sequence — LOAD Instruction

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CPU as CPU Control Unit
    participant MAR as Memory Address Register
    participant MBR as Memory Data Register
    participant MEM as Main Memory
    participant REG as Destination Register
    
    CPU->>MAR: T1 - MAR ← Effective Address
    activate MAR
    CPU->>MEM: T2 - Assert Mem_Read signal
    MEM->>MBR: T2 - Data placed on data bus
    activate MBR
    MEM->>MBR: Memory contents transferred
    deactivate MEM
    CPU->>REG: T3 - REG ← MBR
    deactivate MAR
    deactivate MBR
    Note over CPU,REG: Execute Phase Complete
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**  
**Explain the Execute phase of an instruction cycle with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**

The Execute phase is the third stage of the instruction cycle, occurring after Fetch and Decode. During this phase, the Control Unit activates the appropriate functional units (ALU, memory, register file) to perform the operation specified by the decoded opcode.

For example, for the instruction `ADD R1, R2, R3`:
1. The operands R2 and R3 are loaded into the ALU input latches A and B.
2. The ALU performs the addition in the next cycle.
3. The result is written back into R1.
4. Condition flags (Z, C, N, V) are updated based on the result.
5. The Program Counter is incremented to point to the next instruction.

The Execute phase transforms the **processor state** based on the instruction's semantics.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
**Differentiate between Hardwired Control and Microprogrammed Control.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Hardwired Control | Microprogrammed Control |
|---|---|---|
| Implementation | Combinational logic gates | Microinstructions in control memory |
| Speed | Faster | Slower (extra memory fetch) |
| Flexibility | Inflexible — hard to modify | Easy to modify via firmware |
| Used in | RISC processors (ARM, MIPS) | CISC processors (x86, VAX) |
| Cost | High initial design cost | Lower for complex instruction sets |
| Diagnosability | Hard to debug | Easier fault isolation |

---

### PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 – Modified]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the instruction execution phase of a basic computer. Show all registers involved and their data flow during the execution of the instruction `ADD R1, R2`. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A processor runs at **2.4 GHz** clock frequency. For a given program, the instruction mix is as follows: **(7 Marks)**

| Instruction Type | Count (millions) | CPI |
|------------------|------------------|-----|
| Integer ALU       | 800              | 1   |
| Load              | 300              | 5   |
| Store             | 150              | 4   |
| Branch            | 100              | 3   |

Calculate:
1. Total cycles needed
2. Effective CPI
3. Total execution time
4. MIPS rating

##### Model Solution (a):

The **Execute phase** for `ADD R1, R2` involves the following data flow through registers:

1. **R2 contents** are placed on the **internal bus** and loaded into the **ALU input register B**.
2. **R1 contents** (assuming R1 is also an operand) are loaded into **ALU input register A**.
3. The **ALU** performs addition under the control signal `ALU_op = ADD`.
4. The **ALU output** is routed via the internal bus to **R1** (destination register), enabled by `Reg_Write`.
5. The **Status Flag Register** is updated with `Flag_Write` signal — Zero, Carry, Negative, Overflow flags.
6. The **Program Counter** is incremented by the PC increment circuit.

The block diagram includes: Register File, ALU, Internal Bus, ALU Input Latches A & B, Status Flag Register, PC, Control Unit generating control signals.

##### Model Solution (b):

**Step 1: Total cycles**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Cycles_{total} &= (800 \times 10^6 \times 1) + (300 \times 10^6 \times 5) \\
&\quad + (150 \times 10^6 \times 4) + (100 \times 10^6 \times 3) \\[4pt]
&= 800 \times 10^6 + 1500 \times 10^6 + 600 \times 10^6 + 300 \times 10^6 \\[4pt]
&= 3200 \times 10^6 \text{ cycles} \\[4pt]
&= 3.2 \times 10^9\ \text{cycles}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Effective CPI**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{total} &= 800M + 300M + 150M + 100M = 1350 \times 10^6 \\[4pt]
CPI_{eff} &= \frac{3.2 \times 10^9}{1.35 \times 10^9} = 2.37
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Total execution time**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_c &= \frac{1}{2.4 \times 10^9} = 0.4167\ \text{ns} \\[4pt]
T_{exec} &= 3.2 \times 10^9 \times 0.4167 \times 10^{-9} \\[4pt]
&= 1.333\ \text{seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: MIPS rating**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MIPS} &= \frac{f_c}{CPI_{eff} \times 10^6} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{2.4 \times 10^9}{2.37 \times 10^6} \\[4pt]
&\approx 1012.66\ \text{MIPS} \\[4pt]
&\approx 1.01\ \text{GIPS}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students commonly forget to convert millions (M = 10^6) before multiplication. Always write the units explicitly: `800 × 10^6`, NOT just `800`. Partial marks are awarded for setup, but full marks require unit consistency.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 – Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the role of the Control Unit during the Execute phase. Differentiate between hardwired and microprogrammed control. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider a CPU with three instruction classes: **(7 Marks)**

| Class | CPI |
|-------|-----|
| A     | 1   |
| B     | 3   |
| C     | 5   |

A program consists of **30% Class A**, **50% Class B**, and **20% Class C** instructions.

If the clock rate is increased from **2 GHz to 3 GHz**, calculate:
1. Original CPI and execution time for a program of 10^9 instructions.
2. New execution time and speedup factor.

##### Model Solution (a):

**Role of Control Unit during Execute:**

The Control Unit (CU) orchestrates the Execute phase by generating precise **timed control signals** for every micro-operation. It activates the ALU mode (`ALU_op`), enables register file write-back (`Reg_Write`), drives memory read/write signals (`Mem_Read`, `Mem_Write`), selects the PC source for branches (`PC_Src`), and triggers flag updates (`Flag_Write`).

**Hardwired Control:**

- Implemented using fixed combinational logic, decoders, and PLA/PAL structures.
- Each control signal is a Boolean function of (Opcode, Timing state, Flags).
- **Advantages:** Very fast, suitable for RISC pipelines.
- **Disadvantages:** Difficult to design, modify, or extend.

**Microprogrammed Control:**

- Each machine instruction is decomposed into a sequence of **microinstructions** stored in Control Memory (ROM).
- A Microprogram Counter (μPC) sequences through microinstructions.
- **Advantages:** Easy to modify (firmware updates), suited to complex CISC ISAs.
- **Disadvantages:** Slower due to extra microinstruction fetches.

##### Model Solution (b):

**Step 1: Original CPI (frequencies)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CPI_{avg} &= (0.30 \times 1) + (0.50 \times 3) + (0.20 \times 5) \\[4pt]
&= 0.30 + 1.50 + 1.00 \\[4pt]
&= 2.80
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Original execution time at 2 GHz**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_c &= \frac{1}{2 \times 10^9} = 0.5\ \text{ns} \\[4pt]
Cycles_{total} &= 10^9 \times 2.80 = 2.8 \times 10^9 \\[4pt]
T_{exec,old} &= 2.8 \times 10^9 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-9} = 1.4\ \text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: New execution time at 3 GHz**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_c' &= \frac{1}{3 \times 10^9} \approx 0.333\ \text{ns} \\[4pt]
T_{exec,new} &= 2.8 \times 10^9 \times 0.333 \times 10^{-9} \approx 0.933\ \text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Speedup factor**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \frac{T_{exec,old}}{T_{exec,new}} = \frac{1.4}{0.933} \approx 1.50
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** When computing speedup from clock frequency change, students often incorrectly divide old frequency by new frequency directly. The correct approach uses $S = f_{new} / f_{old}$ **only when** CPI is unchanged — which is true here. Always state your assumptions.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

- ✅ The **Execute phase** is the third step of the Instruction Cycle (Fetch → Decode → Execute).
- ✅ Execute phase performs the **actual operation** via micro-operations on the data path.
- ✅ Different instruction classes require **different micro-operation sequences** (See Section 3.3 Analytical Matrix).
- ✅ The **Control Unit** generates signals based on opcode; implementation is **Hardwired** (fast, RISC) or **Microprogrammed** (flexible, CISC).
- ✅ Key formula: $T_{exec} = N \times CPI \times T_c = \dfrac{N \times CPI}{f_c}$.
- ✅ CPI weighted average formula:
$$CPI_{avg} = \dfrac{\sum IC_i \cdot CPI_i}{\sum IC_i}$$
- ✅ MIPS rating formula:
$$\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{f_c}{CPI \times 10^6}$$
- ✅ **Arithmetic instructions** update flags; **Data transfer** does not; **Control transfer** modifies PC.
- ✅ **Conditional branches** introduce pipeline hazards → branch penalty.
- ✅ Always carry units (M = 10^6, G = 10^9) in numerical problems — KTU evaluators check this.
- ✅ Memory access instructions typically need **3 micro-operations** (MAR load, memory read, MBR transfer).
- ✅ Register-to-register transfers are **fastest** (1 micro-operation via internal bus).
- ✅ In pipelined processors, Execute overlaps with Fetch and Decode of subsequent instructions.
- ✅ **Hardwired control** decodes opcode directly to control signals using combinational logic.
- ✅ **Microprogrammed control** uses Control Memory + μPC to sequence through microinstructions.
- ✅ Amdahl's Law applies when enhancing a portion of execution — formula in Section 2.2.

topics.title: Timing Response
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT403 - COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE AND MICROCONTROLLER
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Timing Response

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Timing Response** in the context of the **8051 Microcontroller** refers to the precise chronological behavior of the internal and external signals of the CPU as it executes instructions, accesses memory, and transfers data through I/O ports. It defines **when** each control signal (ALE, PSEN, RD, WR), address line (A0–A15), and data line (D0–D7) transitions between logic **HIGH** and **LOW** states, measured in **oscillator clock periods**.

In KTU's PCMRT403 framework, Timing Response is formally defined by the relationship:

> The 8051 divides its internal oscillator frequency into standardized time units called **Machine Cycles**, each composed of 12 oscillator periods, and these machine cycles govern the exact instants at which all peripheral signals respond to CPU activity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Definition:** A **Machine Cycle** is the smallest unit of time recognized by the 8051 CPU for executing a fundamental operation (memory read, memory write, or internal computation). **One Machine Cycle = 12 Oscillator Periods = 6 States (S1–S6)**, with each state split into **2 Phases (Phase 1 and Phase 2)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Conductor's Baton

Imagine an orchestra where the **oscillator crystal** is the **conductor's baton**. Every musician (the CPU, memory, ports) listens for each beat. But not every musician needs to play on **every** beat — the drummer plays once every 12 beats (one **machine cycle**), while the violinist may play once every 24 beats (two machine cycles). The sheet music (instruction) tells each musician **when** to respond, but the **baton's beat** is the universal timing reference.

- **Crystal Oscillator** → Conductor's baton (raw timing pulses)
- **Clock Generator** → Orchestra metronome (divides baton into usable rhythms)
- **Machine Cycle** → One full musical measure (12 beats)
- **T-State** → A single beat of the measure
- **Instruction Execution** → A musical phrase spanning multiple measures

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Timing Response Matters in KTU Labs:** When interfacing LCDs, ADCs, or memory chips to the 8051, you must match the **timing response** of the peripheral to the **timing response** of the 8051's bus cycles. A mismatch causes data corruption — the equivalent of the violinist playing before the drummer has even struck the downbeat.

## 1.3 Fundamental Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value (KTU Lab Default) | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Crystal Frequency (f_osc)** | **11.0592 MHz** (or 12 MHz) | Megahertz |
| **Oscillator Period (T)** | **1 / f_osc** | Microseconds |
| **Machine Cycle Duration** | **12 × T** | Microseconds |
| **For 11.0592 MHz crystal** | **1.085 µs** per machine cycle | µs |
| **For 12 MHz crystal** | **1.0 µs** per machine cycle | µs |
| **ALE Pulse Width** | **2 × T − 30 ns** (min) | ns |
| **Address Valid to ALE Low** | **T − 25 ns** (min) | ns |
| **Data Hold after WR High** | **T − 40 ns** (min) | ns |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Fact:** The crystal **11.0592 MHz** is preferred in industrial applications because it generates exact **baud rates** for serial communication (9600, 19200, 57600). At this frequency, one machine cycle = **1.085 µs**, which is a non-integer that divides evenly into standard UART bit periods.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 8051 Clock Generation Hierarchy

The 8051 derives all internal timing from a single external **quartz crystal** connected across pins **XTAL1 (Pin 19)** and **XTAL2 (Pin 18)**. The internal oscillator amplifier, together with the crystal and two load capacitors (typically 30 pF), forms a **Pierce oscillator circuit**.

### Hierarchical Breakdown of Time Units

| Level | Unit | Duration | Symbol |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 (Smallest) | **Oscillator Period (Clock Pulse)** | 1 / f_osc | **T** |
| 2 | **State** | 1 × T | **S** |
| 3 | **Phase** (half-state) | T / 2 | **P1, P2** |
| 4 | **Machine Cycle** | 12 × T | **MC** |
| 5 (Largest) | **Instruction Cycle** | 1, 2, or 4 × MC | **IC** |

## 2.2 The Two-Phase Clock Structure

Every state (S1 through S6) is subdivided into **Phase 1 (P1)** and **Phase 2 (P2)**:

- **Phase 1 (P1)**: The internal CPU registers are updated and address computations are performed.
- **Phase 2 (P2)**: The CPU samples external signals (RD, WR, INT0, INT1, T0, T1) for synchronization.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mnemonic — "Phase 1 Plots, Phase 2 Polls"**: In P1, the CPU plots its address (latches address internally). In P2, the CPU polls external pin states to make decisions.

## 2.3 The 8051 Timing Response Architecture

### 2.3.1 Oscillator and Clock Circuit

The on-chip oscillator requires only:
- A crystal between XTAL1 and XTAL2
- Two ceramic capacitors (C1, C2) — typically **30 pF** — to ground

The **Clock Generator** block converts the crystal oscillation into a stable internal **2-phase clock** that drives the entire CPU.

### 2.3.2 Machine Cycle Sequence

A single machine cycle contains exactly **12 oscillator periods** organized as **6 states (S1, S2, S3, S4, S5, S6)**, each with **2 phases**.

| State | Phase 1 Activity | Phase 2 Activity |
|---|---|---|
| **S1** | PC low-byte latched; address increment | Address bus stabilization |
| **S2** | Program byte fetch initiated | Opcode byte sampled from ROM |
| **S3** | Opcode decoded; internal register update | Internal operation begins |
| **S4** | Second opcode byte fetch (if needed) | Internal data movement |
| **S5** | Final address computation | Internal ALU operation completes |
| **S6** | Result written back; signals return to idle | Cycle terminates |

### 2.3.3 Instruction Execution Time Classification

The 8051 instruction set is divided into timing classes:

| Instruction Class | Machine Cycles | Oscillator Periods | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1-byte, 1-cycle** | 1 MC | 12 T | `INC A`, `DEC A` |
| **1-byte, 2-cycle** | 2 MC | 24 T | `INC DPTR`, `AJMP` |
| **2-byte, 1-cycle** | 1 MC | 12 T | `ADD A, #data`, `MOV Rn, #data` |
| **2-byte, 2-cycle** | 2 MC | 24 T | `MOV direct, direct`, `MOVX @DPTR` |
| **1-byte, 4-cycle** | 4 MC | 48 T | `MUL AB`, `DIV AB` |

## 2.4 External Memory Access Timing Response

When the 8051 accesses **external program memory** (ROM) or **external data memory** (RAM), specific control signals activate in a precise chronological sequence.

### 2.4.1 External Program Memory Read Cycle (PSEN-controlled)

This requires **2 machine cycles (24 T)**:

| Time Index | State | Signal Activity |
|---|---|---|
| **S1P2** | S1, Phase 2 | **ALE → HIGH**; address A0–A7 driven on Port 0 |
| **S2P1** | S2, Phase 1 | **ALE → LOW** (latches A0–A7 into external 74HC573) |
| **S2P2** | S2, Phase 2 | **PSEN → LOW**; ROM enables output |
| **S3–S4** | S3–S4 | A8–A15 driven on Port 2; opcode byte sampled by CPU |
| **S4P2** | S4, Phase 2 | **PSEN → HIGH**; ROM releases bus |
| **S5–S6** | S5–S6 | Internal decode; next fetch preparation |

### 2.4.2 External Data Memory Read Cycle (RD-controlled)

| Time Index | Signal Activity |
|---|---|
| **S1P2** | **ALE → HIGH**; low-byte address on P0 |
| **S2P1** | **ALE → LOW**; latches low address |
| **S2P2** | **RD → LOW**; external RAM drives bus |
| **S3–S4** | High-byte address on P2; data sampled by CPU |
| **S4P2** | **RD → HIGH**; bus released |
| **S5–S6** | Internal processing; PSEN remains HIGH (inactive) |

### 2.4.3 External Data Memory Write Cycle (WR-controlled)

| Time Index | Signal Activity |
|---|---|
| **S1P2** | **ALE → HIGH**; low-byte address on P0 |
| **S2P1** | **ALE → LOW**; latches low address |
| **S2P2** | **WR → LOW**; CPU drives data on P0 |
| **S3–S4** | High-byte address on P2; data written to RAM |
| **S4P2** | **WR → HIGH**; data hold time begins |
| **S5–S6** | Bus released; next instruction fetch |

## 2.5 Port Timing Response

### 2.5.1 Port Output Timing
- Data written to a port latch appears on the physical pins during **S1P1** of the next machine cycle.
- The output driver has a transition time of **~20–50 ns** depending on capacitive load.

### 2.5.2 Port Input Timing
- External pin state is sampled during **S5P2** of the read cycle.
- Input data must be stable **at least 50 ns before S5P2** (setup time) and held **at least 20 ns after S5P2** (hold time).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Read-Modify-Write Anomaly:** When reading a port (e.g., `ANL P1, #0FH`), the 8051 reads the **latch value**, NOT the physical pin. This timing response prevents glitches from being read back. KTU questions often test this distinction.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $$T = \frac{1}{f_{osc}}$$ | Oscillator period | seconds |
| $$T_{MC} = 12 \times T = \frac{12}{f_{osc}}$$ | Machine cycle duration | seconds |
| $$N_{MC} = \frac{T_{instruction}}{T_{MC}}$$ | Number of machine cycles per instruction | dimensionless |
| $$T_{exec} = N_{MC} \times T_{MC}$$ | Total instruction execution time | seconds |
| $$f_{baud} = \frac{2^{mode} \times f_{osc}}{32 \times 12 \times (256 - TH1)}$$ | Serial baud rate (Timer 1 Mode 2) | Hz |
| $$T_{ALE} = \frac{T_{MC}}{2} - 30\,ns$$ | Minimum ALE pulse width | ns |
| $$t_{setup} \geq 50\,ns$$ | Port input setup time before S5P2 | ns |
| $$t_{hold} \geq 20\,ns$$ | Port input hold time after S5P2 | ns |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Understanding timing response is essential for designing **real-time embedded systems** where the 8051 must communicate with peripherals at exact speeds. For example, in automotive airbag controllers, a delay of even **1 µs** in the airbag firing signal can be the difference between safe deployment and injury. Timing response analysis ensures such deterministic behavior.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Machine Cycle Duration for Standard KTU Crystals

### Problem Setup
Given a quartz crystal of frequency **f_osc = 11.0592 MHz**, derive the machine cycle duration, the instruction execution time for `MUL AB` (4 machine cycles), and verify that this crystal supports exact 9600 baud serial communication with Timer 1 in Mode 2 reload value **0xFD (253)**.

---

### Step 1: Compute the Oscillator Period (T)

The oscillator period is the reciprocal of the crystal frequency:

$$T = \frac{1}{f_{osc}}$$

Substituting **f_osc = 11.0592 × 10⁶ Hz**:

$$T = \frac{1}{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}$$

$$\boxed{T \approx 90.422 \times 10^{-9}\,s = 90.422\,ns}$$

**Explanation:** This is the duration of a single oscillator pulse. Every internal 8051 operation is referenced to multiples of this fundamental time unit.

---

### Step 2: Compute the Machine Cycle Duration (T_MC)

By KTU's standard definition, one machine cycle equals **12 oscillator periods**:

$$T_{MC} = 12 \times T$$

$$T_{MC} = 12 \times \frac{1}{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}$$

$$T_{MC} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}$$

$$T_{MC} \approx 1.085 \times 10^{-6}\,s = 1.085\,\mu s$$

**Explanation:** This means every 1.085 µs, the 8051 completes one fundamental memory access or internal operation cycle. This number is critical because all KTU lab timing calculations depend on it.

---

### Step 3: Compute Instruction Execution Time for `MUL AB`

The `MUL AB` instruction is a **1-byte, 4-machine-cycle** instruction per the 8051 instruction timing classification. Its total execution time is:

$$T_{exec} = N_{MC} \times T_{MC}$$

Where **N_MC = 4** for `MUL AB`:

$$T_{exec} = 4 \times 1.085\,\mu s$$

$$\boxed{T_{exec} = 4.34\,\mu s}$$

**Explanation:** During this 4.34 µs window, the 8051's internal ALU performs an 8-bit × 8-bit unsigned multiplication, which mathematically requires checking 8 bit-positions and accumulating partial products. The 4-machine-cycle allocation provides the necessary internal datapath transitions.

---

### Step 4: Verify Baud Rate Calculation for Serial Communication

The 8051's UART baud rate using Timer 1 in **Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload)** is given by:

$$f_{baud} = \frac{2^{SMOD} \times f_{osc}}{32 \times 12 \times (256 - TH1)}$$

With **SMOD = 0** (default), **TH1 = 0xFD = 253**, and **f_osc = 11.0592 MHz**:

$$f_{baud} = \frac{2^{0} \times 11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{32 \times 12 \times (256 - 253)}$$

$$f_{baud} = \frac{1 \times 11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{32 \times 12 \times 3}$$

$$f_{baud} = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{1152}$$

$$\boxed{f_{baud} = 9600\,Hz\,(exact)}$$

**Explanation:** The **0.422 ns granularity** of the 11.0592 MHz crystal, when multiplied by 12, 32, and the reload value 3, produces an **exact** 9600 baud with zero error. This is why KTU-lab-recommended crystals are always 11.0592 MHz, not 12 MHz (which would yield 0.16% error at 9600 baud).

---

### Step 5: Analytical Comparison — 12 MHz vs 11.0592 MHz

| Crystal | Machine Cycle | 9600 Baud Error | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| **11.0592 MHz** | 1.085 µs | **0.00%** | Exact baud rate ✓ |
| **12.000 MHz** | 1.000 µs | **0.16%** | Acceptable for short packets |
| **24.000 MHz** | 0.500 µs | **0.16%** | Faster execution, same error |

**Engineering Insight:** A 0.16% baud rate error accumulates over time. For a continuous data stream of 1000 characters at 9600 baud, the 12 MHz crystal drifts by **~1.6 bit periods**, causing frame errors in the last few characters. This is a **classic KTU viva question**.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Instruction Timing for Common 8051 Instructions

| Mnemonic | Bytes | Machine Cycles | Oscillator Periods | Exec Time @ 12 MHz | Exec Time @ 11.0592 MHz |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `NOP` | 1 | 1 | 12 | 1.000 µs | 1.085 µs |
| `MOV A, Rn` | 1 | 1 | 12 | 1.000 µs | 1.085 µs |
| `MOV Rn, #data` | 2 | 1 | 12 | 1.000 µs | 1.085 µs |
| `MOV direct, direct` | 3 | 2 | 24 | 2.000 µs | 2.170 µs |
| `MOVX @DPTR, A` | 1 | 2 | 24 | 2.000 µs | 2.170 µs |
| `LCALL addr16` | 3 | 2 | 24 | 2.000 µs | 2.170 µs |
| `MUL AB` | 1 | 4 | 48 | 4.000 µs | 4.340 µs |
| `DIV AB` | 1 | 4 | 48 | 4.000 µs | 4.340 µs |
| `SJMP rel` | 2 | 2 | 24 | 2.000 µs | 2.170 µs |
| `CJNE A, direct, rel` | 3 | 2 | 24 | 2.000 µs | 2.170 µs |

## 3.3 Derivation: Power-On Reset Timing Response

The 8051's reset pin (RST, Pin 9) requires a **HIGH pulse of at least 24 oscillator periods** to guarantee a valid reset. The Power-On Reset (POR) circuit uses an RC network:

$$V_{RST}(t) = V_{CC} \times \left(1 - e^{-t / \tau}\right)$$

Where **τ = R × C** is the RC time constant. The reset becomes valid when **V_RST ≥ V_IH (typically 0.7 × V_CC)**:

$$0.7 = 1 - e^{-t_{reset} / \tau}$$

$$e^{-t_{reset} / \tau} = 0.3$$

$$-\frac{t_{reset}}{\tau} = \ln(0.3)$$

$$\frac{t_{reset}}{\tau} = -\ln(0.3) \approx 1.204$$

$$t_{reset} \approx 1.204 \times R \times C$$

For the standard KTU-lab reset circuit with **R = 10 kΩ** and **C = 10 µF**:

$$\tau = 10 \times 10^{3} \times 10 \times 10^{-6} = 0.1\,s$$

$$t_{reset} = 1.204 \times 0.1 = 0.1204\,s \approx 120\,ms$$

**Verification:** This 120 ms reset duration is far greater than the minimum 24 clock periods (24 × 90 ns ≈ 2.2 µs at 11.0592 MHz), confirming the circuit's validity with a safety margin of **5 orders of magnitude**.
