# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define the term "Machine Cycle" as applied to the 8051 microcontroller. How is it related to the oscillator frequency?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Machine Cycle** is the basic unit of time for all 8051 CPU operations, such as opcode fetch, memory read, and memory write. The 8051 divides the oscillator frequency internally by a factor of **12** to generate one machine cycle.

Mathematically:

$$T_{MC} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}}$$

For a **12 MHz crystal**, one machine cycle = **1 µs**. For an **11.0592 MHz crystal**, one machine cycle ≈ **1.085 µs**. Every 8051 instruction's execution time is expressed as an integer multiple (1, 2, or 4) of this machine cycle.

> [1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for numerical example]

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Distinguish between a T-state and a machine cycle. Why is the 8051 designed to use 12 oscillator periods per machine cycle?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | T-State | Machine Cycle |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | One clock pulse from the oscillator | Group of 12 T-states |
| **Duration** | T = 1 / f_osc | 12 × T |
| **Purpose** | Synchronize flip-flops and registers | Complete one memory access or ALU op |

**Why 12 oscillator periods per machine cycle?** The 8051 uses a **divided clock architecture** to allow sufficient time for:
1. External memory access and bus stabilization (especially with slow ROM/RAM chips of the 1980s era).
2. Internal ALU operations (especially 8-bit multiplication/division).
3. Safe signal setup and hold times for peripherals.

This 12T design also conveniently makes the machine cycle frequency equal to **1/12th of f_osc**, which simplifies Timer overflow calculations and serial baud rate generation using Timer 1 in Mode 2.

> [1 mark for distinction, 2 marks for design justification]

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### (a) Explain with a neat timing diagram the timing response of the 8051 when it executes an external program memory read cycle. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

The 8051 reads external program memory (ROM) when the **EA pin is LOW** or when the program counter exceeds the internal 4 KB ROM boundary. This read operation takes **2 machine cycles (24 oscillator periods)**.

**Step 1 — Address Latching Phase (S1P1 to S2P1):**
- During **S1P1**, the CPU drives the low-byte address **A0–A7** onto **Port 0** (P0.0–P0.7).
- At **S1P2**, the **ALE (Address Latch Enable, Pin 30)** signal transitions from **LOW to HIGH**, signaling the external **74HC573 latch** to capture the address.
- At **S2P1**, **ALE goes LOW**, completing the latching. The latch now holds A0–A7; Port 0 is freed for data transfer.

**Step 2 — High-Byte Address Output (S2P1):**
- Simultaneously, the CPU drives the high-byte address **A8–A15** onto **Port 2** (P2.0–P2.7). This address remains stable throughout the read cycle.

**Step 3 — Program Store Enable (S2P2 to S4P2):**
- At **S2P2**, **PSEN (Pin 29)** goes **LOW**, enabling the external ROM's output buffer.
- The ROM decodes the 16-bit address and places the corresponding opcode byte onto Port 0 (now functioning as the data bus).
- At **S4P1**, the CPU samples (reads) the data from Port 0 into its instruction register.
- At **S4P2**, **PSEN goes HIGH**, disabling the ROM output buffer and releasing the bus.

**Step 4 — Internal Execution (S5 and S6):**
- The CPU decodes the opcode and performs internal operations. Port 0 and Port 2 return to their default states (Port 0 floats, Port 2 retains SFR value if not fetching).

**Timing Diagram (Textual Representation):**

```
Time:        |--S1P1--|--S1P2--|--S2P1--|--S2P2--|--S3P1--|--S3P2--|--S4P1--|--S4P2--|--S5P1--|--S5P2--|--S6P1--|--S6P2--|
Oscillator:  _|‾|_|‾|_|‾|_|‾|_|‾|_|‾|_|‾|_|‾|_|‾|_|‾|_|‾|_|‾|_
ALE:         ‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾|____________________________|‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾
Port 0 (Addr):[Addr L ]|     |[Data                ]|[XXXXX]
Port 2 (Addr):[Addr H (stable for entire cycle)                                ]
PSEN:         ‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾|‾‾‾‾‾|‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾
```

**[7 marks breakdown: 2 marks for narrative, 3 marks for timing diagram, 1 mark for ALE/PSEN analysis, 1 mark for port multiplexing explanation]**

---

#### (b) Calculate the execution time of the following 8051 instruction sequence for an 11.0592 MHz crystal: `MOV R0, #45H; MUL AB; MOVX @DPTR, A`. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Identify instruction timings from the 8051 manual:**

| Instruction | Bytes | Machine Cycles |
|---|---|---|
| `MOV R0, #45H` | 2 | 1 |
| `MUL AB` | 1 | 4 |
| `MOVX @DPTR, A` | 1 | 2 |

**Step 2 — Sum total machine cycles:**

$$N_{MC,total} = 1 + 4 + 2 = 7 \text{ machine cycles}$$

**Step 3 — Compute machine cycle duration at 11.0592 MHz:**

$$T_{MC} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^{6}} = 1.085\,\mu s$$

**Step 4 — Compute total execution time:**

$$T_{exec} = N_{MC,total} \times T_{MC} = 7 \times 1.085\,\mu s$$

$$\boxed{T_{exec} = 7.595\,\mu s}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** During this 7.595 µs window, the 8051 performs an immediate register load, an 8-bit unsigned multiplication using its dedicated multiplier hardware, and an external data memory write through Port 0/Port 2 with the WR strobe.

**[7 marks breakdown: 2 marks for cycle identification, 2 marks for T_MC calculation, 2 marks for final result, 1 mark for engineering interpretation]**

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### (a) With a neat timing diagram, explain the timing response of the 8051 during an external data memory write cycle. How does it differ from the external program memory read cycle? (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**External Data Memory Write Cycle:**

The 8051 writes to external data memory when executing instructions like `MOVX @DPTR, A` or `MOVX @R0, A`. This takes **2 machine cycles (24 T)**.

**Step 1 — Address Phase (S1–S2):**
- At **S1P1**, address low byte A0–A7 appears on **Port 0**.
- At **S1P2**, **ALE goes HIGH** to enable the external address latch.
- At **S2P1**, **ALE goes LOW**; the low-byte address is latched into the **74HC573**.
- At **S2P1**, address high byte A8–A15 appears on **Port 2**.

**Step 2 — Write Strobe Phase (S2P2 to S4P2):**
- At **S2P2**, the CPU drives the data byte (e.g., accumulator contents) onto **Port 0**.
- At **S2P2**, **WR (Pin 16)** transitions **LOW**, signaling the external RAM to latch the data.
- At **S4P1**, the data is valid and stable on Port 0.
- At **S4P2**, **WR goes HIGH**, ending the write pulse. **PSEN remains HIGH** throughout (inactive for data memory).

**Step 3 — Recovery Phase (S5–S6):**
- CPU releases the bus; Port 0 returns to high-impedance state.

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | External Program Read | External Data Write |
|---|---|---|
| **Control Signal** | PSEN (LOW active) | WR (LOW active) |
| **Data Direction** | ROM → CPU (Read) | CPU → RAM (Write) |
| **Port 0 Use** | First address, then opcode | First address, then data byte |
| **Port 2 Use** | High-byte address (stable) | High-byte address (stable) |
| **PSEN State** | LOW pulse | HIGH (inactive) |
| **RD State** | HIGH (inactive) | HIGH (inactive) |
| **ALE Pulse** | One pulse in S1P2 | One pulse in S1P2 |
| **Typical Use** | Fetch opcode from ROM | Store result in external RAM |

**[7 marks: 3 marks for write timing narrative + diagram, 2 marks for comparison table, 2 marks for signal-level explanation]**

---

#### (b) An 8051 system uses a 22.1184 MHz crystal. Calculate: (i) the machine cycle duration, (ii) the execution time of `DIV AB`, (iii) the Timer 1 reload value for generating exactly 19200 baud with SMOD = 0. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Machine Cycle Duration:**

$$T_{MC} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}} = \frac{12}{22.1184 \times 10^{6}}$$

$$T_{MC} \approx 0.5425\,\mu s = 542.5\,ns$$

**(ii) Execution Time of `DIV AB`:**

`DIV AB` is a **1-byte, 4-machine-cycle** instruction:

$$T_{DIV} = 4 \times T_{MC} = 4 \times 0.5425\,\mu s$$

$$\boxed{T_{DIV} = 2.170\,\mu s}$$

**(iii) Timer 1 Reload Value for 19200 Baud (SMOD = 0):**

Using the formula:

$$f_{baud} = \frac{2^{SMOD} \times f_{osc}}{32 \times 12 \times (256 - TH1)}$$

Rearranging for TH1:

$$(256 - TH1) = \frac{2^{SMOD} \times f_{osc}}{32 \times 12 \times f_{baud}}$$

$$(256 - TH1) = \frac{1 \times 22.1184 \times 10^{6}}{32 \times 12 \times 19200}$$

$$(256 - TH1) = \frac{22.1184 \times 10^{6}}{7,372,800}$$

$$(256 - TH1) = 3$$

$$TH1 = 256 - 3 = 253 = 0xFD$$

$$\boxed{TH1 = 0xFD}$$

**Verification:** Reload value **0xFD** is identical to the 9600 baud case at 11.0592 MHz, confirming the doubling relationship between crystal frequency and achievable baud rate. **22.1184 MHz supports up to 115200 baud reliably.**

**[7 marks: 2 marks for T_MC, 2 marks for T_DIV, 3 marks for TH1 derivation including formula rearrangement]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing oscillator period with machine cycle** (most common — loses 1–2 marks).
> 2. **Forgetting to multiply by 12** in machine cycle calculation.
> 3. **Using 12 MHz instead of 11.0592 MHz** when the question specifies a baud rate (baud rate problems require 11.0592 MHz for exact results).
> 4. **Confusing PSEN with RD/WR** during program vs data memory access diagrams.
> 5. **Drawing ALE as continuously HIGH** instead of as a one-state pulse during S1P2.
> 6. **Ignoring the 2-phase (P1/P2) subdivision** of each state when asked for "detailed" timing analysis.
> 7. **Misclassifying `MUL AB` / `DIV AB`** as 2-cycle instead of 4-cycle instructions.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- ☐ **One Machine Cycle = 12 Oscillator Periods = 6 States (S1–S6) = 12 Phases**
- ☐ **T-State = 1 oscillator period** = 1 / f_osc
- ☐ **Standard crystal = 11.0592 MHz** (exact baud rates) or 12 MHz (lab default)
- ☐ **11.0592 MHz crystal → T_MC = 1.085 µs**
- ☐ **12 MHz crystal → T_MC = 1.000 µs**
- ☐ **Instruction timing classes:** 1MC (1/2-byte), 2MC (1/2/3-byte), 4MC (`MUL`/`DIV` only)
- ☐ **ALE** = Address Latch Enable, Pin 30, active HIGH, pulses once per machine cycle at S1P2
- ☐ **PSEN** = Program Store Enable, Pin 29, active LOW, used for external ROM reads
- ☐ **RD/WR** = active LOW, used for external data memory
- ☐ **Port 0** is multiplexed (address + data); requires external 10 kΩ pull-ups for proper logic HIGH
- ☐ **Port 2** provides high-byte address (A8–A15)
- ☐ **Read-Modify-Write instructions** (ANL, ORL, XRL, CPL, INC, DEC, DJNZ) read the **latch**, not the pin
- ☐ **MOV A, P1** reads the **physical pin**, useful for true input operations
- ☐ **Reset duration (POR)** ≈ 1.204 × R × C, must exceed 24 oscillator periods
- ☐ **Baud rate formula:** $f_{baud} = \frac{2^{SMOD} \times f_{osc}}{384 \times (256 - TH1)}$ for Timer 1 Mode 2
- ☐ **Power-On SFR defaults:** SP = 07H, PC = 0000H, all ports = FFH, all registers = 00H
- ☐ **External memory access = 2 machine cycles** (address phase + data phase)
- ☐ **Internal memory access = 1 machine cycle** (no bus signaling needed)
- ☐ **ALE pulse width** ≈ T − 30 ns (minimum)
- ☐ **Port input setup time** before S5P2 ≥ 50 ns
- ☐ **Port input hold time** after S5P2 ≥ 20 ns

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Viva Tip:** If asked "Why doesn't the 8051 use 1 clock per machine cycle like modern CPUs?", answer: *"Because the 8051 was designed in 1980 for slow external memory (200 ns access time ROMs like 2732). The 12T architecture provides bus signal setup time and allows external latch propagation delay. Modern cores use pipelining to achieve 1 clock/instruction despite slower individual stages."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Instruction Sequencing and Execution (Basic Concepts)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT403 - COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE AND MICROCONTROLLER
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Instruction Sequencing and Execution (Basic Concepts)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Instruction Sequencing and Execution** refers to the systematic, ordered, and controlled process by which a Central Processing Unit (CPU) fetches, decodes, and executes machine-level instructions stored in main memory. According to the KTU 2024 syllabus for *Computer Architecture and Microcontroller*, this forms the foundational operational backbone of any stored-program computer, where every program—regardless of its high-level language origin—is ultimately reduced to a sequence of binary machine instructions that the hardware interprets and performs sequentially (or in parallel for advanced architectures).

> [!NOTE]
> **Core KTU Definition (Syllabus-Standard)**
> *Instruction sequencing is the procedure of selecting the next instruction to be executed from a sequence of instructions stored in memory, using a Program Counter (PC), while execution is the set of micro-operations performed by the processor's data path to carry out the operation specified by the instruction.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Chef's Recipe

Imagine a **chef (CPU)** in a kitchen (computer) following a **recipe book (program in memory)**. The chef does not read the entire book at once. Instead, the chef:

1. Reads **one step at a time** (Instruction Fetch).
2. Understands what action to perform — *chop, boil, fry* (Instruction Decode).
3. Performs the action using available tools — *knife, stove, pan* (Instruction Execute).
4. Moves the **finger to the next line** (Update Program Counter).
5. Repeats until the recipe is complete (Loop / Sequential Execution).

Similarly, the CPU reads one instruction at a time from memory, decodes it to determine the required operation and operands, executes the operation through the Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU) and registers, and increments the Program Counter to point to the next instruction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams**
> The **Program Counter (PC)** holds the memory address of the *next* instruction to be fetched. After each fetch, the PC is incremented automatically. Branch/Jump instructions modify the PC non-sequentially, breaking the default sequential order.

## 1.3 Why Instruction Sequencing Matters

- **Determinism**: Predictable, reproducible behavior — same input, same output.
- **Von Neumann Architecture**: Both data and instructions share the same memory and bus, requiring a strict fetch-execute cycle.
- **Performance Foundation**: Understanding sequencing is the gateway to pipelining, superscalar execution, and out-of-order execution (advanced modules).
- **Microcontroller Relevance**: In microcontrollers (later modules), deterministic instruction sequencing is critical for real-time embedded applications.

## 1.4 The Three Fundamental Phases of Execution

Every machine instruction, no matter how complex, decomposes into three broad phases:

| Phase | Purpose | Hardware Involved |
|---|---|---|
| **Fetch** | Retrieve instruction from memory | PC, MAR, MBR, Memory |
| **Decode** | Interpret the opcode and locate operands | Instruction Register (IR), Control Unit |
| **Execute** | Perform the operation | ALU, Registers, Data paths |

These three phases together constitute the **Instruction Cycle** (also called the *Fetch-Decode-Execute Cycle* or *FDE Cycle*).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Instruction Cycle — Detailed Phase Breakdown

### Phase 1: Instruction Fetch
The CPU retrieves the next instruction from the memory address held in the **Program Counter (PC)**.

**Step-by-step micro-operations:**

1. The content of PC is transferred to the **Memory Address Register (MAR)**.
   $$T1: \quad PC \rightarrow MAR$$
2. A read signal is sent to memory; the addressed word (instruction) is loaded into the **Memory Buffer Register (MBR)**.
   $$T2: \quad M[MAR] \rightarrow MBR \quad \text{(Memory Read)}$$
3. The content of MBR is transferred to the **Instruction Register (IR)**.
   $$T3: \quad MBR \rightarrow IR$$
4. The PC is incremented by 1 (or by the instruction-word length) to point to the next instruction.
   $$T4: \quad PC \leftarrow PC + 1$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Register Transfer Notation (RTL)**: At KTU, you are expected to use the notation `Bus ← Source` and arithmetic forms like `PC ← PC + 1`. The arrow `←` denotes a *transfer*, while `→` denotes routing onto a common bus.

### Phase 2: Instruction Decode
The control unit examines the **opcode** field of the IR.

1. The opcode is sent to the control logic.
   $$T5: \quad IR[\text{opcode}] \rightarrow \text{Control Unit}$$
2. The control unit determines:
   - The operation to be performed.
   - The addressing mode of the operands.
   - The location of operand fields (registers, memory addresses, or immediates).

### Phase 3: Instruction Execute
The actual operation is carried out. The specific micro-operations depend on the instruction type:

**For an ADD instruction** (e.g., `ADD R1, R2`):
$$T6: \quad R1 \leftarrow R1 + R2$$

**For a LOAD instruction** (e.g., `LOAD R1, [addr]`):
$$T6: \quad MAR \leftarrow \text{addr}$$
$$T7: \quad MBR \leftarrow M[MAR]$$
$$T8: \quad R1 \leftarrow MBR$$

**For a STORE instruction** (e.g., `STORE R1, [addr]`):
$$T6: \quad MAR \leftarrow \text{addr}$$
$$T7: \quad MBR \leftarrow R1$$
$$T8: \quad M[MAR] \leftarrow MBR \quad \text{(Memory Write)}$$

## 2.2 Instruction Formats

KTU 2024 emphasizes the following address-instruction formats. Let `X = (A - B)` be a generic operation, where `A, B, C` are memory locations and `R1, R2` are CPU registers.

| Format | Instruction Syntax | Number of Memory References | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Three-Address** | `SUB C, A, B`  (meaning `M[C] ← M[A] - M[B]`) | 3 | Powerful, used in RISC/early CISC machines |
| **Two-Address** | `SUB A, B` (meaning `A ← A - B`, B unchanged in result) | 2 | Common in commercial machines (e.g., x86) |
| **One-Address** | `SUB B` (uses implicit Accumulator `AC`) | 1 | Early machines, simple microcontrollers |
| **Zero-Address** | `SUB` (Stack-based: `TOS ← TOS - Next`) | 0 | Stack machines (e.g., Java Virtual Machine) |

## 2.3 Evaluation of an Arithmetic Expression

Consider the high-level expression:

$$X = (A \times B) - (C \div D)$$

Using a **Single-Accumulator (One-Address)** machine, the required instruction sequence is:

```
LOAD  A       ; AC ← M[A]
MUL   B       ; AC ← AC × M[B]
STORE T       ; M[T] ← AC
LOAD  C       ; AC ← M[C]
DIV   D       ; AC ← AC ÷ M[D]
SUB   T       ; AC ← AC - M[T]
STORE X       ; M[X] ← AC
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Trap**: In one-address format, **every memory operand reference requires a separate LOAD or STORE instruction**. The accumulator (AC) is the only implicit operand. Examiners frequently deduct marks when students forget the explicit LOAD/STORE instructions.

Using a **Three-Address** machine, the same expression requires only:
```
MUL  R1, A, B    ; R1 ← M[A] × M[B]
DIV  R2, C, D    ; R2 ← M[C] ÷ M[D]
SUB  X, R1, R2   ; M[X] ← R1 - R2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Three-address instructions are shorter in program length but require longer instruction words (more bits per instruction)**. This is a classic KTU trade-off question.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Equation / Notation | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **PC Update** | $PC \leftarrow PC + 1$ | Sequential next instruction |
| **Branch to Target** | $PC \leftarrow \text{target\_address}$ | Unconditional jump |
| **Conditional Branch** | $\text{if } (\text{cond}) \text{ then } PC \leftarrow \text{target}$ | Branch on flag |
| **Address Increment** | $MAR \leftarrow PC$ | Address bus loading |
| **Memory Read** | $MBR \leftarrow M[MAR]$ | Fetch operand or instruction |
| **Memory Write** | $M[MAR] \leftarrow MBR$ | Store result |
| **3-Address Length Ratio** | $\text{Instr}_{\text{3addr}} = 3 \times \text{Instr}_{\text{1addr}}$ | Approx. instruction count ratio for $X=(A \times B)-(C \div D)$ |
| **Program Length (3-addr)** | $L_3 = 3 \text{ instructions} \times 32\text{-bit} = 96 \text{ bits}$ | Example with 32-bit words |
| **Program Length (1-addr)** | $L_1 = 7 \text{ instructions} \times 16\text{-bit} = 112 \text{ bits}$ | Example with 16-bit words |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Assumptions** (used unless stated otherwise):
> - Single Accumulator organization for one-address format.
> - **32-bit instruction word** for three-address format.
> - **16-bit instruction word** for one-address format.
> - All data operands are 32-bit.
> - Memory is byte-addressable OR word-addressable as specified in the question.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Compiler Design**: Compilers like GCC select between three-address IR (used internally) and one-address assembly (generated for x86 accumulator-style operations).
- **Embedded Systems (8051, ARM Cortex-M)**: ARM uses a *load-store* architecture, where arithmetic operates only on registers. Understanding instruction sequencing is critical for assembly-level optimization.
- **Performance Engineering**: Program length and memory access count directly impact CPI (Cycles Per Instruction) and overall execution time.
- **RISC vs CISC**: Three-address RISC machines (MIPS, RISC-V) reduce instruction count but increase program memory; CISC (x86) does the opposite. This trade-off is a perennial KTU essay topic.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation: Instruction Execution Trace

**Problem (KTU 2024 Module-1 Style):**
Consider a single-accumulator CPU. Write the complete instruction sequence and the register transfer micro-operations to evaluate:

$$Y = (P + Q) \times (R - S)$$

Assume `P, Q, R, S` are memory locations and `T1, T2` are temporary memory locations. List each instruction and its corresponding RTL micro-operation.

### Step 1 — Identify Operand Pattern
The expression has two binary sub-operations connected by multiplication:
- `T1 ← P + Q`
- `T2 ← R - S`
- `Y  ← T1 × T2`

The accumulator is implicit in each arithmetic step.

### Step 2 — Construct the Instruction Sequence

| Step | Instruction | RTL Micro-operation | Effect on AC |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `LOAD P` | `MAR ← IR[addr]; MBR ← M[MAR]; AC ← MBR` | `AC ← M[P]` |
| 2 | `ADD Q`  | `MAR ← IR[addr]; MBR ← M[MAR]; AC ← AC + MBR` | `AC ← AC + M[Q]` |
| 3 | `STORE T1` | `MAR ← IR[addr]; MBR ← AC; M[MAR] ← MBR` | `AC` unchanged |
| 4 | `LOAD R` | `MAR ← IR[addr]; MBR ← M[MAR]; AC ← MBR` | `AC ← M[R]` |
| 5 | `SUB S`  | `MAR ← IR[addr]; MBR ← M[MAR]; AC ← AC - MBR` | `AC ← AC - M[S]` |
| 6 | `MUL T1` | `MAR ← IR[addr]; MBR ← M[MAR]; AC ← AC × MBR` | `AC ← AC × M[T1]` |
| 7 | `STORE Y` | `MAR ← IR[addr]; MBR ← AC; M[MAR] ← MBR` | Final result stored |

### Step 3 — Detailed Derivation of the ADD Micro-operation

The `ADD Q` instruction executes as follows. Each clock cycle T is annotated.

**Clock Cycle T1 (Address Phase):**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Step A:} \quad & MAR \leftarrow IR[\text{address field}] \\
\text{Reason:} \quad & \text{The address part of the IR is loaded into the MAR so that the} \\
& \text{memory system knows which operand location to access.}
\end{aligned}$$

**Clock Cycle T2 (Memory Read Phase):**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Step B:} \quad & MBR \leftarrow M[MAR] \\
\text{Reason:} \quad & \text{Read signal is asserted; the memory places the operand on} \\
& \text{the data bus, which is captured into MBR.}
\end{aligned}$$

**Clock Cycle T3 (ALU Phase):**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Step C:} \quad & AC \leftarrow AC + MBR \\
\text{Reason:} \quad & \text{The ALU adds the existing accumulator value with the} \\
& \text{fetched operand from MBR and writes the result back to AC.}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Counting the Program Length and Memory References

For the expression `Y = (P + Q) × (R - S)`:

**Number of instructions in One-Address format:**
$$N_{1} = 7 \text{ instructions}$$

**Number of memory references in One-Address format:**
$$R_{1} = 4 \times \text{LOAD} + 4 \times \text{operands in arithmetic} = 7 \text{ memory accesses}$$

**Equivalent in Three-Address format:**
```
ADD  T1, P, Q     ; 1 instruction
SUB  T2, R, S     ; 1 instruction
MUL  Y,  T1, T2   ; 1 instruction
```

$$N_{3} = 3 \text{ instructions}$$
$$R_{3} = 6 \text{ memory references} \quad \text{(2 reads per ALU instr + 1 read+1 write for MUL)}$$

### Step 5 — Program Length Trade-off Calculation

Assume:
- One-address: **16-bit instruction word** → `LOAD`, `STORE`, `ADD`, `SUB`, `MUL` each fit in 16 bits.
- Three-address: **32-bit instruction word** (8-bit opcode + 3 × 8-bit address fields).

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{1} &= 7 \times 16 \text{ bits} = 112 \text{ bits} \\
L_{3} &= 3 \times 32 \text{ bits} = 96 \text{ bits} \\
\Delta L &= L_{1} - L_{3} = 112 - 96 = 16 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** The three-address format produces a **shorter program (96 bits < 112 bits)** but requires wider memory words (32 bits vs 16 bits). This is a classic KTU module-1 trade-off.

### Step 6 — Conditional Branch Micro-operation

A conditional branch like `BRNZ X` (Branch if Non-Zero to address X) executes as:

$$\begin{aligned}
T1: \quad & \text{If } (Z \text{ flag} = 0) \text{ then } PC \leftarrow \text{IR}[\text{address}] \\
T2: \quad & \text{If } (Z \text{ flag} = 1) \text{ then } PC \leftarrow PC + 1 \quad \text{(no branch)}
\end{aligned}$$

The `Z` (Zero) flag is set by the ALU after the previous arithmetic operation:
$$Z = 1 \iff AC = 0$$
$$Z = 0 \iff AC \neq 0$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Addressing Mode Reference

| Addressing Mode | Effective Address | Use | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Immediate** | Operand is in the instruction itself | Constant loading | `MOV R1, #5` |
| **Direct (Absolute)** | Address field is the effective address | Simple variable access | `LOAD R1, 1000` |
| **Indirect (Register)** | Register contains the effective address | Pointer dereferencing | `LOAD R1, (R2)` |
| **Indexed** | `EA = Base + Index Register` | Array access | `LOAD R1, 100(R2)` |
| **Relative** | `EA = PC + Offset` | Position-independent code | `BRNZ +5` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Frequency Question**: Compute the **effective address (EA)** for each mode. The formula for indexed mode is:
> $$EA_{\text{indexed}} = \text{Base Address} + \text{Index Register Value}$$
> For relative mode:
> $$EA_{\text{relative}} = PC + \text{Signed Offset}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Single-Bus CPU Organization

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the single-bus CPU architecture used in the instruction fetch-decode-execute cycle. All registers communicate through one common bus (typical of KTU textbook problems, e.g., Hamacher/Vranesic-style diagrams).

```mermaid
graph TD
    PC[Program Counter PC]
    MAR[Memory Address Register MAR]
    MBR[Memory Buffer Register MBR]
    IR[Instruction Register IR]
    AC[Accumulator AC]
    ALU[Arithmetic Logic Unit ALU]
    CTRLU[Control Unit CTRLU]
    MEM[Main Memory MEM]
    ZFLAG[Zero Flag ZFLAG]

    PC -- bus --> MAR
    IR -- bus --> MAR
    MAR -- address lines --> MEM
    MEM -- data lines --> MBR
    MBR -- bus --> IR
    MBR -- bus --> AC
    MBR -- bus --> ALU
    AC -- bus --> ALU
    ALU -- result --> AC
    AC -- bus --> MBR
    IR -- opcode field --> CTRLU
    CTRLU -- control signals --> ALU
    CTRLU -- control signals --> MEM
    ALU -- condition codes --> ZFLAG
    CTRLU -- branch decision --> PC
```

**Reading the diagram (KTU-style walkthrough):**
- During **fetch**: `PC → MAR → MEM → MBR → IR`. PC increments.
- During **decode**: `IR[opcode] → CTRLU`. Control signals are generated.
- During **execute**: Operand flows via `MBR → ALU ← AC`, result returns to `AC`.
- After **execute**: `ZFLAG` is updated; `CTRLU` decides whether to increment `PC` (sequential) or load a new address (branch).

## 4.2 Flow Diagram — Instruction Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode([CPU Reset or Power ON])
    fetchAddr[Step 1: MAR gets PC content MAR equals PC]
    readMem[Step 2: Read memory Instruction goes to MBR]
    loadIR[Step 3: Move MBR to IR PC becomes PC plus 1]
    decode[Step 4: Decode opcode in IR]
    branchCheck{Is it a branch instruction?}
    evalAddr[Step 5: Compute effective address of operand]
    readOperand[Step 6: Read operand from memory to MBR]
    executeOp[Step 7: ALU performs the operation]
    storeResult[Step 8: Store result back to AC or memory]
    updateFlags[Step 9: Update condition flags Z N C]
    nextInstr[Return to Step 1 for next instruction]
    branchTaken[Update PC to target address then go to Step 1]

    startNode --> fetchAddr
    fetchAddr --> readMem
    readMem --> loadIR
    loadIR --> decode
    decode --> branchCheck
    branchCheck -- Yes --> evalAddr
    evalAddr --> branchTaken
    branchTaken --> nextInstr
    branchCheck -- No --> evalAddr
    evalAddr --> readOperand
    readOperand --> executeOp
    executeOp --> storeResult
    storeResult --> updateFlags
    updateFlags --> nextInstr
    nextInstr --> fetchAddr
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to interpret this diagram in an exam:** The instruction cycle is *iterative*. After every instruction, the CPU unconditionally returns to Step 1 to fetch the next instruction. The only escape is a HALT instruction (which stops the fetch phase) or an interrupt (advanced topic).

## 4.3 Timing Diagram — Micro-Operations Across Clock Cycles

A typical KTU module-1 question asks to draw a timing table. Here is a representation in Mermaid form for the `LOAD P` instruction:

```mermaid
gantt
    title Micro-operation Timing for LOAD P
    dateFormat X
    axisFormat %s
    section Phase 1 Fetch
    MAR from PC     :a1, 0, 1
    Read Memory     :a2, 1, 1
    MBR from Memory :a3, 2, 1
    IR from MBR     :a4, 3, 1
    PC Increment    :a5, 3, 1
    section Phase 2 Decode
    Opcode to CTRL  :b1, 4, 1
    section Phase 3 Execute
    MAR from IRaddr :c1, 5, 1
    Read Memory     :c2, 6, 1
    MBR from Memory :c3, 7, 1
    AC from MBR     :c4, 8, 1
```

**Reading the timing:**
- **Cycles 0–3** belong to the *Fetch* phase (mandatory for every instruction).
- **Cycle 4** is the *Decode* phase.
- **Cycles 5–8** are *Execute* (specific to `LOAD`; for arithmetic instructions, an ALU cycle replaces the second memory read).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]
**Define instruction sequencing. Explain the role of the Program Counter in the instruction cycle.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — structured as expected by KTU valuators):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Instruction sequencing is the process by which the CPU determines the order in which machine instructions are executed, fetching each instruction from memory in a controlled sequence, decoding it, executing it, and then proceeding to the next.

**Role of PC (2 marks):**
1. The **Program Counter (PC)** is a special-purpose register that always holds the **memory address of the next instruction to be fetched**.
2. After each instruction fetch, the PC is **automatically incremented** by 1 (or by the instruction-word length) to point to the subsequent instruction in memory:
$$PC \leftarrow PC + 1$$
3. For **branch/jump instructions**, the PC is **loaded with a new target address** instead of being incremented, thereby breaking the default sequential order.
4. The PC therefore acts as the **"instruction pointer"** of the CPU — it is the single most important register governing the flow of execution.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]
**Compare three-address, two-address, and one-address instruction formats with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — table + explanation expected):**

| Format | Example (for X = A + B) | Memory Refs | Instruction Length |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Three-Address** | `ADD X, A, B` (meaning M[X] ← M[A] + M[B]) | 3 operands | Longest instruction word |
| **Two-Address** | `ADD A, B` (meaning M[A] ← M[A] + M[B]) | 2 operands | Medium instruction word |
| **One-Address** | `LOAD A; ADD B; STORE X` (uses AC) | 1 operand per instr. | Shortest instruction word |

**Trade-off Note (1 mark):** Three-address format yields shorter programs but requires wider instruction words. One-address format yields longer programs (more instructions) but uses compact instruction words. The choice affects memory size, fetch bandwidth, and CPU complexity.

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert**
> - **Do NOT skip RTL micro-operations**. Writing only the instruction mnemonic without the register-transfer notation costs up to 4 marks.
> - **Always state the addressing mode** explicitly when listing an instruction (e.g., "Direct addressing: `LOAD R1, [0x1000]`").
> - **Show the PC update explicitly** at the end of every fetch phase.
> - **Do not confuse Memory Buffer Register (MBR)** with Memory Data Register (MDR) — they are the same thing; KTU uses MBR consistently. Using both inconsistently is penalized.
> - For expression-evaluation questions, **show the intermediate values in the accumulator** at each step.

### Question A [14 Marks] — KTU Model Question, Module 1

**(a)** [7 Marks] With a neat block diagram, explain the single-bus organization of a CPU. Show the data path and control signals used during the fetch and execute phases of a `LOAD` instruction.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Consider a single-accumulator CPU. Write the complete instruction sequence with RTL micro-operations to evaluate the following expression:

$$Z = (U \times V) + (W - Y)$$

Assume `U, V, W, Y` are memory locations and `T1, T2` are temporary storage.

---

### Model Answer — Question A

#### Part (a) — Block Diagram & LOAD Execution

**Block Diagram Description (3 marks):**

The single-bus CPU consists of the following interconnected units:
- **Registers**: PC, MAR, MBR, IR, AC (accumulator).
- **ALU**: Performs arithmetic and logic operations.
- **Control Unit**: Generates timing and control signals.
- **Main Memory**: Connected via address bus (from MAR) and data bus (to/from MBR).

(Use the Mermaid diagram from Section 4.1 as the reference; in pen-and-paper exam, draw the registers as boxes, the bus as a horizontal line, and show tri-state buffers connecting each register to the bus.)

**LOAD Instruction Execution — Fetch Phase (2 marks):**
$$\begin{aligned}
T1: \quad & MAR \leftarrow PC \\
T2: \quad & MBR \leftarrow M[MAR] \quad \text{(Read)} \\
T3: \quad & IR \leftarrow MBR; \quad PC \leftarrow PC + 1 \\
T4: \quad & \text{Decode: opcode } \rightarrow \text{Control Unit}
\end{aligned}$$

**LOAD Instruction Execution — Execute Phase (2 marks):**
$$\begin{aligned}
T5: \quad & MAR \leftarrow IR[\text{address field}] \\
T6: \quad & MBR \leftarrow M[MAR] \\
T7: \quad & AC \leftarrow MBR
\end{aligned}$$

The operand from memory is now in the accumulator, completing the `LOAD`.

---

#### Part (b) — Expression Evaluation

**Step 1 — Identify Sub-Expressions (1 mark):**

The expression `Z = (U × V) + (W - Y)` decomposes as:
- `T1 ← U × V`
- `T2 ← W - Y`
- `Z  ← T1 + T2`

**Step 2 — Instruction Sequence with RTL (5 marks):**

| # | Instruction | RTL Micro-operation | Effect on AC |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `LOAD U` | `MAR ← IR[addr]; MBR ← M[MAR]; AC ← MBR` | `AC ← M[U]` |
| 2 | `MUL V`  | `MAR ← IR[addr]; MBR ← M[MAR]; AC ← AC × MBR` | `AC ← M[U] × M[V]` |
| 3 | `STORE T1` | `MAR ← IR[addr]; MBR ← AC; M[MAR] ← MBR` | `AC` unchanged |
| 4 | `LOAD W` | `MAR ← IR[addr]; MBR ← M[MAR]; AC ← MBR` | `AC ← M[W]` |
| 5 | `SUB Y`  | `MAR ← IR[addr]; MBR ← M[MAR]; AC ← AC - MBR` | `AC ← M[W] - M[Y]` |
| 6 | `ADD T1` | `MAR ← IR[addr]; MBR ← M[MAR]; AC ← AC + MBR` | `AC ← M[W] - M[Y] + M[T1]` |
| 7 | `STORE Z` | `MAR ← IR[addr]; MBR ← AC; M[MAR] ← MBR` | `M[Z] ← AC` |

**Step 3 — Program Length and Memory Access Count (1 mark):**
- Total instructions: **N₁ = 7**
- Total memory accesses: **7** (each instruction performs exactly one operand reference).

---

### Question B [14 Marks] — KTU Model Question, Module 1 (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the three phases of the instruction cycle with the help of register transfer micro-operations. Use a `SUB R1, R2` instruction as a running example for each phase.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A three-address machine with 32-bit instruction words (8-bit opcode + 3 × 8-bit addresses) and a one-address machine with 16-bit instruction words are both to evaluate:

$$K = (M \div N) \times (P - Q)$$

Determine the program length in bits for each machine and comment on the trade-off.

---

### Model Answer — Question B

#### Part (a) — Three Phases of Instruction Cycle

**Phase 1 — Fetch (3 marks):**
The fetch phase is identical for every instruction and consists of four micro-operations:

$$T1: \quad MAR \leftarrow PC$$
$$T2: \quad MBR \leftarrow M[MAR]$$
$$T3: \quad IR \leftarrow MBR; \quad PC \leftarrow PC + 1$$

For `SUB R1, R2`, after fetch:
- `IR[opcode] = SUB`
- `IR[operand1] = R1`
- `IR[operand2] = R2`
- `PC` points to the next instruction.

**Phase 2 — Decode (2 marks):**
The control unit examines `IR[opcode]` and identifies the operation as `SUB`. It then activates the ALU's subtraction logic and prepares to read the operand field for the source register `R2`.

$$T4: \quad \text{Control signals generated based on } IR[\text{opcode}]$$

**Phase 3 — Execute (2 marks):**
The ALU subtracts the contents of R2 from R1 and stores the result back in R1.

$$T5: \quad R1 \leftarrow R1 - R2$$
$$T6: \quad \text{Update flags: } Z = 1 \text{ if } R1 = 0, \text{ else } Z = 0$$

---

#### Part (b) — Program Length Comparison

**Step 1 — Three-Address Machine (3 marks):**

For `K = (M ÷ N) × (P - Q)`, the instructions are:
```
DIV  T1, M, N     ; T1 ← M[M] ÷ M[N]
SUB  T2, P, Q     ; T2 ← M[P] - M[Q]
MUL  K,  T1, T2   ; M[K] ← M[T1] × M[T2]
```

Number of instructions = 3
Word size = 32 bits
$$L_3 = 3 \times 32 = 96 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 2 — One-Address Machine (3 marks):**

```
LOAD  M     ; AC ← M[M]
DIV   N     ; AC ← AC ÷ M[N]
STORE T1    ; M[T1] ← AC
LOAD  P     ; AC ← M[P]
SUB   Q     ; AC ← AC - M[Q]
STORE T2    ; M[T2] ← AC
LOAD  T1    ; AC ← M[T1]
MUL   T2    ; AC ← AC × M[T2]
STORE K     ; M[K] ← AC
```

Number of instructions = 9
Word size = 16 bits
$$L_1 = 9 \times 16 = 144 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 3 — Trade-off Comment (1 mark):**

| Aspect | Three-Address | One-Address |
|---|---|---|
| Program length | 96 bits (shorter) | 144 bits (longer) |
| Instruction word size | 32 bits (wider memory) | 16 bits (narrower memory) |
| Memory accesses | Fewer | More |
| CPU complexity | Higher (more address logic) | Lower |

> **Conclusion:** The three-address format yields a more compact program (96 < 144 bits, a savings of **48 bits = 33.3%**), but requires wider memory words and more complex CPU hardware. The one-address format is simpler and uses narrower memory, but executes more instructions.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1**

- **Instruction Cycle** = Fetch → Decode → Execute. Every CPU instruction undergoes this cycle, in this order, without exception (until HALT).
- **Program Counter (PC)** always holds the address of the *next* instruction. It increments by 1 (sequential) or is overwritten (branch).
- **Fetch micro-operations (4 standard steps):**
  $$MAR \leftarrow PC \;\to\; MBR \leftarrow M[MAR] \;\to\; IR \leftarrow MBR \;\to\; PC \leftarrow PC+1$$
- **Register Transfer Language (RTL)** is the *expected answer format* in KTU exams. Use `←` for transfers and `→` for routing to bus.
- **Instruction Formats** (memorize the address count and one example each):
  - **3-Address:** `OP dst, src1, src2` (e.g., `ADD X, A, B`).
  - **2-Address:** `OP dst, src` (e.g., `ADD A, B` means `A ← A + B`).
  - **1-Address:** `OP operand` + implicit Accumulator.
  - **0-Address (Stack):** All operands on stack.
- **Effective Address (EA) Formulas:**
  - Direct: `EA = Address field`
  - Indirect: `EA = [Address field]`
  - Indexed: `EA = Base + Index`
  - Relative: `EA = PC + Offset`
- **Zero Flag (Z)** = 1 if the last ALU result is 0; used by conditional branches like `BRZ`, `BRNZ`.
- **Bus Organization:** In a single-bus CPU, only one register can drive the bus at a time; tri-state buffers control access. Multi-bus designs allow simultaneous transfers but are costlier.
- **Standard KTU Sizes (assumed unless stated):**
  - 3-address format → **32-bit** instruction word.
  - 1-address format → **16-bit** instruction word.
  - Data operands → **32-bit**.
- **Common Pitfall:** Forgetting the explicit `PC ← PC + 1` after the fetch phase. The PC update is part of the standard fetch RTL.
- **Common Pitfall:** Confusing the *address* field of an instruction with the *effective address*. The address field is the raw bits in the instruction; the effective address is the memory location actually accessed after address-mode resolution.
- **Higher-Order Thinking:** When asked to *minimize program length*, choose **3-address** format. When asked to *minimize instruction complexity* (for microcontroller design), choose **1-address** or **0-address** (stack) format.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Path
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT403 - COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE AND MICROCONTROLLER
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Data Path

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Data Path** is the **central computational engine of a processor** — the collection of functional units (such as the Arithmetic Logic Unit, registers, multiplexers, decoders, and the internal bus system) that **perform data processing operations and the interconnects (buses/paths) over which data is transferred between these units** during the execution of an instruction.

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme, the data path is one half of the classic **Harvard-style CPU decomposition**:

$$\text{CPU} = \underbrace{\text{Data Path}}_{\text{Handles data flow \& computation}} \;+\; \underbrace{\text{Control Unit}}_{\text{Handles timing \& sequencing}}$$

The data path is *passive* in the sense that it does not make decisions — it merely **moves, stores, and transforms** data as directed by control signals issued by the Control Unit.

### 1.2 Key Terminology Table

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **ALU (Arithmetic Logic Unit)** | Combinational circuit that performs arithmetic (+, −, ×) and logic (AND, OR, XOR) operations. |
| **Register File** | Small, high-speed bank of registers inside the CPU used for temporary data storage. |
| **Internal Bus** | A shared communication pathway that carries data between registers and the ALU. |
| **Multiplexer (MUX)** | A selector circuit that chooses one of several input data lines to forward to a single output. |
| **Tri-state Buffer** | A switch that either passes a signal or presents high-impedance (disconnected) on a bus. |
| **Register Transfer Language (RTL)** | Symbolic notation describing micro-operations and data movement between registers. |
| **Micro-operation** | The smallest functional operation performed by the data path in a single clock cycle. |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Factory Conveyor Belt" Model**

Imagine a **small car assembly factory floor**:

- The **conveyor belts** are the **buses** — they carry car parts (data) between stations.
- The **workstations** (welding robot, paint sprayer, wheel-fitter) are the **functional units** like the **ALU**.
- The **storage racks** beside each workstation are the **registers** — they hold parts temporarily.
- The **forklift drivers** are the **multiplexers** — they pick *which* part goes *where*.
- The **factory supervisor** standing above with a clipboard is the **Control Unit** — she shouts "Next! Take the red part from rack 3, put it in the welding station, and then move the result to the paint station!"

When a customer orders a red car, the supervisor **doesn't build the car** — she only **directs** the flow of parts through the data path. The data path itself does all the physical work: moving, cutting, joining, painting.

This is exactly how a CPU works:

$$\underbrace{\text{Control Unit}}_{\text{Supervisor}} \;\longrightarrow\; \underbrace{\text{Control Signals}}_{\text{Verbal Commands}} \;\longrightarrow\; \underbrace{\text{Data Path}}_{\text{Factory Floor}}$$

The **data path** is the "muscle"; the **control unit** is the "brain."

---

## 1.4 Why Data Path Design Matters (KTU Highlight)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A processor's **performance**, **clock frequency**, and **CPI (Cycles Per Instruction)** are **fundamentally limited by the design of its data path**. A poorly designed data path introduces:
> - **Propagation delays** (long combinational paths → lower $f_{clk}$)
> - **Bus contention** (two registers trying to drive the same bus simultaneously)
> - **Structural hazards** (hardware resource conflicts during pipelining)

The data path is therefore a **direct determinant** of a processor's **throughput** and **latency**.

---

## 1.5 The Two Major Data Path Architectures

There are **two canonical data path designs** studied in the KTU 2024 syllabus:

1. **Single-Bus Data Path (General Purpose Register organization)**
   - One common internal bus shared by all registers.
   - Simple, cheap, but **slow** (only one transfer per cycle).
   - Requires **two micro-operations per instruction fetch-and-execute** (e.g., `R2 ← R1 + R2` needs *two* sequential transfers).

2. **Three-Bus Data Path**
   - **Three internal buses** allow **simultaneous** dual-source operand fetch and result write-back.
   - **Fast** — entire ALU operation in **one clock cycle**.
   - Expensive (more silicon area, more wiring).

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU 2024 board exams, the **Single-Bus organization** is the **most frequently asked data path**, typically tested through a problem like *"Design the data path sequence for the instruction `ADD R1, R2`"*.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Building Blocks of a Data Path

A complete data path is constructed from **six essential blocks**. Let us break down each one in detail.

### 2.1.1 The Register File

The register file is a **small, high-speed memory** (typically **8, 16, 32, or 64 registers** in KTU-level processors) located *inside* the CPU chip itself.

- **Read ports**: Allow two registers to be read simultaneously (one per ALU operand).
- **Write port**: Allows one register to be written per cycle.
- **Access time**: Typically **sub-nanosecond** — orders of magnitude faster than main memory.

**KTU-standard notation:**

| Notation | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `R[i]` | Contents of register $i$ |
| `R[i] ← R[j]` | Copy contents of $R_j$ into $R_i$ |
| `PC ← PC + 1` | Increment program counter (basic flow-of-control) |

### 2.1.2 The Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)

The ALU is the **computational heart** of the data path. It is a **pure combinational circuit** — meaning it has **no memory** of its own; it operates only on its *current* inputs.

**Typical ALU operations:**

| Operation | Symbol | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Add | $A + B$ | Binary addition |
| Subtract | $A - B$ | Binary subtraction (via 2's complement) |
| AND | $A \land B$ | Bitwise logical AND |
| OR | $A \lor B$ | Bitwise logical OR |
| XOR | $A \oplus B$ | Bitwise exclusive OR |
| Shift left | $A \ll 1$ | Multiply by 2 |
| Shift right | $A \gg 1$ | Divide by 2 |
| Compare | $A - B$ | Set flags (Z, N, C, V) |

> [!NOTE]
> The **flags** generated by the ALU (Zero, Negative, Carry, Overflow) are stored in a dedicated **Status Register / Program Status Word (PSW)**. These flags drive **conditional branch decisions** in the control unit.

### 2.1.3 Multiplexers (MUX) and Demultiplexers

A multiplexer of $n$ selection lines can choose between $2^n$ inputs. In a data path, MUXes are used for:

- **Operand selection**: Choosing between register outputs, immediate values, or memory data.
- **ALU input routing**: Selecting which two registers feed the ALU.
- **Shift amount selection**: For variable-shift instructions.

**Mathematical definition of a $2{:}1$ MUX:**

$$Y = \overline{S} \cdot A + S \cdot B$$

where $S$ is the select line, $A$ and $B$ are inputs, and $Y$ is the output.

### 2.1.4 Internal Buses

A bus is **a set of parallel wires** (one per bit) that transfer data between components. The **width** of a bus = the **word length** of the processor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall**: Many students confuse *bus width* (number of wires, e.g., 16/32/64) with *bus capacity/bandwidth*. Bus bandwidth also depends on the **clock frequency**:
> $$\text{Bus Bandwidth} = \text{Bus Width} \times \text{Bus Frequency}$$
> **Units:** bits/sec = bits × Hz

### 2.1.5 Tri-State Buffers

A tri-state buffer has **three possible output states**:
- Logic 0
- Logic 1
- **High-Impedance (Hi-Z)** — effectively *disconnected* from the bus.

This allows **multiple registers to share a single bus** without electrical contention, *provided* that at most **one** source drives the bus at any time.

### 2.1.6 Memory Interface (Address/Data buses to RAM)

The data path must also connect to **external main memory** for `LOAD` and `STORE` instructions. This requires:
- **Address bus** (unidirectional, from CPU → Memory)
- **Data bus** (bidirectional, CPU ↔ Memory)
- **Read/Write control signals** (from Control Unit)

---

## 2.2 Single-Bus Data Path Organization — Deep Analysis

The **single-bus data path** is the **most-asked** architecture in KTU exams. Let us dissect it.

### 2.2.1 Block Diagram of Single-Bus Data Path

```
            ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
            │                                                    │
   BUS A ───┤►[MUX A]──┐                                         │
   BUS B ───┤►[MUX B]──┤                                         │
            │          ├──► [ALU] ──► [MUX Y] ──► SHIFT ──► BUS Y
   IMM ─────┤►[MUX A]──┘                            ▲            │
   IMM ─────┤►[MUX B]──                             │            │
            │                                       │            │
            └───────────────────────────────────────┴────────────┘
```

### 2.2.2 Operational Logic — Step by Step

To perform an ALU operation `R3 ← R1 + R2` on a **single-bus** data path, you need:

**Step 1**: Place contents of $R_1$ on the bus.
- Control signals: `R1out = 1`, `ALUin_A = 1`
- Result: $R_1$ is at the A-input of ALU.

**Step 2**: Perform the operation.
- Control signal: `ALUop = ADD`
- The ALU outputs $R_1 + R_2$ (because $R_2$ is hardwired via MUX B)

**Step 3**: Latch the ALU result into a temporary register (because we cannot write back to a register while reading from it on the same bus).
- Control signal: `Yin = 1` (write to register Y)
- Result: $Y \leftarrow R_1 + R_2$

**Step 4**: Transfer Y back to the destination register.
- Control signals: `Yout = 1`, `R3in = 1`
- Result: $R_3 \leftarrow Y = R_1 + R_2$

**Total micro-operations: 4 micro-operations across 4 clock cycles.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **key KTU 2024 exam insight**: On a **single-bus** data path, an ALU operation like `R3 ← R1 + R2` requires **4 micro-operations**, not 1. The bottleneck is the **shared bus** that prevents simultaneous read and write.

---

## 2.3 Three-Bus Data Path Organization — Deep Analysis

The **three-bus data path** eliminates the bottleneck by using **three dedicated buses**:

| Bus | Purpose |
|---|---|
| **Bus A** | Carries source operand 1 (from any register to ALU input A) |
| **Bus B** | Carries source operand 2 (from any register to ALU input B) |
| **Bus C** | Carries ALU result back to any register (write-back) |

With three buses, the same operation `R3 ← R1 + R2` collapses to:

**Step 1 (Single clock cycle):**
- `Bus A ← R1`, `Bus B ← R2`, `ALU performs ADD`, `Bus C ← Result`, `R3 ← Bus C`
- **Total: 1 micro-operation in 1 clock cycle.**

This is a **4× speedup** over the single-bus design, but at the cost of:
- **3× the bus wiring** (silicon area)
- **3× the number of MUXes**
- **Higher power consumption**

---

## 2.4 Register Transfer Language (RTL) — The Data Path's "Language"

RTL is the **symbolic notation** used to describe every micro-operation performed in the data path. It is the **core tool** for KTU exam answers.

### 2.4.1 RTL Symbols (KTU Mandatory)

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $\leftarrow$ | Transfer / assignment |
| $M \leftarrow R$ | Memory load from address in $R$ |
| $R \leftarrow M$ | Memory store to address in $R$ |
| $P:$ | Label / address of instruction $P$ |

### 2.4.2 Conditional RTL

RTL supports conditional micro-operations:

$$P: \text{ If } (Z = 1) \text{ then } R1 \leftarrow R2$$

This means: "At address $P$, if the Zero flag is set, copy $R_2$ into $R_1$."

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table for last-minute revision.** It contains every formula/equation needed for KTU 2024 board exam problems on Data Path.

| Concept | Formula / Equation | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Bus bandwidth | $\text{BW} = W \times f_{clk}$ | bits/sec |
| ALU propagation delay (single-bus) | $t_{ALU} = 4 \times T_{clk}$ | seconds |
| ALU propagation delay (three-bus) | $t_{ALU} = 1 \times T_{clk}$ | seconds |
| Number of micro-operations (single-bus) | $N_{ops} = 2n + 2$ | where $n$ = number of operands |
| Number of micro-operations (three-bus) | $N_{ops} = 1$ | for any ALU op |
| Clock period lower bound | $T_{clk} \geq t_{comb,max} + t_{setup}$ | seconds |
| Throughput (CPI) | $\text{CPI} = \frac{N_{ops}}{N_{instr}}$ | dimensionless |
| MUX output ($2{:}1$) | $Y = \overline{S}A + SB$ | binary logic |
| Tri-state buffer output | $Y = EN \cdot A$ (else Hi-Z) | $EN$ = enable |
| Memory access time | $t_{mem} \approx 10 \text{ to } 100 \; T_{clk}$ | depends on hierarchy |
| Speedup (3-bus vs 1-bus) | $S = \dfrac{T_{1\text{-bus}}}{T_{3\text{-bus}}} = 4\times$ | for ALU op |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Data path design is **not just academic** — it is the foundation of **every commercial processor**:

| Processor Family | Data Path Type | Width |
|---|---|---|
| Intel 8086 (1978) | Single-bus-like with segment regs | 16-bit |
| Intel Core i9 (2020s) | Multi-bus, super-scalar | 64-bit |
| ARM Cortex-M0 (embedded) | 3-bus AHB-Lite | 32-bit |
| RISC-V SiFive E21 | 3-bus, single-cycle | 32/64-bit |
| Microchip PIC16F (KTU-relevant) | Modified Harvard, 1-bus internal | 8/14-bit |

> [!NOTE]
> The **single-cycle RISC-V** and **MIPS** architectures taught in KTU labs are **direct applications of the three-bus data path**. When you simulate a MIPS instruction in your lab, you are *literally* watching the three-bus data path execute.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: The Single-Bus Data Path for `ADD R1, R2, R3`

**Problem:** Derive the complete sequence of micro-operations (with control signals) for executing the instruction `ADD R1, R2, R3` (i.e., $R_3 \leftarrow R_1 + R_2$) on a single-bus data path.

**Assumed Hardware:** Single internal bus, dedicated ALU, register file with tri-state outputs, temporary register $Y$ for ALU output, status register $Z$ for zero flag.

---

### 3.1.1 Step-by-Step Micro-Operation Sequence

**Cycle 1 — Fetch source operand 1:**

$$R1_{out}, \; Y_{in}$$

**Explanation:**
- Activate the tri-state buffer of $R_1$ ($R1_{out} = 1$); $R_1$'s contents now appear on the bus.
- Activate the write enable of temporary register $Y$ ($Y_{in} = 1$); the value on the bus is latched into $Y$.
- Bus contents after cycle 1: $Y = R_1$.

---

**Cycle 2 — Perform the ALU operation:**

$$Y_{out}, \; ADD, \; Z_{in}$$

**Explanation:**
- Activate $Y$'s output buffer ($Y_{out} = 1$); $Y$'s value (which is $R_1$) appears on the bus.
- Set the ALU's operation select lines to `ADD` ($ADD = 1$). The second ALU input is $R_2$ (hardwired via a MUX in this design).
- Latch the ALU's output and the resulting flags into $Z$ ($Z_{in} = 1$).
- Bus contents during cycle 2: ALU is computing $R_1 + R_2$; result is captured by $Z$ and fed forward.

---

**Cycle 3 — Latch ALU result into Y:**

$$Z_{out}, \; Y_{in}$$

**Explanation:**
- Activate $Z$'s output buffer ($Z_{out} = 1$); the ALU result (which is $R_1 + R_2$) appears on the bus.
- Activate $Y$'s write enable ($Y_{in} = 1$); the ALU result is now latched into $Y$.
- Bus contents after cycle 3: $Y = R_1 + R_2$.

---

**Cycle 4 — Write back to destination register:**

$$Y_{out}, \; R3_{in}$$

**Explanation:**
- Activate $Y$'s output buffer ($Y_{out} = 1$); $Y$'s value (which is $R_1 + R_2$) appears on the bus.
- Activate the write enable of $R_3$ ($R3_{in} = 1$); the value is written into $R_3$.
- **Final result:** $R_3 \leftarrow R_1 + R_2$. ✓

---

### 3.1.2 Compact Summary Table (For KTU Board Answers)

| Cycle | Active Control Signals | Result / State |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R1_{out}, Y_{in}$ | $Y \leftarrow R_1$ |
| 2 | $Y_{out}, ADD, Z_{in}$ | $Z \leftarrow R_1 + R_2$ |
| 3 | $Z_{out}, Y_{in}$ | $Y \leftarrow R_1 + R_2$ |
| 4 | $Y_{out}, R3_{in}$ | $R_3 \leftarrow R_1 + R_2$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Total clock cycles required = 4**. This is the canonical KTU answer for a single-bus data path ALU operation.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Three-Bus Data Path for `ADD R1, R2, R3`

**Problem:** Same instruction, but on a **three-bus** data path.

**Assumed Hardware:** Three dedicated buses (A, B, C); dual-port register file with two read ports and one write port; ALU with direct connections to all three buses.

### 3.2.1 Single-Cycle Execution

**Cycle 1 — All operations in parallel:**

$$R1_{outA}, \; R2_{outB}, \; ALU_{ADD}, \; R3_{inC}$$

**Explanation:**
- Drive $R_1$ onto Bus A ($R1_{outA} = 1$).
- Drive $R_2$ onto Bus B ($R2_{outB} = 1$).
- ALU operation select = `ADD`.
- Latch ALU output (which is on Bus C by default) into $R_3$ ($R3_{inC} = 1$).
- **Final result after one clock cycle:** $R_3 \leftarrow R_1 + R_2$. ✓

### 3.2.2 Speedup Calculation

$$S = \frac{\text{Cycles}_{\text{1-bus}}}{\text{Cycles}_{\text{3-bus}}} = \frac{4}{1} = 4$$

> [!NOTE]
> The three-bus architecture is **4× faster** for ALU operations, at the cost of **3× bus wiring complexity**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Data Path for Common Instruction Types

Below is a **complete analytical matrix** of micro-operations for the most important instruction classes. This is the **single most valuable table** for KTU exam preparation.

| Instruction | RTL | Single-Bus Micro-Ops (Cycles) | Three-Bus Micro-Ops (Cycles) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **MOV R1, R2** | $R1 \leftarrow R2$ | $R2_{out}, R1_{in}$ (2) | $R2_{outA}, R1_{inC}$ (1) |
| **ADD R3, R1, R2** | $R3 \leftarrow R1 + R2$ | 4 cycles (see §3.1) | 1 cycle (see §3.2) |
| **LOAD R1, M[Addr]** | $R1 \leftarrow M[Addr]$ | $MAR_{out}, Read, WMFC, MDR_{in}, MDR_{out}, R1_{in}$ (6) | 1 cycle (parallel) |
| **STORE M[Addr], R1** | $M[Addr] \leftarrow R1$ | $MAR_{out}, R1_{out}, Write, WMFC$ (4) | 1 cycle (parallel) |
| **JUMP Label** | $PC \leftarrow Label$ | $PC_{out}, MAR_{in}, Read, WMFC, MDR_{out}, PC_{in}$ (6) | 1 cycle |
| **BRANCH if Z=1** | If $Z$ then $PC \leftarrow Target$ | Add `Z=1` check, then 6 cycles | 1 cycle (conditional MUX) |
| **SHL R1** | $R1 \leftarrow R1 \ll 1$ | $R1_{out}, SHIFT_{left}, Y_{in}, Y_{out}, R1_{in}$ (5) | 1 cycle |
| **AND R3, R1, R2** | $R3 \leftarrow R1 \land R2$ | Same as ADD but with $AND$ (4) | 1 cycle |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Notice the **asymmetry** between ALU operations and memory operations on a single-bus data path. Memory operations are **even slower** (6 cycles for LOAD) because of the bus arbitration with the memory bus. This is why early microprocessors like the **Intel 8086** had dedicated *segment registers* to reduce memory accesses.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Bus Bandwidth Calculation

**Problem:** A 32-bit data path operates at a clock frequency of $500 \; \text{MHz}$. Calculate the maximum theoretical bus bandwidth.

**Given:**
- Bus width $W = 32$ bits
- Clock frequency $f_{clk} = 500 \; \text{MHz} = 5 \times 10^8 \; \text{Hz}$

**Step 1 — Identify the formula:**

$$\text{BW} = W \times f_{clk}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BW} &= 32 \; \text{bits} \times 5 \times 10^8 \; \text{Hz} \\
\text{BW} &= 160 \times 10^8 \; \text{bits/sec} \\
\text{BW} &= 1.6 \times 10^{10} \; \text{bits/sec}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to GB/s:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BW} &= \frac{1.6 \times 10^{10}}{8 \times 10^9} \; \text{GB/s} \\
\text{BW} &= 2.0 \; \text{GB/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{\text{BW} = 1.6 \times 10^{10} \; \text{bits/sec} = 2.0 \; \text{GB/s}}$

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Clock Period Lower Bound for Data Path

**Problem:** A data path has combinational logic with maximum propagation delay of $12 \; \text{ns}$. The register setup time is $2 \; \text{ns}$ and the clock-to-Q delay is $1 \; \text{ns}$. What is the minimum clock period?

**Given:**
- $t_{comb,max} = 12 \; \text{ns}$
- $t_{setup} = 2 \; \text{ns}$
- $t_{clk \to Q} = 1 \; \text{ns}$

**Step 1 — Write the constraint:**

The data must propagate from a register, through combinational logic, and settle at the next register's input **before the next clock edge**:

$$T_{clk} \geq t_{clk \to Q} + t_{comb,max} + t_{setup}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{clk} &\geq 1 \; \text{ns} + 12 \; \text{ns} + 2 \; \text{ns} \\
T_{clk} &\geq 15 \; \text{ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute maximum clock frequency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{max} &= \frac{1}{T_{clk,min}} \\
f_{max} &= \frac{1}{15 \times 10^{-9}} \; \text{Hz} \\
f_{max} &\approx 66.67 \; \text{MHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{T_{clk,min} = 15 \; \text{ns}, \quad f_{max} \approx 66.67 \; \text{MHz}}$

> [!NOTE]
> This is exactly why **pipelining** is essential: by inserting registers between combinational blocks, you can *shorten* the effective $t_{comb,max}$ per stage and push $f_{clk}$ much higher. This is what makes modern CPUs run at **5+ GHz** instead of 66 MHz.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level CPU Decomposition (Data Path + Control Unit)

The diagram below shows the **canonical KTU-level CPU block diagram**, illustrating the data path's relationship with the control unit, memory, and I/O.

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph CTRL [CONTROL UNIT]
        CU[Instruction Decoder]
        SEQ[Sequencer / State Machine]
        CU --> SEQ
    end

    subgraph DP [DATA PATH]
        RF[Register File R0 to R7]
        MUXA[MUX A]
        MUXB[MUX B]
        ALU[ALU]
        MUXY[MUX Y]
        SHIFT[Barrel Shifter]
        SR[Status Register Flags]
        YREG[Temp Register Y]
        ZREG[Temp Register Z]
        PC[Program Counter PC]
        IR[Instruction Register IR]
        RF --> MUXA
        RF --> MUXB
        MUXA --> ALU
        MUXB --> ALU
        ALU --> MUXY
        MUXY --> SHIFT
        SHIFT --> YREG
        YREG --> ZREG
        ZREG --> RF
        ALU --> SR
    end

    BUS_INTERNAL[Internal Data Bus]
    RF -.-> BUS_INTERNAL
    BUS_INTERNAL -.-> RF

    SEQ ==> CTRL_SIG[Control Signals]
    CTRL_SIG ==> DP
    CTRL_SIG --> RF
    CTRL_SIG --> MUXA
    CTRL_SIG --> MUXB
    CTRL_SIG --> ALU
    CTRL_SIG --> MUXY
    CTRL_SIG --> SHIFT
    CTRL_SIG --> YREG
    CTRL_SIG --> ZREG
    CTRL_SIG --> PC

    MEM[Main Memory RAM ROM]
    ABUS[Address Bus]
    DBUS[Data Bus]
    PC --> ABUS
    ABUS --> MEM
    MEM --> DBUS
    DBUS --> IR
    DBUS --> RF

    IO[I O Devices]
    MEM <--> IO
```

---

## 4.2 Single-Bus Data Path — Functional Flow

```mermaid

        R0[R0]
        R1[R1]
        R2[R2]
```

## 4.4 Micro-Operation Execution Timeline (Gantt-style)

The diagram below illustrates the **4-cycle execution of `ADD R3, R1, R2`** on a single-bus data path.

```mermaid
gantt
    title Single Bus Data Path Micro Operations for ADD R3 R1 R2
    dateFormat X
    axisFormat %s
    section Bus Activity
    R1 onto bus       :a1, 0, 1
    Y latches R1      :a2, 0, 1
    Y onto bus ALU add :a3, 1, 1
    Z latches result  :a4, 1, 1
    Z onto bus Y latch :a5, 2, 1
    Y onto bus R3 write :a6, 3, 1
    section ALU Activity
    ALU idle          :b1, 0, 1
    ALU computes add  :b2, 1, 1
    ALU idle          :b3, 2, 2
```

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Instruction Execution

```mermaid
graph TB
    START([Instruction Fetch IF]) --> DECODE{Instruction Decode ID}
    DECODE -->|R type ALU| EX1[EX Stage ALU op]
    DECODE -->|Load| EX2[EX Stage Memory Address]
    DECODE -->|Store| EX3[EX Stage Memory Data]
    DECODE -->|Branch| EX4[EX Stage PC update]
    EX1 --> WB1[WB Stage write to register]
    EX2 --> MEM1[MEM Stage read from RAM]
    EX3 --> MEM2[MEM Stage write to RAM]
    EX4 --> END1([Next PC])
    MEM1 --> WB2[WB Stage write to register]
    WB1 --> END2([Next Instruction])
    WB2 --> END2
    MEM2 --> END2

    style START fill:#ffeb3b
    style DECODE fill:#ff9800
```
