**Step 1 — Maximum count** = 65,536 (timer rolls over from 0000H to FFFFH + 1).

**Step 2 — Maximum delay:**

$$T_{max} = 65{,}536 \times \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^6}$$

$$T_{max} = 65{,}536 \times 1.085\,\mu s \approx 71.11 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 3 — Engineering insight:** If a delay > 71.11 ms is needed at 11.0592 MHz in Mode 1, a software loop must **poll the TF flag multiple times** to extend the effective delay.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Timer/Counter Delay Path

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Crystal Oscillator f_osc"] --> B["Divide-by-12 Prescaler"]
    B --> C["Machine Cycle Clock T_mc"]
    C --> D["Timer/Counter Register TH0 TL0"]
    D --> E["Overflow Detector TF0 Flag"]
    E -->|TF0 = 1| F["CPU Polling Loop Exits"]
    E -->|TF0 = 0| G["CPU Waits in JNB HERE Loop"]
    G --> D
    F --> H["Program Continues to Next Instruction"]
    H --> I["Effective Delay T_delay = Count x T_mc"]
```

### 4.2 Software Delay Execution Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Subroutine Entry: DELAY"] --> B["MOV R7, outer_count"]
    B --> C["MOV R6, inner_count"]
    C --> D["DJNZ R6, inner_label"]
    D -->|R6 not zero| D
    D -->|R6 is zero| E["DJNZ R7, outer_label"]
    E -->|R7 not zero| C
    E -->|R7 is zero| F["RET"]
    F --> G["Total Delay: N_cycles x T_mc"]
```

### 4.3 Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Delay Method

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Need Delay?"] --> B{"Duration < 10 us?"}
    B -->|Yes| C["Use NOP Instructions"]
    B -->|No| D{"Hardware Timer Available?"}
    D -->|Yes| E["Use Timer 0 or Timer 1"]
    D -->|No| F["Use Nested DJNZ Loop"]
    E --> G{"Need > 71 ms at 11.0592 MHz?"}
    G -->|Yes| H["Poll TF Flag Multiple Times"]
    G -->|No| I["Single Timer Overflow"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — 3 Mark Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]**
*Define machine cycle. Calculate the machine cycle period for an 8051 system using a 12 MHz crystal.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A machine cycle is the basic unit of timing in the 8051, comprising 12 oscillator periods.

$$T_{mc} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}} = \frac{12}{12 \times 10^6} = 1.0\,\mu s$$

The machine cycle period is **1 μs**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**
*List two applications of delay generation in 8051-based embedded systems.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Switch/keypad debouncing** — mechanical contacts require 5–20 ms delay to avoid false triggering.
2. **Multiplexed seven-segment display refresh** — sequential digit activation needs a precise ~1 ms delay.

*(Any two valid applications accepted.)*

---

### PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 3 Typical]**

**(a)** With $f_{osc} = 11.0592\,\text{MHz}$, calculate the total delay generated by the following subroutine. **(7 marks)**

```assembly
DELAY:  MOV R5, #0FFH
LOOP2:  MOV R4, #0FFH
LOOP1:  DJNZ R4, LOOP1
        DJNZ R5, LOOP2
        RET
```

**(b)** Design a 25 ms delay using Timer 1 in Mode 1 with $f_{osc} = 12\,\text{MHz}$. Write the complete 8051 assembly program. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Step-by-step:**

$T_{mc} = 12/11.0592\,\text{MHz} = 1.085\,\mu s$

Inner loop: 255 × 2 = 510 mc
Outer body per iteration: 1 (MOV R4) + 510 + 2 (DJNZ R5) = 513 mc
Subroutine total: 1 + (255 × 513) + 2 = 130,818 mc

$$T_{delay} = 130{,}818 \times 1.085\,\mu s \approx 141.94 \text{ ms}$$

**(b) Step-by-step:**

$T_{mc} = 1.0\,\mu s$, Required count = 25,000

Timer value = 65,536 − 25,000 = 40,536

Hex conversion: 40,536₁₀ = **9E58H**

TH1 = 9EH, TL1 = 58H

**Program:**

```assembly
        MOV TMOD, #10H    ; Timer 1, Mode 1
        MOV TH1, #9EH
        MOV TL1, #58H
        SETB TR1
HERE:   JNB TF1, HERE
        CLR TR1
        CLR TF1
```

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3 Typical]**

**(a)** Explain two methods of generating delay in 8051 with their merits. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An 8051 system uses a 11.0592 MHz crystal. Find the values of TH0 and TL0 to generate a delay of **10 ms** using Timer 0 in Mode 1. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Two methods:**

1. **Software delay** — Uses nested loops of `DJNZ` instructions. Merits: no hardware overhead, simple, deterministic. Demerits: CPU is fully occupied; cannot do other tasks.

2. **Hardware timer delay** — Uses on-chip Timer 0 or Timer 1 in Mode 1/Mode 2. Merits: precise, CPU can poll or use interrupts, suitable for long durations. Demerits: requires careful setup of TMOD, TH, TL registers.

**(b) Step-by-step:**

$T_{mc} = 12/11.0592\,\text{MHz} = 1.085\,\mu s$

Count = 10 ms / 1.085 μs = 9216

Timer value = 65,536 − 9,216 = 56,320

Hex: 56,320₁₀ = **DC00H**

**TH0 = DCH, TL0 = 00H**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting loop overhead** — Students often compute only `DJNZ` cycles and forget the `MOV` setup and `RET`. Each unaccounted instruction costs 1–2 mc.
> 2. **Wrong timer mode** — Mode 1 is 16-bit (max 65,536), Mode 2 is 8-bit auto-reload (max 256). Mixing them up gives 256× wrong delay.
> 3. **Polarity of subtraction** — Timer *preload* = $2^n -$ *count*, not count itself. Many students write the wrong way.
> 4. **Hex/decimal confusion** — Forgetting to convert decimal preload values to hex before splitting into TH/TL.
> 5. **Forgetting the TF0 flag** — Timer cannot auto-restart in Mode 1; flag must be cleared in software.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Machine cycle = 12 / f_osc** — the bedrock of every delay calculation.
- **Each 8051 instruction consumes 1, 2, or 4 machine cycles** — `NOP` = 1 mc, `DJNZ` = 2 mc, `MOV direct,#data` = 2 mc.
- **Nested DJNZ loops** give practical software delays in the microsecond-to-millisecond range.
- **Timer Mode 1 (16-bit):** max delay = 65,536 × T_mc; needs manual flag clear.
- **Timer Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload):** max delay = 256 × T_mc; auto-restart, ideal for serial baud generation.
- **For very long delays**, poll the TF flag inside an outer software loop.
- **Timer preload formula:** $\text{Value} = 2^n - \dfrac{T_{delay}}{T_{mc}}$.
- **Splitting the 16-bit value:** $TH = \text{floor}(\text{Value}/256)$, $TL = \text{Value} \bmod 256$.
- **Always account for subroutine overhead** — the initial `MOV` and final `RET`.
- **11.0592 MHz crystal** is preferred in serial-comm-based designs because it divides cleanly into standard baud rates (9600, 19200, etc.).
- **Hardware timer is always more accurate** than pure software loops because it does not depend on branch prediction or pipeline behavior (none in 8051) but on a precise hardware counter.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Port Programming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMRT403 - COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE AND MICROCONTROLLER > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Port Programming # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Port Programming** in the context of the 8051 microcontroller (the canonical architecture for KTU's PCMRT403 syllabus) refers to the systematic process of configuring, controlling, and manipulating the four bidirectional 8-bit **Input/Output (I/O) ports** — namely **P0, P1, P2, and P3** — through their corresponding **Special Function Registers (SFRs)** located in the internal RAM of the microcontroller. Each port is mapped to an SFR at addresses **80H (P0), 90H (P1), A0H (P2), and B0H (P3)**, and may be byte-addressable or bit-addressable depending on the port. The 8051 microcontroller packs a total of **32 I/O lines** distributed as: - **Port 0 (P0)**: 8 lines (P0.0 – P0.7) — multiplexed address/data bus - **Port 1 (P1)**: 8 lines (P1.0 – P1.7) — dedicated general-purpose I/O - **Port 2 (P2)**: 8 lines (P2.0 – P2.7) — upper address byte - **Port 3 (P3)**: 8 lines (P3.0 – P3.7) — alternate functions > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Port programming is the foundation of all embedded interfacing. Every peripheral interaction — from lighting an LED to driving a 16×2 LCD — is built upon writing 0s/1s to these four SFRs. ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Imagine a **large industrial control room** with **four control panels (P0, P1, P2, P3)**, each panel having exactly **8 toggle switches** (the 8 pins of each port). These switches are wired to machinery, sensors, and indicators in a factory floor. - **Panel P0** is a *dual-purpose panel* — it can either control 8 generic devices OR it can be tied into a master conveyor system that displays the conveyor's destination address (address/data multiplexing). - **Panel P1** is the *general-purpose control panel* — its only job is to flip 8 simple on/off switches. - **Panel P2** is the *upper-floor indicator panel* — it shows which section of the factory (upper address) is currently active. - **Panel P3** is the *VIP control panel* — its 8 switches not only turn generic things on/off but also handle special tasks: ringing an alarm (UART), picking up a phone (interrupts), or synchronizing clocks (timers). When the operator (your assembly/C program) writes a `1` to a switch, current flows OUT of the microcontroller INTO the external device (output mode). When the operator reads a `1`, the external device is PULLING the switch high (input mode). > [!NOTE] > **Key Takeaway:** "Port Programming" is the art of strategically toggling these 32 switches using the right mnemonics (`SETB`, `CLR`, `MOV`, `ANL`, `ORL`) to make the microcontroller interact with the physical world. ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics - **Number of ports:** **4** (P0, P1, P2, P3) - **Pins per port:** **8** - **Total I/O lines:** **32** - **SFR address of P0:** **80H** - **SFR address of P1:** **90H** - **SFR address of P2:** **A0H** - **SFR address of P3:** **B0H** - **Internal pull-ups on P1, P2, P3:** ≈ **30 kΩ – 40 kΩ** (built-in) - **P0 sink/source current per pin:** **≈ 3.2 mA** (typical) - **Bit-addressable ports:** **P0, P1, P2, P3** (all four are bit-addressable from 80H–87H, 90H–97H, A0H–A7H, B0H–B7H) # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Four Ports — Structural Breakdown ### 2.1.1 Port 0 (P0, SFR @ 80H) - **Dual-role pin** — when **NOT** connected to external memory, it functions as a normal I/O port. - When external memory is interfaced, **P0 acts as the multiplexed lower-order address/data bus (AD0–AD7)**. - **No internal pull-up resistor** → external pull-ups (typically **10 kΩ** to Vcc) are MANDATORY when used as output. - The pin's output structure consists of two MOSFETs (Q1 pull-up, Q2 pull-down) that drive the line HIGH or LOW. ### 2.1.2 Port 1 (P1, SFR @ 90H) - **Simplest of all ports** — purely general-purpose bidirectional I/O. - Has an **internal pull-up** (~30–40 kΩ). - No alternate functions, no external memory interface dependency. - **Most preferred for general applications** like LED interfacing and switch inputs. ### 2.1.3 Port 2 (P2, SFR @ A0H) - Functions as a normal I/O port when no external memory is connected. - When external memory is used, **P2 carries the higher-order address byte (A8–A15)**. - Has an **internal pull-up** like P1. ### 2.1.4 Port 3 (P3, SFR @ B0H) — The "Swiss Army Knife" Each of the 8 pins has an **alternate function** that is activated automatically by writing `1` to that bit: | Bit | Pin | Alternate Function | Description | | ---- | ---- | ------------------ | -------------------------------- | | P3.0 | RxD | Serial Input | UART receiver line | | P3.1 | TxD | Serial Output | UART transmitter line | | P3.2 | INT0 | External Interrupt 0 | Low-level/edge-triggered IRQ | | P3.3 | INT1 | External Interrupt 1 | Secondary IRQ line | | P3.4 | T0 | Timer 0 Input | External clock/counter input | | P3.5 | T1 | Timer 1 Input | External clock/counter input | | P3.6 | WR | Write Strobe | External RAM write pulse | | P3.7 | RD | Read Strobe | External RAM read pulse | > [!IMPORTANT] > To use any P3 alternate function, the corresponding bit MUST be set to `1` BEFORE the peripheral (timer/UART/INT) is enabled. Forgetting this step is the #1 bug in beginner 8051 assembly. ## 2.2 Pin Structure Logic (Why P0 is "Open-Drain") - **P1, P2, P3:** Each pin has an internal pull-up FET (Q1) and a pull-down FET (Q2). Writing `1` to the latch turns Q1 ON and Q2 OFF → line floats HIGH. Writing `0` turns Q1 OFF and Q2 ON → line goes LOW. This is a quasi-bidirectional port. - **P0:** Lacks the internal pull-up. Used as open-drain in pure I/O mode → requires external pull-up resistor. ## 2.3 Core Instructions for Port Programming | Mnemonic | Operation | Example | Effect | | ------------------ | ------------------------------------ | ---------------- | ------------------------------------- | | `MOV P1, #0FFH` | Write byte to port | Output 1s to P1 | All P1 pins go HIGH | | `MOV A, P3` | Read byte from port | Read P3 to A | Capture external state | | `SETB P1.0` | Set bit | P1.0 → 1 | Turn ON device on P1.0 | | `CLR P2.7` | Clear bit | P2.7 → 0 | Turn OFF device on P2.7 | | `ANL P1, #0F0H` | AND with immediate mask | Mask lower nibble | Clear lower 4 bits, keep upper 4 | | `ORL P3, #0C0H` | OR with immediate mask | Set upper 2 bits | Force P3.6, P3.7 HIGH | | `CPL P1.2` | Complement bit | Toggle P1.2 | Flip P1.2 logic level | | `JB P3.3, label` | Jump if bit = 1 | Test INT1 pin | Branch on external interrupt | | `JNB P1.7, label` | Jump if bit = 0 | Test switch | Branch on switch open/closed | ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Formula / Constant | Description | Units / Value | | ----------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------ | ------------------------ | | $f_{osc}$ | Crystal oscillator frequency | MHz (typical: 11.0592) | | $T_{machine} = \dfrac{12}{f_{osc}}$ | One machine cycle duration | microseconds (μs) | | $N_{cycles} = \dfrac{T_{delay}}{T_{machine}}$ | Number of machine cycles for required delay | dimensionless integer | | $N_{loops} \approx \dfrac{N_{cycles}}{3}$ | Approximate loop count for 1-N delay loop | integer | | Delay in nested loop: $D = (M \times N) \times 3 \times T_{machine}$ | Nested loop delay formula (per inner iter) | seconds | | P0 SFR address | Port 0 register | 80H | | P1 SFR address | Port 1 register | 90H | | P2 SFR address | Port 2 register | A0H | | P3 SFR address | Port 3 register | B0H | | Internal pull-up (P1/P2/P3) | Pull-up resistance | ≈ 30–40 kΩ | | External pull-up for P0 | Required external resistor | 4.7 kΩ – 10 kΩ | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** Port programming is the *lingua franca* of embedded systems. From washing machines to automotive ECUs to satellite payload controllers, every interaction with the physical world begins with a single `MOV` to a port SFR. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Time-Delay Calculation Derivation (Classic KTU Problem) **Problem:** Calculate the time delay generated by the following 8051 subroutine, assuming $f_{osc} = 11.0592 \text{ MHz}$. ```assembly DELAY: MOV R0, #0FFH ; Load R0 with 255 LOOP1: DJNZ R0, LOOP1 ; Decrement R0, jump if not zero RET ``` ### Step 1: Compute the Machine Cycle $$T_{machine} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}$$ $$T_{machine} = 1.085 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 1.085 \text{ μs}$$ ### Step 2: Count Machine Cycles per Iteration Each `DJNZ` instruction consumes **2 machine cycles**. The `MOV` instruction consumes **1 machine cycle**. | Instruction | Cycles | Executions | Total Cycles | | ----------- | ------ | ---------- | ------------ | | `MOV R0,#FFH` | 1 | 1 | 1 | | `DJNZ R0,LOOP1` | 2 | 255 | 510 | ### Step 3: Total Machine Cycles $$N_{cycles} = 1 + 255 \times 2 = 1 + 510 = 511 \text{ cycles}$$ ### Step 4: Total Time Delay $$T_{delay} = N_{cycles} \times T_{machine} = 511 \times 1.085 \text{ μs}$$ $$T_{delay} = 554.4 \text{ μs} \approx 0.554 \text{ ms}$$ ### Step 5: General Formula Derivation For a single-loop delay with count $N$ in the register: $$T_{delay} = (1 + 2N) \times T_{machine}$$ For a **nested loop** with outer count $M$ and inner count $N$: $$T_{delay} = \big[1 + 2N + 2 \times 2M \times N\big] \times T_{machine}$$ $$T_{delay} = \big[1 + 2N(1 + 2M)\big] \times T_{machine}$$ ## 3.2 Bit-Masking Logic Derivation **Problem:** A 7-segment display is connected to P1. To display digit '5' (segments: a, c, d, f, g ON; b, e OFF), derive the byte to be written to P1 assuming active-HIGH segments with bit mapping: P1.0=a, P1.1=b, P1.2=c, P1.3=d, P1.4=e, P1.5=f, P1.6=g. ### Step 1: Construct Truth Vector for '5' | Segment | Pin | State (a, b, c, d, e, f, g) | | ------- | ---- | -------------------------- | | a | P1.0 | 1 | | b | P1.1 | 0 | | c | P1.2 | 1 | | d | P1.3 | 1 | | e | P1.4 | 0 | | f | P1.5 | 1 | | g | P1.6 | 1 | | dp | P1.7 | 0 (don't care, set 0) | ### Step 2: Binary to Hexadecimal Conversion Reading from MSB (P1.7) to LSB (P1.0): $$\text{P1} = \underbrace{0}_{P1.7}\,\underbrace{1}_{P1.6}\,\underbrace{1}_{P1.5}\,\underbrace{0}_{P1.4}\,\underbrace{1}_{P1.3}\,\underbrace{1}_{P1.2}\,\underbrace{0}_{P1.1}\,\underbrace{1}_{P1.0}$$ $$\text{P1} = 0110\,1101_{2} = 6\text{D}_{H}$$ ### Step 3: Verification via Bitwise Operations $$\text{P1}_{\text{set}} = \text{P1}_{\text{old}} \text{ OR } 0100\,1101_{2} = 6\text{D}_{H}$$ $$\text{P1}_{\text{clear}} = \text{P1}_{\text{old}} \text{ AND } \overline{6\text{D}_{H}} = 92\text{H}$$ ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Port Comparison | Feature | Port 0 | Port 1 | Port 2 | Port 3 | | -------------------------------- | ------------ | ---------------- | --------------- | --------------- | | **SFR Address** | 80H | 90H | A0H | B0H | | **Bit-addressable?** | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | | **Internal Pull-up** | No | Yes (~30-40 kΩ) | Yes (~30-40 kΩ) | Yes (~30-40 kΩ) | | **Output Drive Current (typ)** | ~3.2 mA | ~1.6 mA | ~1.6 mA | ~1.6 mA | | **External Memory Role** | AD0–AD7 | Not used | A8–A15 | RD, WR | | **Alternate Functions** | None | None | None | UART, INT, T0/T1| | **External Pull-up Required** | Yes (10 kΩ) | No | No | No | ## 3.4 LED Blinking — Complete Program Walkthrough **Objective:** Continuously blink an LED connected to P1.0 (active-LOW) at 500 ms intervals. ```assembly ORG 0000H START: MOV A, #0FEH ; 1111 1110 → P1.0 = 0 (LED ON) MOV P1, A ACALL DELAY MOV A, #0FFH ; 1111 1111 → P1.0 = 1 (LED OFF) MOV P1, A ACALL DELAY SJMP START DELAY: MOV R0, #0FFH LOOP1: MOV R1, #0FFH LOOP2: DJNZ R1, LOOP2 DJNZ R0, LOOP1 RET END ``` ### Delay Calculation Walkthrough: **Inner loop cycles:** $2 \times 255 = 510$ **Outer loop iterations:** 255 **Inner loop repetitions:** $2 \times 255 \times 255 = 130050$ **Total cycles (excl. MOVs):** $\approx 130050 + 510 = 130560$ cycles $$T_{delay} = 130560 \times 1.085 \text{ μs} = 141.66 \text{ ms}$$ To achieve ~500 ms, this delay must be called **4 times** or the loop count must be quadrupled. > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfall:** Many students forget to set P3.0 (RxD) to `1` before initializing the UART. If P3.0 is `0`, the UART's receive line is stuck LOW and the system cannot receive any serial data. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: 8051 Port Architecture ```mermaid graph TD CPU["8051 CPU Core"] SFR["Special Function Registers (Internal RAM 80H-FFH)"] P0["Port 0 SFR @ 80H"] P1["Port 1 SFR @ 90H"] P2["Port 2 SFR @ A0H"] P3["Port 3 SFR @ B0H"] EXT_MEM["External Memory Bus (A0-A15, D0-D7)"] LED1["LED on P1.0"] LED2["LED on P1.1"] SW1["Switch on P1.7"] LCD["LCD on P0.0-P0.7"] KEYPAD["Keypad on P2.0-P2.7"] UART["UART via P3.0 RxD, P3.1 TxD"] INT["INT0 / INT1 on P3.2, P3.3"] TMR["T0 / T1 on P3.4, P3.5"] RDWR["RD/WR on P3.6, P3.7"] CPU --> SFR SFR --> P0 SFR --> P1 SFR --> P2 SFR --> P3 P0 -->|"AD0-AD7 (multiplexed)"| EXT_MEM P0 -->|"8-bit I/O"| LCD P1 -->|"General I/O"| LED1 P1 -->|"General I/O"| LED2 P1 -->|"General I/O"| SW1 P2 -->|"A8-A15 (Upper Address)"| EXT_MEM P2 -->|"8-bit I/O"| KEYPAD P3 -->|"P3.0"| UART P3 -->|"P3.2, P3.3"| INT P3 -->|"P3.4, P3.5"| TMR P3 -->|"P3.6, P3.7"| RDWR ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Port Programming Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Start Program"] B["Initialize Ports
MOV P1, #0FFH
MOV P3, #0FFH"] C["Configure Peripherals
Set P3 bits for alt function"] D{"Task Type?"} E["Output to P1
MOV P1, #data
SETB P1.0"] F["Read from P1
MOV A, P1
JB / JNB testing"] G["Toggle bits
CPL P1.x
XRL P1, #mask"] H["Delay loop
DJNZ R0, label"] I["Return / Loop"] A --> B --> C --> D D -->|"Write"| E D -->|"Read"| F D -->|"Toggle"| G E --> H F --> H G --> H H --> I I --> D ``` ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: P0 Pin Cell ```mermaid graph LR LATCH["D-Latch
(1-bit memory)"] Q1["Pull-up MOSFET
(Internal)"] Q2["Pull-down MOSFET
(Internal)"] PIN["Physical Pin Px.y"] READ["Internal Read Bus"] WRITE["CPU write
MOV SFR, #data"] --> LATCH LATCH -->|"Q=1"| Q1 LATCH -->|"Q=0"| Q2 Q1 --> PIN Q2 --> PIN PIN --> READ READ --> CPU_READ["CPU read
MOV A, Px"] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation:** The D-latch stores the bit value written by the CPU. When the bit is `1`, Q1 turns ON and raises the pin to Vcc (HIGH). When the bit is `0`, Q2 turns ON and pulls the pin to GND (LOW). The READ bus samples the actual pin voltage, allowing the CPU to read external signals. ## 4.4 Memory-Mapped I/O Address Map ```mermaid graph LR RAM["8051 Internal RAM
(128 bytes: 00H-7FH)"] SFR["SFR Area
(128 bytes: 80H-FFH)"] P0_ADDR["80H Port 0"] SP["81H Stack Pointer"] DPL["82H Data Pointer Low"] DPH["83H Data Pointer High"] P1_ADDR["90H Port 1"] P2_ADDR["A0H Port 2"] P3_ADDR["B0H Port 3"] IE["A8H Interrupt Enable"] TCON["88H Timer Control"] SCON["98H Serial Control"] RAM --> SFR SFR --> P0_ADDR SFR --> P1_ADDR SFR --> P2_ADDR SFR --> P3_ADDR SFR --> SP SFR --> DPL SFR --> DPH SFR --> IE SFR --> TCON SFR --> SCON ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **Q: Differentiate between bit-addressable and byte-addressable ports of 8051 with examples.** **Model Answer:** - **Byte-addressable ports** are accessed using a full 8-bit `MOV` instruction. All four ports P0, P1, P2, and P3 are byte-addressable via their SFR addresses (80H, 90H, A0H, B0H respectively). - **Bit-addressable ports** allow individual pin manipulation using `SETB` and `CLR` instructions. All four ports (P0, P1, P2, P3) are ALSO bit-addressable from addresses 80H–87H, 90H–97H, A0H–A7H, B0H–B7H respectively. - **Example:** `MOV P1, #0FFH` (byte access) vs `SETB P1.0` (bit access). ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **Q: Why does Port 0 require external pull-up resistors while Port 1, Port 2, and Port 3 do not?** **Model Answer:** Port 0 lacks an internal pull-up MOSFET in its output structure. When used as a general-purpose output port (without external memory), the open-drain transistor can only pull the pin LOW; it cannot drive the pin HIGH by itself. An external pull-up resistor (typically **10 kΩ** connected to Vcc) is required to provide the logic HIGH level. In contrast, P1, P2, and P3 have built-in pull-up transistors (≈30–40 kΩ) that automatically drive the pin HIGH when the latch contains a `1`. --- ## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the structure of Port 1 of 8051 with a neat pin diagram. List any four instructions used for port programming with examples. **(b) [7 Marks]** Write an 8051 assembly program to read Port P1.4 and if it reads HIGH, send 55H to Port P0; otherwise send AAH to Port P0. Assume $f_{osc} = 12 \text{ MHz}$. #### Model Solution for (a): **Port 1 Structure:** Port 1 is an 8-bit bidirectional quasi-bidirectional I/O port with: - **SFR address:** 90H - **Bit addresses:** 90H–97H - **Internal pull-up:** ~30–40 kΩ - **No alternate functions** **Pin Cell Block Diagram (P1.y):** Each pin consists of: 1. **D-Latch** — stores the bit value written by CPU 2. **Pull-up FET (Q1)** — internal transistor, turns ON when latch = 1 3. **Pull-down FET (Q2)** — turns ON when latch = 0 4. **Read Buffer** — samples pin voltage back to CPU 5. **Vcc rail** — supplies the pull-up current **Working Principle:** - **Write 1:** Q1 ON, Q2 OFF → pin goes HIGH - **Write 0:** Q1 OFF, Q2 ON → pin goes LOW - **Read:** CPU reads the actual pin voltage (not the latch) **Four Port Programming Instructions:** | Instruction | Example | Function | | ----------------- | ----------------- | ------------------------------ | | `MOV Px, #data` | `MOV P1, #0FFH` | Write byte to entire port | | `SETB Px.y` | `SETB P1.0` | Set a specific bit HIGH | | `CLR Px.y` | `CLR P2.7` | Clear a specific bit LOW | | `CPL Px.y` | `CPL P3.3` | Toggle / complement bit state | #### Model Solution for (b): **Step 1:** Compute machine cycle. $$T_{machine} = \frac{12}{12 \times 10^{6}} = 1 \text{ μs}$$ **Step 2:** Program logic — read P1.4, branch on its value. ```assembly ORG 0000H START: MOV P1, #0FFH ; Configure P1.4 as input LOOP: JB P1.4, SEND_55 ; If P1.4 = 1, branch to SEND_55 MOV P0, #0AAH ; Else send AAH to P0 SJMP LOOP ; Repeat SEND_55: MOV P0, #055H ; Send 55H to P0 SJMP LOOP ; Repeat END ``` **Instruction Cycle Count:** | Instruction | Bytes | Cycles | | --------------- | ----- | ------ | | `MOV P1,#0FFH` | 3 | 1 | | `JB P1.4, addr` | 3 | 2 | | `MOV P0,#0AAH` | 3 | 1 | | `SJMP LOOP` | 2 | 2 | **Explanation:** The `JB` (Jump if Bit) instruction tests P1.4; if HIGH, control transfers to SEND_55 which writes 55H. Otherwise, 0AAH is written to P0. This creates a branch-controlled port write operation. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a) [7 Marks]** List the alternate functions of Port 3 pins. Explain the role of P3.0 and P3.1 with the SCON register. **(b) [7 Marks]** Calculate the time delay generated by the following nested loop, assuming $f_{osc} = 11.0592 \text{ MHz}$. Write the program to toggle all bits of P1 continuously using this delay. ```assembly DELAY: MOV R0, #200 LOOP1: MOV R1, #250 LOOP2: DJNZ R1, LOOP2 DJNZ R0, LOOP1 RET ``` #### Model Solution for (a): **Alternate Functions of Port 3:** | Pin | Function | Description | | ---- | -------- | ------------------------------------ | | P3.0 | RxD | UART Serial Data Input | | P3.1 | TxD | UART Serial Data Output | | P3.2 | INT0 | External Interrupt 0 input | | P3.3 | INT1 | External Interrupt 1 input | | P3.4 | T0 | Timer 0 external input | | P3.5 | T1 | Timer 1 external input | | P3.6 | WR | External data memory write strobe | | P3.7 | RD | External data memory read strobe | **Role of P3.0 (RxD) and P3.1 (TxD) with SCON:** - **P3.0 (RxD)** is the **receiver line** of the built-in UART. It accepts serial data frames bit-by-bit. - **P3.1 (TxD)** is the **transmitter line** that shifts out parallel data as a serial stream. - **SCON (Serial Control, SFR @ 98H)** configures the UART mode: - **SM0, SM1 (bits 7,6):** Mode selection (Mode 1 = 8-bit UART, 9600 baud default) - **REN (bit 4):** Receive Enable (must be set to `1` to receive data) - **TI (bit 1):** Transmit Interrupt flag, set by hardware when byte sent - **RI (bit 0):** Receive Interrupt flag, set by hardware when byte received - **Initialization sequence:** ```assembly MOV SCON, #50H ; Mode 1, REN enabled MOV TMOD, #20H ; Timer 1, Mode 2 (auto-reload) MOV TH1, #0FDH ; 9600 baud at 11.0592 MHz SETB TR1 ; Start Timer 1 SETB P3.0 ; Configure RxD as input SETB P3.1 ; Configure TxD as output ``` #### Model Solution for (b): **Step 1: Compute Machine Cycle** $$T_{machine} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^{6}} = 1.085 \text{ μs}$$ **Step 2: Count Cycles per Layer** - **MOV R1, #250:** 1 cycle per execution - **DJNZ R1, LOOP2:** 2 cycles per iteration → 250 × 2 = 500 cycles per inner loop pass - **DJNZ R0, LOOP1:** 2 cycles per outer iteration **Step 3: Nested Loop Total Cycles** $$N_{inner} = 1 + 250 \times 2 = 501 \text{ cycles per inner loop}$$ $$N_{outer} = 200 \times (1 + 500) = 200 \times 501 = 100{,}200 \text{ cycles}$$ Adding the outer `MOV R0, #200` (1 cycle): $$N_{total} = 1 + 100{,}200 = 100{,}201 \text{ cycles}$$ **Step 4: Total Time Delay** $$T_{delay} = 100{,}201 \times 1.085 \text{ μs} = 108{,}718 \text{ μs} \approx 108.7 \text{ ms}$$ **Step 5: Complete Program to Toggle P1** ```assembly ORG 0000H MOV P1, #00H ; Initial P1 = 00H MAIN: MOV P1, #0FFH ; All P1 bits HIGH ACALL DELAY MOV P1, #00H ; All P1 bits LOW ACALL DELAY SJMP MAIN DELAY: MOV R0, #200 LOOP1: MOV R1, #250 LOOP2: DJNZ R1, LOOP2 DJNZ R0, LOOP1 RET END ``` **Result:** All 8 LEDs connected to P1 will toggle every ~108.7 ms, giving a total ON+OFF cycle of ~217 ms. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > 1. **Forgetting external pull-up on P0:** When interfacing P0 as a simple output (no external memory), examiners deduct **2–3 marks** if students don't mention the need for an external 10 kΩ pull-up resistor. P0 outputs are **open-drain** without it. > 2. **SFR address errors:** A common error is writing P1's address as 80H (which is P0). Always remember: **80=P0, 90=P1, A0=P2, B0=P3**. A single-digit error can cost 2 marks. > 3. **Bit-addressing vs byte-addressing confusion:** `SETB P1.0` and `SETB 90H` are equivalent. Examiners may ask for either form. The symbolic form is preferred in KTU answer sheets. > 4. **Time delay calculation errors:** Using $f_{osc} = 12 \text{ MHz}$ when 11.0592 MHz is specified is a 1–2 mark penalty. The 11.0592 MHz crystal is used specifically to generate standard baud rates (9600, 4800, etc.) for UART. > 5. **P3 alternate function initialization:** Always instruct students to set P3 bits to `1` BEFORE enabling UART, timers, or interrupts. Forgetting this prevents the alternate function from operating. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist** - ☐ **4 ports × 8 pins = 32 I/O lines**; SFRs at **80H, 90H, A0H, B0H** - ☐ **All four ports are bit-addressable** (80H–87H, 90H–97H, A0H–A7H, B0H–B7H) - ☐ **P0 has NO internal pull-up** → requires external **10 kΩ** pull-up - ☐ **P1, P2, P3 have internal pull-ups** (~30–40 kΩ) - ☐ **P0 = multiplexed AD0–AD7** when external memory is used - ☐ **P2 = upper address A8–A15** when external memory is used - ☐ **P3 has 8 alternate functions** (RxD, TxD, INT0, INT1, T0, T1, WR, RD) - ☐ **P3 bits MUST be set to 1** before using alternate functions - ☐ **Machine cycle formula:** $T_{machine} = 12 / f_{osc}$ - ☐ **Single loop delay:** $T = (1 + 2N) \times T_{machine}$ - ☐ **Nested loop delay:** $T = [1 + 2N(1 + 2M)] \times T_{machine}$ - ☐ **Common instructions:** `MOV`, `SETB`, `CLR`, `CPL`, `ANL`, `ORL`, `XRL`, `JB`, `JNB` - ☐ **Active-LOW vs active-HIGH LED convention** must be clearly stated in programs - ☐ **P1.0 must be configured** (set/cleared) appropriately when used as input or output - ☐ **Always initialize ports** at the start of every 8051 program to avoid floating pin issues - ☐ **11.0592 MHz crystal** is preferred for accurate UART baud rates - ☐ **`CPL Px.y` is used for toggling** — useful for blinking applications - ☐ **`JB Px.y, label` tests bit = 1**; **`JNB` tests bit = 0** — critical for switch interfacing - ☐ **DJNZ is 2 machine cycles**; **MOV direct,#data is 1 machine cycle**; **SJMP is 2 cycles** - ☐ **Bit manipulation is more efficient** than masking in terms of program memory and execution time

topics.title: Code Conversion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT403 - COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE AND MICROCONTROLLER
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Code Conversion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Code Conversion** is the process of translating a numeric or alphanumeric data item from one binary-coded representation (source code) to an equivalent value in another binary-coded representation (destination code), without altering the underlying logical meaning of the information.

In digital systems, the same decimal value **N = 150** can be physically stored as:

- Pure binary : `10010110`
- **BCD (8421)** : `0001 0101 0000`
- **Excess-3** : `0100 1000 0011`
- **Gray Code** : `11000111`
- **ASCII** : `31 35 30` (hex)
- **7-Segment pattern** : segment-drive byte(s)

Because different subsystems (keyboards, displays, processors, memory, communication lines) "speak" different codes, the architecture *must* include conversion logic — either in hardware (encoders/decoders) or in software (microcontroller firmware).

## 1.2 Why We Need Code Conversion (Engineering Motivation)

| Subsystem | Native Code | Why Conversion Required |
|-----------|-------------|--------------------------|
| Human operator | Decimal / ASCII | Decimal arithmetic is natural; ASCII is the universal character code |
| 7-Segment LED / LCD | Segment pattern | Display drivers cannot understand BCD directly |
| K-map simplification | Gray code | Only one bit changes between adjacent cells |
| Position encoders / Shaft encoders | Gray code | Eliminates race-around errors during transition |
| Processor ALU | Pure Binary | Arithmetic operations are binary-efficient |
| Memory storage | BCD / Pure Binary | BCD preserves decimal precision in financial/scientific work |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Translator at a Conference"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine an international conference where the **Japanese speaker** (your keyboard input) wants to talk to the **German audience** (a 7-segment display). They do not share a common alphabet. A **human translator** (the code-conversion circuit / microcode routine) listens to one language and immediately re-speaks the same idea in the other language. The *meaning* (the number itself) never changes — only the *symbols used to express it* change.

## 1.4 Key Codes Used in KTU Syllabus

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus-Mandated Codes (PCMRT403 – Module 3):**
> 1. **BCD (Binary-Coded Decimal)** – 8421 weighted code, 4 bits per decimal digit.
> 2. **Excess-3 (XS3)** – Self-complementing code, derived by adding `0011₂` to BCD.
> 3. **Gray Code** – Unit-distance / reflected code; used in K-maps and position encoders.
> 4. **ASCII (American Standard Code for Information Interchange)** – 7-bit alphanumeric code.
> 5. **7-Segment Code** – Byte pattern that lights the correct segments of a display.

## 1.5 Distinction Between *Weighted* and *Non-Weighted* Codes

| Property | BCD (8421) | Excess-3 | Gray |
|----------|------------|----------|------|
| Weighted? | **Yes** (8-4-2-1) | No (derived) | No (reflected) |
| Self-complementing? | No | **Yes** | No |
| Single-bit change between successive codes? | No | No | **Yes** |
| Direct arithmetic use? | Limited (needs adjustment) | Limited | Not suitable |
| KTU relevance | Very High | High | Very High |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Master Conversions (The KTU Pentagram)

Every KTU Module-3 Code-Conversion problem revolves around these five transformations:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(i)} \;\; & \text{BCD} \;\longleftrightarrow\; \text{Binary} \\[2pt]
\text{(ii)} \;\; & \text{BCD} \;\longleftrightarrow\; \text{Excess-3} \\[2pt]
\text{(iii)} \;\; & \text{Binary} \;\longleftrightarrow\; \text{Gray} \\[2pt]
\text{(iv)} \;\; & \text{ASCII} \;\longleftrightarrow\; \text{BCD / HEX} \\[2pt]
\text{(v)} \;\; & \text{BCD} \;\longlongrightarrow\; \text{7-Segment pattern}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic Behind Each Conversion

### A. BCD → Binary (Weighted Sum Method)
1. **Group** the BCD input into 4-bit nibbles, one per decimal digit.
2. **Multiply** each nibble by its positional weight: `dₙ × 10ⁿ + dₙ₋₁ × 10ⁿ⁻¹ + … + d₀ × 10⁰`.
3. **Add** all products; the sum is the equivalent pure binary number.

> **Why it works:** BCD preserves decimal digits in binary form; positional multiplication by `10ⁿ` recreates the decimal place-value.

### B. Binary → BCD (Double-Dabble / Shift-and-Add-3 Algorithm)
1. **Initialise:** Clear BCD register, load binary number.
2. **Inspect** the next MSB of the binary number.
3. **Shift Left** all BCD nibbles by 1 bit, inserting that MSB.
4. **Test & Adjust:** If any BCD nibble is `≥ 5`, add `3` to that nibble.
5. **Repeat** steps 2–4 until all binary bits are exhausted.
6. The BCD register now holds the equivalent BCD value.

> **Why "Add 3 if ≥ 5":** A BCD nibble can hold only `0–9`. When a shift causes a nibble to exceed `9` (i.e., reaches `10–15`), the half-carry / carry propagation is forced by pre-adding `3` so the next shift rolls the overflow cleanly into the next decade.

### C. BCD → Excess-3 (and Excess-3 → BCD)
- **BCD → XS3:** Add `0011₂` (decimal **3**) to every 4-bit BCD nibble.
- **XS3 → BCD:** Subtract `0011₂` (decimal **3**) from every 4-bit XS3 nibble.

> **Why Excess-3 is self-complementing:** `9's complement of XS3 digit = (9 − d) + 3`. Since `9 = (1111₂)`, the bitwise NOT of an XS3 digit equals the XS3 representation of its 9's complement.

### D. Binary → Gray Code
- The MSB of Gray = MSB of Binary.
- Every subsequent Gray bit = XOR of the corresponding Binary bit with the Binary bit to its **left** (more significant).

$$
G_i = B_i \oplus B_{i+1} \quad \text{for } i = 0, 1, \ldots, n-2, \qquad G_{n-1} = B_{n-1}
$$

### E. Gray Code → Binary
- The MSB of Binary = MSB of Gray.
- Every subsequent Binary bit = XOR of the corresponding Gray bit with the **previous Binary bit** (to its left).

$$
B_{n-1} = G_{n-1}, \qquad B_i = G_i \oplus B_{i+1} \quad \text{for } i = n-2, \ldots, 0
$$

### F. ASCII → Hexadecimal Digit
- ASCII code for `'0'…'9'` lies in `30H–39H`. Strip the upper nibble (`3`).
- ASCII code for `'A'…'F'` lies in `41H–46H`. Subtract `37H` to get the hex value `0AH–0FH`.

### G. BCD → 7-Segment (a-g) — Decoder IC 7447 / 7448
The decoder activates segments according to the truth table:

| BCD (D C B A) | a | b | c | d | e | f | g | Display |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| 0000 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 | `0` |
| 0001 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | `1` |
| 0010 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 | `2` |
| 0011 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | `3` |
| 0100 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | `4` |
| 0101 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 | `5` |
| 0110 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | `6` |
| 0111 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | `7` |
| 1000 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | `8` |
| 1001 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 | `9` |

> `1` = segment ON, `0` = segment OFF. Common-anode displays use an **inverted** logic (`0` = ON).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Reference)

| # | Conversion | Formula / Rule | Engineering Use |
|:-:|:-----------|:---------------|:----------------|
| 1 | BCD → Binary | $N = \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} d_i \cdot 10^{i}$ | Financial / decimal display systems |
| 2 | Binary → BCD | Double-Dabble (Shift + Add 3 if digit ≥ 5) | Decimal I/O for microcontrollers |
| 3 | BCD → XS3 | Add `0011₂` to each nibble | Subtraction using 9's complement |
| 4 | XS3 → BCD | Subtract `0011₂` from each nibble | Recovery of BCD after arithmetic |
| 5 | Binary → Gray | $G_i = B_i \oplus B_{i+1}$, MSB preserved | K-maps, shaft encoders |
| 6 | Gray → Binary | $B_{n-1} = G_{n-1}$; $B_i = G_i \oplus B_{i+1}$ | Position decoding |
| 7 | ASCII `'0'–'9'` → Dec | Mask with `0FH` | Keyboard-to-binary conversion |
| 8 | ASCII `'A'–'F'` → Hex | Subtract `37H` | Hex keypad decoding |
| 9 | BCD → 7-Seg | Apply 7447 / 7448 truth table | LED / LCD display driving |
| 10 | XOR identity | $x \oplus 0 = x, \; x \oplus x = 0$ | Foundation of Gray conversion |

> **Memory anchor for the examination:** *"BCD ⇄ Binary uses arithmetic; Binary ⇄ Gray uses only XOR; BCD ⇄ XS3 uses ±3; ASCII ⇄ Hex uses mask/subtract."*

## 2.4 Engineering Utility Beyond the Exam

- **Embedded displays** (ATmega, 8051, 8086 kits): BCD → 7-segment routines are mandatory.
- **CNC machines & robotics**: Optical / magnetic encoders emit Gray code to avoid transition glitches.
- **POS terminals & calculators**: Internal BCD arithmetic prevents floating-point rounding of currency.
- **Communication protocols (UART, SPI text frames)**: ASCII framing requires hex conversion in firmware.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — BCD → Binary (KTU Favourite)

> **Convert `(1001 0110)` BCD into pure Binary.**

The BCD number has two nibbles: `1001` (high) and `0110` (low).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{High nibble} &= 1001_2 = 9_{10} \\
\text{Low nibble}  &= 0110_2 = 6_{10} \\
\text{Decimal value} &= 9 \times 10^{1} + 6 \times 10^{0} \\
                     &= 90 + 6 = 96_{10}
\end{aligned}
$$

Now convert `96₁₀` to binary:

$$
\begin{aligned}
96 \div 2 &= 48 \text{ remainder } 0 \quad (b_0) \\
48 \div 2 &= 24 \text{ remainder } 0 \quad (b_1) \\
24 \div 2 &= 12 \text{ remainder } 0 \quad (b_2) \\
12 \div 2 &= 6  \text{ remainder } 0 \quad (b_3) \\
6  \div 2 &= 3  \text{ remainder } 0 \quad (b_4) \\
3  \div 2 &= 1  \text{ remainder } 1 \quad (b_5) \\
1  \div 2 &= 0  \text{ remainder } 1 \quad (b_6) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Reading remainders bottom-up:

$$
(1001\;0110)_{\text{BCD}} = (0110\;0000)_2 = 96_{10}
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Binary → BCD using Double-Dabble

> **Convert `(1110100)₂` = 116₁₀ into BCD.**

**Setup:** We have 7 binary bits and need 3 BCD digits (hundreds, tens, units) → 12 BCD bits.

```
BCD registers: [ D2 D1 D0 ]   each 4 bits   →  [ 0000 | 0000 | 0000 ]
Binary input:  [ 1 1 1 0 1 0 0 ]
```

### Iteration Table

| Step | Action | D2 (Hundreds) | D1 (Tens) | D0 (Units) | Shifted-in bit |
|:----:|:-------|:--------------|:----------|:-----------|:---------------|
| 0 | Initial | 0000 | 0000 | 0000 | – |
| 1 | Shift left, insert MSB `1` | 0000 | 0000 | 0001 | `1` from binary |
| 2 | Test: D0 = 1 (<5) → No adjust. Shift, insert `1` | 0000 | 0001 | 0010 | `1` from binary |
| 3 | D0=2, D1=1 (<5). Shift, insert `1` | 0000 | 0010 | 0101 | `1` from binary |
| 4 | **D0=5 ≥ 5 ⇒ Add 3 to D0** → `1000`. Shift, insert `0` | 0000 | 0101 | 0000 | `0` from binary |
| 5 | D1=5 ≥ 5 ⇒ Add 3 to D1 → `1000`. Shift, insert `1` | 0001 | 0000 | 0001 | `1` from binary |
| 6 | D0=1 (<5). Shift, insert `0` | 0010 | 0001 | 0010 | `0` from binary |
| 7 | D0=2, D1=1, D2=2 (<5). Shift, insert `0` | 0100 | 0010 | 0100 | `0` from binary |

After all 7 shifts the BCD register holds:

$$
D2\,D1\,D0 = (0001\;0001\;0110)_{\text{BCD}}
$$

Reading: **Hundreds = 1, Tens = 1, Units = 6** → `116₁₀` ✔

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Binary → Gray and Gray → Binary

> **Let Binary = `10110110`. Compute Gray and verify by inverting.**

### Forward: Binary → Gray

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_7 &= B_7 = 1 \\
G_6 &= B_7 \oplus B_6 = 1 \oplus 0 = 1 \\
G_5 &= B_6 \oplus B_5 = 0 \oplus 1 = 1 \\
G_4 &= B_5 \oplus B_4 = 1 \oplus 1 = 0 \\
G_3 &= B_4 \oplus B_3 = 1 \oplus 0 = 1 \\
G_2 &= B_3 \oplus B_2 = 0 \oplus 1 = 1 \\
G_1 &= B_2 \oplus B_1 = 1 \oplus 1 = 0 \\
G_0 &= B_1 \oplus B_0 = 1 \oplus 0 = 1 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Gray Code} = 11101101}
$$

### Reverse: Gray → Binary (Verification)

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_7 &= G_7 = 1 \\
B_6 &= G_6 \oplus B_7 = 1 \oplus 1 = 0 \\
B_5 &= G_5 \oplus B_6 = 1 \oplus 0 = 1 \\
B_4 &= G_4 \oplus B_5 = 0 \oplus 1 = 1 \\
B_3 &= G_3 \oplus B_4 = 1 \oplus 1 = 0 \\
B_2 &= G_2 \oplus B_3 = 1 \oplus 0 = 1 \\
B_1 &= G_1 \oplus B_2 = 0 \oplus 1 = 1 \\
B_0 &= G_0 \oplus B_1 = 1 \oplus 1 = 0 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Recovered binary: `10110110` ✔ (matches the original).

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — ASCII → Hex

> **Decode the ASCII string `"A3F"` into hexadecimal nibbles.**

From the ASCII table:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{`A'} &= 41\text{H} \quad\Rightarrow\quad 41\text{H} - 37\text{H} = 0\text{AH} \\
\text{`3'} &= 33\text{H} \quad\Rightarrow\quad 33\text{H} - 30\text{H} = 03\text{H} \\
\text{`F'} &= 46\text{H} \quad\Rightarrow\quad 46\text{H} - 37\text{H} = 0\text{FH} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{ASCII "A3F"} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Hex } 0\text{A3F}}
$$

> **Memory anchor:** *"Numeric ASCII = `30H + digit`; Alphabetic ASCII = `37H + hex value`."*

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 5 — BCD → 7-Segment Pattern

> **Display the BCD digit `0110` (decimal 6) on a common-cathode 7-segment LED.**

From the table in §2.2-G:

$$
a\,b\,c\,d\,e\,f\,g \;=\; 1\,0\,1\,1\,1\,1\,1 \;=\; 5\text{FH}
$$

> Verify by inspection: a ON, b OFF (top-right off), c ON, d/e/f ON (lower box complete), g ON — this spells `6`.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Conversion at a Glance

| Input Given | Output Required | Conversion Algorithm | Difficulty for KTU |
|:------------|:----------------|:---------------------|:-------------------|
| BCD nibbles | Pure binary | Weighted sum by `10ⁿ` | Easy |
| Pure binary | BCD nibbles | Double-dabble shift-and-add-3 | Moderate |
| Decimal digit | Excess-3 nibble | Add `0011₂` | Easy |
| Excess-3 nibble | Decimal digit | Subtract `0011₂` | Easy |
| Binary n-bit | Gray n-bit | XOR chain from MSB | Easy |
| Gray n-bit | Binary n-bit | XOR chain from MSB | Easy |
| ASCII char | Hex nibble | Mask `0FH` or subtract `37H` | Easy |
| BCD nibble | 7-seg byte | Lookup / truth table | Easy |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Generic Code Converter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src["SOURCE CODE\nInput Register"] --> dec["DECODER\nn to 2^n minterms"]
    dec --> enc["ENCODER\nm-bit output word"]
    enc --> dst["DESTINATION CODE\nOutput Register"]

    src -.label.-> L1["e.g. BCD 4 bits"]
    enc -.label.-> L2["e.g. Excess-3 / 7-seg"]
```

> **Read this as:** Any code conversion can be realised by a ROM-based **decoder–encoder** pair. The decoder expands the input into minterms; the encoder ORs the appropriate minterms to drive the output code.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — BCD → 7-Segment Display Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    cpu["Microcontroller / CPU\nstores BCD digit"] --> bus["8-bit Data Bus"]
    bus --> ic7447["Decoder IC 7447\nBCD to 7-Segment"]
    ic7447 -->|a,b,c,d,e,f,g| led["7-Segment LED\n(common anode)"]
    led --> user["Decimal digit\nvisible to user"]
```

> **Engineering note:** The 7447 is an *open-collector* BCD-to-7-segment decoder; current-limiting resistors (typically **220 Ω**) are mandatory between each output and the LED anode.

## 4.3 Flowchart — Double-Dabble (Binary → BCD) Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START\nClear BCD register\nLoad binary into shift register"] --> B["Counter = n bits ?"]
    B -- "No" --> C["Examine next MSB of binary"]
    C --> D["Shift BCD register LEFT by 1\nInsert the MSB as LSB"]
    D --> E["For each BCD nibble:\nIs nibble >= 5 ?"]
    E -- "Yes" --> F["Add 3 to that nibble"]
    E -- "No"  --> G["Leave nibble unchanged"]
    F --> H["Decrement bit counter"]
    G --> H
    H --> B
    B -- "Yes (all bits done)" --> I["STOP\nBCD register holds result"]
```

## 4.4 XOR Chain — Visualising Binary ↔ Gray Conversion

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    b7["B7"] --> xor6((XOR))
    b6["B6"] --> xor6
    b6 --> xor5((XOR))
    b5["B5"] --> xor5
    b5 --> xor4((XOR))
    b4["B4"] --> xor4
    b4 --> xor3((XOR))
    b3["B3"] --> xor3
    b3 --> xor2((XOR))
    b2["B2"] --> xor2
    b2 --> xor1((XOR))
    b1["B1"] --> xor1
    b1 --> xor0((XOR))
    b0["B0"] --> xor0

    xor6 --> g6["G6"]
    xor5 --> g5["G5"]
    xor4 --> g4["G4"]
    xor3 --> g3["G3"]
    xor2 --> g2["G2"]
    xor1 --> g1["G1"]
    xor0 --> g0["G0"]
    b7 --> g7["G7 = B7 (direct)"]
```

## 4.5 Code-Conversion Subsystem within a Microcontroller Kit

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph User["User Environment"]
        key["Hex Keypad\nemits ASCII"]
        sw["DIP Switch\nemits BCD"]
    end

    subgraph Core["Microcontroller 8051 / 8086"]
        kproc["ASCII to Hex routine\nMask 0FH / Subtract 37H"]
        bproc["BCD to Binary routine\nWeighted sum"]
        dproc["Double-Dabble routine\nShift + Add 3 if >= 5"]
        gproc["Gray to Binary routine\nXOR chain"]
        xproc["BCD to XS3 routine\nAdd 3 to each nibble"]
    end

    subgraph Out["Output Devices"]
        led7["7-Segment Display\nvia 7447"]
        lcd["LCD module\nshows ASCII"]
        uart["UART link\ntransmits ASCII"]
    end

    key --> kproc
    sw --> bproc
    bproc --> gproc
    bproc --> dproc
    bproc --> xproc
    kproc --> led7
    xproc --> led7
    dproc --> lcd
    gproc --> uart
```

> **Read this as:** A real microcontroller kit exposes *all five KTU code conversions* through small firmware routines; each one is a few assembly instructions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. In **Binary → BCD**, forgetting to apply the *"Add 3 if nibble ≥ 5"* rule *before* the shift on the *next* iteration → entire BCD register goes wrong.
> 2. In **Gray → Binary**, XOR-ing the previous *Gray* bit instead of the previous *Binary* bit → wrong recovery.
> 3. In **ASCII → Hex**, masking numeric ASCII with `0FH` works for `'0'–'9'` but **NOT** for `'A'–'F'` — must subtract `37H`.
> 4. In **BCD → Binary**, multiplying the *high* nibble by `10`, not by `16` — BCD is decimal, not hex.
> 5. Failing to mention the **self-complementing** property of Excess-3 when asked — examiner allocates 1 mark for that key phrase.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]`
**Define BCD and Excess-3 codes. Why is Excess-3 called a self-complementing code?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **BCD (Binary-Coded Decimal, 8421 code):** A 4-bit weighted code in which each decimal digit `0–9` is represented by its natural 4-bit binary equivalent. *(1 mark)*
- **Excess-3 (XS3):** An unweighted code obtained by adding binary `0011` (decimal **3**) to each BCD digit. Thus decimal `0 → 0011`, `9 → 1100`. *(1 mark)*
- **Self-complementing property:** The 1's complement of an XS3 digit equals the XS3 representation of the 9's complement of the original decimal digit, because `9 − d = (1111) − (d + 0011) = bitwise-NOT of XS3(d)`. Hence 9's complement for subtraction is obtained trivially by inverting all bits. *(1 mark)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3]`
**State the rules to convert an n-bit Binary number to Gray code and vice-versa.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Binary → Gray:** The MSB of Gray equals the MSB of Binary. Every other Gray bit $G_i$ is the XOR of the corresponding Binary bit $B_i$ with the **next-more-significant** Binary bit $B_{i+1}$, i.e. $G_i = B_i \oplus B_{i+1}$. *(1.5 marks)*
- **Gray → Binary:** The MSB of Binary equals the MSB of Gray. Every subsequent Binary bit $B_i$ is the XOR of the corresponding Gray bit $G_i$ with the **previously recovered** Binary bit $B_{i+1}$, i.e. $B_i = G_i \oplus B_{i+1}$. *(1.5 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]`

**(a)** Convert the decimal number `486` into (i) BCD, (ii) Excess-3, and (iii) Binary. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the *Double-Dabble* algorithm. Using it, convert `(11010110)₂` into BCD. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to A(a):

**(i) BCD of 486:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
4 &\rightarrow 0100 \\
8 &\rightarrow 1000 \\
6 &\rightarrow 0110 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{(486)_{10} = (0100\;1000\;0110)_{\text{BCD}}}
$$

**(ii) Excess-3 of 486:** Add `0011` to each BCD nibble.

$$
\begin{aligned}
0100 + 0011 &= 0111 \\
1000 + 0011 &= 1011 \\
0110 + 0011 &= 1001 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{(486)_{10} = (0111\;1011\;1001)_{\text{XS3}}}
$$

**(iii) Binary of 486:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
256 + 128 + 64 + 32 + 4 + 2 &= 486 \\
2^8 + 2^7 + 2^6 + 2^5 + 2^2 + 2^1 &= 486 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{(486)_{10} = (111100110)_2}
$$

*(1 mark each for (i) and (ii); 4 marks for (iii) with full expansion shown.)*

---

#### Solution to A(b):

**Double-Dabble Algorithm Steps (3 marks for explanation):**
1. Initialise the BCD result register to zeros.
2. Inspect the next MSB of the binary source.
3. Shift the BCD register left by one bit, shifting in the inspected MSB.
4. After shifting, scan every BCD nibble: if any nibble is `≥ 5` (i.e., `0101` or higher), add `3` (i.e., `0011`) to that nibble.
5. Decrement the bit counter. Repeat from step 2 until all source bits are processed.

**Application to `(11010110)₂` = 214₁₀ — expected BCD = `0010 0001 0100`**

| Iter | Shifted-in Bit | D2 (Hundreds) | D1 (Tens) | D0 (Units) | Adjustment |
|:----:|:--------------:|:-------------:|:---------:|:----------:|:----------:|
| 0 | — | 0000 | 0000 | 0000 | — |
| 1 | 1 | 0000 | 0000 | 0001 | — |
| 2 | 1 | 0000 | 0001 | 0010 | — |
| 3 | 0 | 0000 | 0010 | 0100 | — |
| 4 | 1 | 0000 | 0101 | 0001 | D1=5≥5 → add 3 → 1000 |
| 5 | 0 | 0000 | 1000 | 0010 | — |
| 6 | 1 | 0001 | 0000 | 0101 | D0=5≥5 → add 3 → 1000 |
| 7 | 1 | 0010 | 0001 | 0000 | — |
| 8 | 0 | 0100 | 0010 | 0000 | — |

Final result:

$$
\boxed{(11010110)_2 = (0010\;0001\;0100)_{\text{BCD}} = 214_{10}}
$$

*(4 marks for the iteration table.)*

---

### Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary]`

**(a)** Convert `(10110110)₂` into (i) Gray code and (ii) Excess-3 of its BCD equivalent. Show all working. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the ASCII code for hexadecimal digits. Convert the ASCII string `"C4B"` into its hexadecimal value. Design the 7-segment pattern for the hex digit `C` (i.e., the digit 12) on a common-cathode display. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to B(a):

**(i) Binary → Gray** for `B = 10110110` (MSB to LSB):

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_7 &= B_7 = 1 \\
G_6 &= B_7 \oplus B_6 = 1 \oplus 0 = 1 \\
G_5 &= B_6 \oplus B_5 = 0 \oplus 1 = 1 \\
G_4 &= B_5 \oplus B_4 = 1 \oplus 1 = 0 \\
G_3 &= B_4 \oplus B_3 = 1 \oplus 0 = 1 \\
G_2 &= B_3 \oplus B_2 = 0 \oplus 1 = 1 \\
G_1 &= B_2 \oplus B_1 = 1 \oplus 1 = 0 \\
G_0 &= B_1 \oplus B_0 = 1 \oplus 0 = 1 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Gray} = (11101101)}
$$

*(3 marks for XOR chain.)*

**(ii) BCD of `(10110110)₂`:**
First convert binary to decimal:

$$
128 + 32 + 16 + 4 + 2 = 182_{10}
$$

Now BCD: `1 → 0001`, `8 → 1000`, `2 → 0010`.

$$
\boxed{\text{BCD} = (0001\;1000\;0010)}
$$

Excess-3 of this BCD — add `0011` to each nibble:

$$
\begin{aligned}
0001 + 0011 &= 0100 \\
1000 + 0011 &= 1011 \\
0010 + 0011 &= 0101 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Excess-3} = (0100\;1011\;0101)}
$$

*(4 marks for the conversion chain.)*

---

#### Solution to B(b):

**ASCII for hexadecimal digits:** ASCII is a 7-bit code. Hex digits `0–9` use ASCII `30H–39H`; hex digits `A–F` use ASCII `41H–46H`. To convert: numeric ASCII → mask with `0FH`; alphabetic ASCII → subtract `37H`. *(2 marks)*

**Decoding `"C4B"`:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{'C'} &= 43\text{H} - 37\text{H} = 0\text{CH} \\
\text{'4'} &= 34\text{H} - 30\text{H} = 04\text{H} \\
\text{'B'} &= 42\text{H} - 37\text{H} = 0\text{BH} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{ASCII "C4B"} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Hex } 0\text{C4B} = 3147_{10}}
$$

*(2 marks for the arithmetic.)*

**7-segment pattern for hex digit `C` (= decimal 12):**

Hex `C` has the segments:

$$
a = 1,\; b = 0,\; c = 0,\; d = 1,\; e = 1,\; f = 1,\; g = 0
$$

Reading MSB → LSB as `g f e d c b a` (standard 7448 ordering):

$$
\boxed{\text{Pattern} = (1001110)_2 = 4\text{EH}}
$$

*(3 marks for correct segment activation + final byte value.)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Must-Remember Bullets (last-minute revision):**
> - **BCD (8421)** is a 4-bit weighted code; only digits `0–9` are valid (codes `1010–1111` are *invalid* / forbidden).
> - **Excess-3** = BCD + `0011`; it is **self-complementing**, making 9's complement subtraction trivial.
> - **Double-Dabble** = shift-and-add-3-if-≥5 algorithm for **Binary → BCD**.
> - **Binary → Gray** uses **XOR with the more-significant neighbour**; MSB passes through unchanged.
> - **Gray → Binary** uses **XOR with the previously recovered Binary bit**; MSB passes through unchanged.
> - **Gray code** is essential for K-maps and position encoders because only one bit changes at a time.
> - **ASCII '0'–'9'** = `30H–39H` → strip the `3` (mask `0FH`).
> - **ASCII 'A'–'F'** = `41H–46H` → subtract `37H` to obtain hex values `0AH–0FH`.
> - **7-segment decoding** uses ICs **7447** (active-low output, common-anode LED) or **7448** (active-high, common-cathode).
> - **Conversion by arithmetic** = BCD⇄Binary, BCD⇄XS3, ASCII⇄Hex.
> - **Conversion by XOR chain** = Binary⇄Gray.
> - **Conversion by ROM lookup** = BCD→7-Segment (any arbitrary pattern).
> - **Standard exam pitfall**: never use hex multiplications for BCD; the base is **10**, not **16**.
> - **Engineering tagline**: *"Arithmetic for value codes, XOR for distance codes, lookup for display codes."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interfacing of Stepper Motor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT403 - COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE AND MICROCONTROLLER
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Interfacing of Stepper Motor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 — Interfacing of Stepper Motor

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> A **Stepper Motor** is a brushless, synchronous DC electromechanical device that converts a series of digital electrical pulses into discrete mechanical rotational steps. Each input pulse advances the rotor by a fixed angular increment known as the **Step Angle** ($\theta_s$). In the context of the 8051 microcontroller, *interfacing* refers to the hardware circuitry (using a driver IC like **ULN2003** or **L293D**) and the software logic (sequential excitation of stator coils) required to precisely control the rotation angle, direction, and speed of the motor shaft.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **clock with a second hand that jumps once every second** — never gliding smoothly. Each tick is a fixed, repeatable mechanical increment. A stepper motor behaves exactly like this clock hand, but the "ticks" are caused by electrical pulses sent by the microcontroller, not gravity.

A simpler analogy: think of a **four-person rowing boat** with seats labeled **A, B, C, D**. The boat moves forward only when someone pulls the oar. If we make the rowers pull in the sequence **A → B → C → D → A → B ...**, the boat rotates clockwise. If we reverse the sequence to **A → D → C → B → A ...**, the boat rotates counter-clockwise. The microcontroller does the equivalent of calling out "Pull! Pull! Pull!" in a specific order. The *faster* the calling rate, the faster the boat rotates — and every single call produces exactly one step.

| Term | Value / Symbol |
|---|---|
| Standard Step Angle | **1.8°** (most common) |
| Steps per Revolution | **200** (for 1.8° motor) |
| Operating Voltage (typical) | **5 V – 12 V DC** |
| Standard Driver IC | **ULN2003** (Darlington array) |
| Coil Resistance (typical) | **~ 30 Ω – 100 Ω** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Students must clearly understand the difference between **Variable Reluctance (VR)**, **Permanent Magnet (PM)**, and **Hybrid (HB)** stepper motors, along with the **four excitation sequences**: *Full-Step Wave (One-Phase-On)*, *Full-Step (Two-Phase-On)*, and *Half-Step*.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Stepper Motors

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Focus:** Out of the three types below, **PM and VR types** are most frequently asked in 2-mark and short-answer questions.

**1. Variable Reluctance (VR) Stepper Motor**
- The rotor is made of **soft iron** with salient poles (no permanent magnet).
- It rotates to the position of **minimum magnetic reluctance** when a stator phase is energized.
- Very high step resolution (e.g., 15°) but produces low torque.
- Used in instrumentation requiring fine, low-torque positioning.

**2. Permanent Magnet (PM) Stepper Motor**
- The rotor is a **permanent magnet** (typically radially magnetized).
- Step angles are coarser (commonly 45° or 90°), but torque is much higher.
- Polarity of excitation determines the direction of rotation.
- **Most commonly interfaced with 8051 microcontrollers** in KTU labs.

**3. Hybrid (HB) Stepper Motor**
- Combines features of both PM and VR.
- Has a magnetized rotor plus toothed iron caps to concentrate flux.
- Small step angles (**0.9° or 1.8°**) and high holding torque.
- Industry standard for CNC and robotics.

### 2.2 Construction of a PM Stepper Motor (KTU Lab Standard)

A 4-phase PM stepper motor typically has:
- **4 stator windings** (phases): A, B, C, D, physically placed 90° apart.
- A **2-pole permanent magnet rotor** (N–S).
- The two end terminals of each coil are brought out: **A, A̅, B, B̅, C, C̅, D, D̅** (or in a common-cathode/common-anode configuration).

In practice, most 4-wire unipolar motors (like the one interfaced in KTU labs) have the **common point of all four coils tied together to +Vcc**, and each phase is grounded individually through the driver to energize it.

### 2.3 Step Angle — The Heart of the Motor

The **Step Angle ($\theta_s$)** is the discrete angular displacement produced by one input pulse.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Formula:** Memorize the following relation — it appears in nearly every Part-B question.

$$
\theta_s \;=\; \frac{360^\circ}{N \cdot m}
$$

Where:
- $N$ = **Number of stator teeth (or phases, depending on motor type)**
- $m$ = **Number of rotor poles**

For a typical PM stepper with **4 phases** and **2 rotor poles**:

$$
\theta_s \;=\; \frac{360^\circ}{4 \times 2} \;=\; 45^\circ
$$

For a typical **1.8° hybrid motor** (used in industry, 200 steps/rev):

$$
\theta_s \;=\; \frac{360^\circ}{200} \;=\; 1.8^\circ
$$

### 2.4 Stepping Sequences (The "Rowing Pattern")

Let the four phases be A, B, C, D, energized by port lines **P1.0, P1.1, P1.2, P1.3** of the 8051.

| Sequence Type | P1.3 (D) | P1.2 (C) | P1.1 (B) | P1.0 (A) | Hex | Step Angle Factor |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Full-Step Wave (1-phase-on)** | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0x01 | 1× |
| | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0x02 | 1× |
| | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0x04 | 1× |
| | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0x08 | 1× |
| **Full-Step (2-phase-on)** | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0x03 | 1× (more torque) |
| | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0x06 | 1× |
| | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0x0C | 1× |
| | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0x09 | 1× |
| **Half-Step (alternating)** | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0x01 | 0.5× |
| | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0x03 | 0.5× |
| | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0x02 | 0.5× |
| | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0x06 | 0.5× |
| | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0x04 | 0.5× |
| | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0x0C | 0.5× |
| | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0x08 | 0.5× |
| | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0x09 | 0.5× |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why two-phase-on has more torque:** When two adjacent phases are energized simultaneously, the rotor aligns with the **vector sum** of the two magnetic fields, producing roughly **40% more holding torque** than wave drive.

> [!WARNING]
> **Reverse the table order (bottom-up) to reverse rotation direction.** This is a classic 3-mark question: "How do you change the direction of a stepper motor in software?"

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Step Angle | $\theta_s = \dfrac{360^\circ}{N \cdot m}$ | degrees | $N$ = stator teeth, $m$ = rotor poles |
| 2 | Steps per Revolution | $S = \dfrac{360^\circ}{\theta_s}$ | steps/rev | For 1.8° motor: $S = 200$ |
| 3 | Stepping Rate (pulses/sec) | $f_p = \dfrac{S \cdot N_{\text{rev}}}{60}$ | Hz | Pulses required per second |
| 4 | Motor Speed (RPM) | $N_{\text{rev}} = \dfrac{f_p \cdot 60}{S}$ | RPM | Reverse of above |
| 5 | Time per Step (delay) | $T_{\text{step}} = \dfrac{1}{f_p}$ | seconds | Determines shaft speed |
| 6 | Half-Step effective angle | $\theta_{\text{half}} = \dfrac{\theta_s}{2}$ | degrees | E.g., 0.9° for 1.8° motor |
| 7 | Holding Torque | $T_h$ (motor datasheet) | N·m | Critical for vertical loads |
| 8 | Resonant Speed | $f_{\text{res}}$ (avoid!) | Hz | Causes loss of synchronism |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **CNC Machines & 3D Printers:** Position control of X-Y-Z axes.
> - **Hard Disk Drives:** Read/write head actuator (very fine 1.8° steps).
> - **Robotics:** Wheel odometry and joint positioning.
> - **Printers/Plotters:** Paper feed and print head positioning.
> - **Medical Equipment:** Syringe pumps, sample stages.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Step Angle from Motor Geometry

**Problem Statement:** A 4-phase PM stepper motor has 6 stator teeth per phase. The rotor has 4 poles. Determine the step angle and the number of pulses required for one complete revolution.

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters from the problem statement.**

$$
N \;=\; 6 \times 4 \;=\; 24 \text{ stator teeth}
$$

$$
m \;=\; 4 \text{ rotor poles}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the KTU step-angle formula.**

$$
\theta_s \;=\; \frac{360^\circ}{N \cdot m}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the values.**

$$
\theta_s \;=\; \frac{360^\circ}{24 \times 4}
$$

**Step 4 — Perform the arithmetic.**

$$
\theta_s \;=\; \frac{360^\circ}{96} \;=\; 3.75^\circ
$$

**Step 5 — Calculate steps per revolution.**

$$
S \;=\; \frac{360^\circ}{\theta_s} \;=\; \frac{360^\circ}{3.75^\circ} \;=\; 96 \text{ steps/rev}
$$

**Conclusion:** Every input pulse rotates the shaft by **3.75°**, and **96 pulses** are needed for a full 360° revolution.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Delay Calculation for a Given RPM

**Problem Statement:** A stepper motor with **200 steps/revolution** must rotate at **15 RPM**. The 8051 uses a **11.0592 MHz crystal**. Calculate the required delay between successive steps, in milliseconds, and verify whether the 8051 timer can produce it.

**Step 1 — Relate RPM to pulses per second.**

The motor requires 200 steps for 1 revolution.

$$
\text{Steps per second} \;=\; \frac{N_{\text{rev}} \times S}{60} \;=\; \frac{15 \times 200}{60}
$$

$$
f_p \;=\; \frac{3000}{60} \;=\; 50 \text{ pulses/second (Hz)}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the time period of one step.**

$$
T_{\text{step}} \;=\; \frac{1}{f_p} \;=\; \frac{1}{50} \;=\; 0.02 \text{ s} \;=\; 20 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3 — The 8051 instruction cycle time.**

For an 11.0592 MHz crystal, the oscillator frequency is divided by 12 internally:

$$
f_{\text{osc}} \;=\; \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{12} \;=\; 921{,}600 \text{ Hz}
$$

One machine cycle = $\frac{1}{921{,}600} = 1.085\,\mu\text{s}$.

**Step 4 — Number of machine cycles needed for a 20 ms delay.**

$$
N_{\text{cycles}} \;=\; \frac{20\,000\,\mu\text{s}}{1.085\,\mu\text{s/cycle}} \;\approx\; 18{,}433 \text{ machine cycles}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to a software loop count (each loop iteration ≈ 2 machine cycles for DJNZ).**

$$
N_{\text{iterations}} \;\approx\; \frac{18{,}433}{2} \;\approx\; 9{,}216
$$

A nested `DJNZ` loop in 8051 Assembly easily generates this delay (e.g., outer = 200, inner = 46). Alternatively, **Timer 0 in Mode 1** with `TH0 = 0xB1`, `TL0 = 0xE0` produces exactly 20 ms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** For computing timer reload values, use the exact cycle-based formula. **Never** use the approximate 1 µs assumption unless the crystal is 12 MHz (which makes 1 machine cycle = 1 µs exactly).

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Design Decisions Table

| Design Parameter | Wave Drive (1-phase) | Full Drive (2-phase) | Half-Step Drive |
|---|---|---|---|
| Steps per revolution | $S$ | $S$ | $2S$ |
| Effective step angle | $\theta_s$ | $\theta_s$ | $\theta_s / 2$ |
| Holding Torque | Lowest | **~ 40% higher** | Highest (avg) |
| Power Consumption | Lowest | Highest (~2×) | Intermediate |
| Smoothness | Jerky | Jerky | **Smoothest** |
| Resonance | Severe | Severe | **Reduced** |
| Typical Use | KTU labs (basic) | Industrial | CNC, precision |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often write "Half-step uses 2× the step angle" — the truth is the **opposite**: half-step *doubles* the resolution, so the effective angle is **half** of full step.

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hardware Interface — 8051 to Stepper Motor via ULN2003

The 8051 port pin (e.g., P1.0) can only source/sink about **10–20 mA**, which is **insufficient** for a stepper coil that typically draws **100–300 mA**. Hence a **driver IC** is mandatory.

The **ULN2003** is a high-voltage, high-current **Darlington transistor array** with 7 channels. Each channel acts as a switch: a logic HIGH on the input turns ON the corresponding open-collector output, sinking current from the motor coil to ground.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CPU["8051 Microcontroller"]
        P10["P1.0"]
        P11["P1.1"]
        P12["P1.2"]
        P13["P1.3"]
    end

    subgraph DRIVER["ULN2003 Driver IC"]
        IN1["IN1 (Pin 1)"]
        IN2["IN2 (Pin 2)"]
        IN3["IN3 (Pin 3)"]
        IN4["IN4 (Pin 4)"]
        OUT1["OUT1 (Pin 16)"]
        OUT2["OUT2 (Pin 15)"]
        OUT3["OUT3 (Pin 14)"]
        OUT4["OUT4 (Pin 13)"]
    end

    subgraph MOTOR["4-Phase Stepper Motor"]
        COILA["Coil A"]
        COILB["Coil B"]
        COILC["Coil C"]
        COILD["Coil D"]
    end

    VCC["+5V / +12V Supply"]

    P10 --> IN1
    P11 --> IN2
    P12 --> IN3
    P13 --> IN4

    IN1 --> OUT1
    IN2 --> OUT2
    IN3 --> OUT3
    IN4 --> OUT4

    OUT1 --> COILA
    OUT2 --> COILB
    OUT3 --> COILC
    OUT4 --> COILD

    VCC --> COILA
    VCC --> COILB
    VCC --> COILC
    VCC --> COILD
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Blue arrows = Logic control signal (5V, low current)**
- **Red arrows = Power path to motor coil (from external supply)**
- The ULN2003 *isolates* the weak microcontroller pins from the high-current motor coils.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why ULN2003 and not a BJT directly?** It has **built-in flyback (freewheeling) diodes** at every output, which protect the IC from the high-voltage inductive kick generated when a coil is de-energized.

### 4.2 Software Flow — Rotation Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start / Initialize Port P1 as Output"] --> B["Load First Step Pattern from Lookup Table"]
    B --> C{"Direction Flag = 1?"}
    C -- Yes --> D["Increment Table Index (Forward)"]
    C -- No --> E["Decrement Table Index (Reverse)"]
    D --> F["Output Pattern to P1"]
    E --> F
    F --> G["Call Delay Subroutine (T_step)"]
    G --> H{"Reached Required Steps?"}
    H -- No --> B
    H -- Yes --> I["Stop: De-energize All Coils"]
    I --> J["End"]
```

### 4.3 Pin-Level Truth Table (Wave Drive, Forward Rotation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["Step 1: P1 = 0x01"] --> S2["Step 2: P1 = 0x02"]
    S2 --> S3["Step 3: P1 = 0x04"]
    S3 --> S4["Step 4: P1 = 0x08"]
    S4 --> S5["Repeat from Step 1"]
    S5 -.-> S1
```

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*List any three differences between a stepper motor and a DC motor.*

**Model Answer:**

| # | Stepper Motor | DC Motor |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Rotates in **discrete angular steps** (fixed step angle). | Rotates **continuously** as long as voltage is applied. |
| 2 | Position is **open-loop controlled**; no encoder needed. | Position requires **closed-loop feedback** (encoder/tachogenerator). |
| 3 | Holding torque exists even at **zero speed** (locked rotor). | No holding torque at zero speed; needs a brake. |
| 4 | Speed is proportional to the **pulse frequency**, not voltage. | Speed is proportional to the **applied voltage**. |
| 5 | Brushless in most designs; high reliability. | Has brushes/commutator; more wear. |

*(Any three well-explained points — 3 marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*What is the step angle of a stepper motor that has 4 stator phases and 2 rotor poles? If 600 pulses are sent to the motor, what is the total angular displacement?*

**Model Answer:**

Step angle:
$$
\theta_s = \frac{360^\circ}{N \cdot m} = \frac{360^\circ}{4 \times 2} = 45^\circ
$$

Total angular displacement for 600 pulses:
$$
\theta_{\text{total}} = 600 \times 45^\circ = 27{,}000^\circ
$$

Convert to revolutions:
$$
\text{Revolutions} = \frac{27{,}000^\circ}{360^\circ} = 75 \text{ revolutions}
$$

---

### Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q3. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the construction and working of a **4-phase permanent magnet stepper motor** with a neat diagram. Discuss the **full-step (two-phase-on)** excitation sequence. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Interface a 4-phase stepper motor to the 8051 microcontroller using the **ULN2003 driver IC**. Draw the interfacing circuit, write the 8051 C program to rotate the motor continuously in the **clockwise direction** with a suitable delay, and explain the role of the ULN2003. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Answer for (a):**

**Construction:**
A 4-phase PM stepper motor consists of a **cylindrical permanent magnet rotor** with two poles (N and S) and a **stator** with four salient poles, each carrying a phase winding. The four phases are physically displaced by **90°** in space. The windings are labelled **A, B, C, D**, and in a **unipolar** configuration, one common terminal of all four coils is tied to the DC supply, while the other terminals (A, B, C, D) are brought out for individual switching.

**Working (Full-Step, Two-Phase-On):**

When two adjacent phases are energized simultaneously, the resultant magnetic field lies **midway** between the two stator poles. The rotor aligns with this vector sum.

- **Step 1:** Energize A & B → rotor aligns at 45°.
- **Step 2:** Energize B & C → rotor aligns at 135°.
- **Step 3:** Energize C & D → rotor aligns at 225°.
- **Step 4:** Energize D & A → rotor aligns at 315°.
- **Step 5:** Repeat Step 1 → completes 360° in 4 steps.

**Step angle for a 2-pole PM motor = 90°** when one phase is on; with two phases on, the rotor still steps 90° but with **40% more holding torque** (since the rotor aligns with the **vector sum**).

**Model Answer for (b):**

**Interfacing Circuit:** (Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1.)

- Port pins **P1.0, P1.1, P1.2, P1.3** of the 8051 are connected to **IN1, IN2, IN3, IN4** of the ULN2003.
- The ULN2003 outputs (OUT1–OUT4) drive the four motor coils.
- The **common terminal** of the motor is connected to the **external +5V (or +12V) supply**.
- The 8051 ground and ULN2003 ground must be common.

**Role of ULN2003:**
- **Current amplification:** 8051 pins source/sink only ~10 mA; motor coils need 100–300 mA.
- **Voltage isolation:** Protects the 8051 from inductive kickback via internal flyback diodes.
- **Logic-level translation:** Accepts TTL-level inputs (HIGH = ON).

**8051 C Program (clockwise, full-step two-phase-on, Keil syntax):**

```c
#include <reg51.h>

sbit LED = P1^7;

void delay_ms(unsigned int ms) {
    unsigned int i, j;
    for (i = 0; i < ms; i++)
        for (j = 0; j < 120; j++);  /* ~1 ms at 11.0592 MHz */
}

void main(void) {
    unsigned char i;
    /* Full-step 2-phase-on: 0x03, 0x06, 0x0C, 0x09 */
    unsigned char code seq[] = {0x03, 0x06, 0x0C, 0x09};

    P1 = 0x00;        /* Initialize all coils OFF */

    while (1) {
        for (i = 0; i < 4; i++) {
            P1 = seq[i];
            delay_ms(20);   /* 20 ms per step -> 15 RPM for 200-step motor */
        }
    }
}
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Costing 2–3 marks if missed):**
> 1. **Missing `code` keyword** — students store the lookup table in *RAM* (256 bytes max), wasting precious internal memory. Always use `code` to store in *ROM* (program memory).
> 2. **Wrong port assignment** — the program must use the *same* port pins (P1.0–P1.3) shown in the circuit diagram. Inconsistency loses 2 marks.
> 3. **No initialization of P1 = 0x00** — if a coil is left energized before the loop, the rotor may *jump* on power-up.
> 4. **Hard-coded magic numbers** — explain the delay calculation: 20 ms × 4 phases = 80 ms per cycle, 1/0.08 = 12.5 steps/sec → 12.5 × (360/90) = 50 RPM (for 90° motor) or 3.75 RPM (for 1.8° motor). **Show this arithmetic.**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice**

**Q4. [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a block diagram, explain the interfacing of a stepper motor to the 8051. Discuss the **half-step excitation** mode and state one advantage over full-step mode. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A stepper motor has a step angle of **1.8°** and is driven by a sequence of pulses from an 8051 system. Calculate: *(i)* the number of steps required for one full revolution, *(ii)* the shaft speed in RPM if the pulse rate is 500 pulses/sec, and *(iii)* the time delay (in ms) between consecutive pulses for a desired speed of 30 RPM. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Answer for (a):**

**Block Diagram:** (Same Mermaid diagram from Section 4.1 applies — 8051 → ULN2003 → 4-phase stepper.)

**Half-Step Excitation Sequence:**

Half-stepping **alternates** between one-phase-on and two-phase-on patterns, producing **8 discrete steps per electrical cycle** instead of 4 — thus **doubling the angular resolution**.

| Step | D | C | B | A | Hex |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0x01 |
| 2 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0x03 |
| 3 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0x02 |
| 4 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0x06 |
| 5 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0x04 |
| 6 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0x0C |
| 7 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0x08 |
| 8 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0x09 |

**Effective step angle = 0.9°** (half of 1.8°).

**Advantage over Full-Step:**
- **Smoother rotation** and **higher resolution** — the rotor moves in finer increments, eliminating the jerky discrete motion of full-stepping. This also **reduces resonance** and **mechanical vibration**, critical in CNC and precision instrumentation.

---

**Model Answer for (b):**

**(i) Number of steps per revolution:**

$$
S = \frac{360^\circ}{\theta_s} = \frac{360^\circ}{1.8^\circ} = 200 \text{ steps/rev}
$$

**(ii) Shaft speed in RPM at 500 pulses/sec:**

$$
N_{\text{rev}} = \frac{f_p \times 60}{S} = \frac{500 \times 60}{200} = \frac{30{,}000}{200} = 150 \text{ RPM}
$$

**(iii) Delay between pulses for 30 RPM:**

Step 1: Pulses per second needed:
$$
f_p = \frac{N_{\text{rev}} \times S}{60} = \frac{30 \times 200}{60} = \frac{6000}{60} = 100 \text{ pulses/sec}
$$

Step 2: Time per pulse:
$$
T_{\text{step}} = \frac{1}{f_p} = \frac{1}{100} = 0.01 \text{ s} = 10 \text{ ms}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall in Numerical Questions:**
> - **Mixing up RPM and step rate.** Always convert: $\text{RPM} \times S / 60 = \text{steps/sec}$.
> - **Unit mismatch:** Some students write $T = 0.01$ seconds but answer in **milliseconds**, losing 1 mark. Write **10 ms explicitly**.
> - **Forgetting that 200 steps/rev is for 1.8° motors** — using $S = 4$ (which is for a 90° motor) gives wrong answers. Re-read the problem carefully.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam (Module 3 — Stepper Motor Interfacing):**

- ☐ **Three motor types:** VR (soft iron rotor, fine steps, low torque), PM (magnet rotor, coarse steps, high torque), Hybrid (both, 1.8° standard).
- ☐ **Step Angle Formula:** $\theta_s = 360^\circ / (N \times m)$ — must be memorized verbatim.
