- ☐ **200 steps/rev** corresponds to the **1.8°** industrial standard motor.
- ☐ **Unipolar 4-phase motor** has 5 wires (4 phases + 1 common) and is the most common in KTU labs.
- ☐ **Sequences:** Wave (1-phase) → low torque, Full (2-phase) → 40% higher torque, Half → smoother + 2× resolution.
- ☐ **Direction reversal = reverse the lookup table order** in software. No hardware change required.
- ☐ **ULN2003 is mandatory** for interfacing — provides current gain + flyback protection.
- ☐ **Speed ∝ Pulse frequency**; Position ∝ Number of pulses. (Open-loop control mantra.)
- ☐ **RPM calculation:** $N_{\text{rev}} = (f_p \times 60) / S$ where $f_p$ = pulse rate, $S$ = steps/rev.
- ☐ **8051 Program must use `code` keyword** for the lookup table to save RAM.
- ☐ **Always initialize P1 = 0x00** before starting the stepping loop.
- ☐ **Resonance problem:** Never run a stepper at its natural frequency — use ramping (acceleration/deceleration) in real applications.
- ☐ **Holding torque** is the maximum torque the motor can resist while *stationary* and energized — critical for vertical-axis loads.
- ☐ **Stepper motors lose steps** if overloaded → open-loop limitation. For mission-critical applications, add an encoder for closed-loop control.
- ☐ **11.0592 MHz crystal** is the KTU standard — one machine cycle = 1.085 µs (NOT 1 µs).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DAC and ADC with 8051 and Its Programming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMRT403 - COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE AND MICROCONTROLLER > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** DAC and ADC with 8051 and Its Programming # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC) ### Formal Definition A **Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC)** is an electronic device that converts discrete digital data (binary numbers) into a continuous analog signal (typically a voltage or current). In the context of the 8051 microcontroller, a DAC is an essential peripheral device used to interface the digital domain of the microcontroller with the analog real-world environment (e.g., audio amplifiers, motor speed controllers, signal generators). > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The focus is on the **8-bit DAC0808** and its interfacing with the 8051 using the **MOVX @DPTR/A** instruction (External Memory Write protocol). ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **water tap with 8 separate handles** (each controlled by a 1-bit switch of a byte). - The **MSB handle** releases 128 mL of water per unit. - The next handle releases 64 mL, then 32, 16, 8, 4, 2, and the **LSB handle** releases just 1 mL. - When the microcontroller sends a binary number like `10000000`, only the MSB handle opens, releasing 128 mL. - When it sends `11111111`, all 8 handles open, releasing **255 mL**. - A DAC works exactly like this, but instead of water, it outputs voltage. The "amount" of voltage released per bit is determined by a **Reference Voltage ($V_{ref}$)**. ### Key DAC Performance Metrics - **Resolution:** The smallest change in analog output corresponding to a change of 1 LSB in the digital input. For an $n$-bit DAC: $\text{Resolution} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n - 1}$. - **Reference Voltage ($V_{ref}$):** The full-scale reference against which digital bits are weighed. Standard value: **+5V or +10V**. - **Settling Time:** The time taken for the output to stabilize within $\pm \frac{1}{2}$ LSB after a digital input change. Typical: **100 ns to 1 µs**. - **Linearity:** The deviation of actual output from the ideal straight-line transfer characteristic. --- ## 1.2 Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC) ### Formal Definition An **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** performs the inverse function of a DAC. It samples a continuous analog input signal and converts it into a discrete digital number (binary code) proportional to the input voltage. In 8051-based systems, the ADC0804 (8-bit, single channel) and ADC0808/0809 (8-bit, 8-channel) are the most commonly interfaced devices. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core Definition (Exam Favorite):** *An ADC is a device that translates an analog signal into a digital code, where the size of the digital code represents the magnitude of the input voltage relative to a reference voltage.* ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of an ADC as a **digital weighing scale** for voltage. - You place an unknown voltage (the "weight") on the input. - The ADC uses an **internal comparator** and a **successive approximation register (SAR)** to "guess" the voltage. - It starts with the MSB: "Is your voltage greater than $V_{ref}/2$?" If yes, it sets the MSB to 1; if no, it sets it to 0. - It then asks: "Is the remaining part greater than $V_{ref}/4$?" and so on, halving the range with each bit. - After 8 such "yes/no" questions (for an 8-bit ADC), it outputs an 8-bit digital code that precisely represents the input voltage. ### Key ADC Performance Metrics - **Resolution:** $\dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$. Expressed in **Volts per Bit (V/bit)**. - **Conversion Time ($T_{conv}$):** The time required to complete one full analog-to-digital conversion. For ADC0804: **~110 µs**. - **Quantization Error:** The inherent $\pm \frac{1}{2}$ LSB error due to discrete digital levels representing a continuous analog signal. - **Sampling Rate:** Maximum number of conversions per second, $\dfrac{1}{T_{conv}}$. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Map:** This topic directly maps to **CO3 (Module 3)** – "Interface various peripheral devices (ADC, DAC, sensors) with 8051 microcontroller" and is a high-weightage area in KTU End Semester Examinations (ESE). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 DAC — Theoretical Foundation ### 2.1.1 Working Principle of 8-bit DAC The fundamental DAC equation governing the analog output is: $$V_{out} = V_{ref} \times \dfrac{D}{2^n - 1}$$ Where: - $V_{out}$ = Analog output voltage - $V_{ref}$ = Reference voltage (full-scale) - $D$ = Digital input value (decimal equivalent of the binary input) - $n$ = Number of bits (typically 8 for the 8051 interface) ### 2.1.2 Types of DAC Architectures **A) Weighted Resistor DAC** - Each input bit drives a resistor of value $2^k \cdot R$ (where $k$ is the bit position from 0 to $n-1$). - **Drawback:** The range of resistor values becomes impractically large for high-resolution DACs (e.g., for 12-bit, the LSB resistor is 2048 times the MSB resistor). **B) R-2R Ladder DAC (Used in DAC0808)** - Uses only two resistor values: $R$ and $2R$. - The output is derived from a sum of binary-weighted currents at the inverting input of an op-amp. - **Advantage:** Easily fabricated on IC, scalable to high resolutions. ### 2.1.3 The DAC0808 (National Semiconductor) — Pin Architecture - **8-bit Current Output DAC**. - Has two analog ground pins: **AGND (Pin 1)** and **DGND (Pin 3)**. - Reference current set by external resistor $R_{14}$ and $R_{15}$: $I_{ref} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{R_{14}}$. - Output current: $I_{out} = I_{ref} \times \dfrac{D}{256}$. - Requires an external **Op-Amp (e.g., LM741)** configured as current-to-voltage converter to produce voltage output: $V_{out} = I_{out} \times R_f$, where $R_f$ is the feedback resistor. ### 2.1.4 8051-DAC0808 Interfacing Logic - The 8051 port (e.g., **P1**) is used as the data bus to the DAC. - Since the DAC always converts the latched data, no specific control signal (like $\overline{CS}$ or $\overline{WR}$) is strictly needed for the DAC0808 — the data on the bus is continuously converted. - Hence, the 8051 simply writes the digital byte to Port 1 using `MOV P1, A`. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Key Insight:** Unlike the ADC, the DAC **does not need** the `MOVX` instruction. A simple `MOV P1, A` suffices because the DAC always "sees" the current latched value. --- ## 2.2 ADC — Theoretical Foundation ### 2.2.1 Working Principle The ADC's output is governed by: $$D = \left\lfloor \dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \times (2^n - 1) \right\rfloor$$ Where: - $D$ = Digital output (decimal) - $V_{in}$ = Analog input voltage - $V_{ref}$ = Reference voltage - $n$ = Resolution in bits ### 2.2.2 Types of ADC Architectures 1. **Flash (Parallel) ADC:** Fastest ($\sim$ ns). Uses $2^n - 1$ comparators. Expensive. 2. **Counter-Type ADC:** Slow. Uses a counter and a DAC in a feedback loop. 3. **Successive Approximation (SAR) ADC:** Used in **ADC0804/0808**. Balances speed and cost. Conversion time: $\sim$ 8 clock cycles. 4. **Dual-Slope / Sigma-Delta:** High accuracy, used in digital voltmeters and audio. ### 2.2.3 The ADC0804 — Pin Architecture (Single Channel, 8-bit) - **$V_{in}(+)$ and $V_{in}(-)$ (Pins 6, 7):** Differential analog inputs. - **DB0–DB7 (Pins 11–18):** Tri-state digital outputs. - **$\overline{CS}$ (Pin 1):** Chip Select (Active Low). - **$\overline{WR}$ (Pin 3):** Write/Start of Conversion (Active Low pulse). - **$\overline{RD}$ (Pin 2):** Read (Output Enable, Active Low pulse). - **$\overline{INTR}$ (Pin 5):** End of Conversion (Active Low interrupt). - **CLK R, CLK IN (Pins 19, 4):** External clock input. Frequency range: **100 kHz to 800 kHz** (typical 606 kHz from 8051 ALE divided by 6 via D flip-flop). - **$V_{ref}/2$ (Pin 9):** Reference voltage input. Often left open (internal reference = 5.12V → 20 mV per bit) or connected to a precision reference. ### 2.2.4 8051-ADC0804 Interfacing Logic - The ADC's data lines (DB0–DB7) connect to **P1** of the 8051. - $\overline{CS}$ tied to **GND** (always selected). - $\overline{WR}$ connected to a 8051 port pin (e.g., **P3.0**) — a LOW pulse starts the conversion. - $\overline{RD}$ connected to another 8051 port pin (e.g., **P3.1**) — a LOW pulse reads the converted digital data. - $\overline{INTR}$ connected to a 8051 pin (e.g., **P3.2 / INT0**) to detect End-of-Conversion (polled or interrupt-driven). --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula / Parameter | Typical Value (8051 System) | Engineering Utility | |---|---|---|---| | DAC Output Voltage | $V_{out} = V_{ref} \times \dfrac{D}{2^n - 1}$ | $n=8$, $V_{ref}=5\text{V}$ | Audio output, Motor speed control | | DAC Resolution (Step Size) | $\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n - 1}$ | $\approx 19.6 \text{ mV}$ for 8-bit | Minimum controllable output change | | ADC Digital Output | $D = \dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \times (2^n - 1)$ | Round to nearest integer | Sensor data acquisition | | ADC Resolution | $\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$ | $\approx 20 \text{ mV}$ for 8-bit | Smallest detectable input change | | ADC0804 Clock Frequency | $f_{CLK} = \dfrac{1}{1.1 RC}$ | $\sim 606 \text{ kHz}$ (with $R=10\text{k}\Omega$, $C=150\text{pF}$) | Determines conversion time | | Conversion Time (SAR) | $T_{conv} = \dfrac{n}{f_{CLK}} + \text{overhead}$ | $\sim 110 \text{ µs}$ for 8-bit @ 606 kHz | Maximum sampling rate | | Max Sampling Rate | $f_{sample,max} = \dfrac{1}{T_{conv}}$ | $\sim 9 \text{ kS/s}$ | Real-time signal tracking limit | | DAC0808 Reference Current | $I_{ref} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{R_{14}}$ | $V_{ref}=5\text{V}, R_{14}=5\text{k}\Omega \Rightarrow 1\text{mA}$ | Sets full-scale output current | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** > - **DACs** are used in audio amplifiers (Class-D), waveform generators, programmable voltage sources, and motor drives. > - **ADCs** are used in data loggers, digital oscilloscopes, biomedical instrumentation (ECG/EEG), and IoT sensor nodes. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Digital-to-Analog Conversion Equation **Starting Point:** In an R-2R ladder, each bit $b_k$ (where $b_k \in \{0, 1\}$) contributes a binary-weighted fraction of the reference voltage $V_{ref}$ at the output of the current-to-voltage op-amp. **Step 1:** The output voltage of the op-amp is proportional to the sum of weighted contributions: $$V_{out} \propto b_0 \cdot 2^0 + b_1 \cdot 2^1 + b_2 \cdot 2^2 + \dots + b_{n-1} \cdot 2^{n-1}$$ **Step 2:** Substituting the binary-weighted sum with the decimal equivalent $D$: $$D = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} b_k \cdot 2^k$$ **Step 3:** The proportionality constant is the LSB voltage $\Delta V$. The maximum output occurs when all bits are 1: $$V_{FS} = \Delta V \times (2^n - 1)$$ **Step 4:** Since the LSB voltage equals the full-scale voltage divided by $(2^n - 1)$: $$\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n - 1} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n - 1}$$ **Step 5:** Combining, we obtain the final DAC equation: $$\boxed{V_{out} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n - 1} \times D}$$ --- ## 3.2 Step-by-Step Worked Example: DAC Output Calculation **Problem:** An 8-bit DAC0808 has a reference voltage $V_{ref} = 5.1 \text{ V}$ and the input from the 8051 is `P1 = 7FH`. Calculate the analog output voltage $V_{out}$. **Step 1 — Identify the parameters:** - $n = 8$ - $V_{ref} = 5.1 \text{ V}$ - $D = \text{7FH} = 0111 \, 1111_2 = 127_{10}$ **Step 2 — Compute the resolution (step size):** $$\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^8 - 1} = \dfrac{5.1}{255}$$ $$\Delta V \approx 0.02 \text{ V} = 20 \text{ mV}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the output voltage:** $$V_{out} = \Delta V \times D = 0.02 \times 127$$ $$\boxed{V_{out} = 2.54 \text{ V}}$$ **Step 4 — Verification:** Half of 5.1V is 2.55V. Since 7FH is half of FFH, the result of 2.54V (one step below half) is **physically consistent**. ✓ --- ## 3.3 Step-by-Step Worked Example: ADC Input Reconstruction **Problem:** An ADC0804 with $V_{ref} = 5.12 \text{ V}$ outputs `3AH`. Determine the analog input voltage. **Step 1 — Identify the parameters:** - $n = 8$ - $V_{ref} = 5.12 \text{ V}$ - $D = \text{3AH} = 0011 \, 1010_2 = 58_{10}$ **Step 2 — Compute the ADC resolution:** $$\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^8} = \dfrac{5.12}{256} = 0.02 \text{ V} = 20 \text{ mV/bit}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the input voltage:** $$V_{in} = D \times \Delta V = 58 \times 0.02$$ $$\boxed{V_{in} = 1.16 \text{ V}}$$ **Step 4 — Verification:** 3AH is roughly 22.6% of FFH, and 22.6% of 5.12V is approximately 1.16V. ✓ --- ## 3.4 Conversion Time Derivation for ADC0804 **Step 1:** The ADC0804 uses an internal clock oscillator whose frequency is set by an external RC network connected to CLK R and CLK IN: $$f_{CLK} = \dfrac{1}{1.1 \times R \times C}$$ **Step 2:** With $R = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ and $C = 150 \text{ pF}$: $$f_{CLK} = \dfrac{1}{1.1 \times 10 \times 10^3 \times 150 \times 10^{-12}}$$ $$f_{CLK} \approx 606 \text{ kHz}$$ **Step 3:** The period of one clock cycle is: $$T_{CLK} = \dfrac{1}{f_{CLK}} \approx 1.65 \text{ µs}$$ **Step 4:** Successive Approximation requires $n$ clock cycles (8 for 8-bit) plus fixed overhead (about 2 cycles for SAR register clear and start). Total cycles $\approx 8$ to $10$: $$T_{conv} \approx 8 \times T_{CLK} \approx 8 \times 1.65$$ $$\boxed{T_{conv} \approx 110 \text{ µs}}$$ **Step 5:** Maximum sampling rate: $$f_{sample,max} = \dfrac{1}{T_{conv}} = \dfrac{1}{110 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 9.09 \text{ kS/s}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Note:** The RC-based clock formula $f_{CLK} = \dfrac{1}{1.1RC}$ is a **high-yield formula** and frequently appears in derivations. The factor 1.1 is empirically determined by the internal Schmitt trigger characteristics of the ADC0804. --- ## 3.5 8051 Programming — Waveform Generation using DAC ### Example 1: Generating a Staircase Waveform This 8051 assembly program sends 8 ascending digital values to the DAC to create a staircase ramp, then drops to zero and repeats. ```asm ORG 0000H MOV DPTR, #0FE00H ; DPTR -> DAC port (if memory-mapped) MOV R0, #00H ; Initial count AGAIN: MOV A, R0 MOVX @DPTR, A ; Send value to DAC ACALL DELAY ; Hold the value for visualization INC R0 ; Increment to next step CJNE R0, #0FFH, AGAIN ; Loop until 254, then drop to 255->0 wrap SJMP BACK ; Restart staircase DELAY: MOV R2, #0FFH LOOP1: MOV R3, #0FFH LOOP2: DJNZ R3, LOOP2 DJNZ R2, LOOP1 RET BACK: SJMP AGAIN END ``` ### Example 2: Generating a Triangular Waveform A triangular wave ramps up from 0 to 255, then ramps down from 255 to 0 continuously. ```asm ORG 0000H MOV DPTR, #0FE00H UP: MOV R0, #00H UP_LP: MOV A, R0 MOVX @DPTR, A ACALL DELAY INC R0 CJNE R0, #0FFH, UP_LP ; Ramp up to 254 DOWN: MOV R0, #0FFH DN_LP: MOV A, R0 MOVX @DPTR, A ACALL DELAY DEC R0 CJNE R0, #00H, DN_LP ; Ramp down to 1 SJMP UP ; Repeat DELAY: MOV R2, #0FFH DLY1: MOV R3, #0FFH DLY2: DJNZ R3, DLY2 DJNZ R2, DLY1 RET END ``` ### Example 3: Sine Wave Generation (Lookup Table Method) A sine wave is generated by feeding pre-calculated 8-bit samples from a lookup table to the DAC at fixed time intervals. **Mathematical Pre-computation (Sample Calculation for $0°$ to $90°$):** For 8 samples, the angle step is $45°$. The sine values are: - $\sin(0°) = 0$, $\sin(45°) = 0.7071$, $\sin(90°) = 1.0$, $\sin(135°) = 0.7071$ - Scaling to 0–255 with offset 128 (to make it unipolar 0-5V from 0-10V bipolar): $$\text{Sample}(k) = \left\lfloor 128 + 127 \times \sin\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi k}{32}\right) \right\rfloor \quad \text{for } k = 0, 1, \dots, 31$$ **Code Implementation:** ```asm ORG 0000H MOV DPTR, #SINE_TABLE ; Point DPTR to the lookup table in code memory LOOP: CLR A MOVC A, @A+DPTR ; Fetch sample from code ROM MOV R1, #0FEH ; Port address (if memory-mapped) MOVX @R1, A ; Send to DAC ACALL DELAY ; Hold for sampling period Ts INC DPTR ; Move to next sample CJNE A, #0FFH, LOOP ; Continue until end of table SJMP LOOP ; Repeat ; --- 32-sample sine lookup table (decimal values) --- SINE_TABLE: DB 128, 152, 176, 198, 218, 234, 246, 254 DB 255, 254, 246, 234, 218, 198, 176, 152 DB 128, 103, 79, 57, 37, 21, 9, 1 DB 0, 1, 9, 21, 37, 57, 79, 103 DELAY: MOV R2, #02H D1: MOV R3, #0FFH D2: DJNZ R3, D2 DJNZ R2, D1 RET END ``` --- ## 3.6 8051 Programming — Reading an ADC (ADC0804) **Polled Method (Interfacing via Port 1 and P3.0/P3.1):** ```asm ORG 0000H MOV P1, #0FFH ; Configure P1 as input MAIN: CLR P3.0 ; Apply LOW pulse to WR to start conversion ACALL DELAY SETB P3.0 ; WR goes HIGH (start conversion initiated) WAIT: JB P3.2, WAIT ; Poll INTR pin (P3.2); wait for LOW (EOC) CLR P3.1 ; Apply LOW to RD to read data MOV A, P1 ; Read digital value from ADC SETB P3.1 ; De-assert RD ACALL DISPLAY ; Display or process A SJMP MAIN ; Repeat DELAY: MOV R2, #0FFH D1: DJNZ R2, D1 RET END ``` > [!NOTE] > **Key Programming Insight:** The `JB P3.2, WAIT` instruction continuously checks the INTR line. When the ADC completes conversion, INTR goes LOW, and the loop exits, allowing the 8051 to read the data. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: 8051-DAC0808 Interfacing ```mermaid graph LR MCU["8051 Microcontroller
Port 1 P1.0-P1.7"] --> DAC["DAC0808
8-bit Digital Input"] VREF["Vref = 5.1V
via R14 and R15"] --> DAC DAC --> OPAMP["Op-Amp LM741
Current to Voltage"] OPAMP --> VOUT["Analog Vout
0 to +5V Range"] GND["AGND and DGND"] --> DAC VCC["Vcc = +5V to +15V
Pin 13 and 3"] --> DAC style MCU fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc style DAC fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900 style OPAMP fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#7b3f99 style VOUT fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366 ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Flow: 8051-ADC0804 Interfacing and Conversion ```mermaid graph TD MCU8051["8051 Port 1 P1.0-P1.7
Data Bus Lines"] --> ADCDATA["ADC0804
DB0-DB7"] P30["8051 P3.0"] --> ADCWR["ADC0804 WR pin
Active LOW Start"] P31["8051 P3.1"] --> ADCRD["ADC0804 RD pin
Active LOW Read"] P32["8051 P3.2 INT0"] --> ADCINTR["ADC0804 INTR pin
End of Conversion"] VIN["Analog Vin
0 to 5V"] --> ADCDATA CLOCK["RC Network
R=10k, C=150pF"] --> ADCCLK["ADC0804 CLK IN"] ADCDATA --> RESULT["8-bit Digital Output
Stored in ACC"] style MCU8051 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc style ADCDATA fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900 style VIN fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450 style RESULT fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366 ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart: ADC Conversion Sequence (Successive Approximation) ```mermaid graph TD S0([Start]) --> S1[8051 sends LOW pulse on WR to ADC0804] S1 --> S2[ADC0804 begins conversion
INTR goes HIGH] S2 --> S3{SAR tests MSB
Is Vin greater than Vref/2?} S3 -->|Yes| S4[Set MSB to 1
Keep internal DAC at Vref/2] S3 -->|No| S5[Set MSB to 0
Drop internal DAC to 0] S4 --> S6{SAR tests Bit 6
Is Vin greater than Vref/4?} S5 --> S6 S6 -->|Yes| S7[Set Bit 6 to 1] S6 -->|No| S8[Set Bit 6 to 0] S7 --> S9{Continue for all
8 bits?} S8 --> S9 S9 -->|No| S10[Move to next bit
Halve the range] S10 --> S6 S9 -->|Yes| S11[INTR goes LOW
Conversion complete] S11 --> S12[8051 sends LOW on RD
Reads 8-bit data on Port 1] S12 --> S13([End of one cycle]) style S0 fill:#82b366,stroke:#5a9a3e style S13 fill:#82b366,stroke:#5a9a3e style S1 fill:#cce5ff style S11 fill:#fff4cc style S12 fill:#d5e8d4 ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: Complete 8051 Data Acquisition System ```mermaid graph LR SENSOR["Analog Sensor
Temperature or Light"] --> SIGCON["Signal Conditioning
Amplifier and Filter"] SIGCON --> ADC["ADC0804
8-bit Conversion"] ADC --> PORT1["8051 Port 1
Digital Input"] PORT1 --> CPU["8051 CPU
Processes Data"] CPU --> DISPLAY["LCD or LED Display
7-Segment"] CPU --> DAC["DAC0808
Reconverts to Analog"] DAC --> ACTUATOR["Actuator
Motor or Heater"] style SENSOR fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450 style ADC fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900 style CPU fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc style DAC fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900 style ACTUATOR fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366 style DISPLAY fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#7b3f99 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Pedagogical Note:** The block diagrams above follow the **KTU-recommended architectural notation** for the 2024 Scheme. The data flow arrows represent the actual signal path during real-time operation, and students are advised to reproduce these in their exam answers for full credit (typically 2–3 marks in ESE). # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Q: Define the term "Resolution" of a DAC. For an 8-bit DAC with a reference voltage of 5V, calculate the percentage resolution and the smallest change in output voltage.** **Model Answer:** Resolution is the smallest change in the analog output voltage of a DAC corresponding to a 1-LSB change in the digital input. It is also called the **step size**. $$\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n - 1} = \dfrac{5}{2^8 - 1} = \dfrac{5}{255} \approx 19.6 \text{ mV}$$ Percentage resolution is defined as the ratio of step size to the full-scale output, expressed as a percentage: $$\%\text{Resolution} = \dfrac{1}{2^n - 1} \times 100 = \dfrac{1}{255} \times 100 \approx 0.392\%$$ --- ### Question A2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Q: Differentiate between ADC and DAC. List any three specifications of ADC0804.** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | DAC (Digital-to-Analog Converter) | ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter) | |---|---|---| | Function | Converts digital code → analog signal | Converts analog signal → digital code | | Direction of data flow | Digital system → Real world | Real world → Digital system | | Example IC | DAC0808 | ADC0804 / ADC0808 | | Output type | Continuous voltage/current | Discrete binary code | **Three Specifications of ADC0804:** 1. **Resolution:** 8 bits (256 discrete levels). 2. **Conversion Time:** ~110 µs at $f_{CLK} = 606$ kHz. 3. **Single +5V supply operation** with differential analog inputs and tri-state digital outputs. 4. (Bonus) **On-chip clock generator** with external RC network. 5. (Bonus) **Analog input range:** 0 to 5V (when $V_{ref}/2$ is left open, internal reference = 5.12V). --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified] **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the interfacing of an **8-bit DAC0808** with the 8051 microcontroller. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Write an 8051 ALP to generate a **triangular waveform** using the DAC0808. Explain the algorithm. **(7 marks)** --- ### Model Solution for Question A(a): **Block Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.1 above for the Mermaid block diagram; reproduce on paper.) **Interfacing Explanation:** 1. **Data Bus Connection:** The 8-bit data bus of the DAC0808 (pins 5–12) is connected to **Port 1 (P1.0–P1.7)** of the 8051. This serves as the digital input to the DAC. 2. **Reference Current Setup:** A reference voltage $V_{ref} = +5 \text{V}$ is applied to pin 14 of the DAC0808 through a resistor $R_{14} = 5 \text{ k}\Omega$. This sets the reference current $I_{ref} = V_{ref}/R_{14} = 1 \text{ mA}$. A potentiometer $R_{15}$ is used for fine calibration. 3. **Current-to-Voltage Conversion:** The DAC0808 outputs a current $I_{out}$ (pin 4). This current is converted to a voltage using an external **op-amp LM741** configured as a current-to-voltage converter with feedback resistor $R_f = 5 \text{ k}\Omega$. The output voltage is: $$V_{out} = I_{out} \times R_f = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{R_{14}} \times \dfrac{D}{256} \times R_f$$ 4. **Power Supply:** DAC0808 is powered by $+V_{CC} = +5\text{V}$ to $+15\text{V}$ at pin 13. Analog ground (AGND, pin 1) and digital ground (DGND, pin 3) are connected to the common system ground. 5. **No Control Signals Required:** Unlike the ADC, the DAC continuously converts whatever data is latched on its inputs. Thus, no chip select or write strobe is required. The 8051 simply executes `MOV P1, A` to update the analog output. **Pin Connections Summary Table:** | DAC0808 Pin | Connected To | Function | |---|---|---| | A1–A8 (Pins 5–12) | 8051 P1.0–P1.7 | 8-bit digital data input | | $I_{out}$ (Pin 4) | Op-amp inverting input | Current output | | $V_{ref}$ (Pin 14) | $V_{ref}$ via $R_{14}$ | Reference current set | | $V_{EE}$ (Pin 3) | -15V or -12V | Negative supply | | $V_{CC}$ (Pin 13) | +5V to +15V | Positive supply | --- ### Model Solution for Question A(b): **Algorithm to Generate a Triangular Waveform:** The triangular waveform consists of: - A **rising ramp** from 0V to +5V (digital 0 to 255) - A **falling ramp** from +5V to 0V (digital 255 to 0) - A **small delay** at each step to allow the DAC output to settle. **Assembly Language Program:** ```asm ORG 0000H START: MOV R0, #00H ; Initialize counter to 0 RAMP_UP: MOV A, R0 MOV P1, A ; Send digital value to DAC via Port 1 ACALL DELAY INC R0 ; Increment to next voltage level CJNE R0, #0FFH, RAMP_UP ; Continue until 254 RAMP_DN: MOV A, R0 MOV P1, A ACALL DELAY DEC R0 ; Decrement to lower voltage CJNE R0, #00H, RAMP_DN ; Continue until 1 SJMP START ; Repeat the cycle indefinitely DELAY: MOV R2, #0FFH ; Software delay for waveform period control LOOP1: MOV R3, #0FFH LOOP2: DJNZ R3, LOOP2 DJNZ R2, LOOP1 RET END ``` **Algorithm Steps (in flow):** 1. Initialize R0 = 0 (starting from minimum voltage). 2. Move R0 to Accumulator; output to Port 1 (DAC). 3. Call a delay routine to hold the output. 4. Increment R0; repeat until maximum (255). 5. Decrement R0; output to Port 1; call delay; repeat until minimum (0). 6. Loop back to step 1 for continuous waveform. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often forget to **initialize R0 before the RAMP_DN loop** OR they use `INC R0` beyond `0FFH` (which rolls over to 00H, causing waveform glitches). Always ensure boundary conditions are tested properly using `CJNE` (Compare and Jump if Not Equal) instruction. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified] **(a)** Explain the **Successive Approximation** technique of ADC with a neat block diagram. Describe how the **ADC0804** is interfaced with the 8051 microcontroller. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Write an 8051 ALP to read the digital output of an ADC0804 connected to Port 1, where $\overline{WR}$ is connected to P3.0, $\overline{RD}$ to P3.1, and $\overline{INTR}$ to P3.2. Explain the polled method used. **(7 marks)** --- ### Model Solution for Question B(a): **Block Diagram of Successive Approximation ADC (SAR ADC):** ``` +---------------+ | Analog Input | | Vin | +-------+-------+ | v +---+ +---+----+ +-----------+ | | | | | Successive| |Vin|------>| > |<-------| Approx. | | | | Com- | | Register | +---+ | parator| | (SAR) | | | +-----+-----+ +---+----+ | | End of Conversion v (INTR LOW) +---+ +---+----+ | | | | | |<------| DAC | |Vref| | | | | +--------+ +---+ ``` **Working Principle (8-bit example, $V_{in} = 3.0$ V, $V_{ref} = 5.0$ V):** 1. **Start Conversion:** 8051 applies a LOW pulse on the $\overline{WR}$ pin of ADC0804. 2. **SAR Reset:** The internal 8-bit SAR is reset to `0000 0000`. The MSB (bit 7) is set to 1 → trial code = `1000 0000` (128 decimal). 3. **Internal DAC:** The trial code is converted to an analog voltage by the internal DAC: $V_{DAC} = V_{ref} \times \dfrac{128}{256} = 2.5 \text{ V}$. 4. **Comparison:** The comparator checks: Is $V_{in} > V_{DAC}$? Here, $3.0 \text{ V} > 2.5 \text{ V}$ → **YES** → MSB is **kept at 1**. 5. **Next Bit (Bit 6):** Set to 1 → trial code = `1100 0000` (192). $V_{DAC} = 5.0 \times 192/256 = 3.75 \text{ V}$. Compare: $3.0 \text{ V} < 3.75 \text{ V}$ → **NO** → Bit 6 is **reset to 0**. 6. **Next Bit (Bit 5):** Trial = `1010 0000` (160). $V_{DAC} = 3.125 \text{ V}$. Compare: $3.0 \text{ V} < 3.125 \text{ V}$ → **NO** → Bit 5 is **reset to 0**. 7. **Next Bit (Bit 4):** Trial = `1001 0000` (144). $V_{DAC} = 2.8125 \text{ V}$. Compare: $3.0 \text{ V} > 2.8125 \text{ V}$ → **YES** → Bit 4 is **kept at 1**. 8. **Continue for bits 3, 2, 1, 0** in similar fashion. 9. **Final Result:** After 8 comparisons, the final code (e.g., `1001 1011` ≈ 155 decimal) is latched in the output register, and **INTR goes LOW** to signal end of conversion. **Interfacing ADC0804 with 8051:** | ADC0804 Pin | Connected To | Function | |---|---|---| | DB0–DB7 (Pins 11–18) | 8051 P1.0–P1.7 | 8-bit digital output data | | $\overline{CS}$ (Pin 1) | GND | Always selected | | $\overline{WR}$ (Pin 3) | P3.0 | Start of conversion (LOW pulse) | | $\overline{RD}$ (Pin 2) | P3.1 | Output enable / Read | | $\overline{INTR}$ (Pin 5) | P3.2 (INT0) | End of conversion signal | | CLK R (Pin 19), CLK IN (Pin 4) | R=10kΩ, C=150pF to GND | Internal clock @ 606 kHz | | $V_{in}(+)$ (Pin 6) | Analog input source | Signal to be digitized | | $V_{ref}/2$ (Pin 9) | Open (internal ref = 5.12V) | Sets full-scale range | **Working Sequence:** 1. 8051 writes a LOW pulse on P3.0 → ADC0804 starts conversion. 2. ADC0804 raises INTR HIGH during conversion. 3. On completion, INTR goes LOW. 4. 8051 detects the LOW on P3.2 (polled or interrupt-driven). 5. 8051 writes LOW on P3.1 → ADC0804 places data on DB0–DB7. 6. 8051 reads the data from P1 into Accumulator. 7. 8051 raises P3.1 HIGH → tri-states the ADC outputs. --- ### Model Solution for Question B(b): **Assembly Language Program (Polled Method):** ```asm ORG 0000H MOV P1, #0FFH ; Configure Port 1 as input (set all bits to 1) MAIN: CLR P3.0 ; Apply LOW to WR to start conversion ACALL SHORT_DELAY ; Brief delay (≥ 100 ns minimum pulse width) SETB P3.0 ; De-assert WR; conversion begins POLL: JB P3.2, POLL ; Wait for INTR to go LOW (end of conversion) ; Note: INTR is active LOW, so jump back if P3.2 is still HIGH CLR P3.1 ; Apply LOW to RD to enable output buffer MOV A, P1 ; Read the 8-bit digital data from Port 1 SETB P3.1 ; De-assert RD; tri-state ADC outputs ACALL PROCESS_DATA ; Display on LCD, store in memory, etc. SJMP MAIN ; Repeat the conversion cycle SHORT_DELAY: NOP ; 1 µs delay (one machine cycle at 12 MHz) NOP RET PROCESS_DATA: ; Insert display/storage code here RET END ``` **Explanation of the Polled Method:** The polled method is a **busy-wait** technique where the CPU continuously checks the state of the INTR pin until the ADC signals completion. The flow is: 1. **Initialize:** Port 1 is set as input (logic 1 on all pins). This is critical because Port 1 is the default input-only quasi-bidirectional port on the 8051. 2. **Start Conversion:** The 8051 issues a LOW-to-HIGH transition on P3.0 (the $\overline{WR}$ pin). This low pulse, even briefly, latches the start-of-conversion command into the ADC. 3. **Poll for Completion:** The CPU enters a tight loop `POLL: JB P3.2, POLL`. The `JB` (Jump if Bit set) instruction keeps the CPU stuck in this loop until the INTR line goes LOW. 4. **Read Result:** Once INTR goes LOW, the 8051 issues a LOW on P3.1 (the $\overline{RD}$ pin), which enables the tri-state output buffers of the ADC. The 8-bit result is read via Port 1. 5. **Process and Repeat:** The data is processed, and the cycle restarts. **Advantage:** Simple, no interrupt service routine (ISR) needed. **Disadvantage:** CPU is "busy-waiting" for up to 110 µs, wasting cycles that could be used for other tasks. (For multitasking, an **interrupt-driven** method using INT0 is preferred.) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** > 1. **Do not forget to configure P1 as input** at the start. Many students leave it undefined, leading to port contention. > 2. **Do not use `MOVX A, @DPTR`** for the ADC read — that instruction is for external memory. The ADC is interfaced as an **I/O mapped device**, not memory-mapped. > 3. **Polling logic is `JB P3.2, POLL`** (jump if INTR is HIGH = still converting). Using `JNB` inverts the logic and causes the program to exit too early. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ### High-Density Revision Checklist - [ ] **DAC Equation:** $V_{out} = V_{ref} \times D / (2^n - 1)$. The "minus 1" in the denominator is the most common student error. - [ ] **ADC Equation:** $D = V_{in} / V_{ref} \times 2^n$. The denominator is $2^n$, not $(2^n - 1)$. - [ ] **Resolution values:** For 8-bit DAC with $V_{ref} = 5$V → ~19.6 mV. For ADC0804 with internal $V_{ref} = 5.12$V → exactly 20 mV/bit. - [ ] **DAC0808 vs ADC0804:** DAC0808 is **current output** (needs op-amp). ADC0804 is **voltage output** (direct to data bus). - [ ] **DAC interfacing:** Use simple `MOV P1, A`. **No** `MOVX` needed. - [ ] **ADC interfacing:** Use polled or interrupt-driven control of $\overline{WR}$, $\overline{RD}$, and $\overline{INTR}$ pins. Data bus to P1. - [ ] **ADC0804 clock formula:** $f_{CLK} = 1 / (1.1 RC)$. Typical 606 kHz. - [ ] **Conversion time:** 8 bits × clock period = ~110 µs for ADC0804. - [ ] **Successive Approximation:** Fast, $\sim n$ clock cycles, used in ADC0804/0808. - [ ] **Flash ADC:** Fastest, but uses $2^n - 1$ comparators (expensive). - [ ] **Sine wave generation:** Use a pre-calculated lookup table stored in code memory, accessed via `MOVC A, @A+DPTR`. - [ ] **Triangular wave:** Ramp up from 0 to 255, then ramp down. Use `INC`/`DEC` with `CJNE` boundary checks. - [ ] **Staircase wave:** Increment from 0 to 254, output, delay, drop to 0, repeat. - [ ] **Polled ADC read:** `CLR P3.0 → SETB P3.0 → JB P3.2, POLL → CLR P3.1 → MOV A, P1 → SETB P3.1`. - [ ] **Pitfall to avoid:** For ADC0804, the $V_{ref}/2$ pin sets full-scale. If left open, internal $V_{ref} = 5.12$V (gives 20 mV/bit precision). Do not confuse with $V_{ref}$ of DAC0808. - [ ] **Examiner's favorite:** Always show pin connections in a table (not just a diagram) for full marks. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU Strategy Tip:** When answering a 14-mark question on DAC/ADC interfacing, always include: (1) Block diagram, (2) Pin connection table, (3) Program with comments, (4) Algorithm explanation, and (5) Sample calculation of output voltage or input reconstruction. This structure fetches **14/14** under the strict KTU valuation scheme.

topics.title: 8051 Timers/Counters - Modes and Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMRT403 - COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE AND MICROCONTROLLER > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** 8051 Timers/Counters - Modes and Applications ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **8051 Timer/Counter** is a dedicated on-chip hardware peripheral consisting of two independent 16-bit up-counters designated **Timer 0** and **Timer 1**. Each timer is constructed from two cascaded 8-bit Special Function Registers (SFRs): a high byte (**THx**) and a low byte (**TLx**), where 'x' denotes 0 or 1. These peripherals can operate in two fundamental roles: - **Timer Mode:** Counts internal machine cycles derived from the on-chip oscillator. - **Counter Mode:** Counts external events (1-to-0 or 0-to-1 transitions) arriving on specific pins (**T0/P3.4** and **T1/P3.5**). The behaviour is governed by two key SFRs: the **Timer Mode Register (TMOD, Address 89H)** for configuration and the **Timer Control Register (TCON, Address 88H)** for run-control and flag monitoring. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 8051 contains exactly **two** 16-bit timers. A common pitfall is calling them "counters" when they are actually *timers/counters* — a hybrid peripheral that does both jobs based on the `C/T̄` bit in TMOD. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Stopwatch vs. The Door Counter Imagine a small electronic device on your desk: - **Timer Mode** is like a **stopwatch**. It ticks at a perfectly regular, known interval (one machine cycle = 1.085 µs at 11.0592 MHz crystal). Because we know *exactly* how long each tick takes, we can pre-load a starting number and let it count up to its maximum, knowing the precise elapsed time when it overflows. This is **indirect time measurement**. - **Counter Mode** is like a **turnstile at a stadium entrance**. It does not care about time. Every time someone passes through (an external pulse), it adds one to its tally. It answers the question, *"How many times did an external event happen?"* This is **direct event counting**. The same physical hardware (THx, TLx) does both jobs — only the *source* of the counting pulses is different. ### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Parameters | Parameter | Value | Significance | |---|---|---| | **Oscillator Frequency ($f_{osc}$)** | 11.0592 MHz (standard) or 12 MHz | Crystal connected to XTAL1/XTAL2 pins | | **Machine Cycle ($T_{MC}$)** | **$12 / f_{osc}$** | One tick of the timer in timer mode | | **Timer Resolution** | 1.085 µs @ 11.0592 MHz | Smallest measurable time unit | | **Maximum Count (16-bit)** | $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ | Overflow boundary in Mode 1 | | **Maximum Count (13-bit)** | $2^{13} = 8{,}192$ | Overflow boundary in Mode 0 | | **Maximum Count (8-bit)** | $2^{8} = 256$ | Overflow boundary in Mode 2/3 | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The TMOD Register (Address 89H) — Not Bit-Addressable TMOD configures the *mode* and *function* of both timers. It is byte-addressable only (must be loaded as a whole byte). | Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Symbol** | GATE | C/T̄ | M1 | M0 | GATE | C/T̄ | M1 | M0 | | **Belongs to** | Timer 1 | Timer 1 | Timer 1 | Timer 1 | Timer 0 | Timer 0 | Timer 0 | Timer 0 | - **GATE:** When 1, timer runs only if `INTx` pin is HIGH and `TRx` is set (hardware gating). When 0, software gating (only `TRx` matters). - **C/T̄:** **C**ounter / **T**imer select. `1` = Counter mode (external pin). `0` = Timer mode (internal clock). - **M1, M0:** Mode select bits (00, 01, 10, 11 → Modes 0, 1, 2, 3). ### 2.2 The TCON Register (Address 88H) — Bit-Addressable TCON contains the run-control bits and overflow flags. It is bit-addressable, allowing individual bit manipulation. | Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Symbol** | TF1 | TR1 | TF0 | TR0 | IE1 | IT1 | IE0 | IT0 | - **TR0, TR1 (Timer Run):** Set to 1 to start the timer; cleared to 0 to halt it. - **TF0, TF1 (Timer Flag):** Set to 1 by hardware when the timer overflows. Must be cleared by software. ### 2.3 The Four Operating Modes **Mode 0 — 13-Bit Timer (Legacy 8048 Mode)** Uses an 8-bit counter (TLx) with a 5-bit prescaler (lower 5 bits of THx), giving 13 effective bits. Retained for backward compatibility with the Intel 8048 microprocessor. Rarely used in modern designs. **Mode 1 — 16-Bit Timer (Most Common)** Both THx (8 bits) and TLx (8 bits) operate as a single 16-bit up-counter. Overflow occurs at $2^{16} = 65{,}536$. This is the standard mode for generating programmable delays. **Mode 2 — 8-Bit Auto-Reload Timer** TLx is the actual 8-bit counter (counts 0–255). THx holds the *reload value*. When TLx overflows from 255 to 0, the value in THx is automatically copied into TLx, and TFlag is set. This gives a fixed, repeating time interval without software intervention — ideal for **baud-rate generation** in serial communication. **Mode 3 — Split Timer Mode (Timer 0 Only)** Timer 0 is split into two independent 8-bit timers: TL0 (controlled by Timer 0's bits) and TH0 (which "borrows" Timer 1's TR1 and TF1). Timer 1 in this mode typically stops incrementing and is frozen, though it can still be set to Mode 0, 1, or 2 manually — useful only as a baud-rate generator that does not need its interrupt. ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following equations are **exam-critical**. Memorize them and know when to invert them. | # | Formula | Description | |---|---|---| | 1 | $T_{MC} = \dfrac{12}{f_{osc}}$ | Machine cycle (timer tick duration) | | 2 | $N_{counts} = \dfrac{T_{delay}}{T_{MC}}$ | Number of timer ticks required | | 3 | $V_{initial} = 2^{n} - N_{counts}$ | Initial value to preload (n = 13, 16, or 8) | | 4 | $TH_x = \left\lfloor \dfrac{V_{initial}}{256} \right\rfloor$ | High byte extraction | | 5 | $TL_x = V_{initial} \bmod 256$ | Low byte extraction | | 6 | $T_{delay} = (2^{n} - V_{initial}) \times T_{MC}$ | Verification / forward delay | | 7 | $Baud = \dfrac{2^{SMOD}}{32} \times \dfrac{f_{osc}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}$ | Timer 1 Mode 2 baud-rate equation | > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL ENGINEERING NOTE:** Crystal **11.0592 MHz** is preferred over 12 MHz in serial communication because $11{,}059{,}200 / 12 = 921{,}600$ is exactly divisible by standard baud rates (9600, 19200, etc.), producing **0% error**. A 12 MHz crystal causes ~8.5% error at 9600 baud. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Industrial Automation:** Generating precise time-stamped control signals for relays, solenoids, and motor drivers. - **Automotive ECUs:** Crank-shaft position sensing using counter mode on a Hall-effect sensor input. - **Consumer Electronics:** Digital clocks, washing-machine timers, microwave ovens — all use 8051 timers. - **Telemetry & IoT:** Generating accurate baud rates for UART communication with GPS, Bluetooth, and GSM modules. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Delay Equation The derivation below establishes the universal delay formula for any 8051 timer. **Step 1 — Define the machine cycle.** The 8051 divides the crystal oscillator by 12 to produce its machine cycle. This is the granularity at which the timer increments. $$T_{MC} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}}$$ **Step 2 — Define the relationship between ticks and time.** If the timer counts $N_{counts}$ machine cycles before overflowing, the elapsed time is the product of the number of counts and the duration of each count. $$T_{delay} = N_{counts} \times T_{MC}$$ **Step 3 — Express $N_{counts}$ in terms of the initial value.** The timer starts at $V_{initial}$ and counts up to its maximum $2^{n}$ (where $n$ is the bit-width of the mode). Therefore, the number of increments is: $$N_{counts} = 2^{n} - V_{initial}$$ **Step 4 — Combine to obtain the master delay equation.** $$T_{delay} = (2^{n} - V_{initial}) \times \frac{12}{f_{osc}}$$ **Step 5 — Solve for the initial value (the design formula used in exams).** $$V_{initial} = 2^{n} - \left( T_{delay} \times \frac{f_{osc}}{12} \right)$$ --- ### 3.2 Worked Example: Generate 50 ms Delay Using Timer 0, Mode 1, 11.0592 MHz Crystal This is a **classic KTU problem**. The full numerical solution is shown below. **Given:** - $f_{osc} = 11.0592 \text{ MHz} = 11{,}059{,}200 \text{ Hz}$ - $T_{delay} = 50 \text{ ms} = 50{,}000 \text{ \mu s}$ - Mode 1 (16-bit) → $n = 16$, $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ **Step 1 — Compute the machine cycle.** $$T_{MC} = \frac{12}{11{,}059{,}200} = 1.085068 \text{ \mu s}$$ **Step 2 — Compute the required number of counts.** $$N_{counts} = \frac{T_{delay}}{T_{MC}} = \frac{50{,}000 \text{ \mu s}}{1.085068 \text{ \mu s}} \approx 46{,}082.6$$ Since the counter must be an integer, we round to **46,083** (the timer will actually produce a slightly longer delay — this is the *standard* rounding-down approach used in KTU solutions). **Step 3 — Compute the initial value.** $$V_{initial} = 2^{16} - N_{counts} = 65{,}536 - 46{,}083 = 19{,}453$$ **Step 4 — Convert to hex for SFR loading.** $$V_{initial} = 19{,}453_{10} = 0x4BFD_{16}$$ **Step 5 — Extract high and low bytes.** $$TH_0 = \left\lfloor \frac{19{,}453}{256} \right\rfloor = 75 = 0x4B$$ $$TL_0 = 19{,}453 \bmod 256 = 253 = 0xFD$$ **Step 6 — Verify by reverse calculation.** $$T_{actual} = 46{,}083 \times 1.085068 \text{ \mu s} \approx 50{,}002.8 \text{ \mu s} \approx 50.00 \text{ ms} \quad \checkmark$$ --- ### 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Correct Mode | Application | Best Mode | Reason | Typical Prescaler | |---|---|---|---| | **Long software delays (ms / s range)** | Mode 1 (16-bit) | Maximum range = 65,536 ticks ≈ 71 ms @ 11.0592 MHz; can be cascaded via loops for arbitrary lengths | None | | **Short, precise, repeating intervals** | Mode 2 (Auto-reload) | Hardware reload → no jitter, no software overhead | None | | **Baud-rate generator for UART** | Mode 2 of Timer 1 | Auto-reload + dedicated serial engine | None | | **External event counting (e.g., pulses on P3.4)** | Any mode in Counter | `C/T̄ = 1` redirects clock source | None | | **3rd independent timer needed** | Mode 3 of Timer 0 | Splits T0 into two 8-bit counters | None | | **Legacy 8048 interfacing** | Mode 0 | Hardware-compatible with 8048 peripherals | Internal 5-bit prescaler | --- ### 3.4 Decision Flow: Timer vs. Counter The flowchart below summarizes the decision logic when configuring the 8051. ``` START │ ▼ Q1: What is the input source? │ ├── Internal oscillator (after ÷12) ──► Set C/T̄ = 0 → TIMER MODE │ (Measure time, generate delays) │ └── External pin (T0/P3.4 or T1/P3.5) ──► Set C/T̄ = 1 → COUNTER MODE (Count events, measure frequency) │ ▼ Q2: How many bits do I need? │ ├── 8-bit (0–255, fixed interval) ──► M1 M0 = 1 0 → Mode 2 (Auto-reload) ├── 13-bit (0–8191, legacy) ──► M1 M0 = 0 0 → Mode 0 ├── 16-bit (0–65535, general purpose) ──► M1 M0 = 0 1 → Mode 1 ★ Most used └── Two 8-bit counters (T0 only) ──► M1 M0 = 1 1 → Mode 3 │ ▼ Q3: How will the timer start? │ ├── Software only (TRx = 1) ──► GATE = 0 └── Hardware gated (needs INTx pin HIGH) ──► GATE = 1 │ ▼ CONFIGURE TMOD → LOAD THx, TLx → SET TRx → WAIT for TFx ``` ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Internal Block Diagram of an 8051 Timer/Counter The figure below maps the internal architecture of Timer 0 (Timer 1 is identical). It shows how the clock source, gating logic, and counter registers interact. ```mermaid flowchart LR OSC[Oscillator f_osc] --> DIV12["Divider ÷12
Machine Cycle Clock"] DIV12 --> MUX{MUX C/T-bar selector} P34["External Pin T0/P3.4"] --> MUX MUX -->|Timer Mode| AND1((AND)) MUX -->|Counter Mode| AND1 INT0["INT0 Pin P3.2"] --> AND1 GATE_Bit["GATE bit in TMOD"] --> AND1 TR0_Bit["TR0 bit in TCON"] --> AND1 AND1 --> INC["Increment Logic +1 per tick"] INC --> TL0Reg["TL0 8-bit register low byte"] TL0Reg --> TH0Reg["TH0 8-bit register high byte"] TH0Reg --> OVFL{"Overflow 1 to 0 transition?"} OVFL -->|Yes| SETFLAG[Set TF0 flag in TCON] OVFL -->|No| WAIT[Continue counting] SETFLAG --> INT_REQ["Request Interrupt if Enabled"] TH0Reg -.Reload from TH0.-> TL0Reg style OSC fill:#e1f5ff style DIV12 fill:#fff4e1 style MUX fill:#ffe1e1 style INC fill:#e1ffe1 style SETFLAG fill:#f4e1ff ``` ### 4.2 TMOD and TCON Bit Format Reference ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph TMOD_REG["TMOD Register Address 89H - Not Bit Addressable"] direction LR TMOD7["Bit 7 GATE T1"] TMOD6["Bit 6 C/T-bar T1"] TMOD5["Bit 5 M1 T1"] TMOD4["Bit 4 M0 T1"] TMOD3["Bit 3 GATE T0"] TMOD2["Bit 2 C/T-bar T0"] TMOD1["Bit 1 M1 T0"] TMOD0["Bit 0 M0 T0"] end subgraph TCON_REG["TCON Register Address 88H - Bit Addressable"] direction LR TCON7["Bit 7 TF1"] TCON6["Bit 6 TR1"] TCON5["Bit 5 TF0"] TCON4["Bit 4 TR0"] TCON3["Bit 3 IE1"] TCON2["Bit 2 IT1"] TCON1["Bit 1 IE0"] TCON0["Bit 0 IT0"] end style TMOD_REG fill:#fff9e1 style TCON_REG fill:#e1f9ff ``` ### 4.3 Mode 2 Auto-Reload — Functional State Machine ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> LoadValue LoadValue: Load TH0 into TL0 initial value LoadValue --> Counting Counting: TL0 increments on each tick 0 to 255 Counting --> Overflow: TL0 reaches 0xFF and rolls over Overflow: Hardware copies TH0 to TL0 Overflow: Sets TF0 flag Overflow --> Counting Counting --> Halted: User clears TR0 Halted --> [*] ``` ### 4.4 Typical Application Circuit — Pulse Counting ```mermaid flowchart LR SENSOR["Rotary Encoder or
Hall-Effect Sensor"] --> SCHMITT["Schmitt Trigger
74HC14"] SCHMITT --> P34["8051 Pin P3.4 T0"] P34 --> COUNTER[8051 Internal Counter] COUNTER --> LCD["Display Count on
16x2 LCD via P1"] COUNTER --> UART[("Optional UART
to PC")] SENSOR2[("Object 2nd Sensor")] --> P35["8051 Pin P3.5 T1"] P35 --> COUNTER style SENSOR fill:#e1ffe1 style SCHMITT fill:#fff4e1 style COUNTER fill:#e1f5ff style LCD fill:#f4e1ff ``` > [!NOTE] > **KTU Practical Tip:** When using counter mode, the external input pin must be held HIGH for at least one machine cycle and LOW for at least one machine cycle to be recognized as a valid pulse. Bounce-prone mechanical switches require a Schmitt trigger (e.g., 74HC14) and a debouncing capacitor. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. Differentiate between Timer mode and Counter mode of the 8051.** *[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Model Paper Cycle 2]* **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Parameter | Timer Mode | Counter Mode | |---|---|---| | **C/T̄ bit in TMOD** | 0 | 1 | | **Clock source** | Internal oscillator ÷ 12 (machine cycle) | External pin T0 (P3.4) or T1 (P3.5) | | **Purpose** | Time delay generation, baud-rate | Counting external events | | **Typical use** | Generating 1 ms / 50 ms delays | Counting rotations, persons, pulses | | **Trigger edge** | Automatic, every machine cycle | 1-to-0 transition on the external pin | *(1 mark each for: source difference, C/T̄ bit value, one application difference.)* --- **Q2. List the four operating modes of 8051 Timer and state the width of each.** *[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Mode 0** → **13-bit** timer (5 bits of THx + 8 bits of TLx); legacy 8048 compatibility. *(0.75)* - **Mode 1** → **16-bit** timer (THx + TLx); most commonly used for delay generation. *(0.75)* - **Mode 2** → **8-bit auto-reload** timer (TLx counts, THx holds reload value). *(0.75)* - **Mode 3** → **Split timer** mode (Timer 0 only) — TL0 and TH0 act as two independent 8-bit timers. *(0.75)* --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A — Option 1** *(14 Marks Total)* **(a)** Explain the **TMOD** and **TCON** SFRs of the 8051 with a neat bit-format diagram. Discuss the function of the **GATE** and **C/T̄** bits in detail. *(7 Marks)* **Model Solution:** **TMOD Register (Address 89H):** It is a non-bit-addressable register used to configure the mode and function of Timer 0 and Timer 1. The upper nibble (bits 7–4) configures Timer 1; the lower nibble (bits 3–0) configures Timer 0. The four bits per timer are: - **GATE (bit 7 / bit 3):** Controls hardware vs. software triggering. If `GATE = 1`, the timer runs only when the corresponding `INTx` pin is HIGH **AND** the `TRx` bit is set. If `GATE = 0`, the timer is controlled purely by software through `TRx` (the `INTx` pin is ignored). Hardware gating is used in pulse-width measurement. *(2 marks)* - **C/T̄ (bit 6 / bit 2):** Selects the clock source. `C/T̄ = 0` → internal machine cycle → **Timer mode**. `C/T̄ = 1` → external pin T0 (P3.4) or T1 (P3.5) → **Counter mode**. *(2 marks)* - **M1, M0 (bits 5,4 / 1,0):** Mode select bits. Combinations: `00` = Mode 0 (13-bit), `01` = Mode 1 (16-bit), `10` = Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload), `11` = Mode 3 (split). *(1 mark)* **TCON Register (Address 88H):** Bit-addressable. Holds the run-control bits and overflow flags. - `TR0` (bit 4) and `TR1` (bit 6): Set to 1 to start the respective timer; cleared to 0 to halt. - `TF0` (bit 5) and `TF1` (bit 7): Set to 1 by hardware on overflow; must be cleared by software. - `IE0, IE1, IT0, IT1`: External interrupt control (lower 4 bits). *(1 mark)* - Neat diagram of both registers (0.5 marks each). *(1 mark total)* --- **(b)** With a suitable example, explain how a **delay is generated** using Timer 0 of the 8051 in **Mode 1**. Write the assembly program to flash an LED connected to P1.0 with a 50 ms ON / 50 ms OFF cycle. Assume $f_{osc} = 11.0592 \text{ MHz}$. *(7 Marks)* **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Calculate the initial value (refer to Section 3.2 above).** For 50 ms delay in Mode 1, we obtained $V_{initial} = 19{,}453 = 0x4BFD$, so $TH_0 = 0x4B$ and $TL_0 = 0xFD$. *(1 mark)* **Step 2 — Conceptual Steps for Delay Generation:** *(2 marks)* 1. Load `TMOD` with the value selecting Timer 0 in Mode 1, software gating, timer mode. For Mode 1 of T0: `M1 M0 = 01`, `C/T̄ = 0`, `GATE = 0` → lower nibble = `0000 0001` = `0x01`. 2. Load the calculated values into `TH0` and `TL0`. 3. Set `TR0 = 1` to start the timer. 4. Monitor `TF0` in a loop. When `TF0 = 1`, overflow has occurred. 5. Clear `TR0` and `TF0` for the next iteration. **Step 3 — Assembly Program:** *(3 marks for program, 1 mark for explanation)* ```assembly ORG 0000H MOV TMOD, #01H ; Timer 0, Mode 1, Software gate, Timer mode MOV P1, #00H ; Clear Port 1 LOOP: CPL P1.0 ; Complement LED bit ACALL DELAY_50MS ; Call 50 ms delay subroutine SJMP LOOP ;------ 50 ms Delay Subroutine (Timer 0, Mode 1) ------ DELAY_50MS: MOV TH0, #4BH ; Load high byte MOV TL0, #0FDH ; Load low byte SETB TR0 ; Start Timer 0 WAIT: JNB TF0, WAIT ; Wait until TF0 = 1 (overflow) CLR TR0 ; Stop timer CLR TF0 ; Clear overflow flag RET END ``` The program toggles P1.0 every 50 ms, producing a 100 ms-period square wave (≈ 10 Hz flash). To get the 1-second blink commonly seen in KTU labs, wrap this routine in an outer loop of 10 iterations. --- #### **Question B — Option 2** *(14 Marks Total)* **(a)** Explain the **four operating modes** of the 8051 timers with the help of diagrams. Highlight which mode is used for **baud-rate generation** in serial communication. *(7 Marks)* **Model Solution:** *(2 marks for Mode 0, 2 marks for Mode 1, 2 marks for Mode 2, 1 mark for Mode 3)* **Mode 0 — 13-Bit Timer (8048 Compatibility):** The 13-bit counter is formed by connecting the lower 5 bits of THx to the upper 5 bits of an 8-bit TLx counter, giving an effective 13-bit up-counter that overflows at $2^{13} = 8192$. The upper 3 bits of THx are unused. This mode is rarely used in modern applications and is retained solely for compatibility with the older 8048 microcontroller family. **Mode 1 — 16-Bit Timer (General Purpose):** THx and TLx are cascaded to form a full 16-bit counter. Overflow at $2^{16} = 65{,}536$. This is the most commonly used mode for generating software delays ranging from 1 µs up to ~71 ms (for an 11.0592 MHz crystal). For delays longer than 71 ms, software loops around the timer routine are used. **Mode 2 — 8-Bit Auto-Reload Timer:** TLx acts as the 8-bit counter (0–255), while THx stores the *reload value*. When TLx overflows from 0xFF to 0x00, the hardware **automatically copies THx into TLx** in the same cycle, producing a glitch-free, fixed-period output. **This is the mode used for baud-rate generation in serial communication** (specifically Timer 1, Mode 2, with the serial engine's auto-reload mechanism), because the UART hardware requires a constant, jitter-free clock source at a precise multiple of the baud rate. *(Highlight: 1 mark for baud-rate identification.)* **Mode 3 — Split Timer (Timer 0 Only):** Timer 0 is split into two independent 8-bit timers — TL0 (operates as an 8-bit timer/counter under Timer 0's TR0 and TF0) and TH0 (operates as a fixed 8-bit timer under Timer 1's TR1 and TF1). When Timer 0 is in Mode 3, Timer 1 loses its run control (TR1) and flag (TF1) to TH0, but Timer 1 can still be configured in Modes 0, 1, or 2 — typically used as a baud-rate generator in this configuration. --- **(b)** Design and write an 8051 assembly program to generate a **square wave of 2 kHz** on pin **P1.7** using **Timer 0 in Mode 2**, with $f_{osc} = 11.0592 \text{ MHz}$. Calculate the value to be loaded into **TH0**. *(7 Marks)* **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Calculate the half-period of the square wave.** A 2 kHz square wave has a period of $1/2000 = 500 \text{ \mu s}$. Since the wave must go HIGH for half the period and LOW for the other half, each delay routine must be **250 µs** long. *(1 mark)* **Step 2 — Calculate the machine cycle.** $$T_{MC} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \text{ MHz}} = 1.085 \text{ \mu s}$$ *(0.5 mark)* **Step 3 — Calculate the required counts.** $$N_{counts} = \frac{250 \text{ \mu s}}{1.085 \text{ \mu s}} \approx 230.4 \approx 230$$ *(0.5 mark)* **Step 4 — Calculate the initial value (Mode 2 is 8-bit, $2^8 = 256$).** $$V_{initial} = 256 - 230 = 26 = 0x1A$$ **Step 5 — TH0 (reload value) = 0x1A.** *(1 mark)* **Step 6 — Assembly Program:** *(3 marks)* ```assembly ORG 0000H MOV TMOD, #02H ; Timer 0, Mode 2, auto-reload MOV TH0, #1AH ; Reload value (high byte holds reload) MOV P1, #00H ; Initialize port SETB TR0 ; Start Timer 0 LOOP: JNB TF0, $ ; Wait for overflow (auto-reload happens in HW) CLR TF0 ; Clear flag CPL P1.7 ; Toggle P1.7 SJMP LOOP END ``` **Step 7 — Verification.** $$T_{actual} = 230 \times 1.085 \text{ \mu s} = 249.55 \text{ \mu s} \approx 250 \text{ \mu s} \quad \checkmark$$ $$f_{out} = \frac{1}{2 \times 250 \text{ \mu s}} = 2 \text{ kHz} \quad \checkmark$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully!** > 1. **Forgetting the half-period:** In square-wave problems, the calculated delay is the *half-period* (HIGH time = LOW time). A 2 kHz wave needs a 250 µs delay, not 500 µs. Marks deducted: 1–2. > 2. **Using the wrong crystal in delay calculation:** Mixing 12 MHz and 11.0592 MHz constants is the #1 error. Always write $T_{MC} = 12/f_{osc}$ *first* and plug in the value given in the problem. > 3. **Not clearing TF0 in software:** Unlike some microcontrollers, the 8051 does **not** auto-clear TF0 in most modes. If the student forgets `CLR TF0`, the program will behave as if the timer is permanently overflowed. Marks deducted: 1. > 4. **Loading THx with the initial value in Mode 1:** In Mode 1, the *initial value* must be split into THx and TLx, not loaded entirely into one. Marks deducted: 2. > 5. **Confusing Mode 2 and Mode 3:** Mode 2 is auto-reload (THx = reload). Mode 3 is the split timer (Timer 0 only). Swapping these in a long answer costs 2–3 marks. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - [x] The 8051 has **two 16-bit timers/counters** — Timer 0 and Timer 1. - [x] Each timer is built from two 8-bit SFRs: **THx** (high byte) and **TLx** (low byte). - [x] **TMOD (89H)** configures mode; **TCON (88H)** controls run and flag bits. - [x] The **C/T̄ bit** is the master switch between Timer and Counter modes. - [x] **Mode 1 (16-bit)** is the workhorse mode for delay generation. - [x] **Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload)** is the standard mode for **baud-rate generation**. - [x] **Mode 3** is a special Timer-0-only mode giving two independent 8-bit timers. - [x] Machine cycle $T_{MC} = 12 / f_{osc}$ — at 11.0592 MHz, this is **1.085 µs**. - [x] Initial value formula: $V_{initial} = 2^{n} - (T_{delay} \times f_{osc}/12)$. - [x] $TH_x = \lfloor V_{initial} / 256 \rfloor$, $TL_x = V_{initial} \bmod 256$. - [x] Always **clear TFx in software** after detecting overflow. - [x] **GATE = 1** enables hardware triggering through the `INTx` pin (pulse-width measurement). - [x] Counter mode counts **1-to-0 transitions** on T0 (P3.4) or T1 (P3.5). - [x] **11.0592 MHz** crystal is preferred over 12 MHz for accurate UART baud rates. - [x] For square-wave generation, the delay is **half the period** of the desired frequency.

topics.title: Serial Data Transfer - SFRs of Serial Port
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMRT403 - COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE AND MICROCONTROLLER > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Serial Data Transfer - SFRs of Serial Port ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition **Serial Data Transfer** in the **8051 Microcontroller** refers to the asynchronous, bit-by-bit transmission and reception of data through its on-chip **UART (Universal Asynchronous Receiver/Transmitter)** peripheral. The 8051 dedicates a set of **Special Function Registers (SFRs)** to control, configure, and monitor this serial communication activity. The three primary SFRs governing the 8051's serial port are: 1. **SCON (Serial Control Register)** — Address **98H**, Bit-addressable. 2. **SBUF (Serial Data Buffer Register)** — Address **99H**. 3. **PCON (Power Control Register)** — Address **87H** (specifically its **SMOD** bit at position 7, which controls baud rate doubling). > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Students must be able to draw the **SCON register bit format**, list the **four serial modes (Mode 0, 1, 2, 3)**, and explain the role of **SBUF as a dual register** (one for transmit, one for receive) sharing the same SFR address. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Two-Way Radio" Intuition Imagine the 8051 as a security guard stationed at a gate, holding **two separate mailboxes** at the same physical address: - One **mailbox (TX side)** is for *outgoing* letters — when the guard drops a letter into it, the postman automatically takes it away and sends it out one bit at a time. - The other **mailbox (RX side)** is for *incoming* letters — when a letter arrives bit-by-bit, the postman assembles it and places the complete letter in this box for the guard to read. The guard uses a **control panel (SCON)** to: - Choose the **language/speed** (Mode selection: Mode 0–3), - Decide whether to **accept incoming mail** (REN bit), - Be alerted when a **letter is sent** (TI flag) or **received** (RI flag). A **speed dial (PCON's SMOD bit)** allows the postman to work at double speed if needed. This is exactly how the 8051 serial port functions: writing to SBUF loads the transmit register; reading SBUF accesses the receive register; both share address **99H**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exam Tip:** The "dual-register at one address" concept of SBUF is a **favourite KTU question** — students frequently lose marks by saying SBUF is a *single* register. --- ### 1.3 Why Serial Communication Matters Serial communication requires only **one data line (TXD)** for transmission and **one line (RXD)** for reception, drastically reducing pin count compared to parallel communication (which needs 8 lines). The 8051's UART supports **full-duplex** operation, meaning simultaneous transmit and receive. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The SCON Register (Serial Control Register) — 98H SCON is an **8-bit, bit-addressable** register. Each bit has a specific role in configuring the serial port: | Bit | Name | Function | |:---:|:----:|:---------| | **D7** | **SM0** | Serial Mode bit 0 (combined with SM1 to select Mode 0–3) | | **D6** | **SM1** | Serial Mode bit 1 | | **D5** | **SM2** | Multiprocessor Communication Enable (set for Modes 2 & 3; enables 9th-bit check) | | **D4** | **REN** | Receive Enable — must be set by software to allow reception | | **D3** | **TB8** | 9th transmitted bit (used in Modes 2 & 3 for parity/addressing) | | **D2** | **RB8** | 9th received bit (stores the 9th bit of received data in Modes 2 & 3) | | **D1** | **TI** | Transmit Interrupt flag — set by hardware at end of byte transmission; cleared by software | | **D0** | **RI** | Receive Interrupt flag — set by hardware at end of byte reception; cleared by software | #### Serial Modes Determined by SM0 and SM1 | SM0 | SM1 | Mode | Description | Baud Rate Source | |:---:|:---:|:----:|:------------|:-----------------| | 0 | 0 | **Mode 0** | 8-bit Shift Register (synchronous) | Fixed: $\dfrac{f_{\text{osc}}}{12}$ | | 0 | 1 | **Mode 1** | 8-bit UART (asynchronous) | Variable: Timer 1 overflow | | 1 | 0 | **Mode 2** | 9-bit UART (asynchronous) | Fixed: $\dfrac{f_{\text{osc}}}{32}$ or $\dfrac{f_{\text{osc}}}{64}$ | | 1 | 1 | **Mode 3** | 9-bit UART (asynchronous) | Variable: Timer 1 overflow | > [!NOTE] > **Why TI and RI are NOT auto-cleared:** The 8051 sets TI/RI in hardware to signal completion, but the user program must manually reset them (typically by writing 0). This is a common interview/KTU question. --- ### 2.2 The SBUF Register (Serial Data Buffer) — 99H Although **SBUF** appears as a single SFR at address **99H**, it is physically **two separate 8-bit registers** internally: - **Transmit Buffer Register:** Writing a byte to SBUF loads it here. The UART hardware shifts this byte out serially on **TXD pin (P3.1)**. - **Receive Buffer Register:** When a complete byte is received on **RXD pin (P3.0)**, it is latched here. Reading SBUF retrieves this byte. Because both share the same address, the operation (read vs write) is what selects which internal register is accessed. --- ### 2.3 The PCON Register (Power Control) — 87H (Only SMOD matters here) PCON is **not bit-addressable**. Of its 8 bits, only **bit 7 (SMOD)** is relevant to serial communication: | Bit | Name | Function | |:---:|:----:|:---------| | **D7** | **SMOD** | **Baud Rate Doubler** — when set to 1, doubles the baud rate in Modes 1, 2, and 3 | | D6 | — | Reserved | | D5 | — | Reserved | | D4 | — | Reserved | | D3 | GF1 | General Purpose Flag | | D2 | GF0 | General Purpose Flag | | D1 | PD | Power Down mode bit | | D0 | IDL | Idle mode bit | --- ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Baud Rate Calculations | Mode | Baud Rate Formula | TH1 Reload Value | |:----:|:------------------|:-----------------| | **Mode 0** | $\text{Baud} = \dfrac{f_{\text{osc}}}{12}$ | Not applicable (Timer 1 not used) | | **Mode 2** | $\text{Baud} = \dfrac{2^{\text{SMOD}}}{64} \times f_{\text{osc}}$ | Not applicable | | **Mode 1 & 3** | $\text{Baud} = \dfrac{2^{\text{SMOD}}}{32} \times \dfrac{f_{\text{osc}}}{12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}$ | $\text{TH1} = 256 - \dfrac{2^{\text{SMOD}} \times f_{\text{osc}}}{384 \times \text{Baud}}$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **SMOD Special Cases (KTU Favourite):** > - When **SMOD = 0** → TH1 = $256 - \dfrac{f_{\text{osc}}}{384 \times \text{Baud}}$ > - When **SMOD = 1** → TH1 = $256 - \dfrac{f_{\text{osc}}}{192 \times \text{Baud}}$ ### 2.5 Why and How — The Engineering Rationale - **Why two flags (TI & RI)?** Because the 8051 has only one hardware UART vector (interrupt 4), TI and RI together share the interrupt and the programmer must poll to determine which event triggered it. - **Why is SMOD needed?** It provides flexibility in baud rate generation without changing the crystal frequency — useful when exact standard baud rates (like 9600) cannot be achieved with SMOD=0. - **Real-world utility:** UART serial communication is the backbone of GPS modules, Bluetooth (HC-05), GSM modules, RFID readers, and PC serial debugging — all of which interface with the 8051 via these SFRs. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Baud Rate Formula (Mode 1 and Mode 3) The 8051 generates its variable baud rate in Modes 1 and 3 by **overflowing Timer 1**. The derivation is as follows: **Step 1 — Timer 1 Overflow Frequency:** Timer 1 is configured in **Mode 2** (8-bit auto-reload). The timer increments on every machine cycle. Since one machine cycle = $12 / f_{\text{osc}}$ seconds, the timer increments every $\dfrac{12}{f_{\text{osc}}}$ seconds. **Step 2 — Time for One Full Count Cycle (0 to 256):** The timer overflows when it counts from the reload value TH1 up to 256 (0x100). The number of increments per overflow cycle = $256 - \text{TH1}$. Therefore, the time period of one overflow cycle is: $$ T_{\text{overflow}} = (256 - \text{TH1}) \times \dfrac{12}{f_{\text{osc}}} $$ **Step 3 — Overflow Frequency:** The frequency at which the timer overflows is the reciprocal of this period: $$ f_{\text{overflow}} = \dfrac{f_{\text{osc}}}{12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})} $$ **Step 4 — UART Division by 32:** The 8051's UART divides this overflow frequency by **32** to get the actual baud rate. This division accounts for the start bit, data bits, and stop bit framing. $$ \text{Baud Rate} = \dfrac{1}{32} \times \dfrac{f_{\text{osc}}}{12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})} $$ **Step 5 — Including the SMOD Doubler:** When SMOD = 1, an additional factor of 2 is introduced, giving the final formula: $$ \text{Baud Rate} = \dfrac{2^{\text{SMOD}}}{32} \times \dfrac{f_{\text{osc}}}{12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})} $$ **Step 6 — Solving for TH1 (the reload value):** Rearranging algebraically to find TH1 for a target baud rate: $$ (256 - \text{TH1}) = \dfrac{2^{\text{SMOD}} \times f_{\text{osc}}}{32 \times 12 \times \text{Baud}} $$ $$ \therefore \quad \text{TH1} = 256 - \dfrac{2^{\text{SMOD}} \times f_{\text{osc}}}{384 \times \text{Baud}} $$ --- ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Classic KTU Problem) > **Problem:** An 8051 system uses an **11.0592 MHz crystal**. Calculate the TH1 value to achieve **9600 baud** in **Mode 1** with **SMOD = 0**. **Step 1 — Identify known values:** $f_{\text{osc}} = 11.0592 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}$, Baud = 9600, SMOD = 0 **Step 2 — Substitute into the TH1 formula:** $$ \text{TH1} = 256 - \dfrac{2^{0} \times 11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{384 \times 9600} $$ **Step 3 — Compute the numerator:** $$ 2^{0} \times 11.0592 \times 10^{6} = 11.0592 \times 10^{6} $$ **Step 4 — Compute the denominator:** $$ 384 \times 9600 = 3{,}686{,}400 $$ **Step 5 — Divide:** $$ \dfrac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{3{,}686{,}400} = 3 $$ **Step 6 — Final calculation:** $$ \text{TH1} = 256 - 3 = 253 = \text{FDH (in hexadecimal)} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why 11.0592 MHz?** This peculiar crystal frequency was chosen by Intel **specifically because** it is divisible by standard baud rates (9600, 4800, 2400, 1200) yielding integer TH1 values — making serial communication timing accurate with **0% error**. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Serial Modes at a Glance | Feature | Mode 0 | Mode 1 | Mode 2 | Mode 3 | |:--------|:-------|:-------|:-------|:-------| | Synchronization | Synchronous | Asynchronous | Asynchronous | Asynchronous | | Data Frame Size | 8 bits | 10 bits (start + 8 + stop) | 11 bits (start + 9 + stop) | 11 bits (start + 9 + stop) | | Baud Rate | Fixed ($f_{\text{osc}}/12$) | Variable (Timer 1) | Fixed ($f_{\text{osc}}/32$ or 64) | Variable (Timer 1) | | Clock Source | $f_{\text{osc}}$ | Timer 1 overflow | $f_{\text{osc}}$ | Timer 1 overflow | | SMOD Effect | None | Doubles baud | Doubles baud | Doubles baud | | Common Use | I/O expansion (shift registers) | PC communication | Multiprocessor | Multiprocessor | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — 8051 Serial Port Internal Architecture The following Mermaid diagram illustrates how the three SFRs (SCON, SBUF, PCON) and Timer 1 interconnect to form the complete 8051 serial communication subsystem: ```mermaid flowchart TD CPU["8051 CPU Core"] SCON["SCON Register
Address 98H
Bit Addressable"] SBUFTX["SBUF Transmit
Internal Register"] SBUFRX["SBUF Receive
Internal Register"] PCON["PCON Register
Address 87H
Bit 7 SMOD"] TMR1["Timer 1
Mode 2 Auto Reload
TH1 Register"] TXD["TXD Pin
P3.1"] RXD["RXD Pin
P3.0"] ShiftReg["Serial Shift
Register Engine"] External["External Device
PC or Another MCU"] CPU -->|"Write to 99H"| SBUFTX CPU -->|"Read from 99H"| SBUFRX CPU -->|"Write to 98H"| SCON CPU -->|"Write to 87H"| PCON TMR1 -->|"Overflow pulse divided by 32"| ShiftReg PCON -.->|"SMOD bit doubles rate"| ShiftReg SCON -->|"Mode bits SM0, SM1"| ShiftReg SCON -->|"REN enables receive"| ShiftReg SBUFTX --> ShiftReg ShiftReg -->|"Serial bit stream"| TXD TXD --> External External --> RXD RXD --> ShiftReg ShiftReg --> SBUFRX SCON -->|"TI flag set"| CPU SCON -->|"RI flag set"| CPU ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — SCON Register Bit Layout (Address 98H) ```mermaid graph LR D7["D7: SM0
Mode Select 0"] D6["D6: SM1
Mode Select 1"] D5["D5: SM2
Multiprocessor"] D4["D4: REN
Receive Enable"] D3["D3: TB8
9th TX Bit"] D2["D2: RB8
9th RX Bit"] D1["D1: TI
TX Flag"] D0["D0: RI
RX Flag"] D7 --- D6 --- D5 --- D4 --- D3 --- D2 --- D1 --- D0 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading Convention:** Bits are numbered from **right to left** (D0 is the LSB). SCON's reset value is **00H** — meaning Mode 0, reception disabled, no transmission in progress. ### 4.3 Functional Data Flow — Transmission vs Reception ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph TRANSMIT["TRANSMIT PATH"] A1["CPU writes byte to SBUF"] --> A2["Byte latched in TX buffer"] A2 --> A3["Shift register sends bit by bit"] A3 --> A4["Hardware sets TI = 1"] A4 --> A5["CPU clears TI in ISR"] end subgraph RECEIVE["RECEIVE PATH"] B1["RXD pin receives start bit"] --> B2["Shift register assembles 8 bits"] B2 --> B3["Byte latched in RX buffer"] B3 --> B4["Hardware sets RI = 1"] B4 --> B5["CPU reads SBUF and clears RI"] end ``` ### 4.4 Functional Architecture — How SBUF Duality Works ```mermaid flowchart TD Addr99["SFR Address 99H
SBUF"] Decoder["Internal Address Decoder"] TXBuf["8-bit Transmit Buffer
(Write-only from CPU)"] RXBuf["8-bit Receive Buffer
(Read-only by CPU)"] TXDPin["TXD Pin P3.1"] RXDPin["RXD Pin P3.0"] Addr99 --> Decoder Decoder -->|"If WR signal active"| TXBuf Decoder -->|"If RD signal active"| RXBuf TXBuf -->|"Shifted out"| TXDPin RXDPin -->|"Shifted in"| RXBuf ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exam Diagram Tip:** When asked to draw "SBUF as a dual register," show **two distinct physical registers** connected to address 99H via a **demultiplexer controlled by the RD/WR control signal**. This visualization reliably earns full marks in KTU valuation. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *List any three functions of the SCON register in 8051.* **Model Answer:** The SCON (Serial Control Register, address 98H) is an 8-bit, bit-addressable register used to configure and control the 8051's serial port. Its three primary functions are: 1. **Mode Selection:** Bits SM0 and SM1 select one of the four serial communication modes (Mode 0, 1, 2, or 3), each differing in frame size and baud rate source. 2. **Receive Control:** The REN bit (bit 4) must be set by software to enable serial reception; without REN = 1, the 8051 ignores all incoming data on RXD. 3. **Interrupt Flagging:** The TI (bit 1) and RI (bit 0) flags are set by hardware at the completion of transmission and reception respectively, signalling the CPU via polling or interrupt. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Explain the significance of the SMOD bit of the PCON register.* **Model Answer:** The **SMOD bit** is bit 7 of the **PCON register (address 87H)**. Its sole function related to serial communication is to **double the baud rate** in Modes 1, 2, and 3 when set to 1. Mathematically, the baud rate formula includes a $2^{\text{SMOD}}$ factor — when SMOD = 0, baud rate is unaffected; when SMOD = 1, baud rate is multiplied by 2. This provides flexibility in achieving exact standard baud rates (like 9600) without changing the crystal oscillator. For example, with an 11.0592 MHz crystal and SMOD = 1, the TH1 reload value for 9600 baud becomes **FDH** (versus FDH with SMOD = 0, but the difference manifests for non-standard baud values). --- ### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- #### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** **Q3. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** Draw the SCON register of 8051 and explain the function of each bit. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the working of the SBUF register. Explain how it handles transmission and reception. **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution for (a):** **SCON Register (Address 98H) — Bit Layout:** | D7 | D6 | D5 | D4 | D3 | D2 | D1 | D0 | |:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:| | SM0 | SM1 | SM2 | REN | TB8 | RB8 | TI | RI | **Bit-wise Function Explanation:** 1. **SM0 (D7) and SM1 (D6) — Serial Mode Select Bits:** Together they select one of four UART modes: - **SM0=0, SM1=0 → Mode 0:** 8-bit shift register, baud = $f_{\text{osc}}/12$. - **SM0=0, SM1=1 → Mode 1:** 8-bit UART, variable baud rate from Timer 1. - **SM0=1, SM1=0 → Mode 2:** 9-bit UART, baud = $f_{\text{osc}}/32$ (or 64 if SMOD=0). - **SM0=1, SM1=1 → Mode 3:** 9-bit UART, variable baud rate from Timer 1. 2. **SM2 (D5) — Multiprocessor Communication Enable:** In Modes 2 and 3, when SM2 = 1, RI is only activated if the received 9th bit (RB8) is 1, enabling selective addressing in multiprocessor setups. In Mode 1, RI is activated only if a valid stop bit is received. 3. **REN (D4) — Receive Enable:** Must be set to 1 by software to enable reception. If REN = 0, reception is disabled. 4. **TB8 (D3) — 9th Transmitted Bit:** In Modes 2 and 3, this is the 9th data bit to be transmitted — often used as a parity bit or address/data discriminator. 5. **RB8 (D2) — 9th Received Bit:** In Modes 2 and 3, this holds the 9th data bit of the received frame. 6. **TI (D1) — Transmit Interrupt Flag:** Set by hardware at the **start** of the stop bit in transmission (i.e., at the end of the byte). Must be cleared by software. 7. **RI (D0) — Receive Interrupt Flag:** Set by hardware at the end of a successful reception. Must be cleared by software. --- **Model Solution for (b):** **SBUF Register (Address 99H) — The Dual-Register Concept:** Although SBUF appears as a single SFR at address 99H, the 8051 internally implements it as **two physically separate 8-bit registers** — one dedicated to transmission and the other to reception. **Functional Diagram:** ``` CPU Write to 99H CPU Read from 99H | | v v +-------------+ +-------------+ | TX Buffer | | RX Buffer | | (Write-only)| | (Read-only) | +------+------+ +------+------+ | ^ | shifted out bit-by-bit | latched after v | 8 bits assembled +-------------+ +-------------+ | TXD pin P3.1| | RXD pin P3.0| +------+------+ +------+------+ | ^ v | External Device <------------> External Device ``` **Working:** - **Transmission:** When the CPU executes `MOV SBUF, A` (or `MOV 99H, A`), the accumulator's content is loaded into the **TX buffer**. The UART hardware then automatically shifts this byte out serially on the **TXD pin (P3.1)** at the configured baud rate. Once the last bit (stop bit) is shifted out, hardware sets the **TI flag**. - **Reception:** When the UART detects a start bit on **RXD pin (P3.0)**, it begins sampling and assembling incoming bits into an 8-bit byte. The complete byte is latched into the **RX buffer**, and the **RI flag** is set. The CPU then reads it via `MOV A, SBUF`. The same address (99H) is used because the **read/write control signal** of the 8051's internal bus acts as a demultiplexer — selecting the TX register on writes and the RX register on reads. --- #### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **Q4. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** Explain the four serial communication modes of 8051 with their baud rate formulas. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** An 8051 system uses a **12 MHz crystal** and operates in **Mode 1** with **SMOD = 1**. Calculate the TH1 value to generate a baud rate of **2400**.
