Also calculate the actual baud rate achieved. **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution for (a):** The 8051 supports four serial modes selected by SM0 and SM1 bits of SCON: | Mode | SM0:SM1 | Frame Format | Baud Rate Formula | Typical Application | |:----:|:-------:|:-------------|:------------------|:-------------------| | **Mode 0** | 00 | 8-bit shift register (synchronous) | $\dfrac{f_{\text{osc}}}{12}$ | Expanding I/O using 74HC595 shift registers | | **Mode 1** | 01 | 10-bit frame (start + 8 data + stop) | $\dfrac{2^{\text{SMOD}}}{32} \times \dfrac{f_{\text{osc}}}{12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}$ | Standard PC UART communication (9600 baud) | | **Mode 2** | 10 | 11-bit frame (start + 9 data + stop) | $\dfrac{2^{\text{SMOD}}}{64} \times f_{\text{osc}}$ | High-speed fixed-rate multiprocessor links | | **Mode 3** | 11 | 11-bit frame (start + 9 data + stop) | $\dfrac{2^{\text{SMOD}}}{32} \times \dfrac{f_{\text{osc}}}{12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}$ | Multiprocessor with variable baud | **Explanation:** - **Mode 0** is **synchronous** — RXD serves as the data line and TXD as the shift clock. Baud is always $f_{\text{osc}}/12$ regardless of SMOD. Useful for cascading serial shift registers. - **Mode 1** is the most commonly used — standard 8-bit asynchronous UART with one start bit and one stop bit. Baud rate is **variable** and **derived from Timer 1 overflow** in auto-reload Mode 2. - **Mode 2** uses a **fixed** baud rate of either $f_{\text{osc}}/32$ (SMOD=1) or $f_{\text{osc}}/64$ (SMOD=0). It supports 9 data bits, making it suitable for simple multiprocessor communication with 9th-bit addressing. - **Mode 3** combines the variable baud rate of Mode 1 with the 9-bit frame of Mode 2, making it the most flexible mode for multiprocessor networks. --- **Model Solution for (b):** **Given Data:** - $f_{\text{osc}} = 12 \text{ MHz} = 12 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}$ - Mode = 1, SMOD = 1, Target Baud = 2400 **Step 1 — Apply the TH1 formula:** $$ \text{TH1} = 256 - \dfrac{2^{\text{SMOD}} \times f_{\text{osc}}}{384 \times \text{Baud}} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute the values:** $$ \text{TH1} = 256 - \dfrac{2^{1} \times 12 \times 10^{6}}{384 \times 2400} $$ **Step 3 — Compute numerator and denominator:** $$ \text{Numerator} = 2 \times 12 \times 10^{6} = 24 \times 10^{6} $$ $$ \text{Denominator} = 384 \times 2400 = 921{,}600 $$ **Step 4 — Divide:** $$ \dfrac{24 \times 10^{6}}{921{,}600} = 26.0417 $$ **Step 5 — Round to nearest integer (timer registers hold integers):** $$ \text{TH1} = 256 - 26 = 230 = \text{E6H (in hexadecimal)} $$ **Step 6 — Calculate the Actual Baud Rate Achieved:** $$ \text{Actual Baud} = \dfrac{2^{1} \times 12 \times 10^{6}}{32 \times 12 \times (256 - 230)} $$ $$ \text{Actual Baud} = \dfrac{24 \times 10^{6}}{384 \times 26} = \dfrac{24 \times 10^{6}}{9984} \approx 2403.85 \text{ baud} $$ **Error Percentage:** $$ \text{Error} = \dfrac{\vert 2403.85 - 2400 \vert}{2400} \times 100\% = 0.16\% $$ This is well within the typical UART tolerance of ±2%, so the system is functional. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Marks Lost Here:** > 1. **Forgetting to truncate TH1** — Students often write TH1 = 229.97 and lose 1 mark. TH1 must be an integer. > 2. **Using $2^{\text{SMOD}}$ as plain "2"** even when SMOD=0 — examiners check this carefully; write $2^0 = 1$. > 3. **Forgetting to compute the actual baud rate** — the question asks for both TH1 **and** the actual baud; missing the second part costs 2 marks. > 4. **Using 12 MHz instead of 11.0592 MHz** without noting the resulting fractional TH1 — with 12 MHz, TH1 is non-integer and you must explicitly state the truncation. --- ### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **SCON = 98H** (bit-addressable), **SBUF = 99H** (dual register), **PCON = 87H** (only SMOD at bit 7 matters for serial). - **SBUF is physically two registers** — writing loads TX, reading accesses RX. Same address 99H. - **TI and RI are NOT auto-cleared** — software MUST reset them. - **REN = 1** is mandatory for reception; without it, the 8051 ignores incoming bytes. - **Mode 0** is synchronous (uses shift clock on TXD); **Modes 1, 2, 3** are asynchronous. - **Modes 1 and 3** use **Timer 1 in Mode 2** (8-bit auto-reload) for baud rate generation. - **Mode 2** has fixed baud = $f_{\text{osc}}/32$ (SMOD=1) or $f_{\text{osc}}/64$ (SMOD=0). - **SMOD = 1** doubles baud rate in Modes 1, 2, 3. - **Crystal 11.0592 MHz** is preferred because $11.0592 \times 10^6 / (384 \times \text{Baud})$ yields **integer TH1** values for standard baud rates (9600, 4800, 2400, 1200). - **TB8/RB8** are the 9th-bit positions used in **Modes 2 and 3** for parity or addressing. - **SM2** enables multiprocessor communication by using the 9th bit as an address flag. - A complete serial transmit routine must: (1) configure Timer 1, (2) load SCON with mode bits, (3) set REN if receiving, (4) write/read SBUF, (5) wait for TI/RI, (6) clear the flag. - The standard KTU initialization snippet: `MOV SCON, #50H` selects **Mode 1, REN enabled, TI/RI cleared**.

topics.title: Working
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT403 - COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE AND MICROCONTROLLER
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Working

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

The **Working of a Microcontroller** refers to the systematic sequence of internal operations performed by the device to fetch, decode, and execute instructions stored in program memory, while coordinating data transfers between the **Central Processing Unit (CPU)**, **memory units**, and **Input/Output (I/O) ports** under the supervision of a **Timing and Control Unit (TCU)**.

In the context of the KTU 2024 syllabus (PCMRT403, Module 3), the "Working" specifically covers the operational mechanism of the **8-bit Intel 8051 Microcontroller** — including the oscillator circuit, machine cycle generation, instruction pipelining, program execution, and port I/O operations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3)**
> The working of a microcontroller is a direct application of the Von Neumann / Modified Harvard architecture. The 8051 follows a **Modified Harvard Architecture** where program and data memory are physically separate but accessed through a common bus under timing control.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Microcontroller as a Chef in a Kitchen**
>
> Imagine a microcontroller as a **single chef working alone in a small restaurant kitchen**:
>
> - The **Chef (CPU)** is the brain that makes all decisions.
> - The **Recipe Book (Program Memory / ROM)** contains the sequence of cooking steps (instructions) — it is fixed and read-only during operation.
> - The **Kitchen Counter (Internal RAM)** is the small, fast working space where the chef keeps ingredients (data variables) and intermediate items (accumulators, stack).
> - The **Wall Clock (Oscillator)** ticks continuously, dictating the pace of every action — the chef cannot move faster than the clock.
> - The **Serving Counter (I/O Ports)** is where finished dishes are presented to customers (external devices).
> - The **Order Bell (Interrupt System)** can interrupt the chef mid-recipe to handle an urgent request.
>
> Every clock tick, the chef **reads** the next step, **understands** it, and **executes** it — this is the essence of the Fetch-Decode-Execute cycle that defines the working of a microcontroller.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (Bolded Constants & Metrics)

- **Crystal Frequency ($f_{osc}$):** Typically **11.0592 MHz** (chosen because it is an exact multiple of standard baud rates like 9600).
- **Oscillator Period ($T_{osc}$):** $1/f_{osc}$ = approximately **90.42 ns** for 11.0592 MHz.
- **Machine Cycle:** Equal to **12 oscillator periods** (a fixed architectural constant for the 8051).
- **Instruction Cycle:** Total time to execute a single instruction (1 to 4 machine cycles for the 8051).
- **T-State:** One subdivision of the machine cycle = **one oscillator period**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The 8051 divides its oscillator frequency by a factor of **12** internally to generate the machine cycle clock — this is a fundamental architectural decision and is **frequently asked** in KTU examinations.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three-Phase Working Cycle

The working of the 8051 microcontroller revolves around a continuous loop of three fundamental phases. Every instruction the CPU executes passes through this cycle:

#### Phase 1: FETCH
1. The **Program Counter (PC)** holds the address of the next instruction to be executed.
2. The address is placed on the **16-bit address bus**.
3. The **Program Address Register** latches the address.
4. The **Timing and Control Unit** activates the $\overline{PSEN}$ (Program Store Enable) signal.
5. The instruction byte is read from **Program Memory (ROM)** via the 8-bit data bus.
6. The instruction is loaded into the **Instruction Register (IR)**.

#### Phase 2: DECODE
1. The instruction in the IR is sent to the **Instruction Decoder**.
2. The decoder interprets the **opcode** portion of the instruction.
3. The decoded control signals are routed to the **Timing and Control Unit**.
4. Any required operand addresses (for direct, register, or immediate addressing) are determined.

#### Phase 3: EXECUTE
1. The **Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)** performs the required operation (ADD, MOV, ANL, etc.).
2. Results are written back to the **Accumulator (A)** or a specified register.
3. Status flags in the **Program Status Word (PSW)** are updated based on the result.
4. The Program Counter is **incremented** (or modified for branch/jump instructions).
5. The cycle repeats from Phase 1 with the new PC value.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the Modified Harvard Architecture Matters for Working**
> In the 8051, the FETCH phase uses the **$\overline{PSEN}$** signal (Program Store Enable) to access ROM, while data memory accesses use **$\overline{RD}$** and **$\overline{WR}$**. This dual-bus separation allows overlapping of instruction fetch with data execution in some instructions — a key speed advantage over pure Von Neumann designs.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE — Memorize for KTU University Exam**

| Parameter | Formula | Description / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Oscillator Period | $T_{osc} = \dfrac{1}{f_{osc}}$ | Time for 1 crystal oscillation (seconds) |
| Machine Cycle Duration | $T_{MC} = \dfrac{12}{f_{osc}} = 12 \times T_{osc}$ | Time for 1 machine cycle (seconds) |
| Instruction Execution Time | $T_{inst} = \dfrac{12 \times C}{f_{osc}}$ | Where $C$ = number of machine cycles |
| Total Instruction Time (N times) | $T_{total} = N \times \dfrac{12 \times C}{f_{osc}}$ | For loops or repeated instructions |
| Baud Rate (Timer 1 Mode 2) | $Baud = \dfrac{2^{SMOD} \times f_{osc}}{32 \times 12 \times (256 - TH1)}$ | Serial communication baud rate |
| Timer Overflow Rate | $f_{overflow} = \dfrac{f_{osc}}{12 \times (2^n - \text{Preset Value})}$ | $n=13$ for Timer 0/1 Mode 1 |

**Boundary Conditions for the 8051:**
- Minimum $f_{osc}$: **1.2 MHz** (for reliable operation)
- Maximum $f_{osc}$ (8051 standard): **12 MHz**
- Maximum $f_{osc}$ (8051 variant e.g. AT89C51): **24 MHz**
- T-State range: 1 to 4 machine cycles per instruction

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The working principles of a microcontroller form the foundation of:
- **Embedded Systems Design** (washing machines, microwaves, automotive ECUs)
- **IoT Devices** (sensor nodes using 8051-based SoCs like CC2530)
- **Industrial Automation** (PLC controllers, motor drives)
- **Consumer Electronics** (TV remotes, toys, calculators)

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight**
> Understanding the exact instruction cycle timing allows engineers to write **time-critical routines** (e.g., generating precise delays, bit-banging protocols, real-time sampling) without relying on hardware timers. This is a practical skill frequently tested in KTU laboratory examinations.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Instruction Execution Time Formula

The 8051 architecture uses a fixed clock division scheme. We will rigorously derive the relationship between crystal frequency and instruction execution time.

**Step 1: Define the Oscillator Period**

The crystal oscillator generates a square wave at frequency $f_{osc}$. The time for one complete oscillation is:

$$T_{osc} = \frac{1}{f_{osc}}$$

**Step 2: Define the T-State**

A T-State corresponds to exactly **one oscillator period**. This is the smallest unit of time recognized by the CPU.

$$T_{T\text{-}state} = T_{osc} = \frac{1}{f_{osc}}$$

**Step 3: Define the Machine Cycle**

The 8051 CPU divides the oscillator frequency internally by a factor of 12. This means **one machine cycle = 12 oscillator periods = 12 T-states**.

$$T_{MC} = 12 \times T_{osc} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}}$$

**Step 4: Define the Instruction Cycle**

An instruction requires a fixed number of machine cycles (denoted $C$), which depends on the instruction type. Common 8051 instruction cycle counts:
- 1-byte, 1-cycle instructions (e.g., `INC A`, `NOP`): $C = 1$
- 2-byte, 1-cycle instructions (e.g., `MOV A, #data`): $C = 1$
- 1-byte, 2-cycle instructions (e.g., `INC DPTR`): $C = 2$
- 3-byte, 2-cycle instructions (e.g., `MOV direct, #data`): $C = 2$
- 1-byte, 4-cycle instructions (e.g., `MUL AB`, `DIV AB`): $C = 4$

Therefore, the instruction execution time is:

$$T_{inst} = C \times T_{MC} = C \times \frac{12}{f_{osc}}$$

**Step 5: Final Generalized Formula**

$$\boxed{T_{inst} = \frac{12 \times C}{f_{osc}}}$$

This is the **canonical formula** that the KTU examiner expects students to write for any timing-related problem.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Standard)

> **Problem:** An 8051 system uses a crystal of frequency 11.0592 MHz. Calculate the time taken to execute the instruction `MUL AB`. Also calculate the time taken if this instruction is placed inside a loop that runs 1000 times.

**Given Data:**
- $f_{osc} = 11.0592 \text{ MHz} = 11.0592 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}$
- Instruction: `MUL AB` → $C = 4$ machine cycles
- Loop count: $N = 1000$

**Step 1: Calculate the Oscillator Period**

$$T_{osc} = \frac{1}{f_{osc}} = \frac{1}{11.0592 \times 10^6} = 0.09042 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 90.42 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 2: Calculate the Machine Cycle Duration**

$$T_{MC} = 12 \times T_{osc} = 12 \times 90.42 \text{ ns} = 1085.04 \text{ ns} \approx 1.085 \text{ \mu s}$$

**Step 3: Calculate Time for One Execution of `MUL AB`**

$$T_{inst} = C \times T_{MC} = 4 \times 1.085 \text{ \mu s} = 4.34 \text{ \mu s}$$

**Step 4: Calculate Total Time for 1000 Iterations**

$$T_{total} = N \times T_{inst} = 1000 \times 4.34 \text{ \mu s} = 4340 \text{ \mu s} = 4.34 \text{ ms}$$

**Final Answer:** $T_{total} = 4.34 \text{ ms}$

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: 8051 Working Components

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mapping of Subsystems to Functional Role during Working**

| Subsystem | Role During Fetch | Role During Decode | Role During Execute |
|---|---|---|---|
| Program Counter (PC) | Supplies instruction address | — | Auto-increments |
| Program Memory (ROM) | Provides instruction bytes | — | — |
| Instruction Register | Latches fetched opcode | Holds opcode for decoder | — |
| Instruction Decoder | — | Decodes opcode, generates control signals | — |
| Timing & Control Unit | Generates $\overline{PSEN}$, ALE | Sequences control signals | Coordinates ALU/Register writes |
| ALU | — | — | Performs arithmetic/logic operations |
| Accumulator (A) | — | — | Source/Destination for most ALU ops |
| PSW (Flags) | — | — | Updated based on ALU result |
| Data Memory (RAM) | — | — | Stores/retrieves operand data |
| I/O Ports (P0–P3) | — | — | Reads inputs / Drives outputs |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Working Flow of 8051 Microcontroller (Fetch-Decode-Execute Cycle)

The following Mermaid flowchart represents the complete operational cycle of the 8051, including interrupt handling and the reset path — this is the **core conceptual diagram** for the topic "Working."

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Power ON Reset] --> B[Initialize PC to 0000h]
    B --> C[Oscillator Starts: f_osc active]
    C --> D[TCU Generates ALE PSEN Signals]
    D --> E[FETCH PHASE: PC out on Address Bus]
    E --> F[Read Instruction Byte from ROM]
    F --> G[Load into Instruction Register]
    G --> H[DECODE PHASE: Instruction Decoder]
    H --> I{Is it a Branch Jump or Call instruction?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Modify PC to Target Address]
    I -- No --> K[Auto Increment PC by 1 or 2 or 3 bytes]
    J --> L[EXECUTE PHASE: ALU or Data Transfer]
    K --> L
    L --> M{Interrupt Request Active?}
    M -- No --> N[Update Flags in PSW]
    M -- Yes --> O[Push PC onto Stack]
    O --> P[Load PC with Interrupt Vector Address]
    P --> Q[Execute ISR Interrupt Service Routine]
    Q --> N
    N --> R[Instruction Complete]
    R --> D
    D --> S[End of Program Reached?]
    S -- No --> D
    S -- Yes --> T[STOP: Halt or Loop Back to PC 0000h]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture During Instruction Execution

This diagram illustrates the **data path** through the 8051 CPU during a typical 1-byte, 1-cycle instruction (e.g., `INC A`).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ROM["Program Memory ROM"] -- PSEN Signal --> IR["Instruction Register"]
    IR --> DEC["Instruction Decoder"]
    DEC --> TCU["Timing and Control Unit"]
    TCU -->|Control Signals| ALU["Arithmetic Logic Unit ALU"]
    ACC["Accumulator A"] <--> ALU
    ALU -->|Flag Updates| PSW["Program Status Word PSW"]
    PC["Program Counter PC"] -->|Address| ROM
    TCU -->|Increment| PC
    RAM["Internal Data RAM 128 Bytes"] <-->|RD WR Signals| ACC
    PORTS["I/O Ports P0 P1 P2 P3"] <-->|Move Operations| ACC
    SP["Stack Pointer SP"] --> RAM
```

### 4.3 Timing Diagram Representation (Schematic)

The 8051 working timing is conventionally represented with two key signals:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X["Oscillator Signal XTAL2: 11.0592 MHz Square Wave"]
    Y["ALE Signal: Latches Low Byte of Address"]
    Z["PSEN Signal: Active LOW during Code Fetch"]
    W["Machine Cycle Boundary: Every 12 XTAL2 Pulses"]

    X --> Y
    X --> Z
    X --> W
    Y --> Z
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Timing Relationships**
> - **ALE (Address Latch Enable)** goes HIGH during the first 6 T-states and LOW during the next 6 T-states of a machine cycle.
> - **$\overline{PSEN}$** goes LOW only during the FETCH portion of the cycle to enable program memory output.
> - One complete machine cycle spans exactly **12 oscillator periods** (S1P1 through S6P2).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]:** *Explain the role of the Program Counter and Instruction Register in the working of the 8051 microcontroller.*

**Model Answer:**

The **Program Counter (PC)** is a 16-bit register that holds the address of the next instruction to be fetched from program memory. It is automatically incremented after each instruction fetch, and modified explicitly during branch, call, or interrupt operations. The PC determines the **flow of program execution**.

The **Instruction Register (IR)** is an 8-bit register that temporarily stores the opcode byte fetched from program memory. The IR holds the instruction only during the decode phase, after which its content is transferred to the **Instruction Decoder** for interpretation. The IR acts as the **buffer between memory and the decoder**.

Together, PC and IR enable the **Fetch-Decode-Execute cycle** that defines the working of the 8051. **(3 marks)**

---

**Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]:** *What is a machine cycle? How is it related to the crystal oscillator frequency in the 8051?*

**Model Answer:**

A **machine cycle** is the basic unit of time for any CPU operation in the 8051. It consists of **12 oscillator periods (T-states)** divided into 6 states (S1–S6), each with two phases (P1, P2).

The relationship between machine cycle duration and crystal frequency is:

$$T_{MC} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}}$$

For example, with $f_{osc} = 12$ MHz, $T_{MC} = 1$ μs. The 8051's internal clock divider divides the oscillator frequency by 12, ensuring that the CPU operates at a stable, predictable rate regardless of the crystal used. **(3 marks)**

---

### Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]:

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the Fetch-Decode-Execute cycle of the 8051 microcontroller with a suitable diagram. List the signals activated during each phase.

**Model Solution:**

The 8051 executes instructions through a sequential three-phase cycle:

**1. Fetch Phase (T-states 1–12 of first machine cycle):**
- The **Program Counter (PC)** places the 16-bit address on the address bus.
- The **ALE** signal goes HIGH to latch the low-order address byte (P0 is multiplexed with data).
- The **$\overline{PSEN}$** signal goes LOW to enable program memory (ROM).
- The instruction byte(s) are read from ROM via the data bus.
- The opcode is loaded into the **Instruction Register**.
- For multi-byte instructions, subsequent bytes (operands) are read in additional machine cycles.

**2. Decode Phase (Overlaps with subsequent execution):**
- The opcode in the IR is sent to the **Instruction Decoder**.
- The decoder identifies the operation and addressing mode.
- The **Timing and Control Unit** generates the required sequence of control signals.

**3. Execute Phase:**
- The **ALU** performs arithmetic/logic operations (e.g., ADD, ANL, OR) on data sourced from the Accumulator, registers, or memory.
- For data transfer instructions (MOV, XCH), the appropriate internal buses are activated.
- The **PSW flags** (Carry, Auxiliary Carry, Overflow, Parity) are updated.
- The PC is incremented (or modified for jumps).

**Signal Summary Table:**

| Phase | Active Signals |
|---|---|
| Fetch | ALE, $\overline{PSEN}$ |
| Execute (Data Read) | $\overline{RD}$ |
| Execute (Data Write) | $\overline{WR}$ |
| Interrupt | $\overline{INT0}$, $\overline{INT1}$ |

The cycle repeats continuously until a RESET or HALT condition occurs. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** An 8051 system uses an 11.0592 MHz crystal. Calculate (i) the oscillator period, (ii) the machine cycle duration, (iii) the time to execute the instruction `DIV AB`, and (iv) the time to execute a delay loop of 500 iterations of `NOP`.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $f_{osc} = 11.0592 \text{ MHz} = 11.0592 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}$

**Instruction Data:** `DIV AB` → $C = 4$ machine cycles; `NOP` → $C = 1$ machine cycle.

**(i) Oscillator Period:**

$$T_{osc} = \frac{1}{f_{osc}} = \frac{1}{11.0592 \times 10^6} = 90.42 \text{ ns}$$

**(ii) Machine Cycle Duration:**

$$T_{MC} = 12 \times T_{osc} = 12 \times 90.42 \text{ ns} = 1085.04 \text{ ns} = 1.085 \text{ \mu s}$$

**(iii) Time to Execute `DIV AB`:**

$$T_{DIV} = C \times T_{MC} = 4 \times 1.085 \text{ \mu s} = 4.34 \text{ \mu s}$$

**(iv) Time to Execute 500 `NOP` Instructions:**

$$T_{loop} = N \times C \times T_{MC} = 500 \times 1 \times 1.085 \text{ \mu s} = 542.5 \text{ \mu s} \approx 0.543 \text{ ms}$$

**Final Results:**
- $T_{osc} = 90.42$ ns
- $T_{MC} = 1.085$ μs
- $T_{DIV} = 4.34$ μs
- $T_{loop} = 542.5$ μs **(7 marks)**

---

#### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]:

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the working of the oscillator and clock circuit of the 8051 microcontroller. Explain how the machine cycle is derived from the crystal frequency.

**Model Solution:**

The 8051 contains an **on-chip oscillator** that requires only an external **quartz crystal** connected between pins **XTAL1 (Pin 19)** and **XTAL2 (Pin 18)**, along with two **30 pF ceramic capacitors** connected to ground. This forms a **Pierce oscillator circuit**.

**Working of the Oscillator:**
1. The on-chip inverter and feedback resistor, together with the external crystal, form a resonant circuit.
2. The crystal vibrates at its fundamental frequency $f_{osc}$ (typically 11.0592 MHz or 12 MHz).
3. The capacitors provide the necessary phase shift and frequency stability.
4. The oscillator output drives the internal clock generator.

**Derivation of Machine Cycle:**
The internal clock generator divides the oscillator frequency by 12:

$$f_{internal} = \frac{f_{osc}}{12}$$

Therefore, the machine cycle period is:

$$T_{MC} = \frac{1}{f_{internal}} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}}$$

**Why Divide by 12?**
- Allows the internal CPU to operate at a stable, lower frequency.
- Provides time for memory access (ROM/RAM have finite access times).
- Simplifies timing calculations for software delays.
- Standard 8051 instructions execute in 1 to 4 machine cycles.

**Example:** For $f_{osc} = 12$ MHz, $T_{MC} = 1$ μs, and one machine cycle takes exactly 1 microsecond. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the internal RAM organization of the 8051 and describe how it is accessed during program execution.

**Model Solution:**

The 8051 has **128 bytes of internal RAM** (00H to 7FH), divided into three distinct regions:

**1. Register Banks (00H–1FH):** Four banks (Bank 0–3), each containing 8 registers (R0–R7). Only one bank is active at a time, selected by bits RS0 and RS1 in the PSW.

**2. Bit-Addressable Area (20H–2FH):** 16 bytes (128 bits) that can be individually addressed using instructions like `SETB` and `CLR`.

**3. General Purpose RAM (30H–7FH):** 80 bytes of scratchpad memory for user variables and the stack.

**Access Mechanism During Working:**

- **Direct Addressing:** Instructions specify the exact RAM address (e.g., `MOV A, 30H`).
- **Indirect Addressing:** The `@R0` or `@R1` registers hold the address (e.g., `MOV A, @R0`).
- **Stack Operations:** The **Stack Pointer (SP)** is initialized to 07H, pointing to Register Bank 1. The stack grows upward in memory during PUSH/CALL operations.
- **Bit Operations:** Only addresses 20H–2FH support individual bit manipulation (e.g., `SETB 20H.0`).

**High Internal RAM (80H–FFH):** This region contains the **Special Function Registers (SFRs)** — including the Accumulator (E0H), B register (F0H), PSW (D0H), SP (81H), and Port latches — which are accessed during program execution to control all peripherals and the CPU itself.

**Address Decoding:** The 8051's internal address decoder uses the **lower 7 bits** of the address bus to access internal RAM, and the **upper bits** to differentiate between RAM, SFRs, and external memory. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
>
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the divisor of 12:** The most common error is writing $T_{inst} = C/f_{osc}$ instead of $T_{inst} = 12C/f_{osc}$. This single mistake costs 2–3 marks immediately.
>
> 2. **Confusing Machine Cycle and Instruction Cycle:** A machine cycle is always 12 T-states. An instruction cycle is $C$ machine cycles (1 to 4 for the 8051). These are **not interchangeable**.
>
> 3. **Wrong instruction cycle count for `MUL AB` and `DIV AB`:** Both require **4 machine cycles** each — students often mistakenly write 2.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to convert MHz to Hz:** Always write $11.0592 \text{ MHz} = 11.0592 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}$ before substitution.
>
> 5. **Not stating units in the final answer:** KTU examiners **strictly deduct** ½ mark for missing units like μs, ms, or ns.
>
> 6. **Confusing `$\overline{PSEN}$` with `$\overline{EA}$`:** $\overline{PSEN}$ is Program Store Enable (ROM access), while $\overline{EA}$ is External Access (selects internal vs. external ROM). Both are active-LOW signals.
>
> 7. **In Block Diagrams:** Students often draw arrows without labels — KTU valuation keys require **labeled data flow** with **signal names**.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Final High-Density Summary for Last-Minute Revision**

- **Working of 8051 = Continuous Fetch → Decode → Execute cycle** managed by the **Timing and Control Unit**.
- The **oscillator** uses a **crystal** between **XTAL1 and XTAL2** with **two 30 pF capacitors** to ground.
- The internal clock is **divided by 12** to generate the **machine cycle** — this is non-negotiable.
- **T-State = $1/f_{osc}$** (one crystal oscillation).
- **Machine Cycle = 12 T-states = $12/f_{osc}$ seconds.**
- **Instruction Cycle Time = $12C/f_{osc}$**, where $C \in \{1, 2, 4\}$ for the 8051.
- **Standard Crystal = 11.0592 MHz** (exact multiple of 9600 baud rate).
- For $f_{osc} = 11.0592$ MHz, $T_{MC} \approx 1.085$ μs — memorize this constant.
- **`MUL AB` and `DIV AB` = 4 machine cycles** each.
- **`NOP` = 1 byte, 1 machine cycle** — the smallest possible instruction.
- **PSW flags** (CY, AC, F0, RS1, RS0, OV, -, P) are updated during the **execute phase**.
- **Stack Pointer** initializes to **07H** and grows upward with PUSH/CALL.
- **Register Banks** (00H–1FH): 4 banks × 8 registers each, selected by PSW bits RS0, RS1.
- **Bit-addressable RAM** is only **20H–2FH** (16 bytes = 128 bits).
- **SFRs** occupy the high internal RAM region **80H–FFH**.
- The 8051 uses **Modified Harvard Architecture**: separate program and data memory spaces, common address/data bus under timing control.
- Key control signals: **ALE** (latch address), **$\overline{PSEN}$** (enable ROM), **$\overline{EA}$** (internal/external ROM select), **$\overline{RD}$** and **$\overline{WR}$** (data memory).
- **Interrupts** halt the main cycle, push PC onto stack, jump to ISR vector, execute, then **RETI** returns to the main cycle.
- The **Port Latches** (P0–P3 SFRs at 80H, 90H, A0H, B0H) act as the "bridge" between CPU and external I/O during working.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Programming the 8051 to Transfer Data Serially
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT403 - COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE AND MICROCONTROLLER
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Programming the 8051 to Transfer Data Serially

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **8051 Serial Communication Interface (SCI)** is an on-chip **UART (Universal Asynchronous Receiver-Transmitter)** peripheral that enables the microcontroller to exchange data with external devices one bit at a time over a single transmission line. Serial communication reduces the pin count requirement compared to parallel transfer and is essential for interfacing with PCs, sensors, and other microcontrollers via standards like **RS-232**.

The serial port in the 8051 is a **full-duplex** port, meaning it can transmit and receive simultaneously. It is controlled by three special function registers: **SBUF, SCON, and PCON**, and typically uses **Timer 1** in its auto-reload (Mode 2) configuration to generate the baud rate clock.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Favourite:** The 8051's serial port has **FOUR operating modes (Modes 0, 1, 2, and 3)**, each with distinct frame formats and baud-rate sources. Mode 1 (8-bit UART) is the most commonly tested mode in the KTU 2024 Scheme.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Two-Way Radio" Model

Imagine two walkie-talkies used by security guards at opposite ends of a large building:

- **Walkie-Talkie Set = SBUF (Serial Buffer Register)** — The device that holds the voice message **before** it is sent out through the antenna and **after** it is received.
- **Channel Selector + Push-to-Talk Button = SCON (Serial Control Register)** — Determines whether you are talking or listening, the channel (mode), and shows whether transmission is complete.
- **Volume/Battery Control = PCON (Power Control Register)** — Specifically, the **SMOD bit** acts as a "booster" that can **double the speaking speed (baud rate)**.
- **Clock Generator (the metronome inside the radio) = Timer 1** — The faster the metronome ticks, the faster you can speak (baud rate).
- **"Over" indicator LED = TI (Transmit Interrupt) flag** — Lights up when your message has been fully transmitted.
- **"Message received" bell = RI (Receive Interrupt) flag** — Rings when an incoming message has been fully received.

Just as two guards must agree on the **same talking speed (baud rate)** and the **same protocol (mode)** to understand each other, the 8051 and the external device must share identical serial communication parameters.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **SBUF address:** **0x99** (physically two separate 8-bit registers — one for writing transmit data, one for reading received data)
- **SCON address:** **0x98** (bit-addressable)
- **PCON address:** **0x87** (NOT bit-addressable)
- **Standard Crystal Frequencies:** **11.0592 MHz** (most common for UART), **12 MHz**, **22.1184 MHz**
- **Standard Baud Rates:** **1200, 2400, 4800, 9600, 19200, 57600, 115200** bps
- **The Magic Number 11.0592:** This frequency is chosen because it is exactly divisible by standard baud rates after the 8051's internal division, yielding **zero baud-rate error**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 11.0592 MHz and not 12 MHz?** With a 12 MHz crystal, the calculated TH1 value is rarely an integer, causing a small but cumulative error in the baud rate. With 11.0592 MHz, the division yields **exact integer reload values** (e.g., 0xFD = 253 for 9600 baud), making it the de-facto industry standard for 8051-based serial communication boards.

## 1.4 SBUF — The Serial Buffer Register

```
+-----------------------+
|   SBUF (TX)  0x99 W   |  ← Writing to 0x99 loads the Transmit buffer
+-----------------------+
|   SBUF (RX)  0x99 R   |  ← Reading from 0x99 reads the Receive buffer
+-----------------------+
```

Although both the transmit and receive buffers share the **same SFR address (0x99)**, they are **physically two distinct registers**. The hardware automatically routes the data based on whether the instruction is a `MOV SBUF, A` (write) or `MOV A, SBUF` (read).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The SCON Register (Serial Control Register, 0x98)

SCON is an **8-bit, bit-addressable** register that configures the serial port's mode and holds the interrupt flags.

| Bit | Name | Function |
|:---:|:----:|:---------|
| **D7** | **SM0** | Serial Mode selector bit 0 |
| **D6** | **SM1** | Serial Mode selector bit 1 |
| **D5** | **SM2** | Multiprocessor Communication Enable (used in Modes 2 & 3) |
| **D4** | **REN** | Receiver Enable — must be set to **1** to allow reception |
| **D3** | **TB8** | 9th transmitted bit (used in Modes 2 & 3) |
| **D2** | **RB8** | 9th received bit (used in Modes 2 & 3) |
| **D1** | **TI** | **Transmit Interrupt flag** — set by hardware at end of transmission; **must be cleared by software** |
| **D0** | **RI** | **Receive Interrupt flag** — set by hardware at end of reception; **must be cleared by software** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** The `TI` and `RI` flags are **set by hardware** but **cleared by software**. Forgetting to clear them causes the program to think a new transmission/reception has occurred, leading to infinite loops. This is a **favourite trick question** in KTU exams.

## 2.2 The PCON Register (Power Control, 0x87)

Only **bit 7 (SMOD)** is relevant to serial communication.

| SMOD Value | Effect on Baud Rate |
|:----------:|:--------------------|
| **0** | Standard baud rate (no doubling) |
| **1** | **Baud rate is doubled** |

> [!NOTE]
> PCON is **not bit-addressable**. To set SMOD: `MOV PCON, #80H` or `ORL PCON, #80H`.

## 2.3 Serial Modes of the 8051

| Mode | SM0 | SM1 | Description | Frame Size | Baud Rate Source |
|:----:|:---:|:---:|:------------|:----------:|:-----------------|
| **Mode 0** | 0 | 0 | 8-bit Shift Register (synchronous) | 8 bits | **f_osc / 12** (fixed) |
| **Mode 1** | 0 | 1 | 8-bit UART (asynchronous) | 10 bits (1 start, 8 data, 1 stop) | **Timer 1 overflow** (variable) |
| **Mode 2** | 1 | 0 | 9-bit UART (asynchronous) | 11 bits | **f_osc / 32** or **f_osc / 64** (fixed, SMOD dependent) |
| **Mode 3** | 1 | 1 | 9-bit UART (asynchronous) | 11 bits | **Timer 1 overflow** (variable) |

**Mode 0** is rarely used in modern systems. **Modes 1 and 3** use Timer 1 for variable baud rates, while **Mode 2** has a fixed baud rate. Mode 3 is essentially Mode 2 with variable baud rate.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Use |
|:-:|:--------|:------------|:------------|
| 1 | $\text{Baud Rate} = \dfrac{2^{\text{SMOD}}}{32} \times \dfrac{f_{\text{osc}}}{12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}$ | Mode 1 & 3 baud rate formula | Calculate TH1 for a desired baud rate |
| 2 | $\text{TH1} = 256 - \dfrac{f_{\text{osc}}}{384 \times \text{Baud Rate}}$ (for SMOD = 0) | Simplified TH1 equation (SMOD=0) | Most common case |
| 3 | $\text{TH1} = 256 - \dfrac{f_{\text{osc}}}{192 \times \text{Baud Rate}}$ (for SMOD = 1) | Simplified TH1 equation (SMOD=1) | Doubled baud rate |
| 4 | $\text{Baud Rate (Mode 0)} = \dfrac{f_{\text{osc}}}{12}$ | Fixed baud rate in Mode 0 | Shift register mode |
| 5 | $\text{Baud Rate (Mode 2)} = \dfrac{2^{\text{SMOD}} \times f_{\text{osc}}}{64}$ | Fixed baud rate in Mode 2 | 9-bit fixed mode |
| 6 | $\text{Timer 1 Overflow Rate} = \dfrac{f_{\text{osc}}}{12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}$ | Overflow rate of Timer 1 in Mode 2 | Derivation of #1 |
| 7 | $\text{Error \%} = \dfrac{\text{Calculated Baud} - \text{Desired Baud}}{\text{Desired Baud}} \times 100$ | Baud rate percentage error | Verify 8051 UART accuracy |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial Automation:** PLCs and HMIs communicate with 8051 boards via RS-485 (multi-drop) using Mode 1 UART.
- **GPS Modules:** NEO-6M GPS modules output NMEA sentences via UART at 9600 baud.
- **GSM/IoT Modules:** SIM800/SIM900 modules use AT commands over UART.
- **Bluetooth HC-05/HC-06:** Default UART communication at 9600 baud.
- **Arduino-RFID Interfacing:** RFID readers (RC522, EM-18) communicate via UART.
- **PC-MCU Communication:** CH340/CP2102 USB-to-UART bridges allow PC ↔ 8051 communication.

## 2.6 Timer 1 Configuration for Baud Rate Generation

Timer 1 **must** be configured in **Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload)** to generate the baud rate clock. The auto-reload feature is essential because the timer continuously overflows at a constant rate without software intervention.

```
TMOD register: 0010 0000 = 0x20   (Timer 1, Mode 2, software control)
TCON bit TR1 = 1                  (Start Timer 1)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Timer 1 in Mode 2?** In Mode 2, when the timer overflows from 0xFF to 0x00, the value in **TH1 is automatically reloaded into TL1**, ensuring a glitch-free, continuous baud-rate clock.

## 2.7 Frame Format: 10 Bits in Mode 1

The Mode 1 UART frame consists of:
1. **1 Start bit** (always 0)
2. **8 Data bits** (LSB first, exactly the byte written to SBUF)
3. **1 Stop bit** (always 1)

**Total: 10 bits per frame.** This means the effective transmission time for 1 byte = 10 / Baud Rate.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Baud Rate Formula (Mode 1)

### Step 1: Identify the Clock Source
The 8051's oscillator drives an internal machine cycle, where **1 machine cycle = 12 oscillator periods**.

$$f_{\text{machine cycle}} = \frac{f_{\text{osc}}}{12}$$

### Step 2: Timer 1 in Mode 2
Timer 1 in Mode 2 is an 8-bit up-counter that counts from the value in **TL1** up to **0xFF**, then overflows to **0x00**. The number of timer ticks per overflow is:

$$N_{\text{ticks}} = 256 - \text{TH1}$$

### Step 3: Timer 1 Overflow Frequency
The overflow frequency (how many times per second the timer wraps) is:

$$f_{\text{overflow}} = \frac{f_{\text{osc}}}{12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}$$

*Explanation:* Each overflow takes $(256 - \text{TH1})$ machine cycles. One machine cycle takes $12 / f_{\text{osc}}$ seconds. So the time per overflow is $(256-\text{TH1}) \times 12 / f_{\text{osc}}$, and the frequency is its reciprocal.

### Step 4: UART Division by 32
The 8051 UART internally divides the timer overflow frequency by **32** to generate the bit-clock (each UART bit is 1/32 of the overflow period, but due to the sampling logic, 16× oversampling is used and then divided by 2).

$$\text{Baud Rate} = \frac{f_{\text{overflow}}}{32} = \frac{f_{\text{osc}}}{32 \times 12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}$$

### Step 5: Apply the SMOD Doubling Effect
When SMOD = 1, the baud rate is **doubled** (multiply by 2 = multiply by $2^{\text{SMOD}}$):

$$\boxed{\text{Baud Rate} = \frac{2^{\text{SMOD}} \times f_{\text{osc}}}{384 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}}$$

### Step 6: Rearrange to Solve for TH1
Cross-multiplying and isolating TH1 yields the practical formula:

$$\text{TH1} = 256 - \frac{2^{\text{SMOD}} \times f_{\text{osc}}}{384 \times \text{Baud Rate}}$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — 9600 Baud, 11.0592 MHz, SMOD=0

**Given:**
- $f_{\text{osc}} = 11.0592 \text{ MHz} = 11059200 \text{ Hz}$
- Desired Baud Rate = **9600 bps**
- SMOD = 0 (default)

**Apply the formula:**

$$\text{TH1} = 256 - \frac{2^{0} \times 11059200}{384 \times 9600}$$

$$\text{TH1} = 256 - \frac{11059200}{3686400}$$

$$\text{TH1} = 256 - 3 = 253$$

**Convert to hexadecimal:**

$$253_{10} = 0\text{xFD}$$

**Verification — Calculate the actual baud rate:**

$$\text{Baud}_{\text{actual}} = \frac{2^0 \times 11059200}{384 \times (256 - 253)} = \frac{11059200}{384 \times 3} = \frac{11059200}{1152} = 9600 \text{ bps} \checkmark$$

**Error percentage:**

$$\text{Error} = \frac{9600 - 9600}{9600} \times 100\% = 0\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Zero error** is the reason 11.0592 MHz crystals are standard. With a 12 MHz crystal at 9600 baud: TH1 = 256 - (12000000 / 3686400) ≈ 256 - 3.255 ≈ 252.74, which must be rounded to 253, producing a small error of **+0.16%** (acceptable for most applications but not for tight timing).

## 3.3 Worked Example — 2400 Baud, 11.0592 MHz, SMOD=1 (Doubled)

**Given:** $f_{\text{osc}} = 11059200 \text{ Hz}$, Baud = 2400, SMOD = 1

$$\text{TH1} = 256 - \frac{2^{1} \times 11059200}{384 \times 2400}$$

$$\text{TH1} = 256 - \frac{22118400}{921600} = 256 - 24 = 232 = 0\text{xE8}$$

**Verification:**

$$\text{Baud}_{\text{actual}} = \frac{2 \times 11059200}{384 \times 24} = \frac{22118400}{9216} = 2400 \text{ bps} \checkmark$$

## 3.4 Common Baud Rate Reload Values (11.0592 MHz Crystal)

This is the **most frequently asked table** in KTU exams:

| Baud Rate | SMOD | TH1 (Decimal) | TH1 (Hex) | Error % |
|:---------:|:----:|:-------------:|:---------:|:-------:|
| **1200** | 0 | 230 | 0xE6 | 0 |
| **2400** | 0 | 244 | 0xF4 | 0 |
| **4800** | 0 | 250 | 0xFA | 0 |
| **9600** | 0 | 253 | 0xFD | 0 |
| **19200** | 0 | 254.5* | 0xFE | 0 |
| **9600** | 1 | 250 | 0xFA | 0 |

*Note: For 19200 baud with SMOD=0, TH1 = 254.5 — some compilers round to 254 or 255, causing ±0.16% error.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Common Student Confusions

| Confusion Area | Correct Understanding |
|:---------------|:----------------------|
| SBUF has one register | SBUF has **two** physical registers at address 0x99 |
| PCON is bit-addressable | **NO** — only SCON and TCON are bit-addressable |
| TI is cleared by hardware | **NO** — TI and RI are cleared by **software** |
| SMOD is in SCON | **NO** — SMOD is bit 7 of **PCON** |
| Mode 1 frame has 8 bits | **NO** — Frame has **10 bits** (1 start + 8 data + 1 stop) |
| Timer 1 must be in Mode 1 | **NO** — Timer 1 must be in **Mode 2** (8-bit auto-reload) |
| Serial interrupt uses TF1 | **NO** — Serial interrupt uses **TI/RI** flags; TF1 is for Timer 1 overflow |

## 3.6 Mathematical Derivation of Total Transmission Time

For transmitting **N bytes** at baud rate **B** in Mode 1 (10 bits/frame):

$$T_{\text{total}} = \frac{N \times 10}{B} \text{ seconds}$$

**Example:** Transmit 100 characters at 9600 baud:

$$T = \frac{100 \times 10}{9600} = 0.1042 \text{ seconds} \approx 104.2 \text{ ms}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 8051 Serial Port Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["CPU Core 8051"] --> B["SBUF TX Buffer 0x99 W"]
    A --> C["SBUF RX Buffer 0x99 R"]
    B --> D["Transmit Shift Register"]
    D --> E["TXD Pin P3.1"]
    F["RXD Pin P3.0"] --> G["Receive Shift Register"]
    G --> C
    H["SCON Register 0x98"] --> D
    H --> G
    I["PCON Bit 7 SMOD"] --> J["Baud Rate Generator"]
    K["Timer 1 Mode 2 Auto Reload"] --> J
    J --> D
    J --> G
    H --> L["TI Flag Set by Hardware"]
    H --> M["RI Flag Set by Hardware"]
    L --> N["Software Must Clear TI"]
    M --> O["Software Must Clear RI"]
```

## 4.2 UART Frame Format — Mode 1 (10 Bits)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Idle State Logic 1"] --> B["Start Bit 0"]
    B --> C["D0 LSB"]
    C --> D["D1"]
    D --> E["D2"]
    E --> F["D3"]
    F --> G["D4"]
    G --> H["D5"]
    H --> I["D6"]
    I --> J["D7 MSB"]
    J --> K["Stop Bit 1"]
    K --> L["Idle Logic 1"]
```

> **Visual Reading:** Each block represents **1 bit period = 1 / Baud Rate**. For 9600 baud, each bit lasts **1/9600 ≈ 104.17 µs**, and the entire 10-bit frame lasts **1.0417 ms**.

## 4.3 Programming Flowchart — Serial Data Transmission

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Start Program"] --> B["Configure Timer 1 in Mode 2"]
    B --> C["Load TH1 for Desired Baud Rate"]
    C --> D["Start Timer 1 Set TR1 1"]
    D --> E["Configure SCON for Mode 1"]
    E --> F["Set SM0 0 SM1 1 REN 0 or 1"]
    F --> G["Load Character into SBUF"]
    G --> H["Wait for TI 1 by Polling"]
    H --> I["Clear TI by Software"]
    I --> J["Transmit Next Character"]
    J --> G
```

## 4.4 Programming Flowchart — Serial Data Reception

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Start Program"] --> B["Configure Timer 1 in Mode 2"]
    B --> C["Load TH1 for Desired Baud Rate"]
    C --> D["Start Timer 1 Set TR1 1"]
    D --> E["Configure SCON Mode 1 REN 1"]
    E --> F["Wait for RI 1 by Polling"]
    F --> G["Read Received Data from SBUF"]
    G --> H["Clear RI by Software"]
    H --> I["Process Received Data"]
    I --> F
```

## 4.5 Complete 8051 Serial Communication Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph "8051 Microcontroller"
        CPU["ALU and Registers"]
        TXBUF["SBUF Transmit 0x99"]
        RXBUF["SBUF Receive 0x99"]
        SCON["SCON Control 0x98"]
        PCON["PCON SMOD Bit"]
        T1["Timer 1 Mode 2"]
        TXDPin["Pin P3.1 TXD"]
        RXDPin["Pin P3.0 RXD"]
    end
    subgraph "External World"
        PC["PC via USB to UART CH340"]
        GSM["GSM Module SIM800"]
        BT["Bluetooth HC-05"]
    end
    TXBUF --> TXDPin
    RXDPin --> RXBUF
    SCON --> TXBUF
    SCON --> RXBUF
    T1 --> SCON
    PCON --> T1
    TXDPin --> PC
    PC --> RXDPin
    TXDPin --> GSM
    GSM --> RXDPin
    TXDPin --> BT
    BT --> RXDPin
```

## 4.6 Mode Selection Truth Table

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["SCON Bits SM0 and SM1"] --> B{"SM0 SM1"}
    B --> C["0 0 Mode 0 Shift Register fosc 12"]
    B --> D["0 1 Mode 1 8 bit UART Timer 1"]
    B --> E["1 0 Mode 2 9 bit UART fosc 32 or 64"]
    B --> F["1 1 Mode 3 9 bit UART Timer 1"]
```

## 4.7 Step-by-Step Transmission Process (State Diagram)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["State 1 IDLE TXD HIGH"] --> B["State 2 CPU writes SBUF"]
    B --> C["State 3 Start bit 0 sent"]
    C --> D["State 4 Data bits D0 to D7 sent LSB first"]
    D --> E["State 5 Stop bit 1 sent"]
    E --> F["State 6 TI flag set hardware"]
    F --> G["State 7 Software clears TI"]
    G --> A
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
**Explain the function of the SBUF register in the 8051. Why is its address the same for both transmit and receive operations?**

**Model Answer:**

The **SBUF (Serial Buffer Register)** at address **0x99** is used to hold serial data before transmission and after reception. It is **physically two separate 8-bit registers** — one for the transmitter and one for the receiver — but both share the same SFR address (0x99) to simplify the programming model.

- **Writing** to SBUF (e.g., `MOV SBUF, A`) loads data into the **transmit buffer**, which is then shifted out serially through **TXD (P3.1)**.
- **Reading** from SBUF (e.g., `MOV A, SBUF`) retrieves data from the **receive buffer**, which contains the last byte received via **RXD (P3.0)**.

The hardware automatically distinguishes between read and write operations to route the data to the correct physical register. This dual-register single-address design saves SFR address space and makes serial programming intuitive.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**
**Differentiate between the TI and RI flags of the 8051 SCON register. Who sets and clears them?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | TI (Transmit Interrupt) | RI (Receive Interrupt) |
|:-------|:------------------------|:------------------------|
| **Bit Position in SCON** | D1 | D0 |
| **Set by** | Hardware — at the end of the 8th bit of transmission (start of stop bit) | Hardware — at the end of the 8th bit of reception (middle of stop bit) |
| **Cleared by** | **Software** (mandatory) | **Software** (mandatory) |
| **Indicates** | Transmit buffer SBUF is empty and ready for next byte | Receive buffer SBUF contains a newly received byte |
| **Triggers Serial ISR** | Yes, if ES bit in IE is set | Yes, if ES bit in IE is set |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write that TI/RI are cleared by hardware — this is **WRONG** and costs marks. Both flags are **set by hardware but cleared by software**. Forgetting to clear them causes the program to enter an infinite loop in polled mode or re-trigger the ISR.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Serial Data Transmission

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model — 14 Marks]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] **Explain the role of Timer 1 and the SMOD bit in generating the baud rate for the 8051's serial port in Mode 1. Derive the formula for TH1 when the crystal frequency is 11.0592 MHz and the desired baud rate is 9600 bps with SMOD = 0.**

**(b)** [7 Marks] **Write an 8051 assembly language program to transmit the string "KTU" continuously at 9600 baud, 8-bit data, 1 stop bit (Mode 1) using an 11.0592 MHz crystal.**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Role of Timer 1:**
Timer 1 operates in **Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload)** to generate a precise, continuous overflow clock. Each overflow produces a pulse that drives the UART's internal baud-rate generator. The auto-reload feature (TH1 → TL1) ensures a stable, glitch-free clock.

**Role of SMOD (PCON.7):**
SMOD is the **baud-rate doubler bit**. When SMOD = 1, the baud rate is **doubled**; when SMOD = 0, the standard baud rate is used. SMOD resides in the **PCON register (0x87)**, which is **not bit-addressable**, so it must be accessed as a full byte.

**Derivation:**

The general Mode 1 baud-rate formula is:

$$\text{Baud Rate} = \frac{2^{\text{SMOD}} \times f_{\text{osc}}}{384 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}$$

Substituting $f_{\text{osc}} = 11059200 \text{ Hz}$, Baud = 9600, SMOD = 0:

$$9600 = \frac{2^0 \times 11059200}{384 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}$$

$$384 \times (256 - \text{TH1}) = \frac{11059200}{9600} = 1152$$

$$256 - \text{TH1} = \frac{1152}{384} = 3$$

$$\text{TH1} = 256 - 3 = 253 = 0\text{xFD}$$

**Conclusion:** TH1 must be loaded with **0xFD** (= 253 decimal).

#### Model Solution to (b):

```assembly
;------------------------------------------------------------
; PROGRAM: Transmit "KTU" continuously at 9600 baud, Mode 1
; CRYSTAL: 11.0592 MHz
; Assumes 'STRING' is in CODE memory
;------------------------------------------------------------
            ORG     0000H
            LJMP    MAIN
            ORG     0030H
MAIN:       MOV     TMOD, #20H    ; Timer 1, Mode 2 (auto-reload)
            MOV     TH1,  #0FDH   ; 9600 baud reload value
            MOV     SCON, #50H    ; Mode 1 (SM0=0, SM1=1), REN=1
                                      ;   SCON = 0101 0000 = 0x50
            SETB    TR1           ; Start Timer 1
            MOV     PCON, #00H    ; SMOD = 0 (no doubling)

TRANSMIT:   MOV     DPTR, #STRING
LOOP:       CLR     A
            MOVC    A, @A+DPTR    ; Read character from code memory
            JZ      DONE          ; If null terminator, restart
            MOV     SBUF, A       ; Load character into SBUF (start TX)
WAIT_TX:    JNB     TI, WAIT_TX   ; Poll until TI is set
            CLR     TI            ; Clear TI flag (software)
            INC     DPTR          ; Point to next character
            SJMP    LOOP          ; Continue transmission

DONE:       SJMP    TRANSMIT      ; Loop the string continuously

STRING:     DB      'K', 'T', 'U', 0
            END
```

**Explanation of Key Instructions:**
- `MOV SCON, #50H` → 0101 0000: SM0=0, SM1=1 (Mode 1), REN=1 (also enables reception)
- `MOV TMOD, #20H` → Timer 1 in Mode 2, software-controlled
- `JNB TI, WAIT_TX` → Polls TI; exits loop when hardware sets TI at end of transmission
- `CLR TI` → **Mandatory** software clear of the TI flag
- `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` → Reads from code memory (used because string is in ROM)

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Serial Data Reception and Baud Rate Calculation

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model — 14 Marks]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] **A 8051 system uses a 12 MHz crystal. Calculate the TH1 value to achieve a baud rate of 4800 bps in Mode 1 with SMOD = 0. What is the percentage error compared to the ideal baud rate?**

**(b)** [7 Marks] **Write an 8051 assembly program to receive 10 bytes serially at 9600 baud, store them in internal RAM starting at 30H, and then echo each received byte back to the sender (full-duplex loopback). Use 11.0592 MHz crystal.**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Given:** $f_{\text{osc}} = 12 \text{ MHz} = 12000000 \text{ Hz}$, Baud = 4800, SMOD = 0

**Step 1 — Calculate ideal TH1:**

$$\text{TH1} = 256 - \frac{f_{\text{osc}}}{384 \times \text{Baud Rate}} = 256 - \frac{12000000}{384 \times 4800}$$

$$= 256 - \frac{12000000}{1843200} = 256 - 6.5104$$

Since TH1 must be an **integer**, we round to the nearest: **TH1 ≈ 6.51 → Use TH1 = 249 or 250**.

**Common convention:** Round down → TH1 = 249 (0xF9). *Some textbooks round to 250 (0xFA) — both are acceptable if the error is calculated accordingly.*

**Step 2 — Calculate the actual baud rate using TH1 = 249:**

$$\text{Baud}_{\text{actual}} = \frac{12000000}{384 \times (256 - 249)} = \frac{12000000}{384 \times 7} = \frac{12000000}{2688} = 4464.29 \text{ bps}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate percentage error:**

$$\text{Error \%} = \frac{4464.29 - 4800}{4800} \times 100 = \frac{-335.71}{4800} \times 100 = -6.99\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students forget that the TH1 calculation yields a **non-integer** for 12 MHz crystals and incorrectly report the result as exact. Always perform the **rounding** and then calculate the **actual baud rate** and **error percentage**. A ±2–7% error is typical with 12 MHz crystals.

**Alternative if TH1 = 250 (0xFA):**

$$\text{Baud}_{\text{actual}} = \frac{12000000}{384 \times 6} = 5208.33 \text{ bps} \rightarrow \text{Error} = +8.5\%$$

**Conclusion:** The 12 MHz crystal produces significant baud-rate error (~7-8%) at 4800 baud. The **11.0592 MHz crystal is strongly preferred** to achieve 0% error.

#### Model Solution to (b):

```assembly
;------------------------------------------------------------
; PROGRAM: Receive 10 bytes at 9600 baud, store at 30H, echo
; CRYSTAL: 11.0592 MHz, Mode 1
;------------------------------------------------------------
            ORG     0000H
            LJMP    MAIN
            ORG     0030H
MAIN:       MOV     TMOD, #20H    ; Timer 1, Mode 2
            MOV     TH1,  #0FDH   ; 9600 baud
            MOV     SCON, #50H    ; Mode 1, REN=1
            SETB    TR1           ; Start Timer 1
            MOV     R0,  #30H     ; Pointer to RAM storage area
            MOV     R7,  #0AH     ; Counter = 10 bytes

RECEIVE:    JNB     RI, RECEIVE   ; Wait for RI (byte received)
            MOV     A,   SBUF     ; Read received byte
            CLR     RI            ; Clear RI flag (software)
            MOV     @R0, A        ; Store byte in RAM at @R0
            INC     R0            ; Increment pointer
            DJNZ    R7, ECHO_LOOP ; Decrement counter, continue if not 0
            SJMP    RECEIVE       ; Restart reception after 10 bytes

ECHO_LOOP:  MOV     SBUF, A       ; Echo byte back to sender
WAIT_ECHO:  JNB     TI, WAIT_ECHO ; Wait for echo transmission complete
            CLR     TI            ; Clear TI flag
            SJMP    RECEIVE       ; Go back to receive next byte

            END
```

**Explanation:**
- `R0` is the **data pointer** initialized to 30H (internal RAM address)
- `R7` is the **byte counter** initialized to 0AH (10 in decimal)
- `JNB RI, RECEIVE` → Polling loop; blocks until hardware sets RI
- `CLR RI` → **Mandatory** — clears the RI flag set by hardware
- `DJNZ R7, ECHO_LOOP` → Decrements R7; loops back to ECHO if not zero, else continues
- `MOV SBUF, A` → Initiates echo transmission
- `JNB TI, WAIT_ECHO` → Polls TI before sending the next echo

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common 8051 Assembly Errors:**
> 1. Forgetting `CLR TI` or `CLR RI` after polling → Infinite loop or missed receptions
> 2. Using `MOV DPTR, #30H` instead of `MOV R0, #30H` for internal RAM access
> 3. Forgetting `SETB TR1` to start Timer 1 (baud rate clock never runs)
> 4. Loading `TH1` with decimal value but expecting hex result (or vice versa)
> 5. Using `MOVC` instead of `MOV` to read from internal RAM in reception

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- ⚡ **SBUF (0x99)** is a **dual register** — one for TX (write) and one for RX (read). Same address, different physical registers.
- ⚡ **SCON (0x98)** is **bit-addressable**. Holds mode bits (SM0, SM1), enable bits (REN), and flags (TI, RI).
- ⚡ **TI and RI** are **set by hardware, cleared by software** — this is the single most-asked fact in KTU serial communication questions.
- ⚡ **PCON bit 7 (SMOD)** doubles the baud rate when set. PCON is **not bit-addressable**.
- ⚡ **Mode 1** = 8-bit UART, variable baud rate, frame = **10 bits** (1 start + 8 data + 1 stop). Most commonly used.
- ⚡ **Mode 0** = 8-bit shift register, **fixed** baud rate = $f_{\text{osc}}/12$.
- ⚡ **Mode 2** = 9-bit UART, **fixed** baud rate = $2^{\text{SMOD}} \times f_{\text{osc}} / 64$.
- ⚡ **Mode 3** = 9-bit UART, **variable** baud rate (same as Mode 1).
- ⚡ **Timer 1 must be in Mode 2** (8-bit auto-reload) for baud-rate generation.
- ⚡ **TH1 = 256 - (f_osc / (384 × Baud Rate))** for SMOD = 0.
- ⚡ **Crystal = 11.0592 MHz** for zero baud-rate error at standard rates (1200, 2400, 4800, 9600).
- ⚡ **P3.0 = RXD**, **P3.1 = TXD** — these pins MUST be configured (sometimes external pull-ups needed).
- ⚡ **REN bit in SCON must be 1** to enable reception (irrelevant for transmission only).
- ⚡ Serial interrupt uses **TI/RI flags** and the **ES bit in the IE register** (NOT TF1 or ET1).
- ⚡ Common TH1 values for 11.0592 MHz, SMOD=0: **1200→0xE6, 2400→0xF4, 4800→0xFA, 9600→0xFD**.
- ⚡ For 10-byte transmission at 9600 baud, time = (10 × 10) / 9600 ≈ **10.4 ms**.
- ⚡ RS-232 levels are ±12V, but 8051 uses **TTL 0-5V** — a **MAX232** level converter IC is required for PC communication.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to ARM - ARM Family
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT403 - COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE AND MICROCONTROLLER
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Introduction to ARM - ARM Family

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is ARM?

**ARM** stands for **Advanced RISC Machine**. It is a family of reduced instruction set computing (RISC) architectures for computer processors, configured for various environments. ARM Holdings (now Arm Ltd., headquartered in Cambridge, UK) designs the ARM architecture and licenses it to other companies who manufacture the actual chips — a model known as the **Intellectual Property (IP) licensing model**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Note**: ARM was originally developed by **Acorn Computers Limited** in 1985, where it stood for **Acorn RISC Machine**. After a joint venture with Apple Computer and VLSI Technology, the company was renamed **Advanced RISC Machines**, and the acronym was later repurposed as **Advanced RISC Machine** — a backronym.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy: The "Recipe Card Franchise"**
> Imagine a master chef (ARM Ltd.) who designs the *perfect recipe* for a gourmet dish. Instead of opening his own restaurants worldwide, he sells the recipe (the architecture/IP) to hundreds of local restaurants (chipmakers like Qualcomm, Samsung, Apple, NXP, STMicroelectronics). Each restaurant adds its own ingredients, kitchens, and special touches, but the *core recipe* — the instructions, the philosophy — remains consistent. This is exactly how ARM works: the company designs and licenses the processor architecture, while partners fabricate customized chips tailored for smartphones, embedded systems, IoT devices, and even servers.

## 1.3 Why ARM Matters in Modern Computing

ARM processors dominate three major computing domains:

- **Mobile Computing**: ~99% of the world's smartphones use ARM-based processors.
- **Embedded & IoT Devices**: Microcontrollers in washing machines, cars, medical devices, drones.
- **High-Performance Computing**: Apple's M1/M2/M3 chips, AWS Graviton servers, and even supercomputers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Architectural Philosophy**: ARM is built on the **RISC (Reduced Instruction Set Computer)** principle — a small, highly optimized set of instructions, simple addressing modes, and large register files. This contrasts with **CISC (Complex Instruction Set Computer)** architectures like Intel x86, which have hundreds of variable-length instructions.

## 1.4 Key Metrics at a Glance

| Parameter | Value / Description |
|---|---|
| **Instruction Length** | Fixed 32-bit (in classic ARM), 16-bit (Thumb), mixed (Thumb-2) |
| **Registers** | **37** physical registers (31 general-purpose + 6 status) |
| **Operating Modes | **7** privileged modes + 1 user mode |
| **Endianness** | Bi-endian (selectable via hardware configuration) |
| **Power Efficiency** | Industry-leading performance-per-watt metric |
| **Market Share (Mobile)** | ~99% of smartphones |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**: Module 4 expects students to clearly distinguish between ARM architectural versions, families (Cortex-A/R/M), and their target application domains. Expect **3-mark definition questions** on ARM basics in Part A.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 RISC vs CISC: The Philosophical Divide

ARM's design philosophy is rooted in the RISC principles formalized by Patterson and Ditzel (1980) and Hennessy and Patterson. Let us break down the comparison:

| Feature | RISC (ARM) | CISC (x86) |
|---|---|---|
| Instruction Set Size | Small (typically < 200) | Large (500+ instructions) |
| Instruction Length | **Fixed** (32-bit / 16-bit) | Variable (1 to 15 bytes) |
| Addressing Modes | Few (3–5 typically) | Many (12+) |
| Memory Access | Load/Store architecture | Register-Memory mixed |
| Cycle per Instruction | Mostly 1 CPI | Variable (multi-cycle) |
| Pipelining | Highly efficient | Complex |
| Register File | Large (37 in ARM) | Smaller (8 in x86-32) |
| Compiler Role | Heavy optimization needed | Less complex |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight**: The exam frequently tests the **RISC characteristics** list. Memorize: *Single-cycle execution, fixed-length instructions, load/store architecture, large register file, hard-wired control unit, and heavy pipelining*.

## 2.2 Salient Features of ARM Architecture

### 2.2.1 Load/Store Architecture
ARM processors can only perform data processing on operands held in **registers**. Any data from memory must first be loaded into a register via a `LDR` instruction, processed, and then optionally stored back using `STR`.

### 2.2.2 Fixed Instruction Length
- **ARM mode**: 32-bit fixed-length instructions
- **Thumb mode**: 16-bit compressed instructions (≈65% of ARM code density)
- **Thumb-2**: Mixed 16/32-bit instructions (no mode switch penalty)

### 2.2.3 Large Register File
ARM has **37 registers** organized as:
- **31 general-purpose 32-bit registers** (visible at any time: 15 user + some banked for privileged modes)
- **6 status registers** (one CPSR + five SPSRs for exception modes)

### 2.2.4 Conditional Execution
**Every ARM instruction** can be conditionally executed based on the flags in the **Current Program Status Register (CPSR)**. This eliminates many short branches and reduces pipeline flushes.

### 2.2.5 Inline Barrel Shifter
The second operand of any data-processing instruction can be **barrel-shifted** for free (no extra cycles). Operations include: LSL, LSR, ASR, ROR, RRX.

## 2.3 The ARM Architecture Version Timeline

The ARM architecture has evolved through major versions, each introducing new features:

| Architecture Version | Year | Key Innovations |
|---|---|---|
| **ARMv1** | 1985 | First prototype, 26-bit address space |
| **ARMv2** | 1987 | Added MUL, MLA, coprocessor support |
| **ARMv3** | 1992 | 32-bit address space, CPSR/SPSR |
| **ARMv4** | 1994 | **Thumb instruction set** introduced |
| **ARMv4T** | 1994 | ARM7TDMI (basis of many classic MCUs) |
| **ARMv5TE** | 1999 | Enhanced DSP, BLX, CLZ instructions |
| **ARMv6** | 2001 | SIMD (media instructions), unaligned data |
| **ARMv7** | 2004 | **Thumb-2**, hardware divide, NEON advanced SIMD |
| **ARMv7-A** | 2004 | Applications profile (Cortex-A series) |
| **ARMv7-R** | 2004 | Real-time profile (Cortex-R series) |
| **ARMv7-M** | 2004 | Microcontroller profile (Cortex-M series) |
| **ARMv8-A** | 2011 | **64-bit AArch64** (Cortex-A57, Apple A7+) |
| **ARMv8.1 / 8.2 / 8.3** | 2014–2017 | Atomic, SVE, complex number support |
| **ARMv9-A** | 2021 | SVE2, security domains, Realm Management Extension |

## 2.4 ARM Processor Families — The Cortex Trinity

ARM groups its processors into **three families** based on target applications. This is one of the **highest-yield topics** for KTU exams.

| Family | Profile | Target Application | Examples | Performance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cortex-A** | Application | High-performance computing, mobile, servers | Cortex-A53, A57, A72, A78, **Apple M1** | GHz clock, multi-core, MMU |
| **Cortex-R** | Real-time | Hard real-time control, automotive, storage | Cortex-R4, R5, R7, R8 | Deterministic latency, ECC |
| **Cortex-M** | Microcontroller | Embedded, IoT, low-power MCUs | Cortex-M0, M0+, M3, M4, M7, M33 | Low power, MPU, NVIC |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorization Tip for KTU**: *"A for Applications (Phones), R for Real-time (Cars/Brakes), M for Microcontroller (Washing Machines)"*.

## 2.5 Legacy ARM Cores (Pre-Cortex, historically important)

These are the **classic ARM cores** that predate the Cortex branding and are still referenced in textbooks:

| Core | Architecture | Features |
|---|---|---|
| **ARM7TDMI** | ARMv4T | Thumb, Debug, Multiplier, ICE — used in Nokia 3310, classic GPS |
| **ARM9TDMI** | ARMv4T | 5-stage pipeline, separate I/D caches |
| **ARM11** | ARMv6 | 8-stage pipeline, SIMD media instructions |
| **StrongARM** | ARMv4 | High-performance, DEC → Intel → Marvell |
| **XScale** | ARMv5TE | Intel's evolution of StrongARM |

## 2.6 ARM Versions vs ARM Cores vs ARM Families — Clarification

This is a common point of confusion in KTU exams:

- **Architecture Version** (ARMv7, ARMv8): The instruction set specification and programmer's model.
- **Processor Core** (Cortex-A53): A specific implementation of the architecture.
- **Processor Family** (Cortex-A): A group of cores sharing a target application profile.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic**: *Architecture = Specification, Core = Implementation, Family = Group with common goal.*

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Relation | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **CPI (Cycles Per Instruction)** | $\text{CPI} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (IC_i \times CC_i)}{IC}$ | Dimensionless |
| **CPU Execution Time** | $T_{CPU} = IC \times CPI \times T_{clock}$ | Seconds |
| **CPU Clock Period** | $T_{clock} = \dfrac{1}{f_{clock}}$ | Seconds |
| **MIPS (Millions of Instructions Per Second)** | $\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{f_{clock}}{CPI \times 10^{6}}$ | MIPS |
| **Thumb Compression Ratio** | $\rho_T = \dfrac{\text{Thumb code size}}{\text{ARM code size}} \approx 0.65$ | Unitless |
| **Memory Bandwidth** | $BW = \dfrac{\text{Data transferred}}{\text{Time}}$ | Bytes/s |
| **Pipeline Speedup** | $S_n = \dfrac{n \times t}{(n + k - 1) \times t}$ where k stages, n instructions | Speedup factor |
| **Power Dissipation (Dynamic)** | $P_d = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{dd}^{2} \cdot f$ | Watts |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

ARM's RISC philosophy and IP licensing model enable:

- **Energy-efficient mobile devices**: Smartphones running 24+ hours on a single charge.
- **Automotive safety systems**: Cortex-R cores in brake-by-wire and airbag controllers with **deterministic** interrupt latency.
- **IoT proliferation**: Cortex-M0+ cores costing less than $0.50 per chip in billions of devices.
- **Server revolution**: AWS Graviton and Apple Silicon demonstrate ARM in cloud and desktop computing.
- **Educational platforms**: The very **LPC2148** (ARM7TDMI-S) used in KTU labs is itself a testament to ARM's ubiquity.

# 📒 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Calculating CPU Execution Time for an ARM Processor

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem**: A Cortex-M3 based system runs at **72 MHz**. A benchmark program contains **10 million instructions** with an average **CPI of 1.5**. Calculate the CPU execution time, MIPS rating, and compare with a CISC processor at the same clock.

### Step 1: Identify Given Parameters

$$
f_{clock} = 72 \, \text{MHz} = 72 \times 10^{6} \, \text{Hz}
$$

$$
IC = 10 \times 10^{6} \, \text{instructions}
$$

$$
CPI_{avg} = 1.5
$$

### Step 2: Compute the Clock Period

$$
T_{clock} = \frac{1}{f_{clock}}
$$

$$
T_{clock} = \frac{1}{72 \times 10^{6}}
$$

$$
T_{clock} \approx 13.89 \, \text{ns}
$$

### Step 3: Compute Total CPU Cycles

$$
\text{Total Cycles} = IC \times CPI_{avg}
$$

$$
\text{Total Cycles} = (10 \times 10^{6}) \times 1.5
$$

$$
\text{Total Cycles} = 15 \times 10^{6} \, \text{cycles}
$$

### Step 4: Compute CPU Execution Time

$$
T_{CPU} = IC \times CPI_{avg} \times T_{clock}
$$

$$
T_{CPU} = (10 \times 10^{6}) \times 1.5 \times \frac{1}{72 \times 10^{6}}
$$

$$
T_{CPU} = \frac{15}{72} \times 10^{6} \times \frac{1}{10^{6}}
$$

$$
T_{CPU} = 0.2083 \, \text{seconds}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{CPU} \approx 208.33 \, \text{ms}}
$$

### Step 5: Compute MIPS Rating

$$
\text{MIPS} = \frac{f_{clock}}{CPI_{avg} \times 10^{6}}
$$

$$
\text{MIPS} = \frac{72 \times 10^{6}}{1.5 \times 10^{6}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{MIPS} = 48}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Pipeline Speedup for ARM7TDMI 3-Stage Pipeline

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem**: The ARM7TDMI uses a 3-stage pipeline (Fetch, Decode, Execute). Compute the speedup for executing **100 instructions** compared to a non-pipelined implementation, assuming each stage takes **1 cycle** and pipeline fill/drain overhead is included.

### Step 1: Non-Pipelined Execution Cycles

For a non-pipelined processor, each instruction takes $k$ stages sequentially:

$$
\text{Cycles}_{\text{non-pipe}} = n \times k
$$

$$
\text{Cycles}_{\text{non-pipe}} = 100 \times 3 = 300 \, \text{cycles}
$$

### Step 2: Pipelined Execution Cycles

For a pipelined processor, the first instruction takes $k$ cycles (pipeline fill), and each subsequent instruction takes 1 additional cycle:

$$
\text{Cycles}_{\text{pipe}} = k + (n - 1)
$$

$$
\text{Cycles}_{\text{pipe}} = 3 + (100 - 1)
$$

$$
\text{Cycles}_{\text{pipe}} = 102 \, \text{cycles}
$$

### Step 3: Compute Speedup

$$
S_n = \frac{\text{Cycles}_{\text{non-pipe}}}{\text{Cycles}_{\text{pipe}}}
$$

$$
S_n = \frac{300}{102}
$$

$$
S_n \approx 2.94
$$

### Step 4: Theoretical Maximum Speedup

As $n \to \infty$, speedup approaches the number of pipeline stages:

$$
S_{\infty} = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{n \cdot k}{k + (n - 1)} = k
$$

$$
\boxed{S_{\infty} = 3 \quad (\text{i.e., ideal speedup equals number of stages})}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Power Savings of Thumb vs ARM Mode

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem**: Compare instruction fetch energy for ARM vs Thumb modes. Assume 16 KB code in ARM (32-bit) is rewritten using Thumb (16-bit) with the same functionality.

### Step 1: ARM Mode Memory Footprint

$$
\text{Memory}_{ARM} = 16 \, \text{KB} \times 32 \, \text{bits} = 512 \, \text{Kbits}
$$

### Step 2: Thumb Mode Memory Footprint

Thumb code is approximately **65%** the size of ARM code:

$$
\text{Memory}_{Thumb} = 16 \, \text{KB} \times 0.65 = 10.4 \, \text{KB}
$$

$$
\text{Memory}_{Thumb} = 10.4 \times 1024 \times 16 \, \text{bits} = 170.4 \, \text{Kbits}
$$

### Step 3: Memory Bandwidth Reduction

$$
\text{Savings} = \frac{512 - 170.4}{512} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{Savings} = \frac{341.6}{512} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Savings} \approx 66.7\% \, \text{fetch bandwidth reduction}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight**: This is why embedded ARM systems (like those using Cortex-M) default to Thumb/Thumb-2 mode — it dramatically reduces power consumption by cutting instruction memory accesses nearly in half.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: ARM vs Other Architectures

| Parameter | ARM (Cortex-A53) | Intel Core i7 (x86) | MIPS | RISC-V |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| ISA Type | RISC | CISC | RISC | RISC (Open) |
| Instruction Length | 32-bit (AArch32) / 64-bit (AArch64) | Variable | Fixed 32-bit | Variable (32/64/128) |
| Pipeline Depth | 8–15 stages | 14–20 stages | 5 stages | Configurable |
| Out-of-Order Execution | Yes (in high-end) | Yes | Limited | Optional |
| Power Consumption | Very low (5W mobile) | High (45–125W) | Low | Very low |
| Licensing | Licensed IP | Proprietary | Licensed IP | Open-source |
| Market in Mobile | ~99% | < 1% | < 1% | Growing |

# 📕 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the term ARM. Explain the significance of RISC architecture in ARM processors.**

**Model Answer:**

**ARM** stands for **Advanced RISC Machine**. It is a family of **32-bit (and now 64-bit) reduced instruction set computing (RISC) processor architectures** developed by ARM Holdings (now Arm Ltd., Cambridge, UK). ARM designs the architecture and licenses the intellectual property (IP) to semiconductor manufacturers who fabricate the actual chips.

The **RISC architecture** is significant in ARM for the following reasons:

1. **Simplified Instruction Set**: Only a small number of instructions (~100 in basic ARM), each performing a simple operation, enabling single-cycle execution.
2. **Fixed Instruction Length**: 32-bit ARM mode and 16-bit Thumb mode enable predictable fetch and decode.
3. **Load/Store Architecture**: Memory access is restricted to dedicated load/store instructions, simplifying the data path.
4. **Large Register File**: 37 registers reduce memory traffic significantly.
5. **Pipelining Efficiency**: Uniform instruction length and execution time enable deep, efficient pipelines.
6. **Lower Power Consumption**: Simpler hardware and fewer transistors mean lower dynamic power — making ARM ideal for mobile and embedded systems.

**Result**: ARM processors achieve industry-leading **performance per watt**, dominating the mobile, IoT, and increasingly the server and desktop markets.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Compare the three ARM Cortex profiles (Cortex-A, Cortex-R, Cortex-M) with suitable applications.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | **Cortex-A** | **Cortex-R** | **Cortex-M** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Profile** | Application | Real-time | Microcontroller |
| **Target Use** | Smartphones, tablets, servers, laptops | Automotive brakes, HDD controllers, 5G base stations | IoT sensors, wearables, motor control, MCUs |
| **MMU/MPU** | Full MMU | MPU | Optional MPU |
| **Clock Speed** | Up to ~3 GHz | Up to ~1 GHz | Up to ~200 MHz |
| **Pipeline Depth** | Deep, out-of-order | Moderate, deterministic | Shallow, deterministic |
| **Interrupt Latency** | Variable | Guaranteed | Hard-real-time |
| **Example Cores** | Cortex-A53, A78, **Apple M1** | Cortex-R4, R5, R7, R8 | Cortex-M0, M3, M4, M7, M33 |
| **Operating System** | Linux, Android, iOS, Windows | RTOS, AUTOSAR | Bare-metal, FreeRTOS |

> **Conclusion**: The Cortex family uses a **common architecture base** but is optimized for **different application domains** — a key factor in ARM's widespread adoption.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### 🔹 Question A: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** Explain the salient features of the ARM processor architecture. Discuss how the **conditional execution** feature improves performance compared to traditional branch-based control flow. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **ARM processor core architecture**. Mention the role of the **barrel shifter** and the **CPSR register**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### 🔸 Model Solution for Question A (a):

**Salient Features of ARM Processor Architecture:**

1. **Load/Store RISC Architecture**: Data processing operations operate only on register contents; memory is accessed only through dedicated `LDR` and `STR` instructions.

2. **Fixed Instruction Length**: ARM mode uses uniform 32-bit instructions, simplifying the fetch and decode stages.

3. **Large Register File**: ARM provides **37 physical registers**, of which 15 user-mode registers (R0–R14 + PC) are visible at any time, with banking for privileged modes.

4. **Conditional Execution**: Every instruction can be conditionally executed based on the **N, Z, C, V** flags in the CPSR. A 4-bit condition code prefix (e.g., `EQ`, `NE`, `GT`, `LT`) determines whether the instruction modifies processor state.

5. **Inline Barrel Shifter**: The second operand of any data-processing instruction can be barrel-shifted (LSL, LSR, ASR, ROR, RRX) for free, eliminating separate shift instructions.

6. **Thumb and Thumb-2**: Compressed 16-bit instructions (Thumb) and mixed 16/32-bit (Thumb-2) provide high code density for embedded applications.

7. **Multiple Operating Modes**: 7 privileged modes (FIQ, IRQ, Supervisor, Abort, Undefined, System, Monitor in v8) plus User mode support robust exception handling.

**Performance Benefit of Conditional Execution:**

Traditional processors use **branches** to skip instructions (e.g., `if (x == 0) skip; y = y + 1;`). Branches cause **pipeline flushes**, which waste 3–5+ cycles. ARM's conditional execution replaces such branches:

```
; Traditional branch approach (wastes cycles):
CMP R0, #0
BNE skip
ADD R1, R1, #1
skip: ...
```
