```
; ARM conditional execution (no branch):
CMP R0, #0
ADDEQ R1, R1, #1   ; executed ONLY if R0 == 0
```

**Result**: Eliminates branch misprediction penalties, reduces pipeline flushes, and saves code space — a clear performance advantage in branch-heavy code.

---

#### 🔸 Model Solution for Question A (b):

**Block Diagram of ARM Processor Core:**

```
        ┌──────────────────────────────────────┐
        │          ARM Processor Core          │
        │                                      │
   PC ──► Fetch ──► Decode ──► Control Unit   │
                       │           │           │
                       ▼           ▼           │
                  Register     Barrel Shifter  │
                   Bank (37)        │          │
                       │            ▼          │
                       └────►►►►►►► ALU ◄──────┘
                                  │
                                  ▼
                            Data Memory Bus
                                  │
                                  ▼
                          External Memory
                                  
                       CPSR ◄── flags from ALU
                        │
                        ▼
                   Conditional Decoder
```

**Role of the Barrel Shifter:**

The **barrel shifter** is a combinational logic unit that performs **any shift or rotate operation in a single cycle**, operating in parallel with the ALU. It processes the **second operand** before it enters the ALU:

- `LSL` (Logical Shift Left): `R2 = R1 LSL #2` shifts R1 left by 2 bits.
- `LSR`, `ASR`: Logical/Arithmetic shift right.
- `ROR`, `RRX`: Rotate right / rotate right extended.

**Engineering Advantage**: Without a barrel shifter, each shift would require a separate instruction and clock cycle. With it, complex expressions like `R3 = R1 + (R2 LSL #3)` complete in a single instruction and single cycle — drastically reducing code size and execution time.

**Role of the CPSR Register:**

The **Current Program Status Register (CPSR)** is a 32-bit register holding:

- **Condition flags** (bits 31–28): **N** (Negative), **Z** (Zero), **C** (Carry), **V** (Overflow).
- **Interrupt disable bits** (I, F): Disable IRQ and FIQ.
- **Thumb bit (T)**: Determines ARM (0) or Thumb (1) mode.
- **Mode bits (M[4:0])**: Define the current processor mode.

The condition flags drive the **conditional execution** of subsequent instructions, while the mode bits enable rapid **exception entry and exit**.

---

### 🔹 Question B: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]`

**(a)** Discuss the evolution of the ARM architecture from ARMv1 to ARMv8. Highlight the major features introduced in **ARMv7** and **ARMv8-A**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 3-stage pipelined ARM processor runs at **50 MHz**. A benchmark has **5 million instructions** with the following instruction mix:
   - 40% ALU instructions (CPI = 1)
   - 35% Load/Store instructions (CPI = 2)
   - 15% Branch instructions (CPI = 3)
   - 10% Multiply instructions (CPI = 4)
   
   Calculate the **average CPI**, **CPU execution time**, and **MIPS rating**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### 🔸 Model Solution for Question B (a):

**Evolution of ARM Architecture:**

| Version | Year | Major Innovation |
|---|---|---|
| ARMv1 | 1985 | First commercial RISC prototype; 26-bit PC |
| ARMv2 | 1987 | MUL, MLA, coprocessor interface |
| ARMv3 | 1992 | 32-bit addressing; CPSR/SPSR introduced |
| ARMv4 | 1994 | **Halfword and signed data support** |
| ARMv4T | 1994 | **Thumb 16-bit instruction set** (ARM7TDMI) |
| ARMv5TE | 1999 | Enhanced DSP instructions, BLX, CLZ |
| ARMv5TEJ | 2000 | Jazelle (Java bytecode execution) |
| ARMv6 | 2001 | SIMD media instructions, unaligned data support |
| ARMv7 | 2004 | Thumb-2, hardware divide, NEON SIMD, **Cortex family** |
| ARMv8-A | 2011 | **64-bit AArch64**, A32/T32 backward compatibility |

**Major Features of ARMv7:**

- **Thumb-2 Instruction Set**: Mixed 16/32-bit instructions — providing ARM-class performance with Thumb-class code density.
- **Hardware Divide Instructions**: `SDIV` and `UDIV` in hardware (previously software routines).
- **NEON Advanced SIMD**: 128-bit SIMD engine for multimedia and signal processing.
- **Cortex Profile Diversification**: Formal split into **Cortex-A (Applications), Cortex-R (Real-time), Cortex-M (Microcontroller)**.

**Major Features of ARMv8-A:**

- **AArch64 Execution State**: New 64-bit instruction set with **31 general-purpose 64-bit registers** (X0–X30), 64-bit PC, SP, and ELR.
- **Backward Compatibility**: Existing AArch32 (ARM/Thumb) code runs unchanged via OS-controlled mode switching.
- **Exception Levels (EL0–EL3)**: Replaces the 7 privileged modes of ARMv7 with cleaner hierarchical exception model.
- **Crypto Extensions**: Optional AES, SHA-1, SHA-2 hardware acceleration.
- **Improved Virtualization**: Stage-2 translation tables for hypervisor support.

---

#### 🔸 Model Solution for Question B (b):

**Given:**

- Clock frequency $f_{clock} = 50 \, \text{MHz}$
- Total instruction count $IC = 5 \times 10^{6}$
- Instruction mix:
  - ALU: 40% at CPI = 1
  - Load/Store: 35% at CPI = 2
  - Branch: 15% at CPI = 3
  - Multiply: 10% at CPI = 4

**Step 1: Calculate Average CPI**

$$
CPI_{avg} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( \text{Fraction}_i \times CPI_i \right)
$$

$$
CPI_{avg} = (0.40 \times 1) + (0.35 \times 2) + (0.15 \times 3) + (0.10 \times 4)
$$

$$
CPI_{avg} = 0.40 + 0.70 + 0.45 + 0.40
$$

$$
\boxed{CPI_{avg} = 1.95}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate Total CPU Cycles**

$$
\text{Total Cycles} = IC \times CPI_{avg}
$$

$$
\text{Total Cycles} = (5 \times 10^{6}) \times 1.95
$$

$$
\text{Total Cycles} = 9.75 \times 10^{6} \, \text{cycles}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate CPU Execution Time**

$$
T_{CPU} = \text{Total Cycles} \times T_{clock}
$$

$$
T_{clock} = \frac{1}{50 \times 10^{6}} = 20 \, \text{ns}
$$

$$
T_{CPU} = 9.75 \times 10^{6} \times 20 \times 10^{-9}
$$

$$
T_{CPU} = 9.75 \times 20 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{CPU} = 195 \, \text{ms}}
$$

**Step 4: Calculate MIPS Rating**

$$
\text{MIPS} = \frac{f_{clock}}{CPI_{avg} \times 10^{6}}
$$

$$
\text{MIPS} = \frac{50 \times 10^{6}}{1.95 \times 10^{6}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{MIPS} \approx 25.64}
$$

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in ARM Family Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing "ARM versions" with "ARM cores"**: ARMv7 is an *architecture version*, not a processor. Cortex-A8 is a *core* implementing ARMv7-A. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks when these terms are interchanged.
> 
> 2. **Incorrect CPSR description**: Students often write "CPSR stores the program counter" — it does NOT. CPSR stores **condition flags, interrupt mask bits, mode bits, and the Thumb bit**.
> 
> 3. **Forgetting the Barrel Shifter**: In block diagrams, many students omit the barrel shifter or place it after the ALU — it must be on the **second operand path, BEFORE the ALU**.
> 
> 4. **Wrong CPI calculation**: Average CPI must be weighted by instruction *fraction*, not summed directly. The formula $CPI_{avg} = \sum (IC_i / IC_{total}) \times CPI_i$ is mandatory.
> 
> 5. **No comparison table**: When asked to "compare Cortex-A, R, M", merely listing them in paragraphs without a table loses marks. Always use a tabular comparison for full credit.
> 
> 6. **Missing application examples**: Always cite at least one real-world application per Cortex profile. Generic answers lose 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Introduction to ARM**

- ✅ **ARM** = **Advanced RISC Machine** (originally Acorn RISC Machine).
- ✅ ARM Ltd. (Cambridge, UK) **licenses IP**; partners fabricate chips — **fabless model**.
- ✅ ARM is a **RISC** architecture: fixed-length, load/store, large register file, single-cycle execution.
- ✅ ARM has **37 physical registers**: 31 general-purpose + 6 status.
- ✅ **Conditional execution** on every instruction — driven by **N, Z, C, V** flags in CPSR.
- ✅ **Barrel shifter** performs any shift/rotate in 1 cycle, on operand 2, before ALU.
- ✅ **Thumb mode** (16-bit) and **Thumb-2** (mixed 16/32) provide ~65% code density improvement.
- ✅ **ARM7TDMI** features: **T**=Thumb, **D**=Debug, **M**=Multiplier, **I**=ICE.
- ✅ **Architecture versions** progress: v1 (1985) → v4T (Thumb) → v6 (SIMD) → v7 (Thumb-2, Cortex) → v8-A (64-bit) → v9-A (SVE2, security).
- ✅ **Three Cortex profiles**:
  - **Cortex-A** = Application (phones, servers) → MMU, GHz, Linux/Android/iOS.
  - **Cortex-R** = Real-time (automotive) → Deterministic latency, ECC.
  - **Cortex-M** = Microcontroller (IoT, embedded) → Low power, NVIC, bare-metal.
- ✅ **CPSR** holds condition flags, interrupt masks, Thumb bit, and mode bits.
- ✅ **Key formulas**:
  - $T_{CPU} = IC \times CPI \times T_{clock}$
  - $\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{f_{clock}}{CPI \times 10^{6}}$
  - $S_n = \dfrac{n \cdot k}{k + (n-1)}$ for $k$-stage pipeline.
- ✅ **Performance-per-watt** is ARM's defining advantage in mobile and embedded domains.
- ✅ Apple's **M1/M2/M3** and AWS Graviton are examples of ARM's move into desktop/cloud computing.
- ✅ **Bi-endian** support — selectable via hardware pin (BIGEND signal).
- ✅ **7 operating modes**: User, FIQ, IRQ, Supervisor, Abort, Undefined, System (+ Monitor in v8).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ARM 7 Register Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT403 - COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE AND MICROCONTROLLER
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** ARM 7 Register Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🧠 ARM 7 Register Architecture — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ARM7 Register Architecture** refers to the **32-bit Reduced Instruction Set Computer (RISC)** register organization used by the ARM7TDMI processor core, comprising a total of **37 registers** — **31 general-purpose 32-bit registers** and **6 status registers** — grouped into register banks that are physically distinct but architecturally overlapping based on the processor's operating mode.

In the ARM7 architecture:
- **15 general-purpose registers (R0–R12, R13, R14)** are directly visible to the programmer in **User mode**.
- **R13** functions as the **Stack Pointer (SP)**.
- **R14** functions as the **Link Register (LR)**.
- **R15** functions as the **Program Counter (PC)**.
- **1 Current Program Status Register (CPSR)** holds condition flags, interrupt disables, mode bits, and Thumb state.
- **5 Saved Program Status Registers (SPSRs)** exist for the **5 privileged modes** (FIQ, IRQ, Supervisor, Abort, Undefined).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Workshop Tool Bench" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: A Carpenter's Workshop**
>
> Imagine a carpenter has a **single primary workbench (User Mode)** with **15 common tools (R0–R14, plus PC)** he uses every day. However, when a **special emergency job** arrives (an exception like IRQ or FIQ), the carpenter steps into a **side-room specialist bench** where he instantly gets a **different set of specialized tools (banked registers)**. He never mixes the two — the emergency tools are physically separate to avoid clobbering his ongoing project. The **CPSR** is like the **status board on the main wall** showing current conditions, while each specialist bench has its own **mini status clipboard (SPSR)** to remember the main-board state before switching.

This is precisely the **register banking** concept: the **same architectural register names** refer to **different physical registers** depending on the processor's current **mode bits** in the CPSR.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Highlighted)

| Metric | Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Total registers** | **37** | 31 GPR + 6 Status |
| **Register width** | **32 bits** | All registers |
| **User-visible GPRs** | **15** | R0–R14 |
| **Program Counter** | **R15** | Holds current instruction address + 8 |
| **Banked GPRs (FIQ)** | **7** | R8_fiq–R14_fiq |
| **Banked GPRs (others)** | **2** | R13_mode, R14_mode |
| **Banked SPSRs** | **5** | One per privileged mode |
| **Processor modes** | **7** | 1 User + 6 Privileged |
| **Pipeline stages** | **3** | Fetch, Decode, Execute |
| **Condition flags** | **4** | N, Z, C, V |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 37 Registers — Layered Architecture

ARM7 register architecture is **layered** into three conceptual tiers:

> [!NOTE]
> **Tier 1 — Unbanked Registers (Always Visible)**
> Registers **R0–R7** are *unbanked* — they refer to the **same physical register** across **all processor modes**. They are used for general data operations.
>
> **Tier 2 — Partially Banked Registers**
> Registers **R8–R12** have a **single physical copy in User/System/IRQ/Supervisor/Abort/Undefined** modes, but **FIQ mode has separate banked copies R8_fiq–R12_fiq**. This is because FIQ is designed for **fast interrupt servicing** and needs to preserve user-state registers rapidly.
>
> **Tier 3 — Fully Banked Registers**
> Registers **R13 (SP)** and **R14 (LR)** are **physically distinct for every privileged mode**. Each privileged mode has its own **R13_mode** and **R14_mode**.

## 2.2 The Seven Processor Modes (M[4:0] bits in CPSR)

| Mode | M[4:0] | Use Case | Banked Registers |
|---|---|---|---|
| **User (USR)** | `10000` | Normal program execution | None (uses USR bank) |
| **FIQ** | `10001` | Fast Interrupt | R8–R14, SPSR |
| **IRQ** | `10010` | Normal Interrupt | R13, R14, SPSR |
| **Supervisor (SVC)** | `10011` | SWI / Reset | R13, R14, SPSR |
| **Abort (ABT)** | `10111` | Memory access failure | R13, R14, SPSR |
| **Undefined (UND)** | `11011` | Undefined instruction trap | R13, R14, SPSR |
| **System (SYS)** | `11111` | Privileged OS tasks | None (uses USR bank) |

## 2.3 CPSR / SPSR — Bit-by-Bit Anatomy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **CPSR (Current Program Status Register)** is the most critical status register. Its 32 bits are partitioned as follows:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CPSR} &= \underbrace{N\,Z\,C\,V}_{\text{[31:28] Condition Flags}} \;\underbrace{\_}_{\text{[27:25] Reserved}} \;\underbrace{J}_{\text{[24] Jazelle}} \;\underbrace{\_}_{\text{[23:20] Reserved}} \;\underbrace{I\,F\,T}_{\text{[7:6,5] Control}} \;\underbrace{M[4:0]}_{\text{[4:0] Mode}} \\
&\quad \text{(Bits [19:8] are architecturally reserved / unused on ARM7TDMI)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 2.3.1 Condition Flag Bits (CPSR[31:28])

| Flag | Bit | Set When | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **N — Negative** | 31 | ALU result MSB = 1 | Signed result < 0 |
| **Z — Zero** | 30 | ALU result = 0 | Equality / exhaustion |
| **C — Carry** | 29 | Carry-out of MSB / no borrow | Unsigned overflow |
| **V — Overflow** | 28 | Signed overflow detected | Signed overflow flag |

### 2.3.2 Control Bits (CPSR[7:0])

| Bit | Name | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **I** | IRQ disable | 1 = masks IRQ interrupts |
| **F** | FIQ disable | 1 = masks FIQ interrupts |
| **T** | Thumb state | 1 = executing Thumb (16-bit) instructions |
| **M[4:0]** | Mode bits | Selects one of 7 processor modes |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Equation / Rule | Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total registers | $N_{\text{total}} = 31_{\text{GPR}} + 6_{\text{status}} = 37$ | ARM7TDMI fixed |
| 2 | Register width | $W = 32 \text{ bits}$ | All registers |
| 3 | User-visible GPRs | $R_{\text{user}} = 15 \; (R0 \text{ to } R14)$ | Excludes R15 = PC |
| 4 | Mode count | $M_{\text{count}} = 7$ | 1 user + 6 privileged |
| 5 | Banked SPSR count | $\text{SPSR} = 5$ | One per non-User non-System mode |
| 6 | FIQ banked extras | $\Delta_{\text{FIQ}} = 7$ | R8_fiq, R9_fiq, R10_fiq, R11_fiq, R12_fiq, R13_fiq, R14_fiq |
| 7 | PC read value | $\text{PC}_{\text{read}} = \text{Current instr addr} + 8$ | Due to 3-stage pipeline |
| 8 | Condition flags | $C_{\text{flags}} = 4 \; (N, Z, C, V)$ | MSByte of CPSR |
| 9 | CPSR reserved bits | $R_{\text{reserved}} = 27$ | Should be preserved on write |
| 10 | Pipeline depth | $P_{\text{depth}} = 3$ | Fetch, Decode, Execute |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Embedded RTOS Design:** ARM7 register banking enables **zero-latency context switching** — the OS simply switches mode bits, and the **banked R13/R14** automatically isolate the new task's stack and return address. No explicit register-save is required for SP/LR.
> - **Interrupt Latency Optimization:** The **FIQ mode's extra 5 banked registers (R8_fiq–R12_fiq)** allow an FIQ handler to begin processing **without saving user registers** — critical for deterministic real-time systems like **automotive braking controllers**.
> - **Compiler Code Generation:** GCC/Keil back-ends use **R0–R3 for argument passing**, **R4–R11 as callee-saved**, **R12 (IP) for intra-procedure scratch**, and **R13/R14/R15 for stack/LR/PC** — a fixed **ARM ABI (Procedure Call Standard — AAPCS)**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Total Register Count of ARM7

We are asked by the KTU examiner frequently: *"How many registers does ARM7 have and why exactly 37?"*

### Step 1 — Identify Register Categories

The ARM7 architecture exposes three categories of registers to the programmer:

$$
C = \{C_{\text{GPR}},\; C_{\text{status}}\}
$$

where $C_{\text{GPR}}$ = general-purpose registers and $C_{\text{status}}$ = program status registers.

### Step 2 — Enumerate GPRs

GPRs exist in two forms: **unbanked (shared across modes)** and **banked (mode-specific)**.

**Unbanked R0–R7 (always shared):**

$$
N_{\text{unbanked}} = 8
$$

**R8–R12** are unbanked across **User, System, IRQ, Supervisor, Abort, Undefined** (6 modes) but **banked in FIQ mode**:

$$
N_{R8\text{–}R12}^{\text{shared}} = 5 \;\text{registers} \times 6 \;\text{modes} = 30
$$

$$
N_{R8\text{–}R12}^{\text{FIQ banked}} = 5 \;\text{registers} \times 1 \;\text{mode} = 5
$$

**R13 (SP) and R14 (LR)** are **banked in all 6 privileged modes** (User/System share one bank):

$$
N_{R13,R14}^{\text{shared}} = 2 \;\text{registers} \times 2 \;\text{modes} = 4 \quad (\text{User + System})
$$

$$
N_{R13,R14}^{\text{priv banked}} = 2 \;\text{registers} \times 6 \;\text{privileged modes} = 12
$$

**R15 (PC)** is **never banked** — only **1** instance exists.

$$
N_{\text{PC}} = 1
$$

### Step 3 — Enumerate Status Registers

- **CPSR** — 1 register (active in all modes)
- **SPSR** — 1 per privileged mode, **5** privileged modes that have an SPSR (FIQ, IRQ, SVC, ABT, UND):

$$
N_{\text{SPSR}} = 5
$$

### Step 4 — Total Physical Registers

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{total}} &= N_{\text{unbanked}} + N_{R8\text{–}R12}^{\text{shared}} + N_{R8\text{–}R12}^{\text{FIQ banked}} \\
&\quad + N_{R13,R14}^{\text{shared}} + N_{R13,R14}^{\text{priv banked}} + N_{\text{PC}} + N_{\text{CPSR}} + N_{\text{SPSR}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
N_{\text{total}} = 8 + 30 + 5 + 4 + 12 + 1 + 1 + 5
$$

$$
N_{\text{total}} = 66 \;\text{physical 32-bit storage locations}
$$

### Step 5 — The 37 (Architectural) Register Count

Wait — the **architectural count is 37**, not 66. This is because the *same physical register is reused* across multiple modes for R0–R7, R8–R12 (non-FIQ), and R13/R14 (User/System).

The KTU exam-relevant derivation:

$$
N_{\text{architectural}} = N_{\text{always visible to User}} + N_{\text{banked extras}}
$$

User mode sees R0–R14 (15) + R15/PC (1) + CPSR (1) = **17**.

Additional banked registers **not visible to User**:
- R8_fiq–R12_fiq = **5**
- R13_fiq–R14_fiq = **2**
- R13_irq, R14_irq = **2**
- R13_svc, R14_svc = **2**
- R13_abt, R14_abt = **2**
- R13_und, R14_und = **2**
- SPSR_fiq, SPSR_irq, SPSR_svc, SPSR_abt, SPSr_und = **5**

$$
N_{\text{architectural}} = 17 + (5 + 2 + 2 + 2 + 2 + 2) + 5 = 17 + 15 + 5 = 37
$$

$$
\boxed{N_{\text{architectural}}^{\text{ARM7}} = 37}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation — PC Read Offset of +8

ARM7 uses a **3-stage pipeline**: Fetch → Decode → Execute.

At the time the PC is read by an instruction in the **Execute stage**, the CPU has already fetched **2 instructions ahead**.

$$
\text{PC}_{\text{read}} = \text{Address of executing instruction} + 2 \times 4 = \text{Addr} + 8
$$

(Each instruction is 32 bits = **4 bytes** in ARM state; pipeline depth = 3 → 2 ahead = +8 bytes).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mode vs. Banked Registers

| Mode | R0–R7 | R8–R12 | R13 | R14 | R15 | SPSR |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **User** | Shared | Shared | USR | USR | PC | None |
| **FIQ** | Shared | **FIQ-banked (5)** | **FIQ** | **FIQ** | PC | **SPSR_fiq** |
| **IRQ** | Shared | Shared | **IRQ** | **IRQ** | PC | **SPSR_irq** |
| **Supervisor** | Shared | Shared | **SVC** | **SVC** | PC | **SPSR_svc** |
| **Abort** | Shared | Shared | **ABT** | **ABT** | PC | **SPSR_abt** |
| **Undefined** | Shared | Shared | **UND** | **UND** | PC | **SPSR_und** |
| **System** | Shared | Shared | USR | USR | PC | None |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Style

**Q:** An ARM7 processor is in **Supervisor mode** executing `MOV R0, SP`. What is the actual physical register accessed when SP is read?

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Identify current mode from CPSR M[4:0] = `10011` (Supervisor).

Step 2 — From the banking table: in Supervisor mode, R13 refers to the **R13_svc** banked register.

Step 3 — Therefore:

$$
\text{SP}_{\text{SVC mode}} \equiv R13_{\text{svc}} \neq R13_{\text{user}}
$$

Step 4 — If the program then switches to **User mode** (by clearing CPSR mode bits) and reads SP, it gets a **different physical register** $R13_{\text{user}}$. The two SPs are **completely independent** — writing to one does not affect the other.

$$
\boxed{\text{Result: Physical register } R13_{\text{svc}} \text{ is accessed.}}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — ARM7 Register File Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MODE[Current Mode Decoder via CPSR M 4 0]
        A[Mode Bits 10000 User]
        B[Mode Bits 10001 FIQ]
        C[Mode Bits 10010 IRQ]
        D[Mode Bits 10011 Supervisor]
        E[Mode Bits 10111 Abort]
        F[Mode Bits 11011 Undefined]
        G[Mode Bits 11111 System]
    end

    subgraph UNBANKED[Unbanked Registers Shared Across All Modes]
        R0[R0]
        R1[R1]
        R2[R2]
        R3[R3]
        R4[R4]
        R5[R5]
        R6[R6]
        R7[R7]
    end

    subgraph PARTBANK[Partially Banked R8 to R12]
        SHARED[R8 to R12 Shared by User System IRQ SVC ABT UND]
        FIQBANK[R8 fiq to R12 fiq Banked for FIQ Only]
    end

    subgraph FULLBANK[Fully Banked R13 SP and R14 LR]
        USRBANK[R13 USR and R14 USR]
        SVCBANK[R13 SVC R14 SVC]
        IRQBANK[R13 IRQ R14 IRQ]
        ABORTBANK[R13 ABT R14 ABT]
        UNDBANK[R13 UND R14 UND]
        FIQSBANK[R13 FIQ R14 FIQ]
    end

    PC[R15 Program Counter]
    CPSR[Current Program Status Register]
    SPSR[Banked SPSR one per privileged mode x5]

    MODE --> UNBANKED
    MODE --> PARTBANK
    MODE --> FULLBANK
    UNBANKED --> PC
    FULLBANK --> CPSR
    CPSR --> SPSR
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — CPSR Bit Layout

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CPSR_LAYOUT[CPSR 32 bit Layout]
        N[Bit 31 N Negative Flag]
        Z[Bit 30 Z Zero Flag]
        C[Bit 29 C Carry Flag]
        V[Bit 28 V Overflow Flag]
        RES1[Bits 27 to 25 Reserved]
        J[Bit 24 J Jazelle]
        RES2[Bits 23 to 8 Reserved]
        I[Bit 7 I IRQ Disable]
        F[Bit 6 F FIQ Disable]
        T[Bit 5 T Thumb State]
        M4[Bit 4 M4]
        M3[Bit 3 M3]
        M2[Bit 2 M2]
        M1[Bit 1 M1]
        M0[Bit 0 M0]
    end

    N --> Z --> C --> V --> RES1 --> J --> RES2 --> I --> F --> T --> M4 --> M3 --> M2 --> M1 --> M0
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart — Exception Entry and Register Banking

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User Mode Program
    participant CP as CPSR
    participant HW as Hardware Exception Logic
    participant NM as New Privileged Mode
    participant SP as Banked SP
    participant LR as Banked LR
    participant SP_Reg as SPSR

    U->>CP: Read current mode = 10000
    HW-->>U: IRQ signal arrives
    HW->>CP: Set M 4 0 to 10010 IRQ mode
    HW->>SP_Reg: SPSR irq = CPSR copy
    HW->>LR: LR irq = return address PC plus 4
    HW->>SP: Switch to R13 irq banked stack
    HW->>NM: Jump to IRQ vector at 0x18
    NM->>NM: Service interrupt
    NM->>CP: Restore CPSR from SPSR irq
    NM->>CP: Mode bits back to 10000 User
    NM->>U: Resume original program
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📚 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**List the registers visible in User mode of an ARM7 processor. State the function of R13, R14, and R15.**

**Model Answer:**

The following **15 general-purpose registers** are visible in User mode of ARM7:

| Register | Function |
|---|---|
| **R0 – R12** | General-purpose data / scratch registers |
| **R13 (SP)** | **Stack Pointer** — points to the top of the active stack |
| **R14 (LR)** | **Link Register** — stores the return address after a subroutine call (BL) or exception |
| **R15 (PC)** | **Program Counter** — holds the address of the next instruction to be executed |

Additionally, the **CPSR (Current Program Status Register)** is also visible in User mode for read access.

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is the difference between CPSR and SPSR in ARM7? How many SPSRs exist?**

**Model Answer:**

- **CPSR (Current Program Status Register):** A single **32-bit register** that always reflects the **current condition flags (N, Z, C, V)**, control bits (I, F, T), and **current processor mode bits (M[4:0])** of the processor. It is accessible in **all 7 modes**.

- **SPSR (Saved Program Status Register):** A **banked copy of CPSR** used to **preserve the CPSR value when an exception occurs**, so that on exception return, the original CPSR can be restored.

- **Number of SPSRs = 5**, one for each privileged mode that has its own banking: **FIQ, IRQ, Supervisor, Abort, Undefined**. User and System modes **do not have an SPSR**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **ARM7 processor modes** in detail. List all **7 modes** with their **mode bit values (M[4:0])** and state the **purpose** of each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, describe the **CPSR register format** of ARM7. Explain the **function of each flag bit (N, Z, C, V)** and the **control bits (I, F, T)**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a)

The ARM7 processor supports **7 operating modes**, classified into **User mode** and **6 privileged modes**.

| # | Mode | M[4:0] | Type | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **User (USR)** | `10000` | Normal | Application program execution |
| 2 | **FIQ** | `10001` | Privileged | Fast Interrupt — high-priority data transfer |
| 3 | **IRQ** | `10010` | Privileged | Normal Interrupt Request |
| 4 | **Supervisor (SVC)** | `10011` | Privileged | Software Interrupt (SWI) / OS kernel / Reset |
| 5 | **Abort (ABT)** | `10111` | Privileged | Memory access failure (instruction or data) |
| 6 | **Undefined (UND)** | `11011` | Privileged | Undefined instruction trap (emulation) |
| 7 | **System (SYS)** | `11111` | Privileged | Privileged OS tasks using User-mode registers |

**Key points:**
- Mode change occurs via **CPSR[4:0]** write or hardware exception.
- Privileged modes have **banked R13, R14** and **own SPSR**.
- **System mode** is privileged but **uses User-mode banked registers** (no SPSR).

#### Model Solution — (b)

The **CPSR is a 32-bit register** with the following structure:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CPSR}[31:28] &= N\,Z\,C\,V \quad \text{(Condition Flags)} \\
\text{CPSR}[27:25] &= \text{Reserved} \\
\text{CPSR}[24] &= J \quad \text{(Jazelle — for Jazelle extension, not on ARM7TDMI standard)} \\
\text{CPSR}[23:8] &= \text{Reserved} \\
\text{CPSR}[7] &= I \quad \text{(IRQ disable)} \\
\text{CPSR}[6] &= F \quad \text{(FIQ disable)} \\
\text{CPSR}[5] &= T \quad \text{(Thumb state)} \\
\text{CPSR}[4:0] &= M[4:0] \quad \text{(Mode bits)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Condition Flags:**
- **N (Negative) — CPSR[31]:** Set to 1 when the ALU result's MSB is 1 (i.e., signed result is negative).
- **Z (Zero) — CPSR[30]:** Set to 1 when the ALU result is zero.
- **C (Carry) — CPSR[29]:** Set to 1 on carry-out from MSB during addition, or **no borrow** during subtraction.
- **V (Overflow) — CPSR[28]:** Set to 1 on **signed overflow** (when carry-in and carry-out of MSB differ).

**Control Bits:**
- **I (CPSR[7]):** When 1, **IRQ interrupts are masked**.
- **F (CPSR[6]):** When 1, **FIQ interrupts are masked**.
- **T (CPSR[5]):** When 1, processor executes **16-bit Thumb instructions**; when 0, executes **32-bit ARM instructions**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Alternate]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **register banking** in ARM7. How many **physical registers** are mapped to the **architectural registers R0–R15**, and why is the count **37**? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **exception entry sequence** of ARM7. What happens to **CPSR, SPSR, LR, and PC** when an **IRQ exception** occurs? **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a)

**Register banking** is the technique where **multiple physical registers** are mapped to the **same architectural name** (R0–R15), and the actual physical register accessed depends on the **current processor mode** stored in CPSR[4:0].

**Banked Registers Summary:**

| Architectural Name | Physical Count | Modes Mapped |
|---|---|---|
| **R0 – R7** | 1 each = **8** | All 7 modes (shared) |
| **R8 – R12** | 2 each = **10** | 6 modes share + 1 FIQ-banked |
| **R13 (SP)** | 7 each = **7** | User+System share + 5 priv + 1 = total 7 physical |
| **R14 (LR)** | 7 each = **7** | Same as R13 |
| **R15 (PC)** | 1 = **1** | Never banked |
| **CPSR** | 1 | All modes |
| **SPSR** | 5 | FIQ, IRQ, SVC, ABT, UND |

**Total architectural count** (counting each architectural name once, plus banked extras not visible in User mode):

$$
N_{\text{arch}} = 15_{\text{GPR visible in User}} + 1_{\text{PC}} + 1_{\text{CPSR}} + 15_{\text{banked extras}} + 5_{\text{SPSR}} = 37
$$

The 15 "banked extras not visible in User" are: 5 FIQ-banked R8–R12, 2 FIQ-banked R13/R14, 2 IRQ-banked R13/R14, 2 SVC-banked R13/R14, 2 ABT-banked R13/R14, 2 UND-banked R13/R14.

#### Model Solution — (b)

When an **IRQ exception** is taken, ARM7 hardware performs the following **atomic sequence**:

**Step 1 — Banked LR update:**

$$
\text{LR}_{\text{IRQ}} = \text{PC} + 4
$$

(PC + 4 is the address of the instruction that was **not yet executed**; the +4 accounts for the pipeline.)

**Step 2 — SPSR save:**

$$
\text{SPSR}_{\text{IRQ}} = \text{CPSR}_{\text{current}}
$$

(The current CPSR — including User mode and condition flags — is preserved.)

**Step 3 — CPSR modification:**

$$
\text{CPSR}[4:0] \leftarrow 10010 \quad (\text{IRQ mode bits})
$$

$$
\text{CPSR}[7] \leftarrow 1 \quad (\text{Disable further IRQs — automatic masking})
$$

$$
\text{CPSR}[5] \leftarrow 0 \quad (\text{Force ARM state, not Thumb})
$$

**Step 4 — PC update (vectoring):**

$$
\text{PC} \leftarrow 0x00000018 \quad (\text{IRQ vector address})
$$

**On exception return (using `SUBS PC, LR, #4`):**
- The PC is restored from LR_irq (minus 4 to skip the aborted instruction).
- CPSR is atomically restored from SPSR_irq, simultaneously re-enabling the previous mode and flags.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing 37 vs. 66:** Many students state "ARM7 has **66** physical registers". The KTU textbook reference uses **37 architectural** registers. Mention **both** in your answer with the distinction.
>
> 2. **PC Read offset:** Students write "PC = current instruction address" — **WRONG**. Due to 3-stage pipeline, `PC = current_instruction + 8` when read, but `PC = current_instruction + 4` when saved into LR during a branch.
>
> 3. **SPSR count:** Writing "6 SPSRs" is incorrect. It is **5** (User and System have **no SPSR**).
>
> 4. **Mode bit value errors:** The modes `10111` (Abort) and `11011` (Undefined) are often misquoted. Memorize them correctly.
>
> 5. **Forgetting reserved bits:** Modifying CPSR reserved bits (e.g., [27:25], [23:8]) can cause **unpredictable behavior** in some ARM7 cores. Use **read-modify-write with mask** (e.g., `MRS` / `MSR` instructions).
>
> 6. **FIQ banking extent:** FIQ banks **R8–R14** (7 registers), **not just R13/R14** like other privileged modes. This is a frequent high-value exam point.

---

## 5.4 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ ARM7 has **37 architectural registers** = 31 GPR + 6 status (1 CPSR + 5 SPSR).
- ✅ All registers are **32 bits wide**.
- ✅ **R0–R7 are unbanked** (shared across all 7 modes).
- ✅ **R8–R12 are partially banked** — only FIQ has its own copies.
- ✅ **R13 (SP) and R14 (LR) are fully banked** in every privileged mode.
- ✅ **R15 (PC)** reads as **current + 8**; branches save **current + 4** into LR.
- ✅ **7 modes** total: 1 User + 6 Privileged (FIQ, IRQ, SVC, ABT, UND, SYS).
- ✅ **CPSR[4:0] = M[4:0]** = mode select bits; **CPSR[7,6,5] = I, F, T** control bits.
- ✅ **CPSR[31:28] = N, Z, C, V** condition flags.
- ✅ **5 SPSRs** exist (one per FIQ/IRQ/SVC/ABT/UND); User and System have **no SPSR**.
- ✅ **FIQ mode banks 7 extra registers** (R8_fiq–R14_fiq) for **fast context save** — this is why ARM chose FIQ for time-critical interrupts.
- ✅ **System mode** is privileged but uses User-mode register bank — useful for OS tasks that need privileged access without SPSR overhead.
- ✅ Exception entry sequence: **LR_mode = PC + 4 → SPSR_mode = CPSR → CPSR[4:0] = new mode → PC = vector address**.
- ✅ Use **MRS** to read CPSR/SPSR and **MSR** to write them — never use arithmetic on them directly.
- ✅ Pipeline depth = 3 (Fetch, Decode, Execute) — explains the +8 PC read offset.
- ✅ ARM ABI (AAPCS): R0–R3 = arguments + return, R4–R11 = callee-saved, R12 = IP scratch, R13 = SP, R14 = LR, R15 = PC.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ARM Programmer's Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMRT403 - COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE AND MICROCONTROLLER > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** ARM Programmer's Model # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **ARM Programmer's Model** is the architectural abstraction that specifies the **complete set of registers, processor operating modes, status registers, data types, and memory organization** that are visible and accessible to the software (programmer/compiler). It defines the "contract" between the hardware and the instruction set, independent of the physical implementation. According to the ARM Architecture Reference Model (ARMv4T / ARM7TDMI, as prescribed for PCMRT403 Module 4), the Programmer's Model consists of **37 registers** grouped into: - **31 general-purpose 32-bit registers** (with banked duplicates). - **6 status registers** — **1 CPSR** (Current Program Status Register) + **5 SPSRs** (Saved Program Status Registers). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bank Teller Window" Imagine a bank with **37 lockers** (`registers`) and **7 service windows** (`processor modes`). Most lockers (`R0–R7`) are visible to every teller, but a few lockers (`R8–R14`) have *duplicate spare keys* kept under different counters — these are the **banked registers**. When a teller temporarily switches counters (e.g., from User to IRQ mode), they take the spare keys so the previous teller's work-in-progress is not disturbed. The **CPSR** is the *public notice board* showing flags like "open/closed" (`N`,`Z`,`C`,`V`) and "which window is active" (`Mode bits`). Every time a teller switches window, they file their old notice board into an **SPSR** drawer so they can restore it later. ## 1.3 Key Architectural Constants - **General-purpose registers:** 31 × 32-bit - **Status registers:** 6 × 32-bit (1 CPSR + 5 SPSR) - **Processor modes:** **7** (User, FIQ, IRQ, Supervisor, Abort, Undefined, System) - **Pipeline:** **3-stage** (Fetch → Decode → Execute) - **Register width:** **32 bits** (`word`) - **PC (R15)** always points **2 instructions (8 bytes) ahead** in ARM state. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** "Architecture – ARM programmer's model, Registers, Modes, Exception handling." You will be tested on the *mapping* between modes and banked registers, and the *bit-layout of CPSR/SPSR*. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The 7 Processor Modes ARM operates in exactly **one of 7 modes** at any instant. The current mode is encoded in **CPSR[4:0]**. | # | Mode | Encoding (M[4:0]) | Privilege | Typical Trigger | |---|------|-------------------|-----------|-----------------| | 1 | User (`usr`) | `10000` | Unprivileged | Normal application code | | 2 | FIQ (`fiq`) | `10001` | Privileged | High-priority interrupt | | 3 | IRQ (`irq`) | `10010` | Privileged | Normal interrupt | | 4 | Supervisor (`svc`) | `10011` | Privileged | Reset / SWI instruction | | 5 | Abort (`abt`) | `10111` | Privileged | Memory access failure | | 6 | Undefined (`und`) | `11011` | Privileged | Undefined instruction | | 7 | System (`sys`) | `11111` | Privileged | OS tasks (uses User regs) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Unprivileged** modes (only **User**) cannot write to CPSR control fields (`I`,`F`,`T`) or switch mode directly. All exception modes are **privileged**. ## 2.2 Register File — The 37 Registers ARM provides **30 unbanked + 7 banked** logical registers visible at any time, but physically stores **37** register locations. | Register Group | Count | Notes | |----------------|-------|-------| | `R0 – R7` (unbanked) | 8 | Shared across **all** modes | | `R8 – R12` | 5 | Two physical copies: **User-banked** + **FIQ-banked** | | `R13` (SP), `R14` (LR) | 2 | Each privileged mode has its own banked copy | | `R15` (PC) | 1 | **Not banked** — global | | `CPSR` | 1 | One per mode (global) | | `SPSR_` | 5 | One per privileged mode except User/System | **Convention (ARM ABI / AAPCS):** - `R13` → **Stack Pointer (SP)** - `R14` → **Link Register (LR)** — stores return address on branch-with-link. - `R15` → **Program Counter (PC)** — current instruction address + **8** (in ARM state). ## 2.3 CPSR / SPSR Bit Layout (ARM7TDMI) The CPSR is a **32-bit** register with **8 architecturally-defined fields**. | Bit(s) | Field | Purpose | |--------|-------|---------| | 31 | **N** | Negative / Sign flag | | 30 | **Z** | Zero flag | | 29 | **C** | Carry / Borrow flag | | 28 | **V** | Overflow flag | | 27–8 | Reserved | — | | 7 | **I** | IRQ disable (1 = masked) | | 6 | **F** | FIQ disable (1 = masked) | | 5 | **T** | Thumb state (1 = Thumb, 0 = ARM) | | 4–0 | **M[4:0]** | Processor mode | **Symbolic representation:** $$ \text{CPSR} = \underbrace{N\,Z\,C\,V}_{\text{condition flags (31:28)}}\;\underbrace{00000000\,00000000\,0}_{\text{reserved (27:8)}}\;\underbrace{I\,F\,T}_{\text{control (7:5)}}\;\underbrace{M_4 M_3 M_2 M_1 M_0}_{\text{mode (4:0)}} $$ ## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Rule | Use | |---|----------------|-----| | 1 | $\text{PC}_{\text{effective}} = \text{PC}_{\text{current}} + 8$ | Reading R15 in ARM state | | 2 | $\text{Mode}_{\text{encode}} = M_4 \cdot 16 + M_3 \cdot 8 + M_2 \cdot 4 + M_1 \cdot 2 + M_0$ | CPSR[4:0] | | 3 | $N = \text{bit}_{31}(\text{result})$, $Z = (\text{result} == 0)$ | Status flag update | | 4 | $\text{Stack growth} \rightarrow \text{LR}_{\text{save}} = \text{PC}_{\text{next}}$ | On exception entry | | 5 | $\text{VectorAddr} = 0x00000000 + 4 \cdot \text{offset}_{\text{mode}}$ | ARM vector table | | 6 | $\text{Return} = \text{LR} - 4 \;\;(\text{SUBS PC, LR, } \#4)$ | Standard exception return | | 7 | $\text{Total registers} = 8_{\text{user}} + 5_{\text{FIQ}} + 6 \cdot 2_{\text{other}} + 1_{\text{PC}} = 31$ | Counting GRs | > [!NOTE] > **Real-world utility:** This model is implemented identically across billions of cores — from a Cortex-M0 in a washing machine to the Cortex-A72 in your smartphone. The *programmer sees the same R0–R15*, ensuring portability of low-level firmware and RTOS code. ## 2.5 Banked Register Mapping Matrix | Mode | R13 | R14 | R8–R12 | SPSR | |------|-----|-----|--------|------| | User | R13_usr | R14_usr | R8_usr–R12_usr | — | | FIQ | R13_fiq | R14_fiq | **R8_fiq–R12_fiq** | SPSR_fiq | | IRQ | R13_irq | R14_irq | R8_usr–R12_usr | SPSR_irq | | SVC | R13_svc | R14_svc | R8_usr–R12_usr | SPSR_svc | | ABT | R13_abt | R14_abt | R8_usr–R12_usr | SPSR_abt | | UND | R13_und | R14_und | R8_usr–R12_usr | SPSR_und | | System | R13_usr | R14_usr | R8_usr–R12_usr | — | # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Total Number of Physical Registers ARM exposes 37 physical 32-bit registers. The derivation is shown below. $$ \begin{aligned} N_{\text{regs}} &= \underbrace{8}_{R0\!-\!R7} + \underbrace{(5 + 5)}_{R8\!-\!R12,\;2\text{ copies (User+FIQ)}} \\ &\quad + \underbrace{(2 \times 6)}_{R13,R14\text{ banked across 6 modes (User+5 privileged but System reuses User)}} \\ &\quad + \underbrace{1}_{R15 / PC} + \underbrace{1}_{CPSR} + \underbrace{5}_{\text{SPSR (1 per privileged mode)}} \\[4pt] &= 8 + 10 + 12 + 1 + 1 + 5 \\ &= 37 \end{aligned} $$ **Logical Step-by-Step Explanation:** - **Step 1:** `R0–R7` are *never* banked → always 8 physical registers. - **Step 2:** `R8–R12` exist in two physical sets (User/System + FIQ) → 5 + 5 = 10. - **Step 3:** `R13` and `R14` are banked across the User and 5 privileged modes (System reuses User's, so do not double-count) → 2 regs × 6 modes = 12. - **Step 4:** `R15` (PC) is global → 1. - **Step 5:** One CPSR is always present → 1. - **Step 6:** SPSR exists in each of FIQ, IRQ, SVC, ABT, UND (5 copies) → 5. - **Sum:** 8 + 10 + 12 + 1 + 1 + 5 = **37 registers**. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — CPSR Encoding for "Supervisor Mode" The mode field `M[4:0]` must be set to `10011` (binary) to enter Supervisor mode. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Decimal value} &= 1 \cdot 2^4 + 0 \cdot 2^3 + 0 \cdot 2^2 + 1 \cdot 2^1 + 1 \cdot 2^0 \\ &= 16 + 0 + 0 + 2 + 1 \\ &= 19_{\text{dec}} \end{aligned} $$ So `CPSR[4:0] = 0b10011 = 0x13`. This is exactly the constant used by the assembler directive `CPS #19` and the reset vector's `MOV`/`MSR` sequence. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Reading PC at Run-Time A 14-mark favourite. When the ARM core fetches instruction at address `A`, `R15` reads back as `A + 8` (because of the 3-stage pipeline: two instructions have been pre-fetched ahead). $$ \begin{aligned} A &= 0x00008000 \quad \text{(address of current instruction)}\\ \text{Read } R15 \rightarrow A + 8 &= 0x00008000 + 0x08 = 0x00008008 \end{aligned} $$ **Why "+8"?** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Stage 1 (Fetch)} &: \text{PC}_{\text{F}} = A \\ \text{Stage 2 (Decode)} &: \text{PC}_{\text{D}} = A + 4 \\ \text{Stage 3 (Execute)} &: \text{PC}_{\text{E}} = A + 8 \\ \text{Visible to programmer} &: \text{R15} = A + 8 \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Condition Flag Update for an `ADD` Given `R0 = 0x7FFFFFF0`, `R1 = 0x00000020`, execute `ADDS R2, R0, R1`. $$ \begin{aligned} R2 &= 0x7FFFFFF0 + 0x00000020 \\ &= 0x80000010 \\ \end{aligned} $$ Now compute flags: $$ \begin{aligned} N &= \text{bit}_{31}(0x80000010) = 1 \quad &\text{(negative / signed)}\\ Z &= (0x80000010 == 0) = 0 \\ C &= (\text{unsigned overflow out of bit 31}) = 0 \quad &\text{(lost carry)}\\ V &= (\text{signed overflow into bit 31}) = 1 \quad &\text{(positive + positive = negative)} \end{aligned} $$ So after execution, `CPSR = 0x80000000 | 0x10000000 | ... = 0x90000000` (assuming all control bits and mode unchanged). ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Mode Trigger and Vector Table | Exception / Mode | Vector Address | Mode Encoding | SPSR Saved? | Banked R13/R14? | |------------------|---------------:|--------------:|:-----------:|:---------------:| | Reset | `0x00000000` | SVC (`10011`) | No (forced value) | Yes | | Undefined Instruction | `0x00000004` | UND (`11011`) | Yes | Yes | | Software Interrupt (SWI) | `0x00000008` | SVC (`10011`) | Yes | Yes | | Prefetch Abort | `0x0000000C` | ABT (`10111`) | Yes | Yes | | Data Abort | `0x00000010` | ABT (`10111`) | Yes | Yes | | IRQ | `0x00000018` | IRQ (`10010`) | Yes | Yes | | FIQ | `0x0000001C` | FIQ (`10001`) | Yes | Yes | > [!IMPORTANT] > Vector addresses are spaced **4 bytes apart** (one ARM instruction). FIQ vector is *last* intentionally — it allows the handler to be placed at `0x1C` and continue executing *without needing a branch*, saving one cycle on the most latency-critical interrupt. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 ARM Register File (Banked Layout) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph USR["USER MODE 0b10000"] U_R0["R0"]:::gpr U_R1["R1"]:::gpr U_R7["R7"]:::gpr U_R8U["R8_usr"]:::user U_R12U["R12_usr"]:::user U_R13U["R13_usr / SP"]:::user U_R14U["R14_usr / LR"]:::user U_R15["R15 / PC"]:::pc end subgraph FIQ["FIQ MODE 0b10001"] F_R8["R8_fiq"]:::fiq F_R9["R9_fiq"]:::fiq F_R12["R12_fiq"]:::fiq F_R13["R13_fiq / SP"]:::fiq F_R14["R14_fiq / LR"]:::fiq F_SPSR["SPSR_fiq"]:::spsr end subgraph IRQ["IRQ MODE 0b10010"] I_R13["R13_irq / SP"]:::priv I_R14["R14_irq / LR"]:::priv I_SPSR["SPSR_irq"]:::spsr end subgraph SVC["SVC MODE 0b10011"] S_R13["R13_svc / SP"]:::priv S_R14["R14_svc / LR"]:::priv S_SPSR["SPSR_svc"]:::spsr end subgraph ABT["ABT MODE 0b10111"] A_R13["R13_abt / SP"]:::priv A_R14["R14_abt / LR"]:::priv A_SPSR["SPSR_abt"]:::spsr end subgraph UND["UND MODE 0b11011"] Un_R13["R13_und / SP"]:::priv Un_R14["R14_und / LR"]:::priv Un_SPSR["SPSR_und"]:::spsr end CPSR["CPSR Current Status"]:::cpsr classDef gpr fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000; classDef user fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000; classDef fiq fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000; classDef priv fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000; classDef pc fill:#e2d9f3,stroke:#6f42c1,color:#000; classDef spsr fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#d63384,color:#000; classDef cpsr fill:#212529,stroke:#000,color:#fff; ``` ## 4.2 ARM Core Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR REG["Register Bank
37 x 32-bit"]:::reg DEC["Instruction
Decode"]:::logic EXE["ALU / Execute
(Barrel Shifter + MAC)"]:::logic MEM["Memory
Interface"]:::bus MUL["Multiplier
(32x8 -> 40-bit)"]:::logic CPSR_BLK["CPSR / SPSR
Bank"]:::status REG -->|read operands| EXE EXE -->|write back| REG EXE -->|flags N Z C V| CPSR_BLK CPSR_BLK -->|mode bits M4-M0| REG DEC -->|control signals| EXE EXE -->|load/store addr| MEM MEM -->|data| EXE EXE -->|MAC result| MUL MUL -->|lo/hi 32-bit| REG classDef reg fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000; classDef logic fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000; classDef bus fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000; classDef status fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000; ``` ## 4.3 Exception Entry & Return Sequence ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant U as User Mode participant HW as ARM Core participant N as New Privileged Mode U->>HW: Execute SWI / IRQ fires HW->>HW: Save next PC into LR_new HW->>HW: Copy CPSR -> SPSR_new HW->>HW: Force CPSR mode bits, I=1, F=1 HW->>HW: PC = vector_base + offset HW->>N: Begin executing handler N->>HW: Handler pushes {LR, SPSR} on stack HW->>U: Restore via SUBS PC, LR, #4 (or #8 for FIQ/IRQ) ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Explain the role of the CPSR in the ARM programmer's model. List its control bits and their functions.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Current Program Status Register (CPSR)** holds the live condition flags and control information of the ARM core. It is a 32-bit register divided into: - **Condition flags [31:28]** — `N` (Negative), `Z` (Zero), `C` (Carry), `V` (Overflow): set by arithmetic/logic ops. - **Control bits [7:5]** — `I` (mask IRQ), `F` (mask FIQ), `T` (Thumb state). - **Mode bits [4:0]** — `M[4:0]`: encode the current processor mode (7 modes). On every exception entry, CPSR is *copied* into the banked **SPSR** of the new mode and a new value is forced. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for flags list, 1 for control/mode bits.)* --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Why does the ARM processor have a separate SPSR for each privileged mode?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** When the processor switches from User mode to a privileged mode (e.g., IRQ), the **current CPSR** must be preserved because the User-mode program may need to know which flags were set before the exception. Each privileged mode therefore has its own **Saved Program Status Register (SPSR_xxx)**. On exception *entry*, `SPSR_new = CPSR_old`. On *return*, `CPSR ← SPSR_new`, and the original CPSR is restored exactly. This guarantees **atomic state preservation** during nested interrupts and OS calls. *(3 marks: 1 for concept, 1 for entry-save rule, 1 for return-restore rule.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice Pattern) > [!NOTE] > KTU 2024 Scheme Part-B in ESE offers **internal choice within a module**. Below are **two independent 14-mark questions** on the ARM Programmer's Model — answer **either** for full credit. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Module 4]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Draw and explain the complete ARM Programmer's Model showing all 37 registers. Describe how registers are banked across the 7 processor modes. **(b) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the CPSR format. How are the mode bits encoded? Show, with calculation, the binary value of CPSR[4:0] for "Supervisor mode" and "IRQ mode". #### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]** 1. **Introduction (1 mark):** ARM exposes **37 physical 32-bit registers** to the programmer: 31 general-purpose + 6 status. 2. **Unbanked registers (2 marks):** `R0–R7` are shared across **all** modes. `R15` (PC) is also global and reads as `PC + 8` due to pipelining. 3. **Banked registers (2 marks):** `R8–R12` exist in two physical sets — the *User copy* and the *FIQ copy*. The FIQ mode has dedicated `R8_fiq–R14_fiq` so its handler can run **without saving** those registers, giving it the lowest latency. 4. **Per-mode SP/LR (1 mark):** Each privileged mode has its own `R13_` (SP) and `R14_` (LR). System mode reuses User's. 5. **Status registers (1 mark):** One global `CPSR` + five banked `SPSR_` for FIQ, IRQ, SVC, ABT, UND. (See Section 4.1 mermaid diagram for the complete schematic.) #### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]** 1. **CPSR bit layout (3 marks):** Provide table — `N[31]`, `Z[30]`, `C[29]`, `V[28]`, reserved, `I[7]`, `F[6]`, `T[5]`, `M[4:0]`. 2. **Mode encoding rule (1 mark):** `M[4:0]` is a 5-bit unsigned integer selecting one of the 7 modes. 3. **Calculation for Supervisor mode (2 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{SVC mode} &= 0b10011 \\ &= 1\cdot 2^4 + 0\cdot 2^3 + 0\cdot 2^2 + 1\cdot 2^1 + 1\cdot 2^0 \\ &= 16 + 0 + 0 + 2 + 1 \\ &= 19_{\text{dec}} = 0x13 \end{aligned} $$ 4. **Calculation for IRQ mode (1 mark):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{IRQ mode} &= 0b10010 \\ &= 16 + 2 = 18_{\text{dec}} = 0x12 \end{aligned} $$ **Final answer: SVC = `0x13`, IRQ = `0x12`.** --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Module 4]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the seven processor modes of ARM with their binary encoding and trigger conditions. Why is User mode unprivileged? **(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the exception entry and return sequence in ARM. Show how PC, CPSR, and LR/SPSR interact when an IRQ occurs, with appropriate equations. #### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]** 1. **Concept of modes (1 mark):** A mode determines which *banked registers* and *privilege level* the core uses. Only one mode is active at any instant. 2. **Enumeration of 7 modes with encoding (4 marks):** - `User (0b10000)` — unprivileged, normal code. - `FIQ (0b10001)` — fast interrupt, banked R8–R14. - `IRQ (0b10010)` — normal interrupt. - `Supervisor (0b10011)` — reset, SWI. - `Abort (0b10111)` — memory access failure. - `Undefined (0b11011)` — unknown opcode. - `System (0b11111)` — privileged, uses User registers. 3. **Trigger conditions (1 mark):** Hardware exception, SWI instruction, reset, or explicit `CPS`/`MSR` writes from privileged code. 4. **User mode unprivileged (1 mark):** User cannot write to `CPSR[7:0]` directly (control + mode bits), cannot enable/disable interrupts, and cannot access banked CPSR/SPSR. This protects OS data structures from misbehaving user applications. #### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]** 1. **Exception entry sequence (3 marks):** - $\text{LR}_{\text{new}} = \text{PC}_{\text{next}}$ (address of instruction *after* the one that caused the exception, or after the one being returned to for FIQ/IRQ with `+4` adjustment). - $\text{SPSR}_{\text{new}} = \text{CPSR}_{\text{old}}$. - $\text{CPSR}_{\text{new}} \rightarrow$ mode bits set to new mode, `I = 1` (and `F = 1` for FIQ entry), `T` preserved. - $\text{PC} = 0x00000000 + 4 \times \text{vector\_offset}$. 2. **For an IRQ** specifically: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{LR}_{\text{irq}} &= \text{PC}_{\text{next}} + 4 \\ \text{SPSR}_{\text{irq}} &= \text{CPSR}_{\text{old}} \\ \text{CPSR}[4:0] &= 0b10010 \\ \text{CPSR}[7] &= 1 \quad (\text{IRQ masked})\\ \text{PC} &= 0x00000018 \end{aligned} $$ 3. **Exception return (2 marks):** - Handler pops user state: `LDMIA SP!, {R0–R12, LR}`. - Atomically restores CPSR and PC: $$ \text{SUBS} \;\; \text{PC, LR, } \#4 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \begin{cases} \text{PC} \leftarrow \text{LR} - 4 \\ \text{CPSR} \leftarrow \text{SPSR}_{\text{irq}} \end{cases} $$ - For SWI/Data Abort the offset is `#8`; for FIQ/IRQ it is `#4`. 4. **Diagram reference (2 marks):** Refer to the sequence diagram in Section 4.3. --- > [!WARNING] > **⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Confusing R13/R14 between modes:** Many students write "R13 stores the return address" — **WRONG**. `R13 = SP`, `R14 = LR`. Mixing these costs 2 marks instantly. > 2. **Forgetting that PC = current + 8:** If you answer "R15 holds the address of the current instruction" you lose 1 mark. Always mention the pipeline offset. > 3. **Wrong exception return offset:** IRQ/FIQ use `SUBS PC, LR, #4`, but **Data Abort and SWI use `#8`**. Mixing them shows you don't understand the pipeline adjustment. > 4. **Writing CPSR fields in wrong bit order:** `N` is bit **31**, not bit 0. Examiners explicitly check this. > 5. **Missing SPSR for System/User:** There is **no SPSR** in User or System modes — saying so loses 1 mark if a question asks "total SPSRs". > 6. **Omitting banked R8–R12 in FIQ:** FIQ is the **only** mode with `R8_fiq–R12_fiq`. The whole point of FIQ is zero-overhead context save — students routinely miss this. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist - ✅ ARM has **37 registers** = 31 GPR + 6 status (1 CPSR + 5 SPSR). - ✅ **7 modes**: User, FIQ, IRQ, SVC, ABT, UND, System — encoded in **CPSR[4:0]**. - ✅ `R0–R7` are **unbanked**; `R15` is **unbanked** (global). - ✅ `R8–R12` are banked **only in FIQ** (R8_fiq–R12_fiq). - ✅ `R13 = SP`, `R14 = LR`, `R15 = PC` — memorize these three roles. - ✅ **PC reads as +8** due to 3-stage pipeline. - ✅ **CPSR fields**: `N[31]`, `Z[30]`, `C[29]`, `V[28]`, `I[7]`, `F[6]`, `T[5]`, `M[4:0]`. - ✅ **Mode encodings**: User=0x10, FIQ=0x11, IRQ=0x12, SVC=0x13, ABT=0x17, UND=0x1B, System=0x1F. - ✅ **No SPSR** for User or System modes. - ✅ Exception entry: saves `LR ← PC_next`, `SPSR ← CPSR`, forces mode + masks IRQs, sets `PC` to vector. - ✅ Exception return via `SUBS PC, LR, #4` (FIQ/IRQ) or `#8` (SWI/Abt). - ✅ Vector table base = `0x00000000`, spaced 4 bytes; FIQ at `0x1C` (last) to allow inline handler. - ✅ User mode is the **only unprivileged** mode — cannot touch CPSR control/mode bits. - ✅ FIQ has dedicated `R8_fiq–R14_fiq` → minimum context-switch overhead. > **Final Tip:** For 14-mark questions, *always* draw the **register banking diagram** (Section 4.1). Examiners reward clear visual answers with full marks.

topics.title: System Software - Assembler
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT403 - COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE AND MICROCONTROLLER
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** System Software - Assembler

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition of an Assembler**
> An **Assembler** is a piece of **system software** whose primary function is to translate a program written in **assembly language (mnemonic code)** into its equivalent **machine language (object code / relocatable binary)** that the target processor can directly execute.

The input to an assembler is the **source program** (a `.asm` or `.s` file containing mnemonics, labels, and directives), while the output is the **object program** (a `.obj` file containing binary opcodes and addresses), along with optional **listing file** and **error diagnostics**.

> [!NOTE]
> **System Software Classification** — In the KTU 2024 Scheme, the assembler is grouped along with the **Loader, Linker, Macro-processor, and Compiler** under *Language Translation Software*. Together, they form the **System Programming tool-chain**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Multilingual Diplomat"

Imagine a **diplomat** sitting between an Indian speaker (assembly language programmer) and a Japanese executive (the microprocessor, which only understands binary 0s and 1s). The diplomat translates every spoken sentence *line by line*, sometimes needs a *dictionary* to look up unfamiliar words, and occasionally keeps a *notebook* to remember names mentioned earlier.

| Diplomat's Job | Assembler's Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Hears a sentence (line) | Reads an assembly statement |
| Looks up words in dictionary | Consults **Mnemonic Opcode Table (MOT)** |
| Notes down new names | Maintains **Symbol Table (ST)** |
| Translates into Japanese | Generates **machine code (binary)** |
| Forwards a clarifying note | Resolves **forward references** |

So, the assembler is essentially a **language diplomat** that converts human-readable mnemonics into processor-readable binaries, while maintaining bookkeeping data structures.

## 1.3 Key Entities the Assembler Handles

- **Mnemonic** — A symbolic name for an operation (e.g., `MOV`, `ADD`, `JMP`, `LDA`).
- **Opcode** — The numeric binary code representing the operation (e.g., `MOV A,B` may translate to `78H`).
- **Operand** — The data on which the operation acts (`A`, `B`, `R1`, `100`, `#0AH`).
- **Label** — A symbolic name given to a memory location for later reference (e.g., `LOOP:`).
- **Directive / Pseudo-Opcode** — Instructions to the assembler itself, not translated to machine code (e.g., `ORIGIN`, `EQU`, `END`, `DC`, `DS`).
- **Literal** — A constant written literally in the operand field, whose value is computed and placed in a **literal pool** (e.g., `=5`, `=0AH`).
- **Forward Reference** — A reference to a label that is defined later in the program.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why study the Assembler in PCMRT403?**
> Understanding the assembler is essential because it reveals how **mnemonic → opcode mapping**, **symbol resolution**, and **address binding** happen at the boundary between *software abstraction* and *hardware execution*. It bridges Module 3 (Instruction Set Architecture of 8086 / microcontroller) with practical system programming.

## 1.4 Fundamental Design Challenge

> [!NOTE]
> **The Central Tension:** A label like `BACK:` may be *referenced* before it is *defined*. Without a clever strategy, the assembler cannot generate the correct machine code on a single forward scan. This forces a design choice between **One-Pass Assemblers** (fast, complex, uses back-patching) and **Two-Pass Assemblers** (simple, slower, uses intermediate file).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Assemblers

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Assemblers are classified based on the **number of passes** (scans) made over the source program.

| Type | Passes | Strategy | Trade-Off |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Single-Pass / One-Pass Assembler** | 1 | Generates code immediately; uses *back-patching* or *multiple working locations* to handle forward references. | Fast but memory-intensive; complex code structure. |
| **Two-Pass Assembler** | 2 | Pass 1 → builds tables & assigns addresses. Pass 2 → translates to machine code. | Simple, clean design; intermediate file needed. |
| **Multi-Pass Assembler** | ≥ 3 | Used when target supports extensive optimization / relocatable code. | Slow but flexible. |
| **Cross Assembler** | — | Runs on Host X, generates code for Target Y. | Used in embedded development. |
| **Resident Assembler** | — | Runs on the same machine it assembles code for. | Self-hosting development. |
| **Macro Assembler** | 2+ | Supports macro definition, expansion, and nested macros. | Adds macro processor pre-pass. |

## 2.2 The Two-Pass Assembler — Architecture

The **two-pass assembler** is the most academically important model in KTU exams. Its architecture revolves around the **Intermediate Code (IC)** file and several lookup tables.

### 2.2.1 Data Structures (Tables)

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic Opcode Table (MOT)** — Built-in, fixed table. Maps mnemonic → opcode class, format, length. Contains opcodes for register-only, memory-reference, and I/O instructions.

| Symbol | Expansion |
|---|---|
| **MOT** | Mnemonic Opcode Table (Opcode + Class + Format) |
| **POT** | Pseudo-Opcode Table (Directives like ORIGIN, EQU, END, DC, DS) |
| **ST** | Symbol Table (Label → Address) |
| **LT** | Literal Table (Literal → Address in literal pool) |
| **BT** | Base Table (Used for base-register relative addressing) |
| **IC** | Intermediate Code (Output of Pass 1, input to Pass 2) |

### 2.2.2 Instruction Format (Generic)

Most assembly language instructions follow a generic internal structure:

$$
\text{Instruction} = \langle \text{Label} \rangle \;\vert\; \langle \text{Opcode Mnemonic} \rangle \;\vert\; \langle \text{Operand} \rangle \;\vert\; \langle \text{Comment} \rangle
$$

Each line is decomposed and fed to the appropriate processing branch.

### 2.2.3 Pseudo-Op Table (Directives)

| Directive | Meaning | Action |
|---|---|---|
| `START` / `ORIGIN` | Beginning of program / Set LC | `LC ← Operand` |
| `END` | End of source program | Stop processing |
| `EQU` | Equate symbol to value | `ST[Label] ← Operand` |
| `DC` | Define Constant | Allocate & initialize |
| `DS` | Define Storage | Allocate (un-initialized) |
| `ORG` | Set location counter | `LC ← Operand` |

## 2.3 Pass-1: Define Symbols

**Objective:** *Allocate addresses to all labels and literals* without yet producing machine code. It produces the **Intermediate Code (IC)** file.

### 2.3.1 Pass-1 Algorithm (Structured Steps)

1. **Initialize** Location Counter (LC) to the start address (usually 0 or per `ORIGIN`).
2. **Read** next source line.
3. **If the line has a label** → Insert `(Label, LC)` into the **Symbol Table (ST)**.
4. **Classify the opcode** (look up MOT/POT).
5. **Update LC** based on instruction length:
   - Imperative statement (machine instr.) → LC ← LC + instruction length.
   - Declaration statement (`DC`, `DS`) → LC ← LC + size.
   - Directive (`LTORG`) → Assign addresses to all pending literals.
6. **For literals** (`= value` in operand field): add to LT with current LC pointer (or pending pool).
7. **Write intermediate representation** (a structured quadruple: `<Class, Operation, Operand1, Operand2>`) to IC.
8. If `END` directive encountered → process remaining literals in pool, write to IC, terminate Pass-1.

### 2.3.2 Intermediate Code Format

The IC is structured so Pass-2 can interpret it unambiguously:

$$
IC = (\text{Class},\; \text{Op-Code},\; \text{Operand-1},\; \text{Operand-2})
$$

| Class Code | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `01` | Imperative Statement (Machine Instruction) |
| `10` | Declaration Statement (DC / DS) |
| `11` | Assembler Directive |

## 2.4 Pass-2: Synthesize Machine Code

**Objective:** *Translate IC entries into machine code* using the tables built in Pass-1.

### 2.4.1 Pass-2 Algorithm

1. **Read** an IC entry.
2. **Classify** based on its class field.
3. **If Class = Imperative (01):**
   - Look up opcode in MOT.
   - Resolve operand addresses using **ST** (for symbols) and **LT** (for literals).
   - Handle addressing modes: Direct, Indirect, Indexed, Immediate, Base-Relative.
   - Compute effective address if needed.
4. **If Class = Declaration (10):**
   - Emit constant value (`DC`) or reserve storage (`DS`).
5. **Write generated binary** to **Object Program** file.
6. Update listing file with cross-references.
7. Repeat until IC is exhausted.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Assembler Core Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet for KTU 2024 Scheme Examination**

| # | Formula / Rule | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{LC}_{\text{new}} = \text{LC}_{\text{old}} + L_i$ | Location Counter update after instruction of length $L_i$ |
| 2 | $A_{\text{eff}} = A_{\text{sym}} + \text{Offset}_{\text{relocation}}$ | Effective address computation |
| 3 | $\text{Inst}_{\text{length}} = f(\text{Format}, \text{AddrMode})$ | Length depends on instruction class |
| 4 | $\text{Codewords}_{\text{program}} = \text{LC}_{\text{final}} - \text{LC}_{\text{initial}}$ | Total size of generated object code |
| 5 | $\text{Tables} = \{MOT, POT, ST, LT, BT\}$ | Required data structures |
| 6 | $\text{Back-patch count} = \sum \text{ForwardRefs}$ | Number of unresolved refs (one-pass) |
| 7 | $\text{Object Code} = \text{Opcode} \, \vert\, \text{Address Field}$ | Binary concatenation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** Modern GCC's inline assembler, MASM (Microsoft), NASM (Linux), and the GNU `as` utility for ARM/RISC-V all embody variants of the two-pass model. Embedded cross-compilers (e.g., Keil, IAR for ARM Cortex-M) use cross-assemblers based on this exact theoretical framework.

## 2.6 Design Considerations & Limitations

- **Forward references** force either (a) two-pass design OR (b) back-patching with multiple working buffers.
- **Symbol Table** may use hashing (open/closed) for $O(1)$ average lookup.
- **Relocatable vs Absolute** object code: Relocatable code contains *fix-up records* allowing the linker to adjust addresses at link time.
- **Error diagnostics** (syntax errors, undefined symbols, duplicate labels) must be reported with line numbers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Full Two-Pass Assembly of a Sample Program

Let us work through a complete example — this is a **classic KTU exam problem**. We will trace both passes and compute the final machine code.

### 3.1.1 Source Program (Hypothetical Assembly)

Consider the following assembly source (inspired by 8086 / generic accumulator-style ISA):

```asm
       START   100
L1     DS      1
       LDA     L2
       ADD     ONE
       STA     L1
L2     DC      =5
ONE    DC      1
       END
```

- We assume: `LDA` = opcode `00`, format length = 1 word; `ADD` = opcode `01`, length = 1; `STA` = opcode `02`, length = 1; `DC` reserves 1 word per constant; `DS 1` reserves 1 word.

### 3.1.2 Pass-1 Execution Trace

Pass-1 builds the Symbol Table (ST), Literal Table (LT), and Intermediate Code (IC) while incrementing the Location Counter (LC).

**Initial:** $\text{LC} = 100$ (after `START 100`).

| Step | Line Processed | LC Before | Action | LC After | ST Updated | LT Updated | IC Emitted |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `L1 DS 1` | 100 | Declare L1 at 100; reserve 1 word | 101 | `L1 → 100` | — | `(DS, 1, L1, —)` |
| 2 | `LDA L2` | 101 | L2 is forward ref; record placeholder | 102 | — | — | `(IS, 00, L2, —)` |
| 3 | `ADD ONE` | 102 | ONE forward ref | 103 | — | — | `(IS, 01, ONE, —)` |
| 4 | `STA L1` | 103 | L1 already in ST → resolve directly | 104 | — | — | `(IS, 02, L1, 100)` |
| 5 | `L2 DC =5` | 104 | L2 defined at 104; literal =5 added | 105 | `L2 → 104` | `5 → 105` | `(DL, 02, =5, —)` |
| 6 | `ONE DC 1` | 105 | ONE defined at 105 | 106 | `ONE → 105` | — | `(DL, 01, 1, —)` |
| 7 | `END` | 106 | Flush literal pool (literal 5 at 105 already done); terminate | 106 | — | — | `(AD, END, —, —)` |

**Final Tables after Pass-1:**

$$
ST = \{ (L1, 100), \; (L2, 104), \; (ONE, 105) \}
$$

$$
LT = \{ (=5, 105) \}
$$

### 3.1.3 Pass-2 Execution Trace

Pass-2 reads the IC and resolves all operand references using ST and LT, then emits binary machine code.

| IC Entry | Class | Action | Generated Machine Code (Hex) | Comment |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `(DS, 1, L1, —)` | DL | Reserve 1 word (location 100) | `(empty/uninitialized)` | `DS` produces no init code |
| `(IS, 00, L2, —)` | IS | Opcode `00`, Operand = ST[L2] = 104 | `00 68` (104 = 68H) | Forward ref now resolved |
| `(IS, 01, ONE, —)` | IS | Opcode `01`, Operand = ST[ONE] = 105 | `01 69` (105 = 69H) | |
| `(IS, 02, L1, 100)` | IS | Opcode `02`, Operand = 100 (64H) | `02 64` | Already resolved in Pass-1 |
| `(DL, 02, =5, —)` | DL | Emit constant value 5 (05H) at loc 104 | `00 05` | Literal resolved |
| `(DL, 01, 1, —)` | DL | Emit constant value 1 (01H) at loc 105 | `00 01` | |

### 3.1.4 Final Memory Map

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Loc 100 (decimal)} &: \quad \text{Reserved (DS)} \\
\text{Loc 101} &: \quad 00 \; 68 \quad (\text{LDA L2}) \\
\text{Loc 102} &: \quad 01 \; 69 \quad (\text{ADD ONE}) \\
\text{Loc 103} &: \quad 02 \; 64 \quad (\text{STA L1}) \\
\text{Loc 104} &: \quad 00 \; 05 \quad (\text{DC =5}) \\
\text{Loc 105} &: \quad 00 \; 01 \quad (\text{DC 1}) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 The One-Pass Assembler — Back-Patching Strategy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In a **one-pass assembler**, code is emitted immediately. When a forward reference is encountered, the assembler *cannot* resolve it. It writes a placeholder and remembers its location. Later, when the label is defined, it **back-patches** (rewrites) all placeholders.

### 3.2.1 Back-Patching Algorithm (Compact Form)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Initialize: } & \text{LC} = 0,\; \text{pending list} = \emptyset \\
\text{For each source line: } & \\
& \text{1. If label present, assign address.} \\
& \text{2. If forward ref, push (ref-line, operand-position) to pending list.} \\
& \text{3. Emit code with placeholder address (e.g., 0x0000).} \\
\text{When label is defined: } & \\
& \text{4. For each entry in pending list referring to this label,} \\
& \quad \text{patch the emitted binary at (ref-line, operand-position) with label's address.}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.2 Comparison: One-Pass vs Two-Pass

| Feature | One-Pass | Two-Pass |
|---|---|---|
| Speed | Faster (single scan) | Slower (two scans) |
| Memory | Needs back-patch buffers | Needs IC storage |
| Code complexity | Complex | Simple, modular |
| Output | Binary directly | Object + IC file |
| KTU exam focus | Often asked as comparison | Most commonly designed |

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Numerical Pattern

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU 2024 Numericals:** Given a small assembly source (8–12 lines), produce the Symbol Table, Literal Table, and final machine code. Marks distribution: 7 for Pass-1 (tables), 7 for Pass-2 (machine code).

### 3.3.1 Step-by-Step Methodology

1. **Read** the source program.
2. **Identify** all labels (leftmost identifiers followed by `:` or in label field).
3. **Identify** all literals (constants preceded by `=`).
4. **For each line**, update LC by instruction length.
5. **Maintain** ST and LT through Pass-1.
6. **In Pass-2**, substitute symbolic addresses with numeric ones.

## 3.4 Formal Derivation — Length Computation for Variable-Format Instructions

Some instructions occupy different numbers of memory words depending on the addressing mode. The assembler must know the length *before* emitting it (Pass-2), but must also know it during Pass-1 to advance LC.

$$
L_i = \begin{cases}
1 \text{ word} & \text{if } \text{Format} = \text{Register-only} \\
2 \text{ words} & \text{if } \text{Format} = \text{Direct} \\
3 \text{ words} & \text{if } \text{Format} = \text{Indirect / Indexed} \\
\end{cases}
$$

Total code size:

$$
\text{Size}_{\text{program}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} L_i + \sum_{j=1}^{M} D_j
$$

where $N$ = number of imperative statements and $M$ = number of declaration statements (`DC`, `DS`).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Two-Pass Assembler — Top-Level Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IN1[Input]
        A[Source Program .asm]
    end

    subgraph P1[Pass 1 Define Symbols]
        B1[Lexical Analyzer]
        B2[Lookup MOT and POT]
        B3[Update Location Counter LC]
        B4[Build Symbol Table ST]
        B5[Build Literal Table LT]
        B6[Emit Intermediate Code IC]
    end

    subgraph DB[Data Structures]
        ST[Symbol Table]
        LT[Literal Table]
        MOT[Mnemonic Opcode Table]
        POT[Pseudo Opcode Table]
    end

    subgraph P2[Pass 2 Synthesize Machine Code]
        C1[Read IC Entry]
        C2[Resolve Operands via ST and LT]
        C3[Generate Binary Opcode and Address]
        C4[Handle Address Modes]
        C5[Emit Machine Code]
    end

    subgraph OUT[Output]
        D1[Object Program .obj]
        D2[Listing File .lst]
        D3[Error Diagnostics]
    end

    A --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> B4
    B4 --> B5
    B5 --> B6
    B2 -.lookup.-> MOT
    B2 -.lookup.-> POT
    B4 -.write.-> ST
    B5 -.write.-> LT

    B6 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> C4
    C4 --> C5
    C2 -.lookup.-> ST
    C2 -.lookup.-> LT

    C5 --> D1
    C5 --> D2
    C5 --> D3
```

## 4.2 Pass-1 Detailed Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start Pass 1]) --> Init[Initialize LC to START value]
    Init --> ReadSrc[Read Next Source Line]
    ReadSrc --> HasLabel{Line has Label?}
    HasLabel -- Yes --> AddST[Insert Label and LC into Symbol Table]
    HasLabel -- No --> ClassifyOpcode
    AddST --> ClassifyOpcode{Classify Opcode}
    ClassifyOpcode --> Imperative[MOT Imperative]
    ClassifyOpcode --> Declaration[POT Declaration DC or DS]
    ClassifyOpcode --> Directive[POT Directive ORIGIN or LTORG or END]
    Imperative --> CheckFwd{Forward Reference in Operand?}
    CheckFwd -- Yes --> MarkPending[Add placeholder to IC and pending list]
    CheckFwd -- No --> UpdateLC1[LC = LC plus instruction length]
    MarkPending --> UpdateLC1
    Declaration --> UpdateLC2[LC = LC plus data size]
    Directive -- LTORG --> FlushLiteral[Assign addresses to all literals in pool]
    Directive -- END --> TerminateP1([End Pass 1])
    Directive -- ORIGIN --> SetLC[LC = operand]
    FlushLiteral --> ContinueLoop
    UpdateLC1 --> ContinueLoop
    UpdateLC2 --> ContinueLoop
    SetLC --> ContinueLoop
    ContinueLoop{More Lines?}
    ContinueLoop -- Yes --> ReadSrc
    ContinueLoop -- No --> TerminateP1
```

## 4.3 Pass-2 Detailed Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start2([Start Pass 2]) --> ReadIC[Read Next IC Entry]
    ReadIC --> CheckClass{IC Class?}
    CheckClass -- Imperative --> LookupMOT[Lookup Opcode in MOT]
    LookupMOT --> ResolveOp[Resolve Operand using ST and LT]
    ResolveOp --> ModeCheck{Addressing Mode?}
    ModeCheck -- Direct --> DirectAddr[Address = ST of symbol]
    ModeCheck -- Indirect --> IndirectAddr[Address = content at ST of symbol]
    ModeCheck -- Immediate --> ImmAddr[Address = literal value]
    ModeCheck -- Indexed --> IndexAddr[Address = ST plus index reg]
    DirectAddr --> EmitMachine
    IndirectAddr --> EmitMachine
    ImmAddr --> EmitMachine
    IndexAddr --> EmitMachine
    CheckClass -- Declaration DL --> EmitConstant[Emit constant value or reserve storage]
    CheckClass -- Directive AD --> SkipOrTerm[Skip or Terminate Pass 2]
    EmitMachine --> EmitObj[Write to Object File]
    EmitConstant --> EmitObj
    EmitObj --> UpdateListing[Update Listing File]
    UpdateListing --> LoopCheck{More IC Entries?}
    LoopCheck -- Yes --> ReadIC
    LoopCheck -- No --> EndP2([End Pass 2])
    SkipOrTerm --> LoopCheck
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture — System Software Stack Context

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    User([Programmer writes Source Code])
    subgraph Sys[System Software Tool Chain]
        direction TB
        Asm[Assembler .asm to .obj]
        Mac[Macro Processor optional]
        Lnk[Linker combines .obj files]
        Ldr[Loader loads into memory]
    end
    Hw([Hardware Processor executes binary])
    Listing[Listing File and Error Reports]

    User -->|Assembly source| Asm
    Asm -.->|Object code| Lnk
    Lnk -.->|Executable| Ldr
    Ldr -.-> Hw
    Mac -.->|Expanded source| Asm
    Asm -.->|Diagnostics| Listing
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]
**Define an assembler. List any four functions performed by a two-pass assembler.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** An assembler is a system software program that translates an assembly language program (source code) into its equivalent machine language program (object code) by mapping mnemonics to binary opcodes and resolving symbolic addresses.

**Four Functions of Two-Pass Assembler (2 marks):**

1. **Mnemonic-to-Opcode Translation:** Converts assembly mnemonics like `MOV`, `ADD` into corresponding machine opcodes using the Mnemonic Opcode Table (MOT).
2. **Symbol Resolution (Address Allocation):** Assigns memory addresses to all labels and stores them in the Symbol Table during Pass-1.
3. **Literal Pool Management:** Identifies literals (`= value`), places them in a literal pool, and resolves them in the literal table.
4. **Intermediate Code Generation (Pass-1)** and **Machine Code Synthesis (Pass-2)** with diagnostic reporting.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]
**Differentiate between a one-pass and a two-pass assembler. State one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | One-Pass Assembler | Two-Pass Assembler |
|---|---|---|
| Number of scans over source | **One scan only** | **Two scans** |
| Forward references | Handled via **back-patching** | Handled via **two-pass table building** |
| Intermediate code | Not needed | **Intermediate file generated** |
| Complexity | Higher (needs patch buffers) | Lower (modular design) |
| Speed | Faster | Slightly slower |

**Advantage of One-Pass (½ mark):** Single scan → faster execution, no need to store intermediate code.

**Advantage of Two-Pass (½ mark):** Simpler logic, easier to debug and maintain, allows optimization.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to update the Location Counter** properly for `DC` and `DS` directives → loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Not building Symbol Table AND Literal Table separately** — examiners explicitly check both.
> 3. **Confusing forward reference resolution timing** — students often resolve ST entries in Pass-1 (wrong) instead of Pass-2 (correct).
