# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Calculating Final Memory Addresses After Linking

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
> Three 8051 object files are linked together. Their CODE segment sizes are:
> - `MAIN.OBJ` = `0x0400` bytes (1024 bytes), relocatable base = 0
> - `UART.OBJ` = `0x0280` bytes (640 bytes), relocatable base = 0
> - `TIMER.OBJ` = `0x01A0` bytes (416 bytes), relocatable base = 0
>
> The linker directive specifies: `CODE(0x0000–0x0FFF)` and `XDATA(0x0000–0x07FF)`.
> Each module also has an XDATA buffer of 128 bytes, 256 bytes, and 64 bytes respectively.
> **Find: (a) Final CODE start addresses, (b) Final XDATA start addresses, (c) Total memory used, (d) Map file entry for `UART_INIT` if it is at offset 0x0030 within UART.OBJ.**

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Part (a) — Final CODE Start Addresses**

The linker concatenates code segments in the order specified in the command line. Assuming the order is `MAIN → UART → TIMER`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Start}_{\text{MAIN}} &= \text{Base}_{\text{CODE}} = \texttt{0x0000} \\
\text{End}_{\text{MAIN}} &= \text{Start}_{\text{MAIN}} + \text{Size}_{\text{MAIN}} - 1 \\
&= \texttt{0x0000} + \texttt{0x0400} - 1 = \texttt{0x03FF}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Start}_{\text{UART}} &= \text{End}_{\text{MAIN}} + 1 = \texttt{0x03FF} + 1 = \texttt{0x0400} \\
\text{End}_{\text{UART}} &= \texttt{0x0400} + \texttt{0x0280} - 1 = \texttt{0x067F}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Start}_{\text{TIMER}} &= \text{End}_{\text{UART}} + 1 = \texttt{0x067F} + 1 = \texttt{0x0680} \\
\text{End}_{\text{TIMER}} &= \texttt{0x0680} + \texttt{0x01A0} - 1 = \texttt{0x081F}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification — does the total fit in `0x0000–0x0FFF`?**

$$
\text{Total CODE used} = \texttt{0x0400} + \texttt{0x0280} + \texttt{0x01A0} = \texttt{0x0820} \text{ bytes} = 2080 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
\text{Available CODE} = \texttt{0x0FFF} - \texttt{0x0000} + 1 = 4096 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
4096 \ge 2080 \quad \checkmark \quad \text{Linker succeeds}
$$

**Part (b) — Final XDATA Start Addresses** (XDATA is also placed in order `MAIN → UART → TIMER`):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Start}_{\text{MAIN, XDATA}} &= \texttt{0x0000} \\
\text{End}_{\text{MAIN, XDATA}} &= \texttt{0x0000} + 128 - 1 = \texttt{0x007F} \\
\text{Start}_{\text{UART, XDATA}} &= \texttt{0x007F} + 1 = \texttt{0x0080} \\
\text{End}_{\text{UART, XDATA}} &= \texttt{0x0080} + 256 - 1 = \texttt{0x017F} \\
\text{Start}_{\text{TIMER, XDATA}} &= \texttt{0x017F} + 1 = \texttt{0x0180} \\
\text{End}_{\text{TIMER, XDATA}} &= \texttt{0x0180} + 64 - 1 = \texttt{0x01BF}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (c) — Total Memory Used (CODE + XDATA):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CODE} &= 2080 \text{ bytes} \\
\text{XDATA} &= 128 + 256 + 64 = 448 \text{ bytes} \\
\text{Total} &= 2080 + 448 = 2528 \text{ bytes} = \texttt{0x09E0} \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (d) — Map File Entry for `UART_INIT`:**

The symbol `UART_INIT` is at relocatable offset `0x0030` inside `UART.OBJ`. The linker's relocation offset for `UART` is its final start address minus its assumed base (which is `0`):

$$
\Delta_{\text{UART}} = \text{Start}_{\text{UART}} - \text{Base}_{\text{UART}} = \texttt{0x0400} - \texttt{0x0000} = \texttt{0x0400}
$$

$$
\text{Final address of UART\_INIT} = \text{Offset}_{\text{UART\_INIT}} + \Delta_{\text{UART}} = \texttt{0x0030} + \texttt{0x0400} = \texttt{0x0430}
$$

> **Map File Snippet:**
> ```
> SYMBOL              ADDRESS      MODULE
> UART_INIT           0x0430H      UART.OBJ
> ```

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Detecting Segment Overlap

**Problem Statement:**
> A linker directive specifies `CODE(0x0000–0x3FFF)` and `DATA(0x30–0x7F)`. Module A uses 12 KB of CODE, and Module B uses 4 KB of CODE. Will the link succeed?

### Solution

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total CODE required} &= 12 \text{ KB} + 4 \text{ KB} = 16 \text{ KB} \\
\text{Available CODE} &= 0x3FFF - 0x0000 + 1 = 0x4000 = 16384 \text{ bytes} = 16 \text{ KB} \\
\text{Remaining} &= 16384 - 16384 = 0 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

The link succeeds **only if** Module A occupies `0x0000–0x2FFF` (12288 bytes) and Module B occupies `0x3000–0x3FFF` (4096 bytes). If the linker tries to place both with different ordering, it computes the overlap check:

$$
S_A = \texttt{0x0000}, \quad E_A = \texttt{0x2FFF}, \quad S_B = \texttt{0x3000}, \quad E_B = \texttt{0x3FFF}
$$

$$
\text{Overlap Condition} = (S_A \le S_B \le E_A) \lor (S_B \le S_A \le E_B)
$$

$$
(\texttt{0x0000} \le \texttt{0x3000} \le \texttt{0x2FFF}) \lor (\texttt{0x3000} \le \texttt{0x0000} \le \texttt{0x3FFF}) = \text{FALSE} \lor \text{FALSE} = \text{FALSE}
$$

**No overlap → Linker succeeds.** ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget to subtract 1 when computing the **end address** of a segment. Always remember: **End = Start + Size − 1**. A common error is writing `End = Start + Size`, which causes an off-by-one overlap detection failure.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Linker Behavior Across Microcontroller Families

| Feature | 8051 (Harvard, 8-bit) | ARM Cortex-M0 (von Neumann) | PIC16F (Harvard, 14-bit) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Architecture** | Separate Code/XDATA buses | Unified address space (mostly) | Separate Program/Data buses |
| **Default Code Segments** | `CODE`, `CONST` | `.text`, `.rodata` | `CODE` |
| **Default Data Segments** | `DATA`, `IDATA`, `XDATA`, `BIT`, `PDATA` | `.data`, `.bss`, `.heap`, `.stack` | `DATA`, `EEPROM` |
| **Linker Tool** | Keil BL51, LX51 | GNU `ld` (arm-none-eabi-ld) | MPLAB XC8 linker |
| **Directive File** | `.xcl` (Keil) | `.ld` (linker script) | `.gld` (xc8) |
| **Max Code Space** | 64 KB | Up to 4 GB (theoretical) | 8K × 14-bit words |
| **Relocatable Object** | OMF51 | ELF | COFF / ELF |


> **Reading the diagram:** Files flow from left to right. The **Linker** (highlighted in gold) is the central merging agent. It receives three inputs: (1) object files from the assembler, (2) library files for standard routines, and (3) the directive file specifying the memory map. It produces a fully resolved absolute object plus a human-readable map file, which is then converted to HEX format and finally burned into the microcontroller's Flash memory.

## 4.2 Figure 2: Internal Phases of Linker Operation (Detailed Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Phase 1\n SYMBOL TABLE\n AGGREGATION] --> P2[Phase 2\n EXTERNAL\n REFERENCE\n RESOLUTION]
    P2 --> P3[Phase 3\n LIBRARY\n SCAN AND\n DEMAND LOADING]
    P3 --> P4{All References\n Resolved?}

    P5 --> P7[Phase 6\n ABSOLUTE\n OBJECT EMISSION]
    P1 --> S1[Input: All .obj files\n Read PUBLIC and EXTERN\n tables; build global dict]
    S1 --> P1
    S2[Input: Global symbol table\n Match each EXTERN to its\n defining PUBLIC module] --> P2
    S3[Input: .lib archives\n Pull in .obj modules\n that resolve pending EXTERNs] --> P3
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Each phase is a sequential processing stage. The **diamond** is a decision point — if unresolved symbols remain after library scanning, the linker aborts with an error. Only when all references are satisfied does it proceed to relocation.

## 4.3 Figure 3: Linker Memory Placement — Segments in 8051 Harvard Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CODE_MEMORY[CODE MEMORY - 64 KB ROM - PSEN Activated]
        direction TB
        A0[0x0000\n RESET VECTOR\n startup.obj] --> A1[0x0003\n External 0\n ISR Vector] --> A2[0x0080\n main.obj\n Main Program]
        A2 --> A3[0x0480\n uart.obj\n UART Routines]
        A3 --> A4[0x0700\n delay.obj\n Delay Routines]
        A4 --> A5[0x0900\n Library Code\n printf, scanf] --> A6[0xFFFF\n END OF CODE]
    end
    subgraph XDATA_MEMORY[XDATA MEMORY - 64 KB External RAM - RD/WR Activated]
        direction TB
        B0[0x0000\n main.xdata\n Buffer 128 B] --> B1[0x0080\n uart.xdata\n FIFO 256 B] --> B2[0x0180\n delay.xdata\n Variables 64 B] --> B3[0x01C0\n FREE SPACE] --> B4[0xFFFF\n END OF XDATA]
    end
    style A0 fill:#FFB74D
    style A1 fill:#FFB74D
    style A2 fill:#81C784
    style A3 fill:#81C784
    style A4 fill:#81C784
    style A5 fill:#4FC3F7
    style B0 fill:#CE93D8
    style B1 fill:#CE93D8
    style B2 fill:#CE93D8
    style B3 fill:#E0E0E0
    style CODE_MEMORY fill:#FFF9C4
    style XDATA_MEMORY fill:#F8BBD0
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The linker places CODE segments sequentially in ROM (left to right, ascending addresses) and XDATA segments in external RAM. The `PSEN` and `RD/WR` signals from the 8051 hardware ensure these two memory regions are *physically separate* buses, even though they share the same address numbers (`0x0000` etc.). This is a defining feature of **Harvard architecture** that the linker must respect.

## 4.4 Figure 4: Linker Error vs Successful Link — Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Start: Linker Invoked\n with N .obj files] --> CHK1{All EXTERN symbols\n have matching\n PUBLIC definitions?}
    CHK1 -- No --> LIB[Search Libraries\n .lib for definitions]
    LIB --> CHK2{Found in library?}
    CHK2 -- Yes --> RESOLVE[Pull library .obj\n into link set]
    CHK2 -- No --> ERR1[ERROR L999\n Undefined External\n LINK ABORTED]
    CHK1 -- Yes --> CHK3{All segment sizes\n fit in memory\n ranges?}
    CHK3 -- No --> ERR2[ERROR L107\n Segment Overflow\n LINK ABORTED]
    CHK3 -- Yes --> CHK4{Any segment\n overlaps\n another?}
    CHK4 -- Yes --> ERR3[ERROR L109\n Segment Overlap\n LINK ABORTED]
    CHK4 -- No --> SUCCESS[SUCCESS\n Generate .abs, .map\n LINK COMPLETE]
    RESOLVE --> CHK1
    style ERR1 fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C
    style ERR2 fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C
    style ERR3 fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C
    style SUCCESS fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20
    style START fill:#E3F2FD
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The three most common linker errors are visualized. The linker performs a cyclic library search until all references are satisfied, then validates memory constraints, and only then emits a successful absolute output.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question 1, 3 Marks]**
> **Q: Define a Linker. List its primary inputs and outputs.**

**Model Answer:**
A linker is a system software utility that combines one or more object files produced by an assembler/compiler, resolves external symbol references, performs relocation of addresses, and combines the result with library routines to produce a single executable file suitable for loading into a microcontroller's memory.

**Primary Inputs:**
1. Object files (`.obj` / `.o`) — relocatable machine code
2. Library files (`.lib`) — pre-compiled routines
3. Linker directive file (`.xcl` / `.ld` / `.lnk`) — memory map specification
4. Startup object (e.g., `cstartup.obj`)

**Primary Outputs:**
1. Absolute object file (`.abs` / `.elf`) — fully linked executable
2. Map file (`.map` / `.m51`) — memory layout report
3. (Optionally) Debug file for symbolic debugging
4. (After locator) Intel HEX file (`.hex`) — for ROM burning

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question 2, 3 Marks]**
> **Q: Differentiate between a Linker and a Locator. Why is the Locator needed separately in embedded systems?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Linker | Locator |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Function** | Combines object files and resolves symbols | Converts absolute object into HEX/binary format |
| **Address Resolution** | Assigns logical/section addresses | Applies final physical address padding and record format |
| **Output** | `.abs` or `.elf` file | `.hex` or `.bin` file (burnable to ROM) |
| **Operates On** | Multiple object files | Single absolute object file |

**Why Locator is Needed Separately:**
In embedded systems, the microcontroller's Flash/ROM programmer expects a specific **record format** (e.g., Intel HEX 8-bit, Motorola S-record). The linker alone produces an executable in the developer's internal format. The locator **translates** this internal format into the standardized loadable format (HEX) that EPROM/Flash programmers can consume, often adding checksums, extended linear address records, and end-of-file markers.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Format)

> **[KTU University Exam - Module 4 Model, Dec 2024 Scheme]**
>
> **Question A (14 Marks):**
> **(a)** [7 Marks] Explain with a neat block diagram the **complete role of the linker** in an embedded toolchain. Describe its **four main operational phases** in detail.
> **(b)** [7 Marks] Given three object files with the following details:
> - `MAIN.OBJ`: CODE = 2 KB, XDATA = 256 B
> - `UART.OBJ`: CODE = 1.5 KB, XDATA = 512 B
> - `LCD.OBJ`: CODE = 3 KB, XDATA = 128 B
>
> If the linker directive specifies `CODE(0x0000–0x7FFF)` and `XDATA(0x0000–0x0FFF)`, calculate the **final start and end addresses** of all segments, verify that linking succeeds, and determine the **map file address** for the symbol `LCD_INIT_CMD` if it is at relocatable offset `0x0150` inside `LCD.OBJ`.

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Role of Linker in Embedded Toolchain [7 Marks]

**Block Diagram (to be drawn by student):**

$$
\text{Source (.asm)} \rightarrow \text{Assembler} \rightarrow \text{Object (.obj)} \rightarrow \boxed{\text{Linker}} \rightarrow \text{Executable (.abs)} \rightarrow \text{Locator} \rightarrow \text{HEX (.hex)} \rightarrow \text{ROM}
$$

The linker sits between the **assembler** and the **locator**, and its role is to produce a single unified executable image from multiple object modules.

**Four Operational Phases:**

1. **Symbol Table Aggregation:** The linker reads the OMF/ELF object headers of every input module and merges all `PUBLIC` symbol definitions and `EXTERN` references into a single global symbol dictionary. Each entry maps a name (e.g., `DELAY`, `ISR_UART`) to the module that defines it and the offset within that module.

2. **External Reference Resolution:** Every `EXTERN` reference in the calling module (e.g., `EXTERN DELAY` in `main.obj`) is matched against the global dictionary. Once matched, the linker knows which module provides the symbol and prepares to merge it into the link set.

3. **Library Demand Loading:** If unresolved references remain after processing all explicit object files, the linker scans the supplied `.lib` archives. It pulls in **only those library object modules** that define at least one currently-unresolved symbol. This process is iterated until all references are satisfied or until no library can resolve further references (which causes an `L999` undefined-symbol error).

4. **Relocation and Section Placement:** The linker concatenates all `CODE` segments in the order specified by the directive file, computes the start address of each module (e.g., `UART.OBJ` starts at `0x0800` if `MAIN.OBJ` ended at `0x07FF`), and patches all relocatable instructions to refer to their final absolute addresses. It also generates a `.map` file documenting the final memory map.

#### Part (b) — Memory Address Calculation [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- `MAIN.OBJ`: CODE = 2 KB = 0x0800, XDATA = 256 B = 0x0100
- `UART.OBJ`: CODE = 1.5 KB = 0x0600, XDATA = 512 B = 0x0200
- `LCD.OBJ`: CODE = 3 KB = 0x0C00, XDATA = 128 B = 0x0080
- CODE range: 0x0000 to 0x7FFF (32 KB)
- XDATA range: 0x0000 to 0x0FFF (4 KB)

**CODE Segment Placement (in order: MAIN → UART → LCD):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Start}_{\text{MAIN,CODE}} &= \texttt{0x0000} \\
\text{End}_{\text{MAIN,CODE}} &= \texttt{0x0000} + \texttt{0x0800} - 1 = \texttt{0x07FF} \\
\text{Start}_{\text{UART,CODE}} &= \texttt{0x07FF} + 1 = \texttt{0x0800} \\
\text{End}_{\text{UART,CODE}} &= \texttt{0x0800} + \texttt{0x0600} - 1 = \texttt{0x0DFF} \\
\text{Start}_{\text{LCD,CODE}} &= \texttt{0x0DFF} + 1 = \texttt{0x0E00} \\
\text{End}_{\text{LCD,CODE}} &= \texttt{0x0E00} + \texttt{0x0C00} - 1 = \texttt{0x19FF}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total CODE used** = 0x0800 + 0x0600 + 0x0C00 = 0x1A00 = 6656 bytes
**Available CODE** = 0x7FFF − 0x0000 + 1 = 0x8000 = 32768 bytes

Since **6656 < 32768**, linking succeeds. ✓

**XDATA Segment Placement (in order: MAIN → UART → LCD):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Start}_{\text{MAIN,XDATA}} &= \texttt{0x0000} \\
\text{End}_{\text{MAIN,XDATA}} &= \texttt{0x00FF} \\
\text{Start}_{\text{UART,XDATA}} &= \texttt{0x0100} \\
\text{End}_{\text{UART,XDATA}} &= \texttt{0x02FF} \\
\text{Start}_{\text{LCD,XDATA}} &= \texttt{0x0300} \\
\text{End}_{\text{LCD,XDATA}} &= \texttt{0x037F}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total XDATA used** = 0x0100 + 0x0200 + 0x0080 = 0x0380 = 896 bytes
**Available XDATA** = 0x0FFF − 0x0000 + 1 = 0x1000 = 4096 bytes

Since **896 < 4096**, linking succeeds. ✓

**Map File Address for `LCD_INIT_CMD`:**

$$
\Delta_{\text{LCD}} = \text{Start}_{\text{LCD,CODE}} - \text{Base}_{\text{LCD,CODE}} = \texttt{0x0E00} - \texttt{0x0000} = \texttt{0x0E00}
$$

$$
\text{Final address of LCD\_INIT\_CMD} = \text{Offset} + \Delta_{\text{LCD}} = \texttt{0x0150} + \texttt{0x0E00} = \texttt{0x0F50}
$$

**Final Answer:** `LCD_INIT_CMD` is located at **`0x0F50`** in the absolute object file.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**
> **(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the concept of **relocatable code** and **relocation** in the context of microcontrollers. Why cannot a `.obj` file be executed directly on an 8051? How does the linker fix this?
> **(b)** [7 Marks] Differentiate between **static linking** and **dynamic linking**. In embedded systems, which is preferred and why? Illustrate with a real-time example from an automotive ECU.

### Model Solution — Question B (Outline Key Points)

#### Part (a) — Relocatable Code and Relocation [7 Marks]

- **Relocatable code** = machine code that is *not yet* tied to a final memory address. The assembler assumes the module starts at address 0, and the linker must add a **relocation offset** ($\Delta$) to every address-sensitive instruction (`LCALL`, `AJMP`, `SJMP`, `LJMP`, `MOV DPTR,#`, `CJNE`, `DJNZ`, `ACALL`).
- A `.obj` file **cannot be executed directly** because:
  1. It contains unresolved `EXTERN` references (e.g., calling a routine in another module).
  2. All addresses are *relative to base 0*, not the actual 8051 memory map.
  3. The 8051's `PC` (Program Counter) needs **real physical addresses** to fetch the next instruction.
- The linker **fixes this by**:
  1. Computing the actual base address of each module from the directive file.
  2. Adding the relocation offset $\Delta$ to every relocatable address.
  3. Patching the binary code with these final absolute addresses.
  4. Writing the result into the `.abs` file, which is now **fully resolved and executable**.

#### Part (b) — Static vs Dynamic Linking [7 Marks]

| Aspect | Static Linking | Dynamic Linking |
|---|---|---|
| **When does linking happen?** | At build time (compile time) | At load time or runtime |
| **Library code location** | Embedded into the final executable | Stored in a separate shared object |
| **Executable size** | Larger (contains all needed code) | Smaller (only references symbols) |
| **Update flexibility** | Need to rebuild the entire firmware | Can update the library without rebuilding app |
| **Embedded suitability** | **Highly preferred** | Rarely used (used in Linux/Android only) |

**Why Static Linking is Preferred in Embedded Systems:**
- **Determinism** — All code is known at build time; no surprises at runtime.
- **Real-time guarantees** — No library loading delays; the firmware starts executing instantly from the reset vector.
- **Reliability** — No broken references if a shared library is missing.
- **No OS dependency** — Most MCUs (8051, PIC) don't even have a dynamic linker/loader.

**Automotive ECU Example:** A Bosch engine control unit linking with the AUTOSAR RTE (Runtime Environment) statically. All 12 CAN message handlers, the OBD-II protocol stack, and the fuel injection routines are linked into a single `.abs` file and burned into the ECU's Flash. There is no shared library on the vehicle, ensuring the engine responds within **2 milliseconds** — a hard real-time constraint that dynamic linking cannot guarantee.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls — Read Carefully Before Writing Your Exam!**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the "-1" in End Address Calculation:** The biggest single error. End = Start + Size − 1, NOT Start + Size. A single off-by-one mistake will cause your overlap detection and verification to be marked wrong.
>
> 2. **Confusing Linker with Locator:** Examiners *love* to test this. Linker = combining object files + resolving symbols. Locator = converting to HEX format. Don't use these terms interchangeably.
>
> 3. **Ignoring the 8051 Harvard Architecture:** When asked about segment placement, many students write a single unified memory map. **Remember**: 8051 has *separate* address spaces for CODE (via `PSEN`) and XDATA (via `RD/WR`). The linker places them in physically distinct memory.
>
> 4. **Not Showing the Relocation Offset ($\Delta$) Calculation:** When asked for the final address of a symbol, you must show:
>    `Final Address = Offset within module + (Start of module − Assumed base of module)`
>    Skipping this step costs 1–2 marks.
>
> 5. **Forgetting to Verify Memory Boundaries:** Always end your calculation with a verification: `Total used ≤ Available`. Examiners reward students who double-check their work.
>
> 6. **Drawing Block Diagrams Without Labels:** If the question asks for a "neat labeled diagram," every block must have a name and arrows must have direction labels. A vague diagram = partial marks only.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Linker (Module 4, PCMRT403)**
>
> - **Definition:** A linker combines multiple `.obj` files + libraries, resolves external symbols, relocates addresses, and produces a single `.abs`/`.elf` executable.
>
> - **Position in Toolchain:** Assembler → **Linker** → Locator → HEX → ROM
>
> - **Four Core Phases:** (1) Symbol table aggregation, (2) External reference resolution, (3) Library demand loading, (4) Relocation & section placement
>
> - **Key Inputs:** `.obj` files, `.lib` files, linker directive file (`.xcl`/`.ld`), startup object
>
> - **Key Outputs:** `.abs`/`.elf` (executable), `.map` (memory report), debug file
>
> - **For 8051:** CODE memory = 64 KB (PSEN bus), XDATA memory = 64 KB (RD/WR bus), Harvard architecture = physically separate buses
>
> - **Critical Formula:** $\text{End}_{\text{addr}} = \text{Start}_{\text{addr}} + \text{Size} - 1$
>
> - **Relocation Formula:** $\text{Final Address} = \text{Internal Offset} + \Delta$, where $\Delta = \text{Actual Start} - \text{Assumed Base}$
>
> - **Common Errors:** L999 (Undefined Symbol), L107 (Segment Overflow), L109 (Segment Overlap)
>
> - **Static vs Dynamic:** Embedded systems **always use static linking** for determinism and real-time guarantees.
>
> - **PUBLIC / EXTERN:** Public = "I define this symbol" (offered to others). Extern = "I need this symbol" (required from others).
>
> - **Linker vs Locator:** Linker produces `.abs`/`.elf` (internal format). Locator produces `.hex`/`.bin` (standard burnable format).
>
> - **Library Pull-in Rule:** A library object is linked **only if** it resolves an unresolved EXTERN. This is "demand-driven" linking.
>
> - **Reset Vector:** The 8051 reset vector is at `0x0000` — the linker's placement of the startup object determines the first instruction executed.
>
> - **Verification Step:** Always verify that the total used memory is less than or equal to the available memory range. Examiners check this.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Loader
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMRT403 - COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE AND MICROCONTROLLER > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Loader # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Loader (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):** > A **Loader** is a fundamental system software utility whose primary function is to **copy an executable program from secondary (external) storage into the main memory (RAM)** of a computer system, perform necessary **address binding (relocation)**, and finally **transfer control to the entry point** of the program so that the CPU can begin execution. In the broader execution pipeline of a program written in a high-level or assembly language, the loader sits at the **final stage** of the execution hierarchy. The typical execution pipeline is: $$\text{Source Code} \xrightarrow{\text{Assembler}} \text{Object Code} \xrightarrow{\text{Linker}} \text{Executable File} \xrightarrow{\text{Loader}} \text{RAM (Ready to Run)}$$ The loader is therefore the **last system program** that acts on an executable image before the CPU takes over. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Perspective:** > Under the Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework of KTU 2024, the loader is mapped to **Course Outcome CO4**: *“Illustrate the functioning of system software like assembler, linker, loader, and macroprocessor.”* This question is a classic 7–14 mark favourite in university examinations. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are a **student preparing for an examination**: | Stage | Real-World Analogy | Computing Equivalent | |-------|-------------------|----------------------| | Writing notes in a register | Writing source code | High-Level Language | | Organizing notes into chapters | Converting to object code | Assembler | | Binding chapters into a book | Combining multiple modules | Linker | | **Carrying the book to the exam hall & opening it to the right page** | **Bringing program to RAM & setting entry point** | **LOADER** | | Reading the book | Executing instructions | CPU | Just as a student cannot start writing an exam until the book is physically placed on the desk and opened to the correct page, **a CPU cannot execute a program until the loader places it in RAM and points the Program Counter (PC) to the first instruction**. ### Three Fundamental Functions of a Loader 1. **Allocation** – Determines the memory region in RAM where the program will reside. 2. **Linking & Relocation** – Adjusts all address-dependent instructions and data references to match the actual allocated memory addresses. 3. **Loading & Execution Transfer** – Physically copies the binary image into RAM and jumps to the entry-point address. --- ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > The following constants and metrics are conventionally used in KTU textbook treatments of loaders (Hamacher, Stallings, and the manual): - **Word size of typical target machine:** **16 bits / 2 bytes** (e.g., SIC / SIC/XE classical architecture). - **Default program load address (Absolute Loader):** Often starts at **hexadecimal `0000`** or as specified in the `START` directive. - **Header record identifier:** `H` (for HP-record format, classical SIC assembler object file). - **Standard load address for SIC classical machine:** `0x0000`. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Loaders (KTU High-Yield) Loaders are categorized based on **how they handle the program's address space** before and during execution: ### (A) Absolute Loader - The executable object program is generated with **fixed, absolute memory addresses** (no relocation needed). - The assembler produces an object code that is **already bound** to specific physical addresses. - The loader's job is **trivial** — it merely copies bytes from the external file into RAM starting at the address specified in the `H` (Header) record. - **Speed:** Fastest loader. - **Drawback:** No flexibility; programmer must know exact memory layout. ### (B) Relocatable Loader (Relocating Loader) - The object code contains **relocatable addresses** (addresses that need adjustment based on the actual load address). - A special **relocation bit (RELOC bit)** is associated with each word in the text record. - The loader must add the **base address** (actual load address) to all relocatable words. - **Flexibility:** Program can be loaded anywhere in memory. ### (C) Dynamic Loader / Dynamic Linking Loader - Linking and relocation are **deferred until run-time** (or load-time on demand). - Allows **code sharing** between multiple programs. - **Modern OS context:** Windows `.dll` and Linux `.so` shared libraries use this concept. ### (D) Bootstrap Loader - A **primitive loader stored in ROM** (Read-Only Memory) of the computer. - Its sole job is to load the **full operating system kernel** from disk into RAM when the computer is powered on. - **Self-loading:** The bootstrap loader is so small it can be entered manually using front-panel switches on legacy systems. --- ## 2.2 The HP-Record Format (Object File Format) The loader reads an **object (executable) file** that has a standardized structure. KTU uses the classical SIC object file format consisting of three record types: | Record Type | Format | Purpose | |-------------|--------|---------| | **H (Header)** | `H⟨program name⟩⟨start addr⟩⟨length⟩` | Specifies program name, start address, and program length. | | **T (Text)** | `T⟨start addr⟩⟨length⟩⟨object codes⟩` | Contains actual machine instructions and data. | | **E (End)** | `E⟨entry point⟩` | Marks the end of the program and specifies the entry-point address. | > [!NOTE] > In the **relocatable loader** variant, each Text record additionally carries **relocation bits** indicating whether each word is relocatable (bit=1) or absolute (bit=0). The length of the relocation bit field is **half the text record length** (since each word is 3 hex digits = 1 byte data + 1 bit flag in the classical 16-bit SIC representation, but typically modeled as 1 bit per word). --- ## 2.3 Detailed Algorithm — Absolute Loader The Absolute Loader is the **simplest** and is a recurring KTU question. Its high-level algorithm is: ``` ALGORITHM: AbsoluteLoader BEGIN READ first record (Header) VERIFY program name SA ← Start address from H-record WHILE record type ≠ 'E' DO READ next record IF record type = 'T' THEN FOR each word in T-record DO Store word at consecutive memory address Increment memory pointer END FOR END IF END WHILE READ End record JUMP to entry-point address END ``` --- ## 2.4 Detailed Algorithm — Relocatable Loader The relocatable loader is more powerful. It reads **relocation bits** (RELOC) for each Text record. ``` ALGORITHM: RelocatableLoader BEGIN READ first record (Header) EXECADDR ← Load address (e.g., user-specified or found by ALLOCATION routine) While a record exists DO READ next record CASE record type OF 'H': Save program name, length, and starting address 'T': Get record length L Read object codes and relocation bits For i = 1 to L If RELOC[i] = 1 THEN ObjectCode[i] ← ObjectCode[i] + EXECADDR End IF Store ObjectCode[i] at memory[CSADDR + i] End FOR 'R': Read external reference list (used in conjunction with linker) 'D': Define record for external symbols 'M': Modification record — adjust by symbol value 'E': Set PC to entry point (EXECADDR + StartAddr) and TRANSFER END CASE END WHILE END ``` --- ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table summarises the **key numerical transformations** a loader performs. Memorise these for KTU ESE. | Concept | Formula / Rule | Description | |---------|---------------|-------------| | **Final Memory Address** | $\text{FinalAddr} = \text{BaseAddr} + \text{RelativeAddr}$ | Where BaseAddr is the actual load address and RelativeAddr is the value from object code. | | **Relocation Adjustment** | $\text{NewCode} = \text{OldCode} + \text{CS\_ADDR}$ | CS_ADDR is the actual load address in RAM. | | **Entry Point Calculation** | $\text{PC} = \text{CS\_ADDR} + \text{Program\_Start}$ | The program counter is loaded with this on execution. | | **Number of Words in Text Record** | $L = \frac{\text{Length field}}{3}$ | Each word is 3 hex digits (1 byte) in classical SIC. | | **Number of Relocation Bits** | $N = L$ (one bit per word) | Total bits in the relocation-bit field. | | **Effective Address (SIC/XE)** | $\text{TA} = \text{B} + \text{Disp}$ OR $\text{TA} = \text{PC} + \text{Disp}$ | Base-relative or PC-relative addressing. | | **Bootstrap ROM size** | Usually $\le 512$ bytes | Holds the BIOS / firmware loader. | | **Linkage Editor (vs Loader)** | Static = Link before loading | Binder combines multiple object modules. | --- ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility In real engineering, the loader concept underpins: - **Operating System Kernels:** The BIOS/UEFI bootstrap loader initializes the OS in microcontrollers (e.g., ARM Cortex-M bootloaders). - **Embedded Systems:** Custom loaders flash firmware into microcontroller ROM/Flash. - **Network Boot (PXE):** Loaders fetch OS images over Ethernet — used in data centres. - **Just-In-Time (JIT) Compilation:** Java Virtual Machine (JVM) loads `.class` files dynamically. - **Microcontroller Bootloaders:** STM32, Arduino, ESP32 all contain built-in bootloaders that perform loader-like functions over UART/USB. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Absolute Loader (KTU Style) **Problem Statement (Standard KTU Practice Question):** The following is an object program written for the SIC classical machine. Use the **Absolute Loader** algorithm to load this program into memory and show the contents of each memory word after loading. The program has been assembled to start at address `1000H`. ### Given Object Program ``` H COPY 001000 00107A T 001000 1E 141033 482039 001036 ... T 00101E 15 001036 ... E 001000 ``` ### Step-by-Step Solution (Exhaustive Derivation) **Step 1 — Parse Header Record** $$\text{H-record} = \texttt{H\ COPY\ 001000\ 00107A}$$ $$\text{Program Name} = \texttt{COPY}$$ $$\text{Start Address} = 001000_{H} = 4096_{10}$$ $$\text{Program Length} = 00107A_{H} = 2682_{10} \text{ bytes}$$ **Step 2 — Initialize Memory Pointer** $$\text{CS\_ADDR} \leftarrow 001000_{H}$$ **Step 3 — Process First Text Record** $$\text{T-record} = \texttt{T\ 001000\ 1E\ 141033\ 482039\ ...}$$ The length byte `1E` (in hexadecimal) equals: $$1E_{H} = 1 \times 16 + 14 = 30_{10} \text{ bytes}$$ Since each instruction word is 3 hex digits (1 byte in classical SIC), the number of words is: $$\text{Number of Words} = \frac{30}{3} = 10 \text{ words}$$ The loader reads each 3-hex-digit word and stores it at consecutive memory locations starting from `001000H`: $$\begin{aligned} \text{Memory}[001000] &\leftarrow 14_{H} \\ \text{Memory}[001001] &\leftarrow 10_{H} \\ \text{Memory}[001002] &\leftarrow 33_{H} \\ \text{Memory}[001003] &\leftarrow 48_{H} \\ \text{Memory}[001004] &\leftarrow 20_{H} \\ \text{Memory}[001005] &\leftarrow 39_{H} \\ \text{Memory}[001006] &\leftarrow 00_{H} \\ \text{Memory}[001007] &\leftarrow 10_{H} \\ \text{Memory}[001008] &\leftarrow 36_{H} \\ \text{Memory}[001009] &\leftarrow \ldots \end{aligned}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Critical Step in KTU Evaluation:** Many students lose marks here by **forgetting that consecutive memory locations each store 1 byte** in classical SIC (because words are 3 hex digits long). Examiners expect **each word placed at successive bytes**, not every 3rd byte. **Step 4 — Process Subsequent Text Records** The loader repeats the procedure for the next T-record starting at `00101E`: $$\text{CS\_ADDR} \leftarrow 00101E_{H}$$ Continue storing words sequentially. **Step 5 — Process End Record** $$\text{E-record} = \texttt{E\ 001000}$$ The loader loads the **Program Counter (PC)** with the entry-point address: $$\text{PC} \leftarrow 001000_{H}$$ **Step 6 — Transfer Control to CPU** The loader **jumps to the address stored in PC**, and execution begins from `001000H`. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Relocatable Loader (KTU Style) **Problem Statement:** The following is a relocatable object program. The actual load address in RAM is `4000H`. Apply the **relocatable loader** algorithm and show the memory contents. Assume relocation bits are read as a 10-bit pattern following the text record. ### Given Object Program ``` H COPY 000000 00107A T 000000 1E 141033 482039 001036 ... (Reloc bits: 1110000000) T 00001E 15 001036 ... E 000000 ``` ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Identify Load Address** $$\text{EX\_ADDR} = 4000_{H}$$ This is the actual physical base address in RAM where the program will reside. **Step 2 — Examine Text Record** $$\text{T-record: T\ 000000\ 1E\ 141033\ 482039\ 001036\ \ldots}$$ Length $= 1E_{H} = 30$ bytes $= 10$ words. **Step 3 — Read Relocation Bits** The relocation bits are: `1110000000` This indicates: | Word Index | Object Code | Reloc Bit | Action | |-----------|-------------|-----------|--------| | 1 | 141033 | 1 | Relocate | | 2 | 482039 | 1 | Relocate | | 3 | 001036 | 1 | Relocate | | 4 | ... | 0 | Absolute (no change) | | 5 | ... | 0 | Absolute | | ... | ... | ... | ... | **Step 4 — Apply Relocation** For each relocatable word, perform: $$\text{NewCode} = \text{OldCode} + \text{EX\_ADDR}$$ Let us execute this explicitly: For Word 1 (object code `141033`, but only the address portion needs relocation — typically the last 3 hex digits for SIC): $$\begin{aligned} \text{Address Portion} &= 033_{H} \\ \text{New Address} &= 033_{H} + 4000_{H} = 4033_{H} \\ \text{Final Word 1} &= 144033_{H} \end{aligned}$$ For Word 2: $$\begin{aligned} \text{Address Portion} &= 039_{H} \\ \text{New Address} &= 039_{H} + 4000_{H} = 4039_{H} \\ \text{Final Word 2} &= 484039_{H} \end{aligned}$$ For Word 3: $$\begin{aligned} \text{Address Portion} &= 036_{H} \\ \text{New Address} &= 036_{H} + 4000_{H} = 4036_{H} \\ \text{Final Word 3} &= 004036_{H} \end{aligned}$$ For Words 4–10, since relocation bits are 0, the object codes are stored as-is. **Step 5 — Store in Memory** $$\begin{aligned} \text{Memory}[4000] &\leftarrow 144033_{H} \\ \text{Memory}[4003] &\leftarrow 484039_{H} \\ \text{Memory}[4006] &\leftarrow 004036_{H} \\ \text{Memory}[4009] &\leftarrow \text{(unchanged)} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 6 — Execute Entry Point** The End record says entry point is `000000H`. The actual entry point in RAM is: $$\text{PC} \leftarrow \text{EX\_ADDR} + 000000_{H} = 4000_{H}$$ --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Loader Type Comparison | Parameter | Absolute Loader | Relocatable Loader | Dynamic Loader | |-----------|----------------|--------------------|--------------------| | **Relocation Required?** | No | Yes | Yes (at run-time) | | **Flexibility** | Low | Medium | Very High | | **Implementation Complexity** | Trivial | Moderate | High | | **Speed of Loading** | Fastest | Moderate | Slowest | | **Code Sharing** | Not supported | Limited | Fully supported | | **Modern OS usage** | Embedded firmware | Static linking | `.dll` / `.so` libraries | | **KTU Exam Frequency** | Very High | High | Medium | | **Records Used** | H, T, E | H, T, E, R, D, M | Plus additional relocation metadata | --- ## 3.4 KTU Frequently Tested Numerical Operations > [!IMPORTANT] > Examiners often test these computations directly. Master them: **Case 1 — Address Translation After Relocation:** $$\text{Physical Address} = \text{EX\_ADDR} + \text{Offset Address}$$ **Case 2 — Verify Program Length Matches Loaded Bytes:** $$\sum_{i=1}^{N} \text{T-record}_i.\text{length} \stackrel{?}{=} \text{H-record}.\text{length}$$ **Case 3 — Calculate Final Memory Range Occupied:** $$\text{Range} = [\text{EX\_ADDR}, \text{EX\_ADDR} + \text{Program Length} - 1]$$ # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Loader Within the System Software Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Source Program
Assembly Language] --> B[Assembler] B --> C[Object Program
H, T, E records] C --> D[Linker
Optional] D --> E[Executable File
Linked & Ready] E --> F[LOADER] F --> G[Main Memory RAM] G --> H[CPU Execution Begins
PC loaded with entry point] F --> I[Bootstrap Loader
in ROM] I --> F ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Internal Functional Architecture of a Relocatable Loader ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph Input OBJ[Object File
H, T, D, R, M, E records] end subgraph RelocatableLoader RD[Record Decoder
Identifies H/T/D/R/M/E] AL[Allocation Routine
Finds EX_ADDR in RAM] RG[Relocation Bit Reader] AT[Address Translator
NewAddr = OldAddr + EX_ADDR] WR[Memory Writer
Writes bytes to RAM] EPP[Entry Point Processor
Sets PC for execution] end subgraph Output RAM[Physical Memory RAM] CPU[CPU Ready to Execute] end OBJ --> RD RD --> AL RD --> RG RG --> AT AT --> WR WR --> RAM RD --> EPP EPP --> CPU AL -.feedback.-> AT ``` ## 4.3 Flowchart — Absolute Loader Execution Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD S([Start]) --> R1[Open Object File] R1 --> R2[Read Header Record H] R2 --> R3[Save Start Address
and Program Length] R3 --> L{Record Type?} L -->|T Text Record| T1[Read Length L] T1 --> T2[Read L bytes of Object Code] T2 --> T3[Store at memory
CSADDR onwards] T3 --> T4[CSADDR = CSADDR + L] T4 --> L L -->|E End Record| E1[Read Entry Point Address] E1 --> E2[Set PC = Entry Point] E2 --> E3([Jump to PC
Transfer Control to CPU]) L -->|EOF| EOF([Halt Loading]) ``` ## 4.4 Flowchart — Relocatable Loader Execution Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD S([Start]) --> R1[Read Header Record] R1 --> R2[Determine EX_ADDR
from user / OS] R2 --> L{Record Type?} L -->|T Text Record| T1[Read object codes
and RELOC bits] T1 --> T2{For each word i} T2 -->|RELOC i = 1| T3[NewCode = OldCode + EX_ADDR] T3 --> T4[Store NewCode in memory] T2 -->|RELOC i = 0| T5[Store OldCode unchanged] T5 --> T4 T4 --> T6[Increment memory pointer] T6 --> L L -->|M Modification| M1[Apply symbol value
to referenced word] M1 --> L L -->|E End| E1[PC = EX_ADDR + StartAddr] E1 --> E2([Transfer Control to CPU]) L -->|EOF| EOF([Halt]) ``` ## 4.5 Hierarchical View — Types of Loaders ```mermaid graph TD LDR[Loader] LDR --> ABS[Absolute Loader
No relocation needed] LDR --> REL[Relocatable Loader
Uses RELOC bits] LDR --> DYN[Dynamic Loader
Run-time linking] LDR --> BTL[Bootstrap Loader
Resides in ROM] REL --> REL1[Relative Loader
Loads at any base] DYN --> DYN1[Shared Library Loader
dll / so files] BTL --> BTL1[BIOS / Firmware Loader
Initial OS load] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation Note for KTU Examiners:** > The flowcharts above use **uppercase labels and unidirectional arrows** to clearly show the iterative nature of record processing. Each `T` (Text) record triggers a loop over its constituent words, while the `E` (End) record finalises execution by setting the Program Counter. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 (3 Marks) **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** **Q: Define a Loader. List and briefly explain the various types of loaders.** **Model Answer:** A **Loader** is a system software that loads the executable program into the main memory, performs necessary relocation, and transfers control to the entry-point for execution. **Types of Loaders:** 1. **Absolute Loader:** Loads programs with fixed, absolute addresses; no relocation needed. Simplest and fastest. 2. **Relocatable Loader:** Loads programs that contain relocatable addresses; adjusts them based on the actual load address using relocation bits. 3. **Dynamic Loader / Dynamic Linking Loader:** Performs linking and relocation at run-time; supports shared libraries (e.g., DLLs, .so). 4. **Bootstrap Loader:** A small loader residing in ROM that loads the operating system kernel into RAM at system startup. --- ### Question A2 (3 Marks) **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** **Q: Differentiate between an Absolute Loader and a Relocatable Loader.** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Absolute Loader | Relocatable Loader | |-----------|----------------|--------------------| | Address assignment | Fixed by programmer | Determined by loader at load-time | | Relocation | Not required | Required using relocation bits | | Flexibility | Low | High | | Speed | Faster (less work) | Slower (more processing) | | Object code format | H, T, E records | H, T, E, R, D, M records with RELOC bits | | Memory utilization | Wasteful | Efficient | --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > As per **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern**, Part B questions carry **14 marks each** with **module-level internal choice**. Below are two alternative full questions (either may appear). --- ### Question B-A (14 Marks) **`[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`** **(a)** Explain the **basic functions of a loader** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of each function in the loading process. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Describe the **absolute loader algorithm** in detail. Apply this algorithm to load the following object program into memory, starting at address `2000H`. Show all memory locations after loading. **(7 Marks)** ``` H EXAM 002000 001A2C T 002000 0F 141033 482039 001036 ... T 00200F 12 001036 481000 ... E 002000 ``` #### Model Solution to (a) **Basic Functions of a Loader:** 1. **Allocation:** Determines the memory space in RAM where the program will be loaded. The OS may assign a free block of memory. 2. **Loading (Memory Copy):** Physically copies the bytes of the executable file from secondary storage into the allocated RAM locations. 3. **Relocation:** Modifies the relocatable addresses in the object code by adding the actual base load address. This ensures that all references to memory addresses correctly point to the relocated positions. 4. **Linking:** Resolves external symbol references between different object modules (typically done by the linker before loading, but the dynamic loader may perform it at run-time). 5. **Execution Transfer (Handoff):** Loads the entry-point address into the **Program Counter (PC)** and transfers CPU control to begin execution. **Block Diagram:** ``` +----------------------+ | Object/Executable File| +----------+-----------+ | v +----------------------+ | LOADER | |----------------------| | 1. Allocation | | 2. Loading | | 3. Relocation | | 4. Linking | | 5. Execution Transfer| +----------+-----------+ | v +----------------------+ | Main Memory (RAM) | | | | Program loaded and | | ready to execute | +----------+-----------+ | v +----------------------+ | CPU executes | | from entry point | +----------------------+ ``` #### Model Solution to (b) **Step 1 — Parse Header Record** $$\text{H: EXAM, StartAddr} = 002000_{H}, \text{Length} = 001A2C_{H}$$ **Step 2 — Set CS_ADDR (Current Memory Address)** $$\text{CS\_ADDR} \leftarrow 002000_{H}$$ **Step 3 — Process First Text Record** $$\text{T-record: Start} = 002000, \text{Length} = 0F_{H}$$ $$0F_{H} = 15_{10} \text{ bytes} = 5 \text{ words}$$ $$\begin{aligned} \text{Memory}[002000] &\leftarrow 14_{H} \\ \text{Memory}[002001] &\leftarrow 10_{H} \\ \text{Memory}[002002] &\leftarrow 33_{H} \\ \text{Memory}[002003] &\leftarrow 48_{H} \\ \text{Memory}[002004] &\leftarrow 20_{H} \end{aligned}$$ After processing 5 words, advance: $$\text{CS\_ADDR} = 002000_{H} + 0F_{H} = 00200F_{H}$$ **Step 4 — Process Second Text Record** $$\text{T-record: Start} = 00200F, \text{Length} = 12_{H}$$ $$12_{H} = 18_{10} \text{ bytes} = 6 \text{ words}$$ $$\begin{aligned} \text{Memory}[00200F] &\leftarrow 00_{H} \\ \text{Memory}[002010] &\leftarrow 10_{H} \\ \text{Memory}[002011] &\leftarrow 36_{H} \\ \text{Memory}[002012] &\leftarrow 48_{H} \\ \text{Memory}[002013] &\leftarrow 10_{H} \\ \text{Memory}[002014] &\leftarrow 00_{H} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5 — Process End Record** $$\text{E-record: EntryPoint} = 002000_{H}$$ $$\text{PC} \leftarrow 002000_{H}$$ **Step 6 — Transfer Control** The loader executes a **jump to PC**, and CPU begins execution at `002000H`. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall 1:** Many students make the mistake of **writing each 3-hex-digit word as a single memory location** (e.g., storing `141033` at one address `2000`). This is **WRONG**. In classical SIC, **each 3-hex-digit word = 1 byte** and occupies a **single consecutive memory location**. Examiners deduct up to **2 marks** for this error. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall 2:** Do not forget to **mention the final Program Counter value**. The transfer of control is the closing action of the loader — without it, the loader is incomplete. --- ### Question B-B (14 Marks) **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`** **(a)** Explain the **algorithm of a relocatable loader** with a flowchart. Clearly show how **relocation bits** are used. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Consider the following relocatable object program. The actual load address (EX_ADDR) is `3000H`. Apply the relocatable loader algorithm and show how the relocatable words are modified. Assume each word has a corresponding relocation bit as shown. **(7 Marks)** ``` H TEST 000000 001005 T 000000 0C 142033 483039 002036 111111 (RELOC bits: 1 1 1 0) T 00000C 09 333444 555666 (RELOC bits: 0 0 0) E 000000 ``` #### Model Solution to (a) **Relocatable Loader Algorithm:** The relocatable loader has the following sequence of operations: 1. **Read the Header record (H)** — extract program name, length, and starting address. 2. **Determine EX_ADDR** — the actual load address in RAM (provided by OS or user). 3. **Read each Text record (T)** — for every word in the record: - Read the object code (3 hex digits). - Read the corresponding relocation bit. - **If RELOC bit = 1:** Add EX_ADDR to the object code word: $\text{NewCode} = \text{OldCode} + \text{EX\_ADDR}$. - **If RELOC bit = 0:** Keep the object code unchanged. - Store the (possibly modified) word at consecutive memory locations. 4. **Process End record (E)** — Compute the entry point as $\text{PC} = \text{EX\_ADDR} + \text{StartAddr}$. 5. **Transfer Control** — Jump to the entry-point address in PC. **Relocation Bit Interpretation:** - A relocation bit is associated with each word in the Text record. - A **bit value of 1** means the word contains a relocatable address that needs adjustment. - A **bit value of 0** means the word is absolute and must not be modified. **Flowchart Reference:** See Section 4.4 of these notes for the complete relocatable loader flowchart. #### Model Solution to (b) **Step 1 — Identify Load Address** $$\text{EX\_ADDR} = 3000_{H}$$ **Step 2 — Process First Text Record** The Text record starts at relative address `000000H` with length `0CH`: $$0C_{H} = 12_{10} \text{ bytes} = 4 \text{ words}$$ Object codes and relocation bits: | Word | Object Code | RELOC Bit | Relocate? | |------|-------------|-----------|-----------| | 1 | `142033` | 1 | Yes | | 2 | `483039` | 1 | Yes | | 3 | `002036` | 1 | Yes | | 4 | `111111` | 0 | No | **Step 3 — Apply Relocation** For Word 1 (object code `142033`, address portion `033`): $$\text{New Address} = 033_{H} + 3000_{H} = 3033_{H}$$ $$\text{Final Word 1} = 142033_{H} \rightarrow 143033_{H}$$ For Word 2 (object code `483039`, address portion `039`): $$\text{New Address} = 039_{H} + 3000_{H} = 3039_{H}$$ $$\text{Final Word 2} = 483039_{H} \rightarrow 483039_{H} \quad \text{(already relocated)}$$ > [!NOTE] > Note that in many KTU textbook examples, the **opcode is not relocated** — only the **address portion** is modified. Some questions specify the format. The general SIC convention is that the last 3 hex digits (the address field) get EX_ADDR added. For Word 3 (object code `002036`): $$\text{New Address} = 036_{H} + 3000_{H} = 3036_{H}$$ $$\text{Final Word 3} = 002036_{H} \rightarrow 003036_{H}$$ For Word 4 (object code `111111`, RELOC = 0): **No change**, stored as `111111H`. **Step 4 — Store in Memory** $$\begin{aligned} \text{Memory}[3000] &\leftarrow 143033_{H} \\ \text{Memory}[3001] &\leftarrow 483039_{H} \\ \text{Memory}[3002] &\leftarrow 003036_{H} \\ \text{Memory}[3003] &\leftarrow 111111_{H} \end{aligned}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall 3:** Examiners strictly check **relocation bit handling**. If a student applies relocation to a word with RELOC = 0, they lose **2 marks**. If they forget to relocate a word with RELOC = 1, they also lose **2 marks**. **Step 5 — Process Second Text Record** Second T-record starts at relative `00000C`, length `09H` = 9 bytes = 3 words. All three words have RELOC = 0, so they are stored unchanged at memory `300CH`, `300DH`, `300EH`: $$\begin{aligned} \text{Memory}[300C] &\leftarrow 333_{H} \\ \text{Memory}[300D] &\leftarrow 444_{H} \\ \text{Memory}[300E] &\leftarrow 555_{H} \\ \text{Memory}[300F] &\leftarrow 666_{H} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 6 — End Record & Entry Point** $$\text{E: EntryPoint} = 000000_{H}$$ $$\text{Actual PC} = 3000_{H} + 000000_{H} = 3000_{H}$$ CPU begins execution at `3000H`. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU ESE — Loader Module** ### Core Definitions to Memorise - **Loader:** System software that places an executable program into RAM, performs relocation, and transfers CPU control to the entry point. - **Absolute Loader:** Loads programs with fixed, pre-assigned memory addresses; no relocation logic. - **Relocatable Loader:** Adjusts relocatable addresses at load-time using RELOC bits. - **Dynamic Loader:** Defers linking and relocation to run-time; supports shared libraries. - **Bootstrap Loader:** Tiny ROM-resident loader that initialises the OS at power-on. - **Linkage Editor:** A separate program that combines multiple object modules before loading. - **Allocation:** Determining the load address (EX_ADDR) in RAM. - **Relocation Bit (RELOC bit):** 1-bit flag per word indicating whether the address needs adjustment. ### Key Records (Object File Format) | Record | Purpose | |--------|---------| | **H** | Header — program name, length, start | | **T** | Text — object codes + relocation bits | | **E** | End — entry point address | | **R** | External Reference (for linker) | | **D** | Define (external symbol definitions) | | **M** | Modification (relocation by symbol) | ### Essential Formulas $$\text{Final Address} = \text{Base Address} + \text{Relative Address}$$ $$\text{PC} = \text{EX\_ADDR} + \text{Start Address}$$ $$\text{Number of Words} = \frac{\text{T-record length in hex (decimal value)}}{3}$$ ### Common Mistakes to Avoid 1. **Do not confuse loader with linker.** Linker combines modules; loader places the final executable in RAM. 2. **Each 3-hex-digit word = 1 byte** in classical SIC, not 1 memory word of 3 bytes. 3. **Relocation bits apply only to address fields**, not opcodes or registers. 4. **Bootstrap loader is in ROM**, not RAM — cannot be modified easily. 5. **Dynamic loading ≠ dynamic linking** — sometimes these are used interchangeably, but linking refers to symbol resolution while loading refers to memory placement. 6. **Always state the final Program Counter value** after processing the End record. ### One-Line Exam Tip > *"The loader reads H, processes every T (with relocation if needed), then E hands control to CPU."* ### Bloom's Taxonomy Tag | Cognitive Level | Coverage in This Topic | |----------------|-----------------------| | **Remember (L1)** | Definitions of loader types | | **Understand (L2)** | Algorithm explanation | | **Apply (L3)** | Numerical relocation problems | | **Analyse (L4)** | Tracing through object code | | **Evaluate (L5)** | Comparing absolute vs relocatable vs dynamic |

topics.title: Debugger
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT403 - COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE AND MICROCONTROLLER
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Debugger

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Debugger** is a sophisticated software (or hardware-software hybrid) tool used by embedded systems engineers and firmware developers to **test, trace, monitor, and diagnose** the runtime behaviour of a program executing on a microcontroller (or in a simulated environment). It allows the user to **pause execution, inspect internal CPU registers, monitor memory contents, set breakpoints, single-step through instructions**, and modify program flow in real-time without permanently altering the target firmware.

In the context of **PCMRT403 (Computer Architecture and Microcontroller)**, the term Debugger specifically refers to **On-Chip Debuggers (OCD)** and **In-Circuit Emulators (ICE)** used during the development of 8051, ARM, PIC, or AVR-based embedded applications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PCMRT403 – Module 4):**
> The Debugger forms the critical bridge between the **Integrated Development Environment (IDE)** running on the host PC and the **target hardware (microcontroller board)**. It converts the abstract software model of execution into observable, controllable hardware behaviour.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **detective investigating a crime scene inside a busy, fast-moving factory**:
- The factory keeps running (your microcontroller executes instructions at MHz/GHz speed).
- The crime has already happened (a bug in the firmware causes incorrect sensor readings).
- You cannot stop the entire factory, but you need to inspect everything precisely.

A **Debugger is your magnifying glass and pause button**. It lets you:
1. **Freeze time** at any point (Breakpoint).
2. **Walk through the factory one step at a time** (Single-Stepping).
3. **Open any worker's notebook** to see what they have written (Register & Memory Inspection).
4. **Install silent alarms** that pause execution when a specific condition occurs (Watchpoints).

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Meaning:** A debugger is essentially a "time machine and microscope" for your code. It lets developers slow down, stop, and look inside a running program to find and fix logical or hardware-related errors.

### 1.3 Key Physical / Standard Metrics

| Metric | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|
| Typical Debug Clock Speed | **1 MHz – 50 MHz** (depending on interface) |
| JTAG Clock (TCK) | Up to **~50 MHz** (ARM Cortex standard) |
| SWD Clock | Up to **~50 MHz** (2-wire alternative to JTAG) |
| Breakpoint Count (Hardware) | Typically **4 – 8** breakpoints |
| Data Watchpoint Count | Typically **2 – 4** watchpoints |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why a Debugger is Essential in Microcontroller Development

Unlike desktop applications, microcontroller firmware interacts with **physical hardware (sensors, actuators, timers, ADCs)** in real time. Bugs may be caused by:
- **Timing errors** (debouncing, race conditions)
- **Register misconfiguration** (wrong GPIO mode, ADC channel)
- **Memory corruption** (stack overflow, pointer errors)
- **Hardware-software mismatches** (incorrect peripheral initialization)

A debugger transforms these invisible failures into **observable state transitions**, allowing deterministic diagnosis.

### 2.2 Structural Layers of a Debugger

A modern microcontroller debugger has three logical layers:

| Layer | Function | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Front-End / GUI** | Provides the user interface (buttons for run, pause, step) | Keil µVision, MPLAB X IDE, STM32CubeIDE |
| **Debug Engine / Protocol Translator** | Translates user actions into standardized debug commands | GDB, pyOCD, OpenOCD |
| **Probe / Interface Hardware** | Physically connects the host PC to the target MCU | J-Link, ST-Link V2, PICKit, ICD3 |

### 2.3 Core Operational Features (High-Yield for KTU)

1. **Breakpoints** – A condition that halts CPU execution at a specific address.
   - **Software Breakpoint:** The debugger patches the original instruction at the target address with a special `BKPT` (ARM) or `BREAK` (8051) opcode. The original instruction is restored before continuing.
   - **Hardware Breakpoint:** Uses dedicated **breakpoint comparators** inside the MCU's debug logic to monitor the Program Counter (PC) without modifying Flash memory.

2. **Single-Stepping** – Executes exactly **one instruction** at a time, then halts, allowing the developer to observe register/memory changes after every cycle.

3. **Watchpoints (Data Breakpoints)** – Halt execution when a specific memory address is **read, written, or both**. Implemented using address comparators in the bus matrix.

4. **Register & Memory Inspection** – Reads live values from CPU registers (R0–R15, PC, SP, PSW) and memory-mapped peripherals (UART buffer, ADC data register) in real time.

5. **Run / Continue / Stop** – Standard execution control commands.

6. **Core Reset and Halt** – Resets the CPU core using the debug logic without resetting the entire system clock.

### 2.4 Standard Debug Interfaces (KTU Hot Topic)

| Interface | Pin Count | Used By | Key Signal Lines |
|---|---|---|---|
| **JTAG** (IEEE 1149.1) | 4–5 wires | ARM, AVR, MSP430, many MCUs | TCK, TMS, TDI, TDO, TRST |
| **SWD** (Serial Wire Debug) | 2 wires | ARM Cortex-M | SWDIO, SWCLK |
| **BDM** (Background Debug Mode) | 1–4 wires | Freescale/NXP ColdFire, HCS08 | BKGD, DSCLK, etc. |
| **OCD** (On-Chip Debug) | Varies | Microchip PIC, dsPIC | PGC, PGD |
| **Spy-Bi-Wire** | 2 wires | MSP430 | SBWTCK, SBWTDIO |

> [!NOTE]
> **JTAG vs SWD (Exam Favourite):**
> JTAG is a **4-wire standard** widely used for boundary-scan testing and debug. ARM introduced **SWD** specifically for Cortex-M cores, offering identical debug capability with **only 2 wires**, freeing up pins for application use.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Numerical Metrics)

While debuggers are largely conceptual, the following performance relationships are commonly tested:

$$
f_{\text{debug}} = \frac{1}{T_{\text{TCK}}} \quad \text{(JTAG/SWD clock frequency, in Hz)}
$$

$$
\text{Download Time (sec)} = \frac{\text{Flash Size (bytes)} \times 8}{\text{Interface Bitrate (bps)}}
$$

$$
\text{Number of Instructions Executed in N cycles} = N \quad \text{(single-step mode)}
$$

$$
\text{Breakpoint Compare} : \text{PC}_{\text{current}} \stackrel{?}{=} \text{PC}_{\text{target}}
$$

**Engineering Utility:** These metrics are used in real-time firmware development houses to estimate the time required for **firmware flashing, field updates (FOTA), and production-line programming** of embedded devices.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Workflow: How a Debugger Halts a Running Microcontroller

The following exhaustive step-by-step analysis describes the exact mechanism used by a typical **ARM Cortex-M SWD debugger** (representative of all modern OCDs).

**Step 1 — Host PC Initiates Debug Session**
The IDE (e.g., STM32CubeIDE) is launched. The user clicks "Debug". The host PC sends a USB command to the debug probe (e.g., ST-Link V2).

**Step 2 — Probe Establishes Physical Link**
The probe drives the **SWDCLK** line and toggles the **SWDIO** line to transmit the **JTAG-to-SWD selection sequence** (`0b0111100111100111...` repeated 16+ times). The MCU's debug port logic locks onto this pattern and acknowledges.

**Step 3 — Debugger Reads DPIDR (Debug Port ID Register)**
The probe performs a read transaction on the DPIDR register to identify the target device. This returns the **DPv0 / DPv2** version and the **MIN (Manufacturer ID)**.

**Step 4 — Core Halt Request**
The debugger writes to the **DHCSR (Debug Halting Control and Status Register)** at address `0xE000EDF0` with the bits:
- `C_DEBUGEN = 1` (enable debug)
- `C_HALT = 1` (request halt)

**Step 5 — CPU Enters Debug State**
The Cortex-M core completes the currently executing instruction, then samples the DHCSR. It transitions to **Debug State**, halting the pipeline. The `S_HALT` bit in DHCSR is set by hardware.

**Step 6 — Register Read via DCRDR/DCRSR**
The debugger reads CPU registers by writing the register number to **DCRSR (Debug Core Register Selector, 0xE000EDF4)**, then reading the value from **DCRDR (Debug Core Register Data, 0xE000EDF8)**.

**Step 7 — User Sets Software Breakpoint**
When the user clicks on line 42 in the source code, the IDE calculates the corresponding **Flash address (e.g., 0x0800_1234)**. The debugger writes the **BKPT instruction (opcode 0xBE00)** into that address. The original opcode is **saved in a shadow RAM** inside the debugger.

**Step 8 — User Clicks "Resume"**
The debugger clears `C_HALT`, setting `C_DEBUGEN = 1`. The core resumes normal execution at full speed.

**Step 9 — Breakpoint Hit**
When the Program Counter reaches the patched address (`0x0800_1234`), the CPU fetches the `BKPT` instruction. This causes a **DebugMonitor exception** or **Prefetch Abort**, the core re-enters Debug State, and the `S_HALT` bit is set again.

**Step 10 — User Single-Steps**
With each click of "Step Over" or "Step Into", the debugger sets the `C_STEP` and `C_HALT` bits in DHCSR. The core executes **exactly one instruction** and halts.

**Step 11 — Watchpoint Trigger**
For a watchpoint on address `0x2000_0050`, the debugger programs the **DWT (Data Watchpoint and Trace) comparator 0** with:
- `COMP = 0x2000_0050`
- `MASK = 0x0` (exact match)
- `FUNCTION = 0b001` (watch on write)

When the CPU performs a store to that address, the comparator asserts, the core halts, and the `S_HALT` bit is set.

**Step 12 — Termination**
The user clicks "Stop Debug". The debugger clears `C_DEBUGEN` in DHCSR. The MCU core resumes normal (un-instrumented) execution. The probe releases the SWD pins to high-impedance.

---

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Numerical Example: Estimating Flash Download Time

A KTU numerical problem may ask: *"Estimate the time taken to program a 256 KB Flash using a debug probe operating at 4 MHz SWD clock, assuming 4 bytes per transfer and ACK overhead of 8 clocks."*

**Step 1 — Identify Given Values**

$$
\text{Flash Size} = 256 \text{ KB} = 256 \times 1024 = 262144 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
f_{\text{SWD}} = 4 \text{ MHz} \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_{\text{clock}} = \frac{1}{4 \times 10^6} = 0.25 \,\mu\text{s}
$$

**Step 2 — Bytes per Transfer Packet**

$$
N_{\text{bytes/transfer}} = 4
$$

**Step 3 — Clocks per Transfer Packet**

$$
N_{\text{clocks/transfer}} = 32 \,(\text{data bits}) + 8 \,(\text{ACK}) = 40 \text{ clocks}
$$

**Step 4 — Total Number of Transfers**

$$
N_{\text{transfers}} = \frac{262144}{4} = 65536
$$

**Step 5 — Total Clocks Required**

$$
N_{\text{total clocks}} = 65536 \times 40 = 2{,}621{,}440 \text{ clocks}
$$

**Step 6 — Total Time**

$$
t_{\text{total}} = 2{,}621{,}440 \times 0.25 \,\mu\text{s} = 655360 \,\mu\text{s} = 0.655 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Result:** The flash programming completes in approximately **655 ms** under ideal conditions. Real-world values are higher due to USB protocol overhead.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Modern Microcontroller Debugger

The following Mermaid diagram maps the end-to-end interaction between the developer's PC, the debug probe, and the target microcontroller during a typical debug session.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Host PC with IDE Keil or STM32CubeIDE] --> B[USB or Serial Link to Probe]
    B --> C[Debug Probe J-Link or ST-Link]
    C --> D{Signal Interface Selection}
    D --> E[JTAG 4-wire TCK TMS TDI TDO]
    D --> F[SWD 2-wire SWDIO SWCLK]
    D --> G[BDM or OCD Interface]
    E --> H[Target Microcontroller Debug Port TAP Controller]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[CPU Core and Pipeline]
    H --> J[Breakpoint Comparator Unit]
    H --> K[Watchpoint DWT Unit]
    I --> L[Memory Mapped Peripherals UART ADC GPIO]
    I --> M[Flash and SRAM]
    A -.Debug Commands.- B
    A -.Register and Memory Reads.- C
    C -.Read DHCSR and DCRDR.- H
```

---

### 4.2 Debug Session State Machine

The following diagram shows the precise state transitions the debugger can issue to the CPU core.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> ResetState
    ResetState --> Running : Set C_DEBUGEN equals 1
    Running --> Halted : Breakpoint Hit or User Pause
    Halted --> Stepping : Set C_STEP and C_HALT equals 1
    Stepping --> Halted : After one instruction
    Halted --> Running : Clear C_HALT equals 1
    Stepping --> Running : Clear C_STEP equals 1
    Halted --> ResetState : System Reset or Stop Debug
    Running --> ResetState : User Stop Debug
    Halted --> [*]
    Running --> [*]
```

---

### 4.3 Software Breakpoint Mechanism (Step-by-Step)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Dev as Developer IDE
    participant Probe as Debug Probe
    participant MCU as Target MCU Flash
    Dev->>Probe: Set breakpoint at Line 42 Address 0x08001234
    Probe->>MCU: Read original opcode at 0x08001234
    MCU-->>Probe: Original Opcode 0x4A01
    Probe->>Probe: Save original opcode in shadow buffer
    Probe->>MCU: Write BKPT opcode 0xBE00 to 0x08001234
    Dev->>Probe: Resume execution
    Probe->>MCU: C_HALT equals 0
    MCU->>MCU: CPU executes instructions sequentially
    MCU->>MCU: PC reaches 0x08001234
    MCU->>Probe: S_HALT equals 1 Debug State entered
    Probe->>Dev: Breakpoint hit reported
    Dev->>Probe: Inspect registers and memory
    Probe->>Dev: Return register values via DCRDR
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above schematics represent **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** as per the protocol. They focus on logical interactions and signal flow rather than physical pin layouts.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`
**Define a Debugger. List any four features of a microcontroller debugger.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Debugger** is a software tool (often used in conjunction with hardware probes) that allows the developer to load, execute, and monitor a program running on a microcontroller in a controlled, step-by-step manner. It provides visibility into the internal state of the CPU and memory during runtime.

**Four key features:**
1. **Breakpoints** – Halt execution at predefined instructions.
2. **Single-Stepping** – Execute one instruction at a time.
3. **Register & Memory Inspection** – View live values of CPU registers and memory locations.
4. **Watchpoints** – Halt execution when a specific memory address is accessed/modified.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`
**Differentiate between JTAG and SWD debug interfaces.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | JTAG (IEEE 1149.1) | SWD (Serial Wire Debug) |
|---|---|---|
| **Number of wires** | 4 or 5 (TCK, TMS, TDI, TDO, TRST) | 2 (SWDIO, SWCLK) |
| **Architecture** | Daisy-chain TAP controller | Packet-based, point-to-point |
| **Standard** | Industry standard (IEEE 1149.1) | ARM proprietary (Cortex-M) |
| **Pin count impact** | Occupies more GPIO | Saves pins for application use |
| **Boundary Scan** | Yes (built-in) | No |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks – Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**`[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]`**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of a typical microcontroller debugger with a neat block diagram. Differentiate between software and hardware breakpoints. (7 Marks)

**(b)** With a step-by-step explanation, describe the working of the **On-Chip Debug (OCD)** mechanism in an ARM Cortex-M microcontroller. Mention the role of DHCSR, DCRDR, and DCRSR registers. (7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

A typical microcontroller debugger consists of three layers:

1. **Front-End (GUI)**: The IDE (e.g., Keil µVision, STM32CubeIDE) provides buttons for *Run, Stop, Step Over, Step Into, Reset*. It displays source code, registers, memory, and peripherals.

2. **Debug Engine / Protocol Translator**: Converts user actions into standard debug protocol packets. Examples: GDB (GNU Debugger), OpenOCD, pyOCD.

3. **Probe Hardware**: The physical adapter (J-Link, ST-Link, PICKit) that interfaces between the USB port of the host PC and the debug pins of the target MCU.

**Software vs Hardware Breakpoints:**

| Feature | Software Breakpoint | Hardware Breakpoint |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanism** | Original instruction replaced by `BKPT` opcode in Flash/RAM | Uses on-chip comparator matching PC value |
| **Memory modification** | Yes (Flash/RAM is patched) | No modification of program memory |
| **Count limit** | Unlimited (limited by RAM if running from RAM) | Limited (typically 4–8 comparators) |
| **Read-only memory** | Cannot be set in ROM | Can be set in ROM/Flash |
| **Use case** | Source-level debugging in RAM/Flash | Production code, OTP, ROM debugging |

**Model Solution for (b):**

The **On-Chip Debug (OCD)** mechanism in ARM Cortex-M uses the following sequence:

1. **Debug Entry**: The debug probe (ST-Link/J-Link) drives the SWDIO/SWCLK lines to issue the SWD activation sequence. The MCU's Debug Access Port (DAP) recognizes the pattern and enters debug mode.

2. **Enable Debug**: The debugger writes to **DHCSR (Debug Halting Control and Status Register, address 0xE000EDF0)** with the value that sets `C_DEBUGEN = 1`. This enables the debug logic.

3. **Halt Request**: Setting `C_HALT = 1` in DHCSR requests the CPU to halt. After the current instruction completes, the core enters the **Debug State** and the `S_HALT` bit becomes 1.

4. **Register Access (DCRSR/DCRDR)**:
   - The debugger writes the desired register number (e.g., R0 = 0, PC = 15) to **DCRSR (Debug Core Register Selector, 0xE000EDF4)**.
   - Setting the `REGWnR` bit in DCRSR initiates a write; otherwise, it is a read.
   - The register value is transferred to/from **DCRDR (Debug Core Register Data, 0xE000EDF8)**.

5. **Single-Step**: Setting `C_STEP = 1` and `C_HALT = 1` makes the core execute exactly one instruction and re-enter Debug State.

6. **Breakpoint**: When the PC matches a pre-programmed comparator (in FPB – Flash Patch and Breakpoint unit), the core halts automatically. The debugger reports the halt cause via the `DFSR (Debug Fault Status Register)`.

7. **Resume**: Clearing `C_HALT` resumes execution; the debug probe may disconnect from the SWD pins.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout #1:**
> Many students incorrectly state that a debugger *modifies the Flash memory* for every breakpoint. While **software breakpoints do** patch the Flash with a `BKPT` instruction, **hardware breakpoints use on-chip comparators** and do not alter memory. Confusing these two will cost you 2–3 marks.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`**

**(a)** What are the common debug interfaces used in microcontrollers? Explain JTAG with its signal lines. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Explain the concept of **In-Circuit Emulation (ICE)**. Compare it with **On-Chip Debugging (OCD)**. Mention the advantages of OCD over ICE. (7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Common Debug Interfaces:**
1. **JTAG** (IEEE 1149.1) – 4-wire standard
2. **SWD** (Serial Wire Debug) – 2-wire ARM standard
3. **BDM** (Background Debug Mode) – Freescale/NXP
4. **OCD/PICkit** – Microchip PIC microcontrollers
5. **Spy-Bi-Wire** – Texas Instruments MSP430

**JTAG Signal Lines:**

| Signal | Full Name | Direction | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| **TCK** | Test Clock | Input to target | Provides the clock for TAP controller state transitions |
| **TMS** | Test Mode Select | Input to target | Sampled on rising edge of TCK to sequence the TAP state machine |
| **TDI** | Test Data In | Input to target | Serial data input to the instruction/data registers |
| **TDO** | Test Data Out | Output from target | Serial data output from the instruction/data registers |
| **TRST** | Test Reset (optional) | Input to target | Asynchronous reset of the TAP controller |

The JTAG interface is based on a **16-state finite state machine** (TAP controller) defined by the IEEE 1149.1 standard. It supports boundary-scan testing of PCB interconnects and CPU debug.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**In-Circuit Emulation (ICE):**
In older microcontroller families (e.g., 8051 with bond-out chips), an **In-Circuit Emulator** was a special hardware device that physically replaced the target MCU on the board. It used a **bond-out version** of the silicon with extra debug pins, allowing complete visibility into internal buses, registers, and execution flow.

**Comparison: ICE vs OCD**

| Parameter | ICE | OCD |
|---|---|---|
| **Hardware** | External emulator box | Built into the MCU silicon |
| **Pin access** | Uses bond-out chip (extra pins) | Uses standard debug pins (SWDIO/SWCLK) |
| **Cost** | Very high (₹50,000–₹2,00,000) | Low (probe ~₹500–₹5,000) |
| **Real-time trace** | Full trace buffer | Limited (ETM/ITM for high-end MCUs) |
| **Availability** | Phased out | Universal in modern MCUs |
| **Target MCU replaced** | Yes | No |

**Advantages of OCD over ICE:**
1. **No bond-out chip required** – uses standard production silicon.
2. **Lower cost** – debug probes are inexpensive.
3. **Portability** – works across entire MCU family.
4. **Field debugging possible** – production units can be debugged.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout #2:**
> When asked to "explain JTAG", do NOT merely list the 4 signal lines. The KTU board examiner expects at least one full sentence on the **TAP controller state machine** and the role of JTAG in **boundary scan testing**, not just CPU debug. Skipping this loses 2 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Debugger** = software/hardware tool to test, trace, and diagnose microcontroller firmware.
- **Layers:** Front-end GUI → Debug Engine → Probe Hardware.
- **Core features:** Breakpoints, Single-Stepping, Watchpoints, Register & Memory Inspection.
- **Software Breakpoint** → patches Flash/RAM with `BKPT` opcode; **unlimited count**; cannot be set in ROM.
- **Hardware Breakpoint** → uses on-chip comparator on PC; **limited count (4–8)**; works in ROM.
- **Watchpoint** → halts on memory read/write using DWT comparators in ARM Cortex-M.
- **JTAG** → IEEE 1149.1, 4 wires (TCK, TMS, TDI, TDO), supports boundary scan.
- **SWD** → ARM 2-wire (SWDIO, SWCLK), pin-efficient alternative to JTAG.
- **OCD (On-Chip Debug)** → built into modern MCUs; **no bond-out chip**; low cost.
- **ICE (In-Circuit Emulation)** → legacy technique; **bond-out chip**; phased out.
- **Key ARM Registers:** DHCSR (0xE000EDF0), DCRSR (0xE000EDF4), DCRDR (0xE000EDF8).
- **Debugger stop ≠ CPU off** – CPU is halted but peripherals (timers, UART) may continue running.
- **Numerical tip:** Download time ≈ (Flash size × 8) / interface bitrate + protocol overhead.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Memory - RAM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT403 - COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE AND MICROCONTROLLER
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Types of Memory - RAM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Random Access Memory (RAM)** is a type of **volatile primary memory** in a computer system that allows data to be read from or written to any storage location in approximately the same amount of time, regardless of the physical position of the data within the memory. It is the main working memory of a computer that temporarily holds the data and instructions the CPU is actively using. RAM is termed "volatile" because it loses all stored contents when power is removed.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCMRT403 - Computer Architecture and Microcontroller)**, RAM is classified into two principal semiconductor-based categories:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> - **SRAM (Static RAM)** – Uses bistable latches (flip-flops) to store each bit. Data persists as long as power is supplied, with **no need for periodic refreshing**.
