> - **DRAM (Dynamic RAM)** – Stores each bit as a charge on a tiny capacitor. Because capacitors leak charge, DRAM must be **periodically refreshed (recharged)** every few milliseconds.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Office Desk vs. The Whiteboard**

Imagine a busy office worker (the CPU) at his desk:

- **SRAM** is like a set of **post-it notes** pinned directly to the desk surface. Each note holds information reliably without falling off, and the worker can grab any note instantly. However, these post-it notes are **expensive** (each one needs 6 transistors) and take up a lot of desk space. The information stays as long as the office light (power) is on.

- **DRAM** is like a set of **chalkboards** in the office. Each board stores a small charge (like chalk dust on a board). The boards are very cheap and compact, but the chalk dust **fades with time**, so a cleaning person (the refresh circuit) must come by every few milliseconds to redraw the information. If the office power goes out, all the chalkboards go blank.

**The "Random Access" part** simply means: no matter which post-it or chalkboard you want, you can walk directly to it in the **same time** — you don't have to read from the first to the last like a tape. This is contrasted with **Sequential Access Memory** (like magnetic tapes).

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics

- **Access Time ($t_{acc}$):** Typically **1–10 ns** for SRAM; **50–100 ns** for classic DRAM.
- **Density:** DRAM offers **~5–10× higher density** than SRAM.
- **Power Consumption per bit:** DRAM ≈ **0.1 µW/bit**, SRAM ≈ **1–5 µW/bit** (active).
- **Refresh Interval:** DRAM ≈ **every 64 ms** (standard JEDEC specification).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Classification of RAM

RAM is broadly divided based on the **storage cell technology**:

### 2.1.1 Static RAM (SRAM)

**Operational Characteristics:**
- Each bit is stored in a **6-transistor (6T) bistable latch** made of two cross-coupled CMOS inverters.
- The latch has **two stable states**: logic `1` (Node A high, Node $\bar{A}$ low) and logic `0` (Node A low, Node $\bar{A}$ high).
- Two additional **access transistors** (M5, M6) connect the cell to the **bit lines** (BL and $\overline{BL}$) when the **word line (WL)** is activated.
- **No refresh** is needed — the cross-coupled inverters actively drive the stored value as long as Vdd is present.
- Used for **Cache memory (L1, L2, L3)** and CPU registers.

### 2.1.2 Dynamic RAM (DRAM)

**Operational Characteristics:**
- Each bit is stored as a **charge on a capacitor** ($C_s$) accessed through a single **access transistor** (1T-1C cell).
- Reading is **destructive** — the charge is shared with the bit line, partially or fully discharging the storage capacitor. The cell must be **rewritten** (refreshed) after every read.
- The capacitor **leaks charge** through the transistor's subthreshold leakage, so a periodic refresh cycle is mandatory.
- Used for **main memory (DDR SDRAM)**.

### 2.1.3 Synchronous DRAM (SDRAM) and DDR Family

- **SDRAM** synchronizes operations with the system **clock signal**, enabling pipelined/burst transfers.
- **DDR (Double Data Rate)** transfers data on **both rising and falling edges** of the clock, doubling the bandwidth.
- Variants: **DDR, DDR2, DDR3, DDR4, DDR5, LPDDR** for mobile devices.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Cache uses **SRAM**, Main Memory uses **DRAM/SDRAM**. The CPU-to-Memory speed gap is bridged by the cache hierarchy.

## 2.2 Memory Organization and Address Decoding

A complete memory system is built by arranging multiple memory chips in a structured array:

- **Capacity Word Size:** A memory chip stores $2^n$ words of $m$ bits each, where $n$ = number of address lines.
- **Total bits in chip:** $N = 2^n \times m$.
- **Address Decoding:** Two main methods are used to map CPU address lines to memory chip pins:
  - **Linear Decoding** (1-D or 2-D decoding)
  - **Binary Decoding** using decoder ICs (e.g., 2-to-4, 3-to-8)

### 2.2.1 2D Memory Organization (Row-Column Decoding)

To reduce the number of decoder pins, large memories use a **two-dimensional address split**:

- Address lines $A_{n-1} \dots A_0$ are split into two halves:
  - **Row address** (e.g., upper $n/2$ lines) → drives the **row decoder** (selects one word line out of $2^{n/2}$).
  - **Column address** (e.g., lower $n/2$ lines) → drives the **column decoder / multiplexer** (selects one bit line out of $2^{n/2}$).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Parameter | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Total storage capacity** | $N = 2^n \times m$ | $n$ = address lines, $m$ = data bits per word |
| **Number of chips needed** | $N_{chips} = \dfrac{\text{Required Total Memory}}{\text{Per-Chip Memory}}$ | Dimensionless |
| **Memory expansion (Word size)** | $N_{chips} = \dfrac{\text{New word size } m'}{\text{Existing word size } m}$ | Parallel connection |
| **Memory expansion (Word count)** | $N_{chips} = \dfrac{\text{New word count } 2^{n'}}{\text{Existing word count } 2^n}$ | Series connection |
| **DRAM Refresh time** | $T_{refresh} = \dfrac{\text{Total rows}}{2 \times \text{Refresh rate}}$ | $T_{refresh} \leq 64$ ms (JEDEC) |
| **Memory access time** | $t_{acc} = t_{decoder} + t_{cell} + t_{sense}$ | $t_{sense}$ dominant in DRAM |
| **SDRAM Bandwidth** | $BW = \text{Bus Width} \times \text{Data Rate}$ | Bytes/second |
| **DDR effective frequency** | $f_{eff} = 2 \times f_{clock}$ | Hz |
| **DRAM cell area** | $A_{cell} \propto 6 F^2$ (modern) to $8 F^2$ | $F$ = minimum feature size |
| **SRAM cell area** | $A_{cell} \approx 120 F^2$ (6T) | Larger due to 6 transistors |
| **Dynamic Power (DRAM)** | $P_{dyn} = C \cdot V_{dd}^2 \cdot f$ | $C$ = capacitance being switched |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Table Rule:** In KTU exam answers, when computing number of chips, always show: **Total required memory / Memory per chip**, with units clearly stated.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **SRAM in cache** enables sub-nanosecond CPU access (e.g., L1 cache ~ 1–4 cycles).
- **DRAM in main memory** provides cost-effective gigabytes (e.g., 16 GB DDR4 in laptops).
- **Embedded Microcontrollers** (e.g., 8051, ARM Cortex-M) use on-chip SRAM for variables/stack and **Flash/EEPROM** for program storage.
- **Memory address decoding** is critical in interfacing memory chips to microprocessors (e.g., 8086, ARM) — a frequent KTU 8086 interfacing question.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations

## 3.1 Memory Chip Sizing & Capacity Calculation

**Problem:** A memory chip has **14 address lines** and stores **8-bit words**. Determine:
1. Number of memory locations
2. Total memory capacity in bytes
3. Total number of bits stored

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Identify the number of address lines.**
Given: $n = 14$ address lines, $m = 8$ bits per word.

**Step 2: Calculate the number of memory locations.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Number of locations} &= 2^n \\
&= 2^{14} \\
&= 16384 \text{ locations} \\
&= 16\text{K locations}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate the total memory capacity in bytes.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total capacity (bytes)} &= 2^n \times m \\
&= 2^{14} \times 8 \text{ bits} \\
&= 16384 \times 8 \text{ bits} \\
&= 131072 \text{ bits} \\
&= \dfrac{131072}{8} \text{ bytes} \\
&= 16384 \text{ bytes} \\
&= 16 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Express in standard notation.**
A $16\text{K} \times 8$ memory chip holds $16384$ locations of $8$ bits each.

## 3.2 Memory Expansion: Word Size Expansion (Increasing Data Width)

**Problem:** You have four $16\text{K} \times 4$ memory chips. Design a $16\text{K} \times 16$ memory system. How many chips are needed? Show the address line mapping.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Identify specifications.**
- Available chip: $16\text{K} \times 4$ (capacity $= 2^{14} \times 4$ bits)
- Required memory: $16\text{K} \times 16$ (capacity $= 2^{14} \times 16$ bits)

**Step 2: Compute the number of chips required.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{chips} &= \dfrac{\text{Required word size}}{\text{Existing word size}} \\
&= \dfrac{16 \text{ bits}}{4 \text{ bits}} \\
&= 4 \text{ chips}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a **word-size (horizontal) expansion**. The 4 chips are connected in **parallel**, sharing the same address lines but each contributing 4 bits of the 16-bit data bus.

**Step 3: Address line mapping.**
Since word count is unchanged ($16\text{K}$), the address line count remains $n = 14$.
- Address lines $A_{13} \dots A_0$ are connected in parallel to all four chips.
- Data lines are split: $D_0–D_3$ → Chip 1, $D_4–D_7$ → Chip 2, $D_8–D_{11}$ → Chip 3, $D_{12}–D_{15}$ → Chip 4.
- $\overline{CS}$ (chip select) lines of all four chips are tied together (active simultaneously).

## 3.3 Memory Expansion: Word Count Expansion (Increasing Depth)

**Problem:** You have four $16\text{K} \times 8$ memory chips. Design a $64\text{K} \times 8$ memory system. How many chips are needed? Show the address decoding logic.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Identify specifications.**
- Available chip: $16\text{K} \times 8$ ($2^{14}$ locations, $8$-bit data)
- Required memory: $64\text{K} \times 8$ ($2^{16}$ locations, $8$-bit data)

**Step 2: Compute the number of chips required.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{chips} &= \dfrac{\text{Required word count}}{\text{Existing word count}} \\
&= \dfrac{2^{16}}{2^{14}} \\
&= 2^{2} = 4 \text{ chips}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a **word-count (vertical) expansion**. The 4 chips are connected in **series** (one at a time selected).

**Step 3: Address line mapping.**
Total address lines needed: $n_{new} = 16$ (since $64\text{K} = 2^{16}$).
- Lower **14 address lines** ($A_{13} \dots A_0$) go to every chip (to address $16\text{K}$ locations within each chip).
- Upper **2 address lines** ($A_{15}, A_{14}$) go to a **2-to-4 decoder** to select one of the 4 chips.
- The decoder output $\overline{Y_0}, \overline{Y_1}, \overline{Y_2}, \overline{Y_3}$ is connected to $\overline{CS}$ of chips 1, 2, 3, 4 respectively.

**Step 4: Address ranges for each chip.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Chip 1 (selected when } A_{15}A_{14} = 00) &: 0000\text{H} - 3\text{FFFH} \\
\text{Chip 2 (selected when } A_{15}A_{14} = 01) &: 4000\text{H} - 7\text{FFFH} \\
\text{Chip 3 (selected when } A_{15}A_{14} = 10) &: 8000\text{H} - \text{BFFFH} \\
\text{Chip 4 (selected when } A_{15}A_{14} = 11) &: \text{C000H} - \text{FFFFH}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 DRAM Refresh Calculation

**Problem:** A DRAM has **8192 rows** and the JEDEC standard requires all rows to be refreshed within **64 ms**. Calculate the refresh interval per row.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Identify parameters.**
- Number of rows $= 8192 = 2^{13}$
- Total refresh window $T_{max} = 64$ ms

**Step 2: Calculate time available per refresh row.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{row} &= \dfrac{T_{max}}{2 \times \text{Number of rows}} \\
&= \dfrac{64 \text{ ms}}{2 \times 8192} \\
&= \dfrac{64}{16384} \text{ ms} \\
&= 3.906 \text{ µs per row}
\end{aligned}
$$

The factor of 2 accounts for the auto-refresh (single row per REF command). This refresh interval must be respected by the memory controller.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 SRAM (6T) Cell Schematic

```mermaid
graph LR
    WL[Word Line WL] --> M5
    WL --> M6
    M5 --- BL[Bit Line BL]
    M6 --- BLBAR[Bit Line BLbar]
    M1[M1 PMOS] --- M2[M2 NMOS]
    M2 --- M3[M3 NMOS]
    M3 --- M4[M4 PMOS]
    M1 --- NODE_A[Storage Node A]
    M3 --- NODE_A
    M2 --- NODE_ABAR[Storage Node Abar]
    M4 --- NODE_ABAR
    M5 --- NODE_A
    M6 --- NODE_ABAR
    VDD[Vdd] --- M1
    VDD --- M4
    GND[GND] --- M2
    GND --- M3
```

**Description:** The 6T SRAM cell has two cross-coupled CMOS inverters (M1-M2 and M3-M4) forming a bistable latch with two stable nodes A and $\bar{A}$. Access transistors M5 and M6 connect these nodes to the differential bit lines BL and $\overline{BL}$ only when the word line WL is asserted HIGH during a read/write operation.

## 4.2 DRAM (1T-1C) Cell Schematic

```mermaid
graph LR
    WL[Word Line WL] --> TRANS[Access Transistor M1]
    TRANS --- CAP[Storage Capacitor Cs]
    CAP --- GND[GND]
    TRANS --- BL[Bit Line BL]
    SA[Sense Amplifier] --- BL
    REF[Refresh Circuit] --- SA
    WRC[Write Circuit] --- SA
    RDC[Read Circuit] --- SA
```

**Description:** The DRAM cell uses a single access transistor (M1) controlled by the word line. The bit is stored as a charge in capacitor $C_s$. The sense amplifier detects the small voltage change on the bit line during read; the refresh circuit periodically rewrites the contents. During read, the charge in $C_s$ is shared with the bit-line capacitance, partially destroying the original charge — hence the destructive read followed by a write-back.

## 4.3 Memory System Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    CPU[CPU / Microprocessor] -->|Address Bus ABUS| ADDR_DEC[Address Decoder]
    CPU -->|Data Bus DBUS| MEM[Memory Array]
    CPU -->|Control Signals RD WR| MEM
    ADDR_DEC -->|Chip Select CSbar| MEM
    MEM --- RAM1[SRAM Cache L1 L2 L3]
    MEM --- RAM2[DRAM Main Memory]
    MEM --- ROM[ROM BIOS Firmware]
    RAM1 --> CACHE[Fast Small SRAM]
    RAM2 --> MAIN[Large Cheap DRAM]
    ROM --> BOOT[Non Volatile Boot]
```

**Description:** This block diagram shows a typical computer memory hierarchy. The CPU communicates via a tri-state address bus, bidirectional data bus, and control signals (RD, WR, MREQ). The address decoder activates the appropriate memory chip's $\overline{CS}$ pin based on the upper address bits. SRAM acts as cache (fast, small); DRAM acts as main memory (slow, large); ROM stores boot firmware.

## 4.4 Word-Count Expansion (Vertical) Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    CPU[CPU 16 bit Address] --> A_LO[Address A0 to A13 - 14 lines]
    CPU --> A_HI[Address A14 A15 - 2 lines]
    A_LO --> CHIP1[Chip1 16K x 8]
    A_LO --> CHIP2[Chip2 16K x 8]
    A_LO --> CHIP3[Chip3 16K x 8]
    A_LO --> CHIP4[Chip4 16K x 8]
    A_HI --> DEC[2 to 4 Decoder]
    DEC -->|Y0bar| CHIP1
    DEC -->|Y1bar| CHIP2
    DEC -->|Y2bar| CHIP3
    DEC -->|Y3bar| CHIP4
    CHIP1 --> DBUS[8 bit Data Bus]
    CHIP2 --> DBUS
    CHIP3 --> DBUS
    CHIP4 --> DBUS
```

**Description:** Vertical (depth) expansion using a 2-to-4 decoder. The lower 14 address lines are broadcast to all chips; the upper 2 address lines select exactly one chip at a time via the decoder. The 8-bit data bus is shared among all chips, but only the selected chip drives the bus.

## 4.5 Memory Hierarchy Pyramid

```mermaid
graph TD
    L1[Level 1: Registers - 1 ns - Few KB] --> L2[Level 2: L1 Cache SRAM - 2 to 5 ns - 64 KB]
    L2 --> L3[Level 3: L2 Cache SRAM - 10 to 20 ns - 512 KB to 8 MB]
    L3 --> L4[Level 4: L3 Cache SRAM DRAM - 30 to 50 ns - 8 to 32 MB]
    L4 --> L5[Level 5: Main Memory DRAM - 100 ns - 8 to 32 GB]
    L5 --> L6[Level 6: SSD - 100 us - 500 GB to 2 TB]
    L6 --> L7[Level 7: HDD - 10 ms - 1 to 10 TB]
```

**Description:** The classic memory pyramid — smaller and faster memories near the top, larger and slower at the bottom. SRAM is used for cache levels (L1, L2, L3). DRAM is the main memory. Disk storage (SSD, HDD) is non-volatile secondary storage.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Q: Differentiate between Static RAM (SRAM) and Dynamic RAM (DRAM). Mention one application of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | SRAM | DRAM |
|---|---|---|
| Storage element | 6-transistor bistable latch | 1-transistor + 1 capacitor |
| Refresh required | No | Yes (every ~64 ms) |
| Density | Low (large cell) | High (small cell) |
| Speed | Fast (1–10 ns) | Slower (50–100 ns) |
| Power consumption | Higher per bit | Lower per bit |
| Cost per bit | Higher | Lower |
| Volatile | Yes | Yes |
| **Application** | **Cache memory (L1/L2/L3)** | **Main memory (DDR SDRAM)** |

**[3 Marks: 1 mark definition each + 1 mark application]**

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Q: What is meant by "destructive read" in DRAM? How is it handled?**

**Model Answer:**
In DRAM, the act of reading a bit involves connecting the storage capacitor $C_s$ to the bit line. The charge stored in $C_s$ redistributes onto the bit-line capacitance $C_{BL}$ (where $C_{BL} \gg C_s$), causing a partial discharge of $C_s$. This **destroys the original stored charge** — hence the term **"destructive read."**

**Handling:** Immediately after sensing, the sense amplifier **rewrites (refreshes)** the same data back into the cell by re-driving the bit line to the detected voltage. This is why DRAM reads automatically perform a write-back. Additionally, periodic refresh cycles (every 64 ms) restore charge lost to subthreshold leakage.

**[3 Marks: 1 mark definition + 1 mark cause + 1 mark handling]**

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**Q: (a)** With a neat diagram, explain the internal organization of a 6T SRAM cell. Discuss its read and write operations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A computer system uses a $16\text{K} \times 8$ RAM chip. Calculate the number of address lines, data lines, and total memory capacity. How many such chips are required to design a $64\text{K} \times 16$ memory system? Show the address decoding arrangement. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 6T SRAM Cell (7 marks):**

The **6T SRAM cell** consists of:
- **Two cross-coupled CMOS inverters** (M1-M2 and M3-M4) forming a bistable latch with two stable storage nodes A and $\bar{A}$.
- **Two NMOS access transistors** (M5 and M6) controlled by the **Word Line (WL)**.
- The cell is connected to the **Bit Line (BL)** and its complement **$\overline{BL}$** through the access transistors.

**READ Operation:**
1. The WL is asserted HIGH, turning ON M5 and M6.
2. The stored value at node A drives BL, and its complement at $\bar{A}$ drives $\overline{BL}$ (or vice-versa).
3. A small voltage difference develops between BL and $\overline{BL}$.
4. The **sense amplifier** detects this differential and outputs the stored bit.
5. **Crucially, the read is non-destructive** — the latch actively restores the value during sensing.

**WRITE Operation:**
1. The WL is asserted HIGH.
2. The new data bit is driven strongly onto BL, and its complement onto $\overline{BL}$.
3. The access transistors M5 and M6 force the latch nodes A and $\bar{A}$ to the new values.
4. Once the latch flips, it holds the new value even after WL is de-asserted.

**Part (b) — Memory System Design (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Given and required specifications.**

$$
\text{Given chip} = 16\text{K} \times 8 \quad | \quad \text{Required} = 64\text{K} \times 16
$$

**Step 2: Number of address lines and data lines for given chip.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Address lines} &= \log_2(16\text{K}) = \log_2(2^{14}) = 14 \\
\text{Data lines} &= 8
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Total memory capacity of the given chip.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Capacity} &= 16\text{K} \times 8 = 2^{14} \times 8 = 131072 \text{ bits} = 16 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compute number of chips for the $64\text{K} \times 16$ system.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{chips} &= \dfrac{\text{Required memory}}{\text{Per-chip memory}} = \dfrac{64\text{K} \times 16}{16\text{K} \times 8} = \dfrac{2^{16} \times 16}{2^{14} \times 8} \\
&= 2^{2} \times 2^{1} = 4 \times 2 = 8 \text{ chips}
\end{aligned}
$$

(Equivalent view: word-size expansion needs $16/8 = 2$ chips; word-count expansion needs $64\text{K}/16\text{K} = 4$ such pairs; total $= 2 \times 4 = 8$.)

**Step 5: Address line arrangement.**
- New total address lines $= \log_2(64\text{K}) = 16$.
- Lower $14$ address lines ($A_{13} \dots A_0$) → all 8 chips (in pairs).
- Upper $2$ address lines ($A_{15}, A_{14}$) → **2-to-4 decoder** to select one of 4 chip-pairs.
- Each pair: 2 chips with shared $\overline{CS}$, one giving bits $D_0–D_7$ and the other $D_8–D_{15}$.

**Address ranges:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{15}A_{14} = 00 &: 0000\text{H} - 3\text{FFFH} \\
A_{15}A_{14} = 01 &: 4000\text{H} - 7\text{FFFH} \\
A_{15}A_{14} = 10 &: 8000\text{H} - \text{BFFFH} \\
A_{15}A_{14} = 11 &: \text{C000H} - \text{FFFFH}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**Q: (a)** Explain the structure and operation of a 1T-1C DRAM cell. What is the role of the sense amplifier? Why is refresh necessary? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A $32\text{K} \times 8$ memory system is to be designed using $8\text{K} \times 4$ RAM chips. Determine the number of chips required and draw the expansion structure with a suitable decoder. Show the address range for each chip. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — DRAM Cell, Sense Amplifier, Refresh (7 marks):**

**1T-1C DRAM Cell Structure:**
- A single **NMOS access transistor** (M1) controlled by the **Word Line (WL)**.
- A **storage capacitor** ($C_s$) connected between the transistor and ground.
- Bit stored as **charge** on $C_s$ (charged = 1, discharged = 0).

**Read Operation:**
1. WL is asserted, M1 turns ON.
2. Charge on $C_s$ is **shared** with the bit line capacitance $C_{BL}$ (where $C_{BL} \gg C_s$).
3. A small voltage perturbation $\Delta V$ appears on the bit line.
4. **Sense amplifier** detects this tiny differential and amplifies it to a full logic level.
5. The read is **destructive** — $C_s$ loses charge. The sense amplifier automatically **rewrites** the value back to $C_s$.

**Write Operation:**
1. WL is asserted.
2. The bit line is driven to the desired voltage (Vdd for 1, GND for 0).
3. This voltage charges or discharges $C_s$ through M1.

**Role of Sense Amplifier:**
- Detects a **small voltage change** (a few hundred mV) on the bit line during a read.
- Amplifies it to a **full CMOS logic level** (0 V or Vdd).
- Performs **automatic write-back** after read, refreshing the cell.
- Used during **refresh cycles** to recharge capacitor cells.

**Why Refresh is Necessary:**
- Storage capacitor $C_s$ is **very small** (~ femtofarads).
- Charge **leaks** through the access transistor's subthreshold leakage and capacitor's own leakage.
- If not refreshed periodically, the stored bit would **disappear** within milliseconds.
- **JEDEC standard** requires all rows to be refreshed within **64 ms**.

**Part (b) — Memory Expansion (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Given and required.**

$$
\text{Given chip} = 8\text{K} \times 4 \quad | \quad \text{Required} = 32\text{K} \times 8
$$

**Step 2: Compute number of chips.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{chips} &= \dfrac{32\text{K} \times 8}{8\text{K} \times 4} = \dfrac{2^{15} \times 8}{2^{13} \times 4} = 2^{2} \times 2^{1} = 8 \text{ chips}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Organization.**
- Word-size expansion: $8/4 = 2$ chips per row (horizontal) → gives $8\text{K} \times 8$.
- Word-count expansion: $32\text{K}/8\text{K} = 4$ such rows (vertical) → gives $32\text{K} \times 8$.

**Step 4: Address line mapping.**
- Total new address lines $= \log_2(32\text{K}) = 15$.
- Lower $13$ address lines ($A_{12} \dots A_0$) → all 8 chips (for $8\text{K}$ selection within each chip).
- Upper $2$ address lines ($A_{14}, A_{13}$) → **2-to-4 decoder** to select one of the 4 rows.
- Each row has 2 chips: one provides lower nibble $D_0–D_3$, other provides upper nibble $D_4–D_7$.

**Address ranges:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{14}A_{13} = 00 &: 0000\text{H} - 1\text{FFFH (Row 0)} \\
A_{14}A_{13} = 01 &: 2000\text{H} - 3\text{FFFH (Row 1)} \\
A_{14}A_{13} = 10 &: 4000\text{H} - 5\text{FFFH (Row 2)} \\
A_{14}A_{13} = 11 &: 6000\text{H} - 7\text{FFFH (Row 3)}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Writing 8 chips but failing to clarify that they form 4 rows × 2 chips/row. Examiners expect the **organization** (horizontal/vertical) explicitly mentioned.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Forgetting to compute and state the **new address line count** (n_new) before drawing the decoder. Always compute first, draw second.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Confusing $16\text{K}$ with $16$ KB. $16\text{K}$ means $16384$ locations, not $16000$.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** In DRAM questions, failing to mention the **destructive read** and **auto-refresh** after read. This is a guaranteed 2-mark question component.
> - **Common Mistake #5:** Drawing 1D decoders when 2D memory organization is more efficient. KTU rewards **2D organization** with extra marks for large memories.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **RAM is volatile, primary, random-access memory.** It is the working memory of a computer.
- **SRAM** uses a **6T bistable latch**, requires **no refresh**, is **fast** but **expensive** and **low density** → used in **CPU cache**.
- **DRAM** uses a **1T-1C cell** storing **charge on a capacitor**, requires **periodic refresh** (~64 ms), is **slower** but **cheap and dense** → used in **main memory**.
- **DRAM read is destructive** — sense amplifier must rewrite the value back.
- **SDRAM** synchronizes with the system clock; **DDR** doubles bandwidth by transferring on both clock edges.
- **Memory capacity formula:** $N = 2^n \times m$ (n = address lines, m = bits per word).
- **Word-size (horizontal) expansion** increases data width by connecting chips in **parallel**; address lines shared, $\overline{CS}$ shared, data lines split.
- **Word-count (vertical) expansion** increases depth by connecting chips in **series**; lower address lines shared, upper address lines go to a **decoder**, data lines shared.
- **Total chips = (Word size expansion factor) × (Word count expansion factor)** — this is the **golden rule** for KTU memory design problems.
- **2D memory organization** (row-column decoding) reduces pin count in large memories.
- **Cache** = SRAM; **Main Memory** = DRAM/SDRAM/DDR.
- **JEDEC refresh standard:** all rows refreshed within 64 ms; per-row interval $\approx 3.9 \,\mu s$ for an 8192-row DRAM.
- For 8086/ARM interfacing (KTU Module 5), **chip select** logic and **address range mapping** are direct applications of these concepts.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Hierarchy Rule of Thumb:** Smaller, faster, costlier memories closer to CPU. SRAM → Cache; DRAM → Main; Flash/SSD/HDD → Secondary. This hierarchy minimizes the **CPU-Memory speed gap** (a key KTU Module 4 concept tied to RAM types).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ROM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT403 - COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE AND MICROCONTROLLER
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** ROM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Read-Only Memory (ROM)** is a non-volatile, semi-permanent form of computer memory that permanently stores instructions and data during the manufacturing process or programming phase. Unlike Random Access Memory (RAM), the contents of ROM cannot be modified by normal processor operations during program execution — they are retained even when the computer is powered off.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** ROM is a class of storage medium primarily used in computers and other electronic devices to distribute firmware. Data stored in ROM is, by definition, not easily altered or reprogrammed. The term "Read-Only" is historical; modern ROMs (EEPROM, Flash) are technically rewritable, but the "read-mostly" behavior holds.

### 1.2 Core Characteristics of ROM

| Property | Description |
|---|---|
| **Volatility** | **Non-volatile** — retains data without power |
| **Access Type** | Primarily Read-Only (with limited write in modern types) |
| **Mutability** | Hard-wired (Mask ROM) to electronically erasable (Flash) |
| **Usage** | Boot firmware, BIOS/UEFI, microcontroller programs, lookup tables |
| **Speed** | Generally slower than RAM but faster than magnetic storage |
| **Cost per bit** | Historically lower than RAM for large densities |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Engraved Stone Tablet"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Library's Stone Tablet**
> Imagine a public library's "Rules Board" carved into a **stone tablet** at the entrance:
> - The rules (data) are **engraved permanently** — anyone can READ them (just like a CPU reads ROM).
> - You **cannot easily modify** the rules — erasing stone requires enormous effort (like Mask ROM manufacturing).
> - The board is there every morning, regardless of whether the lights are on — **non-volatile storage**.
> - Now imagine an "Overhead Projector Sheet" you can erase with a special marker — that is **EPROM/EEPROM**, where rewriting is possible but still restricted.

This analogy maps directly to the ROM hierarchy:
- **Mask ROM** = Engraved stone (set during manufacturing, impossible to change)
- **PROM** = Wax tablet (user-programmable ONCE)
- **EPROM** = Chalkboard with UV eraser (erasable with effort)
- **EEPROM/Flash** = Whiteboard with electronic eraser (electrically erasable, repeatedly)

### 1.4 Functional Role in Computer Architecture

ROM sits at the very foundation of the **memory hierarchy**, sitting closest to the CPU in terms of **firmware priority**. During the **boot sequence**, the CPU's program counter is hard-wired (via a reset vector) to the starting address of ROM. This is why ROM must be **non-volatile** — without power, the CPU has no instructions to execute, but ROM preserves them.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of ROM Technologies

ROM is broadly categorized based on the **method of data inscription** and **rewritability**. The following taxonomy represents the evolution of ROM technology:

#### A. Mask ROM (MROM)
- **Manufacturing Process:** Data is physically encoded using a **photolithographic mask** during semiconductor fabrication.
- **Mutability:** **ZERO** — data is permanent at the chip level. Modification requires redesigning the entire mask set.
- **Cost Logic:** Mass production cost is extremely low per chip, but **Non-Recurring Engineering (NRE)** costs are massive.
- **Use Case:** High-volume consumer electronics (calculators, washing machine controllers, toys).
- **Internal Mechanism:** Each memory cell is essentially a transistor with either an active connection (logic 1) or no connection (logic 0) defined by the metal mask layer.

#### B. Programmable ROM (PROM)
- **Manufacturing Process:** Ships with all bits set to a specific default (e.g., all 1s). User **blows fuses** using a PROM programmer by applying high-current pulses.
- **Mutability:** **One-Time Programmable (OTP)** — once a fuse is blown, it cannot be restored.
- **Internal Mechanism:** Each bit uses a **nichrome or polysilicon fuse**. Programming applies ~10–20 mA through the fuse, vaporizing it.
- **Use Case:** Low-volume prototypes, legacy systems, hardware authentication tokens.

#### C. Erasable Programmable ROM (EPROM)
- **Manufacturing Process:** Uses a **floating-gate MOSFET** (FAMOS cell) that traps electrical charge.
- **Mutability:** Erasable via **ultraviolet (UV) light** exposure through a quartz window on the chip package for 20–30 minutes.
- **Operational Cycle:** Write (program) → Read (normal operation) → Erase (UV exposure) → Write again.
- **Limitation:** Must be physically removed from the circuit for erasure; full chip erase only (no selective bit erase).
- **Use Case:** Firmware development phase, microcontroller firmware (older 8051 systems).

#### D. Electrically Erasable Programmable ROM (EEPROM)
- **Manufacturing Process:** Enhanced floating-gate transistor with a thinner oxide layer enabling **tunneling-based erasure**.
- **Mutability:** **Byte-level** electrical erase and rewrite — no UV light, no chip removal.
- **Operational Speed:** Write/erase cycle takes **~5 ms per byte**; endurance ~$10^6$ cycles.
- **Use Case:** BIOS storage, configuration registers, small parameter storage in embedded systems.

#### E. Flash Memory
- **Manufacturing Process:** Evolution of EEPROM using NAND/NOR floating-gate arrays.
- **Mutability:** **Block-level** electrical erase (NOR Flash) or **page-level** operations (NAND Flash).
- **Speed:** Read access ~25–200 ns; write/erase in microseconds to milliseconds.
- **Capacity:** Highest density among ROMs (gigabytes to terabytes).
- **Use Case:** SSDs, USB drives, smartphones, embedded firmware (modern microcontrollers).

> [!NOTE]
> **Hierarchy of Rewritability (Low → High):**
> Mask ROM < PROM < EPROM < EEPROM < Flash Memory
>
> Conversely, **cost per bit** and **density** follow the opposite trend: Mask ROM is cheapest per bit at high volume, Flash is most flexible.

### 2.2 Internal Architecture — ROM Cell

A standard ROM cell uses a single transistor per bit. The selection mechanism:

- **Word Line (WL):** Activates an entire row of cells when asserted.
- **Bit Line (BL):** Carries the output of a single column to the sense amplifier.
- **Cell Content:** Determined by the presence/absence of a connection between the transistor drain and the BL (Mask ROM) or by the threshold voltage of a floating-gate transistor (EPROM/EEPROM).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Relation | Variable Description |
|---|---|---|
| Memory Capacity | $C = 2^n \times m$ bits | $n$ = address lines, $m$ = data lines |
| Number of Address Lines | $n = \log_2(N)$ | $N$ = number of memory locations |
| Number of Memory Locations | $N = 2^n$ | $n$ = address lines |
| Capacity in Bytes | $C_{bytes} = 2^n \times (m/8)$ | When $m$ given in bits |
| ROM Capacity in ICs | $N \times M$ | $N$ locations × $M$ bits/word |
| Address Decoder Output Lines | $K = 2^n$ | For $n$-input decoder |
| Total Memory Map Size | $S = 2^{A}$ bytes | $A$ = total address bus width |
| Hexadecimal Address Calculation | $\text{End Addr} = \text{Start Addr} + \text{Size} - 1$ | Memory mapping |
| Floating Gate Threshold Voltage | $V_T = V_{T0} + \Delta V$ | $\Delta V$ from trapped charge |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Tip:** When calculating the number of ICs required to build a larger memory from smaller ROM chips:
>
> **Number of ICs = (Required Words × Bits per Word) / (Words per IC × Bits per Word per IC)**
>
> For both dimensions (address expansion and word-size expansion), use:
> $$\text{Total ICs} = \left(\frac{W_{req}}{W_{IC}}\right) \times \left(\frac{B_{req}}{B_{IC}}\right)$$
>
> where $W$ = number of words, $B$ = bits per word.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Embedded Systems:** Every microcontroller (Arduino ATmega328P, STM32, PIC) uses Flash ROM to store the user program.
- **Automotive ECUs:** Engine control units rely on Mask ROM or OTP ROM for safety-critical calibration data.
- **Aerospace:** Radiation-hardened PROMs are used in satellites due to their immunity to soft errors.
- **Consumer IoT:** Flash memory dominates as the firmware storage medium in IoT devices.
- **Authentication:** PROM-based fuses create unalterable device IDs and cryptographic keys.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Calculating Address Lines from Capacity

**Problem Statement:** A ROM chip has a storage capacity of **8 K × 8 bits**. Determine the number of address lines and data lines required.

**Given:**
- Capacity = $8\text{K} \times 8 = 8192 \times 8$ bits

**Step 1 — Identify the structure:**
The notation $X \times Y$ means $X$ memory locations, each storing $Y$ bits.

**Step 2 — Determine data lines:**
$$\text{Data lines } (m) = Y = 8 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate address lines:**
The number of locations is $8\text{K} = 8 \times 1024 = 8192$.

Using the relation $N = 2^n$:
$$8192 = 2^n$$

Taking $\log_2$ on both sides:
$$n = \log_2(8192) = \log_2(2^{13}) = 13$$

**Step 4 — Verification:**
$$2^{13} = 8192 \checkmark$$

**Result:**
- **Address lines** = **13**
- **Data lines** = **8**
- Total pin count (Address + Data + Control) = 13 + 8 + 2 (CE̅, OE̅) = **23 pins**

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Memory Expansion — Address and Word-Size Expansion

**Problem Statement:** Design a memory system of **16 K × 16 bits** using ROM chips of size **4 K × 8 bits**.

**Step 1 — Calculate the number of chips required.**

The required memory:
$$\text{Required} = 16\text{K} \times 16 = 16384 \text{ words} \times 16 \text{ bits/word}$$

The available IC:
$$\text{Per IC} = 4\text{K} \times 8 = 4096 \text{ words} \times 8 \text{ bits/word}$$

**Step 2 — Compute address expansion ratio:**
$$\text{Address Expansion} = \frac{W_{req}}{W_{IC}} = \frac{16\text{K}}{4\text{K}} = 4$$

**Step 3 — Compute word-size expansion ratio:**
$$\text{Word Expansion} = \frac{B_{req}}{B_{IC}} = \frac{16}{8} = 2$$

**Step 4 — Total number of ICs:**
$$\text{Total ICs} = \left(\frac{W_{req}}{W_{IC}}\right) \times \left(\frac{B_{req}}{B_{IC}}\right) = 4 \times 2 = 8 \text{ ICs}$$

**Step 5 — Arrange into a 2D matrix:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Array dimensions} &= 4 \text{ (rows) } \times 2 \text{ (columns)} \\
\text{Rows} &= \text{Address expansion (one chip per address group)} \\
\text{Columns} &= \text{Word-size expansion (parallel for wider bus)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Determine address lines for IC:**
$$\text{Per IC: } 4\text{K} = 2^n \Rightarrow n = \log_2(4096) = 12 \text{ address lines}$$

**Step 7 — Determine address lines for full system:**
$$\text{System: } 16\text{K} = 2^n \Rightarrow n = \log_2(16384) = 14 \text{ address lines}$$

**Step 8 — Determine decoder requirement:**
The 2 most significant bits of the 14-bit address (A13, A12) select which row of 4 ICs is active:
$$\text{Decoder inputs} = 14 - 12 = 2 \Rightarrow 2\text{-to-}4 \text{ decoder}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Memory Map Construction

**Problem Statement:** A microprocessor has a 16-bit address bus. Three ROM chips are mapped:
- ROM1: 4 KB starting at address $0000\text{H}$
- ROM2: 8 KB starting at address $4000\text{H}$
- ROM3: 2 KB starting at address $A000\text{H}$

Find the ending address of each ROM and detect any overlap.

**Step 1 — ROM1: 4 KB at $0000\text{H}$**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Size} &= 4 \text{ KB} = 4096 = 1000\text{H} \\
\text{Start} &= 0000\text{H} \\
\text{End} &= \text{Start} + \text{Size} - 1 \\
&= 0000\text{H} + 1000\text{H} - 1 \\
&= 0FFF\text{H}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — ROM2: 8 KB at $4000\text{H}$**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Size} &= 8 \text{ KB} = 8192 = 2000\text{H} \\
\text{Start} &= 4000\text{H} \\
\text{End} &= 4000\text{H} + 2000\text{H} - 1 \\
&= 5FFF\text{H}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — ROM3: 2 KB at $A000\text{H}$**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Size} &= 2 \text{ KB} = 2048 = 0800\text{H} \\
\text{Start} &= A000\text{H} \\
\text{End} &= A000\text{H} + 0800\text{H} - 1 \\
&= A7FF\text{H}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Overlap check:**
- ROM1: $0000\text{H} - 0FFF\text{H}$
- ROM2: $4000\text{H} - 5FFF\text{H}$
- ROM3: $A000\text{H} - A7FF\text{H}$

Gap analysis:
- Between ROM1 end and ROM2 start: $1000\text{H}$ to $3FFF\text{H}$ — free.
- Between ROM2 end and ROM3 start: $6000\text{H}$ to $9FFF\text{H}$ — free.

**Result:** No overlap. Valid memory map.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: ROM Cell Density Calculation

**Problem Statement:** A Mask ROM array has **4096 rows** and **8 columns**. Each cell uses one transistor. Find the total transistor count and equivalent bits stored.

**Step 1 — Total cells:**
$$\text{Total cells} = \text{Rows} \times \text{Columns} = 4096 \times 8 = 32768 \text{ cells}$$

**Step 2 — Bits stored:**
Each cell stores 1 bit.
$$\text{Total bits} = 32768 \text{ bits} = 4 \text{ K} \times 8 = 32 \text{ Kb}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to bytes:**
$$\text{Bytes} = \frac{32768}{8} = 4096 \text{ bytes} = 4 \text{ KB}$$

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Mask ROM | PROM | EPROM | EEPROM | Flash |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Programming Method** | Mask at fab | Fuse blow | Hot electron injection | F-N tunneling | F-N tunneling |
| **Erasure Method** | Not erasable | Not erasable | UV light (20–30 min) | Electric (byte) | Electric (block) |
| **Rewrite Cycles** | 0 | 1 | ~100 | ~$10^6$ | ~$10^5$–$10^6$ |
| **Cell Size** | Smallest | Small | Large (2 transistors) | Large (2 transistors) | Medium (1 transistor NOR) |
| **Write Speed** | N/A | ms | µs per byte | ms per byte | µs–ms per block |
| **Read Speed** | Fast | Fast | 100–250 ns | 100–250 ns | 25–200 ns |
| **Power Consumption** | Lowest | Low | Medium | High | Medium |
| **Cost per Bit** | Lowest (volume) | Medium | High | Higher | Decreasing |
| **Density** | Highest | Medium | Medium | Lower | High |
| **In-Circuit Rewrite** | No | No | No | Yes | Yes |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 ROM Hierarchy and Classification (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    ROM[ROM - Read Only Memory]
    ROM --> MROM[Mask ROM]
    ROM --> PROM[Programmable ROM]
    ROM --> EPROM[Erasable Programmable ROM]
    ROM --> EEPROM[Electrically Erasable PROM]
    ROM --> FLASH[Flash Memory]
    
    MROM --> MROMnote[Photolithographic mask during fab. Permanent. High volume low cost.]
    PROM --> PROMnote[OTP - One Time Programmable. Fuses blown by programmer.]
    EPROM --> EPROMnote[UV erasable through quartz window. Floating gate FAMOS cell.]
    EPROM --> EPROMsub[UVEPROM]
    
    EEPROM --> EEPROMsub1[Serial EEPROM I2C SPI]
    EEPROM --> EEPROMsub2[Parallel EEPROM]
    
    FLASH --> FLASHsub1[NOR Flash - Random access fast read]
    FLASH --> FLASHsub2[NAND Flash - Sequential block access high density]
```

### 4.2 Internal ROM Architecture — Array with Decoder

```mermaid
graph TD
    CPU[CPU - Central Processing Unit]
    ABUS[Address Bus n bits]
    DBUS[Data Bus m bits]
    CTRLBUS[Control Bus CE OE]
    
    DEC[Address Decoder 2 to N]
    
    ROW1[Word Line WL0]
    ROW2[Word Line WL1]
    ROW3[Word Line WL2]
    ROWN[Word Line WLn]
    
    COL[Bit Lines BL0 to BLm]
    SA[Sense Amplifiers]
    BUF[Output Buffer]
    
    CPU --> ABUS
    ABUS --> DEC
    DEC --> ROW1
    DEC --> ROW2
    DEC --> ROW3
    DEC --> ROWN
    
    ROW1 --> COL
    ROW2 --> COL
    ROW3 --> COL
    ROWN --> COL
    
    COL --> SA
    SA --> BUF
    BUF --> DBUS
    
    CTRLBUS --> DEC
    CTRLBUS --> BUF
    
    CPU --> CTRLBUS
```

### 4.3 Memory Expansion Strategy — Word and Address Expansion

```mermaid
graph TD
    SYS[16K x 16 Memory System]
    SYS --> ROWSEL[Row Decoder 2 to 4 using A13 A12]
    
    ROWSEL --> R0[Row 0 - Address 0000H to 3FFFH]
    ROWSEL --> R1[Row 1 - Address 4000H to 7FFFH]
    ROWSEL --> R2[Row 2 - Address 8000H to BFFFH]
    ROWSEL --> R3[Row 3 - Address C000H to FFFFH]
    
    R0 --> IC1A[IC1 - Lower Byte D0 to D7]
    R0 --> IC1B[IC2 - Upper Byte D8 to D15]
    R1 --> IC2A[IC3 - Lower Byte]
    R1 --> IC2B[IC4 - Upper Byte]
    R2 --> IC3A[IC5 - Lower Byte]
    R2 --> IC3B[IC6 - Upper Byte]
    R3 --> IC4A[IC7 - Lower Byte]
    R3 --> IC4B[IC8 - Upper Byte]
    
    IC1A --> BUS[16 bit Data Bus]
    IC1B --> BUS
    IC2A --> BUS
    IC2B --> BUS
    IC3A --> BUS
    IC3B --> BUS
    IC4A --> BUS
    IC4B --> BUS
```

### 4.4 ROM Cell — Floating Gate Transistor (Conceptual)

```mermaid
graph LR
    CG[Control Gate]
    FG[Floating Gate - Traps Electrons]
    OX1[Tunnel Oxide Layer]
    OX2[Gate Oxide Layer]
    SUB[P Substrate - Source Drain]
    
    CG --> OX2
    OX2 --> FG
    FG --> OX1
    OX1 --> SUB
    
    PROGRAM[Programming - Hot Electron Injection] -.-> FG
    ERASE[Erasure - Fowler Nordheim Tunneling] -.-> FG
    READ[Read - Sense Threshold Voltage] -.-> FG
```

### 4.5 ROM Read Cycle Timing — Functional Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[CPU places address on Address Bus]
    B[CPU asserts CE Chip Enable active low]
    C[CPU asserts OE Output Enable active low]
    D[Internal decoder activates target Word Line]
    E[Selected row drives Bit Lines]
    F[Sense amplifiers detect and amplify signal]
    G[Output buffers place data on Data Bus]
    H[CPU latches data and deasserts control signals]
    
    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F --> G
    G --> H
    H --> A
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between Mask ROM, PROM, EPROM, and EEPROM based on programming and erasure methods.**

**Model Answer:**

| Type | Programming Method | Erasure Method | Rewritable? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mask ROM** | Done at fabrication using photolithographic mask | Cannot be erased | No |
| **PROM** | User-programmable via fuse-blowing (high current pulse) | Cannot be erased (one-time) | No (OTP) |
| **EPROM** | Hot electron injection onto floating gate | UV light exposure (20–30 min) | Yes (full-chip erase only) |
| **EEPROM** | Fowler-Nordheim tunneling | Electrical signal (byte-level) | Yes (in-circuit, byte-selectable) |

The key distinction is the **erasure mechanism**: Mask ROM and PROM are write-once, EPROM requires physical UV exposure, while EEPROM supports in-circuit electrical byte-level rewrite. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Explain why ROM is called "non-volatile" memory and list three applications.**

**Model Answer:**

ROM is termed **non-volatile** because the data stored in it is **permanently retained even when power supply is removed**. This is due to the physical storage mechanism:
- In Mask ROM: data is encoded in physical transistor connections.
- In EPROM/EEPROM/Flash: data is stored as **trapped charge on a floating gate**, which persists without power.

**Three Applications:**
1. **Boot Firmware (BIOS/UEFI):** Initial instructions executed at power-on.
2. **Microcontroller Program Storage:** Stores user application code (e.g., Arduino ATmega328P uses 32 KB Flash).
3. **Lookup Tables (LUTs):** Trigonometric, logarithmic, or character generation tables in embedded systems (e.g., 7-segment decoders).

**(3 marks)**

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

**Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the internal organization of a ROM chip and describe its read operation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A ROM has 12 address lines and 8 data lines. Calculate: (i) total memory capacity in bits and bytes, (ii) the number of memory locations, and (iii) the size of the decoder required. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(a):

**Internal Organization of ROM:**

A ROM chip consists of the following functional blocks:

1. **Address Decoder:** An $n$-to-$2^n$ decoder that selects one of $2^n$ word lines based on the input address.
2. **Memory Array:** A matrix of $2^n \times m$ storage cells arranged as rows (word lines) and columns (bit lines).
3. **Sense Amplifiers:** Detect and amplify the weak signals from selected bit lines.
4. **Output Buffers:** Tri-state buffers controlled by the OE̅ (Output Enable) signal.
5. **Control Logic:** Handles CE̅ (Chip Enable) for chip selection.

**Read Operation Sequence:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{CPU places address } A_{n-1} \dots A_0 \text{ on address bus.} \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{CPU asserts } \overline{CE} = 0 \text{ and } \overline{OE} = 0. \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Address decoder activates exactly one Word Line (WL}_i\text{).} \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{All cells in row } i \text{ drive their respective Bit Lines (BL}_j\text{).} \\
\text{Step 5:} \quad & \text{Sense amplifiers convert the weak cell signals to full logic levels.} \\
\text{Step 6:} \quad & \text{Output buffers transfer the } m \text{-bit word to the data bus.} \\
\text{Step 7:} \quad & \text{CPU reads data; control signals are deasserted.}
\end{aligned}$$

The entire cycle typically completes in **$t_{AA}$ (address access time)** ranging from 25 ns to 250 ns depending on ROM type. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(b):

**Given:**
- Address lines: $n = 12$
- Data lines: $m = 8$

**Step 1 — Number of Memory Locations:**
$$N = 2^n = 2^{12} = 4096 \text{ locations} = 4\text{K}$$

**Step 2 — Total Capacity in Bits:**
$$C_{bits} = N \times m = 4096 \times 8 = 32768 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 3 — Total Capacity in Bytes:**
$$C_{bytes} = \frac{C_{bits}}{8} = \frac{32768}{8} = 4096 \text{ bytes} = 4\text{ KB}$$

**Step 4 — Decoder Size:**
An $n$-to-$2^n$ decoder is needed:
$$\text{Decoder} = 12 \text{-to-} 4096 \text{ decoder}$$
$$2^n = 2^{12} = 4096 \text{ output lines}$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) **Capacity = 32,768 bits = 4 KB**
- (ii) **Number of locations = 4096 = 4K**
- (iii) **Decoder = 12-to-4096 line decoder**

**(7 marks)**

---

### **OR**

---

**Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Compare the features of PROM, EPROM, EEPROM, and Flash memory with respect to cell structure, erase mechanism, and typical applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a 16 K × 16 ROM system using 4 K × 8 ROM ICs. Show the address decoding logic and draw the memory map. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(a):

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | PROM | EPROM | EEPROM | Flash |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cell Structure** | Fuse + transistor | Floating-gate MOSFET (FAMOS) | Floating-gate MOSFET with thin oxide | Floating-gate MOSFET (NAND/NOR array) |
| **Program Mechanism** | Fuse blown by high current | Hot-electron injection | Fowler-Nordheim tunneling | F-N tunneling or hot-electron injection |
| **Erase Mechanism** | Not erasable | UV light (~20 min) | Electrical (byte) | Electrical (block/page) |
| **In-Circuit Rewrite** | No | No | Yes | Yes |
| **Granularity** | Bit-level (one-time) | Full chip | Byte-level | Block-level (4 KB–128 KB) |
| **Endurance (cycles)** | 1 | ~100 | ~$10^5$–$10^6$ | ~$10^5$ (NOR), ~$10^5$ (NAND MLC) |
| **Typical Applications** | OTP tags, hardware keys | Legacy firmware, dev kits | BIOS, config storage | SSDs, USB drives, MCU firmware |

**Cell Structure Detail:**
- **PROM:** Each bit uses a **nichrome fuse** in series with a bipolar junction transistor or a polysilicon fuse with a MOSFET.
- **EPROM:** Uses **FAMOS** (Floating-gate Avalanche-injection MOS) — charge stored on an electrically isolated floating gate.
- **EEPROM:** Two-transistor cell — select transistor + storage transistor with thin tunnel oxide.
- **Flash:** Single transistor per cell (NOR) or string architecture (NAND) with shared source line.

**(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(b):

**Step 1 — Identify Requirements and IC Capacity:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Required memory} &= 16\text{K} \times 16 \\
\text{IC available} &= 4\text{K} \times 8
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Number of ICs Required:**
$$\text{Total ICs} = \frac{16\text{K}}{4\text{K}} \times \frac{16}{8} = 4 \times 2 = 8 \text{ ICs}$$

**Step 3 — Address Line Breakdown:**

IC address lines: $\log_2(4\text{K}) = \log_2(4096) = 12$ lines ($A_{11}$ to $A_0$)

System address lines: $\log_2(16\text{K}) = \log_2(16384) = 14$ lines ($A_{13}$ to $A_0$)

Decoder-controlled lines: $A_{13}, A_{12}$ → **2-to-4 decoder**

**Step 4 — Decoder Truth Table:**

| $A_{13}$ | $A_{12}$ | Selected Row (Chip Pair) | Address Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | IC1, IC2 | $0000\text{H}$ – $3FFF\text{H}$ |
| 0 | 1 | IC3, IC4 | $4000\text{H}$ – $7FFF\text{H}$ |
| 1 | 0 | IC5, IC6 | $8000\text{H}$ – $BFFF\text{H}$ |
| 1 | 1 | IC7, IC8 | $C000\text{H}$ – $FFFF\text{H}$ |

**Step 5 — Memory Map:**

```
+--------------------+ 00000H
| ROM - IC1 (D0-D7)  |
| ROM - IC2 (D8-D15) | 16K x 16 - 4 ICs x 2 chips
+--------------------+ 03FFFH
| ROM - IC3 (D0-D7)  |
| ROM - IC4 (D8-D15) |
+--------------------+ 07FFFH
| ROM - IC5 (D0-D7)  |
| ROM - IC6 (D8-D15) |
+--------------------+ 0BFFFH
| ROM - IC7 (D0-D7)  |
| ROM - IC8 (D8-D15) |
+--------------------+ 0FFFFH
```

**Step 6 — Control Logic:**

The 2-to-4 decoder outputs ($Y_0$ to $Y_3$) connect to the $\overline{CE}$ pins of each row's IC pair:
$$\overline{CE}_{IC1,IC2} = Y_0, \quad \overline{CE}_{IC3,IC4} = Y_1, \quad \overline{CE}_{IC5,IC6} = Y_2, \quad \overline{CE}_{IC7,IC8} = Y_3$$

Lines $A_{11}$ to $A_0$ are connected in parallel to the address inputs of all 8 ICs.

**(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Common Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Confusing K with 1000:** Always remember that in KTU memory problems, $1\text{K} = 1024 = 2^{10}$, NOT 1000. Using $1\text{K} = 1000$ will lead to incorrect decoder sizes.
> 2. **Forgetting the "-1" in end address:** End address = Start + Size − 1. Forgetting the −1 is the most common valuation error.
> 3. **Address line count off-by-one:** Always verify $2^n = \text{number of locations}$. An IC with 4K locations needs exactly **12** address lines, not 11 or 13.
> 4. **Decoder input count:** Decoder inputs = (System address lines) − (Per-IC address lines). A 16K system using 4K ICs needs $14 - 12 = 2$ decoder inputs.
> 5. **Word vs. byte expansion:** Do not confuse address expansion (more locations) with word-size expansion (wider data bus). Each requires its own multiplier.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — ROM**
>
> ✅ **Definition:** ROM = Non-volatile memory primarily for reading; retains data without power.
>
> ✅ **Evolution Ladder (low → high flexibility):** Mask ROM → PROM → EPROM → EEPROM → Flash
>
> ✅ **Key Distinguishing Feature:** Erasure mechanism (none / UV / electrical byte / electrical block).
>
> ✅ **Core Formulas:**
> - $N = 2^n$ (locations from address lines)
> - $C = 2^n \times m$ (capacity in bits)
> - $\text{Total ICs} = (W_{req}/W_{IC}) \times (B_{req}/B_{IC})$
> - $\text{End Addr} = \text{Start Addr} + \text{Size} - 1$
>
> ✅ **Endurance Hierarchy:** Mask ROM (0) < PROM (1) < EPROM (~100) < EEPROM ($10^6$) ≤ Flash ($10^5$–$10^6$)
>
> ✅ **Density Hierarchy:** Flash > Mask ROM > NAND > EEPROM > EPROM > PROM
>
> ✅ **Modern Reality:** "Read-Only" is a historical misnomer — Flash is the dominant "ROM" today and is fully rewritable in-circuit.
>
> ✅ **Boot Sequence Role:** CPU reset vector points to ROM; without ROM, the system cannot boot.
>
> ✅ **Pin Count Rule:** Total pins ≈ Address lines + Data lines + 2 (CE̅, OE̅) + 2 (Vcc, GND).
>
> ✅ **Memory Expansion Types:**
> - **Address expansion:** More locations (decoder-controlled)
> - **Word expansion:** Wider data bus (parallel ICs)
> - **Combined:** Both simultaneously (2D array)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Memory Characteristics and Hierarchy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT403 - COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE AND MICROCONTROLLER
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Memory Characteristics and Hierarchy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Memory Characteristics and Hierarchy — Module 4 Notes

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
**Memory** in a computer system is the fundamental subsystem responsible for the storage, retrieval, and management of data and instructions required by the processor (CPU). Under the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned)**, a memory system is not described by a single component but as a **hierarchical pyramid of storage devices** arranged in decreasing order of speed, cost per bit, and increasing order of capacity and access time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** *Memory Characteristics and Hierarchy* is a high-weightage topic in Module 4. It directly maps to **CO4** (Design microcontroller-based systems using memory and I/O interfacing concepts) and is a recurring 14-mark question in KTU ESE.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Office Desk" Model
Imagine you are a working professional (the **CPU**):
- **Your active brain (Registers):** Where you hold the single number you are crunching right now. Extremely fast, but you can only hold a few digits.
- **The papers on your desk (Cache):** Documents you are *currently* reading or editing. Very fast to grab, limited in quantity.
- **The filing cabinet next to you (Main Memory / RAM):** Where all your ongoing project files live. Bigger, but you must stand up and walk to it (slower).
- **The basement archive (Secondary Storage / SSD/HDD):** Where last year's records are stored. Huge capacity, but you must take the elevator (much slower).

The trick is: **You arrange your workspace this way because you cannot afford a basement the size of your desk, nor a desk the size of the basement.** This trade-off between **speed**, **cost**, and **capacity** is the entire philosophy of memory hierarchy.

### 1.3 Key Characteristics of Memory (KTU Mandated Classification)
The KTU 2024 syllabus mandates students to classify any memory technology along these **eight (8) characteristics**:

| # | Characteristic | Description | Common Values / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Location** | Where the memory physically resides | Internal (CPU registers, Cache, Main Memory), External (Magnetic Disks, Tapes, USB) |
| 2 | **Capacity** | The amount of data it can hold | Internal: Bytes to MB; External: GB to TB |
| 3 | **Unit of Transfer** | The smallest chunk transferred per read/write | Word, Block, Sector |
| 4 | **Access Method** | How data is physically retrieved | Random, Sequential, Direct, Associative |
| 5 | **Performance** | Speed of access | Access Time, Transfer Rate, Cycle Time |
| 6 | **Physical Type** | Storage medium | Semiconductor (RAM/ROM), Magnetic (Disk/Tape), Optical (CD/DVD) |
| 7 | **Physical Characteristics** | Volatility | Volatile (RAM), Non-Volatile (ROM, Flash, Disk) |
| 8 | **Organization** | Internal arrangement of bits | Bits per chip, chips per module, error correction |

> [!NOTE]
> **Volatile vs Non-Volatile:** **Volatile** memory (like DRAM) loses its data when power is cut off. **Non-Volatile** memory (like ROM, Flash, HDD) retains data permanently. This is the most frequently asked 3-mark definition in KTU exams.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Memory Access Methods
Memory access methods dictate **how the memory controller finds a piece of data**. KTU examiners love to test this with diagrams.

1. **Sequential Access:** Data is accessed in a linear, ordered sequence (e.g., magnetic tape). To reach block N, you *must* traverse blocks 0 to N-1.
2. **Direct Access:** Individual blocks have unique addresses. Access involves jumping to a vicinity and then sequentially searching (e.g., HDD where each track is a vicinity).
3. **Random Access:** Any location can be accessed in **constant time**, independent of its physical position (e.g., RAM).
4. **Associative Access:** A *content-based search* is performed. Every word is compared in parallel against a tag (e.g., **TLB** - Translation Lookaside Buffer, Cache).
5. **Block / Sector Transfer:** A chunk of data is moved per access (common in modern systems for locality exploitation).

## 2.2 The Performance Metrics (High-Yield Formulas)
The performance of a memory system is governed by three foundational metrics: **Access Time ($t_a$)**, **Memory Cycle Time ($t_c$)**, and **Transfer Rate ($T_r$)**.

### 2.2.1 Access Time & Cycle Time
- **Access Time ($t_a$):** Time between initiating a read request and receiving the data. Asymmetric in some memories (Read vs Write).
- **Cycle Time ($t_c$):** The minimum time between two *consecutive* independent memory operations. $t_c \ge t_a$ because the memory needs to "reset" (restore internal capacitors in DRAM, for example).

### 2.2.2 Transfer Rate
- **For Random Access Memory:**
$$T_r = \frac{1}{t_c} \quad \text{[Words/second]}$$
- **For Non-Random Access Memory (e.g., HDD with rotating disk):**
$$T_r = \frac{T}{N} \quad \text{[Words/second]}$$

where $N$ is the number of words per block and $T$ is the time to read one full block (sum of average seek time, rotational latency, and transfer time).

### 2.2.3 The Hit/Miss Model (Crucial for Hierarchy)
When CPU requests data, two outcomes exist:
- **Hit:** Data is found in the current level. **Hit Ratio ($H$)** = Hits / Total CPU memory references.
- **Miss:** Data is not found. **Miss Ratio ($M$)** = 1 − H.

## 2.3 Average Memory Access Time (AMAT) — The Golden Formula

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **AMAT is the single most important formula in Module 4.** It is the foundation for every KTU 14-mark question on memory performance.

For a **two-level memory hierarchy** (Level 1 = Cache, Level 2 = Main Memory):

$$\text{AMAT} = H \cdot t_1 + (1 - H) \cdot (t_1 + t_2)$$

Simplifying:

$$\boxed{\text{AMAT} = t_1 + (1 - H) \cdot t_2}$$

where:
- $t_1$ = Access time of Level 1 (e.g., Cache) = **Hit Time**
- $t_2$ = Access time of Level 2 (e.g., Main Memory) = **Miss Penalty**
- $H$ = Hit Ratio of Level 1

**Generalized N-Level Formula:**

$$\text{AMAT}_n = t_1 + (1 - H_1) \cdot \left( t_2 + (1 - H_2) \cdot \left( t_3 + \ldots \right) \right)$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Metric / Concept | Formula | Unit | Boundary Conditions & Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hit Ratio** | $H = \frac{\text{Hits}}{\text{Total References}}$ | Dimensionless (0 to 1) | $H + M = 1$ always |
| **Miss Ratio** | $M = 1 - H$ | Dimensionless | Often expressed as percentage |
| **AMAT (2-level)** | $\text{AMAT} = t_1 + M \cdot t_2$ | Time units (ns, µs) | Used for Cache-Main Memory analysis |
| **AMAT (N-level)** | Recursive: $t_1 + (1-H_1)(t_2 + \ldots)$ | Time units | Apply innermost-out |
| **Transfer Rate (RAM)** | $T_r = \frac{1}{t_c}$ | Words/sec | Inverse of cycle time |
| **Transfer Rate (Disk)** | $T_r = \frac{T}{N}$ | Words/sec | $T$ = block read time, $N$ = words/block |
| **Effective Access Time** | $EAT = H \cdot t_c + (1-H)\cdot(t_c + t_m)$ | Time units | $t_m$ = transfer time from lower level |
| **Speed Up** | $S = \frac{t_{\text{no-cache}}}{t_{\text{cache}}} = \frac{t_2}{t_1 + (1-H)\cdot t_2}$ | Dimensionless | $S > 1$ implies hierarchy is beneficial |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility
The AMAT model is not just academic — it is the **backbone of CPU cache design** in every commercial processor. For example, the **Intel Core i9** achieves an L1 hit rate of ~95%. Using the AMAT formula, an engineer can quantify exactly how much performance is gained by upgrading from L3 cache to main memory. This same principle drives the design of **GPU memory hierarchies, TLB lookups in virtual memory, and database buffer pools** in enterprise software.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: AMAT From First Principles

**Step 1: Define the universe of events.**
Let $N$ be the total number of memory references made by the CPU during program execution. Each reference is either a **Hit** or a **Miss**.

$$N = N_{\text{hit}} + N_{\text{miss}}$$

**Step 2: Define the Hit Ratio.**
The Hit Ratio $H$ is the fraction of references that find their data in Level 1.

$$H = \frac{N_{\text{hit}}}{N} \quad \Rightarrow \quad N_{\text{hit}} = H \cdot N$$

Consequently, the Miss Ratio is:

$$M = 1 - H = \frac{N_{\text{miss}}}{N} \quad \Rightarrow \quad N_{\text{miss}} = (1-H) \cdot N$$

**Step 3: Calculate the cumulative time.**
Each **hit** takes $t_1$ time units (L1 access time only). Each **miss** takes $t_1$ time (to look in L1 and fail) **plus** $t_2$ time (to retrieve from L2).

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{total}} &= N_{\text{hit}} \cdot t_1 + N_{\text{miss}} \cdot (t_1 + t_2) \\
&= (H \cdot N) \cdot t_1 + (1-H) \cdot N \cdot (t_1 + t_2) \\
&= N \cdot \left[ H \cdot t_1 + (1-H) \cdot t_1 + (1-H) \cdot t_2 \right] \\
&= N \cdot \left[ t_1 + (1-H) \cdot t_2 \right]
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Compute AMAT.**
AMAT is the *average* time per memory reference, i.e., $T_{\text{total}} / N$.

$$\text{AMAT} = \frac{T_{\text{total}}}{N} = t_1 + (1-H) \cdot t_2$$

**Final Form (KTU Marking Format):**

$$\boxed{\text{AMAT} = t_1 + (1-H) \cdot t_2} \quad \text{(in nanoseconds)}$$

## 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical Example (Typical 7-Mark KTU Sub-Question)

> **[KTU University Exam Pattern Question]**
> A system has a two-level memory hierarchy. Level 1 (Cache) has an access time of **$5$ ns** and a hit ratio of **$0.90$**. Level 2 (Main Memory) has an access time of **$100$ ns**. Calculate:
> 1. AMAT
> 2. Speedup compared to a system with no cache
> 3. New AMAT if hit ratio is improved to $0.95$

### Solution:

**Part (a): AMAT Calculation**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Given:} \quad t_1 &= 5 \text{ ns}, \quad H = 0.90, \quad t_2 = 100 \text{ ns} \\
\text{Miss Ratio } (1-H) &= 1 - 0.90 = 0.10 \\
\text{AMAT} &= t_1 + (1-H) \cdot t_2 \\
&= 5 + (0.10) \cdot 100 \\
&= 5 + 10 \\
&= 15 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (b): Speedup**

$$\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{no-cache}} &= t_2 = 100 \text{ ns} \\
S &= \frac{t_{\text{no-cache}}}{\text{AMAT}} = \frac{100}{15} = 6.67
\end{aligned}$$

The hierarchical system is **6.67× faster** than a single-memory system.

**Part (c): AMAT with Improved Hit Ratio**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{New } H &= 0.95, \quad (1-H) = 0.05 \\
\text{AMAT}_{\text{new}} &= 5 + (0.05) \cdot 100 \\
&= 5 + 5 = 10 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}$$

**Inference:** A 5% improvement in hit ratio reduces AMAT by **33%** (from 15 ns to 10 ns). This is why cache design is so critical.

## 3.3 The Principle of Locality (Theoretical Justification for Hierarchy)

Memory hierarchy works *only* because programs exhibit two predictable patterns of memory access:

1. **Temporal Locality:** *If a memory location is referenced, it is likely to be referenced again in the near future.* (e.g., loop counters, variables in a tight loop are accessed repeatedly).
2. **Spatial Locality:** *If a memory location is referenced, nearby locations are likely to be referenced soon.* (e.g., sequential instruction execution, array traversal).

> [!NOTE]
> **Locality + Hierarchy = Performance.** Without locality, the AMAT would converge to $t_2$ (the slowest memory), making the hierarchy useless. Locality is what allows the "Hit" event to dominate.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Memory Levels

| Level | Technology | Typical Capacity | Access Time | Cost / Bit | Volatile? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Registers** | Flip-flops (in CPU) | 32–128 × 64-bit words | ~0.5 ns | **Highest** | Yes |
| **L1 Cache** | SRAM (on-chip) | 16 KB – 128 KB | ~1 ns | Very High | Yes |
| **L2 Cache** | SRAM (on-chip / on-package) | 256 KB – 1 MB | ~3–10 ns | High | Yes |
| **L3 Cache** | SRAM (shared) | 4 MB – 64 MB | ~10–20 ns | Moderate | Yes |
| **Main Memory** | DRAM | 4 GB – 128 GB | ~50–100 ns | Moderate | Yes |
| **SSD** | NAND Flash | 256 GB – 4 TB | ~50,000 ns (50 µs) | Low | **No** |
| **HDD** | Magnetic Platters | 1 TB – 20 TB | ~5,000,000 ns (5 ms) | **Lowest** | **No** |

> **Reading the table:** As you move **down** the hierarchy, capacity **increases**, access time **increases** (slower), and cost per bit **decreases**. This is the fundamental design triangle.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Memory Hierarchy Pyramid (Mermaid Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["REGISTERS - Fastest - 0.5 ns - Few bytes - Inside CPU Core"] --> B["L1 CACHE - 1 ns - 16 to 128 KB - SRAM on chip"]
    B --> C["L2 CACHE - 3 to 10 ns - 256 KB to 1 MB - SRAM on chip"]
    C --> D["L3 CACHE - 10 to 20 ns - 4 to 64 MB - SRAM shared"]
    D --> E["MAIN MEMORY RAM - 50 to 100 ns - 4 to 128 GB - DRAM modules"]
    E --> F["SSD - 50 microseconds - 256 GB to 4 TB - NAND Flash"]
    F --> G["HDD - 5 to 10 ms - 1 to 20 TB - Magnetic platters"]
    G --> H["ARCHIVAL TAPE / CLOUD - Seconds - Petabytes - Offsite storage"]

    style A fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff
    style B fill:#ffa500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style C fill:#ffd93d,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style D fill:#6bcf7f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style E fill:#4d96ff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style F fill:#9b59b6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style G fill:#34495e,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style H fill:#2c3e50,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Red (top) = highest speed, highest cost, lowest capacity. Blue (bottom) = lowest speed, lowest cost, highest capacity. Data flows *up* on access and *down* on writeback.

## 4.2 Memory Access Flow — Hit vs Miss Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([CPU Issues Memory Request]) --> Q1{Is Data in Level 1 Cache?}
    Q1 -- YES HIT --> H[Access Time t1 ns - Increment Hit Counter]
    Q1 -- NO MISS --> R[Search Level 2 Main Memory]
    R --> Q2{Data Found in L2?}
    Q2 -- YES --> S[Transfer Data to CPU via t2 ns - Copy Block to L1 for Future]
    Q2 -- NO --> T[Access Level 3 Secondary Storage]
    S --> Done([Return Data to CPU])
    T --> Done
    H --> Done

    style Start fill:#4d96ff,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    style Q1 fill:#ffd93d,stroke:#333
    style Q2 fill:#ffd93d,stroke:#333
    style H fill:#6bcf7f,stroke:#333
    style S fill:#ffa500,stroke:#333
    style T fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    style Done fill:#2c3e50,stroke:#333,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram of a Typical Two-Level Memory System

```mermaid
graph LR
    CPU["CPU"] -- "Address Bus - Read/Write Signal" --> CTRL["Memory Controller Unit"]
    CTRL -- "L1 Request - Hit Line" --> L1["L1 CACHE - SRAM - 64 KB"]
    CTRL -- "Miss Trigger" --> MM["MAIN MEMORY - DRAM - 8 GB"]
    L1 -- "Cache Miss Data" --> MM
    MM -- "Data Returned" --> CPU
    CTRL -- "Write-Back" --> L1

    style CPU fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    style CTRL fill:#4d96ff,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    style L1 fill:#6bcf7f,stroke:#333
    style MM fill:#9b59b6,stroke:#333,color:#fff
```

## 4.4 Access Method Classification Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    AM["MEMORY ACCESS METHODS"] --> SEQ["Sequential Access - Magnetic Tape - Linear Scan"]
    AM --> DIR["Direct Access - HDD - Track to Track then Search"]
    AM --> RAN["Random Access - RAM - Constant Time - Any Address"]
    AM --> ASSOC["Associative Access - TLB or Cache - Content Addressed"]

    SEQ --> EX1["Example: Audio Cassette"]
    DIR --> EX2["Example: Hard Disk Drive"]
    RAN --> EX3["Example: DRAM, SRAM"]
    ASSOC --> EX4["Example: L1 Cache Lookup by Tag"]

    style AM fill:#2c3e50,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    style SEQ fill:#ffd93d,stroke:#333
    style DIR fill:#ffa500,stroke:#333
    style RAN fill:#6bcf7f,stroke:#333
    style ASSOC fill:#4d96ff,stroke:#333,color:#fff
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]**

### Q1. Differentiate between SRAM and DRAM. Why is SRAM used in cache memory? **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | SRAM (Static RAM) | DRAM (Dynamic RAM) |
|---|---|---|
| **Basic Cell** | 6 Transistors (Flip-Flop) | 1 Transistor + 1 Capacitor |
| **Refresh** | **Not required** | **Required** (~every 64 ms) |
| **Speed** | Very fast (~1 ns) | Slower (~50–100 ns) |
| **Density** | Low (6T per cell) | High (1T+1C per cell) |
| **Power** | Higher | Lower per bit |
| **Cost / Bit** | Higher | Lower |
| **Use Case** | CPU Cache, Registers | Main Memory |

**Why SRAM in cache?** Cache memory must respond to CPU requests in **1–2 ns** to avoid stalling the processor. SRAM's flip-flop cell is *static* — it holds data as long as power is on, with no need for periodic refreshing. This eliminates the refresh-cycle latency that would otherwise make DRAM-based cache impossible at sub-nanosecond speeds. Additionally, SRAM's cell is bistable, giving **near-instantaneous read/write**.

---

### Q2. What is meant by "Locality of Reference"? Explain its types. **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**
Locality of Reference is the **tendency of a processor to access a relatively small, clustered set of memory locations repeatedly** during a short period of program execution. This predictable behavior is the *theoretical justification* for designing a memory hierarchy.

**Types:**

1. **Temporal Locality:** The same memory location is accessed repeatedly within a small time window. *Example:* A loop variable `i` being incremented in a `for(i=0; i<1000; i++)` loop is accessed 1000 times in rapid succession.

2. **Spatial Locality:** Memory locations *physically near* a recently accessed location are likely to be accessed soon. *Example:* Sequential instruction fetch — the CPU reads instruction at address 1000, then 1001, 1002, etc.

> Both locality types enable the cache to *pre-fetch* blocks of data (Spatial) and *retain* recently used data (Temporal), achieving high hit ratios and hence low AMAT.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Pattern with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Module 4 ESE Pattern]**

### QUESTION A (Choice 1)

> **[a] Explain the different characteristics used to classify memory in a computer system.** **[7 Marks]**
> **[b] A system has a two-level memory hierarchy. Level 1 (Cache) has an access time of 10 ns and a hit rate of 90%. Level 2 (Main Memory) has an access time of 200 ns. Calculate: (i) Average Memory Access Time (AMAT), (ii) Speedup of the cache system over a no-cache system, (iii) New AMAT if hit rate is increased to 95%.** **[7 Marks]**

### Model Solution for Q.A (a):

The eight key characteristics for classifying memory are:

1. **Location** — Internal (registers, cache, RAM) vs External (HDD, USB).
2. **Capacity** — Internal is small (Bytes to MB), External is large (GB to TB).
3. **Unit of Transfer** — Word (for RAM) or Block (for cache/disk).
4. **Access Method** — Sequential, Direct, Random, or Associative.
5. **Performance** — Measured by access time $t_a$, cycle time $t_c$, and transfer rate $T_r$.
6. **Physical Type** — Semiconductor, Magnetic, Optical, or Magneto-Optical.
7. **Physical Characteristics** — Volatile (loses data on power-off) vs Non-Volatile.
8. **Organization** — Internal physical arrangement: bits per chip, chips per module, error-correcting code support.

---

### Model Solution for Q.A (b):

**Given:** $t_1 = 10$ ns, $H = 0.90$, $t_2 = 200$ ns.

**(i) AMAT Calculation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{AMAT} &= t_1 + (1 - H) \cdot t_2 \\
&= 10 + (1 - 0.90) \cdot 200 \\
&= 10 + 0.10 \cdot 200 \\
&= 10 + 20 \\
&= 30 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Speedup:**

$$\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{no-cache}} &= t_2 = 200 \text{ ns} \\
S &= \frac{200}{30} = 6.67
\end{aligned}$$

The cached system is **6.67 times faster** than a system without cache.

**(iii) New AMAT with H = 0.95:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{AMAT}_{\text{new}} &= 10 + (1 - 0.95) \cdot 200 \\
&= 10 + 0.05 \cdot 200 \\
&= 10 + 10 = 20 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}$$

The new AMAT is **20 ns**, a reduction of 10 ns (33% improvement) for a 5% hit-rate increase.

---

### QUESTION B (Choice 2)

> **[a] With a neat diagram, explain the memory hierarchy in a computer system. Discuss the trade-off between speed, cost, and capacity at each level.** **[7 Marks]**
> **[b] What is Average Memory Access Time (AMAT)? Derive the AMAT formula for a two-level memory hierarchy. A computer system has a cache with hit rate 80% and access time 50 ns. Main memory access time is 500 ns. Find the AMAT.** **[7 Marks]**

### Model Solution for Q.B (a):

**Memory Hierarchy (from top to bottom):**
Registers → L1 Cache → L2 Cache → L3 Cache → Main Memory (RAM) → SSD → HDD → Magnetic Tape

**Trade-off Analysis:**

- **Top of Pyramid (Registers):** *Smallest, Fastest, Most Expensive.* Built from flip-flops inside the CPU, holding 32–128 words. Speed (~0.5 ns) matches the CPU clock.
- **Cache (L1/L2/L3):** *Small, Very Fast, Expensive.* Uses SRAM to bridge the speed gap between CPU and RAM.
- **Main Memory (RAM):** *Medium size, Medium speed, Moderate cost.* Uses DRAM. Holds the *currently running* program and data.
- **Secondary Storage (SSD/HDD):** *Very large, Slow, Cheap.* Holds the OS, applications, and user data persistently.

**The Trade-off Triangle:**
As we go **down**, capacity ↑ and cost per bit ↓, but access time ↑. As we go **up**, speed ↑ and cost per bit ↑. The hierarchy balances these three by ensuring the *most frequently accessed* data sits in the *fastest, most expensive* level (Cache), while rarely used data is relegated to slow, cheap storage (HDD).

---

### Model Solution for Q.B (b):

**Definition:** AMAT is the *expected (average) time per memory reference* in a hierarchical system, accounting for both hit and miss scenarios.

**Derivation:** (See Section 3.1 above for the full derivation)

Final formula:

$$\text{AMAT} = t_1 + (1 - H) \cdot t_2$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

**Given:** $H = 0.80$, $t_1 = 50$ ns, $t_2 = 500$ ns.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{AMAT} &= 50 + (1 - 0.80) \cdot 500 \\
&= 50 + 0.20 \cdot 500 \\
&= 50 + 100 \\
&= 150 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}$$

**Answer:** The AMAT of the system is **150 ns**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $t_1$ term in miss cost:** Many students write AMAT = $(1-H) \cdot t_2$, forgetting that even a *miss* requires a full L1 lookup. The correct formula is $t_1 + (1-H) \cdot t_2$. Loss: **2–3 marks per question**.
> 2. **Unit confusion:** AMAT must be in the same time unit throughout. Mixing ns and µs without conversion is a **common 1-mark deduction**.
> 3. **Missing the diagram in Q.A(a):** "With a neat diagram" demands an actual hierarchy pyramid. A textual list without a diagram is penalized **2 marks**.
> 4. **Incomplete derivation:** Writing only the final AMAT formula in Q.B(b) without deriving it from $N$, $N_{\text{hit}}$, $N_{\text{miss}}$ leads to a **2-mark deduction** for missing the "Derive" instruction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Memory Characteristics and Hierarchy**

- [ ] **Memory Characteristics (8 Types):** Location, Capacity, Unit of Transfer, Access Method, Performance, Physical Type, Volatility, Organization. *All 8 must be memorized verbatim for KTU exams.*
- [ ] **Access Methods (4 Types):** Sequential → Direct → Random → Associative. *Order matters — each is faster than the previous.*
- [ ] **AMAT Formula (Golden Equation):** $\text{AMAT} = t_1 + (1 - H) \cdot t_2$. *Use this in every numerical.*
- [ ] **Three Memory Metrics:** Access Time ($t_a$), Cycle Time ($t_c$), Transfer Rate ($T_r = 1/t_c$ for RAM).
- [ ] **Hit Ratio vs Miss Ratio:** $H + M = 1$. Miss penalty = $t_2$ in simple hierarchies.
- [ ] **SRAM vs DRAM:** SRAM = 6T, no refresh, fast, expensive, used in cache. DRAM = 1T+1C, refresh needed, slower, cheap, used in main memory.
- [ ] **Locality of Reference (2 Types):** Temporal (same location again) and Spatial (nearby locations). *This is WHY hierarchy works.*
- [ ] **Hierarchy Order (Top to Bottom):** Register → L1 → L2 → L3 → RAM → SSD → HDD → Tape.
- [ ] **Trade-off Triangle:** Top = Fast + Costly + Small. Bottom = Slow + Cheap + Large.
- [ ] **Volatile vs Non-Volatile:** RAM is volatile. ROM, Flash, HDD, SSD are non-volatile.
- [ ] **Why Cache is Faster than RAM:** SRAM cells are static (no refresh) and physically closer to ALU.
- [ ] **Speedup Formula:** $S = t_2 / \text{AMAT}$. *Always compare to a no-cache baseline.*
