> **💡 Final Exam Tip:** When a KTU question asks for "memory characteristics", always list all **8** in a table. Partially listing only 4–5 is an automatic **2-mark cut**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cache Memory - The Basics of Caches
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT403 - COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE AND MICROCONTROLLER
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cache Memory - The Basics of Caches

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Cache Memory** is a small, high-speed volatile storage element interposed between the CPU and the main memory (RAM) to reduce the average memory access time. It temporarily stores a subset of the data and instructions from the main memory that the processor is most likely to require in the near future, exploiting the **Principle of Locality of Reference**.

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme (PCMRT403), the cache is treated as a *transparent* hardware-managed memory hierarchy layer. The CPU always issues logical/physical memory addresses, and dedicated control hardware determines whether the requested data resides in the cache (**Cache Hit**) or in main memory (**Cache Miss**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 4):** The basics of caches cover the *motivation*, *principle of locality*, *cache structure (lines/tags/indices)*, *mapping techniques* (Direct, Associative, Set-Associative), and *performance metrics* such as Hit Ratio, Miss Penalty, and Average Memory Access Time (AMAT).

### 1.2 Standard Physical Metrics & Terminology

| Term | Standard Notation | Typical Value (Modern Systems) |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Cache Hit Ratio | $h$ | **0.90 to 0.98 (90% – 98%)** |
| Cache Miss Ratio | $1-h$ | **0.02 to 0.10 (2% – 10%)** |
| Cache Access Time | $t_c$ | **1 ns to 10 ns** (L1) |
| Main Memory Access Time | $t_m$ | **50 ns to 100 ns** |
| Line / Block Size | $B$ | **16 / 32 / 64 / 128 Bytes** |
| Total Cache Size | $C$ | **16 KB to 64 MB** (across L1/L2/L3) |

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a busy university librarian (the **CPU**) who constantly needs specific books to answer student queries. The massive central library (the **Main Memory**) contains millions of books, but retrieving a book from there takes a long time — you must walk, search the index, fill a slip, and wait.

To speed things up, the librarian keeps a small personal desk drawer (the **Cache**) right next to his chair. This drawer only holds a tiny fraction (e.g., 50 books) of the entire library, but the librarian is clever:

- He anticipates the books he will likely need shortly (temporal locality).
- He brings related books together (spatial locality).

When a student asks for a book:

1. **Cache Hit:** The book is in the desk drawer → handed over in **seconds**.
2. **Cache Miss:** The book is *not* in the drawer → the librarian must walk to the main library → takes **minutes**.

The goal of cache design is to make "hit" the overwhelming norm, so the average service time is drastically reduced.

> [!NOTE]
> **Principle of Locality of Reference** is the philosophical foundation of caching. It is the empirical observation that programs do *not* access memory randomly; they exhibit two strong patterns:
> - **Temporal Locality:** If a memory location is referenced, it is highly likely to be referenced again in the near future (e.g., loop counters, frequently used variables).
> - **Spatial Locality:** If a memory location is referenced, nearby memory locations are highly likely to be referenced soon (e.g., traversing an array sequentially, executing sequential instructions).

### 1.4 Why Cache Memory Exists — The Memory Wall Problem

The speed gap between the CPU and the main memory (DRAM) has been widening for decades, a phenomenon known as the **Memory Wall**. While CPU clock frequencies have scaled into the GHz range (cycle times in nanoseconds), DRAM access times have improved only marginally.

Without a cache, the CPU would spend the majority of its clock cycles *stalled*, waiting for data from the slow DRAM. The cache acts as a *bandwidth and latency buffer*, ensuring that on the vast majority of cycles, the data the CPU needs is available within one or two clock cycles.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Anatomy of a Cache

A cache is organized as an array of **Cache Lines** (also called *Cache Blocks*). Each line is a small contiguous chunk of memory. To uniquely identify a line, the cache stores metadata alongside the data.

For any given main memory address, it is decomposed by the hardware into three fields:

$$
\text{Address} = \underbrace{\text{TAG}}_{\text{Identifier}} \;\; \vert \;\; \underbrace{\text{INDEX}}_{\text{Set/Line Number}} \;\; \vert \;\; \underbrace{\text{OFFSET}}_{\text{Byte within Line}}
$$

- **Tag:** The most significant bits of the address. It uniquely identifies which main memory block is currently stored in a particular cache line. Used for *validity verification* during a read.
- **Index:** The middle bits. They are used to *select* which line (or set) of the cache to check.
- **Offset:** The least significant bits. They select the exact byte (or word) within the cache line being accessed.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic of a Cache Read

When the CPU requests a memory address, the cache controller performs the following actions synchronously:

1. **Decompose Address:** The physical address is split into **Tag**, **Index**, and **Offset**.
2. **Index Decode:** The Index field is used to directly address a specific row in the cache's tag/data array.
3. **Tag Compare:** The stored Tag at that index is compared (in parallel across all ways in an associative cache) with the incoming Tag.
4. **Valid Bit Check:** Each line has a **Valid Bit (V)**. If V=0, the line is empty; even if the Tag matches, it is a Miss. If V=1, the comparison is meaningful.
5. **Decision Branch:**
   - **HIT (V=1 AND Tag matches):** The data at the specified *Offset* within the line is returned to the CPU in $\approx t_c$ time.
   - **MISS (V=0 OR Tag mismatch):** A block replacement occurs. The line is filled from main memory (a *block fetch*). The Tag is updated, V is set to 1, and the data is forwarded to the CPU.

### 2.3 Cache Mapping Techniques — The Core of KTU Module 4

The mapping technique defines *where* in the cache a particular main memory block may be placed. This is the heart of Module 4.

#### 2.3.1 Direct-Mapped Cache

A block from main memory can be placed in **exactly one** specific cache line. The mapping is deterministic:

$$
i = (\text{Block Address}) \bmod N_c
$$

where $N_c$ is the total number of lines in the cache. If two frequently used blocks map to the same index, they will continually evict each other — this is called a **Conflict Miss**.

#### 2.3.2 Fully Associative Cache

A block from main memory can be placed in **any** cache line. The Index field is conceptually empty (or zero), and the *entire* address (minus offset) becomes the Tag. The tag comparison is done in parallel against every valid line. This eliminates conflict misses but requires a large, power-hungry comparator array.

#### 2.3.3 Set-Associative Cache (K-Way)

A practical compromise. The cache is divided into $S$ sets, each containing $K$ lines (ways). A block maps to a specific set but can occupy *any way* within that set.

$$
\text{Set Index} = (\text{Block Address}) \bmod S
$$

- **K = 1:** Direct-Mapped
- **K = $N_c$:** Fully Associative
- **K = 2, 4, 8:** Typical real-world designs (L1/L2 caches).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are *high-yield* for KTU ESE 2024 and must be memorized verbatim.

| Parameter | Formula / Definition | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Average Memory Access Time (AMAT)** | $T_{avg} = h \cdot t_c + (1-h)(t_c + t_m)$ | seconds (s). Includes both hit and miss cost. |
| **Simplified AMAT** | $T_{avg} = t_c + (1-h) \cdot t_m$ | Used when miss penalty = $t_m$ (single-level model). |
| **Hit Ratio** | $h = \dfrac{\text{Total Hits}}{\text{Total Memory References}}$ | Dimensionless, $0 \le h \le 1$. |
| **Miss Ratio** | $m = 1 - h$ | Dimensionless. |
| **Number of Cache Lines** | $N_c = \dfrac{\text{Total Cache Size}}{\text{Line Size}} = \dfrac{C}{B}$ | Integer. |
| **Number of Sets (K-Way)** | $S = \dfrac{N_c}{K}$ | Integer. |
| **Index Bits** | $\text{Index bits} = \log_2(S)$ | For Set-Associative. |
| **Offset Bits** | $\text{Offset bits} = \log_2(B)$ | Depends on line size. |
| **Tag Bits** | $\text{Tag bits} = \text{Address bits} - \text{Index bits} - \text{Offset bits}$ | Physical address width. |
| **Speedup Factor (S)** | $S = \dfrac{t_m}{T_{avg}}$ | How much faster the system *feels* with cache. |
| **Access Time Efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{t_c}{T_{avg}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage. |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In modern CPU design (Intel Core, AMD Ryzen, Apple M-series), the cache hierarchy is multi-level:
- **L1 Cache:** 32–64 KB per core, split into L1d (data) and L1i (instruction), $\approx$ 1 ns access. Typically 8-way set-associative.
- **L2 Cache:** 256 KB – 1 MB per core, $\approx$ 3–10 ns access. 8-way or 16-way set-associative.
- **L3 Cache:** Several MB shared across cores, $\approx$ 10–20 ns access.

Cache performance directly determines the **Instructions Per Cycle (IPC)**. A 1% increase in L1 cache miss rate can degrade overall CPU performance by 5–10% in memory-bound workloads. Engineers tune block size, associativity, replacement policy (LRU/LFU/Random), and write policy (Write-Back/Write-Through) to optimize this delicate balance.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Average Memory Access Time (AMAT)

Let $h$ be the probability of a cache hit and $(1-h)$ be the probability of a cache miss.

**Step 1: Define the time cost of each outcome.**
- On a **Hit**, the total time spent is exactly the cache access time, $t_c$.
- On a **Miss**, the cache is first checked (spending $t_c$) and, upon failure, the data is fetched from main memory (spending $t_m$). Total miss time = $t_c + t_m$.

**Step 2: Formulate the expectation.**
The average access time is the expected value (weighted sum) of the two outcomes:

$$
T_{avg} = P(\text{Hit}) \cdot T_{\text{hit}} + P(\text{Miss}) \cdot T_{\text{miss}}
$$

**Step 3: Substitute the variables.**

$$
T_{avg} = h \cdot t_c + (1-h) \cdot (t_c + t_m)
$$

**Step 4: Expand and simplify algebraically.**

$$
T_{avg} = h \cdot t_c + (1-h) \cdot t_c + (1-h) \cdot t_m
$$

**Step 5: Group the $t_c$ terms.**

$$
T_{avg} = t_c \bigl[h + (1-h)\bigr] + (1-h) \cdot t_m
$$

**Step 6: Apply the identity $[h + (1-h)] = 1$.**

$$
T_{avg} = t_c + (1-h) \cdot t_m
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **canonical KTU formula**. The term $(1-h) \cdot t_m$ is called the **Miss Penalty**. Note that $t_c$ (cache access time) is *always* paid — even on a miss — because the cache must be checked first.

### 3.2 Derivation of Tag, Index, and Offset Bit Widths

Let:
- Physical Address width = $A$ bits
- Cache Size = $C$ bytes
- Line Size = $B$ bytes
- Associativity = $K$ ways

**Step 1: Compute the number of cache lines (total words in the cache).**

$$
N_c = \frac{C}{B}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the number of sets.**

$$
S = \frac{N_c}{K} = \frac{C}{B \cdot K}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the Offset bits.** To address $B$ bytes within a line:

$$
n_{\text{offset}} = \log_2(B)
$$

**Step 4: Compute the Index bits.** To uniquely select one of $S$ sets:

$$
n_{\text{index}} = \log_2(S) = \log_2\!\left(\frac{C}{B \cdot K}\right)
$$

**Step 5: Compute the Tag bits.** Whatever is left over identifies the block uniquely:

$$
n_{\text{tag}} = A - n_{\text{index}} - n_{\text{offset}}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Worked Example (KTU Standard Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** A system has a 32-bit physical address, a 64 KB direct-mapped cache with a line size of 16 bytes. Find: (i) Number of cache lines, (ii) Tag/Index/Offset bits, (iii) AMAT given $h = 0.95$, $t_c = 5$ ns, $t_m = 100$ ns.

**Solution:**

**(i) Number of Cache Lines:**

$$
N_c = \frac{\text{Cache Size}}{\text{Line Size}} = \frac{64 \text{ KB}}{16 \text{ B}} = \frac{65{,}536 \text{ B}}{16 \text{ B}} = 4096 \text{ lines}
$$

**(ii) Bit Decomposition of the 32-bit Address:**

Offset bits (line size = 16 B = $2^4$):

$$
n_{\text{offset}} = \log_2(16) = 4 \text{ bits}
$$

Index bits (4096 lines, direct-mapped $\Rightarrow$ 1 way $\Rightarrow$ $S = 4096$):

$$
n_{\text{index}} = \log_2(4096) = 12 \text{ bits}
$$

Tag bits (remaining):

$$
n_{\text{tag}} = 32 - 12 - 4 = 16 \text{ bits}
$$

**Address Layout:**

$$
\underbrace{\text{Tag}}_{16 \text{ bits}} \;\; \vert \;\; \underbrace{\text{Index}}_{12 \text{ bits}} \;\; \vert \;\; \underbrace{\text{Offset}}_{4 \text{ bits}}
$$

**(iii) AMAT Calculation:**

$$
T_{avg} = t_c + (1-h) \cdot t_m
$$

$$
T_{avg} = 5 \text{ ns} + (1 - 0.95) \cdot 100 \text{ ns}
$$

$$
T_{avg} = 5 \text{ ns} + 0.05 \cdot 100 \text{ ns} = 5 \text{ ns} + 5 \text{ ns} = 10 \text{ ns}
$$

**Speedup over no-cache system:**

$$
S = \frac{t_m}{T_{avg}} = \frac{100 \text{ ns}}{10 \text{ ns}} = 10
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** A 95% hit ratio yields a **10x speedup**. If we could improve $h$ to 0.98:

$$
T_{avg} = 5 + 0.02 \cdot 100 = 7 \text{ ns} \quad\Rightarrow\quad S = \frac{100}{7} \approx 14.3
$$

A mere 3% improvement in hit ratio nearly doubles the effective speedup — this is why cache tuning is critical in modern CPUs.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Mapping Techniques

| Property | Direct-Mapped | K-Way Set-Associative | Fully Associative |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Placement Constraint** | 1 specific line | 1 specific set, any of K ways | Anywhere |
| **Hardware Complexity** | Lowest | Moderate | Highest |
| **Tag Bits** | Largest | Moderate | Smallest (no index) |
| **Conflict Misses** | Highest | Low | None |
| **Capacity Misses** | Same as associativity | Same as associativity | Same as associativity |
| **Comparator Logic** | 1 comparator | K comparators (parallel) | $N_c$ comparators (parallel) |
| **Typical Use** | Embedded TLB entries | Modern L1/L2 caches | Translation Lookaside Buffers, small specialty caches |
| **Replacement Logic** | Not needed (deterministic) | LRU / Pseudo-LRU / Random | LRU (complex) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram — Cache in the Memory Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CPU["CPU Core\n(Registers, ALU, Control Unit)"]
    L1["L1 Cache\n(Smallest, Fastest)\nSize: 32-64 KB\nAccess: ~1 ns"]
    L2["L2 Cache\nSize: 256 KB - 1 MB\nAccess: ~5 ns"]
    L3["L3 Cache\nSize: 4-32 MB\nAccess: ~15 ns"]
    RAM["Main Memory DRAM\nSize: GBs\nAccess: ~50-100 ns"]
    DISK["Secondary Storage SSD/HDD\nSize: TBs\nAccess: ~ms"]

    CPU ==> L1
    L1 ==> L2
    L2 ==> L3
    L3 ==> RAM
    RAM ==> DISK

    classDef fastBox fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef slowBox fill:#4682B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef veryslowBox fill:#2F4F4F,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF

    class CPU,L1,L2,L3 fastBox
    class RAM slowBox
    class DISK veryslowBox
```

### 4.2 Address Decomposition Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PA["32-bit Physical Address"]
    PA --> TAG["Tag Field\n(Unique Block Identifier)"]
    PA --> IDX["Index Field\n(Selects Cache Set)"]
    PA --> OFF["Offset Field\n(Selects Byte in Line)"]

    TAG --> CMP{"Tag Compare\n(Parallel across K ways)"}
    IDX --> DEC["Cache Set Decoder"]
    OFF --> BYTE["Byte Selector\nwithin selected line"]
    DEC --> CMP
    CMP -->|MATCH and Valid=1| HIT["CACHE HIT\nReturn data to CPU\nTime = t_c"]
    CMP -->|MISMATCH or Valid=0| MISS["CACHE MISS\nFetch block from RAM\nTime = t_c + t_m"]

    classDef inputBox fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    classDef logicBox fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    classDef resultBox fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    classDef missBox fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px

    class PA,TAG,IDX,OFF inputBox
    class CMP,DEC,BYTE logicBox
    class HIT resultBox
    class MISS missBox
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Read Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["CPU Issues Physical Address ADDR"] --> B["Address Decomposer Unit\nSplits into Tag, Index, Offset"]
    B --> C["Cache Array Lookup\n(Index selects Set)"]
    C --> D["Way 0: Stored Tag"]
    C --> E["Way 1: Stored Tag"]
    C --> F["Way K-1: Stored Tag"]
    D --> G["Tag Comparator Array\n(Parallel)"]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    B --> H["Incoming Tag from ADDR"]
    H --> G
    G --> I{"Any Way Match?"}
    I -->|Yes| J["Valid Bit Check"]
    J -->|Valid=1| K["HIT Path\nSelect Mux chooses matched way\nOutput byte via Offset\nTime = t_c"]
    J -->|Valid=0| L["MISS Path\n(Block empty, treat as miss)"]
    I -->|No| L
    L --> M["Miss Handler\nIssue Read to Main Memory\nTime = t_m"]
    M --> N["Block Fill\nWrite into selected way\nUpdate Tag, set Valid=1"]
    N --> O["Forward Data to CPU\nand write to cache"]

    classDef cpuBox fill:#FFFFE0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    classDef cacheBox fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    classDef logicBox fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    classDef hitBox fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    classDef missBox fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px

    class A,B cpuBox
    class C,D,E,F,cacheBox
    class G,H,I,J logicBox
    class K hitBox
    class L,M,N,O missBox
```

### 4.4 Mapping Strategy Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    MM["Main Memory\n(Blocks 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, ...)"]

    MM --> Q1{"Mapping Strategy?"}

    Q1 -->|Direct-Mapped| D1["Block i maps to:\nCache Line = i mod N_c\n(Exactly 1 location)"]
    Q1 -->|Fully Associative| D2["Block i can map to:\nANY of N_c lines\n(Search all tags)"]
    Q1 -->|K-Way Set-Associative| D3["Block i maps to:\nSet = i mod S\n(S = N_c / K)\nAny of K ways within set"]

    D1 --> EX1["Example: 4 lines\nBlock 0 -> Line 0\nBlock 1 -> Line 1\nBlock 4 -> Line 0 (eviction conflict)"]
    D2 --> EX2["Example: 4 lines\nBlock 0 -> Any line 0-3\nBlock 4 -> Any line 0-3\n(No conflict)"]
    D3 --> EX3["Example: 4 lines, 2-way\nSet 0: {Line 0, Line 1}\nSet 1: {Line 2, Line 3}\nBlock 0 -> Set 0, way 0 or 1"]

    classDef inputBox fill:#F0F8FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    classDef mapBox fill:#FAFAD2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    classDef exBox fill:#F5DEB3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px

    class MM,Q1 inputBox
    class D1,D2,D3 mapBox
    class EX1,EX2,EX3 exBox
```

### 4.5 Hit/Miss Performance Timeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph T1["CACHE HIT Timeline"]
        A1["t=0: CPU issues address"] --> A2["t=0+epsilon: Tag compare"]
        A2 --> A3["t=t_c: Data available\nTOTAL = t_c"]
    end

    subgraph T2["CACHE MISS Timeline"]
        B1["t=0: CPU issues address"] --> B2["t=0+epsilon: Tag compare"]
        B2 --> B3["t=t_c: Miss detected"]
        B3 --> B4["t=t_c to t_c+t_m: Block fetch from RAM"]
        B4 --> B5["t=t_c+t_m: Data available\nTOTAL = t_c + t_m"]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024]**
**Define cache memory. Explain the principle of locality of reference with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Cache Memory Definition (1 mark):** Cache memory is a small, high-speed volatile memory located between the CPU and main memory (RAM). It stores a subset of the data and instructions from main memory that the CPU is likely to need, thereby reducing the average memory access time.

**Principle of Locality (1.5 marks):** The principle of locality of reference is the empirical observation that programs do not access memory randomly but in predictable patterns. It has two forms:

- **Temporal Locality:** If a memory location is referenced now, it is likely to be referenced again soon. *Example:* A loop counter variable accessed in every iteration of a `for` loop.
- **Spatial Locality:** If a memory location is referenced, neighboring locations are likely to be referenced soon. *Example:* Sequential traversal of an array `arr[0], arr[1], arr[2]...`

**Why Cache Works (0.5 marks):** Because programs exhibit strong locality, a small cache can serve the vast majority (typically >90%) of memory requests, hiding the slowness of main memory.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2023]**
**Differentiate between cache hit and cache miss. What is meant by hit ratio?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Cache Hit | Cache Miss |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Definition** | The requested data is found in the cache. | The requested data is NOT in the cache. |
| **Time Taken** | $t_c$ (cache access time only) | $t_c + t_m$ (cache check + main memory fetch) |
| **CPU State** | No stall; pipeline continues. | CPU stalls; pipeline is flushed/paused. |
| **Data Location** | In a cache line whose Tag matches. | Must be fetched from DRAM and a line evicted. |

**Hit Ratio (0.5 marks):** The hit ratio $h$ is the fraction of total memory accesses that result in a cache hit:

$$
h = \frac{\text{Number of Cache Hits}}{\text{Total Number of Memory References}}
$$

A higher $h$ indicates better cache performance.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]**
**(a)** A computer system uses a 32-bit physical address. The L1 cache is direct-mapped with a capacity of 32 KB and a line size of 16 bytes. Calculate: (i) Number of cache lines, (ii) Number of bits required for Tag, Index, and Offset. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With reference to part (a), if the cache access time is 4 ns and main memory access time is 80 ns, calculate the average memory access time (AMAT) and speedup for a hit ratio of 0.92. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**(i) Number of Cache Lines (2 marks):**

$$
N_c = \frac{\text{Cache Size}}{\text{Line Size}} = \frac{32 \text{ KB}}{16 \text{ B}} = \frac{32{,}768 \text{ B}}{16 \text{ B}} = 2048 \text{ lines}
$$

**(ii) Address Decomposition (5 marks):**

**Offset bits (line size = 16 B = $2^4$):**

$$
n_{\text{offset}} = \log_2(16) = 4 \text{ bits} \quad \text{[1 mark]}
$$

**Index bits (2048 lines, direct-mapped, so $S = 2048$):**

$$
n_{\text{index}} = \log_2(2048) = 11 \text{ bits} \quad \text{[2 marks]}
$$

**Tag bits (remaining from 32-bit address):**

$$
n_{\text{tag}} = 32 - 11 - 4 = 17 \text{ bits} \quad \text{[2 marks]}
$$

**Address Format:**

$$
\underbrace{\text{Tag}}_{17 \text{ bits}} \;\; \vert \;\; \underbrace{\text{Index}}_{11 \text{ bits}} \;\; \vert \;\; \underbrace{\text{Offset}}_{4 \text{ bits}}
$$

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**AMAT Calculation (4 marks):**

$$
T_{avg} = t_c + (1 - h) \cdot t_m
$$

$$
T_{avg} = 4 \text{ ns} + (1 - 0.92) \cdot 80 \text{ ns}
$$

$$
T_{avg} = 4 \text{ ns} + 0.08 \cdot 80 \text{ ns}
$$

$$
T_{avg} = 4 \text{ ns} + 6.4 \text{ ns} = 10.4 \text{ ns}
$$

**Speedup Calculation (3 marks):**

$$
\text{Speedup} = \frac{t_m}{T_{avg}} = \frac{80 \text{ ns}}{10.4 \text{ ns}} \approx 7.69
$$

> **Conclusion:** With a 92% hit ratio, the system runs approximately 7.7 times faster than a system without a cache.

---

**Q4. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**(a)** Explain the three cache mapping techniques: direct-mapped, fully associative, and set-associative. Compare them based on hardware complexity, conflict misses, and search speed. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 2-way set-associative cache has 8 KB capacity with a block size of 32 bytes. The physical address is 20 bits. Determine: (i) Number of sets, (ii) Tag, Index, and Offset bits, (iii) Total tag memory required (assume 1 valid bit per line). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Direct-Mapped Cache (2.5 marks):**
Each main memory block maps to **exactly one** cache line, determined by:

$$
\text{Line Number} = (\text{Block Address}) \bmod N_c
$$

- *Advantages:* Simple, fast lookup (single comparator), low cost.
- *Disadvantages:* Suffers from **conflict misses** — two frequently used blocks mapping to the same line will repeatedly evict each other (the "ping-pong" effect).

**Fully Associative Cache (2.5 marks):**
A block can be placed in **any** cache line. All tags are searched **in parallel** using $N_c$ comparators.

- *Advantages:* Zero conflict misses, maximum flexibility.
- *Disadvantages:* Hardware-intensive (large parallel comparator array), high power consumption, impractical for large caches.

**Set-Associative Cache (2 marks):**
A **hybrid** that divides the cache into $S$ sets of $K$ ways each. A block maps to a specific set but can occupy any way within that set:

$$
\text{Set Index} = (\text{Block Address}) \bmod S
$$

- *Advantages:* Reduces conflict misses by a factor of $K$ compared to direct-mapped, with moderate hardware cost.
- *Disadvantages:* Requires $K$ parallel comparators and a replacement policy (LRU approximation).

**Comparison Table Summary:**

| Aspect | Direct | Set-Associative | Fully Associative |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Hardware | Low | Moderate | High |
| Conflict Misses | High | Low | None |
| Search Speed | Fastest | Moderate | Slowest (parallel) |

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Given:** 2-way set-associative, Cache = 8 KB, Block = 32 B, Address = 20 bits.

**(i) Number of Sets (2 marks):**

First, compute total lines:

$$
N_c = \frac{8 \text{ KB}}{32 \text{ B}} = \frac{8192}{32} = 256 \text{ lines}
$$

Since it is 2-way, each set has 2 lines:

$$
S = \frac{N_c}{K} = \frac{256}{2} = 128 \text{ sets}
$$

**(ii) Bit Decomposition (3 marks):**

Offset bits (block size 32 B = $2^5$):

$$
n_{\text{offset}} = \log_2(32) = 5 \text{ bits}
$$

Index bits (128 sets):

$$
n_{\text{index}} = \log_2(128) = 7 \text{ bits}
$$

Tag bits:

$$
n_{\text{tag}} = 20 - 7 - 5 = 8 \text{ bits}
$$

**Address Format:**

$$
\underbrace{\text{Tag}}_{8 \text{ bits}} \;\; \vert \;\; \underbrace{\text{Index}}_{7 \text{ bits}} \;\; \vert \;\; \underbrace{\text{Offset}}_{5 \text{ bits}}
$$

**(iii) Total Tag Memory Required (2 marks):**

Each line stores: 8 bits of tag + 1 valid bit = 9 bits of metadata.
Total lines = 256.
Total metadata memory:

$$
256 \times 9 \text{ bits} = 2304 \text{ bits} = 288 \text{ bytes}
$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes — Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to include $t_c$ in the miss path.** Many students incorrectly write $T_{avg} = h \cdot t_c + (1-h) \cdot t_m$. The CPU still pays $t_c$ to *check* the cache before discovering a miss. The correct formula is $T_{avg} = t_c + (1-h) \cdot t_m$ (or expanded form).
>
> 2. **Confusing cache line count with cache size.** A "32 KB cache" does NOT mean 32K lines. Always divide by the line/block size first.
>
> 3. **Tag/Index/Offset bit calculation errors.** A common slip is computing offset as $\log_2(\text{cache size})$ instead of $\log_2(\text{block size})$. Remember: Offset is determined by the *line* size, not the cache size.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the Valid Bit in set-associative designs.** Each line (per way) requires a Valid Bit. Examiners explicitly test for this in tag memory calculations.
>
> 5. **Unit errors in AMAT.** Always carry units (ns, µs) throughout the calculation. Dropping units mid-solution loses a mark.
>
> 6. **In set-associative mapping, the Index selects the SET, not the line.** The line is selected within the set by the tag comparator. Examiners frequently test this conceptual distinction.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Cache Memory Basics**

- **Cache** is a small, fast memory between CPU and RAM that exploits **locality of reference** to reduce AMAT.
- **Locality** has two forms: **Temporal** (recently used → will be used again) and **Spatial** (near addresses → will be used together).
- An address decomposes into **Tag | Index | Offset**.
- **Offset bits** = $\log_2(\text{Block Size})$.
- **Index bits** = $\log_2(\text{Number of Sets})$.
- **Tag bits** = Address bits − Index bits − Offset bits.
- **Direct-Mapped:** 1 block → 1 line. Simple, but conflict-prone.
- **Fully Associative:** 1 block → any line. Best hit rate, most expensive.
- **Set-Associative:** 1 block → 1 set of K lines. Industry standard compromise.
- **Hit Ratio** $h$ = Hits / Total References. Modern CPUs target $h > 0.95$.
- **AMAT** = $t_c + (1-h) \cdot t_m$ — the single most important cache performance equation.
- **Miss Penalty** = $(1-h) \cdot t_m$ — the extra time paid on misses.
- **Speedup** = $t_m / T_{avg}$ — quantifies cache benefit.
- A **Valid Bit** is required per line to distinguish "empty" from "valid block with matching tag."
- Replacement policies (LRU, FIFO, Random) are needed *only* for associative caches.
- Write policies (**Write-Back** vs **Write-Through**) affect consistency vs performance trade-offs — covered in advanced topics.
- **Conflict Misses** are unique to direct-mapped and low-associativity caches.
- **Capacity Misses** depend on cache size, not associativity.
- For 2024 KTU Scheme: focus on numerical problems involving AMAT, bit decomposition, and mapping technique selection.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mapping Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT403 - COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE AND MICROCONTROLLER
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Mapping Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Mapping Techniques** are the systematic hardware strategies used by the memory management unit (MMU) and cache controller to determine **where** a particular block of main memory (RAM) data is physically placed within the smaller, faster cache memory. They define the exact correspondence (translation rule) between the **Main Memory Address (MMA)** issued by the CPU and the **Cache Memory Address (CMA)** where that data resides.

In the KTU 2024 scheme, the three classical mapping techniques studied under Module 4 are:

1. **Direct Mapping**
2. **Fully Associative Mapping**
3. **Set-Associative Mapping** (the industrial compromise)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Library Bookshelf" Intuition

Imagine a huge library (Main Memory) with **millions of books**, but you only have a tiny personal reading desk (Cache) that holds exactly **8 books**.

- When you want a book, you have to decide *which slot* on your desk to place it.
- A **rule** dictates this placement. That rule is the *Mapping Technique*.

| Mapping Type | Library Analogy |
|---|---|
| **Direct Mapping** | Book #1058 MUST go to desk slot #2 (because $1058 \mod 8 = 2$). One fixed slot, no choice. |
| **Fully Associative** | Book #1058 can go to ANY of the 8 desk slots — you search all 8 to find it later. |
| **Set-Associative** | Books are grouped by category (e.g., shelf). Book #1058 MUST go into a specific *shelf*, but you choose any *slot within that shelf*. |

## 1.3 Why Do We Need Mapping Techniques?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Principle — Locality of Reference:** Programs tend to access memory locations that are *near* recently used locations (Spatial Locality) or *repeat* recently used locations (Temporal Locality). Cache memory exploits this principle. However, the cache is **drastically smaller** than main memory (typically $1/100^{th}$ the size), so an algorithmic placement-and-lookup strategy is **mandatory**. This strategy is what we call a *mapping technique*.

## 1.4 Standard Nomenclature (KTU High-Yield Vocabulary)

- **Main Memory (MM)** — Larger, slower, primary storage (RAM).
- **Cache Memory (CM)** — Smaller, faster, expensive storage (SRAM).
- **Block / Line** — The fundamental transfer unit between MM and CM.
- **Tag** — A unique identifier stored alongside each cache line to verify identity.
- **Word** — The smallest addressable unit transferred to the CPU.
- **Hit** — Requested data is found in cache.
- **Miss** — Requested data is NOT in cache (must fetch from MM).
- **Hit Ratio (h)** — Fraction of memory accesses satisfied by cache. **Standard target value: 0.9 to 0.98**.
- **Miss Ratio (1 − h)** — Fraction of accesses that miss cache.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Main Memory Address Format Decomposition

Every memory address issued by the CPU is logically partitioned into **three functional fields**. The width (number of bits) of each field is determined entirely by the mapping technique and the cache architecture.

Let:
- $N$ = Total number of main memory blocks (or words, depending on context)
- $M$ = Total number of cache lines (blocks)
- $W$ = Number of words per cache block
- $K$ = Set-associativity (number of ways per set)
- $S$ = Number of sets $= M / K$

### 2.1.1 Direct Mapping Address Format

$$
\underbrace{\text{TAG}}_{\text{log}_2(N/M) \text{ bits}} \;\; \underbrace{\text{LINE}}_{\text{log}_2(M) \text{ bits}} \;\; \underbrace{\text{WORD}}_{\text{log}_2(W) \text{ bits}}
$$

**Placement Rule:**
$$
\text{Cache Line Number} = (\text{Main Memory Block Number}) \bmod M
$$

### 2.1.2 Fully Associative Mapping Address Format

$$
\underbrace{\text{TAG}}_{\text{log}_2(N) \text{ bits}} \;\; \underbrace{\text{WORD}}_{\text{log}_2(W) \text{ bits}}
$$

**Placement Rule:** *Any* cache line. Hardware must compare the incoming tag with **every** stored tag simultaneously (parallel CAM search).

### 2.1.3 Set-Associative Mapping Address Format

$$
\underbrace{\text{TAG}}_{\text{log}_2(N/S) \text{ bits}} \;\; \underbrace{\text{SET}}_{\text{log}_2(S) \text{ bits}} \;\; \underbrace{\text{WORD}}_{\text{log}_2(W) \text{ bits}}
$$

**Placement Rule:**
$$
\text{Set Number} = (\text{Main Memory Block Number}) \bmod S
$$
The block may occupy **any** of the $K$ ways inside that set.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Notes / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cache Line (Direct Map) | $i = j \bmod M$ | $j$ = MM block, $M$ = cache lines |
| 2 | Set Number (Set-Assoc.) | $s = j \bmod S$ | $S = M/K$ sets |
| 3 | Number of Tags to Compare | $K$ (Set-Associative) | $1$ for Direct, $M$ for Fully-Assoc. |
| 4 | Tag Bits (Direct) | $\log_2(N/M)$ | Stored in tag array |
| 5 | Tag Bits (Fully Assoc.) | $\log_2(N)$ | Largest tag field |
| 6 | Tag Bits (Set-Assoc.) | $\log_2(N/S)$ | Compromise value |
| 7 | Word Field Bits | $\log_2(W)$ | Determines block word-offset |
| 8 | Total MM Address Bits | $\log_2(N \cdot W)$ | Sum of all 3 fields |
| 9 | Average Access Time | $T_a = h \cdot T_c + (1-h)(T_c + T_m)$ | $T_c$ = cache, $T_m$ = main memory |
| 10 | Access Time Ratio | $r = T_m / T_c$ | Often $r = 5, 10, 100$ |
| 11 | Simplified $T_a$ | $T_a = T_c \left[r + (1-r)h \right]$ | Using substitution |
| 12 | Hit Ratio (improved) | $h_{imp} = \dfrac{h_1 + h_2 - h_1 h_2}{1}$ | For hierarchical L1+L2 |
| 13 | Memory Capacity | $\text{Cap} = M \cdot W \cdot (\text{word size})$ | Bits/Bytes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL TABLE NOTE:** All formulas above are universally accepted KTU board-standard expressions. The variable $r$ (access time ratio) is the **single most frequently tested quantity** in Part B numerical problems.

## 2.3 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Direct Mapping** is used in **L1 instruction caches** of low-power embedded microcontrollers (e.g., ARM Cortex-M series) due to its minimal hardware footprint.
- **Fully Associative Mapping** is the theoretical gold standard; its practical cousin is the **Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB)** in CPU MMUs.
- **Set-Associative Mapping** (especially **2-way** and **8-way**) dominates commercial CPUs — Intel Core, AMD Ryzen, Apple M-series all use it for their L1/L2/L3 caches.

## 2.4 Comparison Matrix (Board-Favorite Table)

| Property | Direct Mapping | Fully Associative | Set-Associative |
|---|---|---|---|
| Placement Flexibility | 1 fixed slot | Any slot | Any slot in 1 set |
| Tag Comparison Hardware | 1 comparator | $M$ comparators | $K$ comparators |
| Hardware Cost | **Lowest** | **Highest** | Moderate |
| Speed of Lookup | Fastest | Slowest | Moderate |
| Conflict Misses | High | **Zero** | Low |
| Implementation Complexity | Simplest | Most complex | Balanced |
| KTU Board Frequency | ★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★★ |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Direct Mapping Address Partition

### Problem Setup
Given a system where:
- Main Memory size = $16\text{ K} \times 16$ bits (i.e., $16384$ words)
- Cache Memory size = $1\text{ K} \times 16$ bits (i.e., $1024$ words)
- Block size (W) = 4 words

### Step 1 — Determine Total Main Memory Address Bits

$$
N_{total} = 16\text{K} = 16 \times 1024 = 16384 \text{ words}
$$

$$
\text{Total Address Bits} = \log_2(16384)
$$

$$
\text{Total Address Bits} = \log_2(2^{14}) = 14 \text{ bits}
$$

### Step 2 — Determine Number of Words per Block (W)

$$
W = 4 \text{ words}
$$

$$
\text{Word Offset Bits} = \log_2(W) = \log_2(4) = 2 \text{ bits}
$$

### Step 3 — Determine Number of Cache Lines (M)

$$
M = \frac{\text{Cache Capacity}}{\text{Block Size}} = \frac{1024 \text{ words}}{4 \text{ words/line}} = 256 \text{ lines}
$$

$$
\text{Line Index Bits} = \log_2(M) = \log_2(256) = \log_2(2^{8}) = 8 \text{ bits}
$$

### Step 4 — Determine Tag Bits

$$
\text{Tag Bits} = \text{Total Bits} - \text{Line Bits} - \text{Word Bits}
$$

$$
\text{Tag Bits} = 14 - 8 - 2 = 4 \text{ bits}
$$

### Step 5 — Final Address Partition

$$
\boxed{ \underbrace{\text{TAG}}_{4 \text{ bits}} \;\; \underbrace{\text{LINE}}_{8 \text{ bits}} \;\; \underbrace{\text{WORD OFFSET}}_{2 \text{ bits}} }
$$

### Step 6 — Verification (Total Bits)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total} &= 4 + 8 + 2 \\
&= 14 \text{ bits} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Set-Associative Address Partition

### Problem Setup
- Main Memory size = $32\text{ K} \times 12$ bits
- Cache size = $4\text{ K} \times 12$ bits
- Block size = 4 words
- Associativity $K = 2$ (Two-way set-associative)

### Step 1 — Total Main Memory Words

$$
N_{total} = 32\text{K} = 32768 \text{ words}
$$

$$
\text{Total Address Bits} = \log_2(32768) = \log_2(2^{15}) = 15 \text{ bits}
$$

### Step 2 — Word Offset Bits

$$
\text{Word Bits} = \log_2(4) = 2 \text{ bits}
$$

### Step 3 — Number of Cache Lines

$$
M = \frac{4\text{K}}{4} = \frac{4096}{4} = 1024 \text{ lines}
$$

### Step 4 — Number of Sets (S)

$$
S = \frac{M}{K} = \frac{1024}{2} = 512 \text{ sets}
$$

$$
\text{Set Bits} = \log_2(S) = \log_2(512) = \log_2(2^{9}) = 9 \text{ bits}
$$

### Step 5 — Tag Bits

$$
\text{Tag Bits} = 15 - 9 - 2 = 4 \text{ bits}
$$

### Step 6 — Final Partition

$$
\boxed{ \underbrace{\text{TAG}}_{4} \;\; \underbrace{\text{SET}}_{9} \;\; \underbrace{\text{WORD}}_{2} }
$$

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation — Average Access Time (Numerical)

### Problem Statement
A system has cache access time $T_c = 50\text{ ns}$ and main memory access time $T_m = 500\text{ ns}$. The hit ratio is $h = 0.9$. Compute the average access time $T_a$ using the KTU standard formula.

### Step 1 — Identify Formula

$$
T_a = h \cdot T_c + (1-h)(T_c + T_m)
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute Numerical Values

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_a &= (0.9)(50) + (1 - 0.9)(50 + 500) \\
T_a &= (0.9)(50) + (0.1)(550) \\
T_a &= 45 + 55 \\
T_a &= 100 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Alternate Form Using $r$

$$
r = \frac{T_m}{T_c} = \frac{500}{50} = 10
$$

$$
T_a = T_c [r + (1-r)h]
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_a &= 50 \cdot [10 + (1-10)(0.9)] \\
T_a &= 50 \cdot [10 + (-9)(0.9)] \\
T_a &= 50 \cdot [10 - 8.1] \\
T_a &= 50 \cdot [1.9] \\
T_a &= 95 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Both formulas are accepted on KTU exams**, but the *first* formula $T_a = hT_c + (1-h)(T_c + T_m)$ is the most commonly tested form, as it explicitly models the *miss penalty* as the time to load the cache *plus* the time to read from main memory.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Worked Boundary Cases

| Scenario | $h$ | $T_c$ (ns) | $T_m$ (ns) | $T_a$ (ns) | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| All hits | $1.0$ | 50 | 500 | 50 | Ideal cache, no MM access |
| 90% hit | $0.9$ | 50 | 500 | 100 | Typical real system |
| 50% hit | $0.5$ | 50 | 500 | 325 | Poor cache utilization |
| All misses | $0.0$ | 50 | 500 | 550 | Cache useless |

---

## 3.5 Complete Worked Example — Placement using Direct Mapping

### Problem
Given a 16-block main memory and a 4-line cache, find the cache line number for MM blocks: **0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7** using direct mapping.

### Step 1 — Identify Formula

$$
i = j \bmod M \quad \text{where } M = 4
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Each Placement

$$
\begin{aligned}
j=0 &: \quad 0 \bmod 4 = 0 \\
j=1 &: \quad 1 \bmod 4 = 1 \\
j=2 &: \quad 2 \bmod 4 = 2 \\
j=3 &: \quad 3 \bmod 4 = 3 \\
j=4 &: \quad 4 \bmod 4 = 0 \;\; \text{(CONFLICT with block 0)} \\
j=5 &: \quad 5 \bmod 4 = 1 \;\; \text{(CONFLICT with block 1)} \\
j=6 &: \quad 6 \bmod 4 = 2 \;\; \text{(CONFLICT with block 2)} \\
j=7 &: \quad 7 \bmod 4 = 3 \;\; \text{(CONFLICT with block 3)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** Blocks $j=0$ and $j=4$ both map to line 0. This is a *conflict miss* — the inherent weakness of direct mapping. Set-associative mapping fixes this by giving multiple "homes" to conflicting blocks.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Direct Mapping — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CPU["CPU Address Bus\n(Tag | Line | Word)"]
    TAG_REG["Tag Register\n(Stores incoming tag)"]
    LINE_DEC["Line Decoder\nlog2 M to 1 decoder"]
    CACHE_ARR["Cache Memory Array\nM Lines x W Words"]
    COMP["Comparator\n(1 unit)"]
    HIT_LOGIC["Hit / Miss Logic"]
    DATA_OUT["Data Out to CPU"]
    TAG_ARR["Tag Array\nM entries"]

    CPU --> TAG_REG
    CPU --> LINE_DEC
    LINE_DEC --> CACHE_ARR
    LINE_DEC --> TAG_ARR
    TAG_ARR --> COMP
    TAG_REG --> COMP
    COMP --> HIT_LOGIC
    HIT_LOGIC --> DATA_OUT
    CACHE_ARR --> DATA_OUT
```

**Architectural Insight:** The single comparator is the *defining feature* of direct mapping — only **one tag comparison** is needed per access, making it the fastest and cheapest hardware.

---

## 4.2 Fully Associative Mapping — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CPU_IN["CPU Issues Address\n(Tag | Word)"]
    TAG_IN["Incoming Tag\nlog2 N bits"]
    WORD_IN["Word Offset\nlog2 W bits"]
    CAM["Content Addressable Memory\nTag Array M entries"]
    MULTI_COMP["Parallel Comparator Bank\nM Comparators"]
    ENCODER["Priority Encoder"]
    SELECT["Cache Line Selector"]
    DATA_BLOCK["Cache Data Array\nM x W words"]
    DATA_OUT2["Selected Word Out"]

    CPU_IN --> TAG_IN
    CPU_IN --> WORD_IN
    TAG_IN --> CAM
    CAM --> MULTI_COMP
    MULTI_COMP --> ENCODER
    ENCODER --> SELECT
    SELECT --> DATA_BLOCK
    DATA_BLOCK --> DATA_OUT2
```

**Architectural Insight:** Fully associative uses **M comparators in parallel**. For a 1024-line cache, this means **1024 simultaneous comparisons** — extraordinarily expensive in transistors but eliminates ALL conflict misses.

---

## 4.3 Set-Associative (2-Way) Mapping — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ADDR["CPU Address\n(Tag | Set | Word)"]
    SET_DEC["Set Index Decoder\nSelects ONE set from S"]
    WAY0["WAY 0\nTag + Data"]
    WAY1["WAY 1\nTag + Data"]
    CMP0["Comparator 0"]
    CMP1["Comparator 1"]
    MUX["Output Multiplexer\n(Selects hit way)"]
    OUT["Data Out"]

    ADDR --> SET_DEC
    SET_DEC --> WAY0
    SET_DEC --> WAY1
    WAY0 --> CMP0
    WAY1 --> CMP1
    ADDR --> CMP0
    ADDR --> CMP1
    CMP0 --> MUX
    CMP1 --> MUX
    WAY0 --> MUX
    WAY1 --> MUX
    MUX --> OUT
```

**Architectural Insight:** This is the **industrial sweet spot**. Only **K comparators** (here, 2) activate per access, but each set provides K "homes" for blocks that would otherwise conflict in direct mapping.

---

## 4.4 Conceptual Mapping Comparison Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    MM["MAIN MEMORY\nBlocks: 0,1,2,3,4,5,6,7"]
    DM["DIRECT MAPPING\n4 Cache Lines"]
    FAM["FULLY ASSOCIATIVE\n4 Cache Lines (any)"]
    SAM["SET-ASSOCIATIVE 2-WAY\n2 Sets x 2 Ways"]

    MM -- "0 mod 4 = 0" --> DM
    MM -- "1 mod 4 = 1" --> DM
    MM -- "4 mod 4 = 0 (conflict)" --> DM

    MM -- "any slot" --> FAM
    MM -- "any slot" --> FAM
    MM -- "no restriction" --> FAM

    MM -- "set = j mod 2" --> SAM
    MM -- "way = free slot" --> SAM
    MM -- "no conflict misses" --> SAM

    DM:::redStyle
    FAM:::greenStyle
    SAM:::blueStyle

    classDef redStyle fill:#FFD7D7,stroke:#C33,stroke-width:2px
    classDef greenStyle fill:#D7FFD7,stroke:#3C3,stroke-width:2px
    classDef blueStyle fill:#D7D7FF,stroke:#33C,stroke-width:2px
```

---

## 4.5 Address Partition Visualization (Direct Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Main Memory Address (14 bits)"]
    A --> A1["TAG\n4 bits"]
    A --> A2["LINE INDEX\n8 bits"]
    A --> A3["WORD OFFSET\n2 bits"]

    A1:::tagStyle
    A2:::lineStyle
    A3:::wordStyle

    classDef tagStyle fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px
    classDef lineStyle fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px
    classDef wordStyle fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px
```

**Reading the diagram:** The **TAG** identifies which main-memory block is currently stored in the line. The **LINE INDEX** selects the physical cache line. The **WORD OFFSET** selects the byte/word inside the cache block.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is meant by "mapping" in the context of cache memory? List the three standard mapping techniques.**

**Model Answer:**

Mapping is the algorithmic rule that determines **where** a particular block of main memory data is placed within the cache when it is brought in for the first time. Since the cache is significantly smaller than main memory, a deterministic (or partially deterministic) placement-and-retrieval rule is required.

The three standard techniques are:
1. **Direct Mapping**
2. **Fully Associative Mapping**
3. **Set-Associative Mapping**

*(Award full 3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for naming, 1 for any one-line elaboration.)*

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between "Hit" and "Miss" in cache memory. Define "Hit Ratio".**

**Model Answer:**

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Hit** | The CPU-requested data is **found** in the cache memory. |
| **Miss** | The CPU-requested data is **NOT in the cache**, forcing a fetch from main memory (which is slower). |

**Hit Ratio ($h$):**
$$
h = \frac{\text{Number of cache hits}}{\text{Total number of memory accesses}}
$$

It is a dimensionless fraction between 0 and 1, and a higher $h$ indicates better cache performance.

---

## 📕 PART B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students **frequently lose 2–3 marks** by **forgetting to subtract** the word-offset bits when computing tag bits.
> - Common error: writing *Total bits* instead of *Tag bits* in the final answer.
> - Always draw the **address format partition diagram** — examiners award 1–2 marks for it even when numerical answers are correct.
> - For "Average Access Time" problems, **state the formula before substitution** — half-marks are awarded for the correct formula even if arithmetic fails.
> - Never confuse **cache lines ($M$)** with **sets ($S$)** — they differ by a factor of $K$ (associativity).

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

Consider a cache memory system with the following specifications:
- Main Memory size = $64\text{ K words}$
- Cache Memory size = $4\text{ K words}$
- Block size = 8 words

#### **(a)** **[7 Marks]** Design the address format for **Direct Mapping**. Show all derivations clearly.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Total Address Bits:**
$$
64\text{K} = 2^{16} \Rightarrow 16 \text{ address bits}
$$

**Step 2 — Word Offset:**
$$
W = 8 = 2^3 \Rightarrow 3 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 3 — Number of Cache Lines:**
$$
M = \frac{4\text{K}}{8} = \frac{4096}{8} = 512 = 2^9 \Rightarrow 9 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 4 — Tag Bits:**
$$
\text{Tag} = 16 - 9 - 3 = 4 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 5 — Final Address Format:**

$$
\underbrace{\text{TAG}}_{4} \;\; \underbrace{\text{LINE}}_{9} \;\; \underbrace{\text{WORD}}_{3}
$$

**Placement Rule:**
$$
i = j \bmod 512
$$

#### **(b)** **[7 Marks]** If the cache access time is $T_c = 100$ ns and main memory access time is $T_m = 1000$ ns, find the **average access time** when hit ratio $h = 0.95$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — State Formula:**
$$
T_a = h \cdot T_c + (1-h)(T_c + T_m)
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_a &= (0.95)(100) + (0.05)(100 + 1000) \\
T_a &= 95 + (0.05)(1100) \\
T_a &= 95 + 55 \\
T_a &= 150 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer: $T_a = 150$ ns**

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

A computer system uses **2-way set-associative** cache with the following parameters:
- Main Memory size = $32\text{ K words}$
- Cache size = $2\text{ K words}$
- Block size = 4 words

#### **(a)** **[7 Marks]** Determine the address format, number of sets, and tag comparison hardware required.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Total Bits:**
$$
32\text{K} = 2^{15} \Rightarrow 15 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 2 — Word Bits:**
$$
W = 4 = 2^2 \Rightarrow 2 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 3 — Cache Lines:**
$$
M = \frac{2\text{K}}{4} = \frac{2048}{4} = 512 \text{ lines}
$$

**Step 4 — Number of Sets ($K = 2$):**
$$
S = \frac{M}{K} = \frac{512}{2} = 256 \text{ sets}
$$

**Step 5 — Set Bits:**
$$
\log_2(256) = 8 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 6 — Tag Bits:**
$$
\text{Tag} = 15 - 8 - 2 = 5 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 7 — Address Format:**

$$
\underbrace{\text{TAG}}_{5} \;\; \underbrace{\text{SET}}_{8} \;\; \underbrace{\text{WORD}}_{2}
$$

**Step 8 — Comparators Required:**
$$
K = 2 \text{ comparators per set access}
$$

#### **(b)** **[7 Marks]** Compare the **hardware cost** of this 2-way set-associative design with a direct-mapped and a fully-associative cache of identical capacity.

**Solution:**

Let $M = 512$ cache lines.

| Technique | Comparators Required | Cost Factor | Conflict Misses |
|---|---|---|---|
| Direct Mapping | $1$ | $1 \times$ (cheapest) | Highest |
| 2-Way Set-Associative | $2$ | $2 \times$ (moderate) | Low |
| Fully Associative | $512$ | $512 \times$ (expensive) | **Zero** |

**Conclusion:** The 2-way set-associative design **doubles** the comparator hardware compared to direct mapping but reduces conflict misses significantly. It is the most cost-effective compromise used in real CPUs (e.g., Intel L1 caches).

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ Mapping = **Placement Algorithm** for blocks between MM and cache.
- ✅ Three types: **Direct, Fully Associative, Set-Associative.**
- ✅ **Direct Mapping** uses formula: $i = j \bmod M$.
- ✅ **Set Number** for k-way: $s = j \bmod S$, where $S = M/k$.
- ✅ **Address bits** = Tag + Index + WordOffset — always verify they sum to total MM bits.
- ✅ **Tag Bits (Direct)** = $\log_2(N/M)$; **(Fully Assoc)** = $\log_2(N)$; **(Set-Assoc)** = $\log_2(N/S)$.
- ✅ **Comparators**: Direct = 1, Set-Assoc = $K$, Fully-Assoc = $M$.
- ✅ **Average Access Time**: $T_a = hT_c + (1-h)(T_c + T_m)$.
- ✅ **Higher $h$** ⇒ better performance; industrial target $h \approx 0.9$ to $0.98$.
- ✅ **Set-Associative is the industrial standard** for L1/L2/L3 caches.
- ✅ **Fully Associative** is impractical for large caches but used in TLBs.
- ✅ Draw the **address format partition** in every numerical answer (1–2 marks).
- ✅ Always state the **formula BEFORE substitution** in average access time problems.
- ✅ Watch for units — $T_a$ in **nanoseconds (ns)** unless otherwise specified.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Improving Cache Performance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT403 - COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE AND MICROCONTROLLER
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Improving Cache Performance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Cache Performance** in computer architecture is quantified through the **Average Memory Access Time (AMAT)**, a foundational metric defined by Hennessy and Patterson. The hierarchy of cache optimization techniques is classified along **five distinct axes**:

1. **Reducing the Hit Time**
2. **Increasing Cache Bandwidth**
3. **Reducing the Miss Penalty**
4. **Reducing the Miss Rate**
5. **Reducing Miss Penalty / Miss Rate via Parallelism (Prefetching)**

The canonical KTU 2024 formula governing all five optimization families is:

$$\text{AMAT} = \text{Hit Time} + \text{Miss Rate} \times \text{Miss Penalty}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition**: Cache performance improvement is the systematic application of microarchitectural, compiler-level, and hardware-level techniques designed to minimize the **AMAT** while respecting constraints on **silicon area**, **power dissipation**, and **clock cycle time (T)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Library Research" Model

Imagine you are a researcher working on a thesis inside a small study cabin (the **CPU registers**). The cabin is connected to a bookshelf in your room (**L1 Cache**), a bigger shelf in your house (**L2 Cache**), a neighborhood library (**L3 Cache / Main Memory**), and finally the **National Archives (Disk Storage**).

- **Hit Time** = The time taken to glance at the book already on your cabin desk (fastest).
- **Miss Penalty** = The time wasted when the book isn't on the shelf, forcing you to walk, drive, or travel to the archives.
- **Miss Rate** = The fraction of times you had to go outside the cabin.

Every cache optimization technique is essentially: *putting more books in the cabin*, *guessing the right shelf faster*, or *borrowing multiple books in a single trip*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Takeaway**: A student must know *which knob* (Hit Time, Miss Rate, Miss Penalty) every technique turns. Many students lose marks by claiming "larger cache reduces hit time" — it actually reduces **miss rate** but increases **hit time** due to longer wire delays.

## 1.3 The Five Optimization Axes — A Bird's-Eye View

| Axis | Primary Lever | Trade-off Introduced |
|------|---------------|----------------------|
| Reducing Hit Time | Smaller / simpler caches, Way prediction | May increase miss rate |
| Increasing Bandwidth | Pipelined, multibanked, non-blocking caches | Hardware complexity rises |
| Reducing Miss Penalty | Critical-word-first, early restart, merging writes | Control logic overhead |
| Reducing Miss Rate | Larger blocks, higher associativity, compiler hints | Longer hit time, tag overhead |
| Prefetching (Parallelism) | Hardware & compiler prefetch | Bandwidth pollution |

---

> **End of Section 1 — Proceed to Section 2 for Formulas and Deep Theory.**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Master Equation — AMAT Explained Linearly

The **Average Memory Access Time (AMAT)** is the average time the CPU spends, per memory reference, waiting for data. Every optimization strategy in KTU Module 4 targets exactly one term of:

$$\text{AMAT} = \text{Hit Time} + \text{Miss Rate} \times \text{Miss Penalty}$$

- **Hit Time ($T_{hit}$)**: Time to deliver a cached word to the CPU, including TLB + tag compare + data selection.
- **Miss Rate ($r_{miss}$)**: Fraction of memory references **NOT** found in the cache. Values for L1 caches are typically **1%–5%**.
- **Miss Penalty ($T_{penalty}$)**: Additional time to fetch the block from the next level (L2, L3, or main memory).

## 2.2 Extending AMAT to CPI — The Real CPU Equation

A more practical KTU-level expression translates AMAT into **Cycles Per Instruction (CPI)**:

$$\text{CPI}_{total} = \text{CPI}_{base} + \text{Memory Stalls per Instruction}$$

And:

$$\text{Memory Stalls per Instruction} = \text{Misses per Instruction} \times \text{Miss Penalty}$$

Expanded fully:

$$\text{CPU Time} = \text{IC} \times \left(\text{CPI}_{base} + \text{Mem. accesses/Inst.} \times \text{Miss Rate} \times \text{Miss Penalty}\right) \times T_{clock}$$

This is the equation every KTU 14-mark numerical is built upon.

## 2.3 Optimization Technique 1 — Reducing Hit Time

### 2.3.1 Small and Simple First-Level Caches
- Larger caches increase **signal propagation delay** across long word-lines and bit-lines.
- The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes that an L1 cache size is typically **16 KB–64 KB** with **direct-mapping** for minimal hit time.
- Direct-mapped L1 ≈ **1–2 clock cycles**; 8-way set-associative L1 ≈ **2–4 cycles**.

### 2.3.2 Way Prediction
- Predicts which *way* (set-associative block) will be hit **before** the tag comparison completes.
- Hits return in **~1 cycle**; misses fall back to full tag compare.
- Power savings: prediction gates tag-comparison logic on misses only.

## 2.4 Optimization Technique 2 — Increasing Cache Bandwidth

### 2.4.1 Pipelined Cache Access
- Splits tag-compare and data-array access into **pipelined stages**.
- Allows one new cache access per clock cycle, even if a single access takes multiple cycles.
- Treated as the standard in modern superscalar CPUs.

### 2.4.2 Multibanked Caches
- The cache SRAM is partitioned into **independent banks**, each serving a portion of the address space.
- Allows **multiple simultaneous accesses** to different banks in the same cycle.
- Critical for **L2 / L3 caches** serving multicore workloads.

### 2.4.3 Non-Blocking (Out-of-Order) Caches
- Permit the CPU to continue executing instructions while the cache handles a miss.
- **Hit-under-miss** (1 outstanding miss) and **Miss-under-miss** (multiple outstanding misses).
- Requires a **MSHR (Miss Status Handling Register)** buffer.

## 2.5 Optimization Technique 3 — Reducing Miss Penalty

### 2.5.1 Critical Word First (Early Restart)
- The CPU does **not** need the full block — only the *requested word*.
- On a miss, the cache fetches the requested **critical word first**, then fills the rest of the block.
- CPU resumes as soon as the critical word arrives.
- Known as **"load-word-first"** in some literature.

### 2.5.2 Merging Write Buffer
- Write-through policies use a write buffer between cache and main memory.
- If the buffer holds **writes to the same block**, they are **merged into one transaction**, saving memory bandwidth.
- The buffer must check for both **read-after-write** and **write-after-write** hazards.

### 2.5.3 Victim Cache
- A small, fully-associative auxiliary cache (typically **4–16 entries**) holding blocks discarded from L1 due to conflict misses.
- Acts as a "second chance" for evicted blocks.

## 2.6 Optimization Technique 4 — Reducing Miss Rate

### 2.6.1 Larger Block Size
- Exploits **spatial locality**. Bigger blocks → fewer compulsory misses.
- **Drawback**: Increases **miss penalty** (more data to transfer) and may increase **conflict / capacity misses**.

### 2.6.2 Higher Associativity
- $N$-way set-associative cache reduces **conflict misses** vs. direct-mapped.
- Rule of thumb: 8-way ≈ fully-associative for miss rate.

### 2.6.3 Compiler Optimizations (Software-Level)

| Technique | Description | Targets |
|-----------|-------------|---------|
| **Loop Interchange** | Reorder nested loops to access memory in stride-1 order | Capacity misses |
| **Loop Fusion / Blocking** | Combine or tile loops to fit working set into cache | Capacity misses |
| **Loop Unrolling** | Replicate loop body to amortize branch and address overhead | Control misses |

## 2.7 Optimization Technique 5 — Prefetching (Parallelism)

### 2.7.1 Hardware Prefetching
- A **stream buffer** monitors miss addresses and speculatively fetches the next consecutive blocks.
- Triggers on sequential or strided access patterns.

### 2.7.2 Compiler-Controlled Prefetching
- Compiler inserts **prefetch instructions** (`prefetcht0`, `prefetcht1`, `prefetchnta` on x86).
- Issues a non-binding load that fetches the block into cache *before* the program actually needs it.

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

These techniques are not academic abstractions — they underpin every modern CPU:

- **Intel/AMD**: Multibanked, non-blocking L1/L2 caches with hardware stream prefetchers.
- **ARM Cortex-A series**: Way-prediction in L1, victim caches, AMBA-CHI coherence.
- **Embedded (KTU-relevant)**: Compiler blocking is critical in DSP and image processing on ARM Cortex-M.

---

> **End of Section 2 — Section 3 continues with derivations.**
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivations (KTU 14-Mark Numerical Pattern)

### Problem 1: AMAT Computation
A CPU has an L1 cache with **hit time = 1 ns**, **miss rate = 5%**, and an L2 cache penalty of **20 ns**. L2 hit time is **5 ns** with a local miss rate of **30%**. Main memory access time is **80 ns**. Compute the **AMAT**.

#### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1**: Compute the AMAT for L1.
$$\text{AMAT}_{L1} = \text{Hit Time}_{L1} + \text{Miss Rate}_{L1} \times \text{Miss Penalty}_{L1}$$

The miss penalty of L1 is the time to access L2. Here, the access time to L2 (whether hit or miss) is given as **5 ns** for L2 hit, with a further penalty to main memory on L2 miss.

**Step 2**: Compute the AMAT for L2.
$$\text{AMAT}_{L2} = \text{Hit Time}_{L2} + \text{Miss Rate}_{L2} \times \text{Miss Penalty}_{L2}$$
$$\text{AMAT}_{L2} = 5 + (0.30 \times 80) = 5 + 24 = 29 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 3**: Substitute back into the L1 AMAT.
The miss penalty of L1 is the AMAT of L2.
$$\text{AMAT}_{L1} = 1 + (0.05 \times 29) = 1 + 1.45 = 2.45 \text{ ns}$$

**Result**: $\text{AMAT} = \mathbf{2.45 \text{ ns}}$

---

### Problem 2: CPI with Memory Stalls (Classic KTU Pattern)
A processor has **CPI = 1.0** without memory stalls. The **load/store instructions** make up **30%** of all instructions. The **L1 miss rate is 5%**, and the **miss penalty is 200 cycles**. Compute the **actual CPI**.

#### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1**: Memory accesses per instruction.
The fraction of instructions performing memory accesses is given as 30%. For each such instruction, we count one reference.
$$\text{Memory Accesses/Instruction} = 0.30$$

**Step 2**: Memory stalls per instruction.
$$\text{Stalls/Inst} = \text{Accesses/Inst} \times \text{Miss Rate} \times \text{Miss Penalty}$$
$$\text{Stalls/Inst} = 0.30 \times 0.05 \times 200 = 3.0$$

**Step 3**: Actual CPI.
$$\text{CPI}_{actual} = \text{CPI}_{base} + \text{Stalls/Inst}$$
$$\text{CPI}_{actual} = 1.0 + 3.0 = 4.0$$

**Result**: $\text{CPI} = \mathbf{4.0}$ — a **4× slowdown** purely due to memory hierarchy.

---

### Problem 3: CPU Time Comparison After Cache Upgrade
A program executes **I = 1,000,000,000 instructions**, base CPI is **1.5**, clock period **T = 0.5 ns**. Loads/stores = **40%**, L1 miss rate = **6%**, miss penalty = **150 cycles**. If we **double the L1 size** to halve the miss rate (to **3%**), how much **CPU time is saved**?

#### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1**: CPU time before upgrade.
$$\text{Stalls/Inst}_{old} = 0.40 \times 0.06 \times 150 = 3.6$$
$$\text{CPI}_{old} = 1.5 + 3.6 = 5.1$$
$$\text{CPU Time}_{old} = 10^9 \times 5.1 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-9} = 2.55 \text{ s}$$

**Step 2**: CPU time after upgrade.
$$\text{Stalls/Inst}_{new} = 0.40 \times 0.03 \times 150 = 1.8$$
$$\text{CPI}_{new} = 1.5 + 1.8 = 3.3$$
$$\text{CPU Time}_{new} = 10^9 \times 3.3 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-9} = 1.65 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3**: Time saved.
$$\Delta T = 2.55 - 1.65 = 0.90 \text{ s}$$
$$\text{Speedup} = \frac{2.55}{1.65} \approx 1.545$$

---

### Problem 4: Associativity Impact on Conflict Misses
A direct-mapped cache of size **8 KB** with **16-byte blocks** has **80 conflict misses per 1000 instructions**. Switching to **2-way set-associative** (same size) reduces conflict misses to **30 per 1000 instructions**. Total miss penalty = **100 cycles**. Compute the **% reduction in memory stall cycles**.

#### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1**: Original stalls per 1000 instructions.
$$\text{Stalls}_{old} = 80 \times 100 = 8000 \text{ cycles per 1000 inst.}$$

**Step 2**: New stalls per 1000 instructions.
$$\text{Stalls}_{new} = 30 \times 100 = 3000 \text{ cycles per 1000 inst.}$$

**Step 3**: Percent reduction.
$$\% \text{ Reduction} = \frac{8000 - 3000}{8000} \times 100 = 62.5\%$$

**Result**: Higher associativity gives a **62.5% reduction** in memory stall cycles.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Ten Optimizations at a Glance

| # | Technique | Knob Turned | AMAT Effect | Hardware Cost |
|---|-----------|-------------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | Larger block size | Miss Rate ↓ | Reduces compulsory misses | Increases miss penalty |
| 2 | Higher associativity | Miss Rate ↓ | Reduces conflict misses | Slower hit time |
| 3 | Victim cache | Miss Penalty ↓ | "Second chance" for evictions | Tag storage |
| 4 | Hardware prefetch | Miss Rate ↓ | Stream buffer lookahead | Bandwidth, pollution |
| 5 | Compiler prefetch | Miss Rate ↓ | Non-binding loads | ISA support needed |
| 6 | Small simple L1 | Hit Time ↓ | Faster cycle time | Limited capacity |
| 7 | Way prediction | Hit Time ↓ | Pseudo-associative in 1 cycle | Branch-style predictor |
| 8 | Pipelined cache | Bandwidth ↑ | One access per cycle | Pipeline hazards |
| 9 | Multibanked cache | Bandwidth ↑ | Parallel bank access | Bank conflict logic |
| 10 | Non-blocking cache | Miss Penalty ↓ | Hit-under-miss | MSHR hardware |
| 11 | Critical word first | Miss Penalty ↓ | Fast word delivery | Reordering logic |
| 12 | Merging write buffer | Miss Penalty ↓ | Coalesces writes | Buffer CAM |

---

> **End of Section 3 — Section 4 contains diagrams.**
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Memory Hierarchy Performance Stack

The following block diagram illustrates the **information flow** during a memory access and identifies where each optimization is applied:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CPU Core Issues Load or Store Instruction] --> B[Virtual Address from PC]
    B --> C[TLB Lookup]
    C --> D{Physical Address in TLB}
    D -- Hit --> E[L1 Cache Tag Array Compare]
    D -- Miss --> F[Page Table Walk in Main Memory]
    F --> E
    E --> G{Cache Hit or Miss}
    G -- Hit --> H[Select Data from Way]
    G -- Miss --> I[L2 Cache Lookup]
    I --> J{L2 Hit or Miss}
    J -- Hit --> K[Fill L1 Block Critical Word First]
    J -- Miss --> L[L3 Cache Lookup]
    L --> M{L3 Hit or Miss}
    M -- Hit --> N[Fill L2 then L1]
    M -- Miss --> O[Main Memory Access]
    O --> P[Return Block to L3 L2 L1]
    H --> Q[Data Returned to CPU Register File]
    K --> Q
    N --> Q
    P --> Q

    classDef opt fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px
    classDef cpu fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#EF6C00,stroke-width:2px
    classDef mem fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828,stroke-width:2px

    class A,Q cpu
    class E,G,H,K opt
    class I,J,L,M,N,O,P mem
```

## 4.2 AMAT Optimization Levers — Concept Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    AMAT[Average Memory Access Time AMAT] --> HT[Hit Time T hit]
    AMAT --> MR[Miss Rate r miss]
    AMAT --> MP[Miss Penalty P miss]

    HT --> L1A[Smaller L1]
    HT --> L1B[Way Prediction]
    HT --> L1C[Pipelined Cache]

    MR --> L2A[Larger Block Size]
    MR --> L2B[Higher Associativity]
    MR --> L2C[Compiler Blocking Fusion]
    MR --> L2D[Hardware Prefetch]

    MP --> L3A[Critical Word First]
    MP --> L3B[Non Blocking Cache]
    MP --> L3C[Merging Write Buffer]
    MP --> L3D[Victim Cache]

    classDef lever fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,stroke-width:2px
    class AMAT,HT,MR,MP lever
    class L1A,L1B,L1C,L2A,L2B,L2C,L2D,L3A,L3B,L3C,L3D lever
```

## 4.3 Non-Blocking Cache — MSHR Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[CPU Issues Memory Access 1] --> B0[Miss Detected]
    B0 --> C0[Allocate MSHR Entry 1]
    C0 --> D0[Issue Fill Request to L2]
    D0 --> E0[CPU Continues Issue Access 2]
    E0 --> F0{Access 2 Hit or Miss}
    F0 -- Hit --> G0[Return Data Immediately]
    F0 -- Miss --> H0[Allocate MSHR Entry 2]
    H0 --> I0[Issue Fill Request to L2]
    I0 --> J0[Wait for Both Outstanding Misses]
    J0 --> K0[Fill Returns Processed in Order]

    classDef hit fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:1px
    classDef miss fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#C62828,stroke-width:1px
    classDef buf fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#EF6C00,stroke-width:1px

    class G0 hit
    class B0,C0,H0 miss
    class C0,H0,J0 buf
```

---

> **End of Section 4 — Section 5 follows with KTU exam questions.**
