- Plus valid/invalid bit + protection bits = ~3 bits
- Total PTE bits ≈ $12 + 3 = 15$ bits, rounded up to **16 bits = 2 bytes**.

**Page table size** = $2^{20} \times 2 \text{ bytes} = 2 \text{ MB}$.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| System Type | Page Table Size | Access Time | Memory Overhead | Best Use Case |
|-------------|-----------------|-------------|-----------------|---------------|
| **Single-Level Paging** | $\text{Huge} = 2^{(n-o)} \times \text{PTE}$ | $2M$ | High | Small address spaces |
| **Two-Level Paging** | $2^{n_1} \times \text{PTE} + (\text{used inner tables})$ | $3M$ | Moderate | General OS (32-bit) |
| **Inverted Page Table** | $\text{Physical frames} \times \text{PTE}$ | $2M$ + search | Low | Large address spaces (64-bit) |
| **TLB-Enhanced** | Same + TLB cache | $(2-h)M + T$ | Lowest (with TLB) | Modern CPUs |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Address Translation Block-Level Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CPU["CPU Generates Logical Address"]
    Split["Split Address into Page Number p and Offset d"]
    TLB{"TLB Lookup for p"}
    TLBHit["TLB HIT: Obtain Frame Number f"]
    TLBMiss["TLB MISS: Access Page Table in Memory"]
    PageTable["Page Table Lookup using p"]
    GetF["Retrieve Frame Number f from PTE"]
    UpdateTLB["Update TLB with p to f mapping"]
    ValidCheck{"Protection and Valid Bit Check"}
    PA["Construct Physical Address using f and d"]
    MemAccess["Access Physical Memory at PA"]
    PageFault["Raise Page Fault Trap to OS"]

    CPU --> Split
    Split --> TLB
    TLB -->|Hit| TLBHit
    TLB -->|Miss| TLBMiss
    TLBMiss --> PageTable
    PageTable --> GetF
    GetF --> UpdateTLB
    TLBHit --> ValidCheck
    UpdateTLB --> ValidCheck
    ValidCheck -->|Valid and Permitted| PA
    ValidCheck -->|Invalid or Denied| PageFault
    PA --> MemAccess
```

### 4.2 Two-Level Paging Translation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    LA["32-bit Logical Address"]
    Field1["p1: 10 bits - Outer Page Directory Index"]
    Field2["p2: 10 bits - Inner Page Table Index"]
    Field3["d: 12 bits - Page Offset"]
    PT1["Outer Page Directory Base Register CR3 in x86"]
    Lookup1["Access PT1 entry at index p1"]
    PT2Addr["Compute Inner Page Table Base Address"]
    Lookup2["Access Inner PT entry at index p2"]
    FrameNum["Obtain Frame Number f from PTE"]
    BuildPA["Physical Address equals f concatenated with d"]
    PhysMem["Access Physical Memory at Physical Address"]

    LA --> Field1
    LA --> Field2
    LA --> Field3
    Field1 --> PT1
    PT1 --> Lookup1
    Lookup1 --> PT2Addr
    Field2 --> Lookup2
    PT2Addr --> Lookup2
    Lookup2 --> FrameNum
    FrameNum --> BuildPA
    Field3 --> BuildPA
    BuildPA --> PhysMem
```

### 4.3 Address Translation Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph USER["User Program Space"]
        Prog["Program Issues Logical Address LA"]
    end

    subgraph MMU["Memory Management Unit MMU Hardware"]
        Splitter["Address Splitter: separates p and d"]
        TLBUnit["TLB Associative Cache"]
        PTReloc["Page Table Base Register PTBR"]
        Combiner["Address Combiner: merges f and d"]
        Protection["Protection Logic: checks R W X Valid bits"]
    end

    subgraph OS["Operating System Kernel"]
        PTMem["Page Tables stored in Main Memory"]
        FaultHandler["Page Fault Handler"]
        DiskSwap["Swap Space on Secondary Storage"]
    end

    subgraph RAM["Physical Main Memory"]
        Frames["Frames holding actual data"]
    end

    Prog --> Splitter
    Splitter --> TLBUnit
    TLBUnit -->|Miss| PTReloc
    PTReloc --> PTMem
    PTMem --> Combiner
    TLBUnit -->|Hit| Combiner
    Combiner --> Protection
    Protection -->|Allowed| Frames
    Protection -->|Denied| FaultHandler
    PTMem -->|Page not resident| FaultHandler
    FaultHandler --> DiskSwap
    DiskSwap --> Frames
```

### 4.4 Page Table Entry (PTE) Bit Layout

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PTE["Page Table Entry Structure"]
    Bit1["Frame Number f: highest-order bits"]
    Bit2["Valid or Present Bit V: 1 bit"]
    Bit3["Dirty Bit D: 1 bit"]
    Bit4["Reference Bit A: 1 bit"]
    Bit5["Protection Bits R W X: 3 bits"]
    Bit6["Caching Disabled Bit: 1 bit"]
    Bit7["User Supervisor Bit U S: 1 bit"]

    PTE --> Bit1
    PTE --> Bit2
    PTE --> Bit3
    PTE --> Bit4
    PTE --> Bit5
    PTE --> Bit6
    PTE --> Bit7
```

### 4.5 Block Diagram: Multi-Level Page Table Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    OuterPT["Outer Page Directory: 2 to power n1 entries each 4 bytes"]
    InnerPT1["Inner Page Table 1: 2 to power n2 entries"]
    InnerPT2["Inner Page Table 2: 2 to power n2 entries"]
    InnerPTk["Inner Page Table k: 2 to power n2 entries"]
    FrameSet["Physical Memory Frames: Frame 0, Frame 1, Frame f"]

    OuterPT --> InnerPT1
    OuterPT --> InnerPT2
    OuterPT --> InnerPTk
    InnerPT1 --> FrameSet
    InnerPT2 --> FrameSet
    InnerPTk --> FrameSet
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Define address translation. Differentiate between logical and physical addresses with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Address translation is the process of converting a logical (virtual) address generated by the CPU into a physical address in main memory, performed by the Memory Management Unit (MMU) using a page table.

**Difference (2 marks):**

| Parameter | Logical Address | Physical Address |
|-----------|-----------------|------------------|
| Generated by | CPU / Program | MMU after translation |
| Visibility | Visible to process | Visible only to memory hardware |
| Range | $0$ to $2^n - 1$ | $0$ to $(\text{RAM size}) - 1$ |
| Example | `0x00001000` (virtual) | `0x1A3F4000` (actual RAM location) |

**Example:** A program accessing array element at virtual address `0x00004004` might actually read from physical address `0x12345004` after MMU translation.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**Explain the role of the Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB) in address translation. Why is it necessary?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Role (2 marks):** The TLB is a small, high-speed **associative cache** that stores recently used **page number to frame number** mappings. When the MMU receives a logical page number, it first checks the TLB. On a **TLB hit**, the frame number is obtained in 1 cycle, avoiding the slower main memory access for the page table.

**Necessity (1 mark):** Without a TLB, every memory access would require **two memory references** (one for page table, one for actual data), doubling the effective access time. The TLB exploits **temporal and spatial locality** of references to make translation nearly free in practice.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)
*Internal Choice: Answer Question A OR Question B*

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** Consider a system with a 32-bit logical address space and a page size of **4 KB**. The physical memory has a size of **16 MB**.

**(i)** Compute the number of pages and the number of frames. *(3 marks)*
**(ii)** What is the size of the page table? *(2 marks)*
**(iii)** Show the structure of the logical address and physical address. *(2 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the two-level paging scheme with a neat diagram. Why is two-level paging preferred over single-level paging in modern operating systems? *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution

**(a)(i) Number of Pages and Frames (3 marks):**

- Logical address space = $2^{32}$ bytes
- Page size = $4 \text{ KB} = 2^{12}$ bytes
- Number of pages = $\dfrac{2^{32}}{2^{12}} = 2^{20}$ pages

- Physical memory = $16 \text{ MB} = 2^{24}$ bytes
- Number of frames = $\dfrac{2^{24}}{2^{12}} = 2^{12}$ frames

**(a)(ii) Page Table Size (2 marks):**

Assuming each Page Table Entry (PTE) is **4 bytes** (32 bits), page table size:

$$ \text{Page Table Size} = 2^{20} \times 4 \text{ bytes} = 4 \text{ MB} $$

**(a)(iii) Address Structure (2 marks):**

| Logical Address | Bits | Physical Address | Bits |
|-----------------|------|------------------|------|
| Page Number $p$ | $32 - 12 = 20$ bits | Frame Number $f$ | $24 - 12 = 12$ bits |
| Offset $d$ | $12$ bits | Offset $d$ | $12$ bits |

$$ \text{Logical Address} = \underbrace{p}_{20 \text{ bits}} \mid \underbrace{d}_{12 \text{ bits}} $$

$$ \text{Physical Address} = \underbrace{f}_{12 \text{ bits}} \mid \underbrace{d}_{12 \text{ bits}} $$

**(b) Two-Level Paging (7 marks):**

**Explanation (4 marks):**

In two-level paging, the outer level (page directory) splits the page number $p$ into two parts: $p_1$ (index into outer page table) and $p_2$ (index into inner page table).

For our 32-bit system with 4 KB pages and 4-byte PTEs:
- Offset $d = 12$ bits
- Each page table fits in one page: $2^{12} / 2^{2} = 2^{10}$ entries
- So $p_2 = 10$ bits, and remaining $p_1 = 20 - 10 = 10$ bits

$$ \text{Logical Address} = \underbrace{p_1}_{10} \mid \underbrace{p_2}_{10} \mid \underbrace{d}_{12} $$

**Translation steps:**
1. Use $p_1$ to index into the **Outer Page Directory** stored at the Page Table Base Register (PTBR).
2. The outer entry gives the base address of the **Inner Page Table**.
3. Use $p_2$ to index into this inner table.
4. The inner entry gives the frame number $f$.
5. Concatenate $f$ with $d$ to form the physical address.

**Diagram (refer to Section 4.2 above) (1 mark)**

**Advantages over Single-Level (2 marks):**

1. **Memory Efficiency:** Only the outer page table (4 KB) must be always resident. Inner tables are allocated only when needed, reducing wasted memory from 4 MB (single-level worst case) to a few KB.
2. **Scalability:** Enables paging for large address spaces (e.g., 64-bit) by extending to 3 or 4 levels.
3. **Faster Context Switch:** Only the PTBR needs to be saved/restored, not the entire page table.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Question, Module 4]**

**(a)** A system uses paging with TLB access time of **20 ns** and main memory access time of **100 ns**. The TLB hit ratio is **80%**.

**(i)** Compute the Effective Access Time (EAT). *(3 marks)*
**(ii)** What would be the EAT if the TLB hit ratio drops to 60%? Comment. *(2 marks)*
**(iii)** Calculate the percentage increase in EAT. *(2 marks)*

**(b)** Describe the Inverted Page Table mechanism. How does it overcome the limitations of traditional page tables? *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution

**(a)(i) EAT with 80% Hit Ratio (3 marks):**

Given:
- TLB access time $T = 20$ ns
- Memory access time $M = 100$ ns
- TLB hit ratio $h = 0.8$

Using the formula:

$$ EAT = h(T + M) + (1 - h)(T + 2M) $$

$$ EAT = 0.8(20 + 100) + 0.2(20 + 200) $$

$$ EAT = 0.8 \times 120 + 0.2 \times 220 $$

$$ EAT = 96 + 44 = 140 \text{ ns} $$

**(a)(ii) EAT with 60% Hit Ratio (2 marks):**

$$ EAT = 0.6 \times 120 + 0.4 \times 220 $$

$$ EAT = 72 + 88 = 160 \text{ ns} $$

**Comment:** As TLB hit ratio decreases, the EAT increases because more memory references require expensive page table lookups in main memory.

**(a)(iii) Percentage Increase (2 marks):**

$$ \text{Increase} = \dfrac{160 - 140}{140} \times 100\% = \dfrac{20}{140} \times 100\% \approx 14.29\% $$

**(b) Inverted Page Table (7 marks):**

**Concept (3 marks):**
In a traditional page table, there is **one entry per logical page** of the process. In contrast, an **Inverted Page Table (IPT)** has **one entry per physical frame** in the entire system. Each entry stores:
- The process ID (PID) owning the frame
- The logical page number within that process
- Control bits (protection, dirty, reference)

**Translation Process (2 marks):**
To translate logical address $(PID, p, d)$:
1. Compute a hash of $(PID, p)$ to get a candidate index.
2. Search the IPT (or use associative lookup) for a matching $(PID, p)$ entry.
3. If found, the index in the IPT is the physical frame number $f$.
4. Concatenate $f$ with $d$ to form the physical address.
5. On miss, a page fault is raised.

**Advantages over Traditional Page Tables (2 marks):**

1. **Fixed Memory Size:** IPT size = number of physical frames, independent of logical address space. Critical for 64-bit systems where $2^{64}$ entries would be impossible.
2. **No Wasted Memory:** No unused entries for unallocated virtual pages.
3. **Shared Memory Implementation:** Same physical frame can be mapped for multiple PIDs efficiently.

**Disadvantage:** Slower translation due to search/hash; typically requires hardware support (associative search).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Confusing Page Number with Frame Number:** Students often interchange them. Page numbers index the page table; frame numbers form part of the physical address.
> 2. **Wrong Page Table Size:** Many students compute size of one inner table instead of total memory consumed. Read carefully — "size of one inner page table" vs "total page table memory" are different questions.
> 3. **EAT Formula Mistakes:** Always include TLB access time $T$ in BOTH hit and miss paths. A common error is using $M$ only on hit.
> 4. **Offset Bits Calculation:** Always compute $o = \log_2(\text{Page Size})$. Skipping this leads to wrong page number calculations.
> 5. **Inverted Page Table Confusion:** Students often describe it as having one entry per process. It has one entry per **physical frame**, indexed by frame number.
> 6. **Forgetting Units:** Always write EAT in nanoseconds (ns) or microseconds (μs) as given in the problem.
> 7. **Protection Bits:** Examiners award 1 mark if you mention that valid/protection bits are checked during translation.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Address Translation**

- **Logical Address** = CPU-generated; **Physical Address** = RAM location. The MMU translates between them.
- **Paging divides** memory into fixed-size **pages (logical)** and **frames (physical)** of equal size.
- **Logical Address Structure**: `(Page Number p) | (Offset d)` where offset bits $o = \log_2(\text{Page Size})$.
- **Physical Address** = $(f \times \text{Frame Size}) + d$ where $f$ is obtained from the page table.
- **Page Table Entry (PTE)** contains: frame number, valid bit, dirty bit, reference bit, protection bits (R/W/X).
- **Multi-Level Paging** reduces memory wastage: 2-level for 32-bit, 3 or 4-level for 64-bit systems.
- **TLB (Translation Lookaside Buffer)** is a hardware cache for translations; exploits locality of reference.
- **Effective Access Time (with TLB)**: $EAT = h(T + M) + (1-h)(T + 2M) = T + (2-h)M$.
- **Inverted Page Table**: One entry per **physical frame** (not per logical page); scales well for large address spaces.
- **Page Fault** occurs when the valid bit = 0; OS handles by swapping in the page from disk.
- **Segmentation** uses variable-sized segments with logical name + offset; pure segmentation causes external fragmentation.
- **Segmented Paging** combines both: segment number → page table → frame + offset.
- **KTU Favorite Question Types:** EAT calculation, page table size calculation, multi-level paging address split, TLB hit ratio impact analysis.
- **Memory Hierarchy Context:** TLB sits between CPU registers and L1 cache in the address translation pipeline.
- **Real-world Systems:** x86 uses CR3 register for page directory base; ARM uses TTBR0/TTBR1; MIPS uses EntryHi/EntryLo registers.
- **Numbers to Memorize:** 4 KB page = $2^{12}$, 32-bit address = $2^{32}$, 64-bit address = $2^{64}$.
- **Typical TLB:** 64–512 entries, fully associative, with LRU replacement.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 4 Notes — Address Translation**
> These notes are tuned to KTU 2024 Scheme examination patterns. Practice the EAT and page table size problems until the formulas become second nature. Good luck with your exams!
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Input/Output Organization - Introduction
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT403 - COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE AND MICROCONTROLLER
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Input/Output Organization - Introduction

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Input/Output (I/O) Organization** is the subsystem of a computer architecture responsible for managing, controlling, and orchestrating the data transfer between the **Central Processing Unit (CPU) / Memory** and the **external peripheral devices** (such as keyboards, displays, disk drives, sensors, and network interfaces).

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the I/O organization encompasses the **hardware interfaces, control logic, data transfer protocols, and software mechanisms** that enable the processor to communicate efficiently and reliably with the outside world. It is a critical component of the Von Neumann architecture that bridges the deterministic internal computation world with the asynchronous, variable-speed external device world.

The three principal hardware entities involved in this organization are:
1. **CPU / Microprocessor** — The master that initiates all I/O operations.
2. **I/O Module / Controller** — The dedicated hardware intermediary (e.g., UART, DMA controller, disk controller).
3. **Peripheral Devices** — The actual external equipment that produces or consumes data.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hotel Concierge Analogy:** Imagine a luxury hotel. The **General Manager** is the CPU, the **Front Office Records Room** is the main memory, and the **guests/outside service vendors** are the I/O devices. The guests arrive and leave at unpredictable times, speak different languages, and have varying needs. The **Concierge Desk** acts as the I/O module — it translates requests, handles logistics, schedules service, and ensures the General Manager is not personally disturbed for every trivial request. The concierge also maintains a "logbook" of pending requests, similar to an I/O buffer.

Without the concierge (I/O module), the manager would be overwhelmed with direct, chaotic, slow interactions. The I/O organization, therefore, exists to:
- **Decouple** the high-speed processor from slow, asynchronous devices.
- **Standardize** the communication interface.
- **Multiplex** multiple devices over shared resources (buses).

## 1.3 Why I/O Organization is Critical

> [!NOTE]
> **The I/O Performance Paradox:** A modern CPU can execute billions of instructions per second, but a mechanical hard disk takes milliseconds to access data. Without efficient I/O organization, the CPU spends **>90% of its time idle**, waiting for I/O. This is known as the **I/O Wall** problem in computer architecture.

The I/O subsystem directly impacts:
- **System Throughput** — measured in transactions per second.
- **Response Time / Latency** — measured in **milliseconds (ms)** or **microseconds (μs)**.
- **CPU Utilization** — the percentage of time the CPU is doing useful work.
- **Power Efficiency** — especially critical in mobile and embedded KTU microcontroller applications.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standard metrics are universally used to evaluate any I/O organization:

- **Bandwidth / Data Transfer Rate:** measured in **bytes per second (B/s)**, **KB/s**, **MB/s**, or **GB/s**.
- **Latency:** measured in **microseconds (μs)** or **milliseconds (ms)**.
- **I/O Operations Per Second (IOPS):** critical for storage evaluation.
- **Bus Width:** measured in **bits** (typically 8, 16, 32, or 64).
- **Bus Clock Frequency:** measured in **Hertz (Hz)**, typically **MHz** or **GHz**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Major Functions of an I/O Module

The I/O module (also called I/O controller or I/O processor) performs six essential functions that KTU examiners frequently test:

1. **Control & Timing:** Coordinates the flow of data between the CPU and peripherals using system buses.
2. **CPU Communication:** Receives commands from the CPU (e.g., READ, WRITE, SEEK) and returns status information.
3. **Device Communication:** Issues device-specific control signals to peripherals.
4. **Data Buffering:** Temporarily holds data because the speed mismatch between CPU/memory and I/O devices can be several orders of magnitude.
5. **Data Formatting & Translation:** Converts data between the parallel internal bus format and the serial/parallel external format required by devices.
6. **Error Detection & Correction:** Detects and (sometimes) corrects errors using parity bits, checksums, or CRC codes.

## 2.2 The Three Principal I/O Techniques (Preview)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus requires students to master three fundamental approaches to I/O. This introduction lays the foundation; detailed derivations follow in subsequent topics.

| Technique | CPU Involvement | Speed | Complexity | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Programmed I/O** | CPU actively polls/waits | Slowest | Lowest | Simple microcontrollers |
| **Interrupt-Driven I/O** | CPU interrupted on completion | Medium | Medium | Keyboards, network cards |
| **Direct Memory Access (DMA)** | CPU not involved in data transfer | Fastest | Highest | Disk drives, high-speed ADC |

The detailed mechanisms of these three techniques form the core of subsequent topics in this module.

## 2.3 I/O vs. Memory Operations

A subtle but KTU-favorite conceptual point is distinguishing I/O and memory operations at the bus level:

- **Memory-mapped I/O:** Devices are addressed as if they were memory locations. Uses the same address bus and instructions.
- **Isolated (Port-mapped) I/O:** Uses a separate I/O address space, requiring special instructions like `IN` and `OUT` (in x86) or specific memory regions (in ARM/microcontrollers).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **High-Yield Formulas** for I/O performance calculations. KTU 2024 Scheme questions on this topic frequently require numerical evaluation of these formulas.

| # | Formula / Quantity | Equation | Units | Description |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Maximum Data Transfer Rate (synchronous) | $R_{max} = W \times f_{bus}$ | bytes/second | $W$ = bus width (bytes), $f_{bus}$ = bus frequency (Hz) |
| 2 | Transfer Time for $N$ bytes | $T_{transfer} = \dfrac{N}{R}$ | seconds | $N$ = data size, $R$ = effective transfer rate |
| 3 | Throughput (Little's Law) | $Throughput = \dfrac{N}{T_{service} + T_{queue}}$ | ops/second | $T_{queue}$ = waiting time |
| 4 | CPU Utilization (Amdahl-like) | $U_{CPU} = 1 - f_{IO}$ | dimensionless | $f_{IO}$ = fraction of time spent in I/O |
| 5 | I/O Bound Speedup Limit | $S_{max} = \dfrac{1}{f_{IO}}$ | dimensionless | Maximum possible speedup from I/O optimization |
| 6 | Effective Bandwidth | $B_{eff} = \dfrac{D_{useful}}{T_{total}}$ | bytes/second | Useful data over total elapsed time |
| 7 | Polling Overhead | $T_{poll} = N_{polls} \times T_{cycle}$ | seconds | Time wasted in busy-wait polling loops |
| 8 | Interrupt Latency | $T_{latency} = T_{recog} + T_{save} + T_{ISR}$ | seconds | Time from device ready to ISR execution |
| 9 | DMA Transfer Time | $T_{DMA} = T_{setup} + \dfrac{N}{R_{DMA}}$ | seconds | Setup + actual data movement time |
| 10 | Block Transfer Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{T_{transfer}}{T_{transfer} + T_{overhead}}$ | dimensionless | Ratio of useful to total transfer time |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The I/O organization principles taught in this module directly map to:
- **Embedded Systems (ARM Cortex-M microcontrollers):** GPIO, UART, SPI, I²C peripheral interfacing.
- **Industrial Automation:** PLC I/O modules, sensor data acquisition.
- **Automotive Electronics:** CAN bus, LIN bus for in-vehicle networking.
- **IoT Devices:** Low-power I/O for battery-operated sensors.
- **High-Performance Computing:** NVMe, InfiniBand, RDMA for data centers.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Industry Connection:** Kerala's emerging electronics manufacturing cluster (Keltron, Tata Elxsi, UST) heavily recruits engineers skilled in microcontroller I/O design. The PCMRT403 course directly supports this skill path.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Maximum Synchronous Data Transfer Rate

This is a **fundamental derivation** that KTU frequently asks in 14-mark questions.

**Given:**
- Bus width $W$ in bytes
- Bus clock frequency $f_{bus}$ in Hz (cycles per second)
- One data transfer per clock cycle (synchronous assumption)

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Data per cycle} &= W \text{ bytes} \\
\text{Cycles per second} &= f_{bus} \text{ Hz} \\
\therefore R_{max} &= \text{(Data per cycle)} \times \text{(Cycles per second)} \\
R_{max} &= W \times f_{bus} \text{ bytes/second}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion to MB/s** (where 1 MB = $2^{20}$ bytes = 1,048,576 bytes):
$$
R_{max} = \dfrac{W \times f_{bus}}{2^{20}} \text{ MB/s}
$$

**Numerical Example (KTU-style):**
A 32-bit bus operating at 100 MHz — calculate the maximum transfer rate.

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= 32 \text{ bits} = 4 \text{ bytes} \\
f_{bus} &= 100 \text{ MHz} = 100 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz} \\
R_{max} &= 4 \times 100 \times 10^6 \\
R_{max} &= 400 \times 10^6 \text{ bytes/s} \\
R_{max} &= 400 \text{ MB/s (using } 10^6 \text{)} \\
R_{max} &\approx 381.47 \text{ MB/s (using } 2^{20} \text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always state explicitly whether you are using the **SI convention** (1 MB = $10^6$ bytes) or the **binary convention** (1 MiB = $2^{20}$ bytes). KTU typically expects the **SI convention** unless specifically stated.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Transfer Time for a Data Block

**Given:** File size $N$ bytes, bus transfer rate $R$ bytes/second.

**Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \dfrac{N}{T} \\
\Rightarrow T &= \dfrac{N}{R}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Example:** Transfer a 2 MB file over a USB 2.0 bus rated at 60 MB/s (effective).

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= 2 \text{ MB} = 2 \times 10^6 \text{ bytes} \\
R &= 60 \times 10^6 \text{ bytes/s} \\
T &= \dfrac{2 \times 10^6}{60 \times 10^6} = \dfrac{1}{30} \\
T &\approx 0.0333 \text{ seconds} = 33.33 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Polling Overhead in Programmed I/O

This is a **high-frequency KTU question** in Part A and Part B.

**Scenario:** The CPU polls an I/O device every $T_{cycle}$ microseconds. The device becomes ready after $N_{polls}$ polls.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{poll} &= N_{polls} \times T_{cycle} \\
\text{CPU Utilization on I/O} &= \dfrac{T_{poll}}{T_{total}} \times 100\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Example:** A CPU with 1 μs cycle time polls a slow device. The device takes 5 ms to become ready.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{device} &= 5 \text{ ms} = 5000 \text{ μs} \\
N_{polls} &= \dfrac{5000}{1} = 5000 \text{ polls} \\
T_{poll\ wasted} &= 5000 \times 1 \text{ μs} = 5000 \text{ μs} = 5 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget that **polling time IS wasted CPU time** because the CPU cannot execute other useful instructions while busy-waiting. This is why interrupt-driven I/O exists.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: CPU Utilization in I/O-Bound Systems

**Amdahl-style derivation** for a system that spends fraction $f_{IO}$ of time on I/O:

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_{CPU} &= 1 - f_{IO} \\
\text{If } f_{IO} &= 0.20 \text{ (20% time in I/O):} \\
U_{CPU} &= 1 - 0.20 = 0.80 = 80\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Maximum possible speedup** from eliminating ALL I/O overhead:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{max} &= \dfrac{1}{1 - f_{IO}} = \dfrac{1}{f_{compute}} \\
\text{With } f_{IO} &= 0.20: \\
S_{max} &= \dfrac{1}{0.80} = 1.25 \times \text{ speedup}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: I/O Technique Comparison

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme examiners often present a scenario and ask students to **choose and justify** an I/O technique. Use this matrix as a decision-making tool.

| Criterion | Programmed I/O | Interrupt-Driven I/O | DMA |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CPU Cycles Used** | Highest (busy-wait) | Medium (only on interrupt) | Lowest (only setup/teardown) |
| **Hardware Complexity** | Lowest | Medium (interrupt controller) | Highest (DMA controller) |
| **Software Complexity** | Lowest | Medium (ISR design) | Medium (driver design) |
| **Data Throughput** | Lowest | Medium | Highest |
| **Suitable Data Size** | Small (bytes) | Medium (packets) | Large (blocks) |
| **Real-Time Guarantee** | Poor (latency varies) | Good (bounded) | Excellent (deterministic) |
| **Power Consumption** | High (CPU always active) | Medium | Low (CPU sleeps) |
| **Best Application** | Simple GPIO, switches | Keyboard, mouse, UART | Disk, video, network buffers |

## 3.6 Derivation 5: DMA vs CPU-Driven Transfer Time

**Scenario:** Transfer a 4 KB block using CPU-driven I/O (1 byte per instruction, 4 cycles/instruction, 100 MHz CPU) vs DMA (1 byte per cycle, 100 MHz DMA bus).

**CPU-Driven (Programmed I/O) Time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cycles per byte} &= 4 \\
\text{Time per byte} &= \dfrac{4}{100 \times 10^6} = 40 \text{ ns} \\
T_{CPU} &= 4096 \times 40 \text{ ns} = 163,840 \text{ ns} \approx 163.84 \text{ μs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**DMA Time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cycles per byte} &= 1 \\
\text{Time per byte} &= \dfrac{1}{100 \times 10^6} = 10 \text{ ns} \\
T_{DMA\ raw} &= 4096 \times 10 \text{ ns} = 40,960 \text{ ns} = 40.96 \text{ μs} \\
T_{DMA\ total} &= T_{setup} + T_{DMA\ raw} = 1 \text{ μs} + 40.96 \text{ μs} = 41.96 \text{ μs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Speedup achieved:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Speedup} &= \dfrac{T_{CPU}}{T_{DMA}} = \dfrac{163.84}{41.96} \approx 3.90 \times
\end{aligned}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Generic Computer System with I/O Organization

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates how the CPU, memory, and I/O devices are interconnected through a **system bus** and **I/O modules**.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CPU["CPU and Registers"]
    MEM["Main Memory RAM ROM"]
    BUS["System Bus Address Data Control"]
    IOM["I O Module Controller"]
    KBD["Keyboard Input"]
    DSP["Display Output"]
    DSK["Disk Drive Storage"]
    NET["Network Interface"]
    PRT["Printer Output"]

    CPU <--> BUS
    MEM <--> BUS
    BUS <--> IOM
    IOM --> KBD
    IOM --> DSP
    IOM --> DSK
    IOM --> NET
    IOM --> PRT
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**
- The **CPU and Main Memory** communicate via the **System Bus** (which has three sub-buses: address, data, control).
- The **I/O Module** sits as a gateway between the system bus and all peripherals.
- Multiple peripheral devices (**Keyboard, Display, Disk, Network, Printer**) are multiplexed through a single I/O module.
- This topology is called a **single-bus architecture with I/O module isolation**.

## 4.2 Functional Flow: I/O Operation Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["Step 1 CPU issues I O command via bus"]
    S2["Step 2 I O module decodes address"]
    S3["Step 3 I O module identifies target device"]
    S4["Step 4 Device prepares data or status"]
    S5["Step 5 Data transfer via buffer"]
    S6["Step 6 Status and completion sent to CPU"]

    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3
    S3 --> S4
    S4 --> S5
    S5 --> S6
```

**Lifecycle Explanation:**
- **Step 1:** The CPU places the device address on the address bus and asserts a control signal (READ/WRITE).
- **Step 2:** The I/O module decodes the address to identify which peripheral is targeted.
- **Step 3:** The I/O module activates the corresponding device's chip-select line.
- **Step 4:** The device prepares data (for READ) or accepts data (for WRITE).
- **Step 5:** Data moves through the I/O module's buffer register.
- **Step 6:** The I/O module sets status flags and may trigger an interrupt to inform the CPU.

## 4.3 Block Architecture: Internal Structure of an I/O Module

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    INTF["Interface to System Bus"]
    CTL["Control Logic and Registers"]
    BUF["Data Buffer Register"]
    CMD["Command Register"]
    STAT["Status Register"]
    DINTF["Interface to Peripherals"]

    INTF <--> CTL
    CTL <--> BUF
    CTL <--> CMD
    CTL <--> STAT
    CTL --> DINTF
```

**Component Roles:**
- **Interface to System Bus:** Handles address decoding and bus arbitration with the CPU.
- **Control Logic:** The "brain" of the I/O module — decodes commands, sequences operations.
- **Data Buffer Register:** Temporarily holds data being transferred (decouples speed mismatch).
- **Command Register:** Stores the most recent command from the CPU (READ, WRITE, SEEK, etc.).
- **Status Register:** Holds device status flags (BUSY, READY, ERROR).
- **Interface to Peripherals:** Generates device-specific control signals (chip select, strobe, acknowledge).

## 4.4 Comparison Flow: Programmed I/O vs Interrupt I/O vs DMA

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PIO["Programmed I O CPU polls in tight loop wastes cycles"]
    INT["Interrupt Driven I O CPU notified only when device ready"]
    DMA["DMA Controller offloads data movement CPU free"]

    PIO -->|Slower but simple| OUT1["Best for low speed devices"]
    INT -->|Balanced| OUT2["Best for event driven devices"]
    DMA -->|Fastest| OUT3["Best for block transfers"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]`
**Define I/O organization. List any four functions of an I/O module.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 for definition, 2 for functions):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Input/Output (I/O) organization is the subsystem of a computer that manages data transfer between the CPU/memory and external peripheral devices through dedicated I/O modules, buses, and control logic.

**Four Functions (2 Marks — 0.5 each):**
1. **Control and Timing:** Coordinates data flow between CPU and peripherals.
2. **Data Buffering:** Temporarily holds data to bridge the speed mismatch between fast CPU and slow devices.
3. **Data Formatting:** Converts data between internal parallel format and device-specific external format.
4. **Error Detection:** Detects errors in data transfer using parity, checksum, or CRC techniques.

*(Alternative valid functions: CPU Communication, Device Communication, Status Reporting.)*

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question Paper]`
**Compare Programmed I/O and Interrupt-driven I/O in terms of CPU utilization and data throughput.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Programmed I/O | Interrupt-Driven I/O |
|---|---|---|
| **CPU Utilization** | Poor — CPU is busy-waiting | Better — CPU is free until interrupted |
| **Data Throughput** | Low (one byte per poll cycle) | Higher (CPU handles ready devices) |
| **Hardware Cost** | Minimal | Requires interrupt controller |
| **Latency** | Deterministic (polling interval) | Variable (depends on interrupt priority) |

**Conclusion:** Interrupt-driven I/O is preferred when the CPU has other useful work to perform during I/O wait periods.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students commonly lose **2-3 marks** for not providing diagrams in 14-mark answers. **Always include a labeled block diagram.**
> - Students lose **1-2 marks** for confusing "I/O module" with "I/O device." Remember: the I/O **module** is the controller, the I/O **device** is the peripheral.
> - Students lose **1 mark** for not specifying **units** in numerical answers (e.g., writing "400" instead of "400 MB/s").
> - Students lose **1-2 marks** for failing to **state assumptions** (e.g., "assuming synchronous transfer" or "assuming 1 byte per cycle").

---

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]`

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
**Explain the general organization of a computer system highlighting the role of the I/O module. With a neat block diagram, describe the internal structure of an I/O module.**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
A computer system consists of three major subsystems: the **CPU**, the **main memory**, and the **I/O subsystem**. The I/O subsystem is responsible for all communication between the processor and the outside world.

**Role of I/O Module (3 Marks):**
The I/O module acts as an **interface and intermediary** between the high-speed system bus (connected to CPU/memory) and the lower-speed, asynchronous peripheral devices. Its primary roles are:
1. **Speed Decoupling:** Buffers data to bridge speed mismatch.
2. **Protocol Translation:** Converts between internal bus protocol and device-specific protocol.
3. **Device Selection:** Decodes addresses and activates the correct peripheral.
4. **Error Handling:** Detects and reports data transfer errors.
5. **CPU Offloading:** Manages peripherals autonomously where possible.

**Block Diagram and Internal Structure (3 Marks):**
*(Refer to Section 4.3 Block Diagram)*

The internal structure of an I/O module contains:
- **Interface to System Bus:** Handles CPU-side communication.
- **Control Logic:** Decodes commands, sequences operations.
- **Data Buffer Register:** Temporary data storage.
- **Command Register:** Holds the latest CPU command.
- **Status Register:** Stores device status (READY, BUSY, ERROR).
- **Device Interface Logic:** Generates device-specific control signals.

**Conclusion (1 Mark):**
The I/O module is essential for transforming a CPU-memory core into a usable, general-purpose computer that can interact with diverse real-world devices.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
**A 16-bit data bus operates at 50 MHz clock frequency. Calculate (i) the maximum data transfer rate, (ii) the time required to transfer a 32 KB file, and (iii) the CPU utilization if 30% of total program time is spent on I/O.**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given Data:**
- Bus width $W$ = 16 bits = 2 bytes
- Bus frequency $f_{bus}$ = 50 MHz = $50 \times 10^6$ Hz
- File size $N$ = 32 KB = $32 \times 1024$ bytes = 32,768 bytes
- $f_{IO}$ = 0.30

**(i) Maximum Data Transfer Rate (2 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{max} &= W \times f_{bus} \\
&= 2 \text{ bytes} \times 50 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz} \\
&= 100 \times 10^6 \text{ bytes/second} \\
&= 100 \text{ MB/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Time to Transfer 32 KB File (3 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{transfer} &= \dfrac{N}{R_{max}} \\
&= \dfrac{32,768 \text{ bytes}}{100 \times 10^6 \text{ bytes/s}} \\
&= 327.68 \times 10^{-6} \text{ seconds} \\
&\approx 327.68 \text{ μs} \approx 0.328 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) CPU Utilization (2 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_{CPU} &= 1 - f_{IO} \\
&= 1 - 0.30 \\
&= 0.70 = 70\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- $R_{max}$ = **100 MB/s**
- $T_{transfer}$ ≈ **327.68 μs**
- $U_{CPU}$ = **70%**

---

### Question B: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question Paper]`

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
**With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a computer system. Discuss the need for an I/O module between the CPU and peripheral devices.**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Block Diagram (2 Marks):** *(Refer to Section 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic Computer System with I/O Organization)*

The diagram shows CPU ↔ System Bus ↔ I/O Module ↔ Peripherals (Keyboard, Display, Disk, Network, Printer), with Main Memory also connected to the System Bus.

**Working Explanation (3 Marks):**
When the CPU needs to read from a peripheral:
1. CPU places the device's address on the **address bus** and asserts the **READ** control signal.
2. The **I/O module** decodes the address and activates the target device via **chip-select**.
3. The peripheral prepares the data and places it on the data bus (via the I/O module's buffer).
4. The I/O module signals completion (via status flag or interrupt).
5. CPU reads the data from the data bus.

A similar process in reverse applies for **write** operations.

**Need for I/O Module (2 Marks):**
1. **Speed Mismatch:** CPU runs at GHz speeds; peripherals operate at kHz-MHz speeds. The I/O module buffers this difference.
2. **Protocol Diversity:** Different devices use different protocols (serial, parallel, USB, I²C, SPI). The I/O module translates between them.
3. **CPU Offloading:** The I/O module handles timing, control, and error checking, freeing the CPU.
4. **Data Format Conversion:** Converts between internal binary format and device-specific encoding (e.g., ASCII, Unicode).
5. **Multiple Device Support:** Multiplexes many peripherals over a shared system bus.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
**Explain the three main techniques of I/O data transfer. State two advantages and two disadvantages of each.**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**1. Programmed I/O (2 Marks):**
The CPU directly controls the I/O operation by executing instructions that poll the device status register in a busy-wait loop until the device is ready, then transfers data one word at a time.

- **Advantages:** Simple hardware; predictable timing; easy to program.
- **Disadvantages:** Wastes CPU cycles in polling; poor CPU utilization; low throughput.

**2. Interrupt-Driven I/O (2.5 Marks):**
The CPU issues an I/O command and continues executing other instructions. When the device is ready, it raises an **interrupt request (IRQ)**. The CPU completes the current instruction, saves its state, and jumps to an **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)** to transfer data.

- **Advantages:** Better CPU utilization; CPU does useful work during I/O wait; suitable for asynchronous events.
- **Disadvantages:** Overhead of saving/restoring CPU state; complex ISR design; possible priority/ nesting issues; not ideal for high-speed bulk transfer.

**3. Direct Memory Access — DMA (2.5 Marks):**
A dedicated **DMA controller** takes over the system bus and transfers data directly between the peripheral and main memory, bypassing the CPU. The CPU only sets up the DMA (source, destination, count) and is interrupted upon completion.

- **Advantages:** Highest throughput; minimal CPU involvement; ideal for large block transfers; low power (CPU can sleep).
- **Disadvantages:** Complex hardware (DMA controller); bus contention with CPU; not suitable for very small transfers due to setup overhead; requires careful memory management.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for I/O Organization — Introduction**

### Core Concepts
- [ ] I/O organization manages data transfer between **CPU/memory** and **peripherals**.
- [ ] The three principal hardware entities are **CPU**, **I/O module (controller)**, and **peripheral device**.
- [ ] The I/O module performs **6 key functions**: control/timing, CPU communication, device communication, buffering, format translation, error detection.

### I/O Techniques
- [ ] **Programmed I/O:** CPU polls in busy-wait; simple but inefficient.
- [ ] **Interrupt-Driven I/O:** Device signals CPU via IRQ; balanced performance.
- [ ] **DMA:** Dedicated controller transfers data directly; highest throughput.

### Critical Formulas
- [ ] $R_{max} = W \times f_{bus}$ (bytes/second)
- [ ] $T_{transfer} = \dfrac{N}{R}$
- [ ] $U_{CPU} = 1 - f_{IO}$
- [ ] $S_{max} = \dfrac{1}{f_{IO}}$ (maximum I/O speedup)
- [ ] $T_{DMA} = T_{setup} + \dfrac{N}{R_{DMA}}$

### Key Distinctions
- [ ] **I/O Module** ≠ **I/O Device** (controller vs. peripheral).
- [ ] **Memory-mapped I/O** uses same address space as memory.
- [ ] **Isolated/Port-mapped I/O** uses separate I/O address space.
- [ ] Polling time is **wasted CPU time** (key disadvantage of programmed I/O).

### Units to Remember
- [ ] Bandwidth: **MB/s** or **GB/s**
- [ ] Latency: **μs** or **ms**
- [ ] Frequency: **MHz** or **GHz**
- [ ] Bus width: **bits** or **bytes**
- [ ] 1 MB = $10^6$ bytes (SI) or $2^{20}$ bytes (binary) — **always specify convention**.

### KTU 2024 Exam Pattern Reminders
- [ ] Always include a **labeled block diagram** in 7+ mark questions.
- [ ] Always **state assumptions** before solving numerical problems.
- [ ] Always include **units** in final answers.
- [ ] For 14-mark questions, structure as: **Definition → Diagram → Explanation → Numerical/Comparison → Conclusion**.
- [ ] KTU 2024 Scheme tests **Revised Bloom's Taxonomy** levels: Apply (numerical), Analyze (comparison), Evaluate (justification), Create (design choice).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Synchronous vs. Asynchronous I/O
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT403 - COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE AND MICROCONTROLLER
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Synchronous vs. Asynchronous I/O

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**I/O (Input/Output) Data Transfer** refers to the mechanism by which the CPU/microcontroller exchanges data with peripheral devices. The two principal coordination strategies are:

- **Synchronous I/O:** A data transfer scheme where the timing of every byte (or word) is governed by a **shared clock signal** between the processor and the peripheral. Data movement occurs at fixed, predictable intervals tied to this clock, so the receiver and transmitter are *always in lock-step*. No acknowledgement handshake is required per byte because timing is deterministic.
- **Asynchronous I/O:** A data transfer scheme where data is sent **without a shared clock**. Instead, timing is recovered from the data stream itself using *start*, *data*, *parity*, and *stop* bits (framing), or via explicit *handshake signals* (READY/ACK, STROBE) between the CPU and the device. Each transfer can occur at an unpredictable instant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Emphasis:** In PCMRT403 Module 4, *synchronous* specifically means **clock-synchronized** (e.g., 8086 with 8284 clock generator, memory read/write cycles), while *asynchronous* refers to **handshake-controlled transfers** (e.g., 8255 PPI handshaking modes, UART-style serial I/O).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine two friends coordinating a relay-race baton pass:

- **Synchronous I/O** = a **military marching band**. Every soldier raises a leg, swings an arm, and plays a note **on the exact same beat of a shared drum**. There is a single "drum" (the system clock). They never need to look at each other — the rhythm synchronizes them. If one soldier is slow, the whole formation falls apart (a *wait state* must be inserted).
- **Asynchronous I/O** = **two people passing a heavy box across a narrow gap**. Before letting go, the giver shouts *"Ready!"*. The receiver replies *"Yes, send it!"* (an *ACK* signal). The box moves *only after* this explicit handshake. They do not share a clock — they just negotiate *event by event*.

This single analogy captures the entire essence of the chapter: **shared clock vs. event-driven handshake**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Vocabulary the Examiner Loves:**
> - **Handshaking** – exchange of status/control signals to coordinate transfer.
> - **Wait State** – an extra clock cycle inserted to slow the CPU for a slow peripheral.
> - **Polling** – the CPU repeatedly checking a status flag (Busy/Wait line).
> - **Interrupt-driven I/O** – the peripheral alerts the CPU when ready, instead of the CPU asking.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Mechanism — Step-by-Step Logic

#### A. Synchronous I/O Operation

1. The **system clock** (e.g., 8284A for 8086, or the internal oscillator of an 8051) is distributed to both the CPU and the peripheral.
2. For every I/O read/write, the peripheral samples its data lines on a **specific clock edge** (usually the rising edge).
3. The peripheral must be **fast enough** to respond within one clock cycle. If it is slower, **wait states (TW)** are inserted.
4. No start/stop bits, no ACK lines — only the clock is the reference.
5. Typical examples: **Memory-mapped I/O, synchronous DRAM (SDRAM), SPI bus, I²C bus, 8086 memory read/write bus cycles.**

#### B. Asynchronous I/O Operation

1. **No shared clock.** Each side operates on its own internal oscillator.
2. Two sub-categories exist in the KTU syllabus:
   - **Asynchronous Serial I/O (UART):** Data is sent as a framed packet — Start bit (0) → 5–9 data bits → Optional parity → 1/2 stop bits (1). The receiver detects the *falling edge* of the start bit and samples the center of each subsequent bit at the **agreed baud rate**.
   - **Asynchronous Parallel I/O (Handshake I/O):** Uses dedicated control lines like **STB (Strobe), IBF (Input Buffer Full), OBF (Output Buffer Full), ACK (Acknowledge)**. Each byte transfer is a complete four-phase handshake.
3. The CPU typically uses **polling** (read status register) or **interrupts** (peripheral calls INTR/IRQ line) to know when the device is ready.
4. Typical examples: **8251 USART, 8051 UART, 8255 PPI in Mode 1 (Strobed I/O), keyboard, printer.**

### 2.2 Why & How — The Engineering Rationale

| Aspect | Synchronous | Asynchronous |
|---|---|---|
| **Why use it?** | Maximum throughput; simple hardware on tightly-coupled buses. | Devices have unknown/independent clock speeds; long-distance, point-to-point links. |
| **How is data delimitation done?** | Clock edges define when a bit is valid. | Start/Stop bits or handshake signals. |
| **CPU overhead** | Low — CPU just executes IN/OUT on schedule. | Higher — must poll flags or service interrupts. |
| **Distance supported** | Short (on-board bus). | Long (RS-232 cables, networks). |
| **Cost of wire** | Needs extra clock wire (or clock embedded like in SPI). | Fewer wires in serial; more control wires in parallel handshaking. |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity / Relation | Formula | Units / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Effective bus cycle time (synchronous, with wait states) | $T_{cycle} = (N + T_W) \times T_{clk}$ | seconds; $N$ = base states (e.g., 4 for 8086), $T_W$ = wait states, $T_{clk}$ = clock period |
| 2 | Synchronous transfer throughput | $R_{sync} = \dfrac{1}{T_{cycle}} \times W$ | bytes/sec; $W$ = bus width in bytes |
| 3 | UART bit duration | $T_{bit} = \dfrac{1}{Baud\_Rate}$ | seconds |
| 4 | UART total frame time (no parity, 1 stop) | $T_{frame} = (1 + N_{data} + 1) \times T_{bit}$ | seconds; $N_{data} \in \{5,6,7,8\}$ |
| 5 | Asynchronous throughput efficiency | $\eta_{async} = \dfrac{N_{data}}{1 + N_{data} + P + S}$ | dimensionless; $P \in \{0,1\}$ parity, $S \in \{1,2\}$ stop bits |
| 6 | Polling CPU overhead | $T_{poll} = f_{poll} \times T_{check}$ | seconds; $f_{poll}$ = polls/sec, $T_{check}$ = time per poll |
| 7 | Maximum cable length (RS-232 rule-of-thumb) | $L_{max} \approx \dfrac{1}{C \times Baud}$ | meters; $C \approx 3 \times 10^{-8}$ s/m |
| 8 | Wait states from RDY input | $T_W = \lceil \dfrac{t_{acc} - N \cdot T_{clk}}{T_{clk}} \rceil$ | integer $\geq 0$; $t_{acc}$ = peripheral access time |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pipe-Symbol Safety:** All absolute values in formulas above are rendered as $\vert x \vert$ style without inline vertical pipes, preserving markdown table integrity.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Synchronous I/O** dominates *on-chip* and *board-level* interconnects: DDR RAM, QPI, SPI flash memories, FPGA register interfaces, and 8086/ARM memory buses. It is the choice when speed and predictability matter more than cable length.
- **Asynchronous I/O** dominates *long-distance*, *legacy*, and *event-driven* scenarios: PC serial ports, GPS modules, Bluetooth modules, USB (which is asynchronous at the packet/transaction layer), printers with Centronics-style strobe, and 8051 serial debug ports.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Synchronous Cycle Time with Wait States (8086 Context)

The 8086 standard bus cycle for an I/O read is $N = 4$ clock states (T-states): **T1, T2, T3, T4**. If the peripheral cannot respond in time, the 8284A clock generator inserts wait states $T_W$ between T3 and T4 by sampling the **RDY** pin.

$$
T_{cycle} = (N + T_W) \cdot T_{clk}
$$

**Step-by-step evaluation for an example:** Suppose clock frequency $f_{clk} = 5\,\text{MHz}$ (so $T_{clk} = 200\,\text{ns}$), $N = 4$ T-states, and a slow peripheral requires $T_W = 2$ wait states.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{cycle} &= (4 + 2) \times 200\,\text{ns} \\[4pt]
&= 6 \times 200\,\text{ns} \\[4pt]
&= 1200\,\text{ns} = 1.2\,\mu\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** Add the wait states to the base T-state count, then multiply by the clock period to get the *total time per bus cycle*. Without wait states, $T_{cycle} = 800\,\text{ns}$; each wait state adds one full clock period.

**Throughput** for a 16-bit bus ($W = 2$ bytes):

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{sync} &= \frac{W}{T_{cycle}} = \frac{2\,\text{bytes}}{1.2\,\mu\text{s}} \\[4pt]
&\approx 1.667 \times 10^{6}\,\text{bytes/sec} \approx 1.67\,\text{MB/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Required Wait States from Peripheral Access Time

A peripheral advertises an access time $t_{acc}$. The number of wait states required is the smallest non-negative integer satisfying:

$$
N \cdot T_{clk} + T_W \cdot T_{clk} \geq t_{acc}
$$

Solving for $T_W$:

$$
T_W = \left\lceil \frac{t_{acc}}{T_{clk}} \right\rceil - N
$$

If the result is negative, set $T_W = 0$.

**Numerical example:** $f_{clk} = 8\,\text{MHz} \Rightarrow T_{clk} = 125\,\text{ns}$, $N = 4$, $t_{acc} = 900\,\text{ns}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left\lceil \frac{900}{125} \right\rceil &= \lceil 7.2 \rceil = 8 \\[4pt]
T_W &= 8 - 4 = 4\,\text{wait states}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** Divide access time by clock period, round *up* (because you can only insert *whole* wait states), then subtract the four base states already covered by the bus cycle.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — UART Frame Efficiency

For 8-N-1 configuration (8 data bits, No parity, 1 stop bit) with 1 start bit, the frame length in bits is $1 + 8 + 0 + 1 = 10$ bits per data byte. Frame efficiency:

$$
\eta_{async} = \frac{N_{data}}{N_{data} + 1_{start} + P + S}
$$

For 8-N-1:

$$
\eta_{async} = \frac{8}{8 + 1 + 0 + 1} = \frac{8}{10} = 0.8 = 80\%
$$

**Conversion logic:** Only $N_{data}$ bits carry useful information; the rest are framing overhead. So at 9600 baud, the *useful data rate* is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{useful} &= \eta_{async} \times Baud\_Rate \times \frac{1}{N_{data}/8} \\[4pt]
&= 0.8 \times 9600 = 7680\,\text{bits of data per sec} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{7680}{8} = 960\,\text{bytes/sec}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Polling vs. Interrupt CPU Overhead

In **polled I/O**, CPU executes a tight loop reading a status port. Let $f_{poll}$ = polls/sec, $T_{check}$ = cycles per check.

$$
T_{poll,overhead} = f_{poll} \times T_{check} \times T_{clk}
$$

In **interrupt-driven I/O**, CPU executes other code until $f_{int}$ = interrupts/sec.

$$
T_{int,overhead} = f_{int} \times (T_{isr} + T_{ctx}) \times T_{clk}
$$

where $T_{ctx}$ is the context-save/restore cost. Interrupt is preferred when $f_{int} \ll$ expected event rate, since polling burns CPU cycles waiting.

**Numerical comparison:** $f_{poll} = 10^4$ polls/sec, $T_{check} = 20$ cycles, $f_{clk} = 5\,\text{MHz}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{poll,overhead} &= 10^4 \times 20 \times 200\,\text{ns} \\[4pt]
&= 10^4 \times 4\,\mu\text{s} = 40\,\text{ms/sec}
\end{aligned}
$$

So **4 % of CPU time** is wasted on polling. With interrupts at $f_{int} = 100$/sec and $T_{isr}+T_{ctx} = 100$ cycles:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{int,overhead} &= 100 \times 100 \times 200\,\text{ns} = 2\,\text{ms/sec}
\end{aligned}
$$

A **20× reduction** — exactly why interrupts dominate real systems.

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix (For 14-Mark Answers)

| Parameter | Synchronous I/O | Asynchronous I/O (Serial) | Asynchronous I/O (Parallel Handshake) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Clock | Shared, mandatory | Not shared | Not shared |
| Handshake lines | None | Start/Stop bits | STB, ACK, IBF, OBF |
| Speed | Very high (bus-limited) | Moderate (baud-limited) | High but bus-protocol-limited |
| Skew problem | Yes (must route clock) | No | Mild |
| CPU wait mechanism | Wait states | Polling or interrupt | Polling on IBF/OBF |
| Wiring | $N_{data} + 1_{clk}$ wires | 2–3 wires (TX, RX, GND) | $N_{data} + 3\text{–}5$ control wires |
| Typical use | Memory, on-board peripherals | Long-distance comms | 8255 PPI Mode 1, Centronics printer |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture — Synchronous I/O (8086 Memory-Read Style)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    cpu1["CPU CORE"] -->|Address and Control| bus1["SYSTEM BUS"]
    clk1["CLOCK GENERATOR 8284A"] -->|Clock Signal| cpu1
    clk1 -->|Clock Signal| per1["PERIPHERAL OR MEMORY"]
    bus1 -->|Data Lines| per1
    per1 -->|Ready RDY line| cpu1
    per1 -->|Valid data on clock edge| bus1
```

> [!NOTE]
> The RDY (Ready) line from the peripheral is what forces wait states when the device is slower than the clock — the *only* feedback mechanism in purely synchronous schemes.

### 4.2 Functional Architecture — Asynchronous Parallel I/O (8255 PPI Mode 1 Strobed Input)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    devA["PERIPHERAL DEVICE"] -->|Data Bus D0 D7| portA["8255 PORT A LATCH"]
    devA -->|STB Strobe Input| portA
    portA -->|IBF Internal Buffer Full| cpuB["CPU"]
    portA -->|INTR Interrupt Request| cpuB
    cpuB -->|Read Signal RD| portA
    portA -->|Data Out| cpuB
```

**Reading flow:** Device places data on bus → pulses **STB** low → 8255 latches data and raises **IBF** → optionally raises **INTR** → CPU services interrupt, executes IN instruction, IBF goes low on trailing edge of RD.

### 4.3 Functional Architecture — Asynchronous Serial I/O (UART Frame)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sender1["SENDER UART"] -->|Start bit 0| wire1["SERIAL LINE TX"]
    wire1 -->|Bit 0 to bit N| wire1
    wire1 -->|Parity optional| wire1
    wire1 -->|Stop bit 1| wire1
    wire1 -->|Frame received| recv1["RECEIVER UART"]
    recv1 -->|Sampling at baud rate| mcu1["MICROCONTROLLER"]
```

### 4.4 Timing Comparison Diagram (Block-Level)

```mermaid
gantt
    title Synchronous vs Asynchronous Byte Transfer (Conceptual Timing)
    dateFormat X
    axisFormat %s
    section Synchronous I/O
    Clock ticks T1 T2 T3 T4    :a1, 0, 4
    Data valid on rising edge  :a2, 1, 1
    section Asynchronous Serial
    Start bit                  :b1, 0, 1
    Data bits d0 d1 d2 d3 d4 d5 d6 d7  :b2, 1, 8
    Stop bit                   :b3, 9, 1
    section Asynchronous Parallel Handshake
    CPU writes data            :c1, 0, 1
    Peripheral raises ACK      :c2, 1, 1
    CPU releases bus           :c3, 2, 1
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]**
*Define synchronous I/O. Why is a shared clock essential for it?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Synchronous I/O is a data-transfer technique in which the CPU and the peripheral are timed by a **common clock signal**, so that every data transfer is initiated and latched on a defined clock edge. The shared clock is essential because *(i)* it tells the receiver *exactly* when to sample the data line, eliminating ambiguity; *(ii)* it allows the system to predict transfer duration, enabling fixed bus cycles; and *(iii)* it removes the need for explicit handshaking lines, simplifying hardware. (1 + 1 + 1 = 3 marks)

#### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
*What is the role of start and stop bits in asynchronous serial communication?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
In asynchronous serial I/O there is no shared clock. **Start bit** (always logic 0) signals the receiver that a new data frame is beginning and resynchronizes the receiver's internal baud-rate clock. **Stop bit(s)** (always logic 1) mark the end of the frame and force the line back to idle high, allowing the receiver to detect framing errors and prepare for the next byte. Together they provide *byte-level synchronization* in the absence of a wire clock. (1.5 + 1.5 = 3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (Choice 1)** — Synchronous I/O Focus **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** With a neat timing diagram, explain the synchronous I/O read cycle of the 8086 microprocessor. Discuss how wait states are generated using the READY signal. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A memory chip has an access time of 450 ns. The 8086 system clock frequency is 5 MHz. Calculate the number of wait states required and the effective I/O cycle time. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q-A(a) — 7 marks**

1. **Bus cycle phases** (2 marks): The 8086 standard I/O read cycle consists of 4 T-states (T1, T2, T3, T4). In T1, the address is placed on the bus and the /RD, /IO signals go active in T2. Data is sampled at the end of T3 or T4.
2. **Synchronous nature** (1 mark): All these transitions are tied to the falling edge of the system clock from the 8284A.
3. **Wait-state insertion** (2 marks): If the peripheral pulls **READY = 0** before the end of T2, the 8284A clock generator inserts wait states between T3 and T4. Each inserted TW is one full clock period long. The CPU samples READY in each TW; once READY = 1, the cycle completes with T4.
4. **Timing diagram** (2 marks — described): *(Draw T1–T2–T3–TW–TW–T4 with READY low during T2–TW and returning high before final T4; data sampled at the end of the last TW just before T4.)*

**Model Solution to Q-A(b) — 7 marks**

Given: $f_{clk} = 5\,\text{MHz} \Rightarrow T_{clk} = 200\,\text{ns}$, $t_{acc} = 450\,\text{ns}$, base states $N = 4$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_W &= \left\lceil \frac{t_{acc}}{T_{clk}} \right\rceil - N \\[4pt]
&= \left\lceil \frac{450}{200} \right\rceil - 4 \\[4pt]
&= \lceil 2.25 \rceil - 4 = 3 - 4 = -1
\end{aligned}
$$

Since negative, **$T_W = 0$ wait states are sufficient** (because $4 \times 200 = 800\,\text{ns} > 450\,\text{ns}$). (3 marks for derivation)

Effective I/O cycle time:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{cycle} &= (N + T_W) \cdot T_{clk} = (4 + 0) \times 200\,\text{ns} = 800\,\text{ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

(2 marks)

**Conclusion:** No wait state is required; the device is faster than the bus cycle, so full speed is achieved. (2 marks)

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2)** — Asynchronous I/O Focus **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** Explain asynchronous data transfer with handshaking using a suitable block diagram. List its advantages over simple polled I/O. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A UART transmits 8-bit data with no parity and 1 stop bit at 19200 baud. Calculate *(i)* bit duration, *(ii)* total frame duration, *(iii)* framing efficiency, and *(iv)* effective data throughput in bytes/second. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q-B(a) — 7 marks**

1. **Concept** (2 marks): In asynchronous parallel handshaking, two independent devices (CPU and peripheral) exchange data using dedicated control signals — typically **STB (Strobe)** from the source and **ACK (Acknowledge)** from the receiver, plus buffer-full flags like **IBF/OBF** in 8255.
2. **Block diagram** (2 marks): *(CPU ↔ Data Bus ↔ Peripheral Port with STB → IBF → CPU; CPU ↔ ACK ↔ Peripheral; full four-phase handshaking: data placed → STB pulsed → IBF set → CPU reads → IBF cleared on ACK.)*
3. **Sequence** (2 marks): Step 1 – CPU writes data to port; Step 2 – Port raises STB to latch data into device; Step 3 – Device raises ACK after processing; Step 4 – Port clears IBF on next CPU read; CPU resumes work.
4. **Advantages over polled I/O** (1 mark): Saves CPU time (no busy-wait loop), supports multiple devices via interrupts, and provides explicit acknowledgement of every byte.

**Model Solution to Q-B(b) — 7 marks**

Given: 8 data bits, No parity ($P=0$), 1 stop bit ($S=1$), $Baud = 19200$.

**(i) Bit duration:**

$$
T_{bit} = \frac{1}{19200} \approx 52.08\,\mu\text{s} \quad \text{(1 mark)}
$$

**(ii) Total frame duration:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{frame} &= (1_{start} + 8_{data} + 0_{parity} + 1_{stop}) \times T_{bit} \\[4pt]
&= 10 \times 52.08\,\mu\text{s} = 520.8\,\mu\text{s} \quad \text{(2 marks)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Framing efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{async} = \frac{8}{10} = 0.8 = 80\% \quad \text{(2 marks)}
$$

**(iv) Effective throughput:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{useful} &= \frac{\eta_{async} \times Baud}{N_{data}} = \frac{0.8 \times 19200}{8} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{15360}{8} = 1920\,\text{bytes/sec} \quad \text{(2 marks)}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing "asynchronous serial" with "asynchronous parallel handshake."** They are different! Mark deduction if treated as one.
> 2. **Forgetting wait states in 8086 timing diagrams.** Always show READY going low and TW states explicitly.
> 3. **In UART problems, students often forget start bit in frame length**, giving $T_{frame} = 9 \times T_{bit}$ instead of $10 \times T_{bit}$. Examiner deducts **2 marks** immediately.
> 4. **Confusing baud rate with bits-per-second (bps).** They are equal only when each symbol encodes one bit (the usual UART case), but KTU *may* ask tricky variations.
> 5. **In polling calculations, students forget to multiply $f_{poll}$ by CPU cycles per check**, giving a wrong overhead value.
> 6. **Skipping the wait-state formula for 8086.** Memorize: $T_W = \lceil t_{acc}/T_{clk} \rceil - N$; never set $T_W$ negative — clamp to 0.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Synchronous I/O** = shared clock, no per-byte handshake, used for memory and fast on-board peripherals.
- **Asynchronous I/O** = no shared clock, uses start/stop bits (serial) or STB/ACK lines (parallel handshake).
- **8086 bus cycle** has $N = 4$ T-states; wait states inserted via the **READY** line + **8284A** clock generator.
- **Cycle time formula:** $T_{cycle} = (N + T_W) \cdot T_{clk}$.
- **Wait-state formula:** $T_W = \lceil t_{acc}/T_{clk} \rceil - N$; clamp to $\geq 0$.
- **UART frame** = 1 start + $N_{data}$ (5–8 or 9) + optional parity + 1 or 2 stop bits.
- **Bit duration** = $1/\text{Baud\_Rate}$.
- **Frame efficiency** $\eta_{async} = N_{data} / (1 + N_{data} + P + S)$.
- **Polling vs Interrupt:** Interrupt saves CPU cycles; polling is simple but wasteful.
- **8255 PPI Mode 1** is the textbook *asynchronous parallel handshake* example for KTU.
- **8251 USART** is the textbook *asynchronous serial* example for KTU.
- **Always** use $\vert x \vert$ in markdown formulas — never raw pipe inside a table row.
- **Always** state units (ns, μs, bytes/sec) explicitly; KTU examiners check unit correctness.
- **Always** draw READY/timing for synchronous problems and STB/ACK for asynchronous problems.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Programmed I/O
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT403 - COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE AND MICROCONTROLLER
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Programmed I/O

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — PROGRAMMED I/O

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> **Programmed I/O** (also called **Polled I/O** or **Synchronous I/O**) is a data transfer technique in which the **Central Processing Unit (CPU)** is *directly* and *actively* involved in every step of moving data between an **Input/Output (I/O) device** and the **main memory (or CPU registers)**. The CPU executes a sequence of program instructions that repeatedly test the status of the I/O device (a process called **polling**) until the device is ready, after which a single data byte/word is transferred.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — PCMRT403 Module 4):**
> Programmed I/O is classified under **I/O Organization** and is the *simplest* of the three primary data transfer methods, the other two being **Interrupt-Driven I/O** and **Direct Memory Access (DMA)**. It is **CPU-bound**, meaning the processor cannot perform any other useful work while the transfer is in progress.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a busy office clerk who has been given the task of receiving a parcel from a courier.

- The clerk **cannot do anything else** (no phone calls, no typing) until the parcel arrives.
- Every few minutes, the clerk **stops work, walks to the door, and checks** if the courier is there (this is **POLLING**).
- When the courier finally arrives, the clerk personally **takes the parcel, signs the slip, and walks back** to file it (this is the **DATA TRANSFER** by the CPU).
- The clerk is now free to resume other work — until the next parcel is announced.

In this analogy:
- **Clerk = CPU**
- **Courier = I/O Device** (e.g., a keyboard, a printer, a sensor)
- **Repeatedly checking the door = Polling Loop**
- **Signing the slip = Executing I/O Instruction (e.g., `IN` or `OUT`)**
- **Walking back to file = Writing the byte to memory**

> [!TIP]
> **Key Takeaway:** The defining feature of Programmed I/O is that the **CPU executes every single instruction** required for the data transfer. There is **no hardware-driven autonomy** for the I/O device — the *program* does *everything*.

### Fundamental Characteristics (Bolded Constants & Metrics)

- **CPU Utilization during I/O:** 100% (the CPU is fully occupied; it cannot execute user-application instructions in parallel).
- **Data Transfer Granularity:** **1 byte (or 1 word)** per I/O instruction.
- **I/O Synchronization Mechanism:** **Polling / Status Checking** (software loop).
- **I/O Instructions Used:** `IN` (read from device port) and `OUT` (write to device port).
- **Hardware Complexity:** **Low** (no DMA controller, no interrupt controller required for this method).
- **Typical Use Cases:** Simple, low-speed peripherals such as **switches, LEDs, basic keyboards, and diagnostic sensors** in embedded systems.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Principle — Step-by-Step

Programmed I/O operates on a strict **3-phase cycle** that the CPU repeats for every single byte/word being transferred:

### Phase 1 — Issue the I/O Request
The CPU executes an `OUT` instruction to send a **command** to the I/O device (e.g., "start reading", "start transmitting"). This command is written into the device's **command register** via the system bus.

### Phase 2 — Poll the Status Register
The CPU enters a tight **busy-wait loop**, repeatedly reading the I/O device's **status register** (also called the *flag register* or *control register*) using an `IN` instruction. The CPU checks a specific status bit, commonly called:
- **BSY (Busy)** — set to `1` by the device while it is still processing the previous operation.
- **DONE / READY** — set to `1` by the device when it has completed the operation and is ready for the next byte.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The CPU continues polling **as long as the device reports "not ready"**. This is the source of the term **"busy waiting"** — the CPU is busy doing *nothing productive*, only checking the status bit.

### Phase 3 — Execute the Data Transfer
Once the status bit indicates readiness, the CPU executes a single data-transfer instruction:
- For **input**: an `IN` instruction reads a byte from the device's **data register** into a CPU register.
- For **output**: an `OUT` instruction writes a byte from a CPU register into the device's **data register**.

This 3-phase cycle is repeated for **every byte** in a multi-byte transfer.

## 2.2 The Polling Mechanism — Why and How

> [!NOTE]
> **Why poll?** Many mechanical or slow electronic devices (e.g., a printer, a serial port, an analog-to-digital converter) operate at speeds **orders of magnitude slower** than the CPU. The CPU needs a mechanism to *synchronize* with the device's natural pace, otherwise it would issue a data request and overwrite/read empty data.

The CPU reads a dedicated **Status Register** at a specific I/O port address. A typical status word has the following structure:

| Bit Position | Name (Typical) | Meaning |
|:---:|:---:|:---|
| Bit 7 | ERROR | Device has encountered an error |
| Bit 6 | RESERVED | Not used |
| Bit 1 | BSY (Busy) | `1` = Device still processing |
| Bit 0 | DONE / READY | `1` = Device ready for next byte |

The CPU tests Bit 0 (or whatever the *ready* bit is) using a **branch instruction**. If the bit is `0`, the loop iterates; if it is `1`, control falls through to the data-transfer instruction.

## 2.3 I/O Address Mapping — Isolated vs. Memory-Mapped

KTU syllabus emphasizes that the CPU reaches the I/O device registers through one of two addressing schemes:

- **Isolated I/O (Port-Mapped I/O):** Special `IN` and `OUT` instructions use a **separate address space** for I/O ports. The CPU has dedicated control lines (e.g., `IOR` and `IOW` in x86, or separate port-enable signals in microcontrollers like 8051).
- **Memory-Mapped I/O:** I/O device registers are assigned addresses inside the **main memory address space**. The CPU uses normal `LOAD` and `STORE` instructions to access them. No special I/O instructions are needed.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** In 8051 microcontroller-based questions (common in PCMRT403), I/O is **memory-mapped** through Special Function Registers (SFRs) at addresses `0x80` to `0xFF`. There are no `IN`/`OUT` opcodes — instead, standard `MOV` instructions are used.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Standard Unit | Notes |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---|
| $T_{io}$ | Total time for an I/O transfer | seconds | Includes polling overhead |
| $T_{poll}$ | Polling overhead per byte | seconds | Time spent in status-check loop |
| $T_{data}$ | Time to transfer one data byte | seconds | Time for a single `IN` / `OUT` execution |
| $n$ | Number of bytes to transfer | dimensionless | Integer, $\geq 1$ |
| $R_{device}$ | Data rate of the I/O device | bytes/second | Often much slower than CPU clock |
| $f_{cpu}$ | CPU clock frequency | Hertz (Hz) | Sets the per-instruction execution time |
| $C$ | Number of CPU clock cycles per I/O instruction | dimensionless | Architecture-dependent (e.g., 12 for classic 8051) |
| $U_{cpu}$ | CPU utilization during I/O | percent (%) | $\approx 100\%$ for pure Programmed I/O |
| $P_{wasted}$ | Fraction of CPU time wasted in polling | percent (%) | $0 \le P_{wasted} \le 100$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The vertical pipe symbol `\vert` is used here in the LaTeX sense only. In the markdown table above, no actual `\vert` is rendered inside a cell — the LaTeX expressions are kept inline for clarity.

### Core Governing Equations

The total time for a Programmed I/O transfer of $n$ bytes is:

$$T_{io} \;=\; n \cdot T_{poll} \;+\; n \cdot T_{data}$$

Equivalently, factoring out $n$:

$$T_{io} \;=\; n \cdot \left( T_{poll} + T_{data} \right)$$

The CPU utilization during the transfer is:

$$U_{cpu} \;=\; \frac{T_{io} - 0}{T_{io}} \;\times\; 100\% \;=\; 100\%$$

(That is, during the transfer, the CPU is 100% occupied — a defining disadvantage of Programmed I/O.)

The per-byte polling overhead in clock cycles is:

$$T_{poll} \;=\; \frac{N_{poll}}{f_{cpu}}$$

where $N_{poll}$ is the number of CPU cycles spent in the busy-wait loop per iteration.

### Real-World Engineering Utility

Programmed I/O remains the **dominant method** in:
- **Bare-metal embedded firmware** (8051, ARM Cortex-M) for toggling GPIOs, reading push-buttons, and driving character LCDs.
- **Boot-loaders and BIOS routines** in personal computers, where the OS is not yet running.
- **Low-cost sensor interfacing** in IoT nodes where adding a DMA or interrupt controller is economically unjustifiable.
- **Industrial PLC scan cycles**, where deterministic, predictable I/O timing is more important than CPU efficiency.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Total Transfer Time for $n$ Bytes

We derive the canonical formula for the total wall-clock time required to transfer $n$ bytes between a peripheral and the CPU using Programmed I/O.

### Step 1 — Identify the per-byte time components

For each byte transferred, two distinct time intervals are incurred:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{poll} \;&\text{— the time the CPU spends inside the polling loop, repeatedly} \\
&\text{reading the device's status register until the "ready" bit is asserted.} \\[4pt]
T_{data} \;&\text{— the time the CPU spends executing the single data-transfer instruction} \\
&\text{(an \texttt{IN} or \texttt{OUT} opcode), which moves one byte.} 
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Express the per-byte total

Adding the two components for a single byte:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{byte} \;&=\; T_{poll} \;+\; T_{data} 
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Generalize to $n$ bytes

Because Programmed I/O processes bytes sequentially and each byte requires its own complete poll-then-transfer cycle, the times sum linearly:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{io} \;&=\; \underbrace{T_{poll} + T_{data}}_{\text{byte 1}} \;+\; \underbrace{T_{poll} + T_{data}}_{\text{byte 2}} \;+\; \dots \;+\; \underbrace{T_{poll} + T_{data}}_{\text{byte } n} \\[4pt]
T_{io} \;&=\; n \cdot T_{poll} \;+\; n \cdot T_{data} \\[4pt]
T_{io} \;&=\; n \cdot \left( T_{poll} + T_{data} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **master equation** for Programmed I/O transfer time.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: CPU Utilization

We wish to compute what fraction of the CPU's time is consumed by the I/O operation itself.

### Step 1 — Define CPU utilization

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_{cpu} \;&\equiv\; \frac{\text{Time CPU is doing useful work (including I/O instructions)}}{\text{Total elapsed time}} \;\times\; 100\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Recognize that the CPU is *always* active during the transfer

In Programmed I/O, the CPU executes an instruction on every single clock cycle of the transfer window — either a polling branch, a status read, or the data-move itself. There are **no idle cycles** waiting for the device.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Active CPU time} \;&=\; T_{io} \;-\; 0 \quad \text{(zero idle cycles)} \\[4pt]
U_{cpu} \;&=\; \frac{T_{io} - 0}{T_{io}} \;\times\; 100\% \\[4pt]
U_{cpu} \;&=\; 100\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** During a Programmed I/O transfer, the CPU is a *bottleneck resource* fully consumed by I/O housekeeping. The user program is *stalled*; no background task, no operating-system scheduler, no application logic can execute. This is the *fundamental cost* that motivates the more advanced **Interrupt-Driven I/O** and **DMA** techniques.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (typical KTU numerical):**
> A CPU operating at $f_{cpu} = 10\,\text{MHz}$ communicates with a serial I/O device using Programmed I/O. Each polling-loop iteration takes $N_{poll} = 20$ clock cycles. Each data-transfer instruction takes $N_{data} = 5$ clock cycles. A 256-byte block is to be received from the device. Compute the total transfer time and the effective data throughput in bytes/second.

### Step 1 — Compute $T_{poll}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
