T_{poll} \;&=\; \frac{N_{poll}}{f_{cpu}} \;=\; \frac{20}{10 \times 10^{6}} \;=\; 2 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{s} \;=\; 2\,\mu\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute $T_{data}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{data} \;&=\; \frac{N_{data}}{f_{cpu}} \;=\; \frac{5}{10 \times 10^{6}} \;=\; 0.5 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{s} \;=\; 0.5\,\mu\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute total time for $n = 256$ bytes

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{io} \;&=\; n \cdot (T_{poll} + T_{data}) \\[4pt]
T_{io} \;&=\; 256 \cdot (2\,\mu\text{s} + 0.5\,\mu\text{s}) \\[4pt]
T_{io} \;&=\; 256 \cdot 2.5\,\mu\text{s} \;=\; 640\,\mu\text{s} \;=\; 0.64\,\text{ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute effective throughput

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Throughput} \;&=\; \frac{n}{T_{io}} \;=\; \frac{256}{640 \times 10^{-6}} \;\approx\; 4.0 \times 10^{5}\,\text{bytes/s} \;=\; 400\,\text{KB/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Insight:** Notice that if the *same* device were serviced via **DMA**, the CPU would be free during those $0.64\,\text{ms}$ to execute roughly $0.64 \times 10^{-3} \times 10 \times 10^{6} = 6400$ instruction cycles of *user code*. That recovered CPU bandwidth is the *quantitative justification* for moving beyond Programmed I/O in high-throughput systems.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Programmed I/O vs. Alternatives)

| Performance Axis | Programmed I/O | Interrupt-Driven I/O | DMA |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| CPU involvement per byte | **High** (every byte polled) | **Low** (only on interrupt) | **None** (device-to-memory autonomously) |
| Hardware complexity | **Lowest** | Medium (interrupt controller) | Highest (DMA controller) |
| Suitability for high-speed devices | Poor | Good | Excellent |
| Suitability for low-speed, simple devices | **Excellent** | Overkill | Overkill |
| Predictability of latency | **Highest** (deterministic) | Variable (interrupt latency) | Variable (bus arbitration) |
| CPU utilization during transfer | **100%** | ~5–20% (typical) | ~0–5% (typical) |
| Typical data block size handled | 1 byte at a time | 1 byte at a time | Large blocks (KB to MB) |
| Code complexity in firmware | **Lowest** | Medium | Highest |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Block Diagram — Programmed I/O System

The figure below shows the architectural relationship between the CPU, system bus, I/O interface, and the peripheral device during a Programmed I/O transfer.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    cpu1["CPU CORE"] -->|Address Bus| busA[ADDRESS BUS]
    cpu1 -->|Data Bus| busD[DATA BUS]
    cpu1 -->|Control Signals| busC[CONTROL BUS]
    busA --> iface[IO INTERFACE LOGIC]
    busD --> iface
    busC --> iface
    iface -->|Status Word| sreg[STATUS REGISTER]
    iface -->|Data Byte| dreg[DATA REGISTER]
    iface -->|Command| creg[COMMAND REGISTER]
    sreg --> dev[PERIPHERAL DEVICE]
    dreg --> dev
    creg --> dev
```

**Interpretation of the diagram:**
- The **CPU Core** drives three parallel buses.
- The **I/O Interface Logic** decodes the port address and routes traffic to one of three internal registers inside the device:
  - **Status Register** — polled by the CPU.
  - **Data Register** — the actual payload byte.
  - **Command Register** — where the CPU issues start/stop/direction commands.
- The **Peripheral Device** is the external world (sensor, motor, printer, network interface, etc.).

## 4.2 Flowchart — Programmed I/O Operation (Input from Device)

The flowchart below captures the exact instruction-level decision flow a CPU follows when *reading* a byte from a device using Programmed I/O.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([START]) --> initA[Issue OUT command to device: begin read]
    initA --> pollA[Read device Status Register via IN]
    pollA --> checkA{Is DONE bit equal to 1?}
    checkA -->|NO| pollA
    checkA -->|YES| readA[Execute IN to read data byte into CPU register]
    readA --> doneA{All n bytes received?}
    doneA -->|NO| initA
    doneA -->|YES| stopA([END])
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the flowchart:** Notice the *self-loop* on the "Read Status Register" step — this is the **busy-wait loop** where the CPU spins. The tighter this loop, the more CPU cycles are consumed doing nothing productive.

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Polling & Transfer State Machine

This diagram decomposes the internal state transitions of the CPU while servicing one byte through Programmed I/O.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s1[STATE 1 IDLE] -->|OUT to Command Register| s2[STATE 2 WAIT FOR DEVICE]
    s2 -->|Loop IN from Status Register| s2
    s2 -->|Status Ready equals 1| s3[STATE 3 TRANSFER]
    s3 -->|IN from Data Register| s4[STATE 4 BYTE RECEIVED]
    s4 -->|Decrement counter| s5{Bytes Remaining}
    s5 -->|Greater than 0| s2
    s5 -->|Equal to 0| s6[STATE 5 COMPLETE]
```

**Functional states explained:**

| State | Name | Function |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | IDLE | CPU is executing application code |
| 2 | WAIT FOR DEVICE | CPU is in busy-wait polling loop |
| 3 | TRANSFER | CPU executes the actual `IN` or `OUT` |
| 4 | BYTE RECEIVED | Byte is in a CPU register, ready to be stored |
| 5 | COMPLETE | Block transfer finished; CPU returns to application |

## 4.4 Hand-Off Sequence Diagram — CPU, Bus, and Device Over Time

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CPU
    participant BUS
    participant DEV as IO DEVICE
    CPU->>BUS: OUT command (start read)
    BUS->>DEV: command byte latched
    loop POLLING LOOP
        CPU->>BUS: IN from status register
        BUS->>DEV: read request
        DEV-->>BUS: status word 0x00 (NOT READY)
        BUS-->>CPU: status 0x00
    end
    CPU->>BUS: IN from status register
    BUS->>DEV: read request
    DEV-->>BUS: status word 0x01 (READY)
    BUS-->>CPU: status 0x01
    CPU->>BUS: IN from data register
    BUS->>DEV: read request
    DEV-->>BUS: data byte 0x5A
    BUS-->>CPU: data 0x5A
    CPU->>CPU: store byte to memory
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Time-line insight:** The horizontal "loop" brackets over the polling phase illustrate *exactly* where the CPU is wasting cycles. The longer the device takes to become ready, the more cycles are wasted in that loop.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern Note:** Part A questions test *recall* and *definition-level* understanding. KTU 2024 typically awards **2–3 marks for the core definition** and **0–1 mark for a distinguishing example or drawing**. A diagram is *highly recommended* to secure full marks.

### Q1. Define Programmed I/O. Mention its two main disadvantages. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

> **Programmed I/O** is a data transfer technique in which the **CPU directly controls** the movement of data between an I/O device and the memory by **executing program instructions**, including a **polling loop** that repeatedly checks the device's status register until it is ready.

**Two main disadvantages:**
1. **CPU is fully occupied (100% utilization)** during the entire I/O transfer, wasting processing power that could be used for other tasks.
2. **Slow data throughput** for high-speed or large-volume devices, because the CPU must service every single byte individually.

*(Examiner's expected length: 4–6 lines. Add a small flowchart of the polling loop for an extra mark.)*

---

### Q2. Differentiate between Isolated I/O and Memory-Mapped I/O. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Isolated I/O | Memory-Mapped I/O |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Address space | Separate I/O address space | Shares main memory address space |
| Instructions used | Special `IN` and `OUT` | Normal `LOAD` / `STORE` |
| Memory available to programs | Full memory | Reduced (part consumed by I/O) |
| Address decoding | Simpler | More complex |
| Speed | Often faster | Comparable to memory access |

> [!TIP]
> **Bonus mark:** Mention that the **8051 microcontroller uses memory-mapped I/O** through its Special Function Registers (SFRs), which is a high-yield KTU point.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split typically as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Students must answer *one full question* (with both sub-parts) from the given internal choice.

---

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**(a)** With the help of a flowchart, explain the working of **Programmed I/O** for transferring a block of data from an I/O device to the CPU. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A CPU runs at **$8\,\text{MHz}$** and uses Programmed I/O to read data from a sensor. The polling loop takes **$24$ clock cycles** per iteration, and the data-read instruction takes **$6$ clock cycles**. If a block of **$1\,\text{KB}$** ($1024$ bytes) is to be transferred, calculate the **total transfer time** and the **CPU utilization** during the transfer. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Setup and Command Phase**
The CPU begins by writing a command (e.g., "begin conversion" or "start transmission") into the I/O device's **command register** using an `OUT` instruction.

**Step 2 — Polling Phase (Busy-Wait Loop)**
The CPU enters a tight loop:
1. Read the **status register** using an `IN` instruction.
2. Test the **DONE/READY bit**.
3. If the bit is `0`, branch back to step 1.
4. If the bit is `1`, exit the loop.

**Step 3 — Data Transfer Phase**
The CPU executes an `IN` instruction to read one byte from the device's **data register** into a CPU register, then stores it into memory.

**Step 4 — Loop or Terminate**
The CPU decrements a byte-counter. If more bytes remain, it returns to Step 1; otherwise it terminates the I/O routine.

**Flowchart (textual representation for answer sheet):**

```
[START]
   |
   v
OUT command to device
   |
   v
<LOOP TOP>
   |
   v
IN from status register
   |
   v
Test DONE bit ---- (DONE = 0) ----> back to LOOP TOP
   |
 (DONE = 1)
   |
   v
IN data byte
   |
   v
Store to memory, decrement counter
   |
   v
[COUNTER = 0?] --(NO)--> back to OUT command
   |
 (YES)
   |
   v
[END]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Mark allocation guide (7 marks):**
> - Correct identification of 3 phases: **2 marks**
> - Polling loop mechanism: **2 marks**
> - Flowchart with all decision branches: **2 marks**
> - Conclusion mentioning CPU-100% occupation: **1 mark**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- $f_{cpu} = 8\,\text{MHz} = 8 \times 10^{6}\,\text{Hz}$
- $N_{poll} = 24$ clock cycles per polling iteration
- $N_{data} = 6$ clock cycles per data instruction
- $n = 1024$ bytes

**Step 1 — Compute $T_{poll}$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{poll} \;&=\; \frac{N_{poll}}{f_{cpu}} \;=\; \frac{24}{8 \times 10^{6}} \;=\; 3 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{s} \;=\; 3\,\mu\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $T_{data}$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{data} \;&=\; \frac{N_{data}}{f_{cpu}} \;=\; \frac{6}{8 \times 10^{6}} \;=\; 0.75 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{s} \;=\; 0.75\,\mu\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute total transfer time $T_{io}$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{io} \;&=\; n \cdot (T_{poll} + T_{data}) \\[4pt]
T_{io} \;&=\; 1024 \cdot (3 + 0.75)\,\mu\text{s} \\[4pt]
T_{io} \;&=\; 1024 \cdot 3.75\,\mu\text{s} \;=\; 3840\,\mu\text{s} \;=\; 3.84\,\text{ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — CPU utilization**

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_{cpu} \;&=\; 100\%
\end{aligned}
$$

(Throughout the $3.84\,\text{ms}$ window, the CPU executes *only* I/O instructions and polling branches; no application code runs.)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake (KTU Examiner's Pitfall #1):** Students often forget to *add* $T_{poll}$ and $T_{data}$ *before* multiplying by $n$. The correct order is **inside-the-bracket sum first, then multiplication.** Writing $1024 \times 3 + 1024 \times 0.75$ gives the same answer only if you carry out the sum inside parentheses — many scripts deduct **1 mark** for arithmetic errors here.

---

### Question B (Internal Choice Alternative) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Polling** in Programmed I/O. Why is the CPU considered the bottleneck in this method? Compare Programmed I/O with **Interrupt-Driven I/O** in terms of CPU efficiency. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Draw the **block diagram of an I/O interface** for a Programmed I/O system and label the **Data Register, Status Register, and Command Register**. Describe the function of each register with one example. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Polling concept:**
Polling is a **software technique** in which the CPU repeatedly reads the **status register** of an I/O device to determine whether the device has completed its current operation. The loop continues until a *ready* or *done* flag is observed.

**Why CPU is the bottleneck:**
- The CPU must execute *one status-check instruction* (typically 4–12 cycles) every time it wants to know the device state.
- For a slow device, the CPU performs **thousands of useless reads** before the device is finally ready.
- During this entire interval, the CPU is *not* executing user code — it is a 100% consumed resource.
- The transfer rate is therefore **capped by how fast the CPU can poll and respond**, not by the device's intrinsic speed.

**Comparison with Interrupt-Driven I/O:**

| Aspect | Programmed I/O | Interrupt-Driven I/O |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| Synchronization | Polling loop (CPU-driven) | Hardware interrupt (device-driven) |
| CPU cycles per byte | Many (polling + transfer) | Few (transfer only) |
| CPU free to do other work? | **No** | **Yes** |
| Hardware cost | None extra | Interrupt controller required |
| Response latency | Predictable | Variable (depends on interrupt priority) |
| Best for | Slow, simple, deterministic I/O | Moderate-speed, event-driven I/O |

**Mark split:** Polling concept = 2 marks, CPU bottleneck = 2 marks, comparison table = 3 marks.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Block diagram (drawn in the answer script):**

```
                        +----------------------------+
                        |   I/O INTERFACE (inside    |
   CPU  <----BUS--------|   the peripheral device)   |
                        |                            |
                        |  +-------------------+     |
                        |  | COMMAND REGISTER  | <---+  (CPU issues commands)
                        |  +-------------------+     |
                        |                            |
                        |  +-------------------+     |
                        |  | STATUS REGISTER   | <---+  (CPU polls flags)
                        |  +-------------------+     |
                        |                            |
                        |  +-------------------+     |
                        |  |  DATA REGISTER    | <---+  (CPU reads/writes payload)
                        |  +-------------------+     |
                        |                            |
                        +----------------------------+
```

**Function of each register (with one example each):**

1. **Data Register** — Holds the *actual payload byte* being transferred.
   - *Example:* When typing on a keyboard, each keystroke's ASCII code is placed in the data register for the CPU to read.

2. **Status Register** — Holds *flag bits* describing the device's current condition.
   - *Example:* Bit 0 = `DONE`, Bit 1 = `BSY`. The CPU reads this register to decide whether to proceed with a transfer.

3. **Command Register** — Accepts *control words* from the CPU that direct the device's behavior.
   - *Example:* The CPU writes `0x01` to the command register to instruct a printer to start, or `0x00` to reset it.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake (KTU Examiner's Pitfall #2):** Many students *omit* the distinction between the **status register** (read-only, polled by CPU) and the **command register** (write-only, used by CPU to direct the device). Examiners **deduct 1–2 marks** if these are confused or merged into a single "control register" without explanation.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 4 Ways Students Lose Marks on Programmed I/O Questions (KTU 2024)**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the master equation $T_{io} = n \cdot (T_{poll} + T_{data})$** — many students compute per-byte time correctly but then forget to multiply by $n$. **Cost: 2 marks.**
> 
> 2. **Confusing Polling with Interrupt** — a student who describes the CPU *interrupted* by the device is describing a *different* I/O method. Examiners will mark the question based on whether the answer reflects *polling*. **Cost: 3–4 marks.**
> 
> 3. **No flowchart / block diagram in a 7-mark sub-question** — KTU examiners explicitly allocate marks for diagrams (typically 1.5–2 marks). A textual-only answer loses this. **Cost: 1.5–2 marks.**
> 
> 4. **Not stating the 100% CPU utilization explicitly** — this is the *defining disadvantage* of Programmed I/O and almost always appears as a 1-mark follow-up. **Cost: 1 mark.**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Programmed I/O**

- **Definition:** CPU-driven data transfer using *executed instructions*, including a *polling loop*.
- **Three phases of every byte transfer:** (1) Issue command, (2) Poll status, (3) Transfer data.
- **Key registers inside the I/O interface:** Data Register, Status Register, Command Register.
- **Master equation:** $T_{io} = n \cdot (T_{poll} + T_{data})$.
- **CPU utilization:** $U_{cpu} = 100\%$ during the transfer.
- **Per-component times:** $T_{poll} = N_{poll} / f_{cpu}$ and $T_{data} = N_{data} / f_{cpu}$.
- **Two addressing modes:** Isolated (port-mapped, special `IN`/`OUT` opcodes) vs. Memory-mapped (uses normal load/store).
- **8051 microcontroller** uses **memory-mapped I/O** via **Special Function Registers (SFRs)** — high-yield KTU point.
- **Busy-wait loop** is the *defining feature*; CPU is fully occupied and cannot multitask.
- **Advantages:** simple hardware, deterministic timing, low firmware complexity, ideal for slow/simple devices.
- **Disadvantages:** 100% CPU occupation, poor throughput for large blocks, wastes CPU cycles on polling.
- **Comparison anchor:** Interrupt-Driven I/O and DMA were *invented specifically* to overcome the bottlenecks of Programmed I/O.
- **Typical use cases:** toggling GPIOs, reading push-buttons, character LCDs, simple UART, boot-loader routines, PLC scan cycles.
- **Draw-and-label skill:** Always draw the I/O interface block with the three registers when asked for a "block diagram" — it is a 2-mark item on its own.
- **One-line punch line for the exam:** *"Programmed I/O is the simplest I/O method, but it ties up the CPU completely by polling a status register until the device is ready."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interrupt Driven I/O
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT403 - COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE AND MICROCONTROLLER
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Interrupt Driven I/O

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Interrupt Driven I/O** is an asynchronous data transfer mechanism in which the Input/Output (I/O) device notifies (interrupts) the Central Processing Unit (CPU) only when it is ready to exchange data, rather than the CPU continuously polling the device. The CPU executes its main program normally, and upon receipt of the interrupt signal, control is transferred to a dedicated **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)** that services the device. After service completion, the CPU resumes its suspended main program from the exact point of interruption using the saved **Program Counter (PC)** and **Processor Status Word (PSW)** in the stack.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Interrupt-driven data transfer is a core topic in computer architecture where the CPU is freed from the busy-wait overhead of programmed I/O, allowing concurrent execution of computational tasks and I/O operations. The key sub-concepts include interrupt sources (internal vs external), the **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)** execution flow, the **Interrupt Acknowledge (INTA)** cycle, vectored vs non-vectored interrupts, and the role of the **Interrupt Controller (e.g., 8259 PIC)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Office Worker & Doorbell" Model

Imagine a busy office worker (the **CPU**) who is completing an important report (the **main program**). A client (**I/O device**) may arrive at any time with data to deliver.

- **Programmed I/O (Polling)**: The worker stops writing every 30 seconds, gets up, walks to the door, peeks outside, and returns to the chair — even if no one is there. This wastes enormous time and energy.
- **Interrupt Driven I/O**: The worker installs a **doorbell (interrupt request line IRQ)**. He continues writing the report. When the client arrives, the doorbell rings, the worker pauses, opens the door, receives the data (executes the **ISR**), closes the door, and seamlessly continues writing the report from where he left off (returns via **IRET/RETI**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Realization for Students:** Interrupt-driven I/O is essentially a hardware-assisted "event notification" mechanism that achieves **concurrency** between the CPU and slow I/O devices. The CPU is no longer "married" to the device — they communicate asynchronously through signals.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics (Bolded as Required)

The performance of an Interrupt Driven I/O system is quantified by three primary metrics, each with standardized units:

- **Interrupt Latency ($t_{L}$):** The time delay between the instant the device asserts the interrupt request signal and the instant the CPU begins executing the first instruction of the ISR. Measured in **microseconds ($\mu s$)** or **clock cycles**.
- **Throughput ($TP$):** The number of I/O operations (or data units) processed per unit time. Units: **operations/second** or **bytes/second (B/s)**.
- **CPU Utilization ($U_{CPU}$):** The fraction of total CPU time spent on the main program versus ISR overhead. Unitless ratio, ideally **$0 \le U_{CPU} \le 1$**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The Interrupt Cycle

Interrupt Driven I/O operates through a precise sequence of hardware and software events:

1. **Device Asserts IRQ (Interrupt Request):** The I/O controller activates its dedicated **Interrupt Request Line (IRQ$n$)** connected to either the CPU's INT pin or a programmable interrupt controller (PIC). This is a level-sensitive or edge-triggered electrical signal.
2. **CPU Completes Current Instruction:** The CPU does not respond mid-instruction. It finishes the currently executing instruction to maintain **atomicity** of the program state.
3. **Interrupt Acknowledgement (INTA) Cycle:** The CPU asserts the **INTA** signal, indicating it has recognized the interrupt. For vectored interrupts, the device or PIC places an **interrupt vector number** on the data bus during the INTA cycle.
4. **State Saving (Context Save):** Before jumping to the ISR, the CPU automatically pushes the **Program Counter (PC)** and the **Processor Status Register (flags)** onto the system stack. Some architectures also push general-purpose registers.
5. **Vector Lookup & ISR Dispatch:** The CPU uses the vector number to index into the **Interrupt Vector Table (IVT)** stored in low memory, loading the ISR's starting address into the PC.
6. **ISR Execution:** The Interrupt Service Routine runs. Typical operations: read/write the device's data register, clear the device's interrupt flag, signal end-of-I/O (EOT), and update memory buffers.
7. **Return from Interrupt (IRET/RETI):** The ISR ends with a special **IRET** instruction that pops the saved PC and flags from the stack, restoring the main program to its original state.

### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Step

- **Why INTA exists?** It differentiates a *legitimate* interrupt from electrical noise on the IRQ line. It also allows the CPU to arbitrate when multiple devices interrupt simultaneously.
- **Why save PC and flags?** The ISR may modify flags (e.g., by arithmetic). If not saved, the main program would resume with corrupted condition codes, producing subtle bugs.
- **Why vectored interrupts?** Without vectoring, the CPU would have to poll every device to find the source — defeating the purpose of interrupts. The vector number is a hardware-defined device ID.
- **Why a stack?** The stack supports **nested interrupts** (an ISR may itself be interrupted by a higher-priority device) through LIFO context stacking.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates the analytical expressions used in KTU board problems comparing I/O methods and analyzing interrupt performance. **Note the use of `\vert` instead of `|` for absolute value to preserve markdown table integrity.**

| **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Description** | **Unit** |
|---|---|---|---|
| CPU Utilization (Programmed I/O) | $U_{CPU}^{PIO} = 1$ | CPU is 100% busy in tight polling loop | dimensionless |
| CPU Utilization (Interrupt I/O) | $U_{CPU}^{INT} = \dfrac{T_{prog}}{T_{prog} + T_{ISR}}$ | Fraction of time CPU does useful work | dimensionless |
| Interrupt Latency | $t_{L} = t_{finish} + t_{save} + t_{vector}$ | Time from IRQ assertion to ISR start | $\mu s$ or cycles |
| Maximum Device Data Rate | $R_{max} = \dfrac{1}{t_{L} + t_{ISR}}$ | Highest rate CPU can service one device | transfers/sec |
| Total Interrupt Service Time | $T_{ISR} = t_{save} + t_{routine} + t_{restore}$ | Full duration CPU is inside ISR | $\mu s$ |
| Throughput (n devices, polling) | $TP_{polling} = \dfrac{n}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} t_{poll,i}}$ | Aggregate polling throughput | ops/sec |
| Round-Trip Time per Interrupt | $T_{RT} = t_{L} + T_{ISR} + t_{resume}$ | Total cycle time for one interrupt | $\mu s$ |
| I/O Bound Condition | $t_{ISR} \cdot f_{device} \ge 1$ | Device requests faster than CPU can serve | dimensionless |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Interrupt Driven I/O is the **backbone of every modern computing system**:

- **Operating Systems:** The OS kernel's interrupt handlers (Linux `request_irq()`, Windows ISR/DPC) drive keyboards, mice, network cards, disk controllers, and timers.
- **Microcontrollers (8051, ARM Cortex-M):** The entire concept of "real-time response" in embedded systems (anti-lock braking, pacemakers, industrial PLCs) depends on prioritized interrupts.
- **Networking:** NICs use interrupts to signal packet arrival, freeing the CPU to perform other tasks between packets.
- **Power Management:** Modern CPUs enter low-power sleep states and are **woken exclusively by interrupts** — a concept called "tickless kernel" in Linux.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Maximum Sustainable Data Rate of an Interrupt-Driven Device

**Problem Setup:** Given an Interrupt Service Routine that takes $T_{ISR}$ microseconds to complete and an interrupt latency of $t_{L}$ microseconds, derive the maximum data rate (transfers per second) at which a single I/O device can be serviced without losing data.

**Assumptions (explicitly stated for KTU valuation credit):**

- The device is double-buffered or has a holding register, so one transfer may complete while the previous ISR is still running.
- The CPU is dedicated to this single device (no higher-priority interrupts preempt the current ISR).
- Interrupts arrive in a periodic stream at frequency $f_{d}$ (Hz).

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{cycle} &= t_{L} + T_{ISR} + t_{resume} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

The CPU can only begin servicing the $(n+1)$-th interrupt **after** it has fully completed servicing the $n$-th interrupt. Therefore, the minimum allowable interval between two consecutive interrupt requests equals the total round-trip service time $T_{cycle}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{min} &= t_{L} + T_{ISR} + t_{resume} \\
\dfrac{1}{f_{d,max}} &= t_{L} + T_{ISR} + t_{resume} \\
f_{d,max} &= \dfrac{1}{t_{L} + T_{ISR} + t_{resume}} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* If the device requests data faster than $f_{d,max}$, the request rate exceeds the service rate, and the device's buffer will overflow. This is the **fundamental interrupt saturation condition**.

**Conclusion:** The maximum sustainable data rate is the reciprocal of the total interrupt round-trip time.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: CPU Utilization Comparison Between Programmed and Interrupt I/O

**Problem Setup:** A CPU running at $f_{clk} = 100$ MHz executes a main program loop that takes $T_{prog} = 900\ \mu s$ per iteration. A polled I/O device is checked at the end of every iteration, with each poll consuming $t_{poll} = 100\ \mu s$. Compare CPU utilization for the polled approach versus an interrupt-driven approach where the ISR takes $T_{ISR} = 5\ \mu s$ and the device interrupts once per iteration.

**Step A — Polled I/O Utilization:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{polled,total} &= T_{prog} + t_{poll} = 900 + 100 = 1000\ \mu s \\
U_{CPU}^{polled} &= \dfrac{T_{prog}}{T_{polled,total}} = \dfrac{900}{1000} = 0.90 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* In polled mode, the CPU spends 100% of its time inside the loop, but 10% of that is "wasted" on the device check.

**Step B — Interrupt-Driven Utilization:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{INT,total} &= T_{prog} + T_{ISR} = 900 + 5 = 905\ \mu s \\
U_{CPU}^{INT} &= \dfrac{T_{prog}}{T_{INT,total}} = \dfrac{900}{905} \approx 0.9945 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* The interrupt approach recovers the 95 µs saved by not polling, boosting useful work from 90% to ~99.45%.

**Step C — Wasted-Time Ratio:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta U &= U_{CPU}^{INT} - U_{CPU}^{polled} = 0.9945 - 0.9000 = 0.0945 \\
\text{Percentage Gain} &= \dfrac{\Delta U}{U_{CPU}^{polled}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{0.0945}{0.90} \times 100\% \approx 10.5\% \\
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conclusion:* Interrupt-driven I/O reclaimed 10.5% of CPU bandwidth in this scenario. **For slower devices (larger $t_{poll}$), the savings are dramatically higher** — this is the key insight tested in KTU numericals.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — I/O Method Comparison Table

This is a high-yield comparative matrix frequently appearing in KTU Module 4 questions:

| **Criterion** | **Programmed I/O** | **Interrupt Driven I/O** | **DMA (for reference)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| CPU Involvement | 100% (polling loop) | Only during ISR (low %) | Negligible (only setup) |
| Hardware Complexity | Lowest | Medium (PIC, INTA logic) | Highest (DMA controller) |
| Latency to First Response | Equal to poll interval | $t_{L}$ (very low) | High (bus arbitration) |
| Best Suited For | Simple, fast devices | Moderate-speed, event-driven devices | High-bandwidth block transfers |
| Data Loss Risk | None (CPU is always there) | Yes, if $f_{d} > f_{d,max}$ | Yes, if DMA buffer overflows |
| Example Device | Status register read | Keyboard, UART, Timer | Disk, Network, Audio |
| Throughput Bound | $f_{clk} / t_{poll}$ | $1 / T_{cycle}$ | $f_{bus} \times W_{bus}$ |

---

### 3.4 Step-by-Step Interrupt Vector Calculation (8085 Example)

**Problem:** The 8085 microprocessor receives an interrupt, and the device supplies the vector number $N = 4C_{H}$ on the data bus during the INTA cycle. Calculate the address of the ISR in the Interrupt Vector Table.

**Step-by-Step:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{IVT Base Address (8085)} &= 8 \times N \\
\text{Address of ISR} &= 8 \times (4C_{H}) \\
&= 8 \times 76_{D} \\
&= 608_{D} \\
&= 0260_{H} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* The 8085 multiplies the vector number by 8 (since each vector occupies 8 bytes: 2 bytes for JMP + unused). The ISR is then stored at this computed address. For $N = 4C_{H} = 76_{D}$, the ISR begins at $0260_{H}$, $0261_{H}$, ..., $0267_{H}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Valuation Insight:** Examiners award full marks only if the student explicitly shows the **base-16 to base-10 conversion** of the vector number, the **multiplication by 8** with a clear comment, and the **final hexadecimal conversion** of the result. Skipping any one step costs 1–2 marks.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Interrupt Driven I/O

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the data path and control path interactions between the CPU, the Programmable Interrupt Controller (PIC), and multiple I/O devices. **All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed to avoid Mermaid reserved-keyword collisions.**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CPU_BLOCK[CPU CORE]
        CPU_A[CPU Register File]
        CPU_B[ALU and Control Unit]
        CPU_C[Stack Pointer and PSW]
        CPU_D[Instruction Decoder]
    end

    subgraph PIC_BLOCK[INTERRUPT CONTROLLER 8259A PIC]
        PIC_A[IR Request Register IRR]
        PIC_B[Priority Resolver PR]
        PIC_C[In Service Register ISR_REG]
        PIC_D[Vector Generator VG]
    end

    subgraph IO_BLOCK[PERIPHERAL DEVICES]
        DEV1[Keyboard Controller IRQ1]
        DEV2[UART Serial Port IRQ3]
        DEV3[Timer Chip IRQ0]
        DEV4[DMA Controller IRQ7]
    end

    subgraph MEM_BLOCK[MEMORY SUBSYSTEM]
        MEM_A[Interrupt Vector Table IVT]
        MEM_B[ISR Code Region]
        MEM_C[Main Program Region]
    end

    DEV1 -->|IRQ1 Signal| PIC_A
    DEV2 -->|IRQ3 Signal| PIC_A
    DEV3 -->|IRQ0 Signal| PIC_A
    DEV4 -->|IRQ7 Signal| PIC_A

    PIC_A --> PIC_B
    PIC_B --> PIC_C
    PIC_C --> PIC_D
    PIC_D -->|INT Pulse| CPU_D
    CPU_D -->|INTA Acknowledge| PIC_D

    PIC_D -.->|Vector Number N| CPU_D
    CPU_D -->|Fetch Vector Address 8N| MEM_A
    MEM_A -->|Load ISR Entry Address| CPU_D
    CPU_D -->|Branch to ISR| MEM_B

    CPU_C <-.->|Push and Pop PC and Flags| CPU_D
    CPU_A <-.-> CPU_B

    MEM_B -->|IRET Return| MEM_C
```

### 4.2 Interrupt Lifecycle Sequence Diagram (Temporal Flow)

This diagram shows the **chronological sequence** of an interrupt cycle, including the temporal relationship between the main program execution and ISR invocation.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant MainP as Main Program
    participant CPU as CPU Core
    participant DEV as I/O Device
    participant PIC as Interrupt Controller
    participant ISR as ISR Handler
    participant STK as System Stack

    MainP->>CPU: Execute instruction N
    CPU-->>MainP: Instruction N complete
    MainP->>CPU: Execute instruction N+1
    DEV->>PIC: Assert IRQ line
    PIC->>CPU: INT pin activated
    Note over CPU: Finish current instruction first
    CPU->>PIC: INTA acknowledge
    PIC-->>CPU: Place vector number on data bus
    CPU->>STK: Push PC and flags
    CPU->>ISR: Branch to ISR start address
    ISR->>DEV: Read data register
    DEV-->>ISR: Data word
    ISR->>DEV: Clear interrupt flag
    ISR->>STK: Execute IRET
    STK-->>CPU: Pop PC and flags
    CPU->>MainP: Resume instruction N+2
```

### 4.3 Interrupt Priority Resolution Flow (Nested Interrupts)

This flowchart describes how the **8259 PIC** arbitrates simultaneous interrupts using its priority resolver.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START([Multiple IRQs Asserted]) --> SET[Set corresponding bits in IRR]
    SET --> CHECK{Any higher priority pending in ISR_REG}
    CHECK -- Yes --> WAIT[Mask lower priority, wait]
    CHECK -- No --> GRANT[Grant to highest priority device]
    GRANT --> SETISR[Set bit in ISR_REG]
    SETISR --> GEN[Generate vector and pulse INT to CPU]
    GEN --> INTA[Wait for CPU INTA response]
    INTA --> VEC[Place vector on bus]
    VEC --> RUNISR[CPU jumps to ISR]
    RUNISR --> EOI[End of ISR sends EOI command]
    EOI --> CLR[Clear corresponding ISR_REG bit]
    CLR --> REENTRY[Check for pending IRQs]
    REENTRY --> SET
    WAIT --> SET
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Define interrupt driven I/O. List any two advantages of interrupt driven I/O over programmed I/O.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks: 1 + 2 split):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Interrupt driven I/O is a data transfer technique in which the I/O device alerts the CPU via an interrupt request signal when it is ready for data transfer, allowing the CPU to execute other instructions until interrupted.

**Advantages (2 Marks — 1 each):**

1. **CPU is freed from polling** — The CPU does not waste execution cycles in a busy-wait loop checking device status, so CPU utilization for the main program increases significantly.
2. **Faster response time** — Since the device interrupts the moment it is ready, the average waiting time between "device ready" and "data transfer" is reduced from the poll interval (in programmed I/O) to just the interrupt latency.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** The 1-mark definition must include the words "asynchronous" or "event-driven" or "on request" to be considered complete. A vague "CPU handles I/O" definition loses the mark.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**What is an Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)? Mention the role of the IRET instruction in 8086.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks: 2 + 1 split):**

**ISR (2 Marks):** An Interrupt Service Routine (ISR) is a specialized subroutine stored in memory whose starting address is recorded in the Interrupt Vector Table (IVT). When an interrupt occurs, the CPU automatically transfers control to the ISR, which services the requesting device by reading its data register, clearing its interrupt flag, and performing any required data handling. Multiple ISRs exist in a system — one per interrupt source — and they execute with interrupts potentially disabled or re-enabled based on priority.

**Role of IRET (1 Mark):** The IRET (Interrupt Return) instruction is the final instruction of every ISR. It pops the saved IP, CS, and flags from the stack in the reverse order they were pushed during the interrupt acknowledge, thereby restoring the CPU to the exact state it was in before the interrupt. Execution then continues at the instruction following the one that was interrupted.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Explain the interrupt driven I/O data transfer scheme with a neat block diagram. Compare it with programmed I/O in terms of CPU utilization and response time. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the interrupt handling sequence in the 8086 microprocessor. What is the function of the Interrupt Descriptor Table (IDT)? **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Block Diagram Description (2 Marks):**
In interrupt driven I/O, the I/O device is connected to the CPU through an interrupt request line (IRQ). When the device is ready, it sends an interrupt signal on the IRQ line. The CPU, which is executing the main program, suspends the current instruction, saves its context (PC and flags) onto the stack, and transfers control to the Interrupt Service Routine via the interrupt vector. The ISR performs the data transfer, then returns control to the main program using the IRET instruction.

**Working (3 Marks — 1.5 each for two phases):**
- **Phase 1 — Normal Execution:** The CPU executes the main program. The I/O device operates independently and prepares data in its buffer.
- **Phase 2 — Interrupt Servicing:** Upon device-ready signal → IRQ asserted → CPU completes current instruction → INTA cycle → vector fetched → state saved on stack → ISR executes → IRET restores state → main program resumes.

**Comparison Table (2 Marks):**

| **Parameter** | **Programmed I/O** | **Interrupt Driven I/O** |
|---|---|---|
| CPU Utilization | Low (CPU wastes cycles polling) | High (CPU only enters ISR briefly) |
| Response Time | Equals poll interval (worst case) | Equals interrupt latency (typically much lower) |
| Throughput for fast devices | Poor (overheads swamp CPU) | Excellent (CPU is free for other work) |

---

**Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**8086 Interrupt Handling Sequence (5 Marks — 1 each for steps):**

1. **Interrupt Request (INTR pin asserted):** An external device raises INTR high, signalling an interrupt request to the 8086.
2. **Instruction Completion:** The 8086 finishes the current instruction and checks the IF (Interrupt Flag). If IF=1 (interrupts enabled), it proceeds.
3. **INTA Cycle:** The 8086 performs two consecutive INTA cycles. During the first, no data is read; during the second, the interrupting device places an 8-bit interrupt type number on the data bus.
4. **Stack Pushes:** The 8086 pushes the **Flags Register**, then **CS**, then **IP** onto the stack in that order (high-to-low address).
5. **Vector Lookup:** The 8086 multiplies the interrupt type by 4 to obtain the IDT entry address, then loads the 4-byte pointer (IP and CS) from that location.
6. **IF Clearing:** The 8086 automatically clears IF and TF to disable further maskable interrupts and single-stepping during the ISR.
7. **ISR Execution and IRET:** The ISR runs; the final IRET instruction pops IP, CS, and Flags in reverse order, restoring the main program.

**Function of the IDT (2 Marks):**
The Interrupt Descriptor Table (IDT) in the 8086 is a 1-KB table located in memory at the address specified by the IDTR register. It contains 256 entries, each of 4 bytes (2-byte offset IP and 2-byte segment CS), giving the starting address of every ISR. The interrupt type number supplied by the device indexes into this table. **The IDT replaces the older fixed IVT of the 8085, allowing ISRs to be located anywhere in the 1-MB address space** — a major architectural enhancement.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #1:** A common error is stating that "the IDT stores the ISR code" — it does NOT. The IDT stores the **starting address (pointer)** of the ISR, not the routine itself. Confusing these two loses 1 mark per occurrence.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the working of the 8259 Programmable Interrupt Controller (PIC). What are the functions of the IRR, ISR, and IPR registers inside the 8259? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An interrupt driven system has interrupt latency $t_{L} = 5\ \mu s$ and ISR execution time $T_{ISR} = 20\ \mu s$. If the device generates interrupts at a rate of 30 kHz, determine whether the system can keep up. If not, calculate the minimum ISR time required to handle the load. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**8259 PIC Block Diagram Description (3 Marks):**
The 8259A PIC is an 8-input (cascadeable to 64) interrupt controller. Its eight IRQ pins connect to eight devices. When one or more devices assert IRQs, the corresponding bits in the **Interrupt Request Register (IRR)** are set. The **Priority Resolver (PR)** examines the IRR and the **In-Service Register (ISR_REG)** to choose the highest-priority pending request. The selected request is then signaled to the CPU via the INT pin. The CPU responds with INTA, during which the **Vector Generator (VG)** places the pre-programmed interrupt vector on the data bus. The 8259 also accepts an **End of Interrupt (EOI)** command from the CPU at the end of the ISR to clear the corresponding ISR_REG bit.

**Register Functions (3 Marks — 1 each):**

- **IRR (Interrupt Request Register):** An 8-bit register where each bit corresponds to one IRQ line. A bit is set when the corresponding device requests service and remains set until the request is acknowledged.
- **ISR (In-Service Register):** An 8-bit register that tracks which interrupts are currently being serviced. The bit corresponding to the active interrupt is set when the INTA cycle occurs and is cleared only when the CPU issues an EOI command.
- **IPR (Interrupt Priority Resolver):** A logic block (not a single register) that determines the highest-priority request among the set bits in the IRR, considering the contents of the ISR_REG to enforce priority nesting.

**Additional Note (1 Mark):** The 8259 supports four priority modes — **Fully Nested Mode (FNM)**, **Special Fully Nested Mode (SFNM)**, **Rotating Mode**, and **Special Mask Mode** — selected via the OCW (Operation Command Words).

---

**Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Calculate Maximum Sustainable Rate (2 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{cycle} &= t_{L} + T_{ISR} = 5 + 20 = 25\ \mu s \\
f_{d,max} &= \dfrac{1}{T_{cycle}} = \dfrac{1}{25 \times 10^{-6}} = 40{,}000 \text{ Hz} = 40 \text{ kHz} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compare with Actual Rate (1 Mark):**

$$
f_{device} = 30 \text{ kHz} < f_{d,max} = 40 \text{ kHz}
$$

**Conclusion:** Yes, the system **can keep up** with the 30 kHz interrupt rate because $f_{device} < f_{d,max}$.

**Step 3 — Calculate Minimum ISR Time to Handle 30 kHz Load (2 Marks):**

If we relax the problem and assume we want to *just barely* support 30 kHz:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{cycle}^{min} &= \dfrac{1}{f_{device}} = \dfrac{1}{30{,}000} = 33.33\ \mu s \\
T_{ISR}^{min} &= T_{cycle}^{min} - t_{L} = 33.33 - 5 = 28.33\ \mu s \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation and Safety Margin (2 Marks):**

The ISR can be up to $28.33\ \mu s$ long while still supporting 30 kHz. In practice, designers target **$T_{ISR} \le 0.7 \times T_{ISR}^{min}$** to leave a 30% safety margin for jitter, context-save overhead, and cache misses. Hence, the recommended $T_{ISR} \le 19.83\ \mu s$. Since the current $T_{ISR} = 20\ \mu s$ exceeds this, **the system is functionally adequate but operationally risky** — a single cache miss could cause buffer overrun.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #2:** A frequent mistake is to forget the **units conversion** when computing $f_{d,max}$. The interrupt period must be expressed in **seconds**, not microseconds, before inverting. Another common error is computing $T_{ISR}^{min}$ without subtracting the latency $t_{L}$ — this gives a *total cycle time*, not the *ISR-only* time. Both errors cost 1 mark each.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Interrupt Driven I/O** is an asynchronous mechanism where the I/O device notifies the CPU via an IRQ line; the CPU responds by executing an ISR.
- [x] The **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)** is the device-specific handler stored in memory, indexed by the **interrupt vector number**.
- [x] The **INTA (Interrupt Acknowledge)** cycle differentiates real interrupts from noise and allows vectored dispatch.
- [x] The CPU must **save its state (PC + Flags)** on the stack before entering the ISR, and the **IRET** instruction restores it.
- [x] **Interrupt Latency ($t_{L}$)** = time from IRQ assertion to ISR start; dominated by instruction completion time.
- [x] The **8259 PIC** uses three internal registers: **IRR** (pending requests), **ISR_REG** (in-service), and **IPR** (priority resolver).
- [x] Maximum sustainable device rate: $\mathbf{f_{d,max} = 1 / (t_{L} + T_{ISR})}$.
- [x] CPU Utilization for interrupt I/O: $\mathbf{U_{CPU}^{INT} = T_{prog} / (T_{prog} + T_{ISR})}$.
- [x] Interrupt priority is essential when multiple devices request simultaneously; nested interrupts use the stack for context preservation.
- [x] **Edge-triggered vs Level-triggered** IRQs: edge is latched once, level requires the device to hold the line until acknowledged.
- [x] **Common Mistake:** Confusing the **Interrupt Vector Table (stores pointers)** with the **ISR code itself** — the table holds addresses, not code.
- [x] **Common Mistake:** Forgetting that interrupt-driven I/O can still **lose data** if the device rate exceeds the service rate.
- [x] **KTU Golden Rule:** Always quote the formula $T_{cycle} = t_{L} + T_{ISR} + t_{resume}$ when solving interrupt numericals — partial marks are awarded for the correct framework even if the arithmetic fails.
- [x] The 8086 IDT is **256 entries × 4 bytes = 1 KB**; the index is **type × 4**.
- [x] Real-world usage: every OS kernel, every embedded microcontroller, every network stack, and every low-power sleep mode relies on interrupt-driven I/O.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Direct Memory Access
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMRT403 - COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE AND MICROCONTROLLER > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Direct Memory Access # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Direct Memory Access (DMA)** is a high-performance data transfer technique in computer architecture that allows peripheral devices to transfer data directly to or from main memory **without continuous intervention by the Central Processing Unit (CPU)**. A dedicated hardware controller, known as the **DMA Controller (DMAC)**, takes control of the system bus (Address, Data, and Control buses) from the CPU for the duration of the data transfer, executes the I/O operation autonomously, and returns bus control to the CPU upon completion. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** DMA is a method of transferring data between I/O devices and main memory without involving the CPU in the actual data movement. The DMA controller manages the entire transfer by issuing memory addresses and control signals to read/write data blocks. ### Key Terminology - **DMAC (DMA Controller):** The dedicated LSI peripheral chip (e.g., Intel 8237, 8257) that manages bus arbitration and executes data block transfers. - **HOLD / HLDA (Hold Acknowledge):** Bus request and grant signals between CPU and DMAC in polled/forced mode. - **DREQ / DACK:** DMA Request (from device to DMAC) and DMA Acknowledge (from DMAC to device). - **Burst Transfer:** Consecutive transfer of an entire data block in one uninterrupted bus acquisition. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition) Imagine you are a **head chef (CPU)** in a busy restaurant kitchen. Normally, every time a waiter (I/O device) brings an order, you (CPU) personally walk to the pantry (RAM), fetch the ingredients, and return to cook. This wastes your valuable cooking time on simple fetching tasks. Now, hire a **junior assistant (DMA Controller)**. You hand the assistant a list (channel descriptor) saying: *"Go to shelf B-3, bring 50 tomatoes, and put them on counter C-7. Don't bother me until you're done."* The assistant takes the master key (system bus), fetches all 50 tomatoes in one go, and only interrupts you once the entire batch is delivered. **That is exactly what DMA does.** The CPU is freed from the menial task of byte-by-byte fetching, allowing it to execute compute-bound instructions while data moves in parallel. This is the essence of **I/O parallelism** — the foundational idea behind high-throughput systems like disk controllers, network cards, sound cards, and graphics accelerators. --- ## 1.3 Why DMA is Essential — Performance Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > Without DMA, every I/O byte would require the CPU to execute multiple instructions (at least 3–5 machine cycles per byte transferred via programmed I/O). For high-speed peripherals, this CPU overhead would saturate the processor, leaving no cycles for actual computation. Standard metrics (assume an 8-bit ISA with a 1 MHz clock = **1 µs cycle time**): | Operation | CPU Cycles Required | Time Consumed | |-----------|--------------------|--------------| | Programmed I/O (per byte) | ~5 cycles | **5 µs** | | Interrupt-driven I/O (per byte) | ~7 cycles | **7 µs** | | DMA (per byte, burst) | ~0 (CPU free) | **1 µs hardware** | > **Key Insight:** A DMA transfer of a 1 KB block using burst mode takes roughly **1024 µs** of bus time, but the CPU is free for nearly the entire duration — only interrupted once for setup and once for completion. Programmed I/O would consume **5120 µs** of *CPU* time for the same block. --- ## 1.4 DMA vs. Other I/O Techniques > [!NOTE] > KTU frequently tests the comparison between **Programmed I/O**, **Interrupt-driven I/O**, and **DMA**. Memorize the table below — it is a guaranteed 3-mark question in the ESE. | Feature | Programmed I/O | Interrupt I/O | DMA | |---------|---------------|---------------|-----| | CPU involvement per byte | Yes (polling) | Yes (ISR entry/exit) | No (only setup/teardown) | | Transfer speed | Slow | Moderate | **Fastest** | | Hardware complexity | Low | Medium | High (DMAC needed) | | Suitable for | Simple, slow devices | Moderate-speed devices | **High-speed, block transfers** | | Bus acquisition | CPU always owns | CPU always owns | **DMAC temporarily owns** | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The DMA Transfer Procedure (Step-by-Step Operational Logic) The DMA transfer follows a precise six-stage sequence. Understanding this sequence is critical for solving KTU numerical problems on bus cycle calculation. **Step 1 — Device Request:** The peripheral device (e.g., a hard disk) requires data movement. It asserts the **DREQ** (DMA Request) line to the DMAC, signaling the need for a transfer. **Step 2 — Bus Request to CPU:** The DMAC, recognizing the pending DREQ, sends a **HRQ (Hold Request)** or **BUSREQ** signal to the CPU, requesting control of the system bus. **Step 3 — Bus Grant:** If the CPU is not using the bus (and is not in a critical atomic operation), it completes the current machine cycle, places its address, data, and control lines into **high-impedance (tri-state)**, and asserts **HLDA (Hold Acknowledge)** back to the DMAC. **Step 4 — Address & Control Issuance:** The DMAC now acts as the bus master. It places the **starting memory address** on the address bus, asserts **MEMRD/MEMWR** (memory read/write) and **IOR/IOW** (I/O read/write) control signals as required, and transfers one data word. **Step 5 — Address Update & Count Decrement:** The DMAC internally increments (or decrements) the address register and decrements the word-count register. It repeats Step 4 until the word count reaches zero. **Step 6 — Bus Release:** Upon completion (TC — Terminal Count signal goes high), the DMAC deasserts HRQ. The CPU deasserts HLDA, resumes its address/data lines, and continues program execution from where it was suspended. --- ## 2.2 The Three Modes of DMA Transfer (HIGH-YIELD TOPIC) This is one of the most heavily tested sub-topics in KTU ESE. Every student must master these three modes. ### Mode 1 — Burst Mode (Block Transfer Mode) - The DMAC acquires the bus and transfers the **entire data block** in one continuous operation. - The CPU remains **completely idle** for the entire transfer duration. - **Maximum throughput**, but causes the longest CPU stall. - **Use case:** Hard disk reads/writes, memory-to-memory copies of large buffers. - **Disadvantage:** Can cause **CPU starvation** in real-time systems. ### Mode 2 — Cycle Stealing Mode - The DMAC transfers **one byte/word per bus acquisition**, then releases the bus back to the CPU. - The DMAC "steals" single memory cycles between CPU instruction executions. - **Lower throughput** than burst mode, but **CPU latency** is bounded and predictable. - **Use case:** Serial ports, audio devices, real-time streaming where CPU response time matters. - **Why called "stealing"?** Because each DMA transfer takes one memory cycle from the CPU, slightly slowing CPU execution. ### Mode 3 — Transparent Mode - The DMAC performs data transfer **only when the CPU is executing an operation that does not use the system bus** (e.g., during instruction decode or ALU operation — internal cycles). - The CPU is **never idle** and **never slowed** — pure parallel operation. - **Disadvantage:** Lowest throughput; requires complex bus-cycle detection logic in the DMAC. - **Use case:** Rare in practice; mostly theoretical/historical. --- ## 2.3 DMA Transfer Types (Combinational Logic) The DMAC can execute three combinations of source and destination: | Type | Source | Destination | Example | |------|--------|-------------|---------| | **Memory → I/O** | RAM | Peripheral | Disk write | | **I/O → Memory** | Peripheral | RAM | Disk read | | **Memory → Memory** | RAM Block A | RAM Block B | `memcpy()` acceleration | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memory-to-Memory DMA** requires the DMAC to have **two address registers** and **two count registers** (one set for source, one for destination). This doubles the internal complexity and is supported by advanced DMACs like the Intel 8237. --- ## 2.4 Bus Arbitration in DMA — The Two Master Problem When both CPU and DMAC need the bus simultaneously, a **bus arbiter** resolves the conflict. There are two primary arbitration schemes: ### (a) Centralized Arbitration - A dedicated arbiter chip decides who gets the bus. - Signals: `BUSREQ` (from DMAC) and `BUSGRANT` (to DMAC). - Used in **single-master microcontrollers (8051, 8086)**. ### (b) Daisy-Chain Arbitration - Multiple DMACs are wired in priority order. - Highest-priority device closest to the arbiter. - Used in **multi-master systems (PCI bus, modern PCs)**. > [!NOTE] > In the **8051 microcontroller**, the DMA concept is implemented via **Dual-Port RAM** or **FIFO buffers** rather than a dedicated DMAC chip — the 8051 does NOT have a native HOLD/HLDA pin in its standard architecture, but extended variants (e.g., 80C320) include it. --- ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > This is the master reference card for solving DMA numerical problems. Print this and pin it to your study wall. | # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Unit / Notes | |---|--------------------|-----------|--------------| | 1 | **Total Transfer Time (Burst Mode)** | $T_{DMA} = N \times T_{cycle}$ | $N$ = number of bytes, $T_{cycle}$ = memory cycle time | | 2 | **Effective CPU Utilization (Burst)** | $U_{CPU} = 1 - \dfrac{T_{DMA}}{T_{total}}$ | Ratio; 1.0 = CPU always free | | 3 | **Transfer Rate (Throughput)** | $R_{DMA} = \dfrac{N}{T_{DMA}} = \dfrac{1}{T_{cycle}}$ | Bytes per second | | 4 | **Burst Mode CPU Stall** | $T_{stall} = N \times T_{cycle}$ | Full block duration | | 5 | **Cycle Steal CPU Stall** | $T_{stall} = N \times t_{steal}$ | $t_{steal}$ ≈ 1–2 cycles per byte | | 6 | **Bus Cycle Time (DRAM)** | $T_{cycle} \approx 2 \times T_{access}$ | DRAM typically requires RAS+CAS | | 7 | **Number of Channels** | Typically **4, 8, or 16** channels per DMAC | Each channel = one independent device | | 8 | **Word Count Register Size** | $n$ bits $\Rightarrow$ Max transfer $= 2^n$ bytes | 16-bit counter ⇒ 64 KB max | | 9 | **Setup Overhead** | $T_{setup} \approx 5$–$10$ CPU cycles | One-time cost per DMA operation | | 10 | **Channel Priority** | Fixed, Rotating, or Programmable | Determines which channel wins bus arbitration | --- ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility DMA is not an academic concept — it is the **backbone of modern computing I/O**: - **Storage Controllers (SATA/NVMe):** Transfer megabytes per second via DMA, freeing the CPU for OS scheduling. - **Network Interface Cards (NICs):** Use DMA to move packet data directly to/from RAM buffers (zero-copy networking). - **Graphics Processing Units (GPUs):** Massive DMA engines move textures and framebuffers across PCIe. - **Audio DSPs:** Cycle-stealing DMA feeds sample data to DACs at precise timing. - **Embedded Systems (STM32, ESP32):** DMA peripherals offload ADC, UART, SPI, and I²S transfers from the Cortex-M core. > **Engineering Insight:** In a modern smartphone, **over 70% of all memory traffic** is DMA-initiated. The CPU only directly accesses memory for instruction fetches and compute operands — almost all bulk data movement is invisible to the CPU. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Total Transfer Time in Burst Mode **Problem Setup:** A DMA controller transfers a block of $N$ bytes from a peripheral to memory in burst mode. The system bus cycle time is $T_{cycle}$. Derive an expression for (a) the total transfer time, (b) the percentage of CPU time freed, and (c) the effective throughput. **Given:** - Block size: $N = 1024$ bytes (1 KB) - Memory cycle time: $T_{cycle} = 200$ ns - CPU clock period: $T_{CPU} = 50$ ns - CPU instructions required for programmed I/O of one byte: $K = 5$ ### Step 1 — Write the burst transfer time equation In burst mode, the DMAC transfers all $N$ bytes consecutively without releasing the bus. Each byte consumes exactly one memory cycle. $$ T_{DMA} = N \times T_{cycle} $$ **Explanation:** Each of the $N$ bytes requires the DMAC to perform one memory write cycle (or one read cycle, depending on direction). With no idle gaps, the total time is the product of the number of transfers and the per-transfer cycle duration. ### Step 2 — Substitute numerical values $$ T_{DMA} = 1024 \times 200 \times 10^{-9} $$ $$ T_{DMA} = 204800 \times 10^{-9} = 2.048 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{s} $$ $$ \boxed{T_{DMA} = 204.8\ \mu s} $$ ### Step 3 — Calculate CPU time for equivalent programmed I/O Without DMA, the CPU would execute $K = 5$ instructions per byte. Each instruction takes one CPU cycle. $$ T_{CPU,PIO} = N \times K \times T_{CPU} $$ $$ T_{CPU,PIO} = 1024 \times 5 \times 50 \times 10^{-9} $$ $$ T_{CPU,PIO} = 256000 \times 10^{-9} = 2.56 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{s} $$ $$ \boxed{T_{CPU,PIO} = 256\ \mu s} $$ ### Step 4 — Compute the CPU time saved (efficiency gain) $$ \Delta T = T_{CPU,PIO} - T_{setup,DMA} $$ Assume DMA setup takes $T_{setup} = 10$ CPU cycles = $10 \times 50\ \text{ns} = 500\ \text{ns}$. $$ \Delta T = 256000\ \text{ns} - 500\ \text{ns} = 255500\ \text{ns} $$ $$ \boxed{\Delta T \approx 255.5\ \mu s} $$ This represents **99.8% CPU time freed** for the duration of the transfer. ### Step 5 — Compute effective DMA throughput $$ R_{DMA} = \frac{N}{T_{DMA}} = \frac{1024}{2.048 \times 10^{-4}} $$ $$ \boxed{R_{DMA} = 5 \times 10^{6}\ \text{bytes/sec} = 5\ \text{MB/s}} $$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cycle Stealing vs Burst Mode CPU Stall Comparison **Problem Setup:** Compare the CPU stall characteristics of burst mode vs. cycle stealing for an $N$-byte transfer, where the CPU executes $K$ instructions per cycle on average. ### Burst Mode Stall Analysis The CPU is fully stalled for the entire block duration: $$ T_{stall,burst} = N \times T_{cycle} $$ ### Cycle Stealing Stall Analysis In cycle stealing, the DMAC takes one memory cycle per byte, but these cycles are interleaved with CPU cycles. If the CPU normally uses fraction $\alpha$ of memory cycles, then per byte the DMAC steals only the fraction $(1 - \alpha)$ of available idle cycles. Worst case (CPU always wants the bus): $$ T_{stall,steal} = N \times (1\ \text{cycle steal per byte}) \times T_{cycle} = N \times T_{cycle} $$ But the key difference is **granularity**: burst stalls the CPU for one long period, while cycle stealing causes many short stalls interleaved with CPU work. For real-time response: $$ \text{Max CPU latency (cycle steal)} = 1 \times T_{cycle} $$ $$ \text{Max CPU latency (burst)} = N \times T_{cycle} $$ **Conclusion:** Cycle stealing provides bounded latency; burst mode provides maximum throughput. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Maximum Block Size from Word Count Register **Given:** A DMA controller uses a 16-bit word count register. Each count decrement corresponds to one byte transferred. ### Step 1 — Express the register range A 16-bit unsigned register can hold values from $0$ to $2^{16} - 1$. $$ \text{Max count} = 2^{16} - 1 = 65535 $$ ### Step 2 — Interpret the value in bytes Since each count unit equals one byte: $$ N_{max} = 65535\ \text{bytes} = 64\ \text{KB} - 1\ \text{byte} $$ ### Step 3 — General formula for $n$-bit counter $$ \boxed{N_{max} = 2^n - 1\ \text{bytes}} $$ For a 24-bit counter: $N_{max} = 2^{24} - 1 = 16\ \text{MB} - 1\ \text{byte}$. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right DMA Mode > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU examiners frequently ask: *"Given scenario X, which DMA mode would you choose and why?"* Use this matrix as your decision framework. | Application Scenario | Burst Mode | Cycle Stealing | Transparent Mode | |---------------------|------------|----------------|------------------| | Hard disk block read | ✅ **Best** | ⚠ Acceptable | ❌ Wastes throughput | | Audio streaming playback | ❌ Causes glitches | ✅ **Best** | ⚠ Acceptable | | Memory-to-memory `memcpy` | ✅ **Best** | ⚠ Slower | ❌ Impractical | | Serial port data logging | ❌ Overkill | ✅ **Best** | ⚠ Acceptable | | Real-time control loop | ❌ Catastrophic | ✅ **Best** | ⚠ Marginal | | Video frame capture | ✅ **Best** | ⚠ Causes tearing | ❌ Too slow | | Sensor data acquisition | ⚠ Risky | ✅ **Best** | ✅ Acceptable | **Decision Rule of Thumb:** - **Block size > 1 KB AND latency-insensitive → Burst Mode** - **Block size < 1 KB OR latency-critical → Cycle Stealing** - **Theoretical/educational only → Transparent Mode** # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — DMA Controller Architecture The diagram below illustrates the internal architecture of a generic DMA Controller (modeled after the Intel 8237) and its interface to the CPU, memory, and peripherals. ```mermaid flowchart TB CPU["CPU
Bus Master"] -->|HOLD Request| Arbiter["Bus Arbiter
Centralized Logic"] Arbiter -->|HLDA Grant| CPU Arbiter -->|Bus Grant| DMAC DMAC["DMA Controller DMAC"] --> DR["Direction Register
R or W"] DMAC --> MAR["Memory Address
Register 16-bit"] DMAC --> CR["Word Count

List any two advantages of using DMA over programmed I/O.** **Model Answer:** **Direct Memory Access (DMA)** is a data transfer technique in which a dedicated hardware controller (DMAC) transfers data directly between I/O devices and main memory **without continuous CPU intervention**. The CPU initiates the transfer by providing the DMAC with source address, destination address, and byte count, after which the DMAC independently manages the bus arbitration and data movement. **Advantages of DMA over programmed I/O:** 1. **CPU Offloading:** The CPU is freed from the burden of fetching and storing each byte, allowing it to execute computational tasks in parallel — overall system throughput increases significantly. 2. **Faster Transfer:** DMA can sustain higher data rates (limited only by memory cycle time) since there is no instruction-fetch overhead between successive byte transfers, making it suitable for high-speed devices like disks and network cards. --- ### Question A2 [KTU University Exam - June 2024] **Q: Differentiate between burst mode and cycle stealing mode of DMA transfer.** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Burst Mode | Cycle Stealing Mode | |-----------|------------|---------------------| | **Bus Ownership** | DMAC holds bus for entire block | DMAC returns bus after each byte | | **CPU Activity** | Completely idle during transfer | CPU works between steals | | **Throughput** | Maximum (full bus bandwidth) | Lower (interleaved with CPU) | | **CPU Latency** | Unbounded — long stalls possible | Bounded — one cycle per steal | | **Use Case** | Disk block transfers | Audio, serial, real-time I/O | | **Setup Complexity** | Simple | Requires bus cycle interleaving logic | --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question Choice A [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022] **Q: Explain in detail the working of a DMA controller. Describe the different modes of DMA transfer with suitable diagrams. (14 Marks)** #### Part (a) — DMA Controller Working (7 Marks) A **DMA Controller (DMAC)** is a peripheral LSI chip that manages data transfers between I/O devices and main memory autonomously. The working procedure is as follows: **Step 1 — Initialization:** The CPU programs the DMAC's internal registers with three key parameters: (i) the **starting memory address** (loaded into the Address Register), (ii) the **number of bytes/words** to transfer (loaded into the Word Count Register), and (iii) the **transfer direction** and **mode** (loaded into the Control/Command Register). **Step 2 — Device Request:** When the peripheral device has data ready, it asserts the **DREQ** line connected to the DMAC. **Step 3 — Bus Arbitration:** The DMAC sends a **HOLD** request to the CPU. The CPU completes its current instruction, tri-states its bus drivers, and asserts **HLDA** back to the DMAC. The DMAC is now the **bus master**. **Step 4 — Data Transfer:** The DMAC places the memory address on the address bus, asserts **MEMRD** or **MEMWR** along with **IOR** or **IOW** as needed, and one data word is transferred. The address is auto-incremented (or auto-decremented) and the word count is decremented. **Step 5 — Completion:** When the word count reaches zero, the DMAC asserts the **Terminal Count (TC)** signal, deasserts HOLD, and the CPU resumes execution. **Internal Registers of a Typical DMAC:** - **Base Address Register** — starting address of memory block - **Word Count Register** — number of bytes to transfer - **Mode Register** — selects burst / cycle steal / transparent - **Mask Register** — enables/disables individual channels - **Command Register** — global DMA enable, priority scheme - **Status Register** — indicates TC reached, request pending #### Part (b) — DMA Transfer Modes with Diagrams (7 Marks) The three standard DMA transfer modes are: **1. Burst Mode (Block Transfer):** The DMAC acquires the bus and transfers the entire data block in one continuous stream. The CPU remains completely idle. Used for high-speed, non-real-time transfers like disk reads. **2. Cycle Stealing Mode:** The DMAC transfers one byte/word per bus acquisition, then releases the bus back to the CPU. This interleaves DMA transfers with CPU execution, bounding the maximum CPU latency to one memory cycle per byte. **3. Transparent Mode:** The DMAC transfers data only during CPU cycles when the system bus is not in use (e.g., during internal ALU operations). The CPU is never stalled. Throughput is the lowest of the three modes. **Comparison Summary Table (write this in the exam):** | Feature | Burst | Cycle Stealing | Transparent | |---------|-------|----------------|-------------| | CPU Idle? | Yes, fully | No, partially | No | | Transfer Rate | Highest | Moderate | Lowest | | Hardware Complexity | Low | Medium | High | | Suitable Application | Bulk disk I/O | Real-time audio/video | Rare | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students lose 2–3 marks by **failing to draw a block diagram** of the DMAC in Part (a). Even a simple hand-drawn block with arrows for DREQ, HOLD, HLDA, MEMRD, MEMWR earns full marks. Additionally, **do not confuse "transparent mode" with "cycle stealing"** — transparent means the CPU is unaware of the DMA activity; cycle stealing still causes CPU cycle delays. --- ### Question Choice B [KTU University Exam - June 2023] **Q: With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a DMA controller. Compare DMA-based data transfer with programmed I/O and interrupt-driven I/O in terms of speed, CPU overhead, and complexity. (14 Marks)** #### Part (a) — DMA Controller Architecture (7 Marks) The DMA controller consists of the following major blocks: **1. Address Register:** Holds the current memory address being accessed. It is incremented or decremented after each transfer based on the mode register setting. **2. Word Count Register:** A 16-bit down-counter that holds the number of bytes/words remaining to be transferred. When it reaches zero, the Terminal Count (TC) output is asserted. **3. Control and Mode Register:** Stores configuration bits for transfer direction (R/W), transfer mode (burst/steal/transparent), auto-initialize enable, and address increment/decrement. **4. Mask Register:** Allows the CPU to individually enable or disable DMA channels without affecting other channels. **5. Request and Acknowledge Logic:** Handles DREQ inputs from peripherals and generates DACK outputs. Includes priority resolver (fixed or rotating) for multiple simultaneous requests. **6. Bus Interface Unit:** Drives the system address, data, and control buses when the DMAC is the bus master. #### Part (b) — DMA vs Programmed I/O vs Interrupt I/O (7 Marks) **Programmed I/O:** The CPU actively polls the device and reads/writes each byte using dedicated I/O instructions. Simple to implement but consumes 100% of CPU time during transfer, making it unsuitable for high-speed or concurrent operations. **Interrupt-Driven I/O:** The device signals the CPU via an interrupt when data is ready. The CPU runs an ISR to handle each byte. Better than programmed I/O for slow devices but still incurs overhead (register save/restore) per byte. **DMA:** The DMAC handles the entire transfer; the CPU is interrupted only at the start and end. Maximum throughput and minimum CPU overhead. **Detailed Comparison Table:** | Parameter | Programmed I/O | Interrupt I/O | DMA | |-----------|---------------|---------------|-----| | Speed | Slowest | Moderate | **Fastest** | | CPU Overhead | Very High (polling) | Moderate (ISR) | **Very Low** | | Hardware Complexity | Low | Medium | **High** | | CPU Cycles per Byte | ~5 cycles | ~7 cycles | **0–1 cycles** | | Suitable Devices | Keyboards, switches | Serial ports, printers | **Disks, NICs, GPUs** | | Latency | Determined by poll rate | Determined by ISR entry | **Bounded by mode** | **Conclusion:** For high-bandwidth, block-oriented transfers, DMA is unambiguously superior. Programmed I/O is appropriate only for very slow devices where simplicity outweighs performance considerations. > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark Loss:** Students often write *"DMA is faster than I/O"* — this is too vague. Be specific: state **which mode of DMA** (burst gives highest throughput, cycle stealing bounds latency) and **quantify the speedup** (e.g., "DMA at 5 MB/s vs programmed I/O at 0.2 MB/s"). Quantitative answers earn full marks in KTU. --- ## 5.3 Numerical Practice Problem [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024] **Q: A DMA controller transfers 2048 bytes from memory to an I/O device in burst mode. The memory cycle time is 250 ns and the CPU takes 5 clock cycles per byte if it performs programmed I/O, with a clock period of 100 ns. Calculate:** **(a) Total DMA transfer time** **(b) CPU time saved compared to programmed I/O** **(c) Effective throughput of the DMA transfer** **Model Solution:** **(a) Total DMA transfer time:** $$ T_{DMA} = N \times T_{cycle} = 2048 \times 250 \times 10^{-9} $$ $$ \boxed{T_{DMA} = 5.12 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{s} = 512\ \mu s} $$ **(b) CPU time for programmed I/O:** $$ T_{CPU,PIO} = N \times K \times T_{CPU} = 2048 \times 5 \times 100 \times 10^{-9} $$ $$ T_{CPU,PIO} = 1.024 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{s} = 1024\ \mu s $$ Assuming DMA setup overhead = $T_{setup} = 1\ \mu s$: $$ \Delta T = 1024 - 1 = 1023\ \mu s $$ $$ \boxed{\text{CPU Time Saved} \approx 1023\ \mu s\ (\approx 99.9\%)} $$ **(c) Effective throughput:** $$ R_{DMA} = \frac{N}{T_{DMA}} = \frac{2048}{5.12 \times 10^{-4}} $$ $$ \boxed{R_{DMA} = 4 \times 10^{6}\ \text{bytes/sec} = 4\ \text{MB/s}} $$ --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final 60-second Revision Checklist — Read this before every KTU exam on DMA:** - ✅ DMA = **Direct Memory Access** = data transfer without CPU intervention - ✅ DMAC (DMA Controller) is the dedicated hardware that manages transfers - ✅ **Three modes**: Burst (fastest, CPU idle), Cycle Stealing (bounded latency), Transparent (zero CPU impact, lowest throughput) - ✅ **Key signals**: DREQ (device→DMAC), DACK (DMAC→device), HOLD (DMAC→CPU), HLDA (CPU→DMAC) - ✅ **Three transfer types**: Memory↔I/O, I/O↔Memory, Memory↔Memory - ✅ CPU is interrupted only **twice**: once at start (setup) and once at end (terminal count) - ✅ DMA is faster than both programmed I/O and interrupt I/O for **block transfers** - ✅ Maximum transfer size for $n$-bit counter: $N_{max} = 2^n - 1$ bytes - ✅ Throughput formula: $R_{DMA} = \dfrac{N}{T_{cycle} \times N} = \dfrac{1}{T_{cycle}}$ bytes/sec - ✅ **Always draw the HOLD/HLDA handshake** sequence diagram when explaining DMA working — examiners reward visual answers - ✅ In 8051 microcontrollers, DMA is implemented via **dual-port RAM / FIFOs** rather than dedicated HOLD/HLDA pins - ✅ For KTU numericals: always state the **mode** (burst/cycle steal) before plugging values into formulas - ✅ **Mnemonic:** "**D**evice sends **D**REQ → **D**MAC sends **H**OLD → CPU grants **H**LDA → **D**MAC sends **D**ACK" - ✅ Memory-to-memory DMA needs **two address registers** in the DMAC (advanced feature)
